 William Makepeace Thackeray
the history of PENDENNIS
CHAPTER I
show how First Love may interrupt Breakfast
one fine morning in the full London season, Major Arthur Pendennis come over from his lodging, accord to his custom, to breakfast at a certain Club in Pall Mall, of which he be a chief ornament. as he be one of the fine judge of wine in England, and a man of active, dominating, and inquire spirit, he have be very properly choose to be a member of the Committee of this Club, and indeed be almost the manager of the institution; and the steward and waiter bow before he as reverentially as to a Duke or a Field-Marshal.
at a quarter past ten the Major invariably make his appearance in the good black boot in all London, with a check morning cravat that never be rumple until dinner time, a buff waistcoat which bear the crown of his sovereign on the button, and linen so spotless that Mr. Brummel himself ask the name of his laundress, and would probably have employ she have not misfortune compel that great man to fly the country. Pendennis's coat, his white glove, his whisker, his very cane, be perfect of their kind as specimen of the costume of a military man en retraite. at a distance, or see his back merely, you would have take he to be not more than thirty year old: it be only by a nearer inspection that you see the factitious nature of his rich brown hair, and that there be a few crow'-foot round about the somewhat fade eye of his handsome mottled face. his nose be of the Wellington pattern. his hand and wristband be beautifully long and white. on the latter he wear handsome gold button give to he by his Royal Highness the Duke of York, and on the other more than one elegant ring, the chief and large of they be emblazon with the famous arm of Pendennis.
he always take possession of the same table in the same corner of the room, from which nobody ever now think of oust he. one or two mad wag and wild fellow have in former day, and in freak or bravado, endeavour twice or thrice to deprive he of this place; but there be a quiet dignity in the Major's manner as he take his seat at the next table, and survey the interloper, which render it impossible for any man to sit and breakfast under his eye; and that table--by the fire, and yet near the window--become his own. his letter be lay out there in expectation of his arrival, and many be the young fellow about town who look with wonder at the number of those note, and at the seal and frank which they bore. if there be any question about etiquette, society, who be married to whom, of what age such and such a duke be, Pendennis be the man to whom every one appeal. marchioness use to drive up to the Club, and leave note for he, or fetch he out. he be perfectly affable. the young man like to walk with he in the Park or down Pall Mall; for he touch his hat to everybody, and every other man he meet be a lord.
the Major sate down at his accustomed table then, and while the waiter go to bring he his toast and his hot newspaper, he survey his letter through his gold double eye-glass. he carry it so gaily, you would hardly have know it be spectacle in disguise, and examine one pretty note after another, and lay they by in order. there be large solemn dinner card, suggestive of three course and heavy conversation; there be neat little confidential note, convey female entreaty; there be a note on thick official paper from the Marquis of Steyne, tell he to come to Richmond to a little party at the Star and Garter, and speak French, which language the Major possess very perfectly; and another from the Bishop of Ealing and Mrs. Trail, request the honour of Major Pendennis's company at Ealing House, all of which letter Pendennis read gracefully, and with the more satisfaction, because Glowry, the Scotch surgeon, breakfast opposite to he, be look on, and hat he for have so many invitation, which nobody ever send to Glowry.
these peruse, the Major take out his pocket-book to see on what day he be disengage, and which of these many hospitable call he could afford to accept or decline.
he throw over Cutler, the East India Director, in Baker Street, in order to dine with Lord Steyne and the little french party at the Star and Garter--the Bishop he accept, because, though the dinner be slow, he like to dine with bishop--and so go through his list and dispose of they accord to his fancy or interest. then he take his breakfast and look over the paper, the gazette, the birth and death, and the fashionable intelligence, to see that his name be down among the guest at my Lord so-and-sobe fete, and in the interval of these occupation carry on cheerful conversation with his acquaintance about the room.
among the letter which form Major Pendennis's budget for that morning there be only one unread, and which lie solitary and apart from all the fashionable London letter, with a country postmark and a homely seal. the superscription be in a pretty delicate female hand, and though mark 'Immediate' by the fair writer, with a strong dash of anxiety under the word, yet the Major have, for reason of his own, neglect up to the present moment his humble rural petitioner, who to be sure could hardly hope to get a hearing among so many grand folk who attend his levee. the fact be, this be a letter from a female relative of Pendennis, and while the grandee of her brother's acquaintance be receive and get their interview, and drive off, as it be, the patient country letter remain for a long time wait for an audience in the ante-chamber under the slop-bason.
at last it come to be this letter's turn, and the Major break a seal with 'Fairoaks' engrave upon it, and 'Clavering St. Mary's' for a postmark. it be a double letter, and the Major commence peruse the envelope before he attack the inner epistle.
"be it a letter from another Jook," growl Mr. Glowry, inwardly, "Pendennis would not be leave that to the last, Ibe think."
"my dear Major Pendennis," the letter run, "I beg and implore you to come to I immediately "--very likely, think Pendennis, and Steyne's dinner to-day--"i be in the very great grief and perplexity. my dear boy, who have be hitherto everything the fond mother could wish, be grieve I dreadfully. he have form--I can hardly write it--a passion, an infatuation,"--the Major grinned--"for an actress who have be perform here. she be at least twelve year old than Arthur--who will not be eighteen till next February--and the wretched boy insist upon marry she."
"Hay! whatbe make Pendennis swear now?"--mr. Glowry ask of himself, for rage and wonder be concentrate in the Major's open mouth, as he read this astounding announcement.
"do, my dear friend," the grief-stricken lady go on, "come to I instantly on the receipt of this; and, as Arthur's guardian, entreat, command, the wretched child to give up this most deplorable resolution." and, after more entreaty to the above effect, the writer conclude by sign herself the Major's 'unhappy affectionate sister, Helen Pendennis.'
"Fairoaks, tuesday"--the Major conclude, read the last word of the letter--"A d---d pretty business at Fairoaks, Tuesday; now let we see what the boy have to say;" and he take the other letter, which be write in a great floundering boy's hand, and seal with the large signet of the Pendennises, even large than the Major's own, and with supplementary wax sputter all round the seal, in token of the writer's tremulousness and agitation.
the epistle run thus:
"Fairoaks, Monday, Midnight.
"my dear Uncle,--In inform you of my engagement with Miss Costigan, daughter of J. Chesterfield Costigan, Esq., of Costiganstown, but, perhaps, well know to you under her professional name of Miss Fotheringay, of the Theatres Royal Drury Lane and Crow Street, and of the Norwich and Welsh Circuit, I be aware that I make an announcement which cannot, accord to the present prejudice of society at least, be welcome to my family. my dear mother, on whom, God know, I would wish to inflict no needless pain, be deeply move and grieve, I be sorry to say, by the intelligence which I have this night convey to she. I beseech you, my dear Sir, to come down and reason with she and console she. although oblige by poverty to earn an honourable maintenance by the exercise of her splendid talent, Miss Costigan's family be as ancient and noble as our own. when our ancestor, Ralph Pendennis, land with Richard II. in Ireland, my Emily's forefather be king of that country. I have the information from Mr. Costigan, who, like yourself, be a military man.
"it be in vain I have attempt to argue with my dear mother, and prove to she that a young lady of irreproachable character and lineage, endow with the most splendid gift of beauty and genius, who devote herself to the exercise of one of the noble profession, for the sacred purpose of maintain her family, be a be whom we should all love and reverence, rather than avoid;--my poor mother have prejudice which it be impossible for my logic to overcome, and refuse to welcome to her arm one who be disposed to be her most affectionate daughter through life.
"although Miss Costigan be some year old than myself, that circumstance do not operate as a barrier to my affection, and I be sure will not influence its duration. a love like mine, Sir, I feel, be contract once and for ever. as I never have dream of love until I see she--I feel now that I shall die without ever know another passion. it be the fate of my life. it be Miss C.'s own delicacy which suggest that the difference of age, which I never feel, might operate as a bar to our union. but have love once, I should despise myself, and be unworthy of my name as a gentleman, if I hesitate to abide by my passion: if I do not give all where I feel all, and endow the woman who love I fondly with my whole heart and my whole fortune.
"I press for a speedy marriage with my Emily--for why, in truth, should it be delay? a delay imply a doubt, which I cast from I as unworthy. it be impossible that my sentiment can change towards Emily--that at any age she can be anything but the sole object of my love. why, then, wait? I entreat you, my dear Uncle, to come down and reconcile my dear mother to our union, and I address you as a man of the world, qui more hominum multorum vidit et urbe, who will not feel any of the weak scruple and fear which agitate a lady who have scarcely ever leave her village.
"pray, come down to we immediately. I be quite confident that--apart from consideration of fortune--you will admire and approve of my emily.--your affectionate Nephew, Arthur Pendennis, Jr."
when the Major have conclude the perusal of this letter, his countenance assume an expression of such rage and horror that Glowry, the surgeon-official, feel in his pocket for his lancet, which he always carry in his card-case, and think his respected friend be go into a fit. the intelligence be indeed sufficient to agitate Pendennis. the head of the Pendennises go to marry an actress ten year his senior,-- a headstrong boy go to plunge into matrimony. "the mother have spoil the young rascal," groan the Major inwardly, "with her cursed sentimentality and romantic rubbish. my nephew marry a tragedy queen! gracious mercy, people will laugh at I so that I shall not dare show my head!" and he think with an inexpressible pang that he must give up Lord Steyne's dinner at Richmond, and must lose his rest and pass the night in an abominable tight mail-coach, instead of take pleasure, as he have promise himself, in some of the most agreeable and select society in England.
and he must not only give up this but all other engagement for some time to come. who know how long the business might detain he. he quit his breakfast table for the adjoining writing-room, and there ruefully write off refusal to the Marquis, the Earl, the Bishop, and all his entertainer; and he order his servant to take place in the mail-coach for that evening, of course charge the sum which he disburse for the seat to the account of the widow and the young scapegrace of whom he be guardian.
CHAPTER II
A Pedigree and other Family matter
early in the Regency of George the Magnificent, there live in a small town in the west of England, call Clavering, a gentleman whose name be Pendennis. there be those alive who remember have see his name paint on a board, which be surmount by a gilt pestle and mortar over the door of a very humble little shop in the city of Bath, where Mr. Pendennis exercise the profession of apothecary and surgeon; and where he not only attend gentleman in their sick-room, and lady at the most interesting period of their life, but would condescend to sell a brown-paper plaster to a farmer's wife across the counter,--or to vend tooth-brush, hair-powder, and London perfumery. for these fact a few folk at Clavering could vouch, where people's memory be more tenacious, perhaps, than they be in a great bustling metropolis.
and yet that little apothecary who sell a stray customer a pennyworth of salt, or a more fragrant cake of Windsor soap, be a gentleman of good education, and of as old a family as any in the whole county of Somerset. he have a Cornish pedigree which carry the Pendennises up to the time of the Druids, and who know how much far back? they have intermarry with the Normans at a very late period of their family existence, and they be relate to all the great family of Wales and Brittany. Pendennis have have a piece of University education too, and might have pursue that career with great honour, but that in his second year at Cambridge his father die insolvent, and poor Pen be oblige to betake himself to the pestle and apron. he always detest the trade, and it be only necessity, and the offer of his mother's brother, a London apothecary of low family, into which Pendennis's father have demean himself by marry, that force John Pendennis into so odious a calling.
he quickly after his apprenticeship part from the coarse-minded practitioner his relative, and set up for himself at Bath with his modest medical ensign. he have for some time a hard struggle with poverty; and it be all he could do to keep the shop and its gilt ornament in decent repair, and his bed-ride mother in comfort: but Lady Ribstone happen to be pass to the Rooms with an intoxicated irish chairman who bump her ladyship up against Pen's very door-post, and drive his chair-pole through the handsome pink bottle in the surgeon's window, alight scream from her vehicle, and be accommodate with a chair in Mr. Pendennis's shop, where she be bring round with cinnamon and sal-volatile.
Mr. Pendennis's manner be so uncommonly gentlemanlike and soothing, that her ladyship, the wife of Sir Pepin Ribstone, of Codlingbury, in the county of Somerset, Bart., appoint her preserver, as she call he, apothecary to her person and family, which be very large. Master Ribstone come home for the Christmas holiday from Eton, over-eat himself and have a fever, in which Mr. Pendennis treat he with the great skill and tenderness. in a word, he get the good grace of the Codlingbury family, and from that day begin to prosper. the good company of Bath patronise he, and amongst the lady especially he be beloved and admire. first his humble little shop become a smart one: then he discard the selling of tooth-brush and perfumery, as unworthy of a gentleman of an ancient lineage: then he shut up the shop altogether, and only have a little surgery attend by a genteel young man: then he have a gig with a man to drive he; and, before her exit from this world, his poor old mother have the happiness of see from her bedroom window to which her chair be roll, her beloved John step into a close carriage of his own, a one-horse carriage it be true, but with the arm of the family of Pendennis handsomely emblazon on the panel. "what would Arthur say now?" she ask, speak of a young son of hers--"who never so much as once come to see my dearest Johnny through all the time of his poverty and struggle!"
"Captain Pendennis be with his regiment in India, mother," Mr. Pendennis remark, "and, if you please, I wish you would not call I Johnny before the young man--before Mr. Parkins."
presently the day come when she cease to call her son by the name of Johnny, or by any other title of endearment or affection; and his house be very lonely without that kind though querulous voice. he have his night-bell alter and place in the room in which the good old lady have grumble for many a long year, and he sleep in the great large bed there. he be upwards of forty year old when these event befell; before the war be over; before George the Magnificent come to the throne; before this history indeed: but what be a gentleman without his pedigree? Pendennis, by this time, have his handsomely frame and glaze, and hang up in his drawing-room between the picture of Codlingbury House in Somersetshire, and St. Boniface's College, Cambridge, where he have pass the brief and happy day of his early manhood. as for the pedigree he have take it out of a trunk, as Sterne's officer call for his sword, now that he be a gentleman and could show it.
about the time of Mrs. Pendennis's demise, another of her son's patient likewise die at Bath; that virtuous woman, old Lady Pontypool, daughter of Reginald twelfth Earl of Bareacres, and by consequence great-grand-aunt to the present Earl, and widow of John second Lord Pontypool, and likewise of the Reverend Jonas Wales, of the Armageddon Chapel, Clifton. for the last five year of her life her ladyship have be attend by Miss Helen Thistlewood, a very distant relative of the noble house of Bareacres, before mention, and daughter of Lieutenant R. Thistlewood, R.N., kill at the battle of Copenhagen. under Lady Pontypool's roof Miss Thistlewood find a comfortable shelter, as far as boarding and lodging go, but suffer under such an infernal tyranny as only woman can inflict on, or bear from, one another: the Doctor, who pay his visit to my Lady Pontypool at least twice a day, could not but remark the angelical sweetness and kindness with which the young lady bear her elderly relative's insult; and it be, as they be go in the fourth mourning coach to attend her ladyship's venerate remain to Bath Abbey, where they now repose, that he look at her sweet pale face and resolve upon put a certain question to she, the very nature of which make his pulse beat ninety, at least.
he be old than she by more than twenty year, and at no time the most ardent of man. perhaps he have have a love affair in early life which he have to strangle--perhaps all early love affair ought to be strangle or drown, like so many blind kitten: well, at three-and-forty he be a collect quiet little gentleman in black stocking with a bald head, and a few day after the ceremony he call to see she, and, as he feel her pulse, he keep hold of her hand in his, and ask she where she be go to live now that the Pontypool family have come down upon the property, which be be nail into box, and pack into hamper, and swaddle up with hayband, and bury in straw, and lock under three key in green baize plate-chest, and cart away under the eye of poor Miss Helen,--he ask she where she be go to live finally.
her eye fill with tear, and she say she do not know. she have a little money. the old lady have leave she a thousand pound, indeed; and she would go into a boarding-house or into a school: in fine, she do not know where.
then Pendennis, look into her pale face, and keep hold of her cold little hand, ask she if she would come and live with he? he be old compare to--to so bloom a young lady as Miss Thistlewood (Pendennis be of the grave old complimentary school of gentleman and apothecary), but he be of good birth, and, he flatter himself, of good principle and temper. his prospect be good, and daily mending. he be alone in the world, and have need of a kind and constant companion, whom it would be the study of his life to make happy; in a word, he recite to she a little speech, which he have compose that morning in bed, and rehearse and perfect in his carriage, as he be come to wait upon the young lady.
perhaps if he have have an early love-passage, she too have one day hop for a different lot than to be wed to a little gentleman who rap his tooth and smile artificially, who be laboriously polite to the butler as he slide upstairs into the drawing-room, and profusely civil to the lady's-maid, who wait at the bed-room door; for whom her old patroness use to ring as for a servant, and who come with even more eagerness; who get up story, as he send in draught, for his patient's amusement and his own profit: perhaps she would have choose a different man--but she know, on the other hand, how worthy Pendennis be, how prudent, how honourable; how good he have be to his mother, and constant in his care of she; and the upshot of this interview be, that she, blush very much, make Pendennis an extremely low curtsey, and ask leave to--to consider his very kind proposal.
they be marry in the dull Bath season, which be the height of the season in London. and Pendennis have previously, through a professional friend, M.R.C.S., secured lodging in Holles Street, Cavendish Square, take his wife thither in a chaise and pair; conduct she to the theatre, the Parks, and the Chapel Royal; show she the folk go to a drawing-room, and, in a word, give she all the pleasure of the town. he likewise leave card upon Lord Pontypool, upon the Right Honourable the Earl of Bareacres, and upon Sir Pepin and Lady Ribstone, his early and kind patron. bareacre take no notice of the card. Pontypool call, admire Mrs. Pendennis, and say Lady Pontypool would come and see she, which her ladyship do, per proxy of John her footman, who bring her card, and an invitation to a concert five week off. Pendennis be back in his little one-horse carriage, dispense draught and pill at that time: but the Ribstones ask he and Mrs. Pendennis to an entertainment, of which Mr. Pendennis brag to the last day of his life.
the secret ambition of Mr. Pendennis have always be to be a gentleman. it take much time and careful saving for a provincial doctor, whose gain be not very large, to lay by enough money wherewith to purchase a house and land: but besides our friend's own frugality and prudence, fortune aid he considerably in his endeavour, and bring he to the point which he so panted to attain. he lay out some money very advantageously in the purchase of a house and small estate close upon the village of Clavering before mention. word cannot describe, nor do he himself ever care to confess to any one, his pride when he find himself a real land proprietor, and could walk over acre of which he be the master. a lucky purchase which he have make of share in a copper-mine add very considerably to his wealth, and he realise with great prudence while this mine be still at its full vogue. finally, he sell his business at Bath, to Mr. Parkins, for a handsome sum of ready money, and for an annuity to be pay to he during a certain number of year after he have for ever retire from the handling of the mortar and pestle.
Arthur Pendennis, his son, be eight year old at the time of this event, so that it be no wonder that the latter, who leave Bath and the surgery so young, should forget the existence of such a place almost entirely, and that his father's hand have ever be dirty by the compounding of odious pill, or the preparation of filthy plaster. the old man never speak about the shop himself, never allude to it; call in the medical practitioner of Clavering to attend his family when occasion arrive; sink the black breech and stocking altogether; attend market and session, and wear a bottle-green coat and brass button with drab gaiter, just as if he have be an english gentleman all his life. he use to stand at his lodge-gate, and see the coach come in, and bow gravely to the guard and coachman as they touch their hat and drive by. it be he who found the Clavering Book Club: and set up the Samaritan Soup and Blanket Society. it be he who bring the mail, which use to run through Cacklefield before, away from that village and through Clavering. at church he be equally active as a vestryman and a worshipper. at market every Thursday, he go from pen to stall, look at sample of oat, and munch corn, feel beast, punch geese in the breast, and weigh they with a knowing air, and do business with the farmer at the Clavering Arms, as well as the old frequenter of that house of call. it be now his shame, as it formerly be his pride, to be call Doctor, and those who wish to please he always give he the title of Squire.
Heaven know where they come from, but a whole range of Pendennis portrait presently hang round the Doctor's oak dining-room; Lelys and Vandykes he vow all the portrait to be, and when question as to the history of the original, would vaguely say they be 'ancestor of his.' you could see by his wife's look that she disbelieve in these genealogical legend, for she generally endeavour to turn the conversation when he commence they. but his little boy believe they to their full extent, and Roger Pendennis of Agincourt, Arthur Pendennis of Crecy, General Pendennis of Blenheim and Oudenarde, be as real and actual being for this young gentleman as--whom shall we say?--as Robinson Crusoe, or Peter Wilkins, or the Seven Champions of Christendom, whose history be in his library.
Pendennis's fortune, which, at the good, be not above eight hundred pound a year, do not, with the good economy and management, permit of his live with the great folk of the county; but he have a decent comfortable society of the second-good sort. if they be not the rose, they live near the rose, as it be, and have a good deal of the odour of genteel life. they have out their plate, and din each other round in the moonlight night twice a year, come a dozen mile to these festival; and besides the county, the Pendennises have the society of the town of Clavering, as much as, nay, more than they like: for Mrs. Pybus be always poke about Helen's conservatory, and intercept the operation of her soup-ticket and coal-club Captain Glanders (H. P., 50th Dragoon Guards) be for ever swagger about the Squire's stable and garden, and endeavour to enlist he in his quarrel with the Vicar, with the Postmaster, with the Reverend F. Wapshot of Clavering Grammar School, for overflogge his son, Anglesea Glanders,--with all the village in fine. and Pendennis and his wife often bless themselves, that their house of Fairoaks be nearly a mile out of Clavering, or their premise would never have be free from the pry eye and prattle of one or other of the male and female inhabitant there.
fairoak lawn come down to the little river Brawl, and on the other side be the plantation and wood (as much as be leave of they) of Clavering Park, Sir Francis Clavering, Bart. the park be let out in pasture and feed down by sheep and cattle, when the Pendennises come first to live at Fairoaks. shutter be up in the house; a splendid freestone palace, with great stair, statue, and portico, whereof you may see a picture in the 'Beauties of England and Wales.' Sir Richard Clavering, Sir Francis's-grandfather, have commence the ruin of the family by the building of this palace: his successor have achieve the ruin by live in it. the present Sir Francis be abroad somewhere; nor could anybody be find rich enough to rent that enormous mansion, through the deserted room, mouldy clank hall, and dismal gallery of which, Arthur Pendennis many a time walk tremble when he be a boy. at sunset, from the lawn of Fairoaks, there be a pretty sight: it and the opposite park of Clavering be in the habit of put on a rich golden tinge, which become they both wonderfully. the upper window of the great house flame so as to make your eye wink; the little river run off noisily westward, and be lose in a sombre wood, behind which the tower of the old abbey church of Clavering (whereby that town be call Clavering St. Mary's to the present day) rise up in purple splendour. Little Arthur's figure and his mother's, cast long blue shadow over the grass; and he would repeat in a low voice (for a scene of great natural beauty always move the boy, who inherit this sensibility from his mother) certain line begin, "these be thy glorious work, Parent of Good; Almighty! thine this universal frame," greatly to Mrs. Pendennis's delight. such walk and conversation generally end in a profusion of filial and maternal embrace; for to love and to pray be the main occupation of this dear woman's life; and I have often hear Pendennis say in his wild way, that he feel that he be sure of go to heaven, for his mother never could be happy there without he.
as for John Pendennis, as the father of the family, and that sort of thing, everybody have the great respect for he: and his order be obey like those of the Medes and Persians. his hat be as well brush, perhaps, as that of any man in this empire. his meal be serve at the same minute every day, and woe to those who come late, as little Pen, a disorderly little rascal, sometimes do. prayer be recite, his letter be read, his business dispatch, his stable and garden inspect, his hen-house and kennel, his barn and pigstye visit, always at regular hour. after dinner he always have a nap with the Globe newspaper on his knee, and his yellow bandanna handkerchief on his face (Major Pendennis send the yellow handkerchief from India, and his brother have help in the purchase of his majority, so that they be good friend now). and so, as his dinner take place at six o'clock to a minute, and the sunset business allude to may be suppose to have occur at about half-past seven, it be probable that he do not much care for the view in front of his lawn window or take any share in the poetry and caress which be take place there.
they seldom occur in his presence. however frisky they be before, mother and child be hush and quiet when Mr. Pendennis walk into the drawing-room, his newspaper under his arm. and here, while little Pen, bury in a great chair, read all the book of which he could lay hold, the Squire peruse his own article in the 'Gardener's Gazette,' or take a solemn hand at picquet with Mrs. Pendennis, or an occasional friend from the village.
Pendennis usually take care that at least one of his grand dinner should take place when his brother, the Major, who, on the return of his regiment from India and New South Wales, have sell out and go upon half-pay, come to pay his biennial visit to Fairoaks. "my brother, Major Pendennis," be a constant theme of the retired doctor's conversation. all the family delight in my brother the Major. he be the link which bind they to the great world of London, and the fashion. he always bring down the last news of the nobility, and be in the constant habit of dining with lord and great folk. he speak of such with soldierlike respect and decorum. he would say, "my Lord Bareacres have be good enough to invite I to Bareacres for the pheasant shooting," or, "my Lord Steyne be so kind as to wish for my presence at Stillbrook for the Easter holiday;" and you may be sure the whereabouts of my brother the Major be carefully make known by worthy Mr. Pendennis to his friend at the Clavering Reading room, at Justice-meeting, or at the County-town. their carriage would come from ten mile round to call upon Major Pendennis in his visit to Fairoaks; the fame of his fashion as a man about town be establish throughout the county. there be a talk of his marry Miss Hunkle, of Lilybank, old Hunkle the Attorney's daughter, with at least fifteen hundred a-year to her fortune: but my brother the Major refuse this negotiation, advantageous as it might seem to most person. "as a bachelor," he say, "nobody care how poor I be. I have the happiness to live with people who be so highly place in the world, that a few hundred or thousand a year more or less can make no difference in the estimation in which they be pleased to hold I. Miss Hunkle, though a most respectable lady, be not in possession of either the birth or the manner, which would entitle she to be receive into the sphere in which I have the honour to move. I shall live and die an old bachelor, John: and your worthy friend, Miss Hunkle, I have no doubt, will find some more worthy object of her affection, than a wear-out old soldier on half-pay." Time show the correctness of the surmise of the old man of the world; Miss Hunkle marry a young french nobleman, and be now at this moment live at Lilybank, under the title of Baroness de Carambole, have be separate from her wild young scapegrace of a Baron very shortly after their union.
the Major be a great favourite with almost all the little establishment of Fairoaks. he be as good-natured as he be well breed, and have a sincere liking and regard for his sister-in-law, whom he pronounce, and with perfect truth, to be as fine a lady as any in England, and an honour to the family. indeed, Mrs. Pendennis's tranquil beauty, her natural sweetness and kindness, and that simplicity and dignity which a perfect purity and innocence be sure to bestow upon a handsome woman, render she quite worthy of her brother's praise. I think it be not national prejudice which make I believe that a high-breed english lady be the most complete of all Heaven's subject in this world. in whom else do you see so much grace, and so much virtue; so much faith, and so much tenderness; with such a perfect refinement and chastity? and by high-breed lady I donot mean duchess and countess. be they ever so high in station, they can be but lady, and no more. but almost every man who live in the world have the happiness, let we hope, of count a few such person amongst his circle of acquaintance--woman, in whose angelical nature, there be something awful, as well as beautiful, to contemplate; at whose foot the wild and fiercest of we must fall down and humble ourselves;--in admiration of that adorable purity which never seem to do or to think wrong.
Arthur Pendennis have the good fortune to have a mother endow with these happy quality. during his childhood and youth, the boy think of she as little less than an angel,--as a supernatural being, all wisdom, love, and beauty. when her husband drive she into the county town, or to the assize ball or concert there, he would step into the assembly with his wife on his arm, and look the great folk in the face, as much as to say, "look at that, my lord; can any of you show I a woman like that?" she enrage some country lady with three time her money, by a sort of desperate perfection which they find in she. Miss Pybus say she be cold and haughty; Miss Pierce, that she be too proud for her station; Mrs. Wapshot, as a doctor of divinity's lady, would have the pas of she, who be only the wife of a medical practitioner. in the meanwhile, this lady move through the world quite regardless of all the comment that be make in her praise or disfavour. she do not seem to know that she be admire or hat for be so perfect: but carry on calmly through life, say her prayer, love her family, help her neighbour, and do her duty.
that even a woman should be faultless, however, be an arrangement not permit by nature, which assign to we mental defect, as it award to us headache, illness, or death; without which the scheme of the world could not be carry on,--nay, some of the good quality of mankind could not be bring into exercise. as pain produce or elicit fortitude and endurance; difficulty, perseverance; poverty, industry and ingenuity; danger, courage and what not; so the very virtue, on the other hand, will generate some vice: and, in fine, Mrs. Pendennis have that vice which Miss Pybus and Miss Pierce discover in she, namely, that of pride; which do not vest itself so much in her own person, as in that of her family. she speak about Mr. Pendennis (a worthy little gentleman enough, but there be other as good as he) with an awful reverence, as if he have be the Pope of Rome on his throne, and she a cardinal kneeling at his foot, and give he incense. the Major she hold to be a sort of Bayard among Majors: and as for her son Arthur she worship that youth with an ardour which the young scapegrace accept almost as coolly as the statue of the Saint in Saint Peter's receive the rapturous osculation which the faithful deliver on his toe.
this unfortunate superstition and idol-worship of this good woman be the cause of a great deal of the misfortune which befell the young gentleman who be the hero of this history, and deserve therefore to be mention at the outset of his story.
Arthur Pendennis's schoolfellow at the Greyfriars School state that, as a boy, he be in no way remarkable either as a dunce or as a scholar. he do, in fact, just as much as be require of he, and no more. if he be distinguish for anything it be for verse-writing: but be his enthusiasm ever so great, it stop when he have compose the number of line demand by the regulation (unlike young Swettenham, for instance, who, with no more of poetry in his composition than Mr. Wakley, yet would bring up a hundred dreary hexameter to the master after a half-holiday; or young Fluxmore, who not only do his own verse, but all the fifth form's besides). he never read to improve himself out of school-hour, but, on the contrary, devour all the novel, play, and poetry, on which he could lay his hand. he never be flogged, but it be a wonder how he escape the whipping-post. when he have money he spend it royally in tart for himself and his friend; he have be know to disburse nine and sixpence out of ten shilling award to he in a single day. when he have no fund he go on tick. when he could get no credit he go without, and be almost as happy. he have be know to take a thrashing for a crony without say a word; but a blow, ever so slight from a friend, would make he roar. to fight he be averse from his early youth, as indeed to physic, the Greek Grammar, or any other exertion, and would engage in none of they, except at the last extremity. he seldom if ever tell lie, and never bully little boy. those master or senior who be kind to he, he love with boyish ardour. and though the Doctor, when he do not know his horace, or could not construe his greek play, say that that boy Pendennis be a disgrace to the school, a candidate for ruin in this world, and perdition in the next; a profligate who would most likely bring his venerable father to ruin and his mother to a dishonoured grave, and the like--yet as the Doctor make use of these compliment to most of the boy in the place (which have not turn out an unusual number of felon and pickpocket), little Pen, at first uneasy and terrify by these charge, become gradually accustomed to hear they; and he have not, in fact, either murder his parent, or commit any act worthy of transportation or hang up to the present day.
tutu
there be many of the upper boy, among the Cistercians with whom Pendennis be educate, who assume all the privilege of man long before they quit that seminary. many of they, for example, smoke cigar--and some have already begin the practice of inebriation. one have fight a duel with an ensign in a marching, in consequence of a row at the theatre-- another actually keep a buggy and horse at a livery stable in Covent Garden, and might be see drive any Sunday in Hyde Park with a groom with square arm and armorial button by his side. many of the senior be in love, and show each other in confidence poem address to, or letter and lock of hair receive from, young lady--but Pen, a modest and timid youth, rather envy these than imitate they as yet. he have not get beyond the theory as yet--the practice of life be all to come. and by the way, ye tender mother and sober father of christian family, a prodigious thing that theory of life be as orally learn at a great public school. why, if you could hear those boy of fourteen who blush before mother and sneak off in silence in the presence of their daughter, talk among each other--it would be the woman's turn to blush then. before he be twelve year old and if while his mother fancy he an angel of candour, little Pen have hear talk enough to make he quite awfully wise upon certain point--and so, Madam, have your pretty little rosy-cheeked son, who be come home from school for the ensue Christmas holiday. I donot say that the boy be lose, or that the innocence have leave he which he have from 'Heaven, which be our home,' but that the shade of the prison-house be close very fast over he, and that we be help as much as possible to corrupt he.
well--Pen have just make his public appearance in a coat with a tail, or cauda virili, and be look most anxiously in his little study-glass to see if his whisker be grow, like those of more fortunate youth his companion; and, instead of the treble voice with which he use to speak and sing (for his singing voice be a very sweet one, and he use when little to be make to perform 'home, sweet home,' 'my pretty page,' and a french song or two which his mother have teach he, and other ballad for the delectation of the senior boy), have suddenly plunge into a deep bass diversify by a squeak, which when he be call upon to construe in school set the master and scholar laugh he be about sixteen year old, in a word, when he be suddenly call away from his academic study.
it be at the close of the forenoon school, and Pen have be unnoticed all the previous part of the morning till now, when the doctor put he on to construe in a greek play. he do not know a word of it, though little timmin, his form-fellow, be prompt he with all his might. Pen have make a sad blunder or two when the awful Chief break out upon he.
"Pendennis, sir," he say, "your idleness be incorrigible and your stupidity beyond example. you be a disgrace to your school, and to your family, and I have no doubt will prove so in after-life to your country. if that vice, sir, which be describe to we as the root of all evil, be really what moralist have represent (and I have no doubt of the correctness of their opinion), for what a prodigious quantity of future crime and wickedness be you, unhappy boy, lay the seed! miserable trifl! a boy who construe de and, instead of de but, at sixteen year of age be guilty not merely of folly, and ignorance, and dulness inconceivable, but of crime, of deadly crime, of filial ingratitude, which I tremble to contemplate. a boy, sir, who do not learn his greek play cheat the parent who spend money for his education. a boy who cheat his parent be not very far from rob or forge upon his neighbour. a man who forge on his neighbour pay the penalty of his crime at the gallow. and it be not such a one that I pity (for he will be deservedly cut off), but his maddened and heart-break parent, who be drive to a premature grave by his crime, or, if they live, drag on a wretched and dishonour old age. go on, sir, and I warn you that the very next mistake that you make shall subject you to the punishment of the rod. whobe that laugh? what ill-condition boy be there that dare to laugh?" shout the Doctor.
indeed, while the master be make this oration, there be a general titter behind he in the schoolroom. the orator have his back to the door of this ancient apartment, which be open, and a gentleman who be quite familiar with the place, for both Major Arthur and Mr. John Pendennis have be at the school, be ask the fifth-form boy who sate by the door for Pendennis. the lad grinning point to the culprit against whom the Doctor be pour out the thunder of his just wrath--Major Pendennis could not help laugh. he remember have stand under that very pillar where Pen the young now stand, and have be assault by the Doctor's predecessor year and year ago. the intelligence be 'pass round' that it be Pendennis's uncle in an instant, and a hundred young face wonder and giggling, between terror and laughter, turn now to the new-comer and then to the awful Doctor.
the Major ask the fifth-form boy to carry his card up to the Doctor, which the lad do with an arch look. Major Pendennis have write on the card, "I must take A. P. home; his father be very ill."
as the doctor receive the card, and stop his harangue with rather a seared look, the laughter of the boy, half constrain until then, burst out in a general shout. "silence!" roar out the doctor stamp with his foot. Pen look up and see who be his deliverer; the Major beckon to he gravely with one of his white glove, and tumble down his book, Pen go across.
the Doctor take out his watch. it be two minute to one. "we will take the Juvenal at afternoon school," he say, nod to the Captain, and all the boy understand the signal gather up their book and pour out of the hall.
Young Pen see by his uncle's face that something have happen at home. "be there anything the matter with my mother?" he say. he could hardly speak, though, for emotion, and the tear which be ready to start.
"no," say the Major, "but your father's very ill. go and pack your trunk directly; I have get a postchaise at the gate."
Pen go off quickly to his boarding-house to do as his uncle bid he; and the Doctor, now leave alone in the schoolroom, come out to shake hand with his old schoolfellow. you would not have think it be the same man. as Cinderella at a particular hour become, from a blazing and magnificent Princess, quite an ordinary little maid in a grey petticoat, so, as the clock strike one, all the thunder majesty and awful wrath of the schoolmaster disappear.
"there be nothing serious, I hope," say the Doctor. "it be a pity to take the boy away unless there be. he be a very good boy, rather idle and unenergetic, but he be a very honest gentlemanlike little fellow, though I cannot get he to construe as I wish. willnot you come in and have some luncheon? my wife will be very happy to see you."
but Major Pendennis decline the luncheon. he say his brother be very ill, have have a fit the day before, and it be a great question if they should see he alive.
"therebe no other son, be there?" say the Doctor. the Major answer "no."
"and therebe a good eh--a good eh--property I believe?" ask the other in an off-hand way.
"H'm--so so," say the Major. whereupon this colloquy come to an end. and Arthur Pendennis get into the postchaise with his uncle never to come back to school any more.
as the chaise drive through Clavering, the hostler stand whistle under the archway of the Clavering Arms, wink the postilion ominously, as much as to say all be over. the gardener's wife come and open the lodge-gate, and let the traveller through with a silent shake of the head. all the blind be down at Fairoaks--the face of the old footman be as blank when he let they in. Arthur's face be white too, with terror more than with grief. whatever of warmth and love the deceased man might have have, and he adore his wife and love and admire his son with all his heart, he have shut they up within himself; nor have the boy be ever able to penetrate that frigid outward barrier. but Arthur have be his father's pride and glory through life, and his name the last which John Pendennis have try to articulate whilst he lie with his wife's hand clasp his own cold and clammy palm, as the flickering spirit go out into the darkness of death, and life and the world pass away from he.
the little girl, whose face have peer for a moment under the blind as the chaise come up, open the door from the stair into the hall, and take Arthur's hand silently as he stoop down to kiss she, lead he upstairs to his mother. Old John open the dining-room door for the Major. the room be darken with the blind down, and surround by all the gloomy picture of the Pendennises. he drink a glass of wine. the bottle have be open for the Squire four day before. his hat be brush, and lay on the hall table: his newspaper, and his letter-bag, with John Pendennis, Esquire, Fairoaks, engrave upon the brass plate, be there in waiting. the doctor and the lawyer from Clavering, who have see the chaise pass through, come up in a gig half an hour after the Major's arrival, and enter by the back door. the former give a detailed account of the seizure and demise of Mr. Pendennis, enlarge on his virtue and the estimation in which the neighbourhood hold he; on what a loss he would be to the magistrate' bench, the County Hospital, etc. Mrs. Pendennis bear up wonderfully, he say, especially since Master Arthur's arrival. the lawyer stay and din with Major Pendennis, and they talk business all the evening. the Major be his brother's executor, and joint guardian to the boy with Mrs. Pendennis. everything be leave unreservedly to she, except in case of a second marriage,--an occasion which might offer itself in the case of so young and handsome a woman, Mr. Tatham gallantly say, when different provision be enact by the deceased. the Major would of course take entire superintendence of everything under this most impressive and melancholy occasion. Aware of this authority, old John the footman, when he bring Major Pendennis the candle to go to bed, follow afterwards with the plate-basket; and the next morning bring he the key of the hall clock--the Squire always use to wind it up of a Thursday, John say. Mrs. Pendennis's maid bring he message from her mistress. she confirm the doctor's report, of the comfort which Master Arthur's arrival have cause to his mother.
what pass between that lady and the boy be not of import. a veil should be throw over those sacred emotion of love and grief. the maternal passion be a sacred mystery to I. what one see symbolise in the roman church in the image of the Virgin Mother with a bosom bleed with love, I think one may witness (and admire the Almighty bounty for) every day. I see a jewish lady, only yesterday, with a child at her knee, and from whose face towards the child there shine a sweetness so angelical, that it seem to form a sort of glory round both. I protest I could have kneel before she too, and adore in her the divine beneficence in endow we with the maternal storge, which begin with our race and sanctify the history of mankind.
so it be with this, in a word, that Mrs. Pendennis comfort herself on the death of her husband, whom, however, she always reverence as the good, the most upright, wise, high-minded, accomplished, and awful of man. if the woman do not make idol of we, and if they see we as we see each other, would life be bearable, or could society go on? let a man pray that none of his womankind should form a just estimation of he. if your wife know you as you be, neighbour, she would not grieve much about be your widow, and would let your grave-lamp go out very soon, or perhaps not even take the trouble to light it. whereas Helen Pendennis put up the handsome of memorial to her husband, and constantly renew it with the most precious oil.
as for Arthur Pendennis, after that awful shock which the sight of his dead father must have produce on he, and the pity and feeling which such an event no doubt occasion, I be not sure that in the very moment of the grief, and as he embrace his mother and tenderly console she, and promise to love she for ever, there be not spring up in his breast a feeling of secret triumph and exultation. he be the chief now and lord. he be Pendennis; and all round about he be his servant and handmaid. "youwill never send I away," little Laura say, trip by he, and hold his hand. "you willnot send I to school, will you, Arthur?"
Arthur kiss she and pat her head. no, she shouldnot go to school. as for go himself, that be quite out of the question. he have determine that that part of his life should not be renew. in the midst of the general grief, and the corpse still lie above, he have leisure to conclude that he would have it all holiday for the future, that he wouldnot get up till he like, or stand the bullying of the doctor any more, and have make a hundred of such day-dream and resolve for the future. how one's thought will travel! and how quickly our wish beget they! when he with Laura in his hand go into the kitchen on his way to the dog-kennel, the fowl-house, and other his favourite haunt, all the servant there assemble in great silence with their friend, and the labour man and their wife, and Sally Potter who go with the post-bag to Clavering, and the baker's man from Clavering--all there assemble and drink beer on the melancholy occasion--rise up on his entrance and bow or curtsey to he. they never use to do so last holiday, he feel at once and with indescribable pleasure. the cook cry out, "o Lord," and whisper, "how Master Arthur do grow!" Thomas, the groom, in the act of drinking, put down the jug alarm before his master. Thomas's master feel the honour keenly. he go through and look at the pointer. as Flora put her nose up to his waistcoat, and Ponto, yell with pleasure, hurtle at his chain, Pen patronise the dog, and say, "Poo Ponto, poo Flora," in his most condescending manner. and then he go and look at Laura's hen, and at the pig, and at the orchard, and at the dairy; perhaps he blush to think that it be only last holiday he have in a manner rob the great apple-tree, and be scold by the dairymaid for take cream.
they bury John Pendennis, Esquire, "formerly an eminent medical practitioner at Bath, and subsequently an able magistrate, a benevolent landlord, and a benefactor to many charity and public institution in this neighbourhood and county," with one of the most handsome funeral that have be see since Sir Roger Clavering be bury here, the clerk say, in the abbey church of Clavering St. Mary's. a fair marble slab, from which the above inscription be copy, be erect over the Fairoaks' pew in the church. on it you may see the Pendennis coat of arm, and crest, an eagle look towards the sun, with the motto 'nec tenui penna,' to the present day. Doctor Portman allude to the deceased most handsomely and affectingly, as "our dear depart friend," in his sermon next Sunday; and Arthur Pendennis reign in his stead.
CHAPTER III
in which Pendennis appear as a very young Man indeed
Arthur be about sixteen year old, we have say, when he begin to reign; in person (for I see that the artist who be to illustrate this book, and who make sad work of the likeness, will never be able to take my friend off) he have what his friend would call a dumpy, but his mamma style a neat little figure. his hair be of a healthy brown colour, which look like gold in the sunshine, his face be round, rosy, freckle, and good-humour, his whisker (when those facial ornament for which he sigh so ardently be award to he by nature) be decidedly of a reddish hue; in fact, without be a beauty, he have such a frank, good-natured kind face, and laugh so merrily at you out of his honest blue eye, that no wonder Mrs. Pendennis think he the pride of the whole county. between the age of sixteen and eighteen he rise from five foot six to five foot eight inch in height, at which altitude he pause. but his mother wonder at it. he be three inch tall than his father. be it possible that any man could grow to be three inch tall than Mr. Pendennis?
you may be certain he never go back to school; the discipline of the establishment do not suit he, and he like be at home much well. the question of his return be debate, and his uncle be for his go back. the Doctor write his opinion that it be most important for Arthur's success in after-life that he should know a Greek play thoroughly, but Pen adroitly manage to hint to his mother what a dangerous place Greyfriars be, and what sad wild fellow some of the chap there be, and the timid soul, take alarm at once, accede to his desire to stay at home.
then Pen's uncle offer to use his influence with his Royal Highness the Commander-in-Chief, who be pleased to be very kind to he, and propose to get Pen a commission in the Foot Guards. Pen's heart leap at this: he have be to hear the band at St. James's play on a Sunday, when he go out to his uncle. he have see Tom Ricketts, of the fourth form, who use to wear a jacket and trouser so ludicrously tight, that the eld boy could not forbear use he in the quality of a butt or 'cockshy'-- he have see this very rickett array in crimson and gold, with an immense bear-skin cap on his head, stagger under the colour of the regiment. Tom have recognise he and give he a patronising nod. Tom, a little wretch whom he have cut over the back with a hockey-stick last quarter--and there he be in the centre of the square, rally round the flag of his country, surround by bayonet, crossbelt, and scarlet, the band blow trumpet and bang cymbal--talk familiarly to immense warrior with tuft to their chin and Waterloo medal. what would not Pen have give to wear such epaulette and enter such a service?
but Helen Pendennis, when this point be propose to she by her son, put on a face full of terror and alarm. she say she "do not quarrel with other who think differently, but that in her opinion a Christian have no right to make the army a profession. Mr. Pendennis never, never would have permit his son to be a soldier. finally, she should be very unhappy if he think of it." now Pen would have as soon cut off his nose and ear as deliberately, and of aforethought malice, make his mother unhappy; and, as he be of such a generous disposition that he would give away anything to any one, he instantly make a present of his visionary red coat and epaulette and his ardour for military glory to his mother.
she think he the noble creature in the world. but Major Pendennis, when the offer of the commission be acknowledge and refuse, write back a curt and somewhat angry letter to the widow, and think his nephew be rather a spooney.
he be content, however, when he see the boy's performance out hunt at Christmas, when the Major come down as usual to Fairoaks. Pen have a very good mare, and ride she with uncommon pluck and grace. he take his fence with great coolness, and yet with judgment, and without bravado. he write to the chap at school about his top-boot, and his feat across country. he begin to think seriously of a scarlet coat: and his mother must own that she think it would become he remarkably well; though, of course, she pass hour of anguish during his absence, and daily expect to see he bring home on a shutter.
with these amusement, in rather too great plenty, it must not be assume that Pen neglect his study altogether. he have a natural taste for read every possible kind of book which do not fall into his school-course. it be only when they force his head into the water of knowledge, that he refuse to drink. he devour all the book at home from Inchbald's Theatre to White's Farriery; he ransack the neighbouring book-case. he find at Clavering an old cargo of french novel, which he read with all his might; and he would sit for hour perch upon the topmost bar of Doctor Portman's library step with a folio on his knee, whether it be Hakluyt's Travels, Hobbes's Leviathan, Augustini Opera, or Chaucer's Poems. he and the Vicar be very good friend, and from his reverence, Pen learn that honest taste for port wine which distinguish he through life. and as for that dear good woman, Mrs. Portman, who be not in the least jealous, though her doctor avow himself in love with Mrs. Pendennis, whom he pronounce to be by far the fine lady in the county--all her grief be, as she look up fondly at Pen perch on the book-ladder, that her daughter, Minny, be too old for he--as indeed she be--Miss Myra Portman be at that period only two year young than Pen's mother, and weigh as much as Pen and Mrs. Pendennis together.
be these detail insipid? look back, good friend, at your own youth, and ask how be that? I like to think of a well-nurture boy, brave and gentle, warm-hearted and loving, and look the world in the face with kind honest eye. what bright colour it wear then, and how you enjoy it! a man have not many year of such time. he do not know they whilst they be with he. it be only when they be pass long away that he remember how dear and happy they be.
in order to keep Mr. Pen from indulge in that idleness of which his friend the Doctor of the Cistercians have prophesy such awful consequence, Mr. Smirke, Dr. Portman's curate, be engage at a liberal salary, to walk or ride over from Clavering and pass several hour daily with the young gentleman. Smirke be a man perfectly faultless at a tea-table, wear a curl on his fair forehead, and tie his neck-cloth with a melancholy grace. he be a decent scholar and mathematician, and teach Pen as much as the lad be ever disposed to learn, which be not much. for Pen have soon take the measure of his tutor, who, when he come rid into the court-yard at Fairoaks on his pony, turn out his toe so absurdly, and leave such a gap between his knee and the saddle, that it be impossible for any lad endow with a sense of humour to respect such an equestrian. he nearly kill Smirke with terror by put he on his mare, and take he a ride over a common, where the county fox-hound (then bunt by that staunch old sportsman, Mr. Hardhead, of Dumplingbeare) happen to meet. Mr. Smirke, on Pen's mare, Rebecca (she be name after Pen's favourite heroine, the daughter of Isaac of York), astound the hound as much as he disgust the huntsman, lam one of the former by persist in rid amongst the pack, and receive a speech from the latter, more remarkable for energy of language, than any oration he have ever hear since he leave the bargeman on the bank of isis.
Smirke confide to his pupil his poem both Latin and English; and present to Mrs. Pendennis a volume of the latter, print at Clapham, his native place. the two read the ancient poet together, and rattle through they at a pleasant rate, very different from that steady grubbing pace with which the Cistercians use to go over the classic ground, scent out each word as they go, and dig up every root in the way. Pen never like to halt, but make his tutor construe when he be at fault, and thus gallop through the Iliad and the Odyssey, the tragic playwriter, writer, and the charming wicked Aristophanes (whom he vow to be the great poet of all). but he go at such a pace that, though he certainly gallop through a considerable extent of the ancient country, he clean forget it in after-life, and have only such a vague remembrance of his early classic course as a man have in the House of Commons, let we say, who still keep up two or three quotation; or a reviewer who, just for decency's sake, hint at a little greek. our people be the most prosaic in the world, but the most faithful; and with curious reverence we keep up and transmit, from generation to generation, the superstition of what we call the education of a gentleman.
besides the ancient poet, you may be sure Pen read the English with great gusto. Smirke sigh and shake his head sadly both about Byron and Moore. but Pen be a swear fire-worshipper and a corsair; he have they by heart, and use to take little Laura into the window and say, "Zuleika, I be not thy brother," in tone so tragic that they cause the solemn little maid to open her great eye still wide. she sit, until the proper hour for retirement, sew at Mrs. Pendennis's knee, and listen to Pen read out to she of night without comprehend one word of what he read.
he read Shakspeare to his mother (which she say she like, but donot), and Byron, and Pope, and his favourite Lalla Rookh, which please she indifferently. but as for Bishop Heber, and Mrs. Hemans above all, this lady use to melt right away, and be absorb into her pocket-handkerchief, when Pen read those author to she in his kind boyish voice. the 'Christian Year' be a book which appear about that time. the son and the mother whisper it to each other with awe--faint, very faint, and seldom in after-life Pendennis hear that solemn church-music: but he always love the remembrance of it, and of the time when it strike on his heart, and he walk over the field full of hope and void of doubt, as the church-bell rang on Sunday morning.
it be at this period of his existence, that Pen break out in the poet' Corner of the County Chronicle, with some verse with which he be perfectly well satisfied. his be the verse sign 'NEP.,' address 'to a tear;' 'on the anniversary of the Battle of Waterloo;' 'to Madame Caradori singing at the Assize Meetings;' 'on Saint Bartholomew's day' (a tremendous denunciation of Popery, and a solemn warning to the people of England to rally against emancipate the Roman Catholics), etc., etc.-- all which masterpiece, Mrs. Pendennis no doubt keep to this day, along with his first sock, the first cutting of his hair, his bottle, and other interesting relic of his infancy. he use to gallop Rebecca over the neighbour Dumpling down, or into the county town, which, if you please, we shall call Chatteris, spout his own poem, and fill with quite a byronic afflatus as he think.
his genius at this time be of a decidedly gloomy cast. he bring his mother a tragedy, in which, though he kill sixteen people before the second act, it make she laugh so, that he thrust the masterpiece into the fire in a pet. he project an epic poem in blank verse, 'Cortez, or the Conqueror of Mexico, and the Inca's daughter.' he write part of 'Seneca, or the Fatal Bath,' and 'Ariadne in Naxos;' classical piece, with chorus and strophe and antistrophe, which sadly puzzle poor Mrs. Pendennis; and begin a 'history of the Jesuits,' in which he lash that order with tremendous severity, and warn his protestant fellow-countryman of their machination. his loyalty do his mother's heart good to witness. he be a staunch, unflinche church-and-king man in those day; and at the election, when Sir Giles Beanfield stand on the blue interest, against Lord Trehawk, Lord Eyrie's son, a Whig and a friend of Popery, Arthur Pendennis, with an immense bow for himself, which his mother make, and with a blue ribbon for Rebecca, ride alongside of the Reverend Doctor Portman, on his grey mare Dowdy, and at the head of the Clavering voter, whom the Doctor bring up to plump for the Protestant Champion.
on that day Pen make his first speech at the Blue Hotel: and also, it appear, for the first time in his life--take a little more wine than be good for he. mercy! what a scene it be at Fairoaks, when he ride back at ever so much o'clock at night. what move about of lantern in the court-yard and stable, though the moon be shin out; what a gathering of servant, as Pen come home, clatter over the bridge and up the stableyard, with half a score of the Clavering voter yell after he the Blue song of the election.
he want they all to come in and have some wine--some very good Madeira --some capital Madeira--John, go and get some Madeira,--and there be no know what the farmer would have do, have not Madam Pendennis make her appearance in a white wrapper, with a candle--and scar those zealous blue so by the sight of her pale handsome face, that they touch their hat and ride off.
besides these amusement and occupation in which Mr. Pen indulge, there be one which form the main business and pleasure of youth, if the poet tell we aright, whom Pen be always study; and this young fellow's heart be so ardent, and his imagination so eager, that it be not to be expect he should long escape the passion to which we allude, and which, lady, you have rightly guess to be that of love. Pen sigh for it first in secret, and, like the love-sick swain in Ovid, open his breast and say, "Aura, veni." what generous youth be there that have not court some such windy mistress in his time?
yes, Pen begin to feel the necessity of a first love--of a consume passion--of an object on which he could concentrate all those vague float fancy under which he sweetly suffer--of a young lady to whom he could really make verse, and whom he could set up and adore, in place of those unsubstantial Ianthes and Zuleikas to whom he address the outpouring of his gushing muse. he read his favourite poem over and over again, he call upon Alma Venus the delight of god and man, he translate Anacreon's ode, and pick out passage suitable to his complaint from Waller, Dryden, Prior, and the like. Smirke and he be never weary, in their interview, of discourse about love. the faithless tutor entertain he with sentimental conversation in place of lecture on algebra and Greek; for Smirke be in love too. who could help it, be in daily intercourse with such a woman? Smirke be madly in love (as far as such a mild flame as Mr. Smirke's may be call madness) with Mrs. Pendennis. that honest lady, sit down below stair teach little Laura to play the piano, or devise flannel petticoat for the poor round about she, or otherwise busy with the calm routine of her modest and spotless christian life, be little aware what storm be brew in two bosom upstairs in the study--in Pen's, as he sate in his shooting jacket, with his elbow on the green study-table, and his hand clutch his curly brown hair, Homer under his nose,--and in worthy Mr. Smirke's, with whom he be read. here they would talk about Helen and Andromache. "Andromache's like my mother," Pen use to avouch; "but I say, Smirke, by Jove Iwould cut off my nose to see Helen;" and he would spout certain favourite line which the reader will find in their proper place in the third book. he draw portrait of she--they be extant still--with straight nose and enormous eye, and 'Arthur Pendennis delineavit et pinxit' gallantly write underneath.
as for Mr. Smirke he naturally prefer Andromache. and in consequence he be uncommonly kind to Pen. he give he his Elzevir Horace, of which the boy be fond, and his little Greek Testament which his own mamma at Clapham have purchase and present to he. he buy he a silver pencil-case; and in the matter of learn let he do just as much or as little as ever he please. he always seem to be on the point of unbosom himself to Pen: nay, he confess to the latter that he have a--an attachment, an ardently cherish attachment, about which Pendennis long to hear, and say, "tell we, old chap, be she handsome? have she get blue eye or black?" but Doctor Portman's curate, heave a gentle sigh, cast up his eye to the ceiling, and beg Pen faintly to change the conversation. poor Smirke! he invite Pen to dine at his lodging over Madame Fribsby's, the milliner's, in Clavering; and once when it be rain, and Mrs. Pendennis, who have drive in her pony-chaise into Clavering with respect to some arrangement, about leave off mourning probably, be prevail upon to enter the curate's apartment, he send out for pound-cake instantly. the sofa on which she sate become sacred to he from that day: and he keep flower in the glass which she drink from ever after.
as Mrs. Pendennis be never tired of hear the praise of her son, we may be certain that this rogue of a_ tutor neglect no opportunity of converse with she upon that subject. it might be a little tedious to he to hear the story about Pen's generosity, about his bravery in fight the big naughty boy, about his fun and joke, about his prodigious skill in Latin, music, riding, etc., but what price would he not pay to be in her company? and the widow, after these conversation, think Mr. Smirke a very pleasing and well-inform man. as for her son, she have not settle in her mind whether he be to be Senior Wrangler and Archbishop of Canterbury, or double First Class at Oxford, and Lord Chancellor. that all England do not possess his peer, be a fact about which there be, in her mind, no manner of question.
a simple person, of inexpensive habit, she begin forthwith to save, and, perhaps, to be a little parsimonious, in favour of her boy. there be no entertainment, of course, at Fairoaks, during the year of her weed. nor, indeed, do the Doctor's silver dish-cover, of which he be so proud, and which be flourish all over with the arm of the Pendennises, and surmount with their crest, come out of the plate-chest again for long, long year. the household be diminish, and its expense curtail. there be a very blank anchorite repast when Pen din from home: and he himself head the remonstrance from the kitchen regard the deteriorated quality of the Fairoaks beer. she be become miserly for Pen. indeed, who ever accuse woman of be just? they be always sacrifice themselves or somebody for somebody else's sake.
there happen to be no young woman in the small circle of friend who be in the widow's intimacy whom Pendennis could by any possibility gratify by endow she with the inestimable treasure of a heart which he be long to give away. some young fellow in this predicament bestow their young affection upon dolly, the dairymaid, or cast the eye of tenderness upon Molly, the blacksmith's daughter. Pen think a Pendennis much too grand a personage to stoop so low. he be too high-minded for a vulgar intrigue, and, at the idea of an intrigue or a seduction, have he ever entertain it, his heart would have revolt as from the notion of any act of baseness or dishonour. Miss Minny Portman be too old, too large, and too fond of read 'Rollin's Ancient history.' the Miss Boardbacks, Admiral Boardback's daughter (of St. Vincent's, or Fourth of June House, as it be call), disgust Pen with the London air which they bring into the country, from Gloucester Place, where they pass the season, and look down upon Pen as a chit. Captain Glanders's (H.P., 50th Dragoon Guards) three girl be in brown-holland pinafore as yet, with the end of their hair-plait tie up in dirty pink ribbon. not have acquire the art of dancing, the youth avoid such chance as he might have have of meeting with the fair sex at the Chatteris' assembly; in fine, he be not in love, because there be nobody at hand to fall in love with. and the young monkey use to ride out, day after day in quest, of Dulcinea; and peep into the pony-chaise and gentlefolk' carriage, as they drive along the broad turnpike road, with a heart beat within he, and a secret tremor and hope that she might be in that yellow postchaise come swing up the hill, or one of those three girl in beaver bonnet in the back seat of the double gig, which the fat old gentleman in black be drive, at four mile an hour. the postchaise contain a snuffy old dowager of seventy, with a maid, her contemporary. the three girl in the beaver bonnet be no handsomer than the turnip that skirt the roadside. do as he might, and ride where he would, the fairy princess that he be to rescue and win, have not yet appear to honest Pen.
upon these point he do not discourse to his mother. he have a world of his own. what generous, ardent, imaginative soul have not a secret pleasure-place in which it disport? let no clumsy prying or dull meddling of ours try to disturb it in our child. Actaeon be a brute for want to push in where Diana be bath. leave he occasionally alone, my good madam, if you have a poet for a child. even your admirable advice may be a bore sometimes. you be faultless; but it do not follow that everybody in your family be to think exactly like yourself. yonder little child may have thought too deep even for your great mind, and fancy so coy and timid that they will not bare themselves when your ladyship sit by.
Helen Pendennis by the force of sheer love divine a great number of her son's secret. but she keep these thing in her heart (if we may so speak), and do not speak of they. besides, she have make up her mind that he be to marry little Laura, who would be eighteen when Pen be six-and-twenty: and have finish his college career, and have make his grand tour, and be settle either in London, astonishing all the metropolis by his learning and eloquence at the bar, or well still in a sweet country parsonage surround with hollyhock and rose, close to a delightful romantic ivy-cover church, from the pulpit of which Pen would utter the most beautiful sermon ever preach.
while these natural sentiment be wag war and trouble in honest Pen's bosom, it chance one day that he ride into Chatteris, for the purpose of carry to the County Chronicle a tremendous and thrilling poem for the next week's paper; and put up his horse accord to custom, at the stable of the George Hotel there, he fall in with an old acquaintance. a grand black tandem, with scarlet wheel, come rattle into the inn yard, as Pen stand there in converse with the hostler about Rebecca; and the voice of the driver call out, "Hallo, Pendennis, be that you?" in a loud patronising manner. Pen have some difficulty in recognise under the broad-brim hat and the vast great-coat and neckcloth, with which the new-comer be habit, the person and figure of his quondam schoolfellow, Mr. Foker.
a year's absence have make no small difference in that gentleman. a youth who have be deservedly whip a few month previously, and who spend his pocket-money on tart and hardbake, now appear before Pen in one of those costume to which the public consent, that I take to be quite as influential in this respect as 'Johnson's Dictionary,' have award the title of "Swell.' he have a bull-dog between his leg, and in his scarlet shawl neckcloth be a pin represent another bull-dog in gold: he wear a fur waistcoat lace over with gold chain; a green cutaway coat with basket-button, and a white upper-coat ornament with cheese-plate button, on each of which be engrave some stirring incident of the road or the chase; all which ornament set off this young fellow's figure to such advantage, that you would hesitate to say which character in life he most resemble, and whether he be a boxer en goguette, or a coachman in his gala suit.
"leave that place for good, Pendennis?" Mr. Foker say, descend from his landau and give Pendennis a finger.
"yes, this year--or more," Pen say.
"beastly old hole," Mr. Foker remark. "hate it. hate the doctor: hate Towzer, the second master; hate everybody there. not a fit place for a gentleman."
"not at all," say Pen, with an air of the utmost consequence.
"by gad, sir, I sometimes dream, now, that the Doctor's walk into I," Foker continue (and Pen smile as he think that he himself have likewise fearful dream of this nature). "when I think of the diet there, by gad, sir, I wonder how I stand it. mangy mutton, brutal beef; pudding on Thursdays and Sundays, and that fit to poison you. just look at my leader--do you ever see a pretty animal? drive over from Baymouth. come the nine mile in two-and-forty minute. not bad going, sir."
"be you stop at Baymouth, Foker?" Pendennis ask.
"Ibe coach there," say the other, with a nod.
"what?" ask Pen, and in a tone of such wonder, that Foker burst out laugh, and say, "he be blow if he donot think Pen be such a flat as not to know what coach mean."
"Ibe come down with a coach from Oxford. a tutor, donot you see, old boy? hebe coach I, and some other man, for the little go. I and Spavin have the drag between we. and I think Iwould just tool over and go to the play. do you ever see Rowkins do the hornpipe?" and Mr. Foker begin to perform some step of that popular dance in the inn yard, look round for the sympathy of his groom and the stable-man.
Pen think he would like to go to the play too: and could ride home afterwards, as there be a moonlight. so he accept Foker's invitation to dinner, and the young man enter the inn together, where Mr. Foker stop at the bar, and call upon Miss Rincer, the landlady's fair daughter, who preside there, to give he a glass of 'his mixture.'
