Pen and his family have be know at the George ever since they come into the country; and Mr. Pendennis's carriage and horse always put up there when he pay a visit to the county town. the landlady drop the heir of Fairoaks a very respectful curtsey, and compliment he upon his growth and manly appearance, and ask news of the family at Fairoaks, and of Doctor Portman and the Clavering people, to all of which question the young gentleman answer with much affability. but he speak to Mr. and Mrs. Rincer with that sort of good nature with which a young Prince address his father's subject; never dream that those bonne gen be his equal in life.
Mr. Foker's behaviour be quite different. he inquire for Rincer and the cold in his nose, tell Mrs. Rincer a riddle, ask Miss Rincer when she would be ready to marry he, and pay his compliment to Miss Brett, the other young lady in the bar, all in a minute of time, and with a liveliness and facetiousness which set all these lady in a giggle; and he give a cluck, expressive of great satisfaction, as he toss off his mixture which Miss Rincer prepare and hand to he.
"have a drop," say he to Pen, "itbe recommend to I by the faculty as a what-do-you-call-'em--a stomatic, old boy. give the young one a glass, R., and score it up to yours truly."
Poor Pen take a glass, and everybody laugh at the face which he make as he put it down--gin, bitter, and some other cordial be the compound with which Mr. Foker be so delighted as to call it by the name of Foker's own. as Pen choke, sputter, and make face, the other take occasion to remark to Mr. Rincer that the young fellow be green, very green, but that he would soon form he; and then they proceed to order dinner--which Mr. Foker determine should consist of turtle and venison; caution the landlady to be very particular about ice the wine.
then Messrs. Foker and Pen stroll down the High Street together--the former have a cigar in his mouth, which he have draw out of a case almost as big as a portmanteau. he go in to replenish it at Mr. Lewis's, and talk to that gentleman for a while, sit down on the counter: he then look in at the fruiterer's, to see the pretty girl there, to whom he pay compliment similar to those before address to the bar at the George; then they pass the County Chronicle office, for which Pen have his packet ready, in the shape of 'line to Thyrza,' but poor Pen do not like to put the letter into the editor's box while walk in company with such a fine gentleman as Mr. Foker. they meet heavy dragoon of the regiment always quarter at Chatteris; and stop and talk about the Baymouth ball, and what a pretty girl be Miss Brown, and what a dem fine woman Mrs. Jones be. it be in vain that Pen recall to his own mind what a stupid ass Foker use to be at school-- how he could scarcely read, how he be not cleanly in his person, and notorious for his blunder and dulness. Mr. Foker be no more like a gentleman now than in his school day: and yet Pen feel a secret pride in strut down High Street with a young fellow who own tandem, talk to officer, and order turtle and champagne for dinner. he listen, and with respect too, to Mr. Foker's account of what the man do at the University of which Mr. F. be an ornament, and encounter a long series of story about boat-racing, bumping, College grass-plat, and milk-punch--and begin to wish to go up himself to College to a place where there be such manly pleasure and enjoyment. Farmer Gurnett, who live close by Fairoaks, rid by at this minute and touch his hat to Pen, the latter stop he, and send a message to his mother to say that he have meet with an old schoolfellow, and should dine in Chatteris.
the two young gentleman continue their walk, and be pass round the Cathedral Yard, where they could hear the music of the afternoon service (a music which always exceedingly impress and affect Pen), but whither Mr. Foker come for the purpose of inspect the nursery-maid who frequent the Elms Walk there, and who be uncommonly pretty at Chatteris, and here they stroll until with a final burst of music the small congregation be play out.
Old Doctor Portman be one of the few who come from the venerable gate. spy Pen, he come and shake he by the hand, and eye with wonder Pen's friend, from whose mouth and cigar cloud of fragrance issue, which curl round the Doctor's honest face and shovel hat.
"an old schoolfellow of mine, Mr. Foker," say Pen. the Doctor say "H'm": and scowl at the cigar. he do not mind a pipe in his study, but the cigar be an abomination to the worthy gentleman.
"I come up on Bishop's business," the Doctor say. "wewill ride home, Arthur, if you like?"
"I--i'm engage to my friend here," Pen answer.
"you have well come home with I," say the Doctor.
"his mother know hebe out, sir," Mr. Foker remark; "donot she, Pendennis?"
"but that do not prove that he have not well come home with I," the Doctor growl, and he walk off with great dignity.
"old boy donot like the weed, I suppose," Foker say. "ha! whobe here?-- herebe the General, and Bingley, the manager. how do, cos? how do, Bingley?"
"how do my worthy and gallant young Foker?" say the gentleman address as the General; and who wear a shabby military cape with a mangy collar, and a hat cock very much over one eye.
"trust you be very well, my very dear sir," say the other gentleman, "and that the Theatre Royal will have the honour of your patronage to-night. we perform 'the Stranger,' in which your humble servant will---"
"cannot stand you in tight and Hessians, Bingley," young Mr. Foker say. on which the General, with the irish accent, say, "but I think ye'll like Miss Fotheringay, in Mrs. Haller, or I namebe not Jack Costigan."
Pen look at these individual with the great interest. he have never see an actor before; and he see Dr. Portman's red face look over the Doctor's shoulder, as he retreat from the Cathedral Yard, evidently quite dissatisfied with the acquaintance into whose hand Pen have fall.
perhaps it would have be much well for he have he take the parson's advice and company home. but which of we know his fate?
CHAPTER IV
Mrs. Haller
have return to the George, Mr. Foker and his guest sate down to a handsome repast in the coffee-room; where Mr. Rincer bring in the first dish, and bow as gravely as if he be wait upon the Lord-Lieutenant of the county. Mr. Foker attack the turtle and venison with as much gusto as he have show the year before, when he use to make feast off ginger-beer and smuggled polony. Pen could not but respect his connoisseurship as he pronounce the champagne to be condemn gooseberry, and wink at the port with one eye. the latter he declare to be of the right sort; and tell the waiter there be no way of humbug he. all these attendant he know by their christian name, and show a great interest in their family; and as the London coach drive up, which in those early day use to set off from the George, Mr. Foker fling the coffee-room window open, and call the guard and coachman by their christian name, too, ask about their respective family, and imitate with great liveliness and accuracy the tooting of the horn as Jem the ostler whip the horse' cloth off, and the carriage drive gaily away.
"a bottle of sherry, a bottle of sham, a bottle of port and a shass caffy, it benot so bad, hay, Pen?" Foker say, and pronounce, after all these delicacy and a quantity of nut and fruit have be dispatch, that it be time to "toddle." Pen spring up with very bright eye, and a flushed face; and they move off towards the theatre, where they pay their money to the wheezy old lady slumbering in the money-taker's box. "Mrs. Dropsicum, Bingley's mother-in-law, great in Lady Macbeth," Foker say to his companion. Foker know she, too.
they have almost their choice of place in the box of the theatre, which be no well fill than country theatre usually be in spite of the "universal burst of attraction and galvanic thrill of delight" advertise by Bingley in the play-bill. a score or so of people dot the pit-bench, a few more keep a kicking and whistle in the gallery, and a dozen other, who come in with free admission, be in the box where our young gentleman sate. Lieutenants Rodgers and Podgers, and young Cornet Tidmus, of the Dragoons, occupy a private box. the performer act to they, and these gentleman seem to hold conversation with the player when not engage in the dialogue, and applaud they by name loudly.
Bingley the manager, who assume all the chief tragic and comic part except when he modestly retreat to make way for the London star, who come down occasionally to Chatteris, be great in the character of the 'Stranger.' he be attire in the tight pantaloon and hessian boot which the stage legend have give to that injure man, with a large cloak and beaver and a hearse feather in it droop over his raddled old face, and only partially conceal his great buckle brown wig. he have the stage jewellery on too, of which he select the large and most shiny ring for himself, and allow his little finger to quiver out of his cloak with a sham diamond ring cover the first joint of the finger and twiddle in the face of the pit. Bingley make it a favour to the young man of his company to go on in light comedy part with that ring. they flatter he by ask its history. the stage have its traditional jewel as the Crown and all great family have. this have belong to George Frederick Cooke, who have have it from Mr. Quin, who may have buy it for a shilling. Bingley fancy the world be fascinate with its glitter.
he be read out of the stage-book--that wonderful stage-book which be not bind like any other book in the world, but be rouge and tawdry like the hero or heroine who hold it; and who hold it as people never do hold book: and point with his finger to a passage, and wag his head ominously at the audience, and then lift up eye and finger to the ceiling profess to derive some intense consolation from the work between which and heaven there be a strong affinity. anybody who have ever see one of our great light comedian, X., in a chintz dressing-gown, such as nobody ever wear, and represent himself to the public as a young nobleman in his apartment, and while away the time with light literature until his friend Sir Harry shall arrive, or his father shall come down to breakfast--anybody, I say, who have see the great x. over a sham book have indeed have a great pleasure and an abide matter for thought.
directly the Stranger see the young man, he act at they; eye they solemnly over his gilt volume as he lie on the stage-bank show his hand, his ring, and his Hessians. he calculate the effect that every one of these ornament would produce upon his victim: he be determined to fascinate they, for he know they have pay their money; and he see their family come in from the country and fill the cane chair in his box.
as he lie on the bank reading, his servant, Francis, make remark upon his master.
"again read," say Francis, "thus it be, from morn to night. to he nature have no beauty--life no charm. for three year I have never see he smile" (the gloom of Bingley's face be fearful to witness during these comment of the faithful domestic). "nothing divert he. o, if he would but attach himself to any live thing, be it an animal--for something man must love."
[enter Tobias (Goll) from the hut.] he cry, "o, how refreshing, after seven long week, to feel these warm sunbeam once again. thank, bounteous heaven, for the joy I taste!" he press his cap between his hand, look up and pray. the stranger eye he attentively.
Francis to the Stranger. "this old man's share of earthly happiness can be but little. yet mark how grateful he be for his portion of it."
Bingley. "because though old, he be but a child in the lead-string of hope." (he look steadily at Foker, who, however, continue to suck the top of his stick in an unconcerned manner.)
Francis. "Hope be the nurse of life."
Bingley. "and her cradle--be the grave."
the Stranger utter this with the moan of a bassoon in agony, and fix his eye on Pendennis so steadily, that the poor lad be quite put out of countenance. he think the whole house must be look at he; and cast his eye down. as soon as ever he raise they Bingley's be at he again. all through the scene the manager play at he. when he be about to do a good action, and send off Francis with his book, so that that domestic should not witness the deed of benevolence which he meditate, Bingley mark the page carefully, so that he might continue the perusal of the volume off the stage if he like. but all be do in the direct face of Pendennis, whom the manager be bent upon subjugating. how relieve the lad be when the scene end, and Foker, tap with his cane, cry out "Bravo, Bingley!"
"give he a hand, Pendennis; you know every chap like a hand," Mr. Foker say; and the good-natured young gentleman, and Pendennis laughing, and the dragoon in the opposite box, begin clap hand to the good of their power.
a chamber in Wintersen Castle close over Tobias's hut and the Stranger and his boot; and servant appear bustle about with chair and tables--"that's Hicks and Miss Thackthwaite," whisper Foker. "pretty girl, benot she, Pendennis? but stop--hurray--bravo! herebe the Fotheringay."
the pit thrill and thump its umbrella; a volley of applause be fire from the gallery: the Dragoon officer and Foker clap their hand furiously: you would have think the house be full, so loud be their plaudit. the red face and rag whisker of Mr. Costigan be see peer from the side-scene. Pen's eye open wide and bright as Mrs. Haller enter with a downcast look, then rally at the sound of the applause, sweep the house with a grateful glance, and, fold her hand across her breast, sink down in a magnificent curtsey. More applause, more umbrella; Pen this time, flame with wine and enthusiasm, clap hand and sing "bravo" louder than all. Mrs. Haller see he, and everybody else, and old Mr. Bows, the little first fiddler of the orchestra (which be this night increase by a detachment of the band of the Dragoons, by the kind permission of Colonel Swallowtail), look up from the desk where he be perch, with his crutch beside he, and smile at the enthusiasm of the lad.
those who have only see Miss Fotheringay in later day, since her marriage and introduction into London life, have little idea how beautiful a creature she be at the time when our friend Pen first set eye on she: and I warn my reader, as beforehand, that the pencil which illustrate this work (and can draw an ugly face tolerably well, but be sadly put out when it try to delineate a beauty) can give no sort of notion of she. she be of the tall of woman, and at she then age of six-and-twenty-for six-and-twenty she be, though she vow she be only nineteen--in the prime and fulness of her beauty. her forehead be vast, and her black hair wave over it with a natural ripple (that beauty of late day have try to imitate with the help of the crimping-iron), and be confine in shin and voluminous braid at the back of a neck such as you see on the shoulder of the Louvre Venus--that delight of god and man. her eye, when she lift they up to gaze on you, and ere she drop their purple deep-fringe lid, shine with tenderness and mystery unfathomable. love and Genius seem to look out from they and then retire coyly, as if ashamed to have be see at the lattice. who could have have such a command brow but a woman of high intellect? she never laugh (indeed her tooth be not good), but a smile of endless tenderness and sweetness play round her beautiful lip, and in the dimple of her cheek and her lovely chin. her nose defy description in those day. her ear be like two little pearl shell, which the earring she wear (though the handsome property in the theatre) only insult. she be dress in long flow robe of black, which she manage and sweep to and fro with wonderful grace, and out of the fold of which you only see her sandal occasionally; they be of rather a large size; but Pen think they as ravish as the slipper of Cinderella. but it be her hand and arm that this magnificent creature most excel in, and somehow you could never see she but through they. they surround she. when she fold they over her bosom in resignation; when she drop they in mute agony, or raise they in superb command; when in sportive gaiety her hand flutter and wave before she, like what shall we say?--like the snowy dove before the chariot of Venus--it be with these arm and hand that she beckon, repel, entreat, embrace, her admirer--no single one, for she be arm with her own virtue, and with her father's valour, whose sword would have leapt from its scabbard at any insult offer to his child--but the whole house; which rise to she, as the phrase be, as she curtsey and bow, and charm it.
thus she stand for a minute--complete and beautiful--as Pen star at she. "I say, Pen, benot she a stunner?" ask Mr. Foker.
"hush!" Pen say, "shebe speak."
she begin her business in a deep sweet voice. those who know the play of the 'stranger,' be aware that the remark make by the various character be not valuable in themselves, either for their sound sense, their novelty of observation, or their poetic fancy. in fact, if a man be to say it be a stupid play, he would not be far wrong. nobody ever talk so. if we meet idiot in life, as will happen, it be a great mercy that they do not use such absurdly fine word. the Stranger's talk be sham, like the book he read and the hair he wear, and the bank he sit on, and the diamond ring he make play with--but, in the midst of the balderdash, there run that reality of love, child, and forgiveness of wrong, which will be listen to wherever it be preach, and set all the world sympathising.
with what smothered sorrow, with what gush pathos, Mrs. Haller deliver her part! at first, when as Count Wintersen's housekeeper, and prepare for his Excellency's arrival, she have to give order about the bed and furniture, and the dinner, etc., to be get ready, she do so with the calm agony of despair. but when she could get rid of the stupid servant and give vent to her feeling to the pit and the house, she overflow to each individual as if he be her particular confidant, and she be cry out her grief on his shoulder: the little fiddler in the orchestra (whom she do not seem to watch, though he follow she ceaselessly) twitch, twisted, nod, point about, and when she come to the favourite passage, "I have a William too, if he be still alive-- ah, yes, if he be still alive. his little sister, too! why, Fancy, dost thou rack I so? why dost thou image my poor child faint in sickness, and cry to--to--their mum--um--other," when she come to this passage little bow bury his face in his blue cotton handkerchief, after cry out "Bravo."
all the house be affect. Foker, for his part, take out a large yellow bandanna, weep piteously. as for Pen, he be go too far for that. he follow the woman about and about--when she be off the stage, it and the house be blank; the light and the red officer, reel wildly before his sight. he watch she at the side-scene--where she stand wait to come on the stage, and where her father take off her shawl: when the reconciliation arrive, and she fling herself down on Mr. Bingley's shoulder, whilst the child clung to their knee, and the Countess (Mrs. Bingley) and Baron Steinforth (perform with great liveliness and spirit by garbetts)--while the rest of the character form a group round they, Pen's hot eye only see Fotheringay, Fotheringay. the curtain fall upon he like a pall. he do not hear a word of what Bingley say, who come forward to announce the play for the next evening, and who take the tumultuous applause, as usual, for himself. Pen be not even distinctly aware that the house be call for Miss Fotheringay, nor do the manager seem to comprehend that anybody else but himself have cause the success of the play. at last he understand it--step back with a grin, and presently appear with Mrs. Haller on his arm. how beautiful she look! her hair have fall down, the officer throw her flower. she clutch they to her heart. she put back her hair, and smile all round. her eye meet Pen's. down go the curtain again: and she be go. not one note could he hear of the overture which the brass band of the dragoon blow by kind permission of Colonel Swallowtail.
"she be a crusher, benot she now!" Mr. Foker ask of his companion.
Pen do not know exactly what Foker say, and answer vaguely. he could not tell the other what he feel; he could not have speak, just then, to any mortal. besides, Pendennis do not quite know what he feel yet; it be something overwhelming, madden, delicious; a fever of wild joy and undefined longing.
and now Rowkins and Miss Thackthwaite come on to dance the favourite double hornpipe, and Foker abandon himself to the delight of this ballet, just as he have to the tear of the tragedy, a few minute before. Pen do not care for it, or indeed think about the dance, except to remember that that woman be act with she in the scene where she first come in. it be a mist before his eye. at the end of the dance he look at his watch and say it be time for he to go.
"hang it, stay to see the Bravo of the Battle-Axe," Foker say, "Bingley's splendid in it; he wear red tight, and have to carry Mrs. B. over the Pine-bridge of the Cataract, only shebe too heavy. itbe great fun, do stop."
Pen look at the bill with one linger fond hope that Miss Fotheringay's name might be hide, somewhere, in the list of the actor of the after-piece, but there be no such name. go he must. he have a long ride home. he squeeze Foker's hand. he be choke to speak, but he couldnot. he quit the theatre and walk frantically about the town, he know not how long; then he mount at the George and rode homeward, and Clavering clock sing out one as he come into the yard at Fairoaks. the lady of the house might have be awake, but she only hear he from the passage outside his room as he dash into bed and pull the clothe over his head.
Pen have not be in the habit of pass wakeful night, so he at once fall off into a sound sleep. even in later day and with a great deal of care and other thoughtful matter to keep he awake, a man from long practice or fatigue or resolution begin by go to sleep as usual: and get a nap in advance of Anxiety. but she soon come up with he and jog his shoulder, and say, "come, my man, no more of this laziness, you must wake up and have a talk with I." then they fall to together in the midnight. well, whatever might afterwards happen to he, poor little Pen be not come to this state yet; he tumble into a sound sleep--do not wake until an early hour in the morning, when the rook begin to caw from the little wood beyond his bedroom window; and--at that very instant and as his eye start open, the beloved image be in his mind. "my dear boy," he hear she say, "you be in a sound sleep and I would not disturb you: but I have be close by your pillow all this while: and I donot intend that you shall leave I. I be Love! I bring with I fever and passion: wild longing, madden desire; restless craving and seek. many a long day ere this I hear you call out for I; and behold now I be come."
be Pen frighten at the summon? not he. he do not know what be come: it be all wild pleasure and delight as yet. and as, when three year previously, and on enter the fifth form at the Cistercians, his father have make he a present of a gold watch which the boy take from under his pillow and examine on the instant of wake: for ever rub and polish it up in private and retire into corner to listen to its ticking: so the young man exult over his new delight; feel in his waistcoat pocket to see that it be safe; wound it up at night, and at the very first moment of wake hug it and look at it.--By the way, that first watch of Pen's be a showy ill-manufacture piece: it never go well from the beginning, and be always get out of order. and after put it aside into a drawer and forget it for some time, he swap it finally away for a more useful time-keeper.
Pen feel himself to be ever so many year old since yesterday. there be no mistake about it now. he be as much in love as the good hero in the good romance he ever read. he tell John to bring his shaving water with the utmost confidence. he dress himself in some of his fine clothe that morning: and come splendidly down to breakfast, patronise his mother and little Laura, who have be strum her music lesson for hour before; and who after he have read the prayer (of which he do not heed one single syllable) wonder at his grand appearance, and ask he to tell she what the play be about?
Pen laugh and decline to tell Laura what the play be about. in fact it be quite as well that she should not know. then she ask he why he have get on his fine pin and beautiful new waistcoat?
Pen blush and tell his mother that the old schoolfellow with whom he have din at Chatteris be read with a tutor at Baymouth, a very learn man; and as he be himself to go to College, and as there be several young man pursue their study at Baymouth--he be anxious to ride over-and-and just see what the course of their reading be.
Laura make a long face. Helen Pendennis look hard at her son, trouble more than ever with the vague doubt and terror which have be haunt she ever since the last night, when Farmer Gurnett bring back the news that Pen would not return home to dinner. Arthur's eye defy she. she try to console herself, and drive off her fear. the boy have never tell she an untruth. Pen conduct himself during breakfast in a very haughty and supercilious manner; and, take leave of the eld and young lady, be presently hear rid out of the stablecourt. he go gently at first, but gallop like a madman as soon as he think that he be out of hearing.
Smirke, think of his own affair, and softly rid with his toe out, to give Pen his three hour' reading at Fairoaks, meet his pupil, who shoot by he like the wind. Smirke's pony shy, as the other thunder past he; the gentle curate go over his head among the stinging-nettle in the hedge. Pen laugh as they meet, point towards the Baymouth road, and be go half a mile in that direction before poor Smirke have pick himself up.
Pen have resolve in his mind that he must see Foker that morning; he must hear about she; know about she; be with somebody who know she; and honest Smirke, for his part, sit up among the stinging-nettle, as his pony crop quietly in the hedge, think dismally to himself, ought he to go to Fairoaks now that his pupil be evidently go away for the day. yes, he think he might go, too. he might go and ask Mrs. Pendennis when Arthur would be back; and hear Miss Laura her Watts's Catechism. he get up on the little pony--both be use to his slip off--and advance upon the house from which his scholar have just rush away in a whirlwind.
thus love make fool of all of we, big and little; and the curate have tumble over head and heel in pursuit of it, and Pen have start in the first heat of the mad race.
CHAPTER V
Mrs. Haller at home
without slacken her pace, rebecca the mare gallop on to Baymouth, where Pen put she up at the inn stable, and run straightway to Mr. Foker's lodging, which he know from the direction give to he by that gentleman on the previous day. on reach these apartment, which be over a chemist's shop whose stock of cigar and sodawater go off rapidly by the kind patronage of his young inmate, Pen only find Mr. Spavin, Foker's friend, and part owner of the tandem which the latter have drive into Chatteris, who be smoking, and teach a little dog, a friend of his, trick with a bit of biscuit.
Pen's healthy red face, fresh from the gallop, compare oddly with the waxy debauch little feature of Foker's chum; the latter remark it. "whobe that man?" he think, "he look as fresh as a bean. his hand donot shake of a morning, Iwould bet five to one."
Foker have not come home at all. here be a disappointment!--mr. Spavin could not say when his friend would return. sometimes he stop a day, sometimes a week. of what college be Pen? would he have anything? there be a very fair tap of ale. Mr. Spavin be enable to know Pendennis's name, on the card which the latter take out and lay down (perhaps Pen in these day be rather proud of have a card)--and so the young man take leave.
then Pen go down the rock, and walk about on the sand, bite his nail by the shore of the much-sound sea. it stretch before he bright and immeasurable. the blue water come roll into the bay, foaming and roar hoarsely: Pen look they in the face with blank eye, hardly regard they. what a tide there be pour into the lad's own mind at the time, and what a little power have he to check it! Pen fling stone into the sea, but it still keep come on. he be in a rage at not see Foker. he want to see Foker. he must see Foker. "suppose I go on--on the Chatteris road, just to see if I can meet he," Pen think. rebecca be saddle in another half hour, and gallop on the grass by the Chatteris road. about four mile from Baymouth, the Clavering road branch off, as everybody know, and the mare naturally be for take that turn, but, cut she over the shoulder, Pen pass the turning, and ride on to the turnpike without see any sign of the black tandem and red wheel.
as he be at the turnpike he might as well go on: that be quite clear. so Pen ride to the George, and the hostler tell he that Mr. Foker be there sure enough, and that "hewould be a makin a tremendous row the night afore, a drinkin and a singin, and want to fight Tom the postboy: which Ibe think hewould have have the bad of it," the man add, with a grin. "have you carry up your master's 'ot water to shave with?" he add, in a very satirical manner, to Mr. Foker's domestic, who here come down the yard bear his master's clothe, most beautifully brush and arrange. "show Mr. Pendennis up to 'un," and Pen follow the man at last to the apartment, where, in the midst of an immense bed, Mr. Harry Foker lie reposing.
the feather bed and bolster swell up all round Mr. Foker, so that you could hardly see his little sallow face and red silk nightcap.
"Hullo!" say Pen.
"who go there? brother, quickly tell!" sing out the voice from the bed. "what! Pendennis again? be your Mamma acquaint with your absence? do you sup with we last night? no stop--who sup with we last night, Stoopid?"
"there be the three officer, sir, and Mr. Bingley, sir, and Mr. Costigan, sir," the man answer, who receive all Mr. Foker's remark with perfect gravity.
"ah yes: the cup and merry jest go round. we chant and I remember I want to fight a postboy. do I thrash he, Stoopid?"
"no, sir. Fight donot come off, sir," say Stoopid, still with perfect gravity. he be arrange Mr. Foker's dressing-case--a trunk, the gift of a fond mother, without which the young fellow never travel. it contain a prodigious apparatus in plate; a silver dish, a silver mug, silver box and bottle for all sort of essence, and a choice of razor ready against the time when Mr. Foker's beard should come.
"do it some other day," say the young fellow, yawn and throw up his little lean arm over his head. "no, there be no fight; but there be chant. Bingley chant, I chant, the General chant--Costigan I mean.--Did you ever hear he sing 'the Little Pig under the Bed,' Pen?"
"the man we meet yesterday," say Pen, all in a tremor, "the father of---"
"of the fotheringay,--the very man. ainot she a Venus, Pen?"
"please sir, Mr. Costigan's in the sittin-room, sir, and say, sir, you ask he to breakfast, sir. call five time, sir; but wouldnot wake you on no account; and have be here since eleven o'clock, sir---"
"how much be it now?"
"one, sir."
"what would the good of mother say," cry the little sluggard, "if she see I in bed at this hour? she send I down here with a grinder. she want I to cultivate my neglect genus--he, be! I say, Pen, this benot quite like seven o'clock school,--is it, old boy?"--and the young fellow burst out into a boyish laugh of enjoyment. then he added--"Go in and talk to the General whilst I dress. and I say, Pendennis, ask he to sing you 'the Little Pig under the Bed;' itbe capital." Pen go off in great perturbation, to meet Mr. Costigan, and Mr. Foker commence his toilet.
of Mr. Foker's two grandfather, the one from whom he inherit a fortune be a brewer; the other be an earl, who endow he with the most doting mother in the world. the Fokers have be at the cistercian school from father to son; at which place, our friend, whose name could be see over the playground wall, on a public-house sign, under which 'Foker's Entire' be paint, have be dreadfully bully on account of his trade, his uncomely countenance, his inaptitude for learning and cleanliness, his gluttony and other weak point. but those who know how a susceptible youth, under the tyranny of his schoolfellow, become silent and a sneak, may understand how in a very few month after his liberation from bondage, he develop himself as he have do; and become the humorous, the sarcastic, the brilliant Foker, with whom we have make acquaintance. a dunce he always be, it be true; for learn cannot be acquire by leave school and enter at college as a fellow-commoner; but he be now (in his own peculiar manner) as great a dandy as he before have be a slattern, and when he enter his sitting-room to join his two guest, arrive scented and array in fine linen, and perfectly splendid in appearance.
General or Captain Costigan--for the latter be the rank which he prefer to assume--be seat in the window with the newspaper hold before he at arm's length. the Captain's eye be somewhat dim; and he be spell the paper, with the help of his lip, as well as of those bloodshot eye of his, as you see gentleman do to whom reading be a rare and difficult occupation. his hat be cock very much on one ear; and as one of his foot lie up in the window-seat, the observer of such matter might remark, by the size and shabbiness of the boot which the Captain wear, that time do not go very well with he. poverty seem as if it be dispose, before it take possession of a man entirely, to attack his extremity first: the covering of his head, foot, and hand be its first prey. all these part of the Captain's person be particularly rakish and shabby. as soon as he see Pen he descend from the window-seat and salute the new-comer, first in a military manner, by convey a couple of his finger (cover with a broken black glove) to his hat, and then remove that ornament altogether. the Captain be incline to be bald, but he bring a quantity of lank iron-grey hair over his pate, and have a couple of whisp of the same fall down on each side of his face. much whisky have spoil what complexion Mr. Costigan may have possess in his youth. his once handsome face have now a copper tinge. he wear a very high stock, scar and stain in many place; and a dress-coat tightly button up in those part where the button have not part company from the garment.
"the young gentleman to whom I have the honour to be introjuice yesterday in the Cathadral Yard," say the Captain, with a splendid bow and wave of his hat. "I hope I see you well, sir. I mark ye in the thayatre last night during I daughter's perfawrumance; and miss ye on my return. I do but conduct her home, sir, for Jack Costigan, though poor, be a gentleman; and when I reintere the house to pay I respect to I joyous young friend, Mr. Foker--ye be go. we have a jolly night of ut, sir-- Mr. Foker, the three gallant young dragoon, and your 'umble servant. Gad, sir, it put I in mind of one of our old night when I bear his Majesty's commission in the Foighting Hundtherd and Third." and he pull out an old snuff box, which he present with a stately air to his new acquaintance.
Arthur be a great deal too much flurry to speak. this shabby-look buck be--be her father. the Captain be perfume with the recollection of the last night's cigar, and pull and twist the tuft on his chin as jauntily as any young dandy.
"I hope, Miss f--, Miss Costigan be well, sir," Pen say, flush up. "she--she give I great pleasure, than--than I--I--I ever enjoy at a play. I think, sir--I think shebe the fine actress in the world," he gasp out.
"your hand, young man! for ye speak from your heart," cry the Captain. "thank ye, sir, an old soldier and a fond father thank ye. she be the fine actress in the world. Ihave see the Siddons, sir, and the O'Nale-- they be great, but what be they compare to Miss Fotheringay? I do not wish she should ashume her own name while on the stage. I family, sir, be proud people; and the Costigans of Costiganstown think that an honest man, who have bear her Majesty's colour in the Hundred and Third, would demean himself, by permit his daughter to earn her old father's bread."
"there cannot be a more honourable duty, surely," Pen say.
"honourable! Bedad, sir, Iwould like to see the man who say Jack Costigan would consent to anything dishonourable. I have a heart, sir, though I be poor; I like a man who have a heart. you have: I read it in your honest face and steady eye. and would you believe it"? he add, after a pause, and with a pathetic whisper, "that that Bingley who have make his fortune by I child, give she but two guineas a week: out of which she find herself in dress, and which, add to I own small mean, make our all?"
now the Captain's mean be so small as to be, it may be say, quite invisible. but nobody know how the wind be temper to shorn irish lamb, and in what marvellous place they find pasture. if Captain Costigan, whom I have the honour to know, would but have tell his history, it would have be a great moral story. but he neither would have tell it if he could, nor could if he would; for the Captain be not only unaccustomed to tell the truth,--he be unable even to think it--and fact and fiction reel together in his muzzy, whiskified brain.
he begin life rather brilliantly with a pair of colour, a fine person and leg, and one of the most beautiful voice in the world. to his late day he sing with admirable pathos and humour those wonderful irish ballad which be so mirthful and so melancholy: and be always the first himself to cry at their pathos. poor cos! he be at once brave and maudlin, humorous and an idiot; always good-nature, and sometimes almost trustworthy. up to the last day of his life he would drink with any man, and back any man's bill: and his end be in a spunge-house, where the sheriff's officer, who take he, be fond of he.
in his brief morning of life, cos form the delight of regimental mess, and have the honour of sing his song, bacchanalian and sentimental, at the table of the most illustrious general and commander-in-chief, in the course of which period he drink three time as much claret as be good for he, and spend his doubtful patrimony. what become of he subsequently to his retirement from the army, be no affair of our. I take it, no foreigner understand the life of an irish gentleman without money, the way in which he manage to keep afloat--the wind-raise conspiracy, in which he engage with hero as unfortunate as himself--the mean by which he contrive, during most day of the week, to get his portion of whisky-and-water: all these be mystery to we inconceivable: but suffice it to say, that through all the storm of life Jack have float somehow, and the lamp of his nose have never go out.
before he and Pen have have a half-hour's conversation, the Captain manage to extract a couple of sovereign from the young gentleman for ticket for his daughter's benefit, which be to take place speedily; and be not a bona fide transaction such as that of the last year, when poor Miss Fotheringay have lose fifteen shilling by her venture; but be an arrangement with the manager, by which the lady be to have the sale of a certain number of ticket, keep for herself a large portion of the sum for which they be sell.
Pen have but two pound in his purse, and he hand they over to the Captain for the ticket; he would have be afraid to offer more lest he should offend the latter's delicacy. Costigan scrawl he an order for a box, lightly slip the sovereign into his waistcoat, and slap his hand over the place where they lay. they seem to warm his old side.
"Faith, sir," say he, "the bullion's scarcer with I than it use to be, as be the case with many a good fellow. I win six hundthre of them in a single night, sir, when I kind friend, his Royal Highness the Duke of Kent, be in Gibralther." and he straightway pour out to Pen a series of story regard the claret drunk, the bet make, the race ride by the garrison there, with which he keep the young gentleman amuse until the arrival of their host and his breakfast.
then it be good to see the Captain's behaviour before the devil turkey and the mutton chop! his story pour forth unceasingly, and his spirit rise as he chat to the young man. when he get a bit of sunshine, the old lazzarone bask in it; he prate about his own affair and past splendour, and all the lord, general, and Lord-Lieutenants he have ever know. he describe the death of his darling Bessie, the late Mrs. Costigan, and the challenge he have send to Captain Shanty Clancy, of the Slashers, for look rude at Miss Fotheringay as she be on her kyar in the Phaynix; and then he describe how the Captain apologise, give a dinner at the Kildare Street, where six of they drink twinty-one bottle of claret, etc. he announce that to sit with two such noble and generous young fellow be the happiness and pride of an old soldier's existence; and have have a second glass of Curacoa, be so happy that he begin to cry. altogether we should say that the Captain be not a man of much strength of mind, or a very eligible companion for youth; but there be bad man, hold much well place in life, and more dishonest, who have never commit half so many roguery as he. they walk out, the Captain hold an arm of each of his dear young friend, and in a maudlin state of contentment. he wink at one or two tradesman's shop where, possibly, he owe a bill, as much as to say, "see the company Ibe in--sure Iwill pay you, my boy,"--and they part finally with Mr. Foker at a billiard-room, where the latter have a particular engagement with some gentleman of Colonel Swallowtail's regiment.
Pen and the shabby Captain still walk the street together; the Captain, in his sly way, make inquiry about Mr. Foker's fortune and station in life. Pen tell he how Foker's father be a celebrated brewer, and his mother be Lady Agnes Milton, Lord Rosherville's daughter. the Captain break out into a strain of exaggerated compliment and panegyric about Mr. Foker, whose "native aristocracie," he say, "could be see with the twinkling of an oi--and only serve to adawrun other quality which he possess, a foin intellect and a generous heart,"--in not one word of which speech do the Captain accurately believe.
Pen walk on, listen to his companion's prate, wonder, amused, and puzzle. it have not as yet enter into the boy's head to disbelieve any statement that be make to he; and be of a candid nature himself, he take naturally for truth what other people tell he. Costigan have never have a well listener, and be highly flatter by the attentiveness and modest bearing of the young man.
so much pleased be he with the young gentleman, so artless, honest, and cheerful do Pen seem to be, that the Captain finally make he an invitation, which he very seldom accord to young man, and ask Pen if he would do he the fever to enter his humble abode, which be near at hand, where the Captain would have the honour of inthrojuice his young friend to his daughther, Miss Fotheringay?
Pen be so delightfully shocked at this invitation, and be so stricken down by the happiness thus suddenly offer to he, that he think he should have drop from the Captain's arm at first, and tremble lest the other should discover his emotion. he gasp out a few incoherent word, indicative of the high gratification he should have in be present to the lady for whose--for whose talent he have conceive such an admiration--such an extreme admiration; and follow the Captain, scarcely know whither that gentleman lead he. he be go to see she! he be go to see she! in she be the centre of the universe. she be the kernel of the world for Pen. yesterday, before he know she, seem a period ever so long ago--a revolution be between he and that time, and a new world about to begin.
the Captain conduct his young friend to that quiet little street in Chatteris, which be call Prior's Lane, which lie in the ecclesiastical quarter of the town, close by Dean's Green and the canon' house, and be overlook by the enormous tower of the cathedral; there the Captain dwell modestly in the first floor of a low gable house, on the door of which be the brass plate of 'Creed, Tailor and Robe-maker.' Creed be dead, however. his widow be a pew-opener in the cathedral hard by; his eld son be a little scamp of a choir-boy, who play toss-halfpenny, lead his little brother into mischief, and have a voice as sweet as an angel. a couple of the latter be sit on the door-step, down which you go into the passage of the house; and they jump up with great alacrity to meet their lodger, and plunge wildly, and rather to Pen's surprise, at the swallow-tail of the Captain's dress-coat; for the truth be, that the good-natured gentleman, when he be in cash, generally bring home an apple or a piece of gingerbread for these child. "whereby the widdy never press I for rint when not convanient," as he remark afterwards to Pen, wink knowingly, and lay a finger on his nose.
Pen tumble down the step, and as he follow his companion up the creak old stair, his knee tremble under he. he could hardly see when he enter, follow the Captain, and stand in the room--in her room. he see something black before he, and wave as if make a curtsey, and hear, but quite indistinctly, Costigan make a speech over he, in which the Captain, with his usual magniloquence, express to "I child" his wish to make she know to "his dear and admirable young friend, Mr. Awther Pindinnis, a young gentleman of property in the neighbourhood, a person of refoined moind, and enviable manner, a sincare lover of poethry, and a man possest of a feeling and affectionate heart."
"it be very fine weather," Miss Fotheringay say, in an irish accent, and with a deep rich melancholy voice.
"very," say Mr. Pendennis. in this romantic way their conversation begin; and he find himself seat on a chair, and have leisure to look at the young lady.
she look still handsomer off the stage, than before the lamp. all her attitude be naturally grand and majestical. if she go and stand up against the mantelpiece her robe drape itself classically round she; her chin support itself on her hand, the other line of her form arrange themselves in full harmonious undulation--she look like a Muse in contemplation. if she sate down on a cane-bottom chair, her arm round itself over the back of the seat, her hand seem as if it ought to have a sceptre put into it, the fold of her dress fall naturally round she in order, like lady of honour round a throne, and she look like an empress. all her movement be graceful and imperial. in the morning you could see her hair be blue-black, her complexion of dazzle fairness, with the faint possible blush flickering, as it be, in her cheek. her eye be grey, with prodigious long lash; and as for her mouth, Mr. Pendennis have give I subsequently to understand, that it be of a star red colour, with which the most brilliant geranium, sealing-wax, or Guardsman's coat, could not vie.
"and very warm," continue this empress and queen of Sheba.
Mr. Pen again assent, and the conversation roll on in this manner. she ask Costigan whether he have have a pleasant evening at the George, and he recount the supper and the tumbler of punch. then the father ask she how she have be employ the morning.
"bow come," say she, "at ten, and we study Ophalia. itbe for the twenty-fourth, when I hope, sir, we shall have the honour of see ye."
"indeed, indeed, you will," Mr. Pendennis cry; wonder that she should say 'Ophalia,' and speak with an irish inflection of voice naturally, who have not the least hibernian accent on the stage.
"Ihave secure 'um for your benefit, dear," say the Captain, tap his waistcoat pocket, wherein lie Pen's sovereign, and wink at Pen, with one eye, at which the boy blush.
"Mr---the gentleman's very obleging," say Mrs. Haller.
"my name be Pendennis," say Pen, blushing. "I--i--hope you'll--you'll remember it." his heart thump so as he make this audacious declaration, that he almost choke in utter it.
"pendennis"--she answer slowly, and look he full in the eye, with a glance, so straight, so clear, so bright, so killing, with a voice so sweet, so round, so low, that the word and the glance shoot Pen through and through, and perfectly transfix he with pleasure.
"I never know the name be so pretty before," Pen say.
"'tis a very pretty name," Ophelia say. "Pentweazlebe not a pretty name. remember, papa, when we be on the Norwich Circuit, Young Pentweazle, who use to play second old man, and marry Miss Rancy, the Columbine; theybe both engage in London now, at the Queen's, and get five pound a week. Pentweazle benot his real name. 'Twas Judkin give it he, I donot know why. his name be Harrington; that is, his real name be Potts; fawther a clergyman, very respectable. Harrington be in London, and get in debt. ye remember; he come out in Falkland, to Mrs. Bunce's Julia."
"and a pretty Julia she be," the Captain interpose; "a woman of fifty, and a mother of ten child. 'tis you ought to have be Julia, or my namebe not Jack Costigan."
"I donot take the lead business then," Miss Fotheringay say modestly; "I benot fit for't till bow teach I."
"true for you, my dear," say the Captain: and bend to Pendennis, he add, "rejuice in circumstance, sir, I be for some time a fencing-master in Dublin (therebe only three man in the empire could touch I with the foil once, but Jack Costigan's get old and stiff now, sir), and my daughter have an engagement at the thayater there; and 'twas there that my friend, Mr. Bows, who see her capability, and be an uncommon 'cute man, give her lesson in the dramatic art, and make she what ye see. what have ye do since bow go, emily?"
"sure, Ihave make a pie," Emily say, with perfect simplicity. she pronounce it "Poy."
"if ye'll try it at four o'clock, sir, say the word," say Costigan gallantly. "that girl, sir, make the good veal and ham pie in England, and I think I can promise ye a glass of punch of the right flavour."
Pen have promise to be at home to dinner at six o'clock, but the rascal think he could accommodate pleasure and duty in this point, and be only too eager to accept this invitation. he look on with delight and wonder whilst Ophelia busy herself about the room, and prepare for the dinner. she arrange the glass, and lay and smooth the little cloth, all which dutie she perform with a quiet grace and good humour, which enchant her guest more and more. the "poy" arrive from the baker's in the hand of one of the little choir-boy's brother at the proper hour: and at four o'clock Pen find himself at dinner--actually at dinner with the great tragic actress in the world, and her father--with the handsome woman in all creation--with his first and only love, whom he have adore ever since when?--ever since yesterday, ever since for ever. he eat a crust of her making, he pour she out a glass of beer, he see she drink a glass of punch--just one wine-glass full--out of the tumbler which she mix for her papa. she be perfectly good-natured, and offer to mix one for Pendennis too. it be prodigiously strong; Pen have never in his life drunk so much spirit and water. be it the punch, or the punch-maker who intoxicate he?
during dinner, when the Captain, whom his daughter treat most respectfully, cease prattle about himself and his adventure, Pen try to engage the Fotheringay in conversation about poetry and about her profession. he ask she what she think of Ophelia's madness, and whether she be in love with Hamlet or not? "in love with such a little ojous wretch as that stunt manager of a Bingley?" she bristle with indignation at the thought. Pen explain it be not of she he speak, but of Ophelia of the play. "oh, indeed; if no offence be mean, none be take: but as for Bingley, indeed, she do not value he--not that glass of punch." Pen next try she on Kotzebue. "Kotzebue? who be he?"--"The author of the play in which she have be perform so admirably." "she do not know that--the man's name at the beginning of the book be Thompson," she say. Pen laugh at her adorable simplicity. he tell she of the melancholy fate of the author of the play, and how Sand have kill he. it be for the first time in her life that Miss Costigan have ever hear of Mr. Kotzebue's existence, but she look as if she be very much interested, and her sympathy suffice for honest Pen.
and in the midst of this simple conversation, the hour and a quarter which poor Pen could afford to allow himself, pass away only too quickly; and he have take leave, he be go, and away on his rapid road homeward on the back of Rebecca. she be call upon to show her mettle in the three journey which she make that day.
"what be that he be talk about, the madness of Hamlet, and the theory of the great german critic on the subject?" Emily ask of her father.
"'deed then I donot know, milly dear," answer the Captain. "wewill ask Bows when he come."
"anyhow, hebe a nice, fair-speak pretty young man," the lady say: "how many ticket do he take of you?"
"Faith, then, he take six, and gev I two guineas, Milly," the Captain say. "I suppose they young chap be not too flush of coin."
"hebe full of book-learning," Miss Fotheringay continue. "Kotzebue! he, he, what a droll name indeed, now; and the poor fellow kill by Sand, too! do ye ever hear such a thing? Iwill ask bow about it, papa, dear."
"a queer death, sure enough," ejaculate the Captain, and change the painful theme. "'tis an elegant mare the young gentleman rid," Costigan go on to say; "and a grand breakfast, intirely, that young Mister Foker give we."
"hebe good for two private box, and at lee twenty ticket, I should say," cry the daughter, a prudent lass, who always keep her fine eye on the main chance.
"Iwill go bail of that," answer the papa, and so their conversation continue awhile, until the tumbler of punch be finish; and their hour of departure soon come, too; for at half-past six Miss Fotheringay be to appear at the theatre again, whither her father always accompany she; and stand, as we have see, in the side-scene watch she, and drank spirit-and-water in the green-room with the company there.
"how beautiful she be," think Pen, cantering homeward. "how simple and how tender! how charming it be to see a woman of her commanding genius busy herself with the delightful, though humble, office of domestic life, cook dish to make her old father comfortable, and brewing drink for he with her delicate finger! how rude it be of I to begin to talk about professional matter, and how well she turn the conversation! by the way, she talk about professional matter herself; but then with what fun and humour she tell the story of her comrade, Pentweazle, as he be call! there be no humour like irish humour. her father be rather tedious, but thoroughly amiable; and how fine of he, give lesson in fencing after he quit the army, where he be the pet of the Duke of Kent! fencing! I should like to continue my fencing, or I shall forget what Angelo teach I. Uncle Arthur always like I to fence --he say it be the exercise of a gentleman. hang it. Iwill take some lesson of Captain Costigan. go along, rebecca--up the hill, old lady. Pendennis, Pendennis--how she speak the word! emily, Emily! how good, how noble, how beautiful, how perfect, she be!"
now the reader, who have have the benefit of overhear the entire conversation which Pen have with Miss Fotheringay, can judge for himself about the power of her mind, and may perhaps be dispose to think that she have not say anything astonishingly humorous or intellectual in the course of the above interview. she have marry, and take her position in the world as the most spotless and irreproachable lady since, and I have have the pleasure of make her acquaintance: and must certainly own, against my friend Pen's opinion, that his adore Emily be not a clever woman. the truth be, she have not only never hear of Kotzebue, but she have never hear of Farquhar, or Congreve, or any dramatist in whose play she have not a part: and of these drama she only know the part which concern herself. a wag once tell she that Dante be bear at Algiers: and ask her,--which Dr. Johnson write first, 'Irene,' or 'every man in his Humour.' but she have the good of the joke, for she have never hear of Irene or every Man in his Humour, or Dante, or perhaps Algiers. it be all one to she. she act what little bow tell she--where he tell she to sob, she sob--where he tell she to laugh, she laugh. she give the tirade or the repartee without the slight notion of its meaning. she go to church and go every Sunday, with a reputation perfectly intact, and be (and be) as guiltless of sense as of any other crime.
but what do our Pen know of these thing? he see a pair of bright eye, and he believe in they--a beautiful image, and he fall down and worship it. he supply the meaning which her word want; and create the divinity which he love. be Titania the first who fall in love with an ass, or pygmalion the only artist who have go crazy about a stone? he have find she; he have find what his soul thirst after. he fling himself into the stream and drink with all his might. let those say who have be thirsty once how delicious that first draught be. as he ride down the avenue towards home--Pen shriek with laughter as he see the Reverend Mr. Smirke once more come demurely away from Fairoaks on his pony. Smirke have dawdle and stay at the cottage on the way, and then dawdle with Laura over her lesson--and then look at Mrs. Pendennis's garden and improvement until he have perfectly bore out that lady: and he have take his leave at the very last minute without that invitation to dinner which he fondly expect.
Pen be full of kindness and triumph. "what, pick up and sound?" he cry out laugh. "come along back, old fellow, and eat my dinner--I have have mine: but we will have a bottle of the old wine and drink her health, Smirke."
Poor Smirke turn the pony's head round, and jog along with Arthur. his mother be charm to see he in such high spirit, and welcome Mr. Smirke for his sake, when Arthur say he have force the curate back to dine. he give a most ludicrous account of the play of the night before, and of the acting of Bingley the Manager, in his rickety Hessians, and the enormous Mrs. Bingley as the Countess, in rumpled green satin and a polish cap; he mimic they, and delight his mother and little Laura, who clap her hand with pleasure.
"and Mrs. Haller?" say Mrs. Pendennis.
"shebe a stunner, ma'am," Pen say, laugh, and use the word of his revere friend, Mr. Foker.
"a what, Arthur?" ask the lady.
"what be a stunner, Arthur?" cry Laura, in the same voice.
so he give they a queer account of Mr. Foker, and how he use to be call vat and Grains, and by other contumelious name at school: and how he be now exceedingly rich, and a Fellow Commoner at St. Boniface. but gay and communicative as he be, Mr. Pen do not say one syllable about his ride to Chatteris that day, or about the new friend whom he have make there.
when the two lady retire, Pen, with flash eye, fill up two great bumper of Madeira, and look Smirke full in the face say, "herebe to she!"
"herebe to she," say the curate with a sigh, lift the glass and empty it, so that his face be a little pink when he put it down.
Pen have even less sleep that night than on the night before. in the morning, and almost before dawn, he go out and saddle that unfortunate Rebecca himself, and ride she on the Downs like mad. again Love have rouse he--and say, "awake, Pendennis, I be here." that charming fever --that delicious longing--and fire, and uncertainty; he hug they to he--be would not have lose they for all the world.
CHAPTER VI
contain both Love and War
Cicero and Euripides do not occupy Mr. Pen much for some time after this, and honest Mr. Smirke have a very easy time with his pupil. rebecca be the animal who suffer most in the present state of Pen's mind, for, besides those day when he could publicly announce his intention of go to Chatteris to take a fencing-lesson, and go thither with the knowledge of his mother, whenever he see three hour clear before he, the young rascal make a rush for the city, and find his way to Prior's Lane. he be as frantic with vexation when Rebecca go lame, as Richard at Bosworth, when his horse be kill under he: and get deeply into the book of the man who keep the hunting-stable at Chatteris for the doctoring of his own, and the hire of another animal.
then, and perhaps once in a week, under pretence of go to read a greek play with Smirke, this young reprobate set off so as to be in time for the Competitor down coach, stay a couple of hour in Chatteris, and return on the rival which leave for London at ten at night. once his secret be nearly lose by Smirke's simplicity, of whom Mrs. Pendennis ask whether they have read a great deal the night before, or a question to that effect. Smirke be about to tell the truth, that he have never see Mr. Pen at all, when the latter's boot-heel come grind down on Mr. Smirke's toe under the table, and warn the curate not to betray he.
they have have conversation on the tender subject, of course. it be good sport (if you be not yourself engage in the conversation) to hear two man in love talk. there must be a confidant and depositary somewhere. when inform, under the most solemn vow of secrecy, of Pen's condition of mind, the curate say, with no small tremor, "that he hop it be no unworthy object--no unlawful attachment, which Pen have formed"--for if so, the poor fellow feel it would be his duty to break his vow and inform Pen's mother, and then there would be a quarrel, he feel, with sicken apprehension, and he would never again have a chance of see what he most like in the world.
"unlawful, unworthy!" Pen bounce out at the curate's question. "she be as pure as she be beautiful; I would give my heart to no other woman. I keep the matter a secret in my family, because--because--there be reason of a weighty nature which I be not at liberty to disclose. but any man who breathe a word against her purity insult both her honour and mine, and--and dammy, I willnot stand it."
Smirke, with a faint laugh, only say, "well, well, donot call I out, Arthur, for you know I cannot fight;" but by this compromise the wretched curate be put more than ever into the power of his pupil, and the Greek and mathematic suffer correspondingly.
if the reverend gentleman have have much discernment, and look into the Poet's Corner of the County Chronicle, as it arrive in the Wednesday's bag, he might have see 'Mrs. Haller,' 'passion and Genius,' 'line to Miss Fotheringay, of the Theatre Royal,' appear every week; and other verse of the most gloomy, thrilling, and passionate cast. but as these poem be no long sign NEP by their artful composer, but subscribe EROS, neither the tutor nor Helen, the good soul, who cut all her son's verse out of the paper, know that Nep be no other than that flame Eros, who sing so vehemently the character of the new actress.
"who be the lady," at last ask Mrs. Pendennis, "whom your rival be always sing in the County Chronicle? he write something like you, dear Pen, but yours be much the good. have you see Miss Fotheringay?"
Pen say yes, he have; that night he go to see the "Stranger," she act Mrs. Haller. by the way, she be go to have a benefit, and be to appear in Ophelia--suppose we be to go--Shakspeare, you know, mother-- we can get horse from the Clavering Arms. little Laura spring up with delight, she long for a play.
Pen introduce "Shakspeare, you know," because the deceased Pendennis, as become a man of his character, profess an uncommon respect for the bard of Avon, in whose work he safely say there be more poetry than in all 'Johnson's poet' put together. and though Mr. Pendennis do not much read the work in question, yet he enjoin Pen to peruse they, and often say what pleasure he should have, when the boy be of a proper age, in take he and mother to see some good play of the immortal poet.
the ready tear well up in the kind mother's eye as she remember these speech of the man who be go. she kiss her son fondly, and say she would go. Laura jump for joy. be Pen happy?--wa he ashame? as he hold his mother to he, he long to tell she all, but he keep his counsel. he would see how his mother like she; the play should be the thing, and he would try his mother like Hamlet's.
Helen, in her good humour, ask Mr. Smirke to be of the party. that ecclesiastic have be breed up by a fond parent at Clapham, who have an objection to dramatic entertainment, and he have never yet see a play. but, Shakspeare!--but to go with Mrs. Pendennis in her carriage, and sit a whole night by her side!--he could not resist the idea of so much pleasure, and make a feeble speech, in which he speak of temptation and gratitude, and finally accept Mrs. Pendennis's most kind offer. as he speak he give she a look, which make she exceedingly uncomfortable. she have see that look more than once, of late, pursue she. he become more positively odious every day in the widow's eye.
we be not go to say a great deal about Pen's courtship of Miss Fotheringay, for the reader have already have a speciman of her conversation, much of which need surely not be report. Pen sate with her hour after hour, and pour forth all his honest boyish soul to she. everything he know, or hop, or feel, or have read, or fancy, he tell to she. he never tired of talking and longing. one after another, as his thought rise in his hot eager brain, he clothe they in word, and tell they to she. her part of the tete-a-tete be not to talk, but to appear as if she understand what Pen talk (a difficult matter, for the young fellow blurt out no small quantity of nonsense), and to look exceedingly handsome and sympathising. the fact be, whilst he be make one of his tirade--and delight, perhaps, and wonder at his own eloquence, the lad would go on for twenty minute at a time--the lovely Emily, who could not comprehend a tenth part of his talk, have leisure to think about her own affair, and would arrange in her own mind how they should dress the cold mutton, or how she would turn the black satin, or make herself out of her scarf a bonnet like Miss Thackthwaite's new one, and so forth. Pen spout Byron and Moore; passion and poetry: her business be to throw up her eye, or fix they for a moment on his face, to cry, "oh, 'tis beautiful! ah, how exquisite! repeat those line again." and off the boy go, and she return to her own simple thought about the turn gown, or the hashed mutton.
in fact Pen's passion be not long a secret from the lovely Emily or her father. upon his second visit, his admiration be quite evident to both of they, and on his departure the old gentleman say to his daughter, as he wink at she over his glass of grog, "Faith, Milly darling, I think ye've hook that chap."
"Pooh, 'tis only a boy, papa dear," milly remark. "sure hebe but a child." Pen would have be very much pleased if he have hear that phrase--he be gallop home wild with pleasure, and shout out her name as he ride.
"Ye've hooked 'um any how," say the Captain, "and let I tell ye hebe not a bad fish. I ask Tom at the George, and Flint, the grocer, where his mother dale--fine fortune--drive in her chariot--splendid park and ground--Fairoaks Park--only son--property all his own at twenty-one--ye might go far and not fare so well, Miss Fotheringay."
"Them boy be mostly talk," say Milly, seriously. "ye know at Dublin how ye go on about young Poldoody, and Ihave a whole desk full of verse he write I when he be in Trinity College; but he go abroad, and his mother marry he to an Englishwoman."
"Lord Poldoody be a young nobleman; and in they itbe natural: and ye benot in the position in which ye be now, milly dear. but ye mustnot encourage this young chap too much, for, bedad, Jack Costigan willnot have any thrilling with his daughter."
"no more will his daughter, papa, you may be sure of that," Milly say. "a little sip more of the punch,--sure, 'tis beautiful. ye neednot be afraid about the young chap--I think Ibe old enough to take care of myself, Captain Costigan."
so Pen use to come day after day, rush in and gallop away, and grow more wild about the girl with every visit. sometimes the Captain be present at their meeting; but have a perfect confidence in his daughter, he be more often inclined to leave the young couple to themselves, and cock his hat over his eye, and strut off on some errand when Pen enter. how delightful those interview be! the Captain's drawing-room be a low wainscote room, with a large window look into the Dean's garden. there Pen sate and talk--and talked-- Emily, look beautiful as she sate at her work--look beautiful and calm, and the sunshine come stream in at the great window, and light up her superb face and form. in the midst of the conversation, the great bell would begin to boom, and he would pause smile, and be silent until the sound of the vast music die away--or the rook in the cathedral elm would make a great noise towards sunset--or the sound of the organ and the chorister would come over the quiet air, and gently hush Pen's talking.
by the way, it must be say that Miss Fotheringay, in a plain shawl and a close bonnet and veil, go to church every Sunday of her life, accompany by her indefatigable father, who give the response in a very rich and fine brogue, join in the psalm and chanting, and behave in the most exemplary manner.
Little Bows, the house-friend of the family, be exceedingly wroth at the notion of Miss Fotheringay's marriage with a stripling seven or eight year her junior. bow, who be a cripple, and own that he be a little more deformed even than Bingley the manager, so that he could not appear on the stage, be a singular wild man of no small talent and humour. attract first by Miss Fotheringay's beauty, he begin to teach she how to act. he shriek out in his cracked voice the part, and his pupil learn they from his lip by rote, and repeat they in her full rich tone. he indicate the attitude, and set and move those beautiful arm of her. those who remember this grand actress on the stage can recall how she use always precisely the same gesture, look, and tone; how she stand on the same plank of the stage in the same position, roll her eye at the same instant and to the same degree, and weep with precisely the same heart-rend pathos and over the same pathetic syllable. and after she have come out tremble with emotion before the audience, and look so exhausted and tearful that you fancy she would faint with sensibility, she would gather up her hair the instant she be behind the curtain, and go home to a mutton-chop and a glass of brown stout; and the harrowing labour of the day over, she go to bed and snore as resolutely and as regularly as a porter.
bow then be indignant at the notion that his pupil should throw her chance away in life by bestow her hand upon a little country squire. as soon as a London manager see she he prophesy that she would get a London engagement, and a great success. the misfortune be that the London manager have see she. she have play in London three year before, and fail from utter stupidity. since then it be that bow have take she in hand and teach her part after part. how he work and scream, and twisted, and repeat line over and over again, and with what indomitable patience and dulness she follow he! she know that he make she: and let herself be make. she be not grateful, or ungrateful, or unkind, or ill-humour. she be only stupid; and Pen be madly in love with she.
the post-horse from the Clavering Arms arrive in due time, and carry the party to the theatre at Chatteris, where Pen be gratify in perceive that a tolerably large audience be assemble. the young gentleman from Baymouth have a box, in the front of which sate Mr. Foker and his friend Mr. Spavin, splendidly attire in the most full-blow evening costume. they salute Pen in a cordial manner, and examine his party, of which they approve, for little Laura be a pretty little red-cheeked girl with a quantity of shin brown ringlet, and Mrs. Pendennis, dress in black velvet with the diamond cross which she sport on great occasion, look uncommonly handsome and majestic. behind these sate Mr. Arthur, and the gentle Smirke with the curl repose on his fair forehead, and his white tie in perfect order. he blush to find himself in such a place--but how happy be he to be there! he and Mrs. Pendennis bring book of 'Hamlet' with they to follow the tragedy, as be the custom of honest countryfolk who go to a play in state. Samuel, coachman, groom, and gardener to Mr. Pendennis, take his place in the pit, where Mr. Foker's man be also visible. it be dot with non-commission officer of the Dragoons, whose band, by kind permission of Colonel Swallowtail, be, as usual, in the orchestra; and that corpulent and distinguished warrior himself, with his Waterloo medal and a number of his young man, make a handsome show in the box.
"who be that odd-look person bow to you, Arthur?" Mrs. Pendennis ask of her son.
Pen blush a great deal. "his name be Captain Costigan, ma'am," he say --"a peninsular officer." in fact it be the Captain in a new shoot of clothe, as he call they, and with a large pair of white kid glove, one of which he wave to Pendennis, whilst he lay the other sprawl over his heart and coat-button. Pen do not say any more. and how be Mrs. Pendennis to know that Mr. Costigan be the father of Miss Fotheringay?
Mr. Hornbull, from London, be the Hamlet of the night, Mr. Bingley modestly content himself with the part of Horatio, and reserve his chief strength for William in 'Black-Eyed Susan,' which be the second piece.
we have nothing to do with the play: except to say that Ophelia look lovely, and perform with admirable wild pathos laughing, weeping, gaze wildly, wave her beautiful white arm, and fling about her snatch of flower and song with the most charming madness. what an opportunity her splendid black hair have of toss over her shoulder! she make the most charming corpse ever see; and while Hamlet and Laertes be battle in her grave, she be look out from the back scene with some curiosity towards Pen's box, and the family party assemble in it.
there be but one voice in her praise there. Mrs. Pendennis be in ecstasy with her beauty. little Laura be bewilder by the piece, and the Ghost, and the play within the play (during which, as Hamlet lie at Ophelia's knee, Pen feel that he would have like to strangle Mr. Hornbull), but cry out great praise of that beautiful young creature. Pen be charm with the effect which she produce on his mother--and the clergyman, for his part, be exceedingly enthusiastic.
when the curtain fall upon that group of slaughter personage, who be despatch so suddenly at the end of 'Hamlet,' and whose demise astonish poor little Laura not a little, there be an immense shouting and applause from all quarter of the house; the intrepid Smirke, violently excited, clap his hand, and cry out "Bravo, Bravo," as loud as the Dragoon officer themselves. these be greatly moved,--il s'agitaient sur leurs bancs,--to borrow a phrase from our neighbour. they be lead cheer into action by the portly Swallowtail, who wave his cap--the non-commission officer in the pit, of course, gallantly follow their chief. there be a roar of bravos ring through the house; Pen bellow with the loud, "Fotheringay! Fotheringay!" and Messrs. Spavin and Foker give the view-halloo from their box. even Mrs. Pendennis begin to wave about her pocket-handkerchief, and little Laura dance, laugh, clap, and look up at Pen with wonder.
Hornbull lead the beneficiaire forward, amidst burst of enthusiasm--and she look so handsome and radiant, with her hair still over her shoulder, that Pen hardly could contain himself for rapture: and he lean over his mother's chair, and shout, and hurraye, and wave his hat. it be all he could do to keep his secret from Helen, and not say, "look! thatbe the woman! benot she peerless? I tell you I love she." but he disguise these feeling under an enormous bellowing and hurraying.
as for Miss Fotheringay and her behaviour, the reader be refer to a former page for an account of that. she go through precisely the same business. she survey the house all round with glance of gratitude; and tremble, and almost sink with emotion, over her favourite trap-door. she seize the flower (Foker discharge a prodigious bouquet at she, and even Smirke make a feeble shy with a rose, and blush dreadfully when it fall into the pit). she seize the flower and press they to her swell heart--etc., etc.--in a word--we refer the reader to early page. twinkle in her breast poor old Pen see a locket which he have buy of Mr. Nathan in High Street, with the last shilling he be worth, and a sovereign borrow from Smirke.
'Black-Eyed Susan' follow, at which sweet story our gentle-hearted friend be exceedingly charm and affect: and in which Susan, with a russet gown and a pink ribbon in her cap, look to the full as lovely as Ophelia. Bingley be great in William. Goll, as the Admiral, look like the figure-head of a seventy-four; and Garbetts, as Captain Boldweather, a miscreant who form a plan for carry off black-eyed Susan, and wave an immense cock hat say, "come what may, he will be the ruin of her"--all these perform their part with their accustomed talent; and it be with a sincere regret that all our friend see the curtain drop down and end that pretty and tender story.
if Pen have be alone with his mother in the carriage as they go home, he would have tell she all, that night; but he sate on the box in the moonshine smoke a cigar by the side of Smirke, who warm himself with a comforter. Mr. Foker's tandem and lamp whirl by the sober old Clavering poster as they be a couple of mile on their road home, and Mr. Spavin salute Mrs. Pendennis's carriage with some considerable variation of Rule Britannia on the key-bugle.
it happen two day after the above gaiety that Mr. Dean of Chatteris entertain a few select clerical friend at dinner at his Deanery Home. that they drink uncommonly good port wine, and abuse the Bishop over their dessert, be very likely matter: but with such we have nothing at present to do. our friend Doctor Portman, of Clavering, be one of the Dean's guest, and be a gallant man, and see from his place at the mahogany the Dean's lady walk up and down the grass, with her child sport around she, and her pink parasol over her lovely head--the Doctor stept out of the french window of the dining-room into the lawn, which skirt that apartment, and leave the other white neckcloth to gird at my lord Bishop. then the Doctor go up and offer Mrs. Dean his arm, and they saunter over the ancient velvet lawn, which have be mow and roll for immemorial Deans, in that easy, quiet, comfortable manner, in which people of middle age and good temper walk after a good dinner, in a calm golden summer evening, when the sun have but just sink behind the enormous cathedral-tower, and the sickle-shape moon be grow every instant brighter in the heaven.
