now at the end of the Dean's garden there be, as we have state, Mrs. Creed's house, and the window of the first-floor room be open to admit the pleasant summer air. a young lady of six-and-twenty, whose eye be perfectly wide open, and a luckless boy of eighteen, blind with love and infatuation, be in that chamber together; in which person, as we have before see they in the same place, the reader will have no difficulty in recognise Mr. Arthur Pendennis and Miss Costigan.
the poor boy have take the plunge. tremble with passionate emotion, his heart beat and throb fiercely, tear rush forth in spite of he, his voice almost choke with feeling, poor Pen have say those word which he could withhold no more, and fling himself and his whole store of love, and admiration, and ardour at the foot of this mature beauty. be he the first who have do so? have none before or after he stake all their treasure of life, as a savage do his land and possession against a draught of the fair-skin' fire-water, or a couple of bauble eye?
"do your mother know of this, Arthur?" say Miss Fotheringay, slowly. he seize her hand madly and kiss it a thousand time. she do not withdraw it. "do the old lady know it?" Miss Costigan think to herself, "well, perhaps she may," and then she remember what a handsome diamond cross Mrs. Pendennis have on the night of the play, and think, "sure 'twill go in the family."
"calm yourself, dear Arthur," she say, in her low rich voice, and sniffle sweetly and gravely upon he. then, with her disengage hand, she put the hair lightly off his throbbing forehead. he be in such a rapture and whirl of happiness that he could hardly speak. at last he gasp out, "my mother have see you, and admire you beyond measure. she will learn to love you soon: who can do otherwise? she will love you because I do."
"'deed then, I think you do," say Miss Costigan, perhaps with a sort of pity for Pen.
think she do! of course here Mr. Pen go off into a rhapsody through which, as we have perfect command over our own feeling, we have no reason to follow the lad. of course, love, truth, and eternity be produce: and word be try but find impossible to plumb the tremendous depth of his affection. this speech, we say, be no business of ours. it be most likely not very wise, but what right have we to overhear? let the poor boy fle out his simple heart at the woman's foot, and deal gently with he. it be good to love wisely, no doubt: but to love foolishly be well than not to be able to love at all. some of we cannot: and be proud of our impotence too.
at the end of his speech Pen again kiss the imperial hand with rapture --and I believe it be at this very moment, and while Mrs. Dean and Doctor Portman be engage in conversation, that young Master Ridley Roset, her son, pull his mother by the back of her capacious dress and said--
"I say, ma! look up there"--and he waggle his innocent head.
that be, indeed, a view from the Dean's garden such as seldom be see by Deans--or be write in Chapters. there be poor Pen perform a salute upon the rosy finger of his charmer, who receive the embrace with perfect calmness and good humour. Master Ridley look up and grin, little Miss Rosa look at her brother, and open the mouth of astonishment. Mrs. Dean's countenance defy expression, and as for Dr. Portman, when he behold the scene, and see his prime favourite and dear pupil Pen, he stand mute with rage and wonder.
Mrs. Haller spy the party below at the same moment, and give a start and a laugh. "sure therebe somebody in the Dean's garden," she cry out; and withdraw with perfect calmness, whilst Pen dart away with his face glow like coal. the garden party have re-enter the house when he venture to look out again. the sickle moon be blaze bright in the heaven then, the star be glitter, the bell of the cathedral tolling nine, the Dean's guest (all save one, who have call for his horse dumpling, and ride off early) be partake of tea and butter cake in Mrs. Dean's drawing-room--when Pen take leave of Miss Costigan.
Pen arrive at home in due time afterwards, and be go to slip off to bed, for the poor lad be greatly wear and agitated, and his high-string nerve have be at almost a madden pitch when a summon come to he by John the old footman, whose countenance bear a very ominous look, that his mother must see he below.
on this he tie on his neckcloth again, and go downstairs to the drawing-room. there sate not only his mother, but her friend, the Reverend Doctor Portman. Helen's face look very pale by the light of the lamp--the Doctor's be flushed, on the contrary, and quiver with anger and emotion.
Pen see at once that there be a crisis, and that there have be a discovery. "now for it," he think.
"where have you be, Arthur?" Helen say in a trembling voice.
"how can you look that--that dear lady, and a christian clergyman in the face, sir?" bounce out the Doctor, in spite of Helen's pale, appeal look. "where have he be? where his mother's son should have be ashamed to go. for your mother's an angel, sir, an angel. how dare you bring pollution into her house, and make that spotless creature wretche with the thought of your crime?"
"Sir!" say Pen.
"donot deny it, sir," roar the Doctor. "donot add lie, sir, to your other infamy. I see you myself, sir. I see you from the Dean's garden. I see you kiss the hand of that infernal painted---"
"stop," Pen say, clap his fist on the table, till the lamp flicker up and shake, "I be a very young man, but you will please to remember that I be a gentleman--I will hear no abuse of that lady."
"Lady, sir," cry the Doctor, "that a lady--you--you--you stand in your mother's presence and call that--that woman a lady!---"
"in anybody's presence," shout out Pen. "she be worthy of any place. she be as pure as any woman. she be as good as she be beautiful. if any man but you insult she, I would tell he what I think; but as you be my old friend, I suppose you have the privilege to doubt of my honour."
"no, no, Pen, dear Pen," cry out Helen in an excess of joy. "I tell, I tell you, Doctor, he be not--not what you think:" and the tender creature come tremble forward fling herself on Pen's shoulder.
Pen feel himself a man, and a match for all the doctor in Doctordom. he be glad this explanation have come. "you see how beautiful she be," he say to his mother, with a soothe, protect air, like Hamlet with Gertrude in the play. "I tell you, dear mother, she be as good. when you know she you will say so. she be of all, except you, the simple, the kind, the most affectionate of woman. why should she not be on the stage?--she maintain her father by her labour."
"drunken old reprobate," growl the Doctor, but Pen do not hear or heed.
"if you could see, as I have, how orderly her life be, how pure and pious her whole conduct, you would--as I do--yes, as I do"--(with a savage look at the doctor)--"spurn the slanderer who dare to do she wrong. her father be an officer, and distinguish himself in Spain. he be a friend of his Royal Highness the Duke of Kent, and be intimately know to the Duke of Wellington, and some of the first officer of our army. he have meet my uncle Arthur at Lord Hill's, he think. his own family be one of the most ancient and respectable in Ireland, and indeed be as good as our own. the Costigans be king of Ireland."
"why, God bless my soul," shriek out the doctor, hardly know whether to burst with rage or laughter, "you donot mean to say you want to marry she?"
Pen put on his most princely air. "what else, Dr. Portman," he say, "do you suppose would be my desire?" utterly foil in his attack, and knock down by this sudden lunge of Pen's, the doctor could only gasp out, "Mrs. Pendennis, ma'am, send for the Major."
"send for the Major? with all my heart," say Arthur Prince of Pendennis and Grand Duke of Fairoaks, with a most superb wave of the hand. and the colloquy terminate by the writing of those two letter which be lay on Major Pendennis's breakfast-table, in London, at the commencement of Prince Arthur's most veracious history.
CHAPTER VII
in which the Major make his Appearance
our acquaintance, Major Arthur Pendennis, arrive in due time at Fairoaks, after a dreary night pass in the mail-coach, where a stout fellow-passenger, swell preternaturally with great-coat, have crowd he into a corner, and keep he awake by snore indecently; where a widow lady, opposite, have not only shut out the fresh air by close all the window of the vehicle, but have fill the interior with fume of Jamaica rum and water, which she suck perpetually from a bottle in her reticule; where, whenever he catch a brief moment of sleep, the twanging of the horn at the turnpike-gate, or the scuffling of his huge neighbour wedge he close and close, or the play of the widow's foot on his own tender toe, speedily wake up the poor gentleman to the horror and reality of life--a life which have pass away now and become impossible, and only live in fond memory. eight mile an hour, for twenty or five-and-twenty hour, a tight mail-coach, a hard seat, a gouty tendency, a perpetual change of coachman grumble because you do not fee they enough, a fellow-passenger partial to spirit-and-water,--who have not bear with these evil in the jolly old time? and how could people travel under such difficulty? and yet they do, and be merry too. next the widow, and by the side of the Major's servant on the roof, be a couple of school-boy go home for the midsummer holiday, and Major Pendennis wonder to see they sup at the inn at Bagshot, where they take in a cargo of ham, eggs, pie, pickle, tea, coffee, and boil beef, which surprise the poor Major, sip a cup of very feeble tea, and think with a tender dejection that Lord Steyne's dinner be come off at that very moment. the ingenuous ardour of the boy, however, amuse the Major, who be very good-natured, and he become the more interested when he find that the one who travel inside with he be a lord's son, whose noble father Pendennis, of course, have meet in the world of fashion which he frequent. the little lord sleep all night through, in spite of the squeezing, and the horn-blowing, and the widow; and he look as fresh as paint (and, indeed; pronounce himself to be so) when the Major, with a yellow face, a bristly beard, a wig out of curl, and strong rheumatic grief shoot through various limb of his uneasy body, descend at the little lodge-gate at Fairoaks, where the porteress and gardener's wife reverentially greet he, and, still more respectfully, Mr. Morgan, his man.
Helen be on the look-out for this expected guest, and see he from her window. but she do not come forward immediately to greet he. she know the Major do not like to be see at a surprise, and require a little preparation before he care to be visible. Pen, when a boy, have incur sad disgrace by carry off from the Major's dressing-table a little morocco box, which it must be confess contain the Major's back tooth, which he naturally would leave out of his jaw in a jolting mail-coach, and without which he would not choose to appear. Morgan, his man, make a mystery of mystery of his wig: curl they in private place: introduce they mysteriously to his master's room;--nor without his head of hair would the major care to show himself to any member of his family, or any acquaintance. he go to his apartment then and supply these deficiency; he groan, and moan, and wheeze, and curse Morgan through his toilet, as an old buck will, who have be up all night with a rheumatism, and have a long duty to perform. and finally be belt, curl, and set straight, he descend upon the drawing-room, with a grave majestic air, such as befit one who be at once a man of business and a man of fashion.
Pen be not there, however; only Helen, and little Laura sew at her knee; and to whom he never present more than a forefinger, as he do on this occasion after salute his sister-in-law. Laura take the finger tremble and drop it--and then flee out of the room. Major Pendennis do not want to keep she, or indeed to have she in the house at all, and have his private reason for disapprove of she: which we may mention on some future occasion. meanwhile Laura disappear and wander about the premise seek for Pen: whom she presently find in the orchard, pace up and down a walk there in earnest conversation with Mr. Smirke. he be so occupy that he do not hear Laura's clear voice sing out, until Smirke pull he by the coat and point towards she as she come run.
she run up and put her hand into his. "come in, Pen," she say, "therebe somebody come; uncle Arthur's come."
"he be, be he?" say Pen, and she feel he grasp her little hand. he look round at Smirke with uncommon fierceness, as much as to say, I be ready for he or any man.--mr. Smirke cast up his eye as usual and heave a gentle sigh.
"lead on, Laura," Pen say, with a half fierce, half comic air--"lead on, and say I wait upon my uncle." but he be laugh in order to hide a great anxiety: and be screw his courage inwardly to face the ordeal which he know be now before he.
Pen have take Smirke into his confidence in the last two day, and after the outbreak attendant on the discovery of Doctor Portman, and during every one of those forty-eight hour which he have pass in Mr. Smirke's society, have do nothing but talk to his tutor about Miss fotheringay-- Miss Emily Fotheringay--Emily, etc., to all which talk Smirke listen without difficulty, for he be in love himself, most anxious in all thing to propitiate Pen, and indeed very much himself enrapture by the personal charm of this goddess, whose like, never have be before at a theatrical representation, he have not behold until now. Pen's fire and volubility, his hot eloquence and rich poetical trope and figure, his manly heart, kind, ardent, and hopeful, refuse to see any defect in the person he love, any difficulty in their position that he might not overcome, have half convince Mr. Smirke that the arrangement propose by Mr. Pen be a very feasible and prudent one, and that it would be a great comfort to have emily settle at Fairoaks, Captain Costigan in the yellow room, establish for life there, and Pen marry at eighteen.
and it be a fact that in these two day the boy have almost talk over his mother, too; have parry all her objection one after another with that indignant good sense which be often the perfection of absurdity; and have bring she almost to acquiesce in the belief that if the marriage be doom in heaven, why doom it be--that if the young woman be a good person, it be all that she for her part have to ask; and rather to dread the arrival of the guardian uncle who she foresaw would regard Mr. Pen's marriage in a manner very different to that simple, romantic, honest, and utterly absurd way in which the widow be already dispose to look at question of this sort.
for as in the old allegory of the gold and silver shield, about which the two knight quarrel, each be right accord to the point from which he look: so about marriage; the question whether it be foolish or good, wise or otherwise, depend upon the point of view from which you regard it. if it mean a snug house in Belgravia, and pretty little dinner-party, and a pretty little brougham to drive in the Park, and a decent provision not only for the young people, but for the little Belgravians to come; and if these be the necessary of life (and they be with many honest people), to talk of any other arrangement be an absurdity: of love in lodging--a babyish folly of affection: that cannot pay coach-hire or afford a decent milliner--as mere wicked balderdash and childish romance. if on the other hand your opinion be that people, not with an assured subsistence, but with a fair chance to obtain it, and with the stimulus of hope, health, and strong affection, may take the chance of Fortune for well or bad, and share its good or its evil together, the polite theory then become an absurdity in its turn: bad than an absurdity, a blasphemy almost, and doubt of Providence; and a man who wait to make his choose woman happy, until he can drive she to church in a neat little carriage with a pair of horse, be no well than a coward or a trifl, who be neither worthy of love nor of fortune.
I donot say that the town folk be not right, but Helen Pendennis be a country-breed woman, and the book of life, as she interpret it, tell she a different story to that page which be read in city. like most soft and sentimental woman, matchmaking, in general, form a great part of her thought, and I daresay she have begin to speculate about her son's fall in love and marry long before the subject have ever enter into the brain of the young gentleman. it please she (with that dismal pleasure which the idea of sacrifice themselves give to certain woman) to think of the day when she would give up all to Pen, and he should bring his wife home, and she would surrender the key and the good bedroom, and go and sit at the side of the table, and see he happy. what do she want in life, but to see the lad prosper? as an empress certainly be not too good for he, and would be honour by become Mrs. Pen; so if he select humble Esther instead of Queen Vashti, she would be content with his lordship's choice. never mind how lowly or poor the person might be who be to enjoy that prodigious honour, Mrs. Pendennis be willing to bow before she and welcome she, and yield she up the first place. but an actress--a mature woman, who have long cease blushing except with rouge, as she stand under the eager glance of thousand of eye--an illiterate and ill-breed person, very likely, who must have live with light associate, and have hear doubtful conversation--oh! it be hard that such a one should be choose, and that the matron should be depose to give place to such a Sultana.
all these doubt the widow lay before Pen during the two day which have of necessity to elapse ere the uncle come down; but he meet they with that happy frankness and ease which a young gentleman exhibit at his time of life, and rout his mother's objection with infinite satisfaction to himself. Miss Costigan be a paragon of virtue and delicacy; she be as sensitive as the most timid maiden; she be as pure as the unsullied snow; she have the fine manner, the most graceful wit and genius, the most charming refinement and justness of appreciation in all matter of taste; she have the most admirable temper and devotion to her father, a good old gentleman of high family and fallen fortune, who have live, however, with the good society in Europe: he be in no hurry, and could afford to wait any time,--till he be one-and-twenty. but he feel (and here his face assume an awful and harrowing solemnity) that he be engage in the one only passion of his life, and that DEATH alone could close it.
Helen tell he, with a sad smile and shake of the head, that people survive these passion, and as for long engagement contract between very young man and old woman--she know an instance in her own family-- Laura's poor father be an instance--how fatal they be.
Mr. Pen, however, be resolve that death must be his doom in case of disappointment, and rather than this--rather than baulk he, in fact-- this lady would have submit to any sacrifice or personal pain, and would have go down on her knee and have kiss the foot of a Hottentot daughter-in-law.
Arthur know his power over the widow, and the young tyrant be touch whilst he exercise it. in those two day he bring she almost into submission, and patronise she very kindly; and he pass one evening with the lovely pie-maker at Chatteris, in which he brag of his influence over his mother; and he spend the other night in compose a most flaming and conceited copy of verse to his divinity, in which he vow, like Montrose, that he would make she famous with his sword and glorious by his pen, and that he would love she as no mortal woman have be adore since the creation of womankind.
it be on that night, long after midnight, that wakeful Helen, pass stealthily by her son's door, see a light streaming through the chink of the door into the dark passage, and hear Pen tossing and tumbling, and mumble verse in his bed. she wait outside for a while, anxiously listen to he. in infantile fever and early boyish illness, many a night before, the kind soul have so keep watch. she turn the lock very softly now, and go in so gently, that Pen for a moment do not see she. his face be turn from she. his paper on his desk be scatter about, and more be lie on the bed round he. he be bite a pencil and thinking of rhyme and all sort of folly and passion. he be Hamlet jump into Ophelia's grave: he be the Stranger take Mrs. Haller to his arm, beautiful Mrs. Haller, with the raven ringlet fall over her shoulder. despair and Byron, Thomas Moore and all the love of the Angels, Waller and Herrick, Beranger and all the love-song he have ever read, be work and seethe in this young gentleman's mind, and he be at the very height and paroxysm of the imaginative frenzy when his mother find he.
"Arthur," say the mother's soft silver voice: and he start up and turn round. he clutch some of the paper and push they under the pillow.
"why donot you go to sleep, my dear?" she say, with a sweet tender smile, and sate down on the bed and take one of his hot hand.
Pen look at she wildly for an instant--"i couldnot sleep," he said--" I--I be--I be write."--And hereupon he fling his arm round her neck and say, "o mother! I love she, I love her!"--How could such a kind soul as that help soothe and pity he? the gentle creature do she good: and think with a strange wonderment and tenderness that it be only yesterday that he be a child in that bed; and how she use to come and say her prayer over it before he wake upon holiday morning.
they be very grand verse, no doubt, although Miss Fotheringay do not understand they; but old cos, with a wink and a knowing finger on his nose, say, "put they up with th' other letther, Milly darling. Poldoody's pome be nothing to this." so Milly lock up the manuscript.
when then, the Major be dressed and presentable, present himself to Mrs. Pendennis, he find in the course of ten minute' colloquy that the poor widow be not merely distressed at the idea of the marriage contemplate by Pen, but actually more distressed at think that the boy himself be unhappy about it, and that his uncle and he should have any violent altercation on the subject. she beseech Major Pendennis to be very gentle with Arthur: "he have a very high spirit, and will not brook unkind word," she hint. "Dr. Portman speak to he rather roughly--and I must own unjustly, the other night--for my dear boy's honour be as high as any mother can desire--but Pen's answer quite frighten I, it be so indignant. recollect he be a man now; and be very--very cautious," say the widow, lay a fair long hand on the Major's sleeve.
he take it up, kiss it gallantly and look in her alarmed face with wonder, and a scorn which he be too polite to show. "Bon Dieu!" think the old negotiator, "the boy have actually talk the woman round, and shewould get he a wife as she would a toy if Master cry for it. why be there no such thing as lettre-de-cachet--and a Bastille for young fellow of family?" the Major live in such good company that he might be excuse for feel like an Earl.--He kiss the widow's timid hand, press it in both his, and lay it down on the table with one of his own over it, as he smile and look she in the face.
"Confess," say he, "now, that you be think how you possibly can make it up to your conscience to let the boy have his own way."
she blush and be move in the usual manner of female. "I be think that he be very unhappy--and I be too----"
"to contradict he or to let he have his own wish?" ask the other; and add, with great comfort to his inward self, "Ibe d----d if he shall."
"to think that he should have form so foolish and cruel and fatal an attachment," the widow say, "which can but end in pain whatever be the issue."
"the issue shallnot be marriage, my dear sister," the Major say resolutely. "webe not go to have a Pendennis, the head of the house, marry a stroll mountebank from a booth. no, no, we willnot marry into Greenwich Fair, ma'am."
"if the match be break suddenly off," the widow interpose, "I donot know what may be the consequence. I know Arthur's ardent temper, the intensity of his affection, the agony of his pleasure and disappointment, and I tremble at this one if it must be. indeed, indeed, it must not come on he too suddenly."
"my dear madam," the Major say, with an air of the deep commiseration "Ihave no doubt Arthur will have to suffer confoundedly before he get over the little disappointment. but be he, think you, the only person who have be so render miserable?"
"no, indeed," say Helen, hold down her eye. she be think of her own case, and be at that moment seventeen again--and most miserable.
"I, myself," whisper her brother-in-law, "have undergo a disappointment in early life. a young woman with fifteen thousand pound, niece to an Earl--most accomplished creature--a third of her money would have run up my promotion in no time, and I should have be a lieutenant--colonel at thirty: but it might not be. I be but a penniless lieutenant: her parent interfere: and I embark for India, where I have the honour of be secretary to Lord Buckley, when commander-in-chief without she. what happen? we return our letter, send back our lock of hair (the Major here pass his finger through his wig), we suffer--but we recover. she be now a baronet's wife with thirteen grow-up child; alter, it be true, in person; but her daughter remind I of what she be, and the third be to be present early next week."
Helen do not answer. she be still think of old time. I suppose if one live to be a hundred: there be certain passage of one's early life whereof the recollection will always carry we back to youth again, and that Helen be think of one of these.
"look at my own brother, my dear creature," the Major continue gallantly: "he himself, you know, have a little disappointment when he start in the--the medical profession--an eligible opportunity present itself. Miss Balls, I remember the name, be daughter of an apoth--a practitioner in very large practice; my brother have very nearly succeed in his suit.--but difficulty arise: disappointment supervene, and-- and I be sure he have no reason to regret the disappointment, which give he this hand," say the Major, and he once more politely press Helen's finger.
"those marriage between people of such different rank and age," say Helen, "be sad thing. I have know they produce a great deal of unhappiness.--Laura's father, my cousin, who--who be bring up with me"--she add, in a low voice, "be an instance of that."
"most injudicious," cut in the Major. "I donot know anything more painful than for a man to marry his superior in age or his inferior in station. Fancy marry a woman of low rank of life, and have your house fill with her confound tag-rag-and-bobtail of relation! Fancy your wife attach to a mother who drop her h's, or call Maria Marire! how be you to introduce she into society? my dear Mrs. Pendennis, I will name no name, but in the very good circle of London society I have see man suffer the most excruciating agony, I have know they to be cut, to be lose utterly, from the vulgarity of their wife' connection. what do Lady Snapperton do last year at her dejeune dansant after the Bohemian Ball? she tell Lord Brouncker that he might bring his daughter or send they with a proper chaperon, but that she would not receive Lady Brouncker who be a druggist's daughter, or some such thing, and as Tom Wagg remark of she, never want medicine certainly, for she never have an h in her life. Good Ged, what would have be the trifling pang of a separation in the first instance to the endure infliction of a constant misalliance and intercourse with low people?"
"what, indeed!" say Helen, dimly dispose towards laughter, but yet check the inclination, because she remember in what prodigious respect her deceased husband hold Major Pendennis and his story of the great world.
"then this fatal woman be ten year old than that silly young scapegrace of an Arthur. what happen in such case, my dear creature? I donot mind tell you, now we be alone that in the high state of society, misery, undeviating misery, be the result. look at Lord Clodworthy come into a room with his wife--why, good Ged, she look like Clodworthy's mother. whatbe the case between Lord and Lady Willowbank, whose love match be notorious? he have already cut she down twice when she have hang herself out of jealousy for Mademoiselle de Sainte Cunegonde, the dancer; and mark my word, good Ged, one day hewill not cut the old woman down. no, my dear madam, you be not in the world, but I be: you be a little romantic and sentimental (you know you be--woman with those large beautiful eye always be); you must leave this matter to my experience. marry this woman! marry at eighteen an actress of thirty--bah bah!--I would as soon he send into the kitchen and marry the cook."
"I know the evil of premature engagement," sigh out Helen: and as she have make this allusion no less than thrice in the course of the above conversation, and seem to be so oppress with the notion of long engagement and unequal marriage, and as the circumstance we have to relate will explain what perhaps some person be anxious to know, namely who little Laura be, who have appear more than once before we, it will be as well to clear up these point in another chapter.
CHAPTER VIII
in which Pen be keep wait at the Door, while the Reader be inform who little Laura be.
once upon a time, then, there be a young gentleman of Cambridge University who come to pass the long vacation at the village where young Helen Thistlewood be live with her mother, the widow of the lieutenant slay at Copenhagen. this gentleman, whose name be the Reverend Francis Bell, be nephew to Mrs. Thistlewood, and by consequence, own cousin to Miss Helen, so that it be very right that he should take lodging in his aunt's house, who live in a very small way; and there he pass the long vacation, read with three or four pupil who accompany he to the village. Mr. Bell be fellow of a college, and famous in the University for his learning and skill as a tutor.
his two kinswoman understand pretty early that the reverend gentleman be engage to be marry, and be only wait for a college live to enable he to fulfil his engagement. his intended bride be the daughter of another parson, who have act as Mr. Bell's own private tutor in Bell's early life, and it be whilst under Mr. Coacher's roof, indeed, and when only a boy of seventeen or eighteen year of age, that the impetuous young Bell have fling himself at the foot of Miss Martha Coacher, whom he be help to pick pea in the garden. on his knee, before those pea and she, he pledge himself to an endless affection.
Miss Coacher be by many year the young fellow's senior and her own heart have be lacerate by many previous disappointment in the matrimonial line. no less than three pupil of her father have trifle with those young affection. the apothecary of the village have despicably jilt she. the dragoon officer, with whom she have dance so many many time during that happy season which she pass at Bath with her gouty grandmamma, one day gaily shake his bridle-rein and gallop away never to return. wound by the shaft of repeat ingratitude, can it be wonder at that the heart of Martha Coacher should pant to find rest somewhere? she listen to the proposal of the gawky gallant honest boy, with great kindness and good-humour; at the end of his speech she say, "Law, Bell, Ibe sure you be too young to think of such thing;" but intimate that she too would revolve they in her own virgin bosom. she could not refer Mr. Bell to her mamma, for Mr. Coacher be a widower, and be immerse in his book, be of course unable to take the direction of so frail and wondrous an article as a lady's heart, which Miss Martha have to manage for herself.
a lock of her hair, tie up in a piece of blue ribbon, convey to the happy Bell the result of the Vestal's conference with herself. Thrice before have she snipt off one of her auburn ringlet, and give they away. the possessor be faithless, but the hair have grow again: and Martha have indeed occasion to say that man be deceiver when she hand over this token of love to the simple boy.
number 6, however, be an exception to former passion--Francis Bell be the most faithful of lover. when his time arrive to go to college, and it become necessary to acquaint Mr. Coacher of the arrangement that have be make, the latter cry, "God bless my soul, I havenot the least idea what be go on;" as be indeed very likely, for he have be take in three time before in precisely a similar manner; and Francis go to the University resolve to conquer honour, so as to be able to lay they at the foot of his beloved Martha.
this prize in view make he labour prodigiously. news come, term after term, of the honour he win. he send the prize-book for his college essay to old Coacher, and his silver declamation cup to Miss Martha. in due season he be high among the Wranglers, and a fellow of his college; and during all the time of these transaction a constant tender correspondence be keep up with Miss Coacher, to whose influence, and perhaps with justice, he attribute the success which he have win.
by the time, however, when the Rev. Francis Bell, M.A., and Fellow and tutor of his College, be twenty-six year of age, it happen that Miss Coacher be thirty-four, nor have her charm, her manner, or her temper improve since that sunny day in the springtime of life when he find she pick pea in the garden. have achieve his honour he relax in the ardour of his study, and his judgment and taste also perhaps become cool. the sunshine of the pea-garden fade away from Miss Martha, and poor Bell find himself engage--and his hand pledge to that bond in a thousand letter--to a coarse, ill-temper, ill-favour, ill-mannered, middle-aged woman.
it be in consequence of one of many altercation (in which Martha's eloquence shone, and in which therefore she be frequently pleased to indulge) that Francis refuse to take his pupil to Bearleader's Green, where Mr. Coacher's living be, and where Bell be in the habit of spend the summer: and he bethink he that he would pass the vacation at his aunt's village, which he have not see for many year--not since little Helen be a girl and use to sit on his knee. down then he come and live with they. Helen be grow a beautiful young woman now. the cousin be nearly four month together, from June to October. they walk in the summer evening: they meet in the early morn. they read out of the same book when the old lady doze at night over the candle. what little Helen know, Frank teach she. she sing to he: she give her artless heart to he. she be aware of all his story. have he make any secret?--had he not show the picture of the woman to whom he be engage, and with a blush,--her letter, hard, eager, and cruel?--the day go on and on, happy and close, with more kindness, more confidence, and more pity. at last one morning in October come, when Francis go back to college, and the poor girl feel that her tender heart be go with he.
Frank too waken up from the delightful midsummer dream to the horrible reality of his own pain. he gnash and tear at the chain which bind he. he be frantic to break it and be free. should he confess?--give his saving to the woman to whom he be bind, and beg his release?--there be time yet--he temporise. no living might fall in for year to come. the cousin go on correspond sadly and fondly: the betrothed woman, hard, jealous, and dissatisfied, complain bitterly, and with reason, of her Francis's altered tone.
at last thing come to a crisis, and the new attachment be discover. Francis own it, care not to disguise it, rebuke Martha with her violent temper and angry imperiousness, and, bad of all, with her inferiority and her age.
her reply be, that if he do not keep his promise she would carry his letter into every court in the kingdom--letter in which his love be pledge to her ten thousand time; and, after expose he to the world as the perjurer and traitor he be, she would kill herself.
Frank have one more interview with Helen, whose mother be dead then, and who be live companion with old Lady Pontypool,--one more interview, where it be resolve that he be to do his duty; that be, to redeem his vow; that be, to pay a debt cozen from he by a sharper; that be, to make two honest people miserable. so the two judge their duty to be, and they part.
the living fall in only too soon; but yet Frank Bell be quite a grey and wear-out man when he be induct into it. Helen write he a letter on his marriage, begin "my dear Cousin," and end "always truly your." she send he back the other letter, and the lock of his hair-- all but a small piece. she have it in her desk when she be talk to the Major.
Bell live for three or four year in his living, at the end of which time, the Chaplainship of Coventry Island fall vacant, Frank apply for it privately, and have procure it, announce the appointment to his wife. she object, as she do to everything. he tell she bitterly that he do not want she to come: so she go. Bell go out in Governor Crawley's time, and be very intimate with that gentleman in his later year. and it be in Coventry Island, year after his own marriage, and five year after he have hear of the birth of Helen's boy, that his own daughter be bear.
she be not the daughter of the first Mrs. Bell, who die of island fever very soon after Helen Pendennis and her husband, to whom Helen have tell everything, write to inform Bell of the birth of their child. "I be old, be I?" say Mrs. Bell the first; "I be old, and her inferior, be I? but I marry you, Mr. Bell, and keep you from marry she?" and hereupon she die. Bell marry a colonial lady, whom be love fondly. but he be not doom to prosper in love; and, this lady die in childbirth, Bell give up too: send his little girl home to Helen Pendennis and her husband, with a parting prayer that they would befriend she.
the little thing come to Fairoaks from Bristol, which be not very far off, dress in black, and in company of a soldier's wife, her nurse, at part from whom she weep bitterly. but she soon dry up her grief under Helen's motherly care.
round her neck she have a locket with hair, which Helen have give, ah how many year ago! to poor Francis, dead and bury. this child be all that be leave of he, and she cherish, as so tender a creature would, the legacy which he have bequeath to she. the girl's name, as his die letter state, be Helen Laura. but John Pendennis, though he accept the trust, be always rather jealous of the orphan; and gloomily order that she should be call by her own mother's name; and not by that first one which her father have give she. she be afraid of Mr. Pendennis, to the last moment of his life. and it be only when her husband be go that Helen dare openly to indulge in the tenderness which she feel for the little girl.
thus it be that Laura Bell become Mrs. Pendennis's daughter. neither her husband nor that gentleman's brother, the Major, view she with very favourable eye. she remind the first of circumstance in his wife's life which he be force to accept, but would have forget much more willingly and as for the second, how could he regard she? she be neither relate to his own family of Pendennis, nor to any nobleman in this empire, and she have but a couple of thousand pound for her fortune.
and now let Mr. Pen come in, who have be wait all this while.
have string up his nerve, and prepare himself, without at the door, for the meeting, he come to it, determine to face the awful uncle. he have settle in his mind that the encounter be to be a fierce one, and be resolve on bear it through with all the courage and dignity of the famous family which he represent. and he fling open the door and enter with the most severe and warlike expression, armed cap-a-pie as it be, with lance couch and plume display, and glance at his adversary, as if to say, "come on, Ibe ready."
the old man of the world, as he survey the boy's demeanour, could hardly help a grin at his admirable pompous simplicity. major Pendennis too have examine his ground; and find that the widow be already half win over to the enemy, and have a shrewd notion that threat and tragic exhortation would have no effect upon the boy, who be inclined to be perfectly stubborn and awfully serious, the Major lay aside the authoritative manner at once, and with the most good-humour natural smile in the world, hold out his hand to Pen, shake the lad's passive finger gaily, and say, "well, Pen, my boy, tell we all about it."
Helen be delighted with the generosity of the Major's good-humour. on the contrary, it quite take aback and disappoint poor Pen, whose nerve be string up for a tragedy, and who feel that his grand entree be altogether baulk and ludicrous. he blush and wince with mortified vanity and bewilderment. he feel immensely incline to begin to cry--"I-- I--I donot know that you be come till just now," he say: "be--is-- town very full, I suppose?"
if Pen could hardly gulp his tear down, it be all the Major could do to keep from laughter. he turn round and shoot a comical glance at Mrs. Pendennis, who too feel that the scene be at once ridiculous and sentimental. and so, have nothing to say, she go up and kiss Mr. Pen: as he think of her tenderness and soft obedience to his wish, it be very possible too the boy be melt.
"what a couple of fool they be," think the old guardian. "if I havenot come down, she would have drive over in state to pay a visit and give her blessing to the young lady's family."
"come, come," say he, still grin at the couple, "let we have as little sentiment as possible, and, Pen, my good fellow, tell we the whole story."
Pen get back at once to his tragic and heroical air. "the story be, sir," say he, "as I have write it to you before. I have make the acquaintance of a most beautiful and most virtuous lady; of a high family, although in reduced circumstance: I have find the woman in whom I know that the happiness of my life be centre; I feel that I never, never can think about any woman but she. I be aware of the difference of our age and other difficulty in my way. but my affection be so great that I feel I could surmount all these; that we both could: and she have consent to unite her lot with mine, and to accept my heart and my fortune."
"how much be that, my boy?" say the Major. "have anybody leave you some money? I donot know that you be worth a shilling in the world."
"you know what I have be his," cry out Mrs. Pendennis.
"good heavens, madam, hold your tongue!" be what the guardian be disposed to say; but he keep his temper, not without a struggle. "no doubt, no doubt," he say. "you would sacrifice anything for he. everybody know that. but it be, after all then, your fortune which Pen be offer to the young lady; and of which he wish to take possession at eighteen."
"I know my mother will give I anything," Pen say, look rather disturbed.
"yes, my good fellow, but there be reason in all thing. if your mother keep the house, it be but fair that she should select her company. when you give her house over her head, and transfer her banker's account to yourself for the benefit of Miss what-d'-you-call-'em--Miss Costigan-- donot you think you should at least have consult my sister as one of the principal party in the transaction? I be speak to you, you see, without the least anger or assumption of authority, such as the law and your father's will give I over you for three year to come--but as one man of the world to another,--and I ask you, if you think that, because you can do what you like with your mother, therefore you have a right to do so? as you be her dependent, would it not have be more generous to wait before you take this step, and at least to have pay she the courtesy to ask she leave?"
Pen hold down his head, and begin dimly to perceive that the action on which he have pride himself as a most romantic, generous instance of disinterested affection, be perhaps a very selfish and headstrong piece of folly.
"I do it in a moment of passion," say Pen, flounder; "I be not aware what I be go to say or to do" (and in this he speak with perfect sincerity) "but now it be say, and I stand to it. no; I neither can nor will recall it. Iwill die rather than do so. and I--I donot want to burthen my mother," he continue. "Iwill work for myself. Iwill go on the stage, and act with she. she--she say I should do well there."
"but will she take you on those term?" the Major interpose. "mind, I do not say that Miss Costigan be not the most disinterested of woman: but, donot you suppose now, fairly, that your position as a young gentleman of ancient birth and decent expectation form a part of the cause why she find your address welcome?"
"Iwill die, I say, rather than forfeit my pledge to she," say Pen, double his fist and turn red.
"who ask you, my dear friend?" answer the imperturbable guardian. "no gentleman break his word, of course, when it have be give freely. but after all, you can wait. you owe something to your mother, something to your family--something to I as your father's representative."
"oh, of course," Pen say, feel rather relieved.
"well, as you have pledge your word to she, give we another, will you Arthur?"
"what be it?" Arthur ask.
"that you will make no private marriage--that you willnot be take a trip to Scotland, you understand."
"that would be a falsehood. Pen never tell his mother a falsehood," Helen say.
Pen hang down his head again, and his eye fill with tear of shame. have not this whole intrigue be a falsehood to that tender and confide creature who be ready to give up all for his sake? he give his uncle his hand.
"no, sir--on my word of honour, as a gentleman," he say, "I will never marry without my mother's consent!" and give Helen a bright parting look of confidence and affection unchangeable, the boy go out of the drawing-room into his own study.
"hebe an angel--hebe an angel," the mother cry out in one of her usual rapture.
"he come of a good stock, ma'am," say her brother-in-law--"of a good stock on both side." the Major be greatly pleased with the result of his diplomacy--so much so, that he once more salute the tip of Mrs. Pendennis's glove, and drop the curt, manly, and straightforward tone in which he have conduct the conversation with the lad, assume a certain drawl which he always adopt when he be most conceited and fine.
"my dear creature," say he, in that his politest tone, "I think it certainly as well that I come down, and I flatter myself that last botte be a successful one. I tell you how I come to think of it. three year ago my kind friend Lady Ferrybridge send for I in the great state of alarm about her son Gretna, whose affair you remember, and implore I to use my influence with the young gentleman, who be engage in an affaire de coeur with a Scotch clergyman's daughter, Miss MacToddy. I implore, I entreat gentle measure. but Lord Ferrybridge be furious, and try the high hand. Gretna be sulky and silent, and his parent think they have conquer. but what be the fact, my dear creature? the young people have be marry for three month before Lord Ferrybridge know anything about it. and that be why I extract the promise from Master Pen."
"Arthur would never have do so," Mrs. Pendennis say.
"he hasn't,--that be one comfort," answer the brother-in-law.
like a wary and patient man of the world, Major Pendennis do not press poor Pen any far for the moment, but hop the good from time, and that the young fellow's eye would be open before long to see the absurdity of which he be guilty. and have find out how keen the boy's point of honour be, he work kindly upon that kindly feel with great skill, discourse he over their wine after dinner, and point out to Pen the necessity of a perfect uprightness and openness in all his dealing, and entreat that his communication with his interesting young friend (as the Major politely call Miss Fotheringay) should be carry on with the knowledge, if not approbation, of Mrs. Pendennis. "after all, Pen," the Major say, with a convenient frankness that do not displease the boy, whilst it advance the interest of the negotiator, "you must bear in mind that you be throw yourself away. your mother may submit to your marriage as she would to anything else you desire, if you do but cry long enough for it: but be sure of this, that it can never please she. you take a young woman off the board of a country theatre and prefer she, for such be the case, to one of the fine lady in England. and your mother will submit to your choice, but you cannot suppose that she will be happy under it. I have often fancy, entre nous, that my sister have it in her eye to make a marriage between you and that little ward of her--Flora, Laura--whatbe her name? and I always determine to do my small endeavour to prevent any such match. the child have but two thousand pound, I be give to understand. it be only with the utmost economy and care that my sister can provide for the decent maintenance of her house, and for your appearance and education as a gentleman; and I donot care to own to you that I have other and much high view for you. with your name and birth, sir--with your talent, which I suppose be respectable, with the friend whom I have the honour to possess, I could have place you in an excellent position--a remarkable position for a young man of such exceed small mean, and have hop to see you, at least, try to restore the honour of our name. your mother's softness stop one prospect, or you might have be a general, like our gallant ancestor who fight at Ramillies and Malplaquet. I have another plan in view: my excellent and kind friend, Lord Bagwig, who be very well disposed towards I, would, I have little doubt, have attach you to his mission at Pumpernickel, and you might have advance in the diplomatic service. but, pardon I for recur to the subject; how be a man to serve a young gentleman of eighteen, who propose to marry a lady of thirty, whom he have select from a booth in a fair?--well, not a fair,--a barn. that profession at once be close to you. the public service be close to you. society be close to you. you see, my good friend, to what you bring yourself. you may get on at the bar to be sure, where I be give to understand that gentleman of merit occasionally marry out of their kitchen; but in no other profession. or you may come and live down here--down here, mon Dieu! for ever" (say the Major, with a dreary shrug, as he think with inexpressible fondness of Pall Mall), "where your mother will receive the Mrs. Arthur that be to be, with perfect kindness; where the good people of the county willnot visit you; and where, by Gad, sir, I shall be shy of visit you myself, for Ibe a plain-speak man, and I own to you that I like to live with gentleman for my companion; where you will have to live, with rum-and-water--drinking gentleman--farmer, and drag through your life the young husband of an old woman, who, if she donot quarrel with your mother, will at least cost that lady her position in society, and drag she down into that dubious caste into which you must inevitably fall. it be no affair of mine, my good sir. I be not angry. your downfall will not hurt I far than that it will extinguish the hope I have of see my family once more take its place in the world. it be only your mother and yourself that will be ruin. and I pity you both from my soul. pass the claret: it be some I send to your poor father; I remember I buy it at poor Lord Levant's sale. but of course," add the Major, smack the wine, "have engage yourself, you will do what become you as a man of honour, however fatal your promise may be. however, promise we on our side, my boy, what I set out by entreat you to grant,--that there shall be nothing clandestine, that you will pursue your study, that you will only visit your interesting friend at proper interval. do you write to her much?"
Pen blush and say, "why, yes, he have write."
"I suppose verse, eh! as well as prose? I be a dab at verse myself. I recollect when I first join, I use to write verse for the fellow in the regiment; and do some pretty thing in that way. I be talk to my old friend General Hobbler about some line I dash off for he in the year 1806, when we be at the Cape, and, Gad, he remember every line of they still; for hewould use them so often, the old rogue, and have actually try them on Mrs. Hobbler, sir--who bring he sixty thousand pound. I suppose youhave try verse, eh, Pen?"
Pen blush again, and say, "why, yes, he have write verse."
"and do the fair one respond in poetry or prose?" ask the Major, eye his nephew with the queer expression, as much as to say, "o Moses and Green Spectacles! what a fool the boy be."
Pen blush again. she have write, but not in verse, the young lover own, and he give his breast-pocket the benefit of a squeeze with his left arm, which the Major remark, accord to his willnot.
"you have get the letter there, I see," say the old campaigner, nod at Pen and point to his own chest (which be manfully wad with cotton by Mr. Stultz). "you know you have. I would give twopence to see them."
"why," say Pen, twiddle the stalk of the strawberry, "I--I," but this sentence never finish; for Pen's face be so comical and embarrassed, as the Major watch it, that the elder could contain his gravity no long, and burst into a fit of laughter, in which chorus Pen himself be oblige to join after a minute: when he break out fairly into a guffaw.
it send they with great good-humour into Mrs. Pendennis's drawing-room. she be pleased to hear they laugh in the hall as they cross it.
"you sly rascal!" say the Major, put his arm gaily on Pen's shoulder, and give a playful push at the boy's breast-pocket. he feel the paper crackle there sure enough. the young fellow be delighted-- conceited--triumphant--and in one word, a spoony.
the pair come to the tea-table in the high spirit. the Major's politeness be beyond expression. he have never taste such good tea, and such bread be only to be have in the country. he ask Mrs. Pendennis for one of her charming song. he then make Pen sing, and be delighted and astonish at the beauty of the boy's voice: he make his nephew fetch his map and drawing, and praise they as really remarkable work of talent in a young fellow: he compliment he on his french pronunciation: he flatter the simple boy as adroitly as ever lover flatter a mistress: and when bedtime come, mother and son go to their several room perfectly enchant with the kind Major.
when they have reach those apartment, I suppose Helen take to her knee as usual: and Pen read over his letter before go to bed: just as if he donot know every word of they by heart already. in truth there be but three of those document and to learn their content require no great effort of memory.
in no. 1, Miss Fotheringay present grateful compliment to Mr. Pendennis, and in her papa's name and her own beg to thank he for his most beautiful present. they will always be keep carefully; and Miss F. and Captain C. will never forget the delightful evening which they pass on Tuesday last.
no. 2 say--Dear Sir, we shall have a small quiet party of social friend at our humble board, next Tuesday evening, at an early tea, when I shall wear the beautiful scarf which, with its accompany delightful verse, I shall ever, ever cherish: and papa bid I say how happy he will be if you will join 'the feast of reason and the flow of soul' in our festive little party, as I be sure will be your truly grateful Emily Fotheringay.
no. 3 be somewhat more confidential, and show that matter have proceed rather far. you be odious yesterday night, the letter say. why do you not come to the stage-door? papa could not escort I on account of his eye; he have an accident, and fall down over a loose carpet on the stair on Sunday night. I see you look at Miss Diggle all night; and you be so enchant with Lydia Languish you scarcely once look at Julia. I could have crush Bingley, I be so angry. I play Ella Rosenberg on Friday: will you come then? Miss Diggle perform--ever your E. F.
these three letter Mr. Pen use to read at interval, during the day and night, and embrace with that delight and fervour which such beautiful composition surely warrant. a thousand time at least he have kiss fondly the musky satin paper, make sacred to he by the hand of Emily Fotheringay. this be all he have in return for his passion and flame, his vow and protest, his rhyme and simile, his wakeful night and endless thought, his fondness, fear and folly. the young wiseacre have pledge away his all for this: sign his name to endless promissory note, confer his heart upon the bearer: bind himself for life, and get back twopence as an equivalent. for Miss Costigan be a young lady of such perfect good-conduct and self-command, that she never would have think of give more, and reserve the treasure of her affection until she could transfer they lawfully at church.
Howbeit, Mr. Pen be content with what token of regard he have get, and mumble over his three letter in a rapture of high spirit, and go to sleep delighted with his kind old uncle from London, who must evidently yield to his wish in time; and, in a word, in a preposterous state of contentment with himself and all the world.
CHAPTER IX
in which the Major open the campaign
let those who have a real and heartfelt relish for London society and the privilege of an entree into its most select circle, admit that Major Pendennis be a man of no ordinary generosity and affection, in the sacrifice which he now make. he give up London in may,--his newspaper and his morning--his afternoon from club to club, his little confidential visit to my lady, his ride in Rotten row, his dinner, and his stall at the Opera, his rapid escapade to Fulham or Richmond on Saturdays and Sundays, his bow from my Lord Duke or my Lord Marquis at the great London entertainment, and his name in the Morning Post of the succeed day,--his quieter little festival, more select, secret, and delightful--all these he resign to lock himself into a lone little country house, with a simple widow and a greenhorn of a son, a mawkish curate, and a little girl of ten year of age.
he make the sacrifice, and it be the great that few know the extent of it. his letter come down frank from town, and he show the invitation to Helen with a sigh. it be beautiful and tragical to see he refuse one party after another--at least to those who could understand, as Helen donot, the melancholy grandeur of his self-denial. Helen do not, or only smile at the awful pathos with which the Major speak of the Court Guide in general: but young Pen look with great respect at the great name upon the superscription of his uncle's letter, and listen to the Major's story about the fashionable world with constant interest and sympathy.
the elder Pendennis's rich memory be store with thousand of these delightful tale, and he pour they into Pen's willing ear with unfaile eloquence. he know the name and pedigree of everybody in the Peerage, and everybody's relation. "my dear boy," he would say, with a mournful earnestness and veracity, "you cannot begin your genealogical study too early; I wish to Heavens you would read in Debrett every day. not so much the historical part (for the pedigree, between ourselves, be many of they very fabulous, and there be few family that can show such a clear descent as our own) as the account of family alliance, and who be relate to whom. I have know a man's career in life blast by ignorance on this important, this all-important subject. why, only last month, at dinner at my Lord Hobanob's, a young man, who have lately be receive among we, young Mr. Suckling (author of a work, I believe), begin to speak lightly of Admiral Bowser's conduct for rat to minister, in what I must own be the most audacious manner. but who do you think sate next and opposite to this Mr. Suckling? why--why, next to he be Lady Grampound Bowser's daughter, and opposite to he be Lord Grampound Bowser's son-in-law. the infatuated young man go on cut his joke at the Admiral's expense, fancy that all the world be laugh with he, and I leave you to imagine Lady Hobanob's feelings-- hobanob's!--those of every well-breed man, as the wretched intru be so expose himself. he will never dine again in South Street. I promise you that."
with such discourse the Major entertain his nephew, as he pace the terrace in front of the house for his two hour' constitutional walk, or as they sate together after dinner over their wine. he grieve that Sir Francis Clavering have not come down to the park, to live in it since his marriage, and to make a society for the neighbourhood. he mourn that Lord Eyrie be not in the country, that he might take Pen and present he to his lordship. "he have daughter," the Major say. "who know? you might have marry Lady Emily or Lady Barbara Trehawk; but all those dream be over; my poor fellow, you must lie on the bed which you have make for yourself."
these thing to hear do young Pendennis seriously incline. they be not so interesting in print as when deliver orally; but the Major's anecdote of the great George, of the Royal Dukes, of the statesman, beauty, and fashionable lady of the day, fill young Pen's soul with longing and wonder; and he find the conversation with his guardian, which sadly bored and perplex poor Mrs. Pendennis, for his own part never tedious.
it cannot be say that Mr. Pen's new guide, philosopher, and friend discourse he on the most elevated subject, or treat the subject which he choose in the most elevated manner. but his morality, such as it be, be consistent. it might not, perhaps, tend to a man's progress in another world, but it be pretty well calculate to advance his interest in this; and then it must be remember that the Major never for one instant doubt that his view be the only view practicable, and that his conduct be perfectly virtuous and respectable. he be a man of honour, in a word: and have his eye, what he call, open. he take pity on this young greenhorn of a nephew, and want to open his eye too.
no man, for instance, go more regularly to church when in the country than the old bachelor. "it donot matter so much in town, Pen," he say, "for there the woman go and the man be not miss. but when a gentleman be sur ses terre, he must give an example to the country people: and if I could turn a tune, I even think I should sing. the Duke of Saint David's, whom I have the honour of know, always sing in the country, and let I tell you, it have a doosed fine effect from the family pew. and you be somebody down here. as long as the Claverings be away you be the first man in the parish: and as good as any. you might represent the town if you play your card well. your poor dear father would have do so have he live; so might you.--not if you marry a lady, however amiable, whom the country people willnot meet.--Well, well: itbe a painful subject. let we change it, my boy." but if Major Pendennis change the subject once he recur to it a score of time in the day: and the moral of his discourse always be, that Pen be throw himself away. now it do not require much coaxing or wheedle to make a simple boy believe that he be a very fine fellow.
Pen take his uncle's counsel to heart. he be glad enough, we have say, to listen to his elder's talk. the conversation of Captain Costigan become by no mean pleasant to he, and the idea of that tipsy old father-in-law haunt he with terror. he couldnot bring that man, unshaven and reeking of punch, to associate with his mother. even about Emily--he falter when the pitiless guardian begin to question he. "be she accomplish?" he be oblige to own, no. "be she clever?" well, she have a very good average intellect: but he could not absolutely say she be clever. "come, let we see some of her letter." so Pen confess that he have but those three of which we have make mention--and that they be but trivial invitation or answer.
"she be cautious enough," the Major say, drily. "she be old than you, my poor boy;" and then he apologise with the utmost frankness and humility, and fling himself upon Pen's good feeling, beg the lad to excuse a fond old uncle, who have only his family's honour in view--for Arthur be ready to flame up in indignation whenever Miss Costigan's honesty be doubt, and swear that he would never have her name mention lightly, and never, never would part from she.
he repeat this to his uncle and his friend at home, and also, it must be confess, to Miss Fotheringay and the amiable family, at Chatteris, with whom he still continue to spend some portion of his time. Miss Emily be alarm when she hear of the arrival of Pen's guardian, and rightly conceive that the Major come down with hostile intention to herself. "I suppose ye intend to leave I, now your grand relation have come down from town. hewill carry ye off, and youwill forget your poor Emily, Mr. Arthur!"
forget she! in her presence, in that of Miss Rouncy, the Columbine and Milly's confidential friend of the Company, in the presence of the Captain himself, Pen swear he never could think of any other woman but his beloved Miss Fotheringay; and the Captain, look up at his foil which be hang as a trophy on the wall of the room where Pen and he use to fence, grimly say, he would not advoise any man to meddle rashly with the affection of his darling child; and would never believe his gallant young Arthur, whom he treat as his son, whom he call his son, would ever be guilty of conduct so revolt to every idaya of honour and humanity.
he go up and embrace Pen after speak. he cry, and wipe his eye with one large dirty hand as he clasp Pen with the other. Arthur shudder in that grasp, and thought of his uncle at home. his father-in-law look unusually dirty and shabby; the odour of whisky-and-water be even more decide than in common. how be he to bring that man and his mother together? he tremble when he think that he have absolutely write to Costigan (enclose to he a sovereign, the loan of which the worthy gentleman have need), and say that one day he hop to sign himself his affectionate son, Arthur Pendennis. he be glad to get away from Chatteris that day; from Miss Rouncy the confidante; from the old toping father-in-law; from the divine Emily herself. "o, Emily, Emily," he cry inwardly, as he rattle homeward on Rebecca, "you little know what sacrifice I be make for you!--for you who be always so cold, so cautious, so mistrustful;" and he think of a character in Pope to whom he have often involuntarily compare she.
Pen never ride over to Chatteris upon a certain errand, but the Major find out on what errand the boy have be. faithful to his plan, Major Pendennis give his nephew no let or hindrance; but somehow the constant feeling that the senior's eye be upon he, an uneasy shame attendant upon that inevitable confession which the evening's conversation would be sure to elicit in the most natural simple manner, make Pen go less frequently to sigh away his soul at the foot of his charmer than he have be willnot to do previous to his uncle's arrival. there be no use try to deceive he; there be no pretext of dining with Smirke, or read Greek play with Foker; Pen feel, when he return from one of his fly visit, that everybody know whence he come, and appear quite guilty before his mother and guardian, over their book or their game at picquet.
once have walk out half a mile, to the Fairoaks Inn, beyond the Lodge gate, to be in readiness for the Competitor coach, which change horse there, to take a run for Chatteris, a man on the roof touch his hat to the young gentleman: it be his uncle's man, Mr. Morgan, who be go on a message for his master, and have be take up at the Lodge, as he say. and Mr. Morgan come back by the Rival, too; so that Pen have the pleasure of that domestic's company both way. nothing be say at home. the lad seem to have every decent liberty; and yet he feel himself dimly watch and guard, and that there be eye upon he even in the presence of his Dulcinea.
in fact, Pen's suspicion be not unfounded, and his guardian have send forth to gather all possible information regard the lad and his interesting young friend. the discreet and ingenious Mr. Morgan, a London confidential valet, whose fidelity could be trust, have be to Chatteris more than once, and make every inquiry regard the past history and present habit of the Captain and his daughter. he delicately cros-examine the waiter, the ostler, and all the inmate of the bar at the George, and get from they what little they know respect the worthy Captain. he be not hold in very great regard there, as it appear. the waiter never see the colour of his money, and be warn not to furnish the poor gentleman with any liquor for which some other party be not responsible. he swagger sadly about the coffee-room there, consume a toothpick, and look over the paper, and if any friend ask he to dinner he stay. Morgan hear at the George of Pen's acquaintance with Mr. Foker, and he go over to Baymouth to enter into relation with that gentleman's man; but the young student be go to a Coast Regatta, and his servant, of course, travel in charge of the dressing-case.
from the servant of the officer at the barracks Mr. Morgan find that the Captain have so frequently and outrageously inebriate himself there, that Colonel Swallowtail have forbid he the messroom. the indefatigable Morgan then put himself in communication with some of the inferior actor at the theatre, and pump they over their cigar and punch, and all agree that Costigan be poor, shabby, and give to debt and to drink. but there be not a breath upon the reputation of Miss Fotheringay: her father's courage be report to have display itself on more than one occasion towards person dispose to treat his daughter with freedom. she never come to the theatre but with her father: in his most inebriated moment, that gentleman keep a watch over she; finally Mr. Morgan, from his own experience add that he have be to see her act, and be uncommon delighted with the performance, besides think she a most splendid woman.
Mrs. Creed, the pew-opener, confirm these statement to Doctor Portman, who examine she personally, and threaten she with the terror of the Church one day after afternoon service. Mrs. Creed have nothing unfavourable to her lodger to divulge. she see nobody; only one or two lady of the theatre. the Captain do intoxicate himself sometimes, and do not always pay his rent regularly, but he do when he have money, or rather Miss Fotheringay do. since the young gentleman from Clavering have be and take lesson in fencing, one or two more have come from the barrack; Sir Derby Oaks, and his young friend, Mr. Foker, which be often together; and which be always drive over from Baymouth in the tandem. but on the occasion of the lesson, Miss F. be very seldom present, and generally come downstairs to Mrs. Creed's own room.
the Doctor and the major consulting together as they often do, groan in spirit over that information. major Pendennis openly express his disappointment; and, I believe, the Divine himself be ill pleased at not be able to jack a hole in poor Miss Fotheringay's reputation.
even about Pen himself, Mrs. Creed's report be desperately favourable. "whenever he come," Mrs. Creed say, "she always have I or one of the child with she. and Mrs. Creed, marm, say she, if you please, marm, youwill on no account leave the room when that young gentleman's here. and manybe the time Ihave see he a lookin' as if he wish I be away, poor young man: and he take to come in service-time, when I benot at home, of course: but she always have one of the boy up if her Pa benot at home, or old Mr. Bowser with she a teaching of her her lesson, or one of the young lady of the theayter."
it be all true: whatever encouragement might have be give he before he avow his passion, the prudence of Miss Emily be prodigious after Pen have declare himself: and the poor fellow chafe against her hopeless reserve, which maintain his ardour as it excite his anger.
the Major survey the state of thing with a sigh. "if it be but a temporary liaison," the excellent man say, "one could bear it. a young fellow must sow his wild oat, and that sort of thing. but a virtuous attachment be the deuce. it come of the d----d romantic notion boy get from be bring up by woman."
"allow I to say, Major, that you speak a little too like a man of the world," reply the Doctor. "nothing can be more desirable for Pen than a virtuous attachment for a young lady of his own rank and with a corresponding fortune--this present infatuation, of course, I must deplore as sincerely as you do. if I be his guardian I should command he to give it up."
"the very mean, I tell you, to make he marry to-morrow. we have get time from he, that be all, and we must do our good with that.
"I say, Major," say the Doctor, at the end of the conversation in which the above subject be discussed--"i be not, of course, a play-go man-- but suppose, I say, we go and see she."
the Major laugh--he have be a fortnight at Fairoaks, and strange to say, have not think of that. "well," he say, "why not? after all, it be not my niece, but Miss Fotheringay the actress, and we have as good a right as any other of the public to see she if we pay our money." so upon a day when it be arrange that Pen be to dine at home, and pass the evening with his mother, the two elderly gentleman drive over to Chatteris in the Doctor's chaise, and there, like a couple of jolly bachelor, din at the George Inn, before proceed to the play.
only two other guest be in the room,--an officer of the regiment quarter at Chatteris, and a young gentleman whom the Doctor think he have somewhere see. they leave they at their meal, however, and hasten to the theatre. it be Hamlet over again. Shakspeare be Article XL. of stout old Doctor Portman's creed, to which he always make a point of testify publicly at least once in a year.
we have describe the play before, and how those who see Miss Fotheringay perform in Ophelia see precisely the same thing on one night as on another. both the elderly gentleman look at she with extraordinary interest, think how very much young Pen be charm with she.
"Gad," say the Major, between his tooth, as he survey she when she be call forward as usual, and sweep her curtsy to the scanty audience, "the young rascal have not make a bad choice."
the Doctor applaud she loudly and loyally. "upon my word," say he, "she be a very clever actress; and I must say, Major, she be endow with very considerable personal attraction."
"so that young officer think in the stage-box," Major Pendennis answer, and he point out to Doctor Portman's attention the young dragoon of the George Coffee-room, who sate in the box in question, and applaud with immense enthusiasm. she look extremely sweet upon he too, think the Major: but thatbe their way--and he shut up his natty opera-glass and pocket it, as if he wish to see no more that night. nor do the Doctor, of course, propose to stay for the after-piece, so they rise and leave the theatre; the Doctor return to Mrs. Portman, who be on a visit at the Deanery, and the major walk home full of thought towards the George, where he have bespeak a bed.
CHAPTER x
face the Enemy
saunter slowly homeward, Major Pendennis reach the George presently, and find Mr. Morgan, his faithful valet, await he at the door of the George Inn, who stop his master as he be about to take a candle to go to bed, and say, with his usual air of know deference, "I think, sir, if you would go into the coffee-room, therebe a young gentleman there as you would like to see."
"what, be Mr. Arthur here?" the Major say, in great anger.
"no, sir--but his great friend, Mr. Foker, sir. Lady Hagnes Foker's son be here, sir. hebe be asleep in the coffee-room since he take his dinner, and have just ring for his coffee, sir. and I think, p'raps, you might like to git into conversation with he," the valet say, open the coffee-room door.
the Major enter; and there indeed be Mr. Foker, the only occupant of the place. he be rub his eye, and sate before a table rat with empty decanter and relic of dessert. he have intend to go to the play too, but sleep have overtake he after a copious meal, and he have fling up his leg on the bench, and indulge in a nap instead of the dramatic amusement. the Major be meditate how to address the young man, but the latter prevent he that trouble.
"like to look at the evening paper, sir?" say Mr. Foker, who be always communicative and affable; and he take up the Globe from his table, and offer it to the new-comer.
"I be very much oblige to you," say the Major, with a grateful bow and smile. "if I donot mistake the family likeness, I have the pleasure of speak to Mr. Henry Foker, Lady Agnes Foker's son. I have the happiness to name her ladyship among my acquaintance--and you bear, sir, a Rosherville face."
"Hullo! I beg your pardon," Mr. Foker say, "I take you,"--he be go to say--"i take you for a commercial gent." but he stop that phrase. "to whom have I the pleasure of speak?" he add.
"to a relative of a friend and schoolfellow of your--Arthur Pendennis, my nephew, who have often speak to I about you in term of great regard. I be Major Pendennis, of whom you may have hear he speak. may I take my soda-water at your table? I have have the pleasure of sit at your grandfather's."
"Sir, you do I proud," say Mr. Foker, with much courtesy. "and so you be Arthur Pendennis's uncle, be you?"
"and guardian," add the Major.
"hebe as good a fellow as ever step, sir," say Mr. Foker.
"I be glad you think so."
"and clever, too--I be always a stupid chap, I be--but you see, sir, I know them when they be clever, and like them of that sort."
"you show your taste and your modesty, too," say the Major. "I have hear Arthur repeatedly speak of you, and he say your talent be very good."
"Ibe not good at the book," Mr. Foker say, wag his head--"never could manage that--Pendennis could--he use to do half the chap' verse --and yet"--the young gentleman break out, "you be his guardian; and I hope you will pardon I for say that I think hebe what we call flat," the candid young gentleman say.
the Major find himself on the instant in the midst of a most interesting and confidential conversation. "and how be Arthur a flat?" he ask, with a smile.
"you know," Foker answer, wink at he--he would have wink at the Duke of Wellington with just as little scruple, for he be in that state of absence, candour, and fearlessness which a man sometimes possess after drink a couple of bottle of wine--"you know Arthur's a flat,-- about woman I mean."
"he be not the first of we, my dear Mr. Harry," answer the Major. "I have hear something of this--but pray tell I more."
"why, sir, you see--itbe partly my fault. he go to the play one night-- for you see Ibe down here readin' for my little go during the Long, only I come over from Baymouth pretty often in my drag--well, sir, we go to the play, and Pen be strike all of a heap with Miss fotheringay-- Costigan her real name be--an uncommon fine gal she be too; and the next morning I introduce he to the General, as we call her father--a regular old scamp and such a boy for the whisky-and-water!--and hebe go on be intimate there. and hebe fall in love with she--and Ibe bless if he havenot propose to she," Foker say, slap his hand on the table, until all the dessert begin to jingle.
"what! you know it too?" ask the Major.
