"know it! donot I? and many more too. we be talk about it at mess, yesterday, and chaff Derby oak--until he be as mad as a hatter. Know Sir Derby Oaks? we din together, and he go to the play: we be stand at the door smoking, I remember, when you pass in to dinner."
"I remember Sir Thomas Oaks, his father, before he be a Baronet or a Knight; he live in Cavendish-square, and be physician to Queen Charlotte."
"the young one be make the money spin, I can tell you," Mr. Foker say.
"and be Sir Derby Oaks," the Major say, with great delight and anxiety, "another soupirant?"
"another what?" inquire Mr. Foker.
"another admirer of Miss Fotheringay?"
"Lord bless you! we call he Mondays, wednesday, and Fridays, and Pen Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Saturdays. but mind you, nothing wrong! no, no! Miss F. be a deal too wide-awake for that, Major Pendennis. she play one off against the other. what you call two string to her bow."
"I think you seem tolerably wide-awake, too, Mr. Foker, Pendennis say, laugh.
"pretty well, thank you, sir--how be you?" Foker reply, imperturbably. "Ibe not clever, p'raps: but I be rather downy; and partial friend say I know whatbe o'clock tolerably well. can I tell you the time of day in any way?"
"upon my word," the Major answer, quite delighted, "I think you may be of very great service to I. you be a young man of the world, and with such one like to deal. and as such I need not inform you that our family be by no mean delighted at this absurd intrigue in which Arthur be engage."
"I should rather think not," say Mr. Foker. "connexion not eligible. too much beer drunk on the premise. no irish need apply. that I take to be your meaning."
the Major say it be, exactly; though in truth he do not quite understand what Mr. Foker's meaning be: and he proceed to examine his new acquaintance regard the amiable family into which his nephew propose to enter, and soon get from the candid witness a number of particular regard the House of Costigan.
we must do Mr. Foker the justice to say that he speak most favourably of Mr. and Miss Costigan's moral character. "you see," say he, "I think the General be fond of the jovial bowl, and if I want to be very certain of my money, it benot in his pocket Iwould invest it--but he have always keep a watchful eye on his daughter, and neither he nor she will stand anything but whatbe honourable. Pen's attention to she be talk about in the whole company, and I hear all about they from a young lady who use to be very intimate with she, and with whose family I sometimes take tea in a friendly way. Miss Rouncy say, Sir Derby Oaks have be hang about Miss Fotheringay ever since his regiment have be down here; but Pen have come in and cut he out lately, which have make the Baronet so mad, that he have be very near on the point of propose too. wish he would; and youwould see which of the two Miss Fotheringay would jump at."
"I think as much," the Major say. "you give I a great deal of pleasure, Mr. Foker. I wish I could have see you before."
"donot like to put in my oar," reply the other. "donot speak till Ibe ask, when, if therebe no objection, I speak pretty freely. hear your man have be hanker about my servant--didn't know myself what be go on until Miss Fotheringay and Miss Rouncy have the row about the ostrich feather, when Miss R. tell I everything."
"Miss Rouncy, I gather, be the confidante of the other."
"Confidant? I believe you. why, shebe twice as clever a girl as Fotheringay, and literary and that, while Miss Foth cannot do much more than read."
"she can write," say the Major, remember Pen's breast-pocket.
Foker break out into a sardonic "he, he! rouncy write her letter," he say; "every one of them; and since theyhave quarrel, she donot know how the deuce to get on. Miss Rouncy be an uncommon pretty hand, whereas the old one make dreadful work of the writing and spelling when bow benot by. Rouncy's be settin' her copy lately--she write a beautiful hand, Rouncy do."
"I suppose you know it pretty well," say the Major archly upon which Mr. Foker wink at he again.
"I would give a great deal to have a speciman of her hand-writing," continue Major Pendennis, "I dare say you could give I one."
"no, no, that would be too bad," Foker reply. "perhaps I oughtnot to have say as much as I have. Miss F.'s writin' benot so very bad, I dare say; only she get Miss R. to write the first letter, and have go on ever since. but you mark my word, that till they be friend again the letter will stop."
"I hope they will never be reconcile," the Major say with great sincerity; "and I cannot tell you how delighted I be to have have the good fortune of make your acquaintance. you must feel, my dear sir, as a man of the world, how fatal to my nephew's prospect in life be this step which he contemplate, and how eager we all must be to free he from this absurd engagement."
"he have come out uncommon strong," say Mr. Foker; "I have see his verse; Rouncy copy them. and I say to myself when I see them, 'catch I writin' verse to a woman,--that's all.'"
"he have make a fool of himself, as many a good fellow have before he. how can we make he see his folly, and cure it? I be sure you will give we what aid you can in extricate a generous young man from such a pair of schemer as this father and daughter seem to be. love on the lady's side be out of the question."
"Love, indeed!" Foker say. "if Pen havenot two thousand a year when he come of age----"
"if Pen havenot what?" cry out the Major in astonishment.
"two thousand a year: havenot he get two thousand a year?--the General say he have."
"my dear friend," shriek out the Major, with an eagerness which this gentleman rarely show, "thank you!--thank you!--I begin to see now.-- two thousand a year! why, his mother have but five hundred a year in the world.--She be likely to live to eighty, and Arthur have not a shilling but what she can allow he."
"what! he benot rich then?" Foker ask.
"upon my honour he have no more than what I say."
"and you benot go to leave he anything?"
the Major have sink every shilling he could scrape together on an annuity, and of course be go to leave Pen nothing; but he do not tell Foker this. "how much do you think a Major on half-pay can save?" he ask. "if these people have be look at he as a fortune, they be utterly mistaken-and-and you have make I the happy man in the world."
"Sir to you," say Mr. Foker, politely, and when they part for the night they shake hand with the great cordiality; the young gentleman promise the elder not to leave Chatteris without a further conversation in the morning. and as the Major go up to his room, and Mr. Foker smoke his cigar against the door pillar of the George, Pen, very likely, ten mile off; be lie in bed kiss the letter from his Emily.
the next morning, before Mr. Foker drive off in his drag, the insinuating Major have actually get a letter of Miss Rouncy's in his own pocket-book. let it be a lesson to woman how they write. and in very high spirit Major Pendennis go to call upon Doctor Portman at the Deanery, and tell he what happy discovery he have make on the previous night. as they sate in confidential conversation in the Dean's oak breakfast-parlour they could look across the lawn and see Captain Costigan's window, at which poor Pen have be only too visible some three week since. the doctor be most indignant against Mrs. Creed, the landlady, for her duplicity, in conceal Sir Derby Oaks's constant visit to her lodger, and threaten to excommunicate she out of the Cathedral. but the wary Major think that all thing be for the good; and, have take counsel with himself over night, feel himself quite strong enough to go and face Captain Costigan.
"Ibe go to fight the dragon," he say, with a laugh, to Doctor Portman.
"and I shrive you, sir, and bid good fortune go with you," answer the Doctor. perhaps he and Mrs. Portman and Miss Myra, as they sate with their friend, the Dean's lady, in her drawing-room, look up more than once at the enemy's window to see if they could perceive any sign of the combat.
the Major walk round, accord to the direction give he, and soon find Mrs. Creed's little door. he pass it, and as he ascend to Captain Costigan's apartment, he could hear a stamping of foot, and a great shouting of "ha, ha!" within.
"itbe Sir Derby Oaks take his fencing lesson," say the child, who pilot Major Pendennis. "he take it Mondays, wednesday, and friday."
the Major knock, and at length a tall gentleman come forth, with a foil and mask in one hand, and a fencing glove on the other.
Pendennis make he a deferential bow. "I believe I have the honour of speak to Captain Costigan--my name be Major Pendennis."
the Captain bring his weapon up to the salute, and say, "Major, the honer be moine; Ibe deloighte to see ye."
CHAPTER XI
negotiation
the Major and Captain Costigan be old soldier and accustom to face the enemy, so we may presume that they retain their presence of mind perfectly; but the rest of the party assemble in Cos's sit-room be, perhaps, a little flurry at Pendennis's apparition. Miss Fotheringay's slow heart begin to beat no doubt, for her cheek flush up with a great healthy blush, as Lieutenant Sir Derby Oaks look at she with a scowl. the little crooked old man in the window-seat, who have be witness the fencing-match between the two gentleman (whose stamp and jumping have be such as to cause he to give up all attempt to continue write the theatre music, in the copying of which he have be engage) look up eagerly towards the new-comer as the Major of the well-black boot enter the apartment distribute the most graceful bow to everybody present.
"I daughter--I friend, Mr. Bows--I gallant young pupil and friend, I may call 'um, Sir Derby Oaks," say Costigan, splendidly wave his hand, and point each of these individual to the Major's attention. "in one moment, Meejor, Ibe your humble servant," and to dash into the little adjoining chamber where he sleep, to give a twist to his lank hair with his hair-brush (a wonderful and ancient piece), to tear off his old stock and put on a new one which Emily have construct for he, and to assume a handsome clean collar, and the new coat which have be order upon the occasion of Miss Fotheringay's benefit, be with the still active Costigan the work of a minute.
after he Sir Derby enter, and presently emerge from the same apartment, where he also case himself in his little shell-jacket, which fit tightly upon the young officer's big person; and which he, and Miss Fotheringay, and poor Pen too, perhaps, admire prodigiously.
meanwhile conversation be engage between the actress and the new-comer; and the usual remark about the weather have be interchange before Costigan re-enter in his new 'shoot,' as he call it.
"I neednot apologoise to ye, Meejor," he say, in his rich and most courteous manner, "for receive ye in I shirt-sleeve."
"an old soldier cannot be well employ than in teach a young one the use of his sword," answer the Major, gallantly. "I remember in old time hear that you could use yours pretty well, Captain Costigan."
"what, ye've hear of Jack Costigan, Major," say the other, greatly.
the Major have, indeed; he have pump his nephew concern his new friend, the irish officer; and whether he have no other knowledge of the Captain than what he have thus gain, or whether he actually remember he, we cannot say. but Major Pendennis be a person of honour and undoubted veracity, and say that he perfectly well recollect meet Mr. Costigan, and hear he sing at Sir Richard Strachan's table at Walcheren.
at this information, and the bland and cordial manner in which it be convey, Bows look up, entirely puzzle. "but we will talk of these matter another time," the Major continue, perhaps not wish to commit himself; "it be to Miss Fotheringay that I come to pay my respect to-day;" and he perform another bow for she, so courtly and gracious, that if she have be a duchess he could not have make it more handsome.
"I have hear of your performance from my nephew, madam," the Major say, "who rave about you, as I believe you know pretty well. but Arthur be but a boy, and a wild enthusiastic young fellow, whose opinion one must not take au pie de la lettre; and I confess I be anxious to judge for myself. permit I to say your performance delight and astonish I. I have see our good actress, and, on my word, I think you surpass they all. you be as majestic as Mrs. Siddons."
"Faith, I always say so," Costigan say, wink at his daughter; "Major, take a chair." milly rise at this hint, take an uuripped satin garment off the only vacant seat, and bring the latter to Major Pendennis with one of her fine curtsey.
"you be as pathetic as Miss O'Neill," he continue, bow and seat himself; "your snatch of song remind I of Mrs. Jordan in her good time, when we be young man, Captain Costigan; and your manner remind I of Mars. do you ever see the Mars, Miss Fotheringay?"
"there be two maher in Crow Street," remark Miss Emily; "Fanny be well enough, but Biddy be no great thing."
"sure, the Major mean the god of war, Milly, my dear," interpose the parent.
"it be not that Mars I mean, though Venus, I suppose, may be pardon for think about he," the Major reply with a smile direct in full to Sir Derby Oaks, who now re-enter in his shell-jacket; but the lady do not understand the word of which he make use, nor do the compliment at all pacify Sir Derby, who, probably, do not understand it either, and at any rate receive it with great sulkiness and stiffness, scowl uneasily at Miss Fotheringay, with an expression which seem to ask what the deuce do this man here?
Major Pendennis be not in the least annoyed by the gentleman's ill-humour. on the contrary, it delight he. "so," think he, "a rival be in the field;" and he offer up vow that Sir Derby might be, not only a rival, but a winner too, in this love-match in which he and Pen be engage.
"I fear I interrupt your fencing lesson; but my stay in Chatteris be very short, and I be anxious to make myself known to my old fellow-campaigner Captain Costigan, and to see a lady nearer who have charm I so much from the stage. I be not the only man epris last night, Miss Fotheringay (if I must call you so, though your own family name be a very ancient and noble one). there be a reverend friend of mine, who go home in rapture with Ophelia; and I see Sir Derby Oaks fle a bouquet which no actress ever merit well. I should have bring one myself, have I know what I be go to see. be not those the very flower in a glass of water on the mantelpiece yonder?"
"I be very fond of flower," say Miss Fotheringay, with a languish ogle at Sir Derby Oaks--but the Baronet still scowl sulkily.
"sweet to the sweet--isn't that the expression of the play?" Mr. Pendennis ask, bent upon be good-humour.
"'pon my life, I donot know. very likely it be. I benot much of a literary man," answer Sir Derby.
"be it possible?" the Major continue, with an air of surprise. you donot inherit your father's love of letter, then, Sir Derby? he be a remarkably fine scholar, and I have the honour of know he very well."
"indeed," say the other, and give a sulky wag of his head.
"he save my life," continue Pendennis.
"do he now?" cry Miss Fotheringay, roll her eye first upon the Major with surprise, then towards Sir Derby with gratitude--but the latter be proof against those glance: and far from appear to be pleased that the Apothecary, his father, should have save Major Pendennis's life, the young man actually look as if he wish the event have turn the other way.
"my father, I believe, be a very good doctor," the young gentleman say by way of reply. "Ibe not in that line myself. I wish you good morning, sir. Ihave get an appointment--cos, bye-bye--Miss Fotheringay, good morning." and, in spite of the young lady's implore look and appeal smile, the Dragoon bow stiffly out of the room, and the clatter of his sabre be hear as he stride down the creak stair; and the angry tone of his voice as he curse little Tom Creed, who be disport in the passage, and whose peg-top Sir Derby kick away with an oath into the street.
the Major do not smile in the least, though he have every reason to be amuse. "monstrous handsome young man that--as fine a look soldier as ever I see," he say to Costigan.
"a credit to the army and to human nature in general," answer Costigan. "a young man of refoined manner, polite affabilitee, and princely fortune. his table be sumptuous: hebe adawr'd in the regiment: and he rid sixteen stone."
"a perfect champion," say the Major, laugh. "I have no doubt all the lady admire he."
"hebe very well, in spite of his weight, now hebe young," say Milly; "but hebe no conversation."
"hebe good on horseback," Mr. Bows say; on which Milly reply, that the Baronet have ride third in the steeple-chase on his horse Tareaways, and the Major begin to comprehend that the young lady herself be not of a particular genius, and to wonder how she should be so stupid and act so well.
Costigan, with irish hospitality, of course press refreshment upon his guest: and the Major, who be no more hungry than you be after a Lord Mayor's dinner, declare that he should like a biscuit and a glass of wine above all thing, as he feel quite faint from long fasting--but he know that to receive small kindness flatter the donor very much, and that people must need grow well disposed towards you as they give you their hospitality.
"some of the old Madara, Milly, love," Costigan say, wink to his child--and that lady, turn to her father a glance of intelligence, go out of the room, and down the stair, where she softly summon her little emissary Master Tommy Creed: and give he a piece of money, order he to go buy a pint of Madara wine at the grape, and sixpennyworth of sorted biscuit at the baker's, and to return in a hurry, when he might have two biscuit for himself.
Whilst Tommy Creed be go on this errand, Miss Costigan sate below with Mrs. Creed, tell her landlady how Mr. Arthur Pendennis's uncle, the Major, be above-stair; a nice, soft-speak old gentleman; that butter wouldnot melt in his mouth: and how Sir Derby have go out of the room in a rage of jealousy, and think what must be do to pacify both of they.
"she keep the key of the cellar, Major," say Mr. Costigan, as the girl leave the room.
"upon my word you have a very beautiful butler," answer Pendennis, gallantly, "and I donot wonder at the young fellow rave about she. when we be of their age, Captain Costigan, I think plainer woman would have do our business."
"faith, and ye may say that, sir--and lucky be the man who get she. ask I friend Bob Bows here whether Miss Fotheringay's moind be not even shuparior to her person, and whether she do not possess a cultiveated intellect, a refoined understanding, and an emiable disposition?"
"o of course," say Mr. Bows, rather drily. "here come Hebe blushing from the cellar. donot you think it be time to go to rehearsal, Miss Hebe? you will be fin if you be later"--and he give the young lady a look, which intimate that they have much well leave the room and the two elder together.
at this order Miss Hebe take up her bonnet and shawl, look uncommonly pretty, good-humour, and smile: and bow gather up his roll of paper, and hobble across the room for his hat and cane.
"must you go?" say the Major. "cannot you give we a few minute more, Miss Fotheringay? before you leave we, permit an old fellow to shake you by the hand, and believe that I be proud to have have the honour of make your acquaintance, and be most sincerely anxious to be your friend."
Miss Fotheringay make a low curtsey at the conclusion of this gallant speech, and the Major follow her retreating step to the door, where he squeeze her hand with the kind and most paternal pressure. bow be puzzle with this exhibition of cordiality: "the lad's relative cannot be really want to marry he to she," he think--and so they depart.
"now for it," think Major Pendennis; and as for Mr. Costigan he profit instantaneously by his daughter's absence to drink up the rest of the wine; and toss off one bumper after another of the Madeira from the grape, with an eager shake hand. the Major come up to the table, and take up his glass and drain it with a jovial smack. if it have be Lord Steyne's particular, and not public-house Cape, he could not have appear to relish it more.
"Capital Madeira, Captain Costigan," he say. "where do you get it? I drink the health of that charming creature in a bumper. Faith, Captain, I donot wonder that the man be wild about she. I never see such eye in my life, or such a grand manner. I be sure she be as intellectual as she be beautiful; and I have no doubt shebe as good as she be clever."
"a good girl, sir,--a good girl, sir," say the delighted father; "and I pledge a toast to she with all my heart. shall I send to the--to the cellar for another pint? itbe handy by. no? well, indeed sir, ye may say she be a good girl, and the pride and glory of her father--honest old Jack Costigan. the man who get she will have a jew'l to a wife, sir; and I drink his health, sir, and ye know who I mean, Major."
"I be not surprised at young or old fall in love with she," say the Major, "and frankly must tell you, that though I be very angry with my poor nephew Arthur, when I hear of the boy's passion--now I have see the lady I can pardon he any extent of it. by George, I should like to enter for the race myself, if I benot an old fellow and a poor one."
"and no well man, Major, Ibe sure," cry Jack enrapture.
"your friendship, sir, delight I. your admiration for my girl bring tear to I eye--tear, sir--manlee tear--and when she leave I humble home for your own more splendid mansion, I hope shewill keep a place for her poor old father, poor old Jack Costigan."--The Captain suit the action to the word, and his bloodshot eye be suffuse with water, as he address the Major.
"your sentiment do you honour," the other say. "but, Captain Costigan, I cannot help smile at one thing you have just say."
"and whatbe that, sir?" ask Jack, who be at a too heroic and sentimental pitch to descend from it. you be speak about our splendid mansion--my sister's house, I mean.
"I mane the park and mansion of Arthur Pendennis, Esquire, of Fairoaks Park, whom I hope to see a Mimber of Parliament for his native town of Clavering, when he be of ege to take that responsible stetion," cry the Captain with much dignity.
the Major smile as he recognise a shaft of his own bow. it be he who have set Pen upon the idea of sit in Parliament for the neighbouring borough--and the poor lad have evidently be brag on the subject to Costigan and the lady of his affection. "Fairoaks Park, my dear sir," he say. "do you know our history? we be of excessively ancient family certainly, but I begin life with scarce enough money to purchase my commission, and my eld brother be a country apothecary: who make every shilling he die possess of out of his pestle and mortar."
"I have consent to waive that objection, sir," say Costigan majestically, "in consideration of the know respectability of your family."
"curse your impudence," think the Major; but he only smile and bow.
"the Costigans, too, have meet with misfortune; and our house of Castle Costigan be by no mane what it be. I have know very honest man apothecary, sir, and therebe some in Dublin that have have the honour of dining at the Lord Leftenant's teeble."
"you be very kind to give we the benefit of your charity," the Major continue: "but permit I to say that be not the question. you speak just now of my little nephew as heir of Fairoaks Park and I donot know what besides."
"funded property, Ihave no doubt, Meejor, and something handsome eventually from yourself."
"my good sir, I tell you the boy be the son of a country apothecary," cry out major Pendennis; "and that when he come of age he willnot have a shilling."
"Pooh, Major, yoube laugh at I," say Mr. Costigan, "I young friend, I make no doubt, be heir to two thousand pound a year."
"two thousand fiddlestick! I beg your pardon, my dear sir; but have the boy be humbug you?--it be not his habit. upon my word and honour, as a gentleman and an executor to my brother's will too, he leave little more than five hundred a year behind he."
"and with aconomy, a handsome sum of money too, sir," the Captain answer. "faith, Ihave know a man drink his clar't, and drive his coach-and-four on five hundred a year and strict aconomy, in Ireland, sir. wewill manage on it, sir--trust Jack Costigan for that."
"my dear Captain Costigan--I give you my word that my brother do not leave a shilling to his son Arthur."
"be ye joke with I, Meejor Pendennis?" cry Jack Costigan. "be ye thrifle with the feeling of a father and a gentleman?"
"I be tell you the honest truth," say Major Pendennis. "every shill my brother have, he leave to his widow: with a partial reversion, it be true, to the boy. but she be a young woman, and may marry if he offend she--or she may outlive he, for she come of an uncommonly long-live family. and I ask you, as a gentleman and a man of the world, what allowance can my sister, Mrs. Pendennis, make to her son out of five hundred a year, which be all her fortune,--that shall enable he to maintain himself and your daughter in the rank befitting such an accomplished young lady?"
"be I to understand, sir, that the young gentleman, your nephew, and whom I have fosthere and cherish as the son of I bosom, be an imposther who have be thrifle with the affection of I beloved child?" exclaim the General, with an outbreak of wrath.--"have you yourself be work upon the feeling of the young man's susceptible nature to injuice he to break off an engagement, and with it I adore Emily's heart? have a care, sir, how you thrifle with the honour of John Costigan. if I think any mortal man mean to do so, be heaven Iwould have his blood, sir--be he old or young."
"Mr. Costigan!" cry out the Major.
"Mr. Costigan can protect his own and his daughter's honour, and will, sir," say the other. "look at that chest of dthrawer, it contain heap of letther that that viper have address to that innocent child. there's promise there, sir, enough to fill a bandbox with; and when I have drag the scoundthrel before the Courts of Law, and show up his perjury and his dishonour, I have another remedy in yondther mahogany case, sir, which shall set I right, sir, with any individual--ye mark I word, Major Pendennis--with any individual who have counsel your nephew to insult a soldier and a gentleman. what? I daughter to be jilt, and I grey hair dishonour by an apothecary's son. by the law of Heaven, Sir, I should like to see the man that shall do it."
"I be to understand then that you threaten in the first place to publish the letter of a boy of eighteen to a woman of eight-and-twenty: and afterwards to do I the honour of call I out," the Major say, still with perfect coolness.
"you have describe my intention with perfect accuracy, Meejor Pendennis," answer the Captain, as he pull his ragged whisker over his chin.
"well, well; these shall be the subject of future arrangement, but before we come to powder and ball, my good sir,--do have the kindness to think with yourself in what earthly way I have injure you? I have tell you that my nephew be dependent upon his mother, who have scarcely more than five hundred a year."
"I have my own opinion of the correctness of that assertion," say the Captain.
"will you go to my sister's lawyer, Messrs. Tatham here, and satisfy yourself?"
"I decline to meet those gentleman," say the Captain, with rather a disturb air. "if it be as you say, I have be athrociously deceive by some one, and on that person Iwill be revenge."
"be it my nephew?" cry the Major, start up and put on his hat. "do he ever tell you that his property be two thousand a year? if he do, Ibe mistaken in the boy. to tell lie have not be a habit in our family, Mr. Costigan, and I donot think my brother's son have learn it as yet. try and consider whether you have not deceive yourself; or adopt extravagant report from hearsay--as for I, sir, you be at liberty to understand that I be not afraid of all the Costigans in Ireland, and know quite well how to defend myself against any threat from any quarter. I come here as the boy's guardian to protest against a marriage, most absurd and unequal, that cannot but bring poverty and misery with it: and in prevent it I conceive I be quite as much your daughter's friend (who I have no doubt be an honourable young lady) as the friend of my own family: and prevent the marriage I will, sir, by every mean in my power. there, I have say my say, sir."
"but I have not say mine, Major Pendennis--and ye shall hear more from I," Mr. Costigan say, with a look of tremendous severity.
"'Sdeath, sir, what do you mean?" the Major ask, turn round on the threshold of the door, and look the intrepid Costigan in the face.
"Ye say, in the coorse of conversation, that ye be at the George Hotel, I think," Mr. Costigan say in a stately manner. "a friend shall wait upon ye there before ye leave town, sir."
"let he make haste, Mr. Costigan," cry out the Major, almost beside himself with rage. "I wish you a good morning, sir." and Captain Costigan bow a magnificent bow of defiance to major Pendennis over the landing-place as the latter retreat down the stair.
CHAPTER XII
in which a Shooting Match be propose
early mention have be make in this history of Mr. Garbetts, Principal Tragedian, a promising and athletic young actor, of jovial habit and irregular inclination, between whom and Mr. Costigan there be a considerable intimacy. they be the chief ornament of the convivial club hold at the Magpie Hotel; they help each other in various bill transaction in which they have be engage, with the mutual loan of each other's valuable signature. they be friend, in fine: although Mr. Garbetts seldom call at Costigan's house, be dislike by Miss Fotheringay, of whom in her turn Mrs. Garbetts be considerably jealous. the truth be, that Garbetts have pay his court to Miss Fotheringay and be refuse by she, before he offer his hand to Mrs. G. their history, however, form no part of our present scheme--suffice it, Mr. Garbetts be call in by Captain Costigan immediately after his daughter and Mr. Bows have quit the house, as a friend proper to be consult at the actual juncture. he be a large man, with a loud voice and fierce aspect, who have the fine leg of the whole company, and could break a poker in mere sport across his stalwart arm.
"Run, Tommy," say Mr. Costigan to the little messenger, "and fetch Mr. Garbetts from his lodging over the tripe shop, ye know, and tell them to send two glass of whisky-and-water, hot, from the grape." so Tommy go his way; and presently Mr. Garbetts and the whisky come.
Captain Costigan do not disclose to he the whole of the previous event, of which the reader be in possession; but, with the aid of the spirit-and-water, he compose a letter of a threatening nature to Major Pendennis's address, in which he call upon that gentleman to offer no hindrance to the marriage project between Mr. Arthur Pendennis and his daughter, Miss Fotheringay, and to fix an early day for its celebration: or, in any other case, to give he the satisfaction which be usual between gentleman of honour. and should Major Pendennis be disinclined to this alternative, the Captain hint, that he would force he to accept by the use of a horsewhip, which he should employ upon the Major's person. the precise term of this letter we cannot give, for reason which shall be specify presently; but it be, no doubt, couch in the Captain's fine style, and seal elaborately with the great silver seal of the Costigans--the only bit of the family plate which the Captain possess.
Garbetts be despatch then with this message and letter; and bid Heaven bless 'um the General squeeze his ambassador's hand, and see he depart. then he take down his venerable and murderous duelling-pistol, with flint lock, that have do the business of many a pretty fellow in Dublin: and have examine these, and see that they be in a satisfactory condition, he bring from the drawer all Pen's letter and poem which he keep there, and which he always read before he permit his Emily to enjoy their perusal.
in a score of minute Garbetts come back with an anxious and crestfallen countenance.
"Ye've see 'um?" the Captain say.
"why, yes," say Garbetts.
"and when be it for?" ask Costigan, try the lock of one of the ancient pistol, and bring it to a level with his oi--as he call that bloodshot orb.
"when be what for?" ask Mr. Garbetts.
"the meeting, my dear fellow?"
"you donot mean to say, you mean mortal combat, Captain," Garbetts say, aghast.
"what the devil else do I mean, Garbetts?--I want to shoot that man that have trajuice I honor, or meself dthrop a victim on the sod."
"d--- if I carry challenge," Mr. Garbetts reply. "Ibe a family man, Captain, and will have nothing to do with pistol--take back your letter;" and, to the surprise and indignation of Captain Costigan, his emissary fling the letter down, with its great sprawl superscription and blotched seal.
"ye donot mean to say ye see 'um and donot give 'um the letter?" cry out the Captain in a fury.
"I see he, but I could not have speech with he, Captain," say Mr. Garbetts.
"and why the devil not?" ask the other.
"there be one there I care not to meet, nor would you," the tragedian answer in a sepulchral voice. "the minion Tatham be there, Captain."
"the cowardly scoundthrel!" roar Costigan. "hebe frightened, and already go to swear the peace against I."
"Iwill have nothing to do with the fighting, mark that," the tragedian doggedly say, "and I wish Ihave not see Tatham neither, nor that bit of----"
"hold your tongue, Bob Acres. itbe my belief ye're no well than a coward," say Captain Costigan, quote Sir Lucius O'Trigger, which character he have perform with credit, both off and on the stage, and after some more parley between the couple they separate in not very good humour.
their colloquy have be here condense, as the reader know the main point upon which it turn. but the latter will now see how it be impossible to give a correct account of the letter which the Captain write to Major Pendennis, as it be never open at all by that gentleman.
when Miss Costigan come home from rehearsal, which she do in the company of the faithful Mr. Bows, she find her father pace up and down their apartment in a great state of agitation, and in the midst of a powerful odour of spirit-and-water, which, as it appear, have not succeed in pacify his disordered mind. the Pendennis paper be on the table surround the empty goblet and now useless teaspoon which have serve to hold and mix the Captain's liquor and his friend's. as Emily enter he seize she in his arm, and cry out, "prepare yourself, I child, I bless child," in a voice of agony, and with eye brimful of tear.
"Ye're tipsy again, Papa," Miss Fotheringay say, push back her sire. "Ye promise I ye wouldnot take spirit before dinner."
"itbe to forget I sorrow, I poor girl, that Ihave take just a drop," cry the bereaved father--"it's to drown I care that I drain the bowl."
"your care take a deal of drown, Captain dear," say Bows, mimic his friend's accent; "what have happen? have that soft-speak gentleman in the wig be vex you?"
"the oily miscreant! Iwill have his blood!" roar Cos. Miss Milly, it must be premise, have flee to her room out of his embrace, and be take off her bonnet and shawl there.
"I think he mean mischief. he be so uncommon civil," the other say. "what have he come to say?"
"o bow! he have overwhellum'd I," the Captain say. "therebe a hellish conspiracy on foot against I poor girl; and itbe I opinion that both they pendennise, nephew and uncle, be two infernal thrator and scoundthrel, who should be conshume from off the face of the earth."
"what be it? what have happen?" say Mr. Bows, grow rather excited.
Costigan then tell he the Major's statement that the young Pendennis have not two thousand, nor two hundred pound a year; and express his fury that he should have permit such an impostor to coax and wheedle his innocent girl, and that he should have nourish such a viper in his own personal bosom. "I have shake the reptile from I, however," say Costigan; "and as for his uncle, Iwill have such a revenge on that old man, as shall make 'um rue the day he ever insult a Costigan."
"what do you mean, general?" say Bows.
"I mean to have his life, bow--his villanous, skulk life, my boy;" and he rap upon the batter old pistol-case in an ominous and savage manner. bow have often hear he appeal to that box of death, with which he propose to sacrifice his enemy; but the Captain do not tell he that he have actually write and send a challenge to major Pendennis, and Mr. Bows therefore rather disregard the pistol in the present instance.
at this juncture Miss Fotheringay return to the common sitting-room from her private apartment, look perfectly healthy, happy, and unconcerned, a striking and wholesome contrast to her father, who be in a delirious tremor of grief, anger, and other agitation. she bring in a pair of ex-white satin shoe with she, which she propose to rub as clean as might be with bread-crumb: intend to go mad with they upon next Tuesday evening in Ophelia, in which character she be to reappear on that night.
she look at the paper on the table; stop as if she be go to ask a question, but think well of it, and go to the cupboard, select an eligible piece of bread wherewith she might operate on the satin slipper: and afterwards come back to the table, seat herself there commodiously with the shoe, and then ask her father, in her honest, irish brogue, "what have ye get they letther, and pothry, and stuff, of Master Arthur's out for, Pa? sure ye donot want to be read over that nonsense."
"O Emilee!" cry the Captain, "that boy whom I love as the boy of mee bosom be only a scoundthrel, and a deceiver, mee poor girl:" and he look in the most tragical way at Mr. Bows, opposite; who, in his turn, gaze somewhat anxiously at Miss Costigan.
"he! pooh! sure the poor lad's as simple as a schoolboy," she say. "all they child write verse and nonsense."
"hebe be act the part of a viper to this fireside, and a traitor in this familee," cry the Captain. "I tell ye hebe no well than an impostor."
"what have the poor fellow do, Papa?" ask Emily.
"do? he have deceive we in the most athrocious manner," Miss Emily's papa say. "he have thrifle with your affection, and outrage my own fine feeling. he have represent himself as a man of property, and it turrun out that he be no betther than a beggar. havenot I often tell ye he have two thousand a year? hebe a pauper, I tell ye, Miss Costigan; a depindent upon the bountee of his mother; a good woman, who may marry again, whobe likely to live for ever, and who have but five hundred a year. how dar he ask ye to marry into a family which have not the mean of provide for ye? Ye've be grossly deceive and put upon, Milly, and itbe my belief his old ruffian of an uncle in a wig be in the plot against we."
"that soft old gentleman? what have he be do, Papa?" continue emily, still imperturbable.
Costigan inform Milly, that when she be go, Major Pendennis tell he in his double-faced Pall Mall polite manner, that young Arthur have no fortune at all, that the Major have ask he (Costigan) to go to the lawyer ("wherein he know the scoundthrel have a bill of mine, and I cannot meet they," the Captain parenthetically remark), and see the lad's father's will and finally, that an infernal swindle have be practise upon he by the pair, and that he be resolve either on a marriage, or on the blood of both of they.
milly look very grave and thoughtful, rub the white satin shoe. "sure, if hebe no money, therebe no use marry he, Papa," she say sententiously.
"why do the villain say he be a man of prawpertee?" ask Costigan.
"the poor fellow always say he be poor," answer the girl. "'twas you would have it he be rich, Papa--and make I agree to take he."
"he should have be explicit and tell we his income, Milly," answer the father. "a young fellow who rid a blood mare, and make present of shawl and bracelet, be an impostor if he have no money;--and as for his uncle, bedad Iwill pull off his wig whenever I see 'um. bow, here, shall take a message to he and tell he so. either itbe a marriage, or he meet I in the field like a man, or I tweak 'um on the nose in front of his hotel or in the gravel walk of Fairoaks Park before all the county, bedad."
"Bedad, you may send somebody else with the message," say Bows, laugh. "Ibe a fiddler, not a fight man, Captain."
"Pooh, youhave no spirit, sir," roar the General. "Iwill be my own second, if no one will stand by and see I injure. and Iwill take my case of pistol and shoot 'um in the Coffee-room of the George."
"and so poor Arthur have no money?" sigh out Miss Costigan, rather plaintively. "poor lad, he be a good lad too: wild and talk nonsense, with his verse and pothry and that, but a brave, generous boy, and indeed I like he--and he like I too," she add, rather softly, and rub away at the shoe.
"why donot you marry he if you like he so?" Mr. Bows say, rather savagely. "he be not more than ten year young than you be. his mother may relent, and you might go and live and have enough at Fairoaks Park. why not go and be a lady? I could go on with the fiddle, and the General live on his half-pay. why donot you marry he? you know he like you."
"therebe other that like I as well, Bows, that have no money and thatbe old enough," Miss Milly say sententiously.
"yes, d--- it," say Bows, with a bitter curse--"that be old enough and poor enough and fool enough for anything."
"therebe old fool, and young fool too. youhave often say so you silly man," the imperious beauty say, with a conscious glance at the old gentleman. "if Pendennis have not enough money to live upon, itbe folly to talk about marry he: and thatbe the long and short of it."
"and the boy?" say Mr. Bows. "by Jove! you throw a man away like an old glove, Miss Costigan."
"I donot know what you mean, bow," say Miss Fotheringay, placidly, rub the second shoe. "if he have have half of the two thousand a year that Papa give he, or the half of that, I would marry he. but what be the good of take on with a beggar? webe poor enough already. therebe no use in my go to live with an old lady thatbe testy and cross, maybe, and would grudge I every morsel of meat." (sure, itbe near dinner time, and Suky not lay the cloth yet.) "and then," add Miss Costigan quite simply, "suppose there be a family?--why, Papa, we shouldnot be as well off as we be now."
"'Deed, then, you would not, milly dear," answer the father.
"and therebe an end to all the fine talk about Mrs. Arthur Pendennis of Fairoaks Park--the member of Parliament's lady," say Milly, with a laugh. "pretty carriage and horse we should have to ride!--that you be always talk about, Papa! but itbe always the same. if a man look at I, you fancy he be go to marry I; and if he have a good coat, you fancy he be as rich as Crazes."
"--as Croesus," say Mr. Bows.
"well, call 'um what ye like. but itbe a fact now that Papa have marry I these eight year a score of time. benot I to be my Lady Poldoody of Oystherstown Castle? then there be the Navy Captain at Portsmouth, and the old surgeon at Norwich, and the Methodist preacher here last year, and who know how many more? well, I bet a penny, with all your scheming, I shall die Milly Costigan at last. so poor little Arthur have no money? stop and take dinner, bow; wehave a beautiful beef-steak pudding."
"I wonder whether she be on with Sir Derby Oaks," think Bows, whose eye and thought be always watch she. "the dodge of woman beat all comprehension; and I be sure she wouldnot let the lad off so easily, if she have not some other scheme on hand."
it will have be perceive that Miss Fotheringay, though silent in general, and by no mean brilliant as a conversationist, where poetry, literature, or the fine art be concern, could talk freely, and with good sense, too, in her own family circle. she cannot justly be call a romantic person: nor be her literary acquirement great: she never open a Shakspeare from the day she leave the stage, nor, indeed, understand it during all the time she adorn the board: but about a pudding, a piece of needle-work, or her own domestic affair, she be as good a judge as could be find; and not be mislead by a strong imagination or a passionate temper, be well enable to keep her judgment cool. when, over their dinner, Costigan try to convince himself and the company, that the Major's statement regard Pen's finance be unworthy of credit, and a mere ruse upon the old hypocrite's part so as to induce they, on their side, to break off the match, Miss Milly would not, for a moment, admit the possibility of deceit on the side of the adversary: and point out clearly that it be her father who have deceive himself, and not poor little Pen who have try to take they in. as for that poor lad, she say she pity he with all her heart. and she eat an exceedingly good dinner; to the admiration of Mr. Bows, who have a remarkable regard and contempt for this woman, during and after which repast, the party devise upon the good mean of bring this love-matter to a close. as for Costigan, his idea of tweak the Major's nose vanish with his supply of after-dinner whisky-and-water; and he be submissive to his daughter, and ready for any plan on which she might decide, in order to meet the crisis which she see be at hand.
the Captain, who, as long as he have a notion that he be wrong, be eager to face and demolish both Pen and his uncle, perhaps shrank from the idea of meet the former, and ask "what the juice they be to say to the lad if he remain steady to his engagement, and they break from theirs?" "what? donot you know how to throw a man over?" say bow; "ask a woman to tell you?" and Miss Fotheringay show how this feat be to be do simply enough--nothing be more easy. "Papa write to Arthur to know what settlement he propose to make in event of a marriage; and ask what his mean be. Arthur write back and say what hebe get, and youwill find itbe as the Major say, Iwill go bail. then papa write, and say itbe not enough, and the match have well be at an end."
"and, of course, you enclose a parting line, in which you say you will always regard he as a brother," say Mr. Bows, eye she in his scornful way.
"of course, and so I shall," answer Miss Fotheringay. "hebe a most worthy young man, Ibe sure. Iwill thank ye hand I the salt. they filbert be beautiful."
"and there will be no nose pull, cos, my boy? Ibe sorry yoube baulk," say Mr. Bows.
"Dad, I suppose not," say cos, rub his own.--"What'll ye do about they letter, and verse, and pome, Milly, darling?--ye must send them back."
"Wigsby would give a hundred pound for them," bow say, with a sneer.
"'Deed, then, he would," say Captain Costigan, who be easily lead.
"Papa!" say Miss milly.--"ye wouldnot be for not send the poor boy his letter back? they letter and pome be mine. they be very long, and full of all sort of nonsense, and Latin, and thing I couldnot understand the half of; indeed Ihave not read them all; but wewill send them back to he when the proper time come." and go to a drawer, Miss Fotheringay take out from it a number of the County Chronicle and Chatteris Champion, in which Pen have write a copy of flame verse celebrate her appearance in the character of Imogen, and put by the leaf upon which the poem appear (for, like lady of her profession, she keep the favourable print notice of her performance), she wrap up Pen's letter, poem, passion, and fancy, and tie they with a piece of string neatly, as she would a parcel of sugar.
nor be she in the least move while perform this act. what hour the boy have pass over those paper! what love and longing: what generous faith and manly devotion--what watchful night and lonely fever might they tell of! she tie they up like so much grocery, and sate down and make tea afterwards with a perfectly placid and contented heart: while Pen be yearn after her ten mile off: and hug her image to his soul.
CHAPTER XIII
A Crisis
meanwhile they be wonder at Fairoaks that the Major have not return. Dr. Portman and his lady, on their way home to Clavering, stop at Helen's lodge-gate, with a brief note for she from Major Pendennis, in which he say he should remain at Chatteris another day, be anxious to have some talk with Messrs. Tatham, the lawyer, whom he would meet that afternoon; but no mention be make of the transaction in which the writer have be engage during the morning. indeed the note be write at the pause after the first part of the engagement, and when the Major have decidedly have the bad of the battle.
Pen do not care somehow to go into the town whilst his uncle be there. he do not like to have to fancy that his guardian might be spy at he from that abominable Dean's grass-plat, whilst he be make love in Miss Costigan's drawing-room; and the pleasure of a walk (a delight which he be very rarely permit to enjoy) would have be spoil if he have meet the man of the polished boot on that occasion. his modest love could not show in public by any outward sign, except the eye (with which the poor fellow ogle and gaze violently to be sure), but it be dumb in the presence of third party; and so much the well, for of all the talk which take place in this world, that of love-maker be surely, to the uninitiated, the most silly. it be the vocabulary without the key; it be the lamp without the flame. let the respected reader look or think over some old love-letter that he (or she) have have and forget, and try they over again. how blank and meaningless they seem! what glamour of infatuation be it which make that nonsense beautiful? one wonder that such puling and trash could ever have make one happy. and yet there be date when you kiss those silly letter with rapture--live upon six absurd line for a week, and until the reactionary period come, when you be restless and miserable until you get a fresh supply of folly.
that be why we decline to publish any of the letter and verse which Mr. Pen write at this period of his life, out of mere regard for the young fellow's character. they be too spooney and wild. young lady ought not to be call upon to read they in cold blood. bide your time, young woman; perhaps you will get and write they on your own account soon. meanwhile we will respect Mr. Pen's first outpouring, and keep they tie up in the newspaper with Miss Fotheringay's string, and seal with Captain Costigan's great silver seal.
the Major come away from his interview with Captain Costigan in a state of such concentrated fury as render he terrible to approach! "the impudent bog-trot scamp," he think, "dare to threaten I! dare to talk of permit his damned Costigans to marry with the Pendennises! send I a challenge! if the fellow can get anything in the shape of a gentleman to carry it, I have the great mind in life not to baulk him.--Psha! what would people say if I be to go out with a tipsy mountebank, about a row with an actress in a barn!" so when the Major see Dr. Portman, who ask anxiously regard the issue of his battle with the dragon, Mr. Pendennis do not care to inform the divine of the General's insolent behaviour, but state that the affair be a very ugly and disagreeable one, and that it be by no mean over yet.
he enjoin Doctor and Mrs. Portman to say nothing about the business at Fairoaks; whither he content himself with despatch the note we have before mention. and then he return to his hotel, where he vent his wrath upon Mr. Morgan his valet, "dammin and cussin upstairs and downstair," as that gentleman observe to Mr. Foker's man, in whose company he partook of dinner in the servant' room of the George.
the servant carry the news to his master; and Mr. Foker have finish his breakfast about this time, it be two o'clock in the afternoon, remember that he be anxious to know the result of the interview between his two friend, and have inquire the number of the Major's sitting-room, go over in his brocade dressing-gown, and knock for admission.
Major Pendennis have some business, as he have state, respect a lease of the widow's, about which he be desirous of consult old Mr. Tatham, the lawyer, who have be his brother's man of business, and who have a branch-office at Clavering, where he and his son attend market and other day three or four in the week. this gentleman and his client be now in consultation when Mr. Foker show his grand dressing-gown and embroider skull-cap at Major Pendennis's door.
see the Major engage with paper and red-tape, and an old man with a white head, the modest youth be for draw back--and say, "o, yoube busy--call again another time." but Mr. Pendennis want to see he, and beg he, with a smile, to enter: whereupon Mr. Foker take off the embroidered tarboosh or fez (it have be work by the fond of mother) and advance, bow to the gentleman and smile on they graciously. Mr. Tatham have never see so splendid an apparition before as this brocade youth, who seat himself in an arm-chair, spread out his crimson skirt, and look with exceed kindness and frankness on the other two tenant of the room. "you seem to like my dressing-gown, sir," he say to Mr. Tatham. "a pretty thing, benot it? neat, but not in the least gaudy. and how do you do, Major Pendennis, sir, and how do the world treat you?"
there be that in Foker's manner and appearance which would have put an Inquisitor into good humour, and it smooth the wrinkle under Pendennis's head of hair.
"I have have an interview with that Irishman (you may speak before my friend, Mr. Tatham here, who know all the affair of the family), and it have not, I own, be very satisfactory. he willnot believe that my nephew be poor: he say we be both liar: he do I the honour to hint that I be a coward, as I take leave. and I think when you knock at the door, that you might be the gentleman whom I expect with a challenge from Mr. Costigan--that be how the world treat I, Mr. Foker."
"you donot mean that Irishman, the actress's father?" cry Mr. Tatham, who be a dissenter himself, and do not patronise the drama.
"that Irishman, the actress's father--the very man. have not you hear what a fool my nephew have make of himself about the girl?"--mr. Tatham, who never enter the wall of a theatre, have hear nothing: and Major Pendennis have to recount the story of his nephew's love to the lawyer, Mr. Foker come in with appropriate comment in his usual familiar language.
Tatham be lose in wonder at the narrative. why have not Mrs. Pendennis marry a serious man, he think--Mr. Tatham be a widower--and keep this unfortunate boy from perdition? as for Mr. Costigan's daughter, he would say nothing: her profession be sufficient to characterise she. Mr. Foker here interpose to say he have know some uncommon good people in the booth, as he call the Temple of the Muses. well, it might be so, Mr. Tatham hop so--but the father, Tatham know personally--a man of the bad character, a wine-bibber and an idler in tavern and billiard-room, and a notorious insolvent. "I can understand the reason, Major," he say, "why the fellow would not come to my office to ascertain the truth of the statement which you make him.--we have a writ out against he and another disreputable fellow, one of the play-actor, for a bill give to Mr. Skinner of this city, a most respectable Grocer and Wine and Spirit Merchant, and a Member of the Society of Friends. this Costigan come cry to Mr. skinner,--crye in the shop, sir,--and we have not proceed against he or the other, as neither be worth powder and shot."
it be whilst Mr. Tatham be engage in tell this story that a third knock come to the door, and there enter an athletic gentleman in a shabby braid frock, bear in his hand a letter with a large blotch red seal.
"can I have the honour of speak with Major Pendennis in private?" he began--"i have a few word for your ear, sir. I be the bearer of a mission from my friend Captain Costigan,"--but here the man with the bass voice pause, falter, and turn pale--he catch sight of the red and well-remember face of Mr. Tatham.
"Hullo, Garbetts, speak up!" cry Mr. Foker, delight.
"why, bless my soul, it be the other party to the bill!" say Mr. Tatham. "I say, sir; stop I say." but Garbetts, with a face as blank as Macbeth's when Banquo's ghost appear upon he, gasp some inarticulate word, and flee out of the room.
the Major's gravity be also entirely upset, and he burst out laugh. so do Mr. Foker, who say, "by Jove, it be a good 'un." so do the attorney, although by profession a serious man.
"I donot think therewill be any fight, Major," young Foker say; and begin mimic the tragedian. "if there be, the old gentleman--your name tatham?--very happy to make your acquaintance, Mr. Tatham--may send the bailiff to separate the man;" and Mr. Tatham promise to do so. the Major be by no mean sorry at the ludicrous issue of the quarrel. "it seem to I, sir," he say to Mr. Foker, "that you always arrive to put I into good-humour."
nor be this the only occasion on which Mr. Foker this day be destine to be of service to the Pendennis family. we have say that he have the entree of Captain Costigan's lodging, and in the course of the afternoon he think he would pay the General a visit, and hear from his own lip what have occur in the conversation, in the morning, with Mr. Pendennis. Captain Costigan be not at home. he have receive permission, nay, encouragement from his daughter, to go to the convivial club at the Magpie Hotel, where no doubt he be brag at that moment of his desire to murder a certain ruffian; for he be not only brave, but he know it too, and like to take out his courage, and, as it be, give it an airing in company.
Costigan then be absent, but Miss Fotheringay be at home washing the tea-cup whilst Mr. Bows sate opposite to she.
"just do breakfast I see--how do?" say Mr. Foker, pop in his little funny head.
"get out, you funny little man," cry Miss Fotheringay.
"you mean come in, answer the other.--here we be!" and enter the room he fold his arm and begin twirl his head round and round with immense rapidity, like Harlequin in the pantomime when he first issue from his cocoon or envelope. Miss Fotheringay laugh with all her heart: a wink of Foker's would set she off laughing, when the bitter joke Bows ever make could not get a smile from she, or the fine of poor Pen's speech would only puzzle she. at the end of the harlequinade he sink down on one knee and kiss her hand. "yoube the droll little man," she say, and give he a great good-humour slap. Pen use to tremble as he kiss her hand. Pen would have die of a slap.
these preliminary over, the three begin to talk; Mr. Foker amuse his companion by recount to they the scene which he have just witness of the discomfiture of Mr. Garbetts, by which they learn, for the first time, how far the General have carry his wrath against Major Pendennis. Foker speak strongly in favour of the Major's character for veracity and honour, and describe he as a tip-top swell, move in the upper-circle of society, who would never submit to any deceit--much more to deceive such a charming young woman as Miss Foth.
he touch delicately upon the delicate marriage question, though he couldnot help show that he hold Pen rather cheap. in fact, he have a perhaps just contempt for Mr. Pen's high-fly sentimentality; his own weakness, as he think, not lie that way. "I know it wouldnot do, Miss Foth," say he, nod his little head. "couldnot do. donot like to put my hand into the bag, but know it couldnot do. hebe too young for you: too green: a deal too green: and he turn out to be poor as Job. cannot have he at no price, can she, Mr. Bo?"
"indeed hebe a nice poor boy," say the Fotheringay rather sadly.
"poor little beggar," say Bows, with his hand in his pocket, and steal up a queer look at Miss Fotheringay. perhaps he think and wonder at the way in which woman play with man, and coax they and win they and drop they.
but Mr. Bows have not the least objection to acknowledge that he think Miss Fotheringay be perfectly right in give up Mr. Arthur Pendennis, and that in his idea the match be always an absurd one: and Miss Costigan own that she think so herself, only she couldnot send away two thousand a year. "it all come of believe Papa's silly story," she say; "faith Iwill choose for meself another time"--and very likely the large image of Lieutenant Sir Derby Oaks enter into her mind at that instant.
after praise Major Pendennis, whom Miss Costigan declare to be a proper gentleman entirely, smell of lavender, and as neat as a pin,-- and who be pronounce by Mr. Bows to be the right sort of fellow, though rather too much of an old buck, Mr. Foker suddenly bethink he to ask the pair to come and meet the Major that very evening at dinner at his apartment at the George. "he agree to dine with I, and I think after the--after the little shindy this morning, in which I must say the General be wrong, it would look kind, you know.--I know the Major fall in love with you, Miss Foth: he say so."
"so she may be Mrs. Pendennis still," bow say with a sneer--"no, thank you, Mr. F.--I've din."
"sure, that be at three o'clock," say Miss Costigan, who have an honest appetite, "and I cannot go without you."
"wewill have lobster-salad and champagne," say the little monster, who could not construe a line of Latin, or do a sum beyond the Rule of Three. now, for lobster-salad and champagne in an honourable manner, Miss Costigan would have go anywhere--and Major Pendennis actually find himself at seven o'clock seat at a dinner-table in company with Mr. Bows, a professional fiddler, and Miss Costigan, whose father have want to blow his brain out a few hour before.
to make the happy meeting complete, Mr. Foker, who know Costigan's haunt, despatch Stoopid to the club at the Magpie, where the General be in the act of sing a pathetic song, and bring he off to supper. to find his daughter and bow seat at the board be a surprise indeed-- Major Pendennis laugh, and cordially hold out his hand, which the General Officer grasp avec effusion as the French say. in fact he be considerably inebriate, and have already be cry over his own song before he join the little party at the George. he burst into tear more than once, during the entertainment, and call the Major his dear friend. Stoopid and Mr. Foker walk home with he: the major gallantly give his arm to Miss Costigan. he be receive with great friendliness when he call the next day, when many civility pass between the gentleman. on take leave he express his anxious desire to serve Miss Costigan on any occasion in which he could be useful to she, and he shake hand with Mr. Foker most cordially and gratefully, and say that gentleman have do he the very great service.
"all right," say Mr. Foker: and they part with mutual esteem.
on his return to Fairoaks the next day, Major Pendennis do not say what have happen to he on the previous night, or allude to the company in which he have pass it. but he engage Mr. Smirke to stop to dinner; and any person accustom to watch his manner might have remark that there be something constrain in his hilarity and talkativeness, and that he be unusually gracious and watchful in his communication with his nephew. he give Pen an emphatic God-bless-you when the lad go to bed; and as they be about to part for the night, he seem as if he be go to say something to Mrs. Pendennis, but he bethink he that if he speak he might spoil her night's rest, and allow she to sleep in peace.
the next morning he be down in the breakfast-room early than be his custom, and salute everybody there with great cordiality. the post use to arrive commonly about the end of this meal. when John, the old servant, enter, and discharge the bag of its letter and paper, the Major look hard at Pen as the lad get his--Arthur blush, and put his letter down. he know the hand, it be that of old Costigan, and he do not care to read it in public. Major Pendennis know the letter, too. he have put it into the post himself in Chatteris the day before.
he tell little Laura to go away, which the child do, have a thorough dislike to he; and as the door close on she, he take Mrs. Pendennis's hand, and give she a look full of meaning, point to the letter under the newspaper which Pen be pretend to read. "will you come into the drawing-room?" he say. "I want to speak to you." and she follow he, wonder, into the hall.
"what be it?" she say nervously.
"the affair be at an end," Major Pendennis say. "he have a letter there give he his dismissal. I dictate it myself yesterday. there be a few line from the lady, too, bid he farewell. it be all over."
Helen run back to the dining-room, her brother follow. Pen have jump at his letter the instant they be go. he be read it with a stupefied face. it state what the Major have say, that Mr. Costigan be most gratified for the kindness with which Arthur have treat his daughter, but that he be only now make aware of Mr. Pendennis's peecupiary circumstance. they be such that marriage be at present out of the question, and consider the great disparity in the age of the two, a future union be impossible. under these circumstance, and with the deep regret and esteem for he, Mr. Costigan bade Arthur farewell, and suggest that he should cease visit, for some time at least, at his house.
a few line from Miss Costigan be enclose. she acquiesce in the decision of her Papa. she point out that she be many year old than Arthur, and that an engagement be not to be think of. she would always be grateful for his kindness to she, and hop to keep his friendship. but at present, and until the pain of the separation should be over, she entreat they should not meet.
Pen read Costigan's letter and its enclosure mechanically, hardly know what be before his eye. he look up wildly, and see his mother and uncle regard he with sad face. Helen's, indeed, be full of tender maternal anxiety.
"what--what be this?" Pen say. "itbe some joke. this be not her writing. this be some servant's writing. whobe play these trick upon I?"
"it come under her father's envelope," the Major say. "those letter you have before be not in her hand: that be her."
"how do you know?" say Pen very fiercely.
"I see she write it," the uncle answer, as the boy start up; and his mother, come forward, take his hand. he put she away.
"how come you to see she? how come you between I and she? what have I ever do to you that you should--oh, itbe not true! itbe not true!"--pen break out with a wild execration. "she cannot have do it of her own accord. she cannot mean it. shebe pledge to I. who have tell she lie to break she from I?"
"lie be not tell in the family, Arthur," Major Pendennis reply. "I tell she the truth, which be, that you have no money to maintain she, for her foolish father have represent you to be rich. and when she know how poor you be, she withdraw at once, and without any persuasion of mine. she be quite right. she be ten year old than you be. she be perfectly unfit to be your wife, and know it. look at that handwriting, and ask yourself, be such a woman fit to be the companion of your mother?"
"I will know from herself if it be true," Arthur say, crumple up the paper.
"willnot you take my word of honour? her letter be write by a confidant of her, who write well than she can--look here. herebe one from the lady to your friend, Mr. Foker. you have see she with Miss Costigan, as whose amanuensis she acted"--the Major say, with ever so little of a sneer, and lay down a certain billet which Mr. Foker have give to he.
"itbe not that," say Pen, burn with shame and rage. "I suppose what you say be true, sir, but Iwill hear it from herself."
"Arthur!" appeal his mother.
"I will see she," say Arthur. "Iwill ask she to marry I, once more. I will. no one shall prevent I."
"what, a woman who spell affection with one f? nonsense, sir. be a man, and remember that your mother be a lady. she be never make to associate with that tipsy old swindler or his daughter. be a man and forget she, as she do you."
"be a man and comfort your mother, my Arthur," Helen say, go and embrace he: and see that the pair be greatly move, Major Pendennis go out of the room and shut the door upon they, wisely judge that they be well alone.
he have win a complete victory. he actually have bring away Pen's letter in his portmanteau from Chatteris: have compliment Mr. Costigan, when he return they, by give he the little promissory note which have disquiet himself and Mr. Garbetts; and for which the Major settle with Mr. Tatham.
Pen rush wildly off to Chatteris that day, but in vain attempt to see Miss Fotheringay, for whom he leave a letter, enclose to her father. the enclosure be return by Mr. Costigan, who beg that all correspondence might end; and after one or two further attempt of the lad's, the indignant General desire that their acquaintance might cease. he cut Pen in the street. as Arthur and Foker be pace the Castle walk, one day, they come upon Emily on her father's arm. she pass without any nod of recognition. Foker feel poor Pen tremble on his arm.
his uncle want he to travel, to quit the country for a while, and his mother urge he too: for he be grow very ill, and suffer severely. but he refuse, and say point-blank he would not go. he would not obey in this instance: and his mother be too fond, and his uncle too wise to force he. whenever Miss Fotheringay act, he ride over to the Chatteris Theatre and see she. one night there be so few people in the house that the Manager return the money. Pen come home and go to bed at eight o'clock, and have a fever. if this continue, his mother will be go over and fetch the girl, the major thought, in despair. as for Pen, he think he should die. we be not go to describe his feeling, or give a dreary journal of his despair and passion. have not other gentleman be baulk in love besides Mr. Pen? yes, indeed: but few die of the malady.
CHAPTER XIV
in which Miss Fotheringay make a new Engagement
within a short period of the event above narrate, Mr. Manager Bingley be perform his famous character of 'rolla,' in 'Pizarro,' to a house so exceedingly thin, that it would appear as if the part of Rolla be by no mean such a favourite with the people of Chatteris as it be with the accomplished actor himself. scarce anybody be in the theatre. Poor Pen have the box almost all to himself, and sate there lonely, with bloodshot eye, lean over the ledge, and gaze haggardly towards the scene, when Cora come in. when she be not on the stage he see nothing. spaniard and Peruvians, procession and battle, priest and virgin of the sun, go in and out, and have their talk, but Arthur take no note of any of they; and only see Cora whom his soul long after. he say afterwards that he wonder he have not take a pistol to shoot she, so mad be he with love, and rage, and despair; and have it not be for his mother at home, to whom he do not speak about his luckless condition, but whose silent sympathy and watchfulness greatly comfort the simple half heart-break fellow, who know but he might have do something desperate, and have end his day prematurely in front of Chatteris gaol? there he sate then, miserable, and gaze at she. and she take no more notice of he than he do of the rest of the house.
the Fotheringay be uncommonly handsome, in a white raiment and leopard skin, with a sun upon her breast, and fine tawdry bracelet on her beautiful glance arm. she spout to admiration the few word of her part, and look it still well. the eye, which have overthrow Pen's soul, roll and gleam as lustrous as ever; but it be not to he that they be direct that night. he do not know to whom, or remark a couple of gentleman, in the box next to he, upon whom Miss Fotheringay's glance be perpetually shin.
nor have Pen notice the extraordinary change which have take place on the stage a short time after the entry of these two gentleman into the theatre. there be so few people in the house, that the first act of the play languish entirely, and there have be some question of return the money, as upon that other unfortunate night when poor Pen have be drive away. the actor be perfectly careless about their part, and yawn through the dialogue, and talk loud to each other in the interval. even Bingley be listless, and Mrs. B. in Elvira speak under her breath.
how come it that all of a sudden Mrs. Bingley begin to raise her voice and bellow like a bull of Bashan? whence be it that Bingley, fling off his apathy, dart about the stage and yell like Dean? why do Garbetts and Rowkins and Miss Rouncy try, each of they, the force of their charm or grace, and act and swagger and scowl and spout their very loud at the two gentleman in box no. 3?
one be a quiet little man in black, with a grey head and a jolly shrewd face--the other be in all respect a splendid and remarkable individual. he be a tall and portly gentleman with a hooked nose and a profusion of curl brown hair and whisker; his coat be cover with the rich frog-braid and velvet. he have under-waistcoat, many splendid ring, jewel pin and neck-chain. when he take out his yellow pocket-handkerchief with his hand that be case in white kid, a delightful odour of musk and bergamot be shake through the house. he be evidently a personage of rank, and it be at he that the little Chatteris company be act.
he be, in a word, no other than Mr. Dolphin, the great manager from London, accompany by his faithful friend and secretary Mr. William Minns: without whom he never travel. he have not be ten minute in the theatre before his august presence there be perceive by Bingley and the rest: and they all begin to act their good and try to engage his attention. even Miss Fotheringay's dull heart, which be disturb at nothing, feel perhaps a flutter, when she come in presence of the famous London Impresario. she have not much to do in her part, but to look handsome, and stand in picturesque attitude encircle her child and she do this work to admiration. in vain the various actor try to win the favour of the great stage Sultan. Pizarro never get a hand from he. Bingley yell, and Mrs. Bingley bellow, and the Manager only take snuff out of his great gold box. it be only in the last scene, when Rolla come in staggering with the infant (Bingley be not so strong as he be and his fourth son Master Talma Bingley be a monstrous large child for his age)--when Rolla come staggering with the child to Cora, who rush forward with a shriek, and says--"o God, therebe blood upon he!" --that the London manager clap his hand, and break out with an enthusiastic bravo.
then have conclude his applause, Mr. Dolphin give his secretary a slap on the shoulder, and say, "by Jove, Billy, shewill do!"
"who teach she that dodge?" say old Billy, who be a sardonic old gentleman. "I remember she at the Olympic, and hang I if she could say Bo to a goose."
it be little Mr. bow in the orchestra who have teach she the 'dodge' in question. all the company hear the applause, and, as the curtain go down, come round she and congratulate and hat Miss Fotheringay.
now Mr. Dolphin's appearance in the remote little Chatteris theatre may be account for in this manner. in spite of all his exertion, and the perpetual blaze of triumph, coruscation of talent, victory of good old english comedy, which his play-bill advertise, his theatre (which, if you please, and to injure no present susceptibility and vested interest, we shall call the Museum Theatre) by no mean prosper, and the famous Impresario find himself on the verge of ruin. the great Hubbard have act legitimate drama for twenty night, and fail to remunerate anybody but himself: the celebrate Mr. and Mrs. Cawdor have come out in Mr. Rawhead's tragedy, and in their favourite round of piece, and have not attract the public. Herr Garbage's lion and tiger have draw for a little time, until one of the animal have bite a piece out of the Herr's shoulder; when the Lord Chamberlain interfere, and put a stop to this specie of performance: and the grand Lyrical Drama, though bring out with unexampled splendour and success, with Monsieur Poumons as first tenor, and an enormous orchestra, have almost crush poor dolphin in its triumphant progress: so that great as his genius and resource be, they seem to be at an end. he be drag on his season wretchedly with half salary, small opera, feeble old comedy, and his ballet company; and everybody be look out for the day when he should appear in the Gazette.
one of the illustrious patron of the Museum Theatre, and occupant of the great proscenium-box, be a gentleman whose name have be mention in a previous history; that refined patron of the art, and enlighten lover of music and the drama, the most Noble the Marquis of Steyne. his lordship's avocation as a statesman prevent he from attend the playhouse very often, or come very early. but he occasionally appear at the theatre in time for the ballet, and be always receive with the great respect by the Manager, from whom he sometimes condescend to receive a visit in his box. it communicate with the stage, and when anything occur there which particularly please he, when a new face make its appearance among the coryphee, or a fair dancer execute a pas with especial grace or agility, Mr. Wenham, Mr. Wagg, or some other aide-de-camp of the noble Marquis, would be commission to go behind the scene, and express the great man's approbation, or make the inquiry which be prompt by his lordship's curiosity, or his interest in the dramatic art. he could not be see by the audience, for Lord Steyne sate modestly behind a curtain, and look only towards the stage--but you could know he be in the house, by the glance which all the corps-de-ballet, and all the principal dancer, cast towards his box. I have see many score of pair of eye (as in the Palm Dance in the ballet of Cook at Otaheite, where no less than a hundred-and-twenty lovely female savage in palm leaf and feather apron, be make to dance round Floridor as Captain Cook) ogle that box as they perform before it, and have often wonder to remark the presence of mind of Mademoiselle Sauterelle, or Mademoiselle de Bondi (know as la petite Caoutchoue), who, when actually up in the air quiver like so many shuttlecock, always keep their lovely eye wink at that box in which the great Steyne sate. now and then you would hear a harsh voice from behind the curtain cry, "Brava, Brava," or a pair of white glove wave from it, and begin to applaud. Bondi, or Sauterelle, when they come down to earth, curtsy and smile, especially to those hand, before they walk up the stage again, pant and happy.
one night this great Prince surround by a few choice friend be in his box at the Museum, and they be make such a noise and laughter that the pit be scandalise, and many indignant voice be bawl out silence so loudly, that Wagg wonder the police do not interfere to take the rascal out. Wenham be amuse the party in the box with extract from a private letter which he have receive from Major Pendennis, whose absence in the country at the full London season have be remark, and of course deplore by his friend.
"the secret be out," say Mr. Wenham, "therebe a woman in the case."
"why, d--- it, Wenham, hebe your age," say the gentleman behind the curtain. "pour les ame bien nees, l'amour ne compte pas le nombre des annees," say Mr. Wenham, with a gallant air. "for my part, I hope to be a victim till I die, and to break my heart every year of my life." the meaning of which sentence be, "my lord, you need not talk; Ibe three year young than you, and twice as well conserve."
