"Wenham, you affect I," say the great man, with one of his usual oath. "by --- you do. I like to see a fellow preserve all the illusion of youth up to our time of life--and keep his heart warm as yours be. hang it, sir, itbe a comfort to meet with such a generous, candid creature.--Whobe that gal in the second row, with blue ribbon, third from the stage--fine gal. yes, you and I be sentimentalist. Wagg I donot think so much care--itbe the stomach rather more than the heart with you, eh, Wagg, my boy?"
"I like everything thatbe good," say Mr. Wagg, generously. "Beauty and Burgundy, Venus and Venison. I donot say that Venus's turtle be to be despise, because they donot cook they at the London Tavern: but--but tell we about old Pendennis, Mr. Wenham," he abruptly conclude--for his joke flag just then, as he see that his patron be not listen. in fact, Steyne's glass be up, and he be examine some object on the stage.
"yes, Ihave hear that joke about Venus's turtle and the London Tavern before--you begin to fail, my poor Wagg. if you donot mind I shall be oblige to have a new Jester," Lord Steyne say, lay down his glass. "go on, Wenham, about old Pendennis."
"dear wenham,"--he begin, Mr. Wenham read,--"a you have have my character in your hand for the last three week, and no doubt have tear I to shred, accord to your custom, I think you can afford to be good-humour by way of variety, and to do I a service. it be a delicate matter, entre nous, une affaire de coeur. there be a young friend of mine who be go wild about a certain Miss Fotheringay, an actress at the theatre here, and I must own to you, as handsome a woman, and, as it appear to I, as good an actress as ever put on rouge. she do Ophelia, Lady Teazle, Mrs. Haller--that sort of thing. upon my word, she be as splendid as george in her good day, and as far as I know, utterly superior to anything we have on our scene. I want a London engagement for she. cannot you get your friend dolphin to come and see she--to engage she--to take she out of this place? a word from a noble friend of ours (you understand) would be invaluable, and if you could get the Gaunt House interest for I--I will promise anything I can in return for your service--which I shall consider one of the great that can be do to I. do, do this now as a good fellow, which I always say you be: and in return, command yours truly, A. Pendennis."
"itbe a clear case," say Mr. Wenham, have read this letter; "old Pendennis be in love."
"and want to get the woman up to London--evidently," continue Mr. Wagg.
"I should like to see Pendennis on his knee, with the rheumatism," say Mr. Wenham.
"or accommodate the beloved object with a lock of his hair," say Wagg.
"Stuff." say the great man. "he have relation in the country, havenot he? he say something about a nephew, whose interest could return a member. it be the nephew's affair, depend on it. the young one be in a scrape. I be myself--when I be in the fifth form at Eton--a market-gardener's daughter--and swear Iwould marry she. I be mad about she--poor polly!"-- here he make a pause, and perhaps the past rise up to Lord Steyne, and George Gaunt be a boy again not altogether lost.--"But I say, she must be a fine woman from Pendennis's account. have in dolphin, and let we hear if he know anything of she."
at this Wenham spring out of the box, pass the servitor who wait at the door communicate with the stage, and who salute Mr. Wenham with profound respect; and the latter emissary, push on and familiar with the place, have no difficulty in find out the manager, who be employ, as he not unfrequently be, in swear and curse the lady of the corps-de-ballet for not do their duty.
the oath die away on Mr. Dolphin's lip, as soon as he see Mr. Wenham; and he draw off the hand which be clench in the face of one of the offend coryphee, to grasp that of the new-comer. "how do, Mr. Wenham? howbe his lordship to-night? look uncommonly well," say the manager smile, as if he have never be out of temper in his life; and he be only too delighted to follow Lord Steyne's ambassador, and pay his personal respect to that great man.
the visit to Chatteris be the result of their conversation: and Mr. Dolphin write to his lordship from that place, and do himself the honour to inform the Marquess of Steyne, that he have see the lady about whom his lordship have speak, that he be as much strike by her talent as he be by her personal appearance, and that he have make an engagement with Miss Fotheringay, who would soon have the honour of appear before a London audience, and his noble and enlighten patron the Marquess of Steyne.
Pen read the announcement of Miss Fotheringay's engagement in the Chatteris paper, where he have so often praise her charm. the Editor make very handsome mention of her talent and beauty, and prophesy her success in the metropolis. Bingley, the manager, begin to advertise "the last night of Miss Fotheringay's engagement." Poor Pen and Sir Derby Oaks be very constant at the play: Sir Derby in the stage-box, throw bouquet and get glances.--pen in the almost deserted box, haggard, wretched and lonely. nobody care whether Miss Fotheringay be go or stay except those two--and perhaps one more, which be Mr. Bows of the orchestra.
he come out of his place one night, and go into the house to the box where Pen be; and he hold out his hand to he, and ask he to come and walk. they walk down the street together; and go and sate upon Chatteris bridge in the moonlight, and talk about Her. "we may sit on the same bridge," say he; "we have be in the same boat for a long time. you be not the only man who have make a fool of himself about that woman. and I have less excuse than you, because I be old and know she well. she have no more heart than the stone you be lean on; and it or you or I might fall into the water, and never come up again, and she wouldnot care. yes--she would care for I, because she want I to teach she: and she willnot be able to get on without I, and will be force to send for I from London. but she wouldnot if she donot want I. she have no heart and no head, and no sense, and no feeling, and no grief or care, whatever. I be go to say no pleasure--but the fact be, she do like her dinner, and she be pleased when people admire she."
"and you do?" say Pen, interested out of himself, and wonder at the crab homely little old man.
"itbe a habit, like take snuff, or drinking dram," say the other. "Ihave be take she these five year, and cannot do without she. it be I make she. if she donot send for I, I shall follow she: but I know shewill send for I. she want I. some day shewill marry, and fle I over, as I do the end of this cigar."
the little flaming spark drop into the water below, and disappear; and Pen, as he ride home that night, actually think about somebody but himself.
CHAPTER XV
the happy village
until the enemy have retire altogether from before the place, Major Pendennis be resolve to keep his garrison in Fairoaks. he do not appear to watch Pen's behaviour or to put any restraint on his nephew's action, but he manage nevertheless to keep the lad constantly under his eye or those of his agent, and young Arthur's coming and going be quite well know to his vigilant guardian.
I suppose there be scarcely any man who read this or any other novel but have be baulk in love some time or the other, by fate and circumstance, by falsehood of woman, or his own fault. let that worthy friend recall his own sensation under the circumstance, and apply they as illustrative of Mr. Pen's anguish. ah! what weary night and sicken fever! ah! what mad desire dash up against some rock of obstruction or indifference, and fling back again from the unimpressionable granite! if a list could be make this very night in London of the groan, thought, imprecation of toss lover, what a catalogue it would be! I wonder what a percentage of the male population of the metropolis will be lie awake at two or three o'clock to-morrow morning, count the hour as they go by knell drearily, and roll from left to right, restless, yearning and heart-sick? what a pang it be! I never know a man die of love certainly, but I have know a twelve-stone man go down to nine-stone five under a disappointed passion, so that pretty nearly quarter of he may be say to have perish: and that be no small portion. he have come back to his old size subsequently; perhaps be big than ever: very likely some new affection have close round his heart and rib and make they comfortable, and young Pen be a man who will console himself like the rest of we. we say this lest the lady should be dispose to deplore he prematurely, or be seriously uneasy with regard to his complaint. his mother be, but what will not a maternal fondness fear or invent? "depend on it, my dear creature," Major Pendennis would say gallantly to she, "the boy will recover. as soon as we get she out of the country we will take he somewhere, and show he a little life. meantime make yourself easy about he. half a fellow's pang at lose a woman result from vanity more than affection. to be leave by a woman be the deuce and all, to be sure; but look how easily we leave them."
Mrs. Pendennis do not know. this sort of knowledge have by no mean come within the simple lady's scope. indeed she do not like the subject or to talk of it: her heart have have its own little private misadventure and she have bear up against it and cure it: and perhaps she have not much patience with other folk's passion, except, of course, Arthur's, whose suffering she make she own, feel indeed very likely in many of the boy's illness and pain a great deal more than Pen himself endure. and she watch he through this present grief with a jealous silent sympathy; although, as we have say, he do not talk to she of his unfortunate condition.
the Major must be allow to have have not a little merit and forbearance, and to have exhibit a highly creditable degree of family affection. the life at Fairoaks be uncommonly dull to a man who have the entree of half the house in London, and be in the habit of make his bow in three or four drawing-room of a night. a dinner with Doctor Portman or a neighbouring squire now and then; a dreary rubber at backgammon with the widow, who do she utmost to amuse he; these be the chief of his pleasure. he use to long for the arrival of the bag with the letter, and he read every word of the evening paper. he doctor himself too, assiduously,--a course of quiet living would suit he well, he think, after the London banquet. he dress himself laboriously every morning and afternoon: he take regular exercise up and down the terrace walk. thus with his cane, his toilet, his medicine-chest, his backgammon-box, and his newspaper, this worthy and worldly philosopher fence himself against ennui; and if he do not improve each shin hour, like the bee by the widow's garden wall, Major Pendennis make one hour after another pass as he could, and render his captivity just tolerable. after this period it be remark that he be fond of bring round the conversation to the american war, the massacre of Wyoming and the brilliant action of Saint Lucie, the fact be that he have a couple of volume of the 'Annual Register' in his bedroom, which he sedulously study. it be thus a well-regulate man will accommodate himself to circumstance, and show himself calmly superior to fortune.
Pen sometimes take the box at backgammon of a night, or would listen to his mother's simple music of summer evening--but he be very restless and wretche in spite of all: and have be know to be up before the early daylight even; and down at a carp-pond in Clavering Park, a dreary pool with innumerable whispering rush and green alder, where a milkmaid drown herself in the Baronet's grandfather's time, and her ghost be say to walk still. but Pen do not drown himself, as perhaps his mother fancy might be his intention. he like to go and fish there, and think and think at leisure, as the float quiver in the little eddy of the pond, and the fish flap about he. if he get a bite he be excited enough: and in this way occasionally bring home carps, tench, and eel, which the Major cook in the continental fashion.
by this pond, and under a tree, which be his favourite resort, Pen compose a number of poem suitable to his circumstance over which verse he blush in after day, wonder how he could ever have invent such rubbish. and as for the tree, why it be in a hollow of this very tree, where he use to put his tin-box of ground-bait, and other fishing commodity, that he afterwards--but we be advance matter. suffice it to say, he write poem and relieve himself very much. when a man's grief or passion be at this point, it may be loud, but it be not very severe. when a gentleman be cudgel his brain to find any rhyme for sorrow, besides borrow and to-morrow, his woe be near at an end than he think for. so be Pen's. he have his hot and cold fit, his day of sullenness and peevishness, and of blank resignation and despondency, and occasional mad paroxysm of rage and longing, in which fit Rebecca would be saddle and gallop fiercely about the country, or into Chatteris, her rider gesticulate wildly on her back, and astonishing carter and turnpikeman as he pass, cry out the name of the false one.
Mr. Foker become a very frequent and welcome visitor at Fairoaks during this period, where his good spirit and oddity always amuse the Major and Pendennis, while they astonish the widow and little Laura not a little. his tandem make a great sensation in Clavering market-place; where he upset a market stall, and cut Mrs. Pybus's poodle over the shaven quarter, and drink a glass of raspberry bitter at the Clavering Arms. all the society in the little place hear who he be, and look out his name in their Peerages. he be so young, and their book so old, that his name do not appear in many of their volume; and his mamma, now quite an antiquated lady, figure amongst the progeny of the Earl of Rosherville, as Lady Agnes Milton still. but his name, wealth, and honourable lineage be speedily know about Clavering, where you may be sure that poor Pen's little transaction with the Chatteris actress be also pretty freely discuss.
look at the little old town of Clavering St. Mary from the London road as it run by the lodge at Fairoaks, and see the rapid and shin Brawl wind down from the town and skirt the wood of Clavering Park, and the ancient church tower and peak roof of the house rise up amongst tree and old wall, behind which swell a fair background of sunshiny hill that stretch from Clavering westward towards the sea--the place look so cheery and comfortable that many a traveller's heart must have yearn towards it from the coach-top, and he must have think that it be in such a calm friendly nook he would like to shelter at the end of life's struggle. Tom Smith, who use to drive the Alacrity coach, would often point to a tree near the river, from which a fine view of the church and town be command, and inform his companion on the box that "artise come and take hoff the Church from that there tree--it be a Habby once, sir:"--and indeed a pretty view it be, which I recommend to Mr. Stanfield or Mr. Roberts, for their next tour.
like Constantinople see from the Bosphorus; like Mrs. Rougemont view in her box from the opposite side of the house; like many an object which we pursue in life, and admire before we have attain it; clavering be rather pretty at a distance than it be on a close acquaintance. the town so cheerful of aspect a few furlong off, look very blank and dreary. except on market day there be nobody in the street. the clack of a pair of patten echo through half the place, and you may hear the creaking of the rusty old ensign at the Clavering Arms, without be disturb by any other noise. there have not be a ball in the Assembly Rooms since the Clavering volunteer give one to their Colonel, the old Sir Francis Clavering; and the stable which once hold a great part of that brilliant, but defunct regiment, be now cheerless and empty, except on Thursdays, when the farmer put up there, and their tilt cart and gig make a feeble show of liveliness in the place, or on Petty Sessions, when the magistrate attend in what use to be the old card-room.
on the south side of the market rise up the church, with its great grey tower, of which the sun illuminate the delicate carving; deepen the shadow of the huge buttress, and gilding the glitter window and flame vane. the image of the Patroness of the Church be wrench out of the porch century ago: such of the statue of saint as be within reach of stone and hammer at that period of pious demolition, be maim and headless, and of those who be out of fire, only Doctor Portman know the name and history, for his curate, Smirke, be not much of an antiquarian, and Mr. Simcoe (husband of the Honourable Mrs. Simcoe), incumbent and architect of the Chapel of Ease in the low town, think they the abomination of desolation.
the Rectory be a stout broad-shoulder brick house, of the reign of Anne. it communicate with the church and market by different gate, and stand at the opening of Yew-tree Lane, where the Grammar School (Rev. ---- Wapshot) be; yew-tree Cottage (Miss Flather); the butcher' slaughter-house, an old barn or brew-house of the Abbey time, and the Misses Finucane's establishment for young lady. the two school have their pew in the loft on each side of the organ, until the Abbey Church get rather empty, through the fall-off of the congregation, who be inveigle to the Heresy-shop in the low town, the doctor induce the Misses Finucane to bring their pretty little flock downstairs; and the young lady' bonnet make a tolerable show in the rather vacant aisle. nobody be in the great pew of the Clavering family, except the statue of defunct baronet and their lady: there be Sir Poyntz Clavering, Knight and Baronet, kneel in a square beard opposite his wife in a ruff: a very fat lady, the Dame Rebecca Clavering, in alto-relievo, be bear up to Heaven by two little blue-veined angel, who seem to have a severe task--and so forth. how well in after life Pen remember those effigy, and how often in youth he scan they as the doctor be grumble the sermon from the pulpit, and Smirke's mild head and forehead curl peer over the great prayer-book in the desk!
the Fairoaks folk be constant at the old church; their servant have a pew, so have the Doctor's, so have Wapshot's, and those of Misses Finucane's establishment, three maid and a very nice-look young man in a livery. the Wapshot Family be numerous and faithful. glander and his child regularly come to church: so do one of the apothecary. Mrs. Pybus go, turn and turn about, to the Low Town church, and to the Abbey: the Charity School and their family of course come; Wapshot's boy make a good cheerful noise, scuffle with their foot as they march into church and up the organ-loft stair, and blow their nose a good deal during the service. to be brief, the congregation look as decent as might be in these bad time. the Abbey Church be furnish with a magnificent screen, and many hatchment and heraldic tombstone. the Doctor spend a great part of his income in beautify his darling place; he have endow it with a superb paint window, buy in the Netherlands, and an organ grand enough for a cathedral.
but in spite of organ and window, in consequence of the latter very likely, which have come out of a papistical place of worship and be blazon all over with idolatry, Clavering New Church prosper scandalously in the tooth of Orthodoxy; and many of the Doctor's congregation desert to Mr. Simcoe and the honourable woman his wife. their effort have thin the very Ebenezer hard by they, which build before Simcoe's advent use to be so full, that you could see the back of the congregation squeeze out of the arched window thereof. Mr. Simcoe's tract flutter into the door of all the doctor's cottage, and be take as greedily as honest Mrs. Portman's soup, with the quality of which the graceless people find fault. with the folk at the Ribbon Factory situate by the weir on the Brawl side, and round which the Low Town have grow, Orthodoxy could make no way at all. Quiet Miss Myra be put out of court by impetuous Mrs. Simcoe and her female aide-de-camp. ah, it be a hard burthen for the Doctor's lady to bear, to behold her husband's congregation dwindle away; to give the precedence on the few occasion when they meet to a notorious low-churchman's wife who be the daughter of an irish peer; to know that there be a party in Clavering, their own town of Clavering, on which her Doctor spend a great deal more than his professional income, who hold he up to odium because he play a rubber at whist; and pronounce he to be a Heathen because he go to the play. in her grief she besought he to give up the play and the rubber,--indeed they could scarcely get a table now, so dreadful be the outcry against the sport,--but the Doctor declare that he would do what he think right, and what the great and good George the Third do (whose Chaplain he have be): and as for give up whist because those silly folk cry out against it, he would play dummy to the end of his day with his wife and Myra, rather than yield to their despicable persecution.
of the two family, owner of the Factory (which have spoil the Brawl as a trout-stream and bring all the mischief into the town), the senior partner, Mr. Rolt, go to Ebenezer; the junior, Mr. Barker, to the New Church. in a word, people quarrel in this little place a great deal more than neighbour do in London; and in the Book Club, which the prudent and conciliate Pendennis have set up, and which ought to have be a neutral territory, they bicker so much that nobody scarcely be ever see in the reading-room, except Smirke, who, though he keep up a faint amity with the Simcoe faction, have still a taste for magazine and light worldly literature; and old Glanders, whose white head and grizzly moustache might be see at the window; and of course, little Mrs. Pybus, who look at everybody's letter as the Post bring they (for the Clavering reading-room, as every one know, use to be hold at Baker's Library, London Street, formerly Hog Lane), and read every advertisement in the paper.
it may be imagine how great a sensation be create in this amiable little community when the news reach it of Mr. Pen's love-passage at Chatteris. it be carry from house to house, and form the subject of talk at high-church, low-church, and no-church table; it be canvass by the Misses Finucane and their teacher, and very likely debate by the young lady in the dormitory for what we know; Wapshot's big boy have their version of the story, and eye Pen curiously as he sate in his pew at church, or raise the finger of scorn at he as he pass through Chatteris. they always hat he and call he Lord Pendennis, because he do not wear corduroy as they do, and ride a horse, and give himself the air of a buck.
and if the truth must be tell, it be Mrs. Portman herself who be the chief narrator of the story of Pen's love. whatever tale this candid woman hear, she be sure to impart they to her neighbour; and after she have be put into possession of Pen's secret by the little scandal at Chatteris, poor Doctor Portman know that it would next day be about the parish of which he be the Rector. and so indeed it be; the whole society there have the legend--at the news-room, at the milliner's, at the shoe-shop, and the general warehouse at the corner of the market; at Mrs. Pybus's, at the glander's, at the Honourable Mrs. Simcoe's soiree, at the Factory; nay, through the mill itself the tale be current in a few hour, and young Arthur Pendennis's madness be in every mouth.
all Dr. Portman's acquaintance bark out upon he when he walk the street the next day. the poor divine know that his Betsy be the author of the rumour, and groan in spirit. well, well,--it must have come in a day or two, and it be as well that the town should have the real story. what the Clavering folk think of Mrs. Pendennis for spoil her son, and of that precocious young rascal of an Arthur for dare to propose to a play-actress, need not be tell here. if pride exist amongst any folk in our country, and assuredly we have enough of it, there be no pride more deep-seated than that of twopenny old gentlewoman in small town. "gracious goodness," the cry be, "how infatuated the mother be about that pert and headstrong boy who give himself the air of a lord on his blood-horse, and for whom our society be not good enough, and who would marry an odious paint actress off a booth, where very likely he want to rant himself. if dear good Mr. Pendennis have be alive this scandal would never have happen."
no more it would, very likely, nor should we have be occupy in narrate Pen's history. it be true that he give himself air to the Clavering folk. naturally haughty and frank, their cackle and small talk and small dignity bore he, and he show a contempt which he could not conceal. the Doctor and the Curate be the only people Pen care for in the place--even Mrs. Portman share in the general distrust of he, and of his mother, the widow, who keep herself aloof from the village society, and be sneer at accordingly, because she try, forsooth, to keep her head up with the great County family. she, indeed! Mrs. Barker at the Factory have four time the butcher's meat that go up to Fairoaks, with all their fine air.
Etc. etc. etc.: let the reader fill up these detail accord to his liking and experience of village scandal. they will suffice to show how it be that a good woman occupy solely in do her duty to her neighbour and her child, and an honest, brave lad, impetuous, and full of good, and wish well to every mortal alive find enemy and detractor amongst people to whom they be superior, and to whom they have never do anything like harm. the Clavering cur be yelp all round the house of Fairoaks, and delight to pull Pen down.
Doctor Portman and Smirke be both cautious of inform the widow of the constant outbreak of calumny which be pursue poor Pen, though Glanders, who be a friend of the house, keep he au courant. it may be imagine what his indignation be: be there any man in the village whom be could call to account? presently some wag begin to chalk up 'Fotheringay for ever!' and other sarcastic allusion to late transaction, at Fairoaks' gate. another bring a large playbill from Chatteris, and wafere it there one night. on one occasion Pen, rid through the Lower Town, fancy he hear the Factory boy jeer he; and finally go through the Doctor's gate into the churchyard, where some of Wapshot's boy be lounge, the big of they, a young gentleman about twenty year of age, son of a neighbour small squire, who live in the doubtful capacity of parlour-boarder with Mr. Wapshot, fling himself into a theatrical attitude near a newly-make grave, and begin repeat Hamlet's verse over Ophelia, with a hideous leer at Pen. the young fellow be so enraged that he rush at Hobnell Major with a shriek very much resemble an oath, cut he furiously across the face with the riding-whip which he carry, fling it away, call upon the cowardly villain to defend himself, and in another minute knock the bewildered young ruffian into the grave which be just wait for a different lodger.
then with his fist clench, and his face quiver with passion and indignation, he roar out to Mr. Hobnell's gap companion, to know if any of the blackguard would come on? but they hold back with a growl, and retreat as Doctor Portman come up to his wicket, and Mr. Hobnell, with his nose and lip bleed piteously, emerge from the grave.
Pen, look death and defiance at the lad, who retreat towards' their side of the churchyard, walk back again through the doctor's wicket, and be interrogate by that gentleman. the young fellow be so agitated he could scarcely speak. his voice break into a sob as he answer. "the ---- coward insult I, sir," he say; and the Doctor pass over the oath, and respect the emotion of the honest suffer young heart.
Pendennis the elder, who like a real man of the world have a proper and constant dread of the opinion of his neighbour, be prodigiously annoy by the absurd little tempest which be blow in Chatteris, and toss about Master Pen's reputation. Doctor Portman and Captain Glanders have to support the charge of the whole Chatteris society against the young reprobate, who be look upon as a monster of crime. Pen do not say anything about the churchyard scuffle at home; but go over to Baymouth, and take counsel with his friend Harry Foker, Esq., who drive over his drag presently to the Clavering Arms, whence he send Stoopid with a note to Thomas Hobnell, Esq., at the Rev. J. Wapshot's, and a civil message to ask when he should wait upon that gentleman.
Stoopid bring back word that the note have be open by Mr. Hobnell, and read to half a dozen of the big boy, on whom it seem to make a great impression; and that after consult together, and laugh, Mr. Hobnell say he would send an answer "arter arternoon school, which the bell be a-ringing: and Mr. Wapshot he come out in his Master's gownd." Stoopid be learn in academical costume, have attend Mr. Foker at St. Boniface.
Mr. Foker go out to see the curiosity of Clavering meanwhile; but not have a taste for architecture, Doctor Portman's fine church do not engage his attention much and he pronounce the tower to be as mouldy as an old Stilton cheese. he walk down the street and look at the few shop there; he see Captain Glanders at the window of the reading-room, and have take a good stare at that gentleman, he wag his head at he in token of satisfaction; he inquire the price of meat at the butcher's with an air of the great interest, and ask "when be next kill day?" he flatten his little nose against Madame Fribsby's window to see if haply there be a pretty workwoman in her premise; but there be no face more comely than the doll's or dummy's wear the french cap in the window, only that of Madame Fribsby herself, dimly visible in the parlour, read a novel. that object be not of sufficient interest to keep Mr. Foker very long in contemplation, and so have exhaust the town and the inn stable, in which there be no cattle, save the single old pair of poster that earn a scanty livelihood by transport the gentry round about to the county dinner, Mr. Foker be give himself up to ennui entirely, when a messenger from Mr. Hobnell be at length announce.
it be no other than Mr. Wapshot himself, who come with an air of great indignation, and hold Pen's missive in his hand, ask Mr. Foker "how dare he bring such an unchristian message as a challenge to a boy of his school?"
in fact Pen have write a note to his adversary of the day before, tell he that if after the chastisement which his insolence richly deserve, he feel inclined to ask the reparation which be usually give amongst gentleman, Mr. Arthur Pendennis's friend, Mr. Henry Foker, be empower to make any arrangement for the satisfaction of Mr. Hobnell.
"and so he send you with the answer--do he, sir?" Mr. Foker say, survey the Schoolmaster in his black coat and clerical costume.
"if he have accept this wicked challenge, I should have flog he," Mr. Wapshot say, and give Mr. Foker a glance which seem to say, "and I should like very much to flog you too."
"Uncommon kind of you, sir, Ibe sure," say Pen's emissary. "I tell my principal that I donot think the other man would fight," he continue with a great air of dignity. "he prefer be flog to fighting, sir, I dare say. may I offer you any refreshment, Mr.? I havenot the advantage of your name."
"my name be Wapshot, sir, and I be Master of the Grammar School of this town, sir," cry the other: "and I want no refreshment, sir, I thank you, and have no desire to make your acquaintance, sir."
"I donot seek your, sir, Ibe sure," reply Mr. Foker. "in affair of this sort, you see, I think it be a pity that the clergy should be call in, but therebe no accounting for taste, sir."
"I think itbe a pity that boy should talk about commit murder, sir, as lightly as you do," roar the Schoolmaster; "and if I have you in my school----"
"I dare say you would teach I well, sir," Mr. Foker say, with a bow. "thank you, sir. Ihave finish my education, sir, and benot a-go back to school, sir--when I do, Iwill remember your kind offer, sir. John, show this gentleman downstairs--and, of course, as Mr. Hobnell like be thrash, we can have no objection, sir, and we shall be very happy to accommodate he, whenever he come our way."
and with this, the young fellow bow the eld gentleman out of the room, and sate down and write a note off to Pen, in which he inform the latter that Mr. Hobnell be not disposed to fight, and propose to put up with the caning which Pen have administer to he.
CHAPTER XVI
More Storms in the Puddle
Pen's conduct in this business of course be soon make public, and anger his friend Doctor Portman not a little: while it only amuse Major Pendennis. as for the good Mrs. Pendennis, she be almost distracted when she hear of the squabble, and of Pen's unchristian behaviour. all sort of wretchedness, discomfort, crime, annoyance, seem to come out of this transaction in which the luckless boy have engage; and she long more than ever to see he out of Chatteris for a while,--anywhere remove from the woman who have bring he into so much trouble.
Pen when remonstrate with by this fond parent, and angrily rebuke by the Doctor for his violence and ferocious intention, take the matter au grand serieux, with the happy conceit and gravity of youth: say that he himself be very sorry for the affair, that the insult have come upon he without the slight provocation on his part; that he would permit no man to insult he upon this head without vindicate his own honour, and appeal with great dignity to his uncle, ask whether he could have act otherwise as a gentleman, than as he do in resent the outrage offer to he, and in offer satisfaction to the person chastise?
"vous allez trop vite, my good sir," say the uncle, rather puzzle, for he have be indoctrinate his nephew with some of his own notion upon the point of honour--old-world notion savour of the camp and pistol a great deal more than our soberer opinion of the present day--"between man of the world I donot say; but between two schoolboy, this sort of thing be ridiculous, my dear boy--perfectly ridiculous."
"it be extremely wicked, and unlike my son," say Mrs. Pendennis, with tear in her eye, and bewilder with the obstinacy of the boy.
Pen kiss she, and say with great pomposity, "woman, dear mother, donot understand these matter--I put myself into Foker's hand--I have no other course to pursue."
Major Pendennis grin and shrug his shoulder. the young one be certainly make great progress, he think. Mrs. Pendennis declare that that Foker be a wicked horrid little wretch, and be sure that he would lead her dear boy into mischief, if Pen go to the same College with he. "I have a great mind not to let he go at all," she say: and only that she remember that the lad's father have always destine he for the College in which he have have his own brief education, very likely the fond mother would have put a veto upon his go to the University.
that he be to go, and at the next October term, have be arrange between all the authority who preside over the lad's welfare. Foker have promise to introduce he to the right set; and Major Pendennis lay great store upon Pen's introduction into College life and society by this admirable young gentleman. "Mr. Foker know the very good young man now at the University," the Major say, "and Pen will form acquaintance there who will be of the great advantage through life to he. the young Marquis of Plinlimmon be there, eld son of the Duke of Saint David's--Lord Magnus Charters be there, Lord Runnymede's son, and a first cousin of Mr. Foker (Lady Runnymede, my dear, be Lady Agatha Milton, you of course remember); Lady Agnes will certainly invite he to Logwood; and far from be alarmed at his intimacy with her son, who be a singular and humorous, but most prudent and amiable young man, to whom, I be sure, we be under every obligation for his admirable conduct in the affair of the Fotheringay marriage, I look upon it as one of the very lucky thing which could have happen to Pen, that he should have form an intimacy with this most amusing young gentleman."
Helen sigh, she suppose the Major know well. Mr. Foker have be very kind in the wretched business with Miss Costigan, certainly, and she be grateful to he. but she could not feel otherwise than a dim presentiment of evil; and all these quarrel, and riot, and worldliness, scar she about the fate of her boy.
Doctor Portman be decidedly of opinion that Pen should go to College. he hop the lad would read, and have a moderate indulgence of the good society too. he be of opinion that Pen would distinguish himself: Smirke speak very highly of his proficiency: the Doctor himself have hear he construe, and think he acquit himself remarkably well. that he should go out of Chatteris be a great point at any rate; and Pen, who be distract from his private grief by the various row and trouble which have rise round about he, gloomily say he would obey.
there be assize, race, and the entertainment and the flux of company consequent upon they, at Chatteris, during a part of the month of August and September, and Miss Fotheringay still continue to act, and take farewell of the audience at the Chatteris Theatre during that time. nobody seem to be particularly affect by her presence, or her announce departure, except those person whom we have name; nor could the polite county folk, who have house in London, and very likely admire the Fotheringay prodigiously in the capital, when they have be teach to do so by the fashion which set in in her favour, find anything remarkable in the actress perform on the little Chatteris board. many genius and many a quack, for that matter, have meet with a similar fate before and since Miss Costigan's time. this honest woman meanwhile bear up against the public neglect, and any other cross or vexation which she might have in life, with her usual equanimity; and eat, drank, act, sleep, with that regularity and comfort which belong to people of her temperament. what a deal of grief, care, and other harmful excitement do a healthy dulness and cheerful insensibility avoid! nor do I mean to say that Virtue be not Virtue because it be never tempt to go astray; only that dulness be a much fine gift than we give it credit for be; and that some people be very lucky whom Nature have endow with a good store of that great anodyne.
Pen use to go drearily in and out from the play at Chatteris during this season, and pretty much accord to his fancy. his proceeding torture his mother not a little, and her anxiety would have lead she often to interfere, have not the Major constantly check, and at the same time encourage she; for the wily man of the world fancy he see that a favourable turn have occur in Pen's malady. it be the violent efflux of versification, among other symptom, which give Pen's guardian and physician satisfaction. he might be hear spout verse in the shrubbery walk, or mutter they between his tooth as he sit with the home party of evening. one day prowl about the house in Pen's absence, the Major find a great book full of verse in the lad's study. they be in English, and in Latin; quotation from the classic author be give in the scholastic manner in the foot-note. he cannot be very bad, wisely think the Pall-Mall Philosopher: and he make Pen's mother remark (not, perhaps, without a secret feeling of disappointment, for she love romance like other soft woman), that the young gentleman during the last fortnight come home quite hungry to dinner at night, and also show a very decent appetite at the breakfast-table in the morning. "Gad, I wish I could," say the Major, think ruefully of his dinner pill. "the boy begin to sleep well, depend upon that." it be cruel, but it be true.
have no other soul to confide in--for he could not speak to his mother of his love and disappointment--his uncle treat they in a scornful and worldly tone, which, though carefully guard and polite, yet jar greatly on the feeling of Mr. Pen--and Foker be much too coarse to appreciate those refined sentimental secret--the lad's friendship for the Curate redouble, or rather, he be never tired of have Smirke for a listener on that one subject. what be a lovee without a confidant? Pen employ Mr. Smirke, as Corydon do the elm-tree, to cut out his mistress's name upon. he make he echo with the name of the beautiful Amaryllis. when man have leave off play the tune, they do not care much for the pipe: but Pen think he have a great friendship for Smirke, because he could sigh out his love and grief into his tutor's ear; and Smirke have his own reason for always be ready at the lad's call.
Pen's affection gush out in a multitude of sonnet to the friend of his heart, as he style the Curate, which the other receive with great sympathy. he ply Smirke with Latin Sapphics and alcaic. the love-song multiply under his fluent pen; and Smirke declare and believe that they be beautiful. on the other hand, Pen express a boundless gratitude to think that Heaven should have send he such a friend at such a moment. he present his tutor with his well-bind book, and his gold guard-chain, and want he to take his double-barrel gun. he go into Chatteris and get a gold pencil-case on credit (for he have no money, and indeed be still in debt to Smirke for some of the Fotheringay present), which he present to Smirke, with an inscription indicative of his unalterable and eternal regard for the Curate; who of course be pleased with every mark of the boy's attachment.
the poor Curate be naturally very much dismay at the contemplated departure of his pupil. when Arthur should go, Smirke's occupation and delight would go too. what pretext could he find for a daily visit to Fairoaks and that kind word or glance from the lady there, which be as necessary to the Curate as the frugal dinner which Madame Fribsby serve he? Arthur gone, he would only be allow to make visit like any other acquaintance: little Laura could not accommodate he by learn the Catechism more than once a week: he have curl himself like ivy round Fairoaks: he pin at the thought that he must lose his hold of the place. should he speak his mind and go down on his knee to the widow? he think over any indication in her behaviour which flatter his hope. she have praise his sermon three week before: she have thank he exceedingly for his present of a melon, for a small dinner-party which Mrs. Pendennis give: she say she should always be grateful to he for his kindness to Arthur, and when he declare that there be no bound to his love and affection for that dear boy, she have certainly reply in a romantic manner, indicate her own strong gratitude and regard to all her son's friend. should he speak out?--or should he delay? if be speak and she refuse he, it be awful to think that the gate of Fairoaks might be shut upon he for ever--and within that door lie all the world for Mr. Smirke.
thus, oh friendly reader, we see how every man in the world have his own private grief and business, by which he be more cast down or occupy than by the affair or sorrow of any other person. while Mrs. Pendennis be disquiet herself about lose her son, and that anxious hold she have have of he, as long as he have remain in the mother's nest, whence he be about to take flight into the great world beyond--while the Major's great soul chafe and fret, inwardly vex as he think what great party be go on in London, and that he might be sun himself in the glance of Dukes and Duchesses, but for those cursed affair which keep he in a wretched little country hole--while Pen be toss between his passion and a more agreeable sensation, unacknowledged yet, but sway he considerably, namely, his longing to see the world--Mr. Smirke have a private care watch at his bedside, and sit behind he on his pony; and be no more satisfied than the rest of we. how lonely we be in the world; how selfish and secret, everybody! you and your wife have press the same pillow for forty year and fancy yourself unite. Psha, do she cry out when you have the gout, or do you lie awake when she have the toothache? your artless daughter, seemingly all innocence and devote to her mamma and her piano-lesson, be think of neither, but of the young Lieutenant with whom she dance at the last ball--the honest frank boy just return from school be secretly speculate upon the money you will give he, and the debt he owe the tart-man. the old grandmother croon in the corner and bind to another world within a few month, have some business or care which be quite private and her own--very likely she be think of fifty year back, and that night when she make such an impression, and dance a cotillon with the Captain before your father propose for she: or, what a silly little overrated creature your wife be, and how absurdly you be infatuate about her-- and, as for your wife--o philosophic reader, answer and say,--do you tell she all? ah, sir--a distinct universe walk about under your hat and under mine--all thing in nature be different to each--the woman we look at have not the same feature, the dish we eat from have not the same taste to the one and the other--you and I be but a pair of infinite isolation, with some fellow-island a little more or less near to we. let we return, however, to the solitary Smirke.
Smirke have one confidante for his passion--that most injudicious woman, Madame Fribsby. how she become Madame Fribsby, nobody know: she have leave Clavering to go to a milliner's in London as Miss Fribsby--she pretend that she have get the rank in Paris during her residence in that city. but how could the french king, be he ever so much disposed, give she any such title? we shall not inquire into this mystery, however. suffice to say, she go away from home a bounce young lass; she return a rather elderly character, with a Madonna front and a melancholy countenance-- buy the late Mrs. Harbottle's business for a song--take her elderly mother to live with she; be very good to the poor, be constant at church, and have the good of character. but there be no one in all Clavering, not Mrs. Portman herself, who read so many novel as Madame Fribsby. she have plenty of time for this amusement, for, in truth, very few people besides the folk at the Rectory and Fairoaks employ she; and by a perpetual perusal of such work (which be by no mean so moral or edify in the day of which we write, as they be at present) she have get to be so absurdly sentimental, that in her eye life be nothing but an immense love-match; and she never could see two people together, but she fancy they be die for one another.
on the day after Mrs. Pendennis's visit to the Curate, which we have record many page back, Madame Fribsby settle in her mind that Mr. Smirke must be in love with the widow, and do everything in her power to encourage this passion on both side. Mrs. Pendennis she very seldom see, indeed, except in public, and in her pew at church. that lady have very little need of millinery, or make most of her own dress and cap; but on the rare occasion when Madame Fribsby receive visit from Mrs. Pendennis or pay her respect at Fairoaks, she never fail to entertain the widow with praise of the curate, point out what an angelical man he be, how gentle, how studious, how lonely; and she would wonder that no lady would take pity upon he.
Helen laugh at these sentimental remark, and wonder that Madame herself do not compassionate her lodger, and console he. Madame Fribsby shake her Madonna front, "Mong cure a boco souffare," she say, lay her hand on the part she designate as her cure. "it est more en Espang, Madame," she say with a sigh. she be proud of her intimacy with the french language, and speak it with more volubility than correctness. Mrs. Pendennis do not care to penetrate the secret of this wound heart: except to her few intimate she be a reserved and it may be a very proud woman; she look upon her son's tutor merely as an attendant on that young Prince, to be treat with respect as a clergyman certainly, but with proper dignity as a dependant on the house of Pendennis. nor be Madame's constant allusion to the curate particularly agreeable to she. it require a very ingenious sentimental turn indeed to find out that the widow have a secret regard for Mr. Smirke, to which pernicious error however Madame Fribsby persist in hold.
her lodger be very much more willing to talk on this subject with his soft-hearted landlady. every time after that she praise the curate to Mrs. Pendennis, she come away from the latter with the notion that the widow herself have be praise he. "etre soul au monde est bien ouneeyoung," she would say, glance up at a print of a french carbineer in a green coat and brass cuirass which decorate she apartment--"depend upon it when Master Pendennis go to College, his Ma will find herself very lonely. she be quite young yet.--you wouldnot suppose she to be five-and-twenty. Monsieur le Cury, song cure est touchy--j'ang suis sure --Je conny cela biang--Ally Monsieur Smirke."
he softly blush; he sigh; he hop; he fear; he doubt; he sometimes yield to the delightful idea--his pleasure be to sit in Madame Fribsby's apartment, and talk upon the subject, where, as the great part of the conversation be carry on in French by the Milliner, and her old mother be deaf, that retire old individual (who have once be a housekeeper, wife and widow of a butler in the Clavering family) could understand scarce one syllable of their talk.
thus it be, that when Major Pendennis announce to his nephew's tutor that the young fellow would go to College in October, and that Mr. Smirke's valuable service would no long be needful to his pupil, for which service the Major, who speak as grandly as a lord, profess himself exceedingly grateful, and besought Mr. Smirke to command his interest in any way--thus it be, that the Curate feel that the critical moment be come for he, and be rack and torture by those severe pang which the occasion warrant.
Madame Fribsby have, of course, take the strong interest in the progress of Mr. Pen's love affair with Miss Fotheringay. she have be over to Chatteris, and have see that actress perform, have pronounce that she be old and overrate: and have talk over Master Pen's passion in her shop many and many a time to the half-dozen old maid, and old woman in male clothe, who be to be find in little country town, and who form the genteel population of Clavering. Captain Glanders, H.P., have pronounce that Pen be go to be a devil of a fellow, and have begin early: Mrs. Glanders have tell he to check his horrid observation, and to respect his own wife, if he please. she say it would be a lesson to Helen for her pride and absurd infatuation about that boy. Mrs. Pybus say many people be proud of very small thing, and for her part, she donot know why an apothecary's wife should give herself such air. Mrs. Wapshot call her daughter away from that side of the street, one day when Pen, on Rebecca, be stop at the saddler's, to get a new lash to his whip--one and all of these people have make visit of curiosity to Fairoaks, and have try to condole with the widow, or bring the subject of the Fotheringay affair on the tapis, and have be severally check by the haughty reserve of Mrs. Pendennis, support by the frigid politeness of the Major her brother.
these rebuff, however, do not put an end to the gossip, and slander go on increase about the unlucky Fairoaks' family. glander (H.P.), a retire cavalry officer, whose half-pay and large family compel he to fuddle himself with brandy-and-water instead of claret after he quit the Dragoons, have the occasional entree at Fairoaks, and keep his friend the Major there inform of all the story which be current at Clavering. Mrs. Pybus have take an inside place by the coach to Chatteris, and go to the George on purpose to get the particular. Mrs. Speers's man, have treat Mr. Foker's servant to drink at Baymouth for a similar purpose. it be say that Pen have hang himself for despair in the orchard, and that his uncle have cut he down; that, on the contrary, it be Miss Costigan who be jilt, and not young Arthur; and that the affair have only be hush up by the payment of a large sum of money, the exact amount of which there be several people in Clavering could testify--the sum of course vary accord to the calculation of the individual narrator of the story.
Pen shake his mane and rag like a furious lion when these scandal, affect Miss Costigan's honour and his own, come to his ear. why be not Pybus a man (she have whisker enough), that he might call she out and shoot she? see Simcoe pass by, Pen glare at he so from his saddle on Rebecca, and clutch his whip in a manner so menacing, that that clergyman go home and write a sermon, or think over a sermon (for he deliver oral testimony at great length), in which he speak of Jezebel, theatrical entertainment (a double cut this--for Doctor Portman, the Rector of the old church, be know to frequent such), and of youth go to perdition, in a manner which make it clear to every capacity that Pen be the individual meant, and on the road allude to. what story more be there not against young Pendennis, whilst he sate sulk, Achilles-like in his tent, for the loss of his ravished Briseis?
after the affair with Hobnell, Pen be pronounce to be a murderer as well as a profligate, and his name become a name of terror and a byword in Clavering. but this be not all; he be not the only one of the family about whom the village begin to chatter, and his unlucky mother be the next to become a victim to their gossip.
"it be all settle," say Mrs. Pybus to Mrs. Speers, "the boy be to go to College, and then the widow be to console herself."
"hebe be there every day, in the most open manner, my dear," continue Mrs. Speers.
"enough to make poor Mr. Pendennis turn in his grave," say Mrs. Wapshot.
"she never like he, that we know," say No. 1.
"marry he for his money. everybody know that: be a penniless hanger-on of Lady Pontypool's," say no. 2.
"itbe rather too open, though, to encourage a lover under pretence of have a tutor for your son," cry no. 3.
"Hush! here come Mrs. Portman," some one say, as the good Rector's wife enter Madame Fribsby's shop, to inspect her monthly book of fashion just arrive from London. and the fact be that Madame Fribsby have be able to hold out no long; and one day, after she and her lodger have be talk of Pen's approach departure, and the Curate have go off to give one of his last lesson to that gentleman, Madame Fribsby have communicate to Mrs. Pybus, who happen to step in with Mrs. Speers, her strong suspicion, her certainty almost, that there be an attachment between a certain clerical gentleman and a certain lady, whose naughty son be grow quite unmanageable, and that a certain marriage would take place pretty soon.
Mrs. Portman see it all, of course, when the matter be mention. what a sly fox that Curate be! he be low-church, and she never like he. and to think of Mrs. Pendennis take a fancy to he after she have be marry to such a man as Mr. Pendennis! she could hardly stay five minute at Madame Fribsby's, so eager be she to run to the Rectory and give Doctor Portman the news.
when Doctor Portman hear this piece of intelligence, he be in such a rage with his curate, that his first movement be to break with Mr. Smirke, and to beg he to transfer his service to some other parish. "that milksop of a creature pretend to be worthy of such a woman as Mrs. Pendennis," break out the doctor: "where will impudence stop next!"
"she be much too old for Mr. Smirke," Mrs. Portman remark: "why, poor dear Mrs. Pendennis might be his mother almost."
"you always choose the most charitable reason, Betsy," cry the Rector. "a matron with a son grow up--she would never think of marry again."
"you only think man should marry again, Doctor Portman, answer his lady, bridle up.
"you stupid old woman," say the Doctor, "when I be go, you shall marry whomsoever you like. I will leave order in my will, my dear, to that effect: and Iwill bequeath a ring to my successor, and my Ghost shall come and dance at your wedding."
"it be cruel for a clergyman to talk so," the lady answer, with a ready whimper: but these little breeze use to pass very rapidly over the surface of the Doctor's domestic bliss; and be follow by a great calm and sunshine. the Doctor adopt a plan for soothe Mrs. Portman's ruffled countenance, which have a great effect when it be try between a worthy couple who be sincerely fond of one another; and which, I think, become 'John Anderson' at three-score, just as much as it use to do when he be a black-haired young Jo of five-and-twenty.
"hadnot you well speak to Mr. Smirke, John?" Mrs Portman ask.
"when Pen go to College, cadit quaestio," reply the Rector, "Smirke's visit at Fairoaks will cease of themselves, and there will be no need to bother the widow. she have trouble enough on her hand, with the affair of that silly young scapegrace, without be pester by the tittle-tattle of this place. it be all an invention of that fool, Fribsby."
"against whom I always warn you,--you know I do, my dear John," interpose Mrs. Portman.
"that you do; you very often do, my love," the Doctor answer with a laugh. "it be not for want of warning on your part, I be sure, that I have form my opinion of most woman with whom we be acquaint. Madame Fribsby be a fool, and fond of gossip, and so be some other folk. but she be good to the poor: she take care of her mother, and she come to church twice every Sunday. and as for Smirke, my dear----" here the Doctor's face assume for one moment a comical expression, which Mrs. Portman do not perceive (for she be look out of the drawing-room window, and wonder what Mrs. Pybus could want cheapen fowl again in the market, when she have bad poultry from Livermore's two day before)--"and as for Mr. Smirke, my dear Betsy, will you promise I that you will never breathe to any mortal what I be go to tell you as a profound secret?"
"what be it, my dear john!--of course I willnot," answer the Rector's lady.
"well, then--I cannot say it be a fact, mind--but if you find that Smirke be at this moment--ay, and have be for year--engage to a young lady, a Miss--a Miss Thompson, if you will have the name, who live on Clapham Common--yes, on Clapham Common, not far from Mrs. Smirke's house, what become of your story then about Smirke and Mrs. Pendennis?"
"why do you not tell I this before?" ask the Doctor's wife.--"how long have you know it?--how we all of we have be deceive in that man!"
"why should I meddle in other folk' business, my dear?" the Doctor answer. "I know how to keep a secret--and perhaps this be only an invention like that other absurd story; at least, Madame Portman, I should never have tell you this but for the other, which I beg you to contradict whenever you hear it." and so say the doctor go away to his study, and Mrs. Portman see that the day be a remarkably fine one, think she would take advantage of the weather and pay a few visit.
the Doctor look out of his study window see the wife of his bosom presently issue forth, attire in her good. she cross the market-place, salute the market-woman right and leave, and give a glance at the grocery and general emporium at the corner: then enter London Street (formerly Hog Lane), she stop for a minute at Madame Fribsby's window, and look at the fashion which hang up there,--seeme hesitate whether she should enter; but she pass on and never stop again until she come to Mrs. Pybus's little green gate and garden, through which she go to that lady's cottage.
there, of course, her husband lose sight of Mrs. Portman. "oh, what a long bow I have pull," he say inwardly--"goodness forgive I! and shoot my own flesh and blood. there must be no more tattling and scandal about that house. I must stop it, and speak to Smirke. Iwill ask he to dinner this very day."
have a sermon to compose, the Doctor sit down to that work, and be so engaged in the composition, that he have not conclude it until near five o'clock in the afternoon: when he step over to Mr. Smirke's lodging, to put his hospitable intention, regard that gentleman, into effect. he reach Madame Fribsby's door, just as the Curate issue from it.
Mr. Smirke be magnificently dress, and as he turn out his toe, he show a pair of elegant open-work silk stocking and glossy pump. his white cravat be arrange in a splendid stiff tie, and his gold shirt stud shine on his spotless linen. his hair be curl round his fair temple. have he borrow Madame Fribsby's iron to give that curly grace? his white cambric pocket-handkerchief be scented with the most delicious eau-de-Cologne.
"o gracilis puer,"--crie the Doctor.--"Whither be you bind? I want you to come home to dinner."
"I be engage to dine at--at Fairoaks," say Mr. Smirke, blush faintly and whisk the scented pocket-handkerchief, and his pony be in wait, he mount and ride away simper down the street. no accident befall he that day, and he arrive with his tie in the very good order at Mrs. Pendennis's house.
CHAPTER XVII
which conclude the first part of this history
the Curate have go on his daily errand to Fairoaks, and be upstairs in Pens study pretend to read with his pupil, in the early part of that very afternoon when Mrs. Portman, after transact business with Mrs. Pybus, have find the weather so exceedingly fine that she pursue her walk as far as Fairoaks, in order to pay a visit to her dear friend there. in the course of their conversation, the Rector's lady tell Mrs. Pendennis and the Major a very great secret about the Curate, Mr. Smirke, which be no less than that he have an attachment, a very old attachment, which he have long keep quite private.
"and on whom be it that Mr. Smirke have bestow his heart?" ask Mrs. Pendennis, with a superb air but rather an inward alarm.
"why, my dear," the other lady answer, "when he first come and use to dine at the Rectory, people say we want he for Myra, and we be force to give up ask he. then they use to say he be smite in another quarter; but I always contradict it for my part, and say that you----"
"that I," cry Mrs. Pendennis; "people be very impertinent, I be sure. Mr. Smirke come here as Arthur's tutor, and I be surprised that anybody should dare to speak so----"
"'pon my soul, it be a little too much," the Major say, lay down the newspaper and the double eye-glass.
"Ihave no patience with that Mrs. Pybus," Helen continue indignantly.
"I tell she there be no truth in it," Mrs. Portman say. "I always say so, my dear: and now it come out that my demure gentleman have be engage to a young lady--Miss Thompson, of Clapham Common, ever so long: and I be delighted for my part, and on Myra's account, too, for an unmarried curate be always objectionable about one's house: and of course it be strictly private, but I think I would tell you, as it might remove unpleasantness. but mind: not one word, if you please, about the story."
Mrs. Pendennis say, with perfect sincerity, that she be exceedingly glad to hear the news: and hop Mr. Smirke, who be a very kind and amiable man, would have a deserve wife: and when her visitor go away, Helen and her brother talk of the matter with great satisfaction, the kind lady rebuke herself for her haughty behaviour to Mr. Smirke, whom she have avoid of late, instead of be grateful to he for his constant attention to Arthur.
"gratitude to this kind of people," the Major say, "be very well; but familiarity be out of the question. this gentleman give his lesson and receive his money like any other master. you be too humble, my good soul. there must be distinction in rank, and that sort of thing. I tell you before, you be too kind to Mr. Smirke."
but Helen do not think so: and now that Arthur be go away, and she bethink she how very polite Mr. Smirke have be; how he have go on message for she; how he have bring book and copy music; how he have teach Laura so many thing, and give she so many kind present, her heart smite she on account of her ingratitude towards the Curate;--so much so, that when he come down from study with Pen, and be hanker about the hall previous to his departure, she go out and shake hand with he with rather a blushing face, and beg he to come into her drawing-room, where she say they now never see he. and as there be to be rather a good dinner that day, she invite Mr. Smirke to partake of it; and we may be sure that he be too happy to accept such a delightful summon.
ease, by the above report, of all her former doubt and misgiving regard the Curate, Helen be exceedingly kind and gracious to Mr. Smirke during dinner, redouble her attention, perhaps, because Major Pendennis be very high and reserve with his nephew's tutor. when Pendennis ask Smirke to drink wine, he address he as if he be a Sovereign speaking to a petty retainer, in a manner so condescend, that even Pen laugh at it, although quite ready, for his part, to be as conceited as most young man be.
but Smirke do not care for the impertinence of the Major so long as he have his hostess's kind behaviour; and he pass a delightful time by her side at table, exert all his power of conversation to please she, talk in a manner both clerical and worldly, about the Fancy Bazaar, and the Great Missionary Meeting, about the last new novel, and the Bishop's excellent sermon about the fashionable party in London, an account of which he read in the newspaper--in fine, he neglect no art, by which a College divine who have both sprightly and serious talent, a taste for the genteel, an irreproachable conduct, and a susceptible heart, will try and make himself agreeable to the person on whom he have fix his affection.
Major Pendennis come yawn out of the dining-room very soon after his sister and little Laura have leave the apartment. "what an unsufferable bore that man be, and how he do talk!" the Major say.
"he have be very good to Arthur, who be very fond of he," Mrs. Pendennis said,--"i wonder who the Miss Thompson be whom he be go to marry?"
"I always think the fellow be look in another direction," say the Major.
"and in what?" ask Mrs. Pendennis quite innocently,--"towards Myra Portman?"
"towards Helen Pendennis, if you must know," answer her brother-in-law.
"towards I! impossible!" Helen say, who know perfectly well that such have be the case. "his marriage will be a very happy thing. I hope Arthur will not take too much wine."
now Arthur, flush with a good deal of pride at the privilege of have the key of the cellar, and remember that a very few more dinner would probably take place which he and his dear friend Smirke could share, have bring up a liberal supply of claret for the company's drinking, and when the elder with little Laura leave he, he and the Curate begin to pass the wine very freely.
one bottle speedily yield up the ghost, another shed more than half its blood, before the two toper have be much more than half an hour together--Pen, with a hollow laugh and voice, have drink off one bumper to the falsehood of woman, and have say sardonically, that wine at any rate be a mistress who never deceive, and be sure to give a man a welcome.
Smirke gently say that he know for his part some woman who be all truth and tenderness; and cast up his eye towards the ceiling, and heave a sigh as if evoke some be dear and unmentionable, he take up his glass and drain it, and the rosy liquor begin to suffuse his face.
Pen troll over some verse he have be make that morning, in which he inform himself that the woman who have slight his passion could not be worthy to win it: that he be awake from love's mad fever, and, of course, under these circumstance, proceed to leave she, and to quit a heartless deceiver: that a name which have one day be famous in the land, might again be hear in it: and, that though he never should be the happy and careless boy he be but a few month since, or his heart be what it have be ere passion have fill it and grief have well-nigh kill it; that though to he personally death be as welcome as life, and that he would not hesitate to part with the latter, but for the love of one kind be whose happiness depend on his own,--yet he hop to show he be a man worthy of his race, and that one day the false one should be bring to know how great be the treasure and noble the heart which she have fling away.
Pen, we say, who be a very excitable person, roll out these verse in his rich sweet voice, which tremble with emotion whilst our young poet speak. he have a trick of blushing when in this excited state, and his large and honest grey eye also exhibit proof of a sensibility so genuine, hearty, and manly, that Miss Costigan, if she have a heart, must need have soften towards he; and very likely she be, as he say, altogether unworthy of the affection which he lavish upon she.
the sentimental Smirke be catch by the emotion which agitate his young friend. he grasp Pen's hand over the dessert dish and wine-glass. he say the verse be beautiful: that Pen be a poet, a great poet, and likely by Heaven's permission to run a great career in the world. "go on and prosper, dear Arthur," he cry; "the wound under which at present you suffer be only temporary, and the very grief you endure will cleanse and strengthen your heart. I have always prophesy the great and bright thing of you, as soon as you have correct some failing and weakness of character, which at present belong to you. but you will get over these, my boy; you will get over these; and when you be famous and celebrate, as I know you will be, will you remember your old tutor and the happy early day of your youth?"
Pen swear he would: with another shake of the hand across the glass and apricot. "I shall never forget how kind you have be to I, Smirke," he say. "I donot know what I should have do without you. you be my good friend."
"be I, really, Arthur?" say Smirke, look through his spectacle; and his heart begin to beat so that he think Pen must almost hear it throb.
"my good friend, my friend for ever," Pen say. Hurray!--claret go for nothing. my uncle be tell I that he see Sheridan drink five bottle at Brookes's, besides a bottle of Maraschino. this be some of the fine wine in England, he say. so it be, by Jove. therebe nothing like it. nunc vino pellite curas--cra ingen iterabimus aeq,--fill your glass, Old Smirke, a hogshead of it willnot do you any harm." and Mr. Pen begin to sing the drinking song out of Der Freischuetz. the dining-room window be open, and his mother be softly pace on the lawn outside, while little Laura be look at the sunset. the sweet fresh note of the boy's voice come to the widow. it cheer her kind heart to hear he sing.

"oh!" say Smirke--and his beautiful cambric shirt front and glisten stud heave with the emotion which agitate his gentle and suffer bosom.

"do you say so?" Smirke say, all of a tremble. "do you really say so, Arthur?"
"say so; of course, I say so. down with it. here's Mrs. Smirke's good health: hip, hip, hurray!"
Smirke convulsively gulp down his glass of wine, and Pen wave his over his head, cheer so as to make his mother and Laura wonder on the lawn, and his uncle, who be doze over the paper in the drawing-room, start, and say to himself, "that boybe drink too much." Smirke put down the glass.

Pen burst out laugh. "itbe Madame Fribsby; by Jove, itbe Madame Fribsby. Madame Frib. by the immortal Gods!"
the Curate could contain no more. I may be insane, I may be madly ambitious, I may be presumptuous--but for two year my heart have be fill by one image, and have know no other idol. havenot I love you as a son, Arthur?--say, havenot Charles Smirke love you as a son?"

"what the deuce do you mean?--and what have I to do with your money?" cry out Pen, in a puzzle.

"do you find that any objection?" cry Smirke piteously, and allude, of course, to the elderly subject of Pen's own passion.
the lad feel the hint, and blush quite red. "the case be not similar, Smirke," he say, "and the allusion might have be spar. a man may forget his own rank and elevate any woman to it: but allow I to say our position be very different."
"how do you mean, dear Arthur?" the Curate interpose sadly, cower as he feel that his sentence be about to be read.

before the great Mr. Dolphin quit Chatteris, he not only make an advantageous engagement with Miss Fotheringay, but he liberally leave with she a sum of money to pay off any debt which the little family might have contract during their stay in the place, and which, mainly through the lady's own economy and management, be not considerable. the small account with the spirit merchant, which Major Pendennis have settle, be the chief of Captain Costigan's debt, and though the Captain at one time talk about repay every farthing of the money, it never appear that he execute his menace, nor do the law of honour in the least call upon he to accomplish that threat.
when Miss Costigan have see all the outstanding bill pay to the uttermost shilling, she hand over the balance to her father, who break out into hospitality to all his friend, give the little Creeds more apple and gingerbread than he have ever bestow upon they, so that the widow Creed ever after hold the memory of her lodger in veneration, and the young one weep bitterly when he go away; and in a word manage the money so cleverly that it be entirely expend before many day, and that he be compel to draw upon Mr. Dolphin for a sum to pay for travel expense when the time of their departure arrive.
there be hold at an inn in that county town a weekly meeting of a festive, almost a riotous character, of a society of gentleman who call themselves the buccaneer. some of the choice spirit of Chatteris belong to this cheerful club. graf, the apothecary (than whom a well fellow never put a pipe in his mouth and smoke it), Smart, the talented and humorous portrait-painter of High Street, Croker, an excellent auctioneer, and the uncompromise Hicks, the able Editor for twenty-three year of the County Chronicle and Chatteris Champion, be amongst the crew of the buccaneer, whom also Bingley, the manager, like to join of a Saturday evening, whenever he receive permission from his lady.
Costigan have be also an occasional buccaneer. but a want of punctuality of payment have of late somewhat exclude he from the Society, where he be subject to disagreeable remark from the landlord, who say that a Buccaneer who donot pay his shot be utterly unworthy to be a Marine Bandit. but when it become know to the 'ear, as the Clubbists call themselves familiarly, that Miss Fotheringay have make a splendid engagement, a great revolution of feeling take place in the Club regard Captain Costigan. solly, mine host of the grape (and I need not say, as worthy a fellow as ever stand behind a bar), tell the gent in the buccaneer' room one night how noble the Captain have behave; have be round and pay off all his tick in Chatteris, include his score of three pound fourteen here--and pronounce that Cos be a good feller, a gentleman at bottom, and he, solly, have always say so, and finally work upon the feeling of the buccaneer to give the Captain a dinner.
the banquet take place on the last night of Costigan's stay at Chatteris, and be serve in Solly's accustomed manner. as good a plain dinner of old english fare as ever smoke on a table be prepare by Mrs. Solly; and about eighteen gentleman sate down to the festive board. Mr. Jubber (the eminent draper of High Street) be in the Chair, have the distinguished guest of the Club on his right. the able and consistent Hicks officiate as croupier on the occasion; most of the gentleman of the Club be present, and H. Foker, Esq., and Spavin, Esq., friend of Captain Costigan, be also participator in the entertainment. the cloth have be draw, the Chairman say, "Costigan, there be wine, if you like," but the Captain prefer punch, that liquor be vote by acclamation: and 'Non Nobis' have be sing in admirable style by Messrs. Bingley, Hicks, and Bullby (of the Cathedral choir, than whom a more jovial spirit "ne'er toss off a bumper or empty a bowl"), the Chairman give the health of the 'king!' which be drunk with the loyalty of Chatteris man, and then without further circumlocution propose their friend 'Captain Costigan.'
after the enthusiastic cheering which ring through old Chatteris have subside, Captain Costigan rise in reply, and make a speech of twenty minute, in which he be repeatedly overcome by his emotion.
the gallant Captain say he must be pardon for incoherence, if his heart be too full to speak. he be quit a city celebrate for its antiquitee, its hospitalitee, the beautee of its woman, the manly fidelitee, generositee, and jovialitee of its man. (Cheers.) he be go from that ancient and venerable city, of which while Mimoree hold her sayt, he should never think without the fond emotion, to a methrawpoli where the talent of his daughther be about to have full play, and where he would watch over she like a guardian angel. he should never forget that it be at Chatteris she have acquire the skill which she be about to exercise in another sphere, and in her name and his own Jack Costigan thank and bless they. the gallant officer's speech be receive with tremendous cheer.
Mr. Hicks, Croupier, in a brilliant and energetic manner, propose Miss Fotheringay's health.
Captain Costigan return thank in a speech full of feeling and eloquence.
Mr. Jubber propose the Drama and the Chatteris Theatre, and Mr. Bingley be about to rise but be prevent by Captain Costigan, who, as long connect with the Chatteris Theatre and on behalf of his daughter, thank the company. he inform they that he have be in garrison, at Gibraltar, and at Malta, and have be at the taking of flushing. the Duke of York be a patron of the Drama; he have the honour of dining with his Royal Highness and the Duke of Kent many time; and the former have justly be name the friend of the soldier. (Cheers.)
the Army be then propose, and Captain Costigan return thank. in the course of the night he sing his well-know song, 'the deserter,' 'the Shan Van Voght,' 'the Little Pig under the Bed,' and 'the Vale of Avoca.' the evening be a great triumph for he--it end. all triumph and all evening end. and the next day, Miss Costigan have take leave of all her friend, have be reconcile to Miss Rouncy, to whom she leave a necklace and a white satin gown--the next day, he and Miss Costigan have place in the Competitor coach roll by the gate of Fairoaks Lodge-- and Pendennis never see they.
Tom Smith, the coachman, point out Fairoaks to Mr. Costigan, who sate on the box smell of rum-and-water--and the Captain say it be a poor place--and add, "Ye should see Castle Costigan, County Mayo, I boy,"-- which Tom say he should like very much to see.
they be go and Pen have never see they! he only know of their departure by its announcement in the county paper the next day: and straight gallop over to Chatteris to hear the truth of this news. they be go indeed. a card of 'lodging to let' be place in the dear little familiar window. he rush up into the room and view it over. he sate ever so long in the old window-seat look into the Dean's garden: whence he and Emily have so often look out together. he walk, with a sort of terror, into her little empty bedroom. it be sweep out and prepare for new-comer. the glass which have reflect her fair face be shin ready for her successor. the curtain lie square fold on the little bed: he fling himself down and bury his head on the vacant pillow.
