Laura have net a purse into which his mother have put some sovereign, and Pen have find it on his dressing-table that very morning. he give one to the little servant who have be use to wait upon the Costigans, and another to the child, because they say they be very fond of she. it be but a few month back, yet what year ago it seem since he have first enter that room! he feel that it be all do. the very miss she at the coach have something fatal in it. Blank, weary, utterly wretched and lonely the poor lad feel.
his mother see she be go by his look when he come home. he be eager to fly too now, as be other folk round about Chatteris. Poor Smirke want to go away from the sight of the syren widow. Foker begin to think he have have enough of Baymouth, and that a few supper-party at Saint Boniface would not be unpleasant. and Major Pendennis long to be off, and have a little pheasant-shooting at Stillbrook, and get rid of all annoyance and tracasserie of the village. the widow and Laura nervously set about the preparation for Pen's kit, and fill trunk with his book and linen. Helen write card with the name of Arthur Pendennis, Esq., which be duly nail on the box; and at which both she and Laura look with tearful wistful eye. it be not until long, long after he be go, that Pen remember how constant and tender the affection of these woman have be, and how selfish his own conduct be.
a night soon come, when the mail, with echo horn and blaze lamp, stop at the lodge-gate of Fairoaks, and Pen's trunk and his uncle's be place on the roof of the carriage, into which the pair presently afterwards enter. Helen and Laura be stand by the evergreen of the shrubbery, their figure light up by the coach lamp; the guard cry all right: in another instant the carriage whirl onward; the light disappear, and Helen's heart and prayer go with they. her sainted benediction follow the depart boy. he have leave the home-nest in which he have be chafing, and whither, after his very first flight, he return bleed and wound; he be eager to go forth again, and try his restless wing.
how lonely the house look without he! the cord trunk and book-box be there in his empty study. Laura ask leave to come and sleep in Helen's room: and when she have cry herself to sleep there, the mother go softly into Pen's vacant chamber, and kneel down by the bed on which the moon be shin, and there pray for her boy, as mother only know how to plead. he know that her pure blessing be follow he, as he be carry mile away.
CHAPTER xviii
Alma Mater
every man, however brief or inglorious may have be his academical career, must remember with kindness and tenderness the old university comrade and day. the young man's life be just begin: the boy's lead-string be cut, and he have all the novel delight and dignity of freedom. he have no idea of care yet, or of bad health, or of roguery, or poverty, or to-morrow's disappointment. the play have not be act so often as to make he tired. though the after drink, as we mechanically go on repeat it, be stale and bitter, how pure and brilliant be that first sparkling draught of pleasure!--how the boy rush at the cup, and with what a wild eagerness he drain it! but old epicure who be cut off from the delight of the table, and be restrict to a poached egg and a glass of water, like to see people with good appetite; and, as the next good thing to be amuse at a pantomime one's-self be to see one's child enjoy it, I hope there may be no degree of age or experience to which mortal may attain, when he shall become such a glum philosopher as not to be please by the sight of happy youth. come back a few week since from a brief visit to the old University of Oxbridge, where my friend Mr. Arthur Pendennis pass some period of his life, I make the journey in the railroad by the side of a young fellow at present a student of Saint Boniface. he have get an exeat somehow, and be bent on a day's lark in London: he never stop rattle and talk from the commencement of the journey until its close (which be a great deal too soon for I, for I never be tired of listen to the honest young fellow's joke and cheery laughter); and when we arrive at the terminus nothing would satisfy he but a hansom cab, so that he might get into town the quick, and plunge into the pleasure await he there. away the young lad go whirling, with joy light up his honest face; and as for the reader's humble servant, have but a small carpet-bag, I get up on the outside of the omnibus, and sate there very contentedly between a Jew-pedlar smoke bad cigar, and a gentleman's servant take care of a poodle-dog, until we get our fat complement of passenger and box, when the coachman drive leisurely away. we benot in a hurry to get to town. neither one of we be particularly eager about rush into that near smoke Babylon, or thought of dining at the Club that night, or dance at the Casino. yet a few year more, and my young friend of the railroad will be not a whit more eager.
there be no railroad make when Arthur Pendennis go to the famous University of Oxbridge; but he drive thither in a well-appoint coach, fill inside and out with don, gownsman, young freshman about to enter, and their guardian, who be conduct they to the university. a fat old gentleman, in grey stocking, from the City, who sate by Major Pendennis inside the coach, have his pale-face son opposite, be frighten beyond measure when he hear that the coach have be drive for a couple of stage by young Mr. Foker, of Saint Boniface College, who be the friend of all man, include coachman, and could drive as well as Tom Hicks himself. Pen sate on the roof, examine coach, passenger, and country with great delight and curiosity. his heart jump with pleasure as the famous university come in view, and the magnificent prospect of venerable tower and pinnacle, tall elm and shin river, spread before he.
Pen have pass a few day with his uncle at the Major's lodging, in Bury Street, before they set out for Oxbridge. Major Pendennis think that the lad's wardrobe want renewal; and Arthur be by no mean averse to any plan which be to bring he new coat and waistcoat. there be no end to the sacrifice which the self-deny uncle make in the youth's behalf. London be awfully lonely. the Pall Mall pavement be desert; the very red jacket have go out of town. there be scarce a face to be see in the bow-window of the club. the Major conduct his nephew into one or two of those desert mansion, and write down the lad's name on the candidate-list of one of they; and Arthur's pleasure at this compliment on his guardian's part be excessive. he read in the parchment volume his name and title, as 'Arthur Pendennis, Esquire, of Fairoaks Lodge, ----shire and Saint Boniface College, Oxbridge; propose by Major Pendennis, and second by Viscount Colchicum,' with a thrill of intense gratification. "you will come in for ballot in about three year, by which time you will have take your degree," the guardian say. Pen long for the three year to be over, and survey the stucco-hall, and vast library, and drawing-room as already his own property. the Major laugh slyly to see the pompous air of the simple young fellow as he strut out of the building. he and Foker drive down in the latter's cab one day to the Grey Friars, and renew acquaintance with some of their old comrade there. the boy come crowd up to the cab as it stand by the Grey Friars gate, where they be enter, and admire the chestnut horse, and the tight and livery and gravity of Stoopid, the tiger. the bell for afternoon-school rang as they be swagger about the play-ground talk to their old crony. the awful doctor pass into school with his grammar in his hand. Foker slunk away uneasily at his presence, but Pen go up blush, and shake the dignitary by the hand. he laugh as he think that well-remember Latin Grammar have box his ear many a time. he be generous, good-natured, and, in a word, perfectly conceited and satisfied with himself.
then they drive to the parental brew-house. Foker's Entire be compose in an enormous pile of building, not far from the Grey Friars, and the name of that well-know firm be gild upon innumerable public-house sign, tenant by its vassal in the neighbourhood; and the venerable junior partner and manager do honour to the young lord of the vat and his friend, and serve they with silver flagon of brown-stout, so strong, that you would have think, not only the young man, but the very horse Mr. Harry Foker drive, be affect by the potency of the drink, for he rush home to the west-end of the town at a rapid pace, which endanger the pie-stall and the woman on the crossing, and bring the cab-step into collision with the post at the street corner, and cause Stoopid to swing fearfully on his board behind.
the Major be quite pleased when Pen be with his young acquaintance; listen to Mr. Foker's artless story with the great interest; give the two boy a fine dinner at a Covent Garden Coffee-house, whence they proceed to the play; but be above all happy when Mr. and Lady Agnes Foker, who happen to be in London, request the pleasure of Major Pendennis and Mr. Arthur Pendennis's company at dinner in Grosvenor Street. "have obtain the entree into Lady Agnes Foker's house," he say to Pen with an affectionate solemnity which befit the importance of the occasion, "it behove you, my dear boy, to keep it. you must mind and never neglect to call in Grosvenor Street when you come to London. I recommend you to read up carefully, in Debrett, the alliance and genealogy of the Earls of Rosherville, and if you can, to make some trifling allusion to the family, something historical, neat, and complimentary, and that sort of thing, which you, who have a poetic fancy, can do pretty well. Mr. Foker himself be a worthy man, though not of high extraction or indeed much education. he always make a point of have some of the family porter serve round after dinner, which you will on no account refuse, and which I shall drink myself, though all beer disagree with I confoundedly." and the heroic martyr do actually sacrifice himself, as he say he would, on the day when the dinner take place, and old Mr. Foker, at the head of his table, make his usual joke about Foker's Entire. we should all of we, I be sure, have like to see the Major's grin, when the worthy old gentleman make his time-honour joke.
Lady Agnes, who, wrap up in Harry, be the fond of mother, and one of the most good-natured though not the wise of woman, receive her son's friend with great cordiality: and astonish Pen by account of the severe course of study which her darling boy be pursue, and which she fear might injure his dear health. Foker the eld burst into a horse-laugh at some of these speech, and the heir of the house wink his eye very knowingly at his friend. and Lady Agnes then go through her son's history from the early time, and recount his miraculous suffering in the measle and hooping-cough, his escape from drown, the shocking tyranny practise upon he at that horrid school, whither Mr. Foker would send he because he have be bring up there himself, and she never would forgive that disagreeable Doctor, no never--Lady Agnes, we say, have prattle away for an hour incessantly about her son, vote the two Messieurs Pendennis most agreeable man; and when pheasant come with the second course, which the Major praise as the very fine bird he ever see, her ladyship say they come from Logwood (as the Major know perfectly well), and hop that they would both pay she a visit there--at Christmas, or when dear Harry be at home for the vacation.
"God bless you, my dear boy," Pendennis say to Arthur, as they be light their candle in Bury Street afterwards to go to bed. "you make that little allusion to Agincourt, where one of the Roshervilles distinguish himself, very neatly and well, although Lady Agnes do not quite understand it: but it be exceedingly well for a beginner--though you oughtnot to blush so, by the way--and I beseech you, my dear Arthur, to remember through life, that with an entree--with a good entree, mind-- it be just as easy for you to have good society as bad, and that it cost a man, when properly introduce, no more trouble or soin to keep a good footing in the good house in London than to dine with a lawyer in Bedford Square. mind this when you be at Oxbridge pursue your study, and for Heaven's sake be very particular in the acquaintance which you make. the premier pa in life be the most important of all--do you write to your mother to-day?--No?--well, do, before you go, and call and ask Mr. Foker for a frank--they like it--good night. God bless you."
Pen write a droll account of his doing in London, and the play, and the visit to the old Friars, and the brewery, and the party at Mr. Foker's, to his dear mother, who be say her prayer at home in the lonely house at Fairoaks, her heart full of love and tenderness unutterable for the boy: and she and Laura read that letter and those which follow, many, many time, and brood over they as woman do. it be the first step in life that Pen be make--ah! what a dangerous journey it be, and how the brave may stumble and the strong fail. brother wayfarer! may you have a kind arm to support yours on the path, and a friendly hand to succour those who fall beside you. may truth guide, mercy forgive at the end, and love accompany always. without that lamp how blind the traveller would be, and how black and cheerless the journey!
so the coach drive up to that ancient and comfortable inn the Trencher, which stand in Main Street, Oxbridge, and Pen with delight and eagerness remark, for the first time, gownsman go about, chapel bell clink (bell in Oxbridge be ring from morning-tide till even-song)--tower and pinnacle rise calm and stately over the gable and antique house-roof of the homely busy city. previous communication have take place between Dr. Portman on Pen's part, and Mr. Buck, Tutor of Boniface, on whose side Pen be enter; and as soon as Major Pendennis have arrange his personal appearance, so that it should make a satisfactory impression upon Pen's tutor, the pair walk down Main Street, and pass the great gate and belfry-tower of Saint George's College, and so come, as they be direct, to Saint Boniface: where again Pen's heart begin to beat as they enter at the wicket of the venerable ivy-mantled gate of the College. it be surmount with an ancient dome almost cover with creeper, and adorn with the effigy of the Saint from whom the House take its name, and many coat-of-arm of its royal and noble benefactor.
the porter point out a queer old tower at the corner of the quadrangle, by which Mr. Buck's room be approach, and the two gentleman walk acrosse the square, the main feature of which be at once and for ever stamp in Pen's mind--the pretty fountain playing in the centre of the fair grass plat; the tall chapel window and buttress rise to the right; the hall with its taper lantern and oriel window; the lodge, from the door of which the Master issue with rustle silk; the line of the surround room pleasantly break by carve chimney, grey turret, and quaint gable--all these Mr. Pen's eye drink in with an eagerness which belong to first impression; and Major Pendennis survey with that calmness which belong to a gentleman who do not care for the picturesque, and whose eye have be somewhat dim by the constant glare of the pavement of Pall Mall.
Saint George's be the great College of the University of Oxbridge, with its four vast quadrangle, and its beautiful hall and garden, and the Georgians, as the man be call wear gown of a peculiar cut, and give themselves no small air of superiority over all other young man. little Saint Boniface be but a petty hermitage in comparison of the huge consecrate pile alongside of which it lie. but consider its size it have always keep an excellent name in the university. its ton be very good: the good family of certain county have time out of mind send up their young man to Saint Boniface: the college living be remarkably good: the fellowship easy; the Boniface man have have more than their fair share of university honour; their boat be third upon the river; their chapel-choir be not inferior to Saint George's itself; and the Boniface ale the good in Oxbridge. in the comfortable old wainscote College-Hall, and round about Roubilliac's statue of Saint Boniface (who stand in an attitude of seraphic benediction over the uncommonly good cheer of the fellow' table) there be portrait of many most eminent Bonifacians. there be the learned Doctor Griddle, who suffer in Henry VIII.'s time, and Archbishop Bush who roast he--there be Lord Chief Justice Hicks-- the Duke of St. David's, K.G., Chancellor of the University and Member of this College--Sprott the Poet, of whose fame the college be justly proud --doctor Blogg, the late master, and friend of Doctor Johnson, who visit he at Saint Boniface--and other lawyer, scholar, and divine, whose portraiture look from the wall, or whose coat-of-arm shine in emerald and ruby, gold and azure, in the tall window of the refectory. the venerable cook of the college be one of the good artist in Oxbridge (his son take the high honour in the other University of Camford), and the wine in the fellow' room have long be fame for its excellence and abundance.
into this certainly not the least snugly sheltered arbour amongst the grove of Academe, Pen now find his way, lean on his uncle's arm, and they speedily reach Mr. Buck's room, and be conduct into the apartment of that courteous gentleman.
he have receive previous information from Dr. Portman regard Pen, with respect to whose family, fortune, and personal merit the honest Doctor have speak with no small enthusiasm. indeed Portman have describe Arthur to the tutor as "a young gentleman of some fortune and landed estate, of one of the most ancient family in the kingdom, and possess such a character and genius as be sure, under the proper guidance, to make he a credit to the college and the university." under such recommendation the tutor be, of course, most cordial to the young freshman and his guardian, invite the latter to dine in hall, where he would have the satisfaction of see his nephew wear his gown and eat his dinner for the first time, and request the pair to take wine at his room after hall, and in consequence of the highly favourable report he have receive of Mr. Arthur Pendennis, say, he should be happy to give he the good set of room to be have in college--a gentleman-pensioner's set, indeed, which be just luckily vacant. so they part until dinner-time, which be very near at hand, and Major Pendennis pronounce Mr. Buck to be uncommonly civil indeed. indeed when a College Magnate take the trouble to be polite, there be no man more splendidly courteous. immerse in their book and exclude from the world by the gravity of their occupation, these reverend man assume a solemn magnificence of compliment in which they rustle and swell as in their grand robe of state. those silk and brocade be not put on for all comer or every day.
when the two gentleman have take leave of the tutor in his study, and have return to Mr. Buck's ante-room, or lecture-room, a very handsome apartment, turkey-carpet, and hang with excellent print and richly frame picture, they find the tutor's servant already in wait there, accompany by a man with a bag full of cap and a number of gown, from which Pen might select a cap and gown for himself, and the servant, no doubt, would get a commission proportionable to the service do by he. Mr. Pen be all in a tremor of pleasure as the bustling tailor try on a gown and pronounce that it be an excellent fit; and then he put the pretty college cap on, in rather a dandified manner and somewhat on one side, as he have see Fiddicombe, the young master at Grey Friars, wear it. and he inspect the entire costume with a great deal of satisfaction in one of the great gilt mirror which ornament Mr. Buck's lecture-room: for some of these college divine be no more above look--glass than a lady be, and look to the set of their gown and cap quite as anxiously as folk do of the lovelier sex. the Major smile as he see the boy dandify himself in the glass: the old gentleman be not displeased with the appearance of the comely lad.
then Davis, the skip or attendant, lead the way, key in hand, across the quadrangle, the Major and Pen follow he, the latter blushing, and please with his new academical habiliment, across the quadrangle to the room which be destine for the freshman; and which be vacate by the retreat of the gentleman-pensioner, Mr. Spicer. the room be very comfortable, with large cross beam, high wainscot, and small window in deep embrasure. Mr. Spicer's furniture be there, and to be sell at a valuation, and Major Pendennis agree on his nephew's behalf to take the available part of it, laughingly however decline (as, indeed, Pen do for his own part) six sporting print, and four group of opera-dancer with gauze drapery, which form the late occupant's pictorial collection.
then they go to hall, where Pen sate down and eat his common with his brother freshman, and the Major take his place at the high-table along with the college dignitary and other father or guardian of youth, who have come up with their son to Oxbridge; and after hall they go to Mr. Buck's to take wine; and after wine to chapel, where the major sate with great gravity in the upper place, have a fine view of the Master in his carve throne or stall under the organ-loft, where that gentleman, the learn Doctor Donne, sate magnificent, with his great prayer-book before he, an image of statuesque piety and rigid devotion. all the young freshman behave with gravity and decorum, but Pen be shock to see that atrocious little Foker, who come in very late, and half a dozen of his comrade in the gentleman-pensioner' seat, giggle and talk as if they have be in so many stall at the Opera. but these circumstance, it must be remember, take place some year back, when William the Fourth be king. young man be much well behave now, and besides, Saint Boniface be rather a fast college.
Pen could hardly sleep at night in his bedroom at the Trencher: so anxious be he to begin his college life, and to get into his own apartment. what do he think about, as he lie tossing and awake? be it about his mother at home; the pious soul whose life be bind up in his? yes, let we hope he think of she a little. be it about Miss Fotheringay, and his eternal passion, which have keep he awake so many night, and create such wretchedness and such longing? he have a trick of blushing, and if you have be in the room, and the candle have not be out, you might have see the youth's countenance redden more than once, as be break out into passionate incoherent exclamation regard that luckless event of his life. his uncle's lesson have not be throw away upon he; the mist of passion have pass from his eye now, and he see she as she be. to think that he, Pendennis, have be enslave by such a woman, and then jilt by she! that he should have stoop so low, to be trample on the mire! that there be a time in his life, and that but a few month back, when he be willing to take Costigan for his father-in-law!
"poor old Smirke!" Pen presently laugh out--"well, Iwill write and try and console the poor old boy. he willnot die of his passion, ha, ha!" the Major, have he be awake, might have hear a score of such ejaculation utter by Pen as he lie awake and restless through the first night of his residence at Oxbridge.
it would, perhaps, have be well for a youth, the battle of whose life be go to begin on the morrow, to have pass the eve in a fferent sort of vigil: but the world have get hold of Pen in the shape of his selfish old mentor: and those who have any interest in his character must have perceive ere now, that this lad be very weak as well as very impetuous, very vain as well as very frank, and if of a generous disposition, not a little selfish in the midst of his profuseness, and also rather fickle, as all eager pursuer of self-gratification be.
the six month' passion have age he very considerably. there be an immense gulf between Pen the victim of love, and Pen the innocent boy of eighteen, sigh after it: and so Arthur Pendennis have all the experience and superiority, besides that command which afterwards conceit and imperiousness of disposition give he over the young man with whom he now begin to live.
he and his uncle pass the morning with great satisfaction in make purchase for the well comfort of the apartment which the lad be about to occupy. Mr. Spicer's china and glass be in a dreadfully dismantle condition, his lamp smash, and his bookcase by no mean so spacious as those shelf which would be requisite to receive the content of the box which be lie in the hall at Fairoaks, and which be address to Arthur in the hand of poor Helen.
the box arrive in a few day, that his mother have pack with so much care. Pen be touch as he read the superscription in the dear well-know hand, and he arrange in their proper place all the book, his old friend, and all the linen and table-cloth which Helen have select from the family stock, and all the jam-pot which little Laura have bind in straw, and the hundred simple gift of home. Pen have another Alma Mater now. but it be not all child who take to she kindly.
CHAPTER XIX
Pendennis of Boniface
our friend Pen be not sorry when his mentor take leave of the young gentleman on the second day after the arrival of the pair in Oxbridge, and we may be sure that the Major on his part be very glad to have discharge his duty, and to have the duty over. More than three month of precious time have that martyr of a Major give up to his nephew--be ever selfish man call upon to make a great sacrifice? do you know many man or major who would do as much? a man will lay down his head, or peril his life for his honour, but let we be shy how we ask he to give up his ease or his heart's desire. very few of we can bear that trial. say, worthy reader, if thou hast peradventure a beard, wouldst thou do as much? I will not say that a woman will not. they be use to it: we take care to accustom they to sacrifice but, my good sir, the amount of self-denial which you have probably exert through life, when put down to your account elsewhere, will not probably swell the balance on the credit side much. well, well, there be no use in speaking of such ugly matter, and you be too polite to use a vulgar to quoque. but I wish to state once for all that I greatly admire the Major for his conduct during the past quarter, and think that he have quite a right to be pleased at get a holiday. Foker and Pen see he off in the coach, and the former young gentleman give particular order to the coachman to take care of that gentleman inside. it please the eld Pendennis to have his nephew in the company of a young fellow who would introduce he to the good set of the university. the Major rush off to London and thence to Cheltenham, from which watering-place he descend upon some neighbour great house, whereof the family be not go abroad, and where good shooting and company be to be have.
a quarter of the space which custom have award to work style the Serial Nature, have be assign to the account of one passage in Pen's career, and it be manifest that the whole of his adventure cannot be treat at a similar length, unless some descendant of the chronicler of Pen's history should take up the pen at his decease, and continue the narrative for the successor of the present generation of reader. we be not about to go through the young fellow's academical career with, by any mean, a similar minuteness. alas, the life of such boy do not bear tell altogether. I wish it do. I ask you, do yours? as long as what we call our honour be clear, I suppose your mind be pretty easy. woman be pure, but not man. woman be unselfish, but not man. and I would not wish to say of poor Arthur Pendennis that he be bad than his neighbour, only that his neighbour be bad for the most part. let we have the candour to own as much at least. can you point out ten spotless man of your acquaintance? mine be pretty large, but I cannot find ten saint in the list.
during the first term of Mr. Pen's academical life, he attend classical and mathematical lecture with tolerable assiduity; but discover before very long time that he have little taste or genius for the pursuing of the exact science, and be perhaps rather annoyed that one or two very vulgar young man, who do not even use strap to their trouser so as to cover the abominably thick and coarse shoe and stocking which they wear, beat he completely in the lecture-room, he give up his attendance at that course, and announce to his fond parent that he propose to devote himself exclusively to the cultivation of greek and Roman Literature. Mrs. Pendennis be, for her part, quite satisfied that her darling boy should pursue that branch of learn for which he have the great inclination; and only besought he not to ruin his health by too much study, for she have hear the most melancholy story of young student who, by over-fatigue, have bring on brain-fever and perish untimely in the midst of their university career. and Pen's health, which be always delicate, be to be regard, as she justly say, beyond all consideration or vain honour. Pen, although not aware of any lurking disease which be likely to end his life, yet kindly promise his mamma not to sit up read too late of night, and stick to his word in this respect with a great deal more tenacity of resolution than he exhibit upon some other occasion, when perhaps he be a little remiss.
presently he begin too to find that he learn little good in the classical lecture. his fellow-student there be too dull, as in mathematic they be too learn for he. Mr. Buck, the tutor, be no well a scholar than many a fifth-form boy at Grey Friars; might have some stupid humdrum notion about the metre and grammatical construction of a passage of Aeschylus or Aristophanes, but have no more notion of the poetry than Mrs. Binge, his bed-maker; and Pen grow weary of hear the dull student and tutor blunder through a few line of a play, which he could read in a tenth part of the time which they give to it. after all, private reading, as he begin to perceive, be the only study which be really profitable to a man; and he announce to his mamma that he should read by himself a great deal more, and in public a great deal less. that excellent woman know no more about Homer than she do about Algebra, but she be quite content with Pen's arrangement regard his course of study, and feel perfectly confident that her dear boy would get the place which he merit.
Pen do not come home until after Christmas, a little to the fond mother's disappointment, and Laura's, who be long for he to make a fine snow fortification, such as he have make three winter before. but he be invite to Logwood, Lady Agnes Foker's, where there be private theatrical, and a gay Christmas party of very fine folk, some of they whom Major Pendennis would on no account have his nephew neglect. however, he stay at home for the last three week of the vacation, and Laura have the opportunity of remark what a quantity of fine new clothe he bring with he, and his mother admire his improved appearance and manly and decide tone.
he do not come home at Easter; but when he arrive for the long vacation, he bring more smart clothe; appear in the morning in wonderful shooting jacket, with remarkable button; and in the evening in gorgeous velvet waistcoat, with richly-embroider cravat, and curious linen. and as she pry about his room, she see, oh, such a beautiful dressing-case, with silver mounting, and a quantity of lovely ring and jewellery. and he have a new french watch and gold chain, in place of the big old chronometer, with its bunch of jingle seal, which have hang from the fob of John Pendennis, and by the second-hand of which the defunct doctor have feel many a patient's pulse in his time. it be but a few month back Pen have long for this watch, which he think the most splendid and august timepiece in the world; and just before he go to college, Helen have take it out of her trinket-box (where it have remain unwound since the death of her husband) and give it to Pen with a solemn and appropriate little speech respect his father's virtue and the proper use of time. this portly and valuable chronometer Pen now pronounce to be out of date, and, indeed, make some comparison between it and a warming-pan, which Laura think disrespectful, and he leave the watch in a drawer, in the company of soiled primrose glove, cravat which have go out of favour, and of that other school watch which have once before be mention in this history. our old friend, Rebecca, Pen pronounce to be no long up to his weight, and swap she away for another and more powerful horse, for which he have to pay rather a heavy figure. Mr. Pendennis give the boy the money for the new horse; and Laura cry when Rebecca be fetch away.
also Pen bring a large box of cigar brand Colorados, Afrancesados, Telescopios, Fudson Oxford Street, or by some such strange title, and begin to consume these not only about the stable and green-house, where they be very good for Helen's plant, but in his own study, of which practice his mother do not at first approve. but he be at work upon a prize-poem, he say, and could not compose without his cigar, and quote the late lamented it Lord Byron's line in favour of the custom of smoking. as he be smoke to such good purpose, his mother could not of course refuse permission: in fact, the good soul come into the room one day in the midst of Pen's labour (he be consult a novel which have recently appear, for the cultivation of the light literature of his own country as well as of foreign nation become every student)--helen, we say, come into the room and find Pen on the sofa at this work, rather than disturb he go for a light-box and his cigar-case to his bedroom which be adjacent, and actually put the cigar into his mouth and light the match at which he kindle it. Pen laugh, and kiss his mother's hand as it hang fondly over the back of the sofa. "dear old mother," he say, "if I be to tell you to burn the house down, I think you would do it." and it be very likely that Mr. Pen be right, and that the foolish woman would have do almost as much for he as he say.
besides the work of english "light literature" which this diligent student devour, he bring down box of the light literature of the neighbouring country of France: into the leaf of which when Helen dip, she read such thing as cause she to open her eye with wonder. but Pen show she that it be not he who make the book, though it be absolutely necessary that he should keep up his French by an acquaintance with the most celebrated writer of the day, and that it be as clearly his duty to read the eminent Paul de Kock, as to study Swift or Moliere. and Mrs. Pendennis yield with a sigh of perplexity. but Miss Laura be warn off the book, both by his anxious mother, and that rigid moralist Mr. Arthur Pendennis himself, who, however he might be call upon to study every branch of literature in order to form his mind and to perfect his style, would by no mean prescribe such a course of reading to a young lady whose business in life be very different.
in the course of this long vacation Mr. Pen drink up the bin of claret which his father have lay in, and of which we have hear the son remark that there be not a headache in a hogshead; and this wine be exhaust, he write for a further supply to "his wine merchant," Messrs. Binney and Latham of Mark Lane, London: from whom, indeed, old Doctor Portman have recommend Pen to get a supply of port and sherry on go to college. "you will have, no doubt, to entertain your young friend at Boniface with wine-party," the honest rector have remark to the lad. "they use to be customary at college in my time, and I would advise you to employ an honest and respectable house in London for your small stock of wine, rather than to have recourse to the Oxbridge tradesman, whose liquor, if I remember rightly, be both deleterious in quality and exorbitant in price." and the obedient young gentleman take the Doctor's advice, and patronise Messrs. Binney and Latham at the rector's suggestion.
so when he write order for a stock of wine to be send down to the cellar at Fairoaks, he hint that Messrs. B. and L. might send in his university account for wine at the same time with the Fairoaks bill. the poor widow be frighten at the amount. but Pen laugh at her old-fashioned view, say that the bill be moderate, that everybody drink claret and champagne now, and, finally, the widow pay, feel dimly that the expense of her household be increase considerably, and that her narrow income would scarce suffice to meet they. but they be only occasional. Pen merely come home for a few week at the vacation. Laura and she might pinch when he be go. in the brief time he be with they, ought they not to make he happy?
Arthur's own allowance be liberal all this time; indeed, much more so than those of the son of far more wealthy man. year before, the thrifty and affectionate John Pendennis, whose darling project it have ever be to give his son a university education, and those advantage of which his own father's extravagance have deprive he, have begin lay by a store of money which he call Arthur's Education Fund. year after year in his book his executor find entry of sum vest as A. E. F., and during the period subsequent to her husband's decease, and before Pen's entry at college, the widow have add sundry sum to this fund, so that when Arthur go up to Oxbridge it reach no inconsiderable amount. let he be liberally allowance, be Major Pendennis's maxim. let he make his first entree into the world as a gentleman, and take his place with man of good rank and station: after give it to he, it will be his own duty to hold it. there be no such bad policy as stint a boy--or put he on a low allowance than his fellow. Arthur will have to face the world and fight for himself presently. meanwhile we shall have procure for he good friend, gentlemanly habit, and have he well back and well train against the time when the real struggle come. and these liberal opinion the Major probably advance both because they be just, and because he be not deal with his own money.
thus young Pen, the only son of an estated country gentleman, with a good allowance, and a gentlemanlike bearing and person, look to be a lad of much more consequence than he be really; and be hold by the Oxbridge authority, tradesman, and undergraduate, as quite a young buck and member of the aristocracy. his manner be frank, brave, and perhaps a little impertinent, as become a high-spirited youth. he be perfectly generous and free-handed with his money, which seem pretty plentiful. he love joviality, and have a good voice for a song. boat-racing have not rise in Pen's time to the fureur which, as we be give to understand, it have since attain in the university; and rid and tandem-driving be the fashion of the ingenuous youth. Pen ride well to hound, appear in pink, as become a young buck, and, not particularly extravagant in equestrian or any other amusement, yet manage to run up a fine bill at Nile's, the livery-stable keeper, and in a number of other quarter. in fact, this lucky young gentleman have almost every taste to a considerable degree. he be very fond of book of all sort: Doctor Portman have teach he to like rare edition, and his own taste lead he to like beautiful binding. it be marvellous what tall copy, and gilding, and marbling, and blind-tooling, the bookseller and binder put upon Pen's bookshelf. he have a very fair taste in matter of art, and a keen relish for print of a high school--none of your french Opera Dancers, or tawdry race Prints, such as have delight the simple eye of Mr. Spicer, his predecessor--but your Stranges, and Rembrandt etching, and Wilkies before the letter, with which his apartment be furnish presently in the most perfect good taste, as be allow in the university, where this young fellow get no small reputation. we have mention that he exhibit a certain partiality for ring, jewellery, and fine raiment of all sort; and it must be own that Mr. Pen, during his time at the university, be rather a dressy man, and love to array himself in splendour. he and his polite friend would dress themselves out with as much care in order to go and dine at each other's room, as other folk would who be go to enslave a mistress. they say he use to wear ring over his kid glove, which he always deny; but what folly will not youth perpetrate with its own admirable gravity and simplicity? that he take perfume bath be a truth; and he use to say that he take they after meet certain man of a very low set in hall.
in Pen's second year, when Miss Fotheringay make her chief hit in London, and score of print be publish of she, Pen have one of these hang in his bedroom, and confide to the man of his set how awfully, how wildly, how madly, how passionately, he have love that woman. he show they in confidence the verse that he have write to she, and his brow would darken, his eye roll, his chest heave with emotion as he recall that fatal period of his life, and describe the woe and agony which he have suffer. the verse be copy out, hand about, sneer at, admire, pass from coterie to coterie. there be few thing which elevate a lad in the estimation of his brother boy, more than to have a character for a great and romantic passion. perhaps there be something noble in it at all time--among very young man it be consider heroic--Pen be pronounce a tremendous fellow. they say he have almost commit suicide: that he have fight a duel with a baronet about she. freshman point he out to each other. as at the promenade time at two o'clock he swagger out of college, surround by his crony, he be famous to behold. he be elaborately attire. he would ogle the lady who come to lionise the university, and pass before he on the arm of happy gownsman, and give his opinion upon their personal charm, or their toilette, with the gravity of a critic whose experience entitle he to speak with authority. man use to say that they have be walk with Pendennis, and be as pleased to be see in his company as some of we would be if we walk with a duke down Pall Mall. he and the proctor cap each other as they meet, as if they be rival power, and the man hardly know which be the great.
in fact, in the course of his second year, Arthur Pendennis have become one of the man of fashion in the university. it be curious to watch that facile admiration, and simple fidelity of youth. they hang round a leader; and wonder at he, and love he, and imitate he. no generous boy ever live, I suppose, that have not have some wonderment of admiration for another boy; and Monsieur Pen at Oxbridge have his school, his faithful band of friend and his rival. when the young man hear at the haberdasher' shop that Mr. Pendennis, of Boniface, have just order a crimson satin-cravat, you would see a couple of dozen crimson satin cravat in Main Street in the course of the week--and Simon, the Jeweller, be know to sell no less than two gross of Pendennis pin, from a pattern which the young gentleman have select in his shop.
now if any person with an arithmetical turn of mind will take the trouble to calculate what a sum of money it would cost a young man to indulge freely in all the above propensity which we have say Mr. Pen possess, it will be see that a young fellow, with such liberal taste and amusement, must need in the course of two or three year spend or owe a very handsome sum of money. we have say our friend Pen have not a calculate turn. no one propensity of his be outrageously extravagant; and it be certain that Paddington's tailor's account; Guttlebury's cook's bill for dinner; Dillon Tandy's bill with Finn, the print seller, for Raphael-Morgheus and Landseer proof, and Wormall's dealing with Parkton, the great bookseller, for Aldine edition, black-letter folio, and richly illuminate Missals of the XVI. Century; and Snaffle's or Foker's score with Nile the horsedealer, be, each and all of they, incomparably great than any little bill which Mr. Pen might run up with the above-mention tradesman. but Pendennis of Boniface have the advantage over all these young gentleman, his friend and associate, of a universality of taste: and whereas young Lord Paddington do not care twopence for the most beautiful print, or to look into any gilt frame that have not a mirror within it; and Guttlebury do not mind in the least how he be dress, and have an aversion for horse exercise, nay a terror of it; and Snaffle never read any print work but the 'Racing Calendar' or 'Bell's life,' or care for any manuscript except his greasy little scrawl of a bet-book:--our catholic-minded young friend occupy himself in every one of the branch of science or pleasure above-mention, and distinguish himself tolerably in each.
hence young Pen get a prodigious reputation in the university, and be hail as a sort of Crichton; and as for the english verse prize, in competition for which we have see he busily engage at Fairoaks, Jones of Jesus carry it that year certainly, but the undergraduate think Pen's a much fine poem, and he have his verse print at his own expense, and distribute in gilt morocco cover amongst his acquaintance. I find a copy of it lately in a dusty corner of Mr. Pen's bookcase, and have it before I this minute, bind up in a collection of old Oxbridge tract, university statute, prize-poem by successful and unsuccessful candidate, declamation recite in the college chapel, speech deliver at the Union Debating Society, and inscribe by Arthur with his name and college, Pendennis--Boniface; or present to he by his affectionate friend Thompson or Jackson, the author. how strange the epigraph look in those half-boyish hand, and what a thrill the sight of the document give one after the lapse of a few lustre! how fate, since that time, have remove some, estranged other, deal awfully with all! many a hand be cold that write those kindly memorial, and that we press in the confident and generous grasp of youthful friendship. what passion our friendship be in those old day, how artless and void of doubt! how the arm you be never tired of have link in yours under the fair college avenue or by the river side, where it wash Magdalen Gardens, or Christ Church Meadows, or wind by Trinity and King's, be withdraw of necessity, when you enter presently the world, and each part to push and struggle for himself through the great mob on the way through life! be we the same man now that write those inscription--that read those poem? that deliver or hear those essay and speech so simple, so pompous, so ludicrously solemn; parody so artlessly from book, and speak with smug chubby face, and such an admirable aping of wisdom and gravity? here be the book before I: it be scarcely fifteen year old. here be Jack moan with despair and byronic misanthropy, whose career at the university be one of unmixed milk-punch. here be Tom's daring Essay in defence of suicide and of republicanism in general, apropos of the death of Roland and the Girondins--Tom's, who wear the starchest tie in all the diocese, and would go to Smithfield rather than eat a beefsteak on a Friday in Lent. here be Bob of the ---- Circuit, who have make a fortune in Railroad Committees, and whose dinner be so good --bellowing out with Tancred and Godfrey, "on to the breach, ye soldier of the cross, Scale the red wall and swim the choke foss. ye dauntless archer, twang your cross-bows well; on, bill and battle-axe and mangonel! ply battering-ram and hurtling catapult, Jerusalem be ours--id Deus vult." after which come a mellifluous description of the garden of Sharon and the maid of Salem, and a prophecy that rose shall deck the entire country of Syria, and a speedy reign of peace be establish--all in undeniably decasyllabic line, and the queer aping of sense and sentiment and poetry. and there be Essays and Poems along with these grave parody, and boyish exercise (which be at once so frank and false and mirthful, yet, somehow, so mournful) by youthful hand, that shall never write more. fate have interpose darkly, and the young voice be silent, and the eager brain have cease to work. this one have genius and a great descent, and seem to be destine for honour which now be of little worth to he: that have virtue, learning, genius--every faculty and endowment which might secure love, admiration, and worldly fame: an obscure and solitary churchyard contain the grave of many fond hope, and the pathetic stone which bid they farewell--I see the sun shin on it in the fall of last year, and hear the sweet village choir raise anthem round about. what boot whether it be Westminster or a little country spire which cover your ash, or if, a few day soon or later, the world forget you?
amidst these friend, then, and a host more, Pen pass more than two brilliant and happy year of his life. he have his fill of pleasure and popularity. no dinner- or supper-party be complete without he; and Pen's jovial wit, and Pen's song, and dash courage and frank and manly bearing, charm all the undergraduate, and even disarm the tutor who cry out at his idleness, and murmur about his extravagant way of life. though he become the favourite and leader of young man who be much his superior in wealth and station, he be much too generous to endeavour to propitiate they by any meanness or cringe on his own part, and would not neglect the humble man of his acquaintance in order to curry favour with the rich young grandee in the university. his name be still remember at the Union Debating Club, as one of the brilliant orator of his day. by the way, from have be an ardent Tory in his freshman's year, his principle take a sudden turn afterwards, and he become a liberal of the most violent order. he avow himself a Dantonist, and assert that Louis the Sixteenth be serve right. and as for Charles the First, he vow that he would chop off that monarch's head with his own right hand be he then in the room at the Union Debating Club, and have Cromwell no other executioner for the traitor. he and Lord Magnus Charters, the Marquis of Runnymede's son, before-mention, be the most truculent republican of their day.
there be reputation of this sort make, quite independent of the collegiate hierarchy, in the republic of gownsman. a man may be famous in the honour-list and entirely unknown to the undergraduate: who elect king and chieftain of their own, whom they admire and obey, as negro-gang have private black sovereign in their own body, to whom they pay an occult obedience, besides that which they publicly profess for their owner and driver. among the young one Pen become famous and popular: not that he do much, but there be a general determination that he could do a great deal if he choose. "ah, if Pendennis of Boniface would but try," the man say, "he might do anything." he be back for the Greek Ode win by Smith of Trinity; everybody be sure he would have the latin hexameter prize which Brown of St. John's, however, carry off, and in this way one university honour after another be lose by he, until, after two or three failure, Mr. Pen cease to compete. but he get a declamation prize in his own college, and bring home to his mother and Laura at Fairoaks a set of prize-book begilt with the college arm, and so big, well-bind, and magnificent, that these lady think there have be no such prize ever give in a college before as this of Pen's, and that he have win the very large honour which Oxbridge be capable of award.
as vacation after vacation and term after term pass away without the desire news that Pen have sate for any scholarship or win any honour, Doctor Portman grow mightily gloomy in his behaviour towards Arthur, and adopt a sulky grandeur of deportment towards he, which the lad return by a similar haughtiness. one vacation he do not call upon the Doctor at all, much to his mother's annoyance, who think that it be a privilege to enter the Rectory-house at Clavering, and listen to Dr. Portman's antique joke and story, though ever so often repeat, with unfaile veneration. "I cannot stand the Doctor's patronising air", Pen say. "hebe too kind to I, a great deal fatherly. I have see in the world well man than he, and be not go to bore myself by listen to his dull old story and drink his stupid old port wine." the tacit feud between Pen and the doctor make the widow nervous, so that she too avoid Portman, and be afraid to go to the Rectory when Arthur be at home.
one Sunday in the last long vacation, the wretched boy push his rebellious spirit so far as not to go to church, and he be see at the gate of the Clavering Arms smoke a cigar, in the face of the congregation as it issue from St. Mary's. there be an awful sensation in the village society, Portman prophesy Pen's ruin after that, and groan in spirit over the rebellious young prodigal.
so do Helen tremble in her heart, and little Laura--Laura have grow to be a fine young stripling by this time, graceful and fair, cling round Helen and worship she, with a passionate affection. both of these woman feel that their boy be change. he be no long the artless Pen of old day, so brave, so artless, so impetuous, and tender. his face look careworn and haggard, his voice have a deep sound, and tone more sarcastic. Care seem to be pursue he; but he only laugh when his mother question he, and parry her anxious query with some scornful jest. nor do he spend much of his vacation at home; he go on visit to one great friend or another, and scar the quiet pair at Fairoaks by story of great house whither he have be invite; and by talk of lord without their title.
Honest Harry Foker, who have be the mean of introduce Arthur Pendennis to that set of young man at the university, from whose society and connexion Arthur's uncle expect that the lad would get so much benefit; who have call for Arthur's first song at his first supper-party; and who have present he at the Barmecide Club, where none but the very good man of Oxbridge be admit (it consist in Pen's time of six nobleman, eight gentleman-pensioner, and twelve of the most select commoner of the university), soon find himself leave far behind by the young freshman in the fashionable world of Oxbridge, and be a generous and worthy fellow, without a spark of envy in his composition, be exceedingly pleased at the success of his young protege, and admire Pen quite as much as any of the other youth do. I be he who follow Pen now, and quote his saying; learn his song, and retail they at minor supper-party, and be never weary of hear they from the gifted young poet's own mouth--for a good deal of the time which Mr. Pen might have employ much more advantageously in the pursuit of the regular scholastic study, be give up to the composition of secular ballad, which he sing about at party accord to university willnot.
it have be as well for Arthur if the honest Foker have remain for some time at college, for, with all his vivacity, he be a prudent young man, and often curb Pen's propensity to extravagance: but Foker's collegiate career do not last very long after Arthur's entrance at Boniface. repeat difference with the university authority cause Mr. Foker to quit Oxbridge in an untimely manner. he would persist in attend race on the neighbouring Hungerford Heath, in spite of the injunction of his academic superior. he never could be get to frequent the chapel of the college with that regularity of piety which Alma Mater demand from her child; tandem, which be abomination in the eye of the head and tutor, be Foker's great delight, and so reckless be his driving and frequent the accident and upset out of his drag, that Pen call take a drive with he take the "diversion of Purley;" finally, have a dinner-party at his room to entertain some friend from London, nothing would satisfy Mr. Foker but paint Mr. Buck's door vermilion, in which freak he be catch by the proctor; and although young Black Strap, the celebrate negro fighter, who be one of Mr. Foker's distinguished guest, and be hold the can of paint while the young artist operate on the door, knock down two of the proctor's attendant and perform prodigy of valour, yet these feat rather injure than serve Foker, whom the proctor know very well and who be take with the brush in his hand, and who be summarily convene and send down from the university.
the tutor write a very kind and feel letter to Lady Agnes on the subject, state that everybody be fond of the youth; that he never mean harm to any mortal creature; that he for his own part would have be delighted to pardon the harmless little boyish frolic, have not its unhappy publicity render it impossible to look the freak over, and breathe the most fervent wish for the young fellow's welfare--wish no doubt sincere, for Foker, as we know, come of a noble family on his mother's side, and on the other be heir to a great number of thousand pound a year.
"it donot matter," say Foker, talk over the matter with pen,--"a little soon or a little later, what be the odd? I should have be pluck for my little-go again, I know I should--that Latin I cannot screw into my head, and my mamma's anguish would have break out next term. the Governor will blow like an old grampus, I know he will,--well, we must stop till he get his wind again. I shall probably go abroad and improve my mind with foreign travel. yes, parly-voo's the ticket. It'ly, and that sort of thing. Iwill go to Paris and learn to dance and complete my education. but itbe not I Ibe anxious about, Pen. as long as people drink beer I donot care,--it's about you Ibe doubtful, my boy. yoube go too fast, and cannot keep up the pace, I tell you. itbe not the fifty you owe me,--pay it or not when you like,--but itbe the every-day pace, and I tell you it will kill you. yoube livin' as if there be no end to the money in the stockin' at home. you oughtnot to give dinner, you ought to eat them. fellow be glad to have you. you oughtnot to owe horse bill, you ought to ride other chap' nag. you know no more about bet than I do about Algebra: the chap will win your money as sure as you sport it. hang I if you be not try everything. I see you sit down to ecarte last week at Trumpington's, and take your turn with the bone after Ringwood's supper. theywill beat you at it, Pen, my boy, even if they play on the square, which. I donot say they donot, nor which I donot say they do, mind. but I willnot play with them. yoube no match for them. you benot up to their weight. itbe like little Black Strap stand up to Tom Spring,--the Blackbe a pretty fighter but, Law bless you, his arm benot long enough to touch tom,--and I tell you, yoube go it with feller beyond your weight. look here--if youwill promise I never to bet nor touch a box nor a card, Iwill let you off the two pony."
but Pen, laughingly, say, "that though it benot convenient to he to pay the two pony at that moment, he by no mean wish to be let off any just debt he owe;" and he and Foker part, not without many dark foreboding on the latter's part with regard to his friend, who Harry think be travel speedily on the road to ruin.
"one must do at Rome as Rome do," Pen say, in a dandified manner, jingle some sovereign in his waistcoat-pocket. "a little quiet play at ecarte cannot hurt a man who play pretty well--I come away fourteen sovereign rich from Ringwood's supper, and, gad! I want the money." --and he walk off, after have take leave of poor Foker, who go away without any beat of drum, or offer to drive the coach out of Oxbridge, to superintend a little dinner which he be go to give at his own room in Boniface, about which dinner, the cook of the college, who have a great respect for Mr. Pendennis, always take especial pain for his young favourite.
CHAPTER XX
Rake's Progress
some short time before Mr. Foker's departure from Oxbridge, there have come up to Boniface a gentleman who have once, as it turn out, belong to the other University of Camford, which he have quit on account of some difference with the tutor and authority there. this gentleman, whose name be Horace Bloundell, be of the ancient Suffolk family of Bloundell-Bloundell, of Bloundell-Bloundell Hall, Bloundell-Bloundellshire, as the young wag use to call it; and no doubt it be on account of his descent, and because Dr. Donne, the Master of Boniface, be a Suffolk man, and relate perhaps to the family, that Mr. Horace Bloundell be take in at Boniface, after St. George's and one or two other Colleges have refuse to receive he. there be a living in the family, which it be important for Mr. Bloundell to hold; and, be in a dragoon regiment at the time when his third brother, for whom the living be originally intend, sicken and die, Mr. Bloundell determine upon quit crimson pantaloon and sable shakos, for the black coat and white neckcloth of the english divine. the misfortune which occur at Camford, occasion some slight disturbance to Mr. Bloundell's plan; but although defeat upon one occasion, the resolute ex-dragoon be not dismayed, and set to work to win a victory elsewhere.
in Pen's second year Major Pendennis pay a brief visit to his nephew, and be introduce to several of Pen's university friend--the gentle and polite Lord Plinlimmon, the gallant and open-hearted Magnus Charters, the sly and witty Harland; the intrepid Ringwood, who be call Rupert in the Union Debating Club, from his opinion and the bravery of his blunder; Broadbent, style Barebones Broadbent from the republican nature of his opinion (he be of a dissent family from Bristol and a perfect boanerge of debate); Mr. Bloundell-Bloundell finally, who have at once take his place among the select of the university.
Major Pendennis, though he do not understand Harland's greek quotation, or quite appreciate Broadbent's thick shoe and dingy hand, be nevertheless delighted with the company assemble round his nephew, and highly approve of all the young man with the exception of that one who give himself the great air in the society, and affect most to have the manner of a man of the world.
as he and Pen sate at breakfast on the morning after the party in the room of the latter, the Major give his opinion regard the young man, with whom he be in the great good-humour. he have regale they with some of his story, which, though not quite so fresh in London (where people have a diseased appetite for novelty in the way of anecdote), be entirely new at Oxbridge, and the lad hear they with that honest sympathy, that eager pleasure, that boisterous laughter, or that profound respect, so rare in the metropolis, and which must be so delightful to the profess raconteur. only once or twice during the telling of the anecdote Mr. Bloundell's face wear a look of scorn, or betray by its expression that he be acquaint with the tale narrate. once he have the audacity to question the accuracy of one of the particular of a tale as give by Major Pendennis, and give his own version of the anecdote, about which he know he be right, for he hear it openly talk of at the Club by so-and-so and t'other who be present at the business. the youngster present look up with wonder at their associate, who dare to interrupt the Major--few of they could appreciate that melancholy grace and politeness with which Major Pendennis at once accede to Mr. Bloundell's version of the story, and thank he for correct his own error. they star on the next occasion of meeting, when Bloundell speak in contemptuous term of old Pen; say everybody know old Pen, regular old trencherman at Gaunt House, notorious old bore, regular old fogy.
Major Pendennis on his side like Mr. Bloundell not a whit. these sympathy be pretty sure to be mutual amongst man and woman, and if, for my part, some kind friend tell I that such and such a man have be abuse I, I be almost sure, on my own side, that I have a misliking to such and such a man. we like or dislike each other, as folk like or dislike the odour of certain flower, or the taste of certain dish or wine, or certain book. we cannot tell why--but as a general rule, all the reason in the world will not make we love Dr. Fell, and as sure as we dislike he, we may be sure that he dislike we.
so the Major say, "Pen, my boy, your dinner go off a merveille; you do the honour very nicely--you carve well--I be glad you learn to carve--it be do on the sideboard now in most good house, but be still an important point, and may aid you in middle-life--young Lord Plinlimmon be a very amiable young man, quite the image of his dear mother (whom I know as Lady Aquila Brownbill); and Lord Magnus's republicanism will wear off--it sit prettily enough on a young patrician in early life, though nothing be so loathsome among person of our rank--Mr. Broadbent seem to have much eloquence and considerable read your friend Foker be always delightful: but your acquaintance, Mr. Bloundell, strike I as in all respect a most ineligible young man."
"bless my soul, sir, Bloundell-Bloundell!" cry Pen, laughing; "why, sir, hebe the most popular man of the university. we elect he of the Barmecides the first week he come up--have a special meeting on purpose-- hebe of an excellent family--Suffolk Bloundells, descend from Richard's Blondel, bear a harp in chief--and motto O Mong Roy."
"a man may have a very good coat-of-arm, and be a tiger, my boy," the Major say, chip his egg; "that man be a tiger, mark my word--a low man. I will lay a wager that he leave his regiment, which be a good one (for a more respectable man than my friend Lord Martingale never sate in a saddle), in bad odour. there be the unmistakable look of slang and bad habit about this Mr. Bloundell. he frequent low gambling-house and billiard-hell, sir--he haunt third-rate club--I know he do. I know by his style. I never be mistaken in my man yet. do you remark the quantity of ring and jewellery he wear? that person have Scamp write on his countenance, if any man ever have. mark my word and avoid he. let we turn the conversation. the dinner be a leetle too fine, but I donot object to your make a few extra frais when you receive friend. of course, you donot do it often, and only those whom it be your interest to feter. the cutlet be excellent, and the souffle uncommonly light and good. the third bottle of champagne be not necessary; but you have a good income, and as long as you keep within it, I shall not quarrel with you, my dear boy."
Poor Pen! the worthy uncle little know how often those dinner take place, while the reckless young Amphitryon delight to show his hospitality and skill in gourmandise. there be no art than that (so long to learn, so difficult to acquire, so impossible and beyond the mean of many unhappy people!) about which boy be more anxious to have an air of knowingness. a taste and knowledge of wine and cookery appear to they to be the sign of an accomplished roue and manly gentleman. I like to see they wink at a glass of claret, as if they have an intimate acquaintance with it, and discuss a salmi--poor boy--it be only when they grow old that they know they know nothing of the science, when perhaps their conscience whisper they that the science be in itself little worth, and that a leg of mutton and content be as good as the dinner of pontiff. but little Pen, in his character of Admirable Crichton, think it necessary to be a great judge and practitioner of dinner; we have just say how the college cook respect he, and shall soon have to deplore that that worthy man so blindly trust our Pen. in the third year of the lad's residence at Oxbridge, his staircase be by no mean encumber with dish-cover and dessert, and waiter carry in dish, and skip open iced champagne; crowd of different sort of attendant, with face sulky or piteous, hang about the outer oak, and assail the unfortunate lad as he issue out of his den.
nor do his guardian's advice take any effect, or induce Mr. Pen to avoid the society of the disreputable Mr. Bloundell. what young man like in their companion be, what have get Pen a great part of his own repute and popularity, a real or suppose knowledge of life. a man who have see the world, or can speak of it with a knowing air--a roue, or Lovelace, who have his adventure to relate, be sure of an admire audience among boy. it be hard to confess, but so it be. we respect that sort of prowess. from our school-day we have be teach to admire it. be there five in the hundred, out of the hundred and hundred of english school-boy, bring up at our great school and college, that must not own at one time of their life to have read and like Don Juan? awful propagation of evil!--the idea of it should make the man tremble who hold the pen, lest untruth, or impurity, or unjust anger, or unjust praise escape it.
one such diseased creature as this be enough to infect a whole colony, and the tutor of Boniface begin to find the moral tone of their college lower and their young man grow unruly, and almost ungentleman-like, soon after Mr. Bloundell's arrival at Oxbridge. the young magnate of the neighbour great College of St. George's, who regard Pen, and in whose society he live, be not take in by Bloundell's flashy grace, and rakish air of fashion. broadbent call he Captain Macheath, and say he would live to be hang. Foker, during his brief stay at the university with Macheath, with characteristic caution decline to say anything in the Captain's disfavour, but hint to Pen that he have well have he for a partner at whist than play against he, and well back he at ecarte than bet on the other side. "you see, he play well than you do, Pen," be the astute young gentleman's remark: "he play uncommon well, the Captain does;--and Pen, I wouldnot take the odd too freely from he, if I be you. I donot think hebe too flush of money, the Captain benot." but beyond these dark suggestion and generality, the cautious Foker could not be get to speak.
not that his advice would have have more weight with a headstrong young man, than advice commonly have with a lad who be determined on pursue his own way. Pen's appetite for pleasure be insatiable, and he rush at it wherever it present itself, with an eagerness which bespoke his fiery constitution and youthful health. he call take pleasure "see life," and quote well-know maxim from Terence, from Horace, from Shakspeare, to show that one should do all that might become a man. he bid fair to be utterly use up and a roue, in a few year, if he be to continue at the pace at which he be go.
one night after a supper-party in college, at which Pen and Macheath have be present, and at which a little quiet vingt-et-un have be play (an amusement much pleasanter to man in their second and third year than the boisterous custom of sing song, which bring the proctor about the room, and which have grow quite stale by this time, every man have expend his budget)--as the man have take their cap and be go away, after no great loss or winning on any side, Mr. Bloundell playfully take up a green wine-glass from the supper-table, which have be destine to contain iced cup, but into which he insert something still more pernicious, namely a pair of dice, which the gentleman take out of his waistcoat-pocket, and put into the glass. then give the glass a graceful wave which show that his hand be quite experienced in the throwing of dice, he call seven the main, and whisk the ivory cube gently on the table, sweep they up lightly again from the cloth, and repeat this process two or three time. the other man look on, Pen, of course, among the number, who have never use the dice as yet, except to play a humdrum game of backgammon at home.
Mr. Bloundell, who have a good voice, begin to troll out the chorus from Robert the Devil, an Opera then in great vogue, in which chorus many of the man join, especially Pen, who be in very high spirit, have win a good number of shilling and half-crown at the vingt-et-un--and presently, instead of go home, most of the party be seat round the table play at dice, the green glass go round from hand to hand until Pen finally shiver it, after throw six main.
from that night Pen plunge into the delight of the game of hazard, as eagerly as it be his custom to pursue any new pleasure. Dice can be play of morning as well as after dinner or supper. Bloundell would come into Pen's room after breakfast, and it be astonish how quick the time pass as the bone be rattle. they have little quiet party with closed door, and Bloundell devise a box line with felt, so that the dice should make no noise, and their tell-tale rattle not bring the sharp-eare tutor up to the room. Bloundell, Ringwood, and Pen be once very nearly catch by Mr. Buck, who, pass in the Quadrangle, think he hear the word "two to one on the caster," through Pen's open window; but when the tutor get into Arthur's room he find the lad with three homer before they, and Pen say he be try to coach the two other man, and ask Mr. Buck with great gravity what be the present condition of the River Scamander, and whether it be navigable or no?
Mr. Arthur Pendennis do not win much money in these transaction with Mr. Bloundell, or indeed gain good of any kind except a knowledge of the odd at hazard, which he might have learn out of book.
Captain Macheath have other accomplishment which he exercise for Pen's benefit. the Captain's story have a great and unfortunate charm for Arthur, who be never tired of hear Bloundell's history of garrison conquest, and of his feat in country-quarters.--He have be at Paris, and have plenty of legend about the Palais Royal, and the Salon, and Frascati's. he have go to the Salon one night, after a dinner at the Cafe de Paris, "when we be all devilishly cut, by Jove; and on wake in the morning in my own room, I find myself with twelve thousand franc under my pillow, and a hundred and forty-nine Napoleons in one of my boot. benot that a coup, hay?" the Captain say. Pen's eye glisten with excitement as he hear this story. he respect the man who could win such a sum of money. he sigh, and say it would set he all right. Macheath laugh, and tell he to drink another drop of Maraschino. "I could tell you story much more wonderful than that," he add; and so indeed the Captain could have do, without any further trouble than that of invention, with which portion of the poetic faculty nature have copiously endow he.
he laugh to scorn Pen's love for Miss Fotheringay, when he come to hear of that amour from Arthur, as he pretty soon do, for, we have say, Pen be not averse to tell the story now to his confidential friend, and he and they be rather proud of the transaction. but Macheath take away all Pen's conceit on this head, not by demonstrate the folly of the lad's passion for an uneducated woman much his senior in year, but by expose his absurd desire of gratify his passion in a legitimate way. "marry she," say he, "you might as well marry ----," and he name one of the most notorious actress on the stage.
"she havenot a shred of a character." he know twenty man who be openly admirer of she, and name they, and the sum each have spend upon she. I know no kind of calumny more frightful or frequent than this which take away the character of woman, no man more reckless and mischievous than those who lightly use it, and no kind of coward more despicable than the people who invent these slander.
be it, or not, a misfortune that a man, himself of a candid disposition, and dispose, like our friend Pen, to blurt out the truth on all occasion, begin life by believe all that be say to he? would it be well for a lad to be less trustful, and so less honest? it require no small experience of the world to know that a man, who have no especial reason thereto, be tell you lie. I be not sure whether it be not good to go on be dupe for a certain time. at all event, our honest Pen have a natural credulity, which enable he to accept all statement which be make to he, and he take every one of Captain Macheath's figment as if they have be the most unquestioned fact of history.
so Bloundell's account about Miss Fotheringay pain and mortify Pen exceedingly. if he have be ashamed of his passion before,--what be his feeling regard it now, when the object of so much pure flame and adoration turn out to be only a worthless impostor, an impostor detect by all but he? it never occur to Pen to doubt the fact, or to question whether the story of a man who, like his new friend, never speak well of any woman, be likely to be true.
one Easter vacation, when Pen have announce to his mother and uncle his intention not to go down, but stay at Oxbridge and read, Mr. Pen be nevertheless induce to take a brief visit to London in company with his friend Mr. Bloundell. they put up at a hotel in Covent Garden, where Bloundell have a tick, as he call it, and take the pleasure of the town very freely after the willnot of young university man. Bloundell still belong to a military club, whither he take Pen to dine once or twice (the young man would drive thither in a cab, tremble lest they should meet Major Pendennis on his beat in Pall Mall), and here Pen be introduce to a number of gallant young fellow with spur and mustachio, with whom he drink pale-ale of morning and beat the town of a night. here he see a deal of life, indeed: nor in his career about the theatre and singing-house which these roar young blade frequent, be he very likely to meet his guardian. one night, nevertheless, they be very near to each other: a plank only separate Pen, who be in the box of the Museum Theatre, from the Major, who be in Lord Steyne's box, along with that venerate nobleman. the Fotheringay be in the pride of her glory. Shad make a hit: that is, she have draw very good house for nearly a year, have star the province with great eclat, have come back to shine in London with somewhat diminish lustre, and now be act with "ever increase attraction; etc.," "triumph of the good old british drama," as the play-bill avow, to house in which there be plenty of room for anybody who want to see she.
it be not the first time Pen have see she, since that memorable day when the two have part in Chatteris. in the previous year, when the town be make much of she, and the press laud her beauty, Pen have find a pretext for come to London in term-time, and have rush off to the theatre to see his old flame. he recollect it rather than renew it. he remember how ardently he use to be on the look-out at Chatteris, when the speech before Ophelia's or Mrs. Haller's entrance on the stage be make by the proper actor. now, as the actor speak, he have a sort of feeble thrill: as the house begin to thunder with applause, and Ophelia enter with her old bow and sweeping curtsey, Pen feel a slight shock and blush very much as he look at she, and could not help think that all the house be regard he. he hardly hear she for the first part of the play: and he think with such rage of the humiliation to which she have subject he, that he begin to fancy he be jealous and in love with she still. but that illusion do not last very long. he run round to the stage-door of the theatre to see she if possible, but he do not succeed. she pass indeed under his nose with a female companion, but he do not know her,--nor do she recognise he. the next night he come in late, and stay very quietly for the afterpiece, and on the third and last night of his stay in London--why, Taglioni be go to dance at the Opera,--Taglioni! and there be to be Don Giovanni, which he admire of all thing in the world: so Mr. Pen go to Don Giovanni and Taglioni.
