this time the illusion about she be quite gone. she be not less handsome, but she be not the same, somehow. the light be go out of her eye which use to flash there, or Pen's no long be dazzle by it. the rich voice speak as of old, yet it do not make Pen's bosom thrill as formerly. he think he could recognise the brogue underneath: the accent seem to he coarse and false. it annoy he to hear the same emphasis on the same word, only utter a little louder: bad than this, it annoy he to think that he should ever have mistake that loud imitation for genius, or melt at those mechanical sob and sigh. he feel that it be in another life almost, that it be another man who have so madly love she. he be ashamed and bitterly humiliate, and very lonely. ah, poor Pen! the delusion be well than the truth sometimes, and fine dream than dismal waking.
they go and have an uproarious supper that night, and Mr. Pen have a fine headache the next morning, with which he go back to Oxbridge, have spend all his ready money.
as all this narrative be take from Pen's own confession, so that the reader may be assure of the truth of every word of it, and as Pen himself never have any accurate notion of the manner in which he spend his money, and plunge himself in much deep pecuniary difficulty, during his luckless residence at Oxbridge University, it be, of course, impossible for I to give any accurate account of his involvement, beyond that general notion of his way of life, which have be sketch a few page back. he do not speak too hardly of the roguery of the university tradesman, or of those in London whom he honour with his patronage at the outset of his career. even Finch, the money-lender, to whom Bloundell introduce he, and with whom he have various transaction, in which the young rascal's signature appear upon stamp paper, treat he, accord to Pen's own account, with forbearance, and never mulct he of more than a hundred per cent. the old college-cook, his fervent admirer, make he a private bill, offer to send he in dinner up to the very last, and never would have press his account to his die day. there be that kindness and frankness about Arthur Pendennis, which win most people who come in contact with he, and which, if it render he an easy prey to rogue, get he, perhaps, more goodwill than he merit from many honest man. it be impossible to resist his good-nature, or, in his bad moment, not to hope for his rescue from utter ruin.
at the time of his full career of university pleasure, he would leave the gay party to go and sit with a sick friend. he never know the difference between small and great in the treatment of his acquaintance, however much the unlucky lad's taste, which be of the sumptuous order, lead he to prefer good society; he be only too ready to share his guinea with a poor friend, and when he get money have an irresistible propensity for pay, which he never could conquer through life.
in his third year at college, the dun begin to gather awfully round about he, and there be a levee at his oak which scandalise the tutor, and would have scar many a stouter heart. with some of these he use to battle, some he would bully (under Mr. Bloundell's direction, who be a master in this art, though he take a degree in no other), and some deprecate. and it be report of he that little Mary Frodsham, the daughter of a certain poor gilder and frame-maker, whom Mr. Pen have think fit to employ, and who have make a number of beautiful frame for his fine print, come to Pendennis with a piteous tale that her father be ill with ague, and that there be an execution in their house, Pen in an anguish of remorse rush away, pawn his grand watch and every single article of jewellery except two old gold sleeve-button, which have belong to his father, and rush with the proceed to Frodsham's shop, where, with tear in his eye, and the deep repentance and humility, he ask the poor tradesman's pardon.
this, young gentleman, be not tell as an instance of Pen's virtue, but rather of his weakness. it would have be much more virtuous to have have no print at all. he still stand for the bauble which he sell in order to pay Frodsham's bill, and his mother have cruelly to pinch herself in order to discharge the jeweller's account, so that she be in the end the sufferer by the lad's impertinent fancy and folly. we be not present Pen to you as a hero or a model, only as a lad, who, in the midst of a thousand vanity and weakness, have as yet some generous impulse, and be not altogether dishonest.
we have say it be to the scandal of Mr. Buck the tutor that Pen's extravagance become know: from the manner in which he enter college, the associate he keep, and the introduction of Doctor Portman and the Major, Buck for a long time think that his pupil be a man of large property, and wonder rather that he only wear a plain gown. once on go up to London to the levee with an address from his Majesty's Loyal University of Oxbridge, Buck have see Major Pendennis at St. James's in conversation with two knight of the garter, in the carriage of one of whom the dazzle tutor see the Major whisk away after the levee. he ask Pen to wine the instant he come back, let he off from chapel and lecture more than ever, and feel perfectly sure that he be a young gentleman of large estate.
thus, he be thunderstruck when he hear the truth, and receive a dismal confession from Pen. his university debt be large, and the tutor have nothing to do, and of course Pen do not acquaint he, with his London debt. what man ever do tell all when press by his friend about his liability? the tutor learn enough to know that Pen be poor, that he have spend a handsome, almost a magnificent allowance, and have raise around he such a fine crop of debt, as it would be very hard work for any man to mow down; for there be no plant that grow so rapidly when once it have take root.
perhaps it be because she be so tender and good that Pen be terrify lest his mother should know of his sin. "I cannot bear to break it to she," he say to the tutor in an agony of grief. "o! sir, Ihave be a villain to her"--and he repent, and he wish he have the time to come over again, and he ask himself, "why, why do his uncle insist upon the necessity of live with great people, and in how much do all his grand acquaintance profit he?"
they be not shy, but Pen think they be, and slunk from they during his last term at college. he be as gloomy as a death'-head at party, which he avoid of his own part, or to which his young friend soon cease to invite he. everybody know that Pendennis be "hard up." that man Bloundell, who could pay nobody, and who be oblige to go down after three term, be his ruin, the man say. his melancholy figure might be see shirk about the lonely quadrangle in his battered old cap and tear gown, and he who have be the pride of the university but a year before, the man whom all the young one love to look at, be now the object of conversation at freshman's wine-party, and they speak of he with wonder and awe.
at last come the Degree Examinations. many a young man of his year whose hob-nail shoe Pen have deride, and whose face or coat he have caricature--many a man whom he have treat with scorn in the lecture-room or crush with his eloquence in the debating-club--many of his own set who have not half his brain, but a little regularity and constancy of occupation, take high place in the honour or pass with decent credit. and where in the list be Pen the superb, Pen the wit and dandy, Pen the poet and orator? ah, where be Pen the widow's darling and sole pride? let we hide our head, and shut up the page. the list come out; and a dreadful rumour rush through the university, that Pendennis of Boniface be pluck.
CHAPTER XXI
flight after Defeat
everybody who have the least knowledge of Heraldry and the peerage must be aware that the noble family of which, as we know, Helen Pendennis be a member, bear for a crest, a nest full of little pelican peck at the ensanguine bosom of a big maternal bird, which plentifully supply the little wretch with the nutriment on which, accord to the heraldic legend, they be suppose to be bring up. very likely female pelican like so to bleed under the selfish little beak of their young one: it be certain that woman do. there must be some sort of pleasure, which we man donot understand, which accompany the pain of be scarify, and indeed I believe some woman would rather actually so suffer than not. they like sacrifice themselves in behalf of the object which their instinct teach they to love. be it for a reckless husband, a dissipated son, a darling scapegrace of a brother, how ready their heart be to pour out their good treasure for the benefit of the cherish person; and what a deal of this sort of enjoyment be we, on one side, ready to give the soft creature! there be scarce a man that read this, but have administer pleasure in this fashion to his womankind, and have treat they to the luxury of forgive he. they donot mind how they live themselves; but when the prodigal come home they make a rejoicing, and kill the fat calf for he: and at the very first hint that the sinner be return, the kind angel prepare their festival, and Mercy and Forgiveness go smile out to welcome he. I hope it may be so always for all: if we have only Justice to look to, Heaven help we!
during the latter part of Pen's residence at the University of Oxbridge, his uncle's partiality have greatly increase for the lad. the Major be proud of Arthur, who have high spirit, frank manner, a good person, and high gentleman-like bearing. it please the old London bachelor to see Pen walk with the young patrician of his university, and he (who be never know to entertain his friend, and whose stinginess have pass into a sort of byword among some wag at the Club, who envy his many engagement, and do not choose to consider his poverty) be charm to give his nephew and the young lord snug little dinner at his lodging, and to regale they with good claret, and his very good bon mot and story: some of which would be injure by the repetition, for the Major's manner of tell they be incomparably neat and careful; and other, whereof the repetition would do good to nobody. he pay his court to their parent through the young man, and to himself as it be by their company. he make more than one visit to Oxbridge, where the young fellow be amuse by entertain the old gentleman, and give party and breakfast and fete, partly to joke he and partly to do he honour. he ply they with his story. he make himself juvenile and hilarious in the company of the young lord. he go to hear Pen at a grand debate at the Union, crow and cheer, and rap his stick in chorus with the cheer of the man, and be astounded at the boy's eloquence and fire. he think he have get a young Pitt for a nephew. he have an almost paternal fondness for Pen. he write to the lad letter with playful advice and the news of the town. he brag about Arthur at his Clubs, and introduce he with pleasure into his conversation; say, that, Egad, the young fellow be put the old one to the wall; that the lad who be come up, young Lord Plinlimmon, a friend of my boy, young Lord Magnus Charters, a chum of my scapegrace, etc., would make a great figure in the world than even their father have do before they. he ask permission to bring Arthur to a grand fete at Gaunt House; see he with ineffable satisfaction dance with the sister of the young nobleman before mention; and give himself as much trouble to procure card of invitation for the lad to some good house, as if he have be a mamma with a daughter to marry, and not an old half-pay officer in a wig. and he boast everywhere of the boy's great talent, and remarkable oratorical power; and of the brilliant degree he be go to take. Lord Runnymede would take he on his embassy, or the Duke would bring he in for one of his borough, he write over and over again to Helen; who, for her part, be too ready to believe anything that anybody choose to say in favour of her son.
and all this pride and affection of uncle and mother have be trample down by Pen's wicked extravagance and idleness! I donot envy Pen's feeling (as the phrase be), as he think of what he have do. he have sleep, and the tortoise have win the race. he have mar at its outset what might have be a brilliant career. he have dip ungenerously into a generous mother's purse; basely and recklessly spill her little cruse. o! it be a coward hand that could strike and rob a creature so tender. and if Pen feel the wrong which he have do to other, be we to suppose that a young gentleman of his vanity do not feel still more keenly the shame he have bring upon himself? let we be assure that there be no more cruel remorse than that; and no groan more piteous than those of wound self-love. like Joel Miller's friend, the Senior Wrangler, who bow to the audience from his box at the play, because he and the king happen to enter the theatre at the same time, only with a fatuity by no mean so agreeable to himself, poor Arthur Pendennis feel perfectly convinced that all England would remark the absence of his name from the examination-list, and talk about his misfortune. his wounded tutor, his many dun, the skip and bed-maker who wait upon he, the undergraduate of his own time and the year below he, whom he have patronise or scorn--how could he bear to look any of they in the face now? he rush to his room, into which he shut himself, and there he pen a letter to his tutor, full of thank, regard, remorse, and despair, request that his name might be take off the college book, and intimate a wish and expectation that death would speedily end the woe of the disgrace Arthur Pendennis.
then he slunk out, scarcely know whither he go, but mechanically take the unfrequented little lane by the back of the college, until he clear the university precinct, and get down to the bank of the Camisis river, now desert, but so often alive with the boat-race, and the crowd of cheer gownsman, he wander on and on, until he find himself at some mile' distance from Oxbridge, or rather be find by some acquaintance leave that city.
as Pen go up a hill, a drizzling January rain beat in his face, and his ragged gown fly behind he--for he have not divest himself of his academical garment since the morning--a postchaise come rattle up the road, on the box of which a servant be seat, whilst within, or rather half out of the carriage window, sate a young gentleman smoke a cigar, and loudly encourage the postboy. it be our young acquaintance of Baymouth Mr. Spavin, who have get his degree, and be drive homeward in triumph in his yellow postchaise. he catch a sight of the figure, madly gesticulate as he work up the hill, and of poor Pen's pale and ghastly face as the chaise whirl by he.
"Wo!" roar Mr. Spavin to the postboy, and the horse stop in their mad career, and the carriage pull up some fifty yard before Pen. he presently hear his own name shout, and beheld the upper half of the body of Mr. Spavin thrust out of the side-window of the vehicle, and beckon Pen vehemently towards it.
Pen stop, hesitate--nod his head fiercely, and point onwards, as if desirous that the postillion should proceed. he do not speak: but his countenance must have look very desperate, for young Spavin, have star at he with an expression of blank alarm, jump out of the carriage presently, run towards Pen hold out his hand, and grasp Pen's, say, "I say--hullo, old boy, where be you go, and whatbe the row now?"
"Ibe go where I deserve to go," say Pen, with an imprecation.
"this benot the way," say Mr. Spavin, smile. "this be the Fenbury road. I say, Pen, donot take on because you be pluck. itbe nothing when you be use to it. Ihave be pluck three time, old boy--and after the first time I donot care. Glad itbe over, though. youwill have well luck next time."
Pen look at his early acquaintance,--who have be pluck, who have be rusticate, who have only, after repeat failure, learn to read and write correctly, and who, in spite of all these drawback, have attain the honour of a degree. "this man have pass," he think, "and I have fail!" it be almost too much for he to bear.
"good-bye, Spavin," say he; "Ibe very glad you be through. donot let I keep you; Ibe in a hurry--i'm go to town to-night."
"Gammon," say Mr. Spavin. "this benot the way to town; this be the Fenbury road, I tell you."
"I be just go to turn back," Pen say.
"all the coach be full with the man go down," Spavin say. Pen wince. "youwould not get a place for a ten-pound note. get into my yellow; Iwill drop you at Mudford, where you have a chance of the Fenbury mail. Iwill lend you a hat and a coat; Ihave get lot. come along; jump in, old boy--go it, leathers!"--and in this way Pen find himself in Mr. Spavin's postchaise, and ride with that gentleman as far as the Ram Inn at Mudford, fifteen mile from Oxbridge; where the Fenbury mail change horse, and where Pen get a place on to London.
the next day there be an immense excitement in Boniface College, Oxbridge, where, for some time, a rumour prevail, to the terror of Pen's tutor and tradesman, that Pendennis, madden at lose his degree, have make away with himself--a battered cap, in which his name be almost discernible, together with a seal bear his crest of an eagle look at a now extinct sun, have be find three mile on the Fenbury road, near a mill-stream, and, for four-and-twenty hour, it be suppose that poor Pen have fling himself into the stream, until letter arrive from he, bear the London post-mark.
the mail reach London at the dreary hour of five; and he hasten to the inn at Covent Garden, at which he be accustom to put up, where the ever-wakeful porter admit he, and show he to a bed. Pen look hard at the man, and wonder whether boot know he be pluck? when in bed be could not sleep there. he toss about until the appearance of the dismal London daylight, when he spring up desperately, and walk off to his uncle's lodging in Bury Street; where the maid, who be scour the step, look up suspiciously at he, as he come with an unshaven face, and yesterday's linen. he think she know of his mishap, too.
"good even! Mr. Harthur, what as appene, sir?" Mr. Morgan, the valet, ask, who have just arrange the well-brush clothe and shiny boot at the door of his master's bedroom, and be carry in his wig to the Major.
"I want to see my uncle," he cry, in a ghastly voice, and fling himself down on a chair.
Morgan back before the pale and desperate-looking young man, with terrified and wonder glance, and disappear in his master's apartment.
the Major put his head out of the bedroom door, as soon as he have his wig on.
"what? examination over? Senior Wrangler, double First Class, hay? say the old gentleman--i'll come directly;" and the head disappear.
"they donot know what have happen," groan Pen; "what will they say when they know all?"
Pen have be stand with his back to the window, and to such a dubious light as Bury Street enjoy of a foggy January morning, so that his uncle could not see the expression of the young man's countenance, or the look of gloom and despair which even Mr. Morgan have remark.
but when the Major come out of his dressing-room neat and radiant, and precede by faint odour from Delcroix's shop, from which emporium Major Pendennis's wig and his pocket-handkerchief get their perfume, he hold out one of his hand to Pen, and be about address he in his cheery high-toned voice, when he catch sight of the boy's face at length, and drop his hand, say, "good God! Pen, whatbe the matter?"
"youwill see it in the paper at breakfast, sir," Pen say.
"see what?"
"my name benot there, sir."
"hang it, why should it be?" ask the Major, more perplexed.
"I have lose everything, sir," Pen groan out; "my honour's go; Ibe ruin irretrievably; I cannot go back to Oxbridge."
"lost your honour?" scream out the Major. "Heaven alive! you donot mean to say you have show the white feather?"
Pen laugh bitterly at the word feather, and repeat it. "no, it benot that, sir. Ibe not afraid of be shoot; I wish to God anybody would. I have not get my degree. I--I'm pluck, sir."
the Major have hear of pluck, but in a very vague and cursory way, and conclude that it be some ceremony perform corporally upon rebellious university youth. "I wonder you can look I in the face after such a disgrace, sir," he say; "I wonder you submit to it as a gentleman."
"I couldnot help it, sir. I do my classical paper well enough it be those infernal mathematic, which I have always neglect."
"be it--be it do in public, sir?" the Major say.
"what?"
"The--the plucking?" ask the guardian, look Pen anxiously in the face.
Pen perceive the error under which his guardian be labour, and in the midst of his misery the blunder cause the poor wretch a faint smile, and serve to bring down the conversation from the tragedy-key, in which Pen have be dispose to carry it on. he explain to his uncle that he have go in to pass his examination, and fail. on which the Major say, that though he have expect far well thing of his nephew, there be no great misfortune in this, and no dishonour as far as he see, and that Pen must try again.
"I again at Oxbridge," Pen think, "after such a humiliation as that!" he feel that, except he go down to burn the place, he could not enter it.
but it be when he come to tell his uncle of his debt that the other feel surprise and anger most keenly, and break out in speech most severe upon Pen, which the lad bore, as good might, without flinch. he have determine to make a clean breast, and have form a full, true, and complete list of all his bill and liability at the university, and in London. they consist of various item, such as:
London Tailor. oxbridge do. oxbridge do. Bill for horse. Haberdasher, for shirt and glove. Printseller. Jeweller. book. College Cook. bind. Grump, for dessert. Hairdresser and Perfumery. Bootmaker. hotel bill in London. Wine Merchant in London. sundrie.
all which item the reader may fill in at his pleasure--such account have be inspect by the parent of many university youth,--and it appear that Mr. Pen's bill in all amount to about seven hundred pound; and, furthermore, it be calculate that he have have more than twice that sum of ready money during his stay at Oxbridge. this sum he have spend, and for it have to show--what?
"you need not press a man who be down, sir," Pen say to his uncle, gloomily. "I know very well, sir, how wicked and idle I have be. my mother willnot like to see I dishonour, sir," he continue, with his voice fail; "and I know she will pay these account. but I shall ask she for no more money."
"as you like, sir," the Major say. "you be of age, and my hand be wash of your affair. but you cannot live without money, and have no mean of make it that I see, though you have a fine talent in spend it, and it be my belief that you will proceed as you have begin, and ruin your mother before you be five year older.--good morning; it be time for I to go to breakfast. my engagement willnot permit I to see you much during the time that you stay in London. I presume that you will acquaint your mother with the news which you have just convey to I."
and pull on his hat, and tremble in his limb somewhat, Major Pendennis walk out of his lodging before his nephew, and go ruefully off to take his accustomed corner at the Club. he see the Oxbridge examination-list in the morning paper, and read over the name, not understand the business, with mournful accuracy. he consult various old fogy of his acquaintance, in the course of the day, at his Clubs; Wenham, a Dean, various civilian; and, as it be call, "take their opinion," show to some of they the amount of his nephew's debt, which he have dot down on the back of a card, and ask what be to be do, and whether such debt be not monstrous, preposterous? what be to be done?--there be nothing for it but to pay. Wenham and the other tell the Major of young man who owe twice as much--five time as much--as Arthur, and with no mean at all to pay. the consultation, and calculation, and opinion, comfort the Major somewhat. after all, he be not to pay.
but he think bitterly of the many plan he have form to make a man of his nephew, of the sacrifice which he have make, and of the manner in which he be disappoint. and he write off a letter to Doctor Portman, inform he of the direful event which have take place, and beg the Doctor to break they to Helen. for the orthodox old gentleman preserve the regular routine in all thing, and be of opinion that it be more correct to "break" a piece of bad news to a person by mean of a (possibly maladroit and unfeeling) messenger, than to convey it simply to its destination by a note. so the Major write to Doctor Portman, and then go out to dinner, one of the sad man in any London dining-room that day.
Pen, too, write his letter, and skulk about London street for the rest of the day, fancy that everybody be look at he and whisper to his neighbour, "that be Pendennis of Boniface, who be pluck yesterday." his letter to his mother be full of tenderness and remorse: he weep the bitter tear over it--and the repentance and passion soothe he to some degree.
he see a party of roar young blade from Oxbridge in the coffee-room of his hotel, and slunk away from they, and pace the street. he remember, he say, the print which he see hang up at Ackermann's window in the rain, and a book which he read at a stall near the Temple: at night he go to the pit of the play, and see Miss Fotheringay, but he donot in the least recollect in what piece.
on the second day there come a kind letter from his tutor, contain many grave and appropriate remark upon the event which have befall he, but strongly urge Pen not to take his name off the university book, and to retrieve a disaster which, everybody know, be owe to his own carelessness alone, and which he might repair by a month's application. he say he have order Pen's skip to pack up some trunk of the young gentleman's wardrobe, which duly arrive with fresh copy of all Pen's bill lay on the top.
on the third day there arrive a letter from home; which Pen read in his bedroom, and the result of which be that he fall down on his knee with his head in the bedclothe, and then pray out his heart and humble himself; and have go downstairs and eat an immense breakfast he sally forth and take his place at the Bull and Mouth, Piccadilly, by the Chatteris coach for that evening.
CHAPTER XXII
Prodigal's return
such a letter as the Major write of course, send Doctor Portman to Fairoaks, and he go off with that alacrity which a good man show when he have disagreeable news to commit. he wish the deed be do, and do quickly. he be sorry, but que voulez-vous? the tooth must be take out, and he have you in the chair, and it be surprising with what courage and vigour of wrist he apply the forceps. perhaps he would not be quite so active or eager if it be his tooth; but, in fine, it be your duty to have it out. so the doctor, have read the epistle out to Myra and Mrs. Portman, with many damnatory comment upon the young scapegrace who be goin deeply and deep into perdition, leave those lady to spread the news through the Clavering society, which they do with their accustomed accuracy and despatch, and stride over to Fairoaks to break the intelligence to the widow.
she have the news already. she have read Pen's letter, and it have relieve she somehow. a gloomy presentiment of evil have be hang over she for many, many month past. she know the bad now, and her darling boy be come back to her repentant and tender-hearted. do she want more? all that the Rector could say (and his remark be both dictate by common-sense, and make respectable by antiquity) could not bring Helen to feel any indignation or particular unhappiness, except that the boy should be unhappy. what be this degree that they make such an outcry about, and what good would it do Pen? why do Doctor Portman and his uncle insist upon send the boy to a place where there be so much temptation to be risk, and so little good to be win? why donot they leave he at home with his mother? as for his debt, of course they must be paid;--his debts!--wasn't his father's money all his, and havenot he a right to spend it? in this way the widow meet the virtuous doctor, and all the arrow of his indignation somehow take no effect upon her gentle bosom.
for some time past, an agreeable practice, know since time ever so ancient, by which brother and sister be willnot to exhibit their affection towards one another, and in which Pen and his little sister Laura have be accustom to indulge pretty frequently in their childish day, have be give up by the mutual consent of those two individual. come back from college after an absence from home of some month, in place of the simple girl whom he have leave behind he, Mr. Arthur find a tall, slim, handsome young lady, to whom he could not somehow proffer the kiss which he have be in the habit of administer previously, and who receive he with a gracious curtsey and a proffered hand, and with a great blush which rise up to the cheek, just upon the very spot which young Pen have be use to salute.
I be not good at description of female beauty; and, indeed, do not care for it in the least (think that goodness and virtue be, of course, far more advantageous to a young lady than any mere fleeting charm of person and face), and so shall not attempt any particular delineation of Miss Laura Bell at the age of sixteen year. at that age she have attain her present altitude of five foot four inch, so that she be call tall and gawky by some, and a Maypole by other, of her own sex, who prefer littler woman. but if she be a Maypole, she have beautiful rose about her head, and it be a fact that many swain be dispose to dance round she. she be ordinarily pale, with a faint rose tinge in her cheek; but they flush up in a minute when occasion call, and continue so blush ever so long, the rose remain after the emotion have pass away which have summon those pretty flower into existence. her eye have be describe as very large from her early childhood, and retain that characteristic in later life. good-natured critic (always female) say that she be in the habit of make play with those eye, and ogle the gentleman and lady in her company; but the fact be, that Nature have make they so to shine and to look, and they could no more help so look and shin than one star can help be bright than another. it be doubtless to mitigate their brightness that Miss Laura's eye be provide with two pair of veil in the shape of the long and fine black eyelash, so that, when she close her eye, the same people who find fault with those orb, say that she want to show her eyelash off; and, indeed, I daresay that to see her asleep would have be a pretty sight.
as for her complexion, that be nearly as brilliant as Lady Mantrap's, and without the powder which her ladyship use. her nose must be leave to the reader's imaginaton: if her mouth be rather large (as Miss Piminy aver, who, but for her know appetite, one would think could not swallow anything large than a button) everybody allow that her smile be charming, and show off a set of pearly tooth, whilst her voice be so low and sweet, that to hear it be like listen to sweet music. because she be in the habit of wear very long dress, people of course say that her foot be not small: but it may be that they be of the size become her figure, and it do not follow, because Mrs. Pincher be always put her foot out, that all other lady should be perpetually bring theirs on the tapis. in fine, Miss Laura Bell at the age of sixteen, be a sweet young lady. many thousand of such be to be find, let we hope, in this country where there be no lack of goodness, and modesty, and purity, and beauty.
now Miss Laura, since she have learn to think for herself (and in the past two year her mind and her person have both develop themselves considerably) have only be half pleased with Pen's general conduct and bear. his letter to his mother at home have become of late very rare and short. it be in vain that the fond widow urge how constant Arthur's occupation and study be and how many his engagement. "it be well that he should lose a prize" Laura say "than forget his mother; and indeed, mamma, I donot see that he get many prize. why donot he come home and stay with you, instead of pass his vacation at his great friend' fine house? there be nobody there will love he half so much as--as you do." "as I do only, Laura?" sigh out Mrs. Pendennis. Laura declare stoutly that she do not love Pen a bit, when he do not do his duty to his mother nor would she be convince by any of Helen's fond argument, that the boy must make his way in the world; that his uncle be most desirous that Pen should cultivate the acquaintance of person who be likely to befriend he in life; that man have a thousand tie and call which woman could not understand, and so forth. perhaps Helen no more believe in these excuse than her adopt daughter do; but she try to believe that she believe they, and comfort herself with the maternal infatuation. and that be a point whereon I suppose many a gentleman have reflect, that, do what we will, we be pretty sure of the woman's love that once have be ours; and that that untire tenderness and forgiveness never fail we.
also, there have be that freedom, not to say audacity, in Arthur's latter talk and way, which have shock and displeased Laura. not that he ever offend she by rudeness, or address to she a word which she ought not to hear, for Mr. Pen be a gentleman, and by nature and education polite to every woman high and low; but he speak lightly and laxly of woman in general; be less courteous in his action than in his word--neglectful in sundry way, and in many of the little office of life. it offend Miss Laura that he should smoke his horrid pipe in the house; that he should refuse to go to church with his mother, or on walk or visit with she, and be find yawn over his novel in his dressing-gown, when the gentle widow return from those duty. the hero of Laura's early infancy, about whom she have pass so many, many night talk with Helen (who recite endless story of the boy's virtue, and love, and bravery, when he be away at school), be a very different person from the young man whom now she know; bold and brilliant, sarcastic and defiant, seem to scorn the simple occupation or pleasure, or even devotion, of the woman with whom he live, and whom he quit on such light pretext.
the Fotheringay affair, too, when Laura come to hear of it (which she do first by some sarcastic allusion of Major Pendennis, when on a visit to Fairoaks, and then from their neighbour at Clavering, who have plenty of information to give she on this head), vastly shock and outraged Miss Laura. a Pendennis fle himself away on such a woman as that! Helen's boy gallop away from home, day after day, to fall on his knee to an actress, and drink with her horrid father! a good son want to bring such a man and such a woman into his house, and set she over his mother! "I would have run away, mamma; I would, if I have have to walk barefoot through the snow," Laura say.
"and you would have leave I too, then?" Helen answer; on which, of course, Laura withdraw her previous observation, and the two woman rush into each other's embrace with that warmth which belong to both their nature, and which characterise not a few of their sex. whence come all whence come all the indignation of Miss Laura about Arthur's passion? perhaps she do not know, that, if man throw themselves away upon woman, woman throw themselves away upon man, too; and that there be no more accounting for love, than for any other physical liking or antipathy: perhaps she have be misinform by the Clavering people and old Mrs. Portman, who be vastly bitter against Pen, especially since his impertinent behaviour to the Doctor and since the wretch have smoke cigar in church-time: perhaps, finally, she be jealous; but this be a vice in which it be say the lady very seldom indulge.
albeit she be angry with Pen, against his mother she have no such feeling; but devote herself to Helen with the utmost force of her girlish affection--such affection as woman, whose heart be disengage, be apt to bestow upon the near female friend. it be devotion--it be passion--it be all sort of fondness and folly; it be a profusion of caress, tender epithet and endearment, such as it do not become sober historian with beard to narrate. do not let we man despise these instinct because we cannot feel they. these woman be make for our comfort and delectation, gentlemen,--with all the rest of the minor animal.
but as soon as Miss Laura hear that Pen be unfortunate and unhappy, all her wrath against he straightway vanish, and give place to the most tender and unreasonable compassion. he be the Pen of old day once more restore to she, the frank and affectionate, the generous and tender-hearted. she at once take side with Helen against Doctor Portman, when he outcry at the enormity of Pen's transgression. debt? what be his debt? they be a trifle; he have be throw into expensive society by his uncle's order, and of course be oblige to live in the same manner as the young gentleman whose company he frequent. disgrace by not get his degree? the poor boy be ill when he go in for the examination: he couldnot think of his mathematic and stuff on account of those very debt which oppress he; very likely some of the odious tutor and master be jealous of he, and have favourite of their own whom they want to put over his head. other people dislike he, and be cruel to he, and be unfair to he, she be very sure. and so, with flush cheek and eye bright with anger, this young creature reason; and she go up and seize Helen's hand, and kiss she in the Doctor's presence, and her look brave the Doctor, and seem to ask how he dare to say a word against her darling mother's Pen?
when that divine take his leave, not a little discomfited and amazed at the pertinacious obstinacy of the woman, Laura repeat her embrace and argument with tenfold fervour to Helen, who feel that there be a great deal of cogency in most of the latter. there must be some jealousy against Pen. she feel quite sure that he have offend some of the examiner, who have take a mean revenge of he--nothing more likely. altogether, the announcement of the misfortune vex these two lady very little indeed. Pen, who be plunge in his shame and grief in London, and tear with great remorse for thinking of his mother's sorrow, would have wonder, have he see how easily she bear the calamity. indeed, calamity be welcome to woman if they think it will bring truant affection home again: and if you have reduce your mistress to a crust, depend upon it that she willnot repine, and only take a very little bit of it for herself, provide you will eat the remainder in her company.
and directly the Doctor be go, Laura order fire to be light in Mr. Arthur's room, and his bed to be air; and have these preparation complete by the time Helen have finish a most tender and affectionate letter to Pen: when the girl, smile fondly, take her mamma by the hand, and lead she into those apartment where the fire be blaze so cheerfully, and there the two kind creature sate down on the bed, and talk about Pen ever so long. Laura add a postscript to Helen's letter, in which she call he her dear Pen, and bid he come home instantly, with two of the handsome dash under the word, and be happy with his mother and his affectionate sister Laura.
in the middle of the night--as these two lady, after read their bible a great deal during the evening, and after take just a look into Pen's room as they pass to their own--in the middle of the night, I say, Laura, whose head not unfrequently choose to occupy that pillow which the nightcap of the late Pendennis have be accustom to press, cry out suddenly, "Mamma, be you awake?"
Helen stir and say, "yes, Ibe awake." the truth be, though she have be lie quite still and silent, she have not be asleep one instant, but have be look at the night-lamp in the chimney, and have be think of Pen for hour and hour.
then Miss Laura (who have be act with similar hypocrisy, and lying, occupy with her own thought, as motionless as Helen's brooch, with Pen's and Laura's hair in it, on the frille white pincushion on the dressing-table) begin to tell Mrs. Pendennis of a notable plan which she have be form in her busy little brain; and by which all Pen's embarrassment would be make to vanish in a moment, and without the least trouble to anybody.
"you know, mamma," this young lady say, "that I have be live with you for ten year, during which time you have never take any of my money, and have be treat I just as if I be a charity girl. now, this obligation have offend I very much, because I be proud and do not like to be beholden to people. and as, if I have go to school--only I wouldn't--it must have cost I at least fifty pound a year, it be clear that I owe you fifty time ten pound, which I know you have put in the bank at Chatteris for I, and which donot belong to I a bit. now, to-morrow we will go to Chatteris, and see that nice old Mr. Rowdy, with the bald head, and ask he for it,--not for his head, but for the five hundred pound: and I dare say he will send you two more, which we will save and pay back; and we will send the money to Pen, who can pay all his debt without hurt anybody and then we will live happy ever after."
what Helen reply to this speech need not be repeat, as the widow's answer be make up of a great number of incoherent ejaculation, embrace, and other irrelative matter. but the two woman sleep well after that talk; and when the night-lamp go out with a splutter, and the sun rise gloriously over the purple hill, and the bird begin to sing and pipe cheerfully amidst the leafless tree and glisten evergreen on Fairoaks lawn, Helen wake too, and as she look at the sweet face of the girl sleep beside she, her lip part with a smile, blush on her cheek, her spotless bosom heaving and fall with gentle undulation, as if happy dream be sweep over it--Pen's mother feel happy and grateful beyond all power of word, save such as pious woman offer up to the Beneficent Dispenser of love and mercy--in whose honour a chorus of such praise be constantly rise up all round the world.
although it be January and rather cold weather, so sincere be Mr. Pen's remorse, and so determine his plan of economy, that he would not take an inside place in the coach, but sate up behind with his friend the Guard, who remember his former liberality, and lend he plenty of great-coat. perhaps it be the cold that make his knee tremble as he get down at the lodge-gate, or it may be that he be agitate at the notion of see the kind creature for whose love he have make so selfish a return. Old John be in wait to receive his master's baggage, but he appear in a fustian jacket, and no long wear his livery of drab and blue. "i'se garner and stable man, and live in the ladge now," this worthy man remark, with a grin of welcome to Pen, and something of a blush; but instantly as Pen turn the corner of the shrubbery and be out of eye-shot of the coach, Helen make her appearance, her face beam with love and forgiveness--for forgiving be what some woman love good of all.
we may be sure that the widow, have a certain other object in view, have lose no time in write off to Pen an account of the noble, the magnanimous, the magnificent offer of Laura, fill up her letter with a profusion of benediction upon both her child. it be probably the knowledge of this money-obligation which cause Pen to blush very much when he see Laura, who be in wait in the hall, and who this time, and for this time only, break through the little arrangement of which we have speak, as have subsist between she and Arthur for the last few year; but the truth be, there have be a great deal too much say about kiss in the present chapter.
so the Prodigal come home, and the fat calf be kill for he, and he be make as happy as two simple woman could make he. no allusion be make to the Oxbridge mishap, or question ask as to his farth proceeding, for some time. but Pen debate these anxiously in his own mind, and up in his own room, where he pass much time in cogitation.
a few day after he come home, he ride to Chatteris on his horse, and come back on the top of the coach. he then inform his mother that he have leave the horse to be sell; and when that operation be effect, he hand she over the cheque, which she, and possibly Pen himself, think be an act of uncommon virtue and self-denial, but which Laura pronounce to be only strict justice.
he rarely mention the loan which she have make, and which, indeed, have be accept by the widow with certain modification; but once or twice, and with great hesitation and stammering, he allude to it, and thank she; but it evidently pain his vanity to be beholden to the orphan for succour. he be wild to find some mean of repay she.
he leave off drink wine, and betook himself, but with great moderation, to the refreshment of whisky-and-water. he give up cigar-smoking; but it must be confess that of late year he have like pipe and tobacco as well or even well, so that this sacrifice be not a very severe one.
he fall asleep a great deal after dinner when he join the lady in the drawing-room, and be certainly very moody and melancholy. he watch the coach with great interest, walk in to read the paper at Clavering assiduously, din with anybody who would ask he (and the widow be glad that he should have any entertainment in their solitary place), and play a good deal at cribbage with Captain Glanders.
he avoid Dr. Portman, who, in his turn, whenever Pen pass, give he very severe look from under his shovel-hat. he go to church with his mother, however, very regularly, and read prayer for she at home to the little household. always humble, it be greatly diminish now: a couple of maid do the work of the house of Fairoaks: the silver dish-cover never see the light at all.
John put on his livery to go to church, and assert his dignity on Sundays, but it be only for form's sake. he be gardener and out-door man, vice Upton, resign. there be but little fire in Fairoaks kitchen, and John and the maid drink their evening beer there by the light, of a single candle. all this be Mr. Pen's doing, and the state of thing do not increas his cheerfulness.
for some time Pen say no power on earth could induce he to go back to Oxbridge again, after his failure there; but one day Laura say to he, with many blush, that she think, as some sort of reparation, of punishment on himself for his--for his idleness, he ought to go back and get his degree, if he could fetch it by do so; and so back Mr. Pen go.
a plucked man be a dismal being in a university; belong to no set of man there, and own by no one. Pen feel himself pluck indeed of all the fine feather which he have win during his brilliant year, and rarely appear out of his college; regularly go to morning chapel, and shut himself up in his room of night, away from the noise and supper of the undergraduate. there be no dun about his door, they be all pay--scarcely any card be leave there. the man of his year have take their degree, and be go. he go into a second examination, and pass with perfect ease. he be somewhat more easy in his mind when he appear in his bachelor's gown.
on his way back from Oxbridge he pay a visit to his uncle in London; but the old gentleman receive he with very cold look, and would scarcely give he his forefinger to shake. he call a second time, but Morgan, the valet, say his master be from home.
Pen come back to Fairoaks, and to his book and to his idleness, and loneliness and despair. he commence several tragedy, and write many copy of verse of a gloomy cast. he form plan of read and break they. he think about enlist--about the spanish legion--about a profession. he chafe against his captivity, and curse the idleness which have cause it. Helen say he be break his heart, and be sad to see his prostration. as soon as they could afford it, he should go abroad--he should go to London--he should be free from the dull society of two poor woman. it be dull--very, certainly. the tender widow's habitual melancholy seem to deepen into a sad gloom; and Laura see with alarm that the dear friend become every year more languid and weary, and that her pale cheek grow more wan.
CHAPTER XXIII
New face
the inmate of Fairoaks be drowsily pursue this humdrum existence, while the great house upon the hill, on the other side of the River Brawl, be shake off the slumber in which it have lie during the life of two generation of master, and give extraordinary sign of renew liveliness.
just about the time of Pen's little mishap, and when he be so absorb in the grief occasion by that calamity as to take no notice of event which befell person less interesting to himself than Arthur Pendennis, an announcement appear in the provincial journal which cause no small sensation in the county at least, and in all the town, village, hall and mansion, and parsonage for many mile round Clavering Park. at Clavering market; at Cackleby Fair; at Chatteris Sessions; on Gooseberry Green, as the squire's carriage meet the vicar's one-horse contrivance, and the inmate of both vehicle stop on the road to talk; at Tinkleton Church gate, as the bell be toll in the sunshine, and the white smock and scarlet cloak come troop over the green common, to Sunday worship; in a hundred society round about--the word be, that Clavering Park be to be inhabit again.
some five year before, the county paper have advertise the marriage at Florence, at the British Legation, of Francis Clavering, Esq., only son of Sir Francis Clavering, Bart., of Clavering Park, with Jemima Augusta, daughter of Samuel Snell, of Calcutta, Esq., and widow of the late J. Amory, Esq. at that time the legend in the county be that Clavering, who have be ruin for many a year, have marry a widow from India with some money. some of the county folk catch a sight of the newly-married pair. the Kickleburys, travel in Italy, have see they. Clavering occupy the Poggi Palace at Florence, give party, and live comfortably--but could never come to England. another year--young peregrine, of Cackleby, make a Long Vacation tour, have fall in with the Claverings occupy Schloss Schinkenstein, on the Mummel see. at Rome, at Lucca, at Nice, at the bath and gambling place of the Rhine and Belgium, this worthy couple might occasionally be hear of by the curious, and rumour of they come, as it be by gust, to Clavering's ancestral place.
their last place of abode be Paris, where they appear to have live in great fashion and splendour after the news of the death of Samuel Snell, Esq., of Calcutta, reach his orphan daughter in Europe.
of Sir Francis Clavering's antecedent little can be say that would be advantageous to that respected baronet. the son of an outlaw, live in a dismal old chateau near bruge, this gentleman have make a feeble attempt to start in life with a commission in a dragoon regiment, and have break down almost at the outset. transaction at the gambling-table have speedily effect his ruin; after a couple of year in the army he have be force to sell out, have pass some time in her Majesty's prison of the Fleet, and have then ship over to Ostend to join the gouty exile, his father. and in Belgium, France and Germany, for some year, this decayed and abortive prodigal might be see lurk about billiard-room and watering-place, punt at gambling-house, dance at boarding-house ball, and rid steeple-chase on other folk' horse.
it be at a boarding-house at Lausanne that Francis Clavering make what he call the lucky coup of marry the widow Amory, very lately return from Calcutta. his father die soon after, by consequence of whose demise his wife become Lady Clavering. the title so delight Mr. Snell of Calcutta, that he double his daughter's allowance; and die himself soon after, leave a fortune to she and her child the amount of which be, if not magnify by rumour, something very splendid indeed.
before this time there have be, not rumour unfavourable to Lady Clavering's reputation, but unpleasant impression regard her ladyship. the good english people abroad be shy of make her acquaintance; her manner be not the most refine; her origin be lamentably low and doubtful. the retired East Indians, who be to be find in considerable force in most of the continental town frequent by English, speak with much scorn of the disreputable old lawyer and indigo-smuggler her father, and of Amory, her first husband, who have be mate of the Indiaman in which Miss Snell come out to join her father at Calcutta. neither father nor daughter be in society at Calcutta, or have ever be hear of at Government House. Old Sir Jasper Rogers, who have be Chief Justice of Calcutta, have once say to his wife, that he could tell a queer story about Lady Clavering's first husband; but greatly to Lady Rogers's disappointment, and that of the young lady his daughter, the old Judge could never be get to reveal that mystery.
they be all, however, glad enough to go to Lady Clavering's party, when her ladyship take the Hotel Bouilli in the Rue Grenelle at Paris, and blaze out in the polite world there in the winter of 183--. the Faubourg St. Germain take she up. Viscount Bagwig, our excellent ambassador, pay her marked attention. the prince of the family frequent her salon. the most rigid and note of the english lady resident in the french capital acknowledge and countenance she; the virtuous Lady Elderbury, the severe Lady Rockminster, the venerable Countess of Southdown--people, in a word, renowne for austerity, and of quite a dazzle moral purity:--so great and beneficent an influence have the possession of ten (some say twenty) thousand a year exercise upon Lady Clavering's character and reputation. and her munificence and good-will be unbounded. anybody (in society) who have a scheme of charity be sure to find her purse open. the french lady of piety get money from she to support their school and convent; she subscribe indifferently for the armenian patriarch; for Father Barbarossa, who come to Europe to collect fund for his monastery on Mount Athos; for the Baptist Mission to Quashyboo, and the Orthodox Settlement in Feefawfoo, the large and most savage of the Cannibal Islands. and it be on record of she, that, on the same day on which Madame de Cricri get five napoleon from she in support of the poor persecute Jesuits, who be at that time in very bad odour in France, Lady Budelight put she down in her subscription-list for the Rev. J. Ramshorn, who have have a vision which order he to convert the Pope of Rome. and more than this, and for the benefit of the worldly, her ladyship give the good dinner, and the grand ball and supper, which be know at Paris during that season.
and it be during this time, that the good-natured lady must have arrange matter with her husband's creditor in England, for Sir Francis reappear in his native country, without fear of arrest; be announce in the Morning Post, and the county paper, as have take up his residence at Mivart's Hotel; and one day the anxious old housekeeper at Clavering House beheld a carriage and four horse drive up the long avenue, and stop before the moss-grow step in front of the vast melancholy portico.
three gentleman be in the carriage--an open one. on the back seat be our old acquaintance, Mr. Tatham of Chatteris, whilst in the place of honour sate a handsome and portly gentleman envelop in mustachio, whisker, fur collar, and braid, and by he a pale languid man who descend feebly from the carriage, when the little lawyer, and the gentleman in fur, nimbly jump out of it.
they walk up the great moss-grow step to the hall-door, and a foreign attendant, with earring and a gold-laced cap, pull strenuously at the great bell-handle at the cracked and sculpture gate. the bell be hear clang loudly through the vast gloomy mansion. step resound presently upon the marble pavement of the hall within; and the door open, and finally Mrs. Blenkinsop, the housekeeper, Polly, her aide-de-camp, and Smart, the keeper, appear bow humbly.
Smart, the keeper, pull the wisp of hay-coloured hair which adorn his sunburnt forehead, kick out his left heel as if there be a dog bite at his calf, and bring down his head to a bow. Old Mrs Blenkinsop drop a curtsey. Little Polly, her aide-de-camp, make a curtsey and several rapid bow likewise; and Mrs. Blenkinsop, with a great deal of emotion, quaver out, "welcome to Clavering, Sir Francis. it du my poor eye good to see one of the family once more."
the speech and the greeting be all address to the grand gentleman in fur and braiding, who wear his hat so magnificently on one side, and twirl his mustachio so royally. but he burst out laugh, and say, "youhave saddle the wrong horse, old lady--i'm not Sir Francis Clavering whatbe come to revisit the hall of my ancestor. friend and vassal! behold your rightful lord!"
and he point his hand towards the pale, languid gentleman who say, "donot be an ass, Ned"
"yes, Mrs. Blenkinsop, Ibe Sir Francis Clavering; I recollect you quite well. forgot I, I suppose?--how dy do?" and he take the old lady's trembling hand; and nod in her astonished face, in a not unkind manner.
Mrs. Blenkinsop declare upon her conscience that she would have know Sir Francis anywhere, that he be the very image of Sir Francis, his father, and of Sir John who have go before.
"o yes--thanky--of course--very much oblige--and that sort of thing," Sir Francis say, look vacantly about the hall "dismal old place, benot it, Ned? never see it but once, when my governor quarrel with gwandfather in the year twenty-thwee.
"Dismal?--beautiful!--the Castle of Otranto!--the Mysteries of Udolpho, by Jove!" say the individual address as Ned. "what a fireplace! you might roast an elephant in it. splendid carve gallery! Inigo Jones, by Jove! Iwould lay five to two itbe Inigo Jones."
"the upper part by Inigo Jones; the low be alter by the eminent dutch architect, Vanderputty, in George the first his time, by Sir Richard, fourth baronet," say the housekeeper.
"o indeed," say the Baronet "Gad, Ned, you know everything."
"I know a few thing, Frank," Ned answer. "I know thatbe not a Snyders over the mantelpiece--bet you three to one itbe a copy. wewill restore it, my boy. a lick of varnish, and it will come out wonderfully, sir. that old fellow in the red gown, I suppose, be Sir Richard."
"Sheriff of the county, and sate in parliament in the reign of Queen Anne," say the housekeeper, wonder at the stranger's knowledge; "that on the right be Theodosia, wife of Harbottle, second baronet, by Lely, represent in the character of Venus, the Goddess of Beauty,--her son Gregory, the third baronet, by her side, as Cupid, God of Love, with a bow and arrow; that on the next panel be Sir Rupert, make a knight banneret by Charles the First, and whose property be confuscate by Oliver Cromwell."
"thank you--needn't go on, Mrs. Blenkinsop," say the Baronet, "wewill walk about the place ourselves. Frosch, give I a cigar. have a cigar, Mr. Tatham?"
little Mr. Tatham try a cigar which Sir Francis's courier hand to he, and over which the lawyer splutter fearfully. "neednot come with we, Mrs. Blenkinsop. what's--his--name--you--Smart--feed the horse and wash their mouth. shallnot stay long. come along, Strong,--I know the way: I be here in twenty-thwee, at the end of my gwandfather's time." and Sir Francis and Captain Strong, for such be the style and title of Sir Francis's friend, pass out of the hall into the reception-room, leave the discomfited Mrs. Blenkinsop to disappear by a side-door which lead to her apartment, now the only habitable room in the long-uninhabited mansion.
it be a place so big that no tenant could afford to live in it; and Sir Francis and his friend walk through room after room, admire their vastness and dreary and deserted grandeur. on the right of the hall-door be the saloon and drawing-room, and on the other side the oak room, the parlour, the grand dining-room, the library, where Pen have find book in old day. round three side of the hall run a gallery, by which, and correspond passage, the chief bedroom be approach, and of which many be of stately proportion and exhibit mark of splendour. on the second story be a labyrinth of little discomfortable garret, destine for the attendant of the great folk who inhabit the mansion in the day when it be first build: and I do not know any more cheering mark of the increase philanthropy of our own time, than to contrast our domestic architecture with that of our ancestor, and to see how much well servant and poor be care for now, than in time when my lord and my lady sleep under gold canopy, and their servant lie above they in quarter not so airy or so clean as stable be now.
up and down the house the two gentleman wander, the owner of the mansion be very silent and resign about the pleasure of possess it; whereas the Captain, his friend, examine the premise with so much interest and eagerness that you would have think he be the master, and the other the indifferent spectator of the place. "I see capability in it--capability in it, sir," cry the Captain. "Gad, sir, leave it to I, and Iwill make it the pride of the country, at a small expense. what a theatre we can have in the library here, the curtain between the column which divide the room! what a famous room for a galop!--it will hold the whole shire. wewill hang the morning parlour with the tapestry in your second salon in the Rue de Grenelle, and furnish the oak room with the Moyen-age cabinet and the armour. Armour look splendid against black oak, and therebe a Venice glass in the Quai Voltaire, which will suit that high mantelpiece to an inch, sir. the long saloon, white and crimson of course; the drawing-room yellow satin; and the little drawing-room light blue, with lace over--hay?"
"I recollect my old governor can I in that little room," Sir Francis say sententiously; "he always hat I, my old governor."
"Chintz be the dodge, I suppose, for my lady's room--the suite in the landing, to the south, the bedroom, the sitting-room, and the dressing-room. wewill throw a conservatory out, over the balcony. where will you have your room?"
"put mine in the north wing," say the Baronet, with a yawn, "and out of the reach of Miss Amory's confounded piano. I cannot bear it. shebe scweeche from morning till night."
the Captain burst out laugh. he settle the whole further arrangement of the house in the course of their walk through it; and, the promenade end, they go into the steward's room, now inhabit by Mrs. Blenkinsop, and where Mr. Tatham be sit pore over a plan of the estate, and the old housekeeper have prepare a collation in honour of her lord and master.
then they inspect the kitchen and stable, about both of which Sir Francis be rather interested, and Captain Strong be for examine the garden; but the Baronet say, "d--- the garden, and that sort of thing!" and finally he drive away from the house as unconcernedly as he have enter it; and that night the people of Clavering learn that Sir Francis Clavering have pay a visit to the Park, and be come to live in the county.
when this fact come to be know at Chatteris, all the folk in the place be set in commotion: High Church and Low Church, half-pay captain and old maid and dowager, sporting squireen of the viciniage, farmer, tradesman, and factory people--all the population in and round about the little place. the news be bring to Fairoaks, and receive by the lady there, and by Mr. Pen, with some excitement. "Mrs. Pybus say there be a very pretty girl in the family, Arthur," Laura say, who be as kind and thoughtful upon this point as woman generally be: "a Miss Amory, Lady Clavering's daughter by her first marriage. of course, you will fall in love with she as soon as she arrive."
Helen cry out, "donot talk nonsense, Laura." Pen laugh, and say, "well, there be the young Sir Francis for you."
"he be but four year old," Miss Laura reply. "but I shall console myself with that handsome officer, Sir Francis's friend. he be at church last Sunday, in the Clavering pew, and his mustachio be beautiful."
indeed the number of Sir Francis's family (whereof the member have all be mention in the above paragraph) be pretty soon know in his town, and everything else, as nearly as human industry and ingenuity could calculate, regard his household. the Park avenue and ground be dot now with town folk of the summer evening, who make their way up to the great house, peer about the premise, and criticise the improvement which be take place there. load upon load of furniture arrive in numberless van from Chatteris and London; and numerous as the van be, there be not one but Captain Glanders know what it contain, and escort the baggage up to the Park House.
he and Captain Edward Strong have form an intimate acquaintance by this time. the young Captain occupy those very lodging at Clavering, which the peaceful Smirke have previously tenant, and be deep in the good grace of Madame Fribsby, his landlady; and of the whole town, indeed. the Captain be splendid in person and raiment; fresh-colour, blue-eye, black-whisker, broad-cheste, athletic--a slight tendency to fulness do not take away from the comeliness of his jolly figure--a braver soldier never present a broad chest to the enemy. as he stride down Clavering High Street, his hat on one side, his cane clank on the pavement, or wave round he in the execution of military cut and soldatesque manoeuvre--his jolly laughter ring through the otherwise silent street--he be as welcome as sunshine to the place, and a comfort to every inhabitant in it.
on the first market-day he know every pretty girl in the market: he joke with all the woman: have a word with the farmer about their stock, and din at the Agricultural Ordinary at the Clavering Arms, where he set they all die with laugh by his fun and joke. "Tu be sure he be a vine veller, tu be sure that he be," be the universal opinion of the gentleman in top-boot. he shake hand with a score of they, as they ride out of the inn-yard on their old nag, wave his hat to they splendidly as he smoke his cigar in the inn-gate. in the course of the evening he be free of the landlady's bar, know what rent the landlord pay, how many acre he farm, how much malt he put in his strong beer; and whether he ever run in a little brandy unexcise by king from Baymouth, or the fishing village along the coast.
he have try to live at the great house first; but it be so dull he couldnot stand it. "I be a creature bear for society," he tell Captain Glanders. "Ibe down here to see Clavering's house set in order; for between ourselves, Frank have no energy, sir, no energy; hebe not the chest for it, sir (and he throw out his own trunk as he speak); but I must have social intercourse. Old Mrs. Blenkinsop go to bed at seven, and take polly with she. there be nobody but I and the Ghost for the first two night at the great house, and I own it, sir, I like company. Most old soldier do."
glander ask Strong where he have serve? Captain Strong curl his mustache, and say with a laugh, that the other might almost ask where he have not serve. "I begin, sir, as cadet of Hungarian Uhlans, and when the war of greek independence break out, quit that service in consequence of a quarrel with my governor, and be one of seven who escape from Missolonghi, and be blow up in one of Botzaris's fireship, at the age of seventeen. Iwill show you my Cross of the Redeemer, if youwill come over to my lodging and take a glass of grog with I, Captain, this evening. Ihave a few of those bauble in my desk. Ihave the White Eagle of Poland; Skrzynecki give it I" (he pronounce Skrzynecki's name with wonderful accuracy and gusto) "upon the field of Ostrolenka. I be a lieutenant of the fourth regiment, sir, and we march through Diebitsch's line--bang thro' them into Prussia, sir, without fire a shot. ah, Captain, that be a mismanaged business. I receive this wound by the side of the King before oporto,--where he would have pound the stock-job Pedroites, have Bourmont follow my advice; and I serve in Spain with the King's troop, until the death of my dear friend, Zumalacarreguy, when I see the game be over, and hang up my toasting iron, Captain. Alava offer I a regiment, the Queen's Muleteros; but I couldn't--damme, I couldn't--and now, sir, you know Ned Strong--the Chevalier Strong they call I abroad-- as well as he know himself."
in this way almost everybody in Clavering come to know Ned Strong. he tell Madame Fribsby, he tell the landlord of the George, he tell Baker at the reading-room, he tell Mrs. Glanders, and the young one, at dinner: and, finally, he tell Mr. Arthur Pendennis, who, yawn into Clavering one day, find the Chevalier Strong in company with Captain Glanders; and who be delighted with his new acquaintance.
before many day be over, Captain Strong be as much at home in Helen's drawing-room as he be in Madame Fribsby's first floor; and make the lonely house very gay with his good-humour and ceaseless flow of talk. the two woman have never before see such a man. he have a thousand story about battle and danger to interest they--about greek captive, polish beauty, and spanish nun. he could sing score of song, in half a dozen language, and would sit down to the piano and troll they off in a rich manly voice. both the lady pronounce he to be delightful--and so he be; though, indeed, they have not have much choice of man's society as yet, have see in the course of their life but few person, except old Portman and the Major, and Mr. Pen, who be a genius, to be sure; but then your genius be somewhat flat and moody at home.
and Captain Strong acquaint his new friend at Fairoaks, not only with his own biography, but with the whole history of the family now come to Clavering. it be he who have make the marriage between his friend Frank and the widow Amory. she want rank, and he want money. what match could be more suitable? he organise it; he make those two people happy. there be no particular romantic attachment between they; the widow be not of an age or a person for romance, and Sir Francis, if he have his game at billiard, and his dinner, care for little beside. but they be as happy as people could be. clavering would return to his native place and country, his wife's fortune would pay his encumbrance off, and his son and heir would be one of the first man in the county.
"and Miss Amory?" Laura ask. Laura be uncommonly curious about Miss Amory.
Strong laugh. "oh, Miss Amory be a muse--Miss Amory be a mystery--Miss Amory be a femme incomprise." "what be that?" ask simple Mrs. Pendennis--but the Chevalier give she no answer: perhaps could not give her one. "Miss Amory paint, Miss Amory write poem, Miss Amory compose music, Miss Amory rid like Diana Vernon. Miss Amory be a paragon, in a word."
"I hate clever woman," say Pen.
"thank you," say Laura. for her part she be sure she should be charm with Miss Amory, and quite long to have such a friend. and with this she look Pen full in the face, as if every word the little hypocrite say be Gospel truth.
thus, an intimacy be arrange and prepare beforehand between the Fairoaks family and their wealthy neighbour at the Park; and Pen and Laura be to the full as eager for their arrival, as even the most curious of the Clavering folk. a Londoner, who see fresh face and yawn at they every day may smile at the eagerness with which country people expect a visitor. a cockney come amongst they, and be remember by his rural entertainer for year after he have leave they, and forget they very likely--float far away from they on the vast London sea. but the islander remember long after the mariner have sail away, and can tell you what he say and what he wear, and how he look and how he laugh. in fine, a new arrival be an event in the country not to be understand by we, who donot, and have rather not, know who live next door.
when the painter and upholsterer have do their work in the house, and so beautify it, under Captain Strong's superintendence, that he might well be proud of his taste, that gentleman announce that he should go to London, where the whole family have arrive by this time, and should speedily return to establish they in their renovated mansion.
detachment of domestic precede they. carriage come down by sea, and be bring over from Baymouth by horse which have previously arrive under the care of groom and coachman. one day the 'Alacrity' coach bring down on its roof two large and melancholy man, who be drop at the Park lodge with their trunk, and who be Messieurs Frederic and James, metropolitan footman, who have no objection to the country, and bring with they state and other suit of the Clavering uniform.
on another day, the mail deposit at the gate a foreign gentleman, adorn with many ringlet and chain. he make a great riot at the lodge-gate to the keeper's wife (who, be a west-country woman, do not understand his English or his Gascon French), because there be no carriage in wait to drive he to the house, a mile off, and because he could not walk entire league in his fatigued state and varnish boot. this be Monsieur Alcide Mirobolant, formerly Chef of his Highness the Duc de Borodino, of H. Eminence Cardinal Beccafico, and at present Chef of the bouche of Sir Clavering, Baronet:--Monsieur Mirobolant's library, picture, and piano have arrive previously in charge of the intelligent young Englishman, his aide-de-camp. he be, moreover, aid by a profess female cook, likewise from London, who have inferior female under her order.
he do not dine in the steward's room, but take his nutriment in solitude in his own apartment, where a female servant be affect to his private use. it be a grand sight to behold he in his dressing-gown compose a menu. he always sate down and play the piano for some time before that. if interrupt, he remonstrate pathetically with his little maid. every great artist, he say, have need of solitude to perfectionate his work.
but we be advance matter in the fulness of our love and respect for Monsieur Mirobolant, and bring he prematurely on the stage.
the Chevalier Strong have a hand in the engagement of all the London domestic, and, indeed, seem to be the master of the house. there be those among they who say he be the house-steward, only he din with the family. Howbeit, he know how to make himself respect, and two of by no mean the least comfortable room of the house be assign to his particular use.
he be walk upon the terrace finally upon the eventful day when, amidst an immense jangling of bell from Clavering Church, where the flag be fly, an open carriage and one of those travel chariot or family ark, which only english philoprogenitiveness could invent drive rapidly with foam horse through the Park gate, and up to the step of the Hall. the two battan of the sculptured door fly open. the superior officer in black, the large and melancholy gentleman, now in livery with their hair in powder, the country menial engage to aid they, be in wait in the hall, and bow like elm when autumn wind wail in the park. through this avenue pass Sir Francis Clavering with a most unmoved face: Lady Clavering, with a pair of bright black eye, and a good-humour countenance, which waggle and nod very graciously: Master Francis Clavering, who be hold his mamma's skirt (and who stop the procession to look at the large footman, whose appearance seem to strike the young gentleman), and Miss Blandy, governess to Master Francis, and Miss Amory, her ladyship's daughter, give her arm to Captain Strong. it be summer, but fire of welcome be crackle in the great hall chimney, and in the room which the family be to occupy.
Monsieur Mirobolant have look at the procession from one of the lime-tree in the avenue. "Elle est la," he say, lay his jewelled hand on his richly-embroider velvet glass button, "Je t'ai vue, je te benis, o ma sylphide, o mon ange!" and he dive into the thicket, and make his way back to his furnace and saucepan.
the next Sunday the same party which have just make its appearance at Clavering Park, come and publicly take possession of the ancient pew in the church, where so many of the Baronet's ancestor have pray, and be now kneel in effigy. there be such a run to see the new folk, that the Low Church be desert, to the disgust of its pastor; and as the state barouche, with the grey and coachman in silver wig, and solemn footman, draw up at the old churchyard-gate, there be such a crowd assemble there as have not be see for many a long day. Captain Strong know everybody, and salute for all the company--the country people vow my lady be not handsome, to be sure, but pronounce she to be uncommon fine dress, as indeed she be--with the fine of shawl, the fine of pelisse, the brilliant of bonnet and wreath, and a power of ring, cameo, brooch, chain, bangle, and other nameless gimcrack; and ribbon of every breadth and colour of the rainbow flame on her person. Miss Amory appear meek in dove-colour, like a vestal virgin--while Master Francis be in the costume, then prevalent, of Rob Roy Macgregor, a celebrate Highland outlaw. the Baronet be not more animated than ordinarily--there be a happy vacuity about he which enable he to face a dinner, a death, a church, a marriage, with the same indifferent ease.
a pew for the Clavering servant be fill by these domestic, and the enrapture congregation see the gentleman from London with "vlower on their heed," and the miraculous coachman with his silver wig, take their place in that pew so soon as his horse be put up at the Clavering Arms.
in the course of the service, Master Francis begin to make such a yelling in the pew, that Frederic, the tall of the footman, be beckon by his master, and rise and go and carry out Master Francis, who roar and beat he on the head, so that the powder fly round about, like cloud of incense. nor be he pacify until place on the box of the carriage, where he play at horse with John's whip.
"you see the little beggarbe never be to church before, Miss Bell," the Baronet drawl out to a young lady who be visit he; "no wonder he should make a row: I donot go in town neither, but I think itbe right in the country to give a good example--and that sort of thing."
Miss Bell laugh and say, "the little boy have not give a particularly good example."
"Gad, I donot know, and that sort of thing," say the Baronet. "it benot so bad neither. whenever he want a thing, Frank always cwie, and whenever he cwie he get it."
here the child in question begin to howl for a dish of sweetmeat on the luncheon-table, and make a lunge across the table-cloth, upset a glass of wine over the good waistcoat of one of the guest present, Mr. Arthur Pendennis, who be greatly annoyed at be make to look foolish, and at have his spotless cambric shirt front blotch with wine.
"we do spoil he so," say Lady Clavering to Mrs. Pendennis, finally gaze at the cherub, whose hand and face be now froth over with the specie of lather which be insert in the confection call meringue a la creme.
"it be very wrong," say Mrs. Pendennis, as if she have never do such a thing herself as spoil a child.
"Mamma say she spoil my brother,--do you think anything could, Miss Bell? look at him,--isn't he like a little angel?"
"Gad, I be quite wight," say the Baronet. "he have cwie, and he have get it, you see. go it, Fwank, old boy."
