"Sir Francis be a very judicious parent," Miss Amory whisper. donot you think so, Miss Bell? I shallnot call you Miss Bell--I shall call you Laura. I admire you so at church. your robe be not well make, nor your bonnet very fresh. but you have such beautiful grey eye, and such a lovely tint."
"thank you," say Miss Bell, laugh.
"your cousin be handsome, and think so. he be uneasy de sa personne. he have not see the world yet. have he genius? have he suffer? a lady, a little woman in a rumpled satin and velvet shoe--a Miss Pybus--come here, and say he have suffer. I, too, have suffered,--and you, Laura, have your heart ever be touch?"
Laura say "no!" but perhaps blush a little at the idea or the question, so that the other said,--
"Ah Laura! I see it all. it be the beau cousin. tell I everything. I already love you as a sister."
"you be very kind," say Miss Bell, smile, "and--and it must be own that it be a very sudden attachment."
"all attachment be so. it be electricity--spontaneity. it be instantaneous. I know I should love you from the moment I see you. do you not feel it yourself?"
"not yet," say Laura; "but--I daresay I shall if I try."
"call I by my name, then."
"but I donot know it," Laura cry out.
"my name be Blanche--isn't it a pretty name? call I by it."
"Blanche--it be very pretty, indeed."
"and while mamma talk with that kind-look lady--what relation be she to you? she must have be pretty once, but be rather passee; she be not well gantee, but she have a pretty hand--and while mamma talk to she, come with I to my own room,--my own, own room. itbe a darling room, though that horrid creature, Captain Strong, do arrange it. be you eprise of he? he say you be, but I know well; it be the beau cousin. yes--il a de beaux yeux. Je n'aime pas les blond, ordinairement. car je suis blonde moi--je suis Blanche et blonde,"--and she look at her face and make a moue in the glass; and never stop for Laura's answer to the question which she have put.
Blanche be fair, and like a sylph. she have fair hair, with green reflection in it. but she have dark eyebrow. she have long black eyelash, which veil beautiful brown eye. she have such a slim waist, that it be a wonder to behold; and such a slim little foot, that you would have think the grass would hardly bend under they. her lip be of the colour of faint rosebud, and her voice warble limpidly over a set of the sweet little pearly tooth ever see. she show they very often, for they be very pretty. she be very good-natured, and a smile not only show her tooth wonderfully, but likewise exhibit two lovely little pink dimple, that nestle in either cheek.
she show Laura her drawing, which the other think charming. she play she some of her waltz, with a rapid and brilliant finger, and Laura be still more charmed. and she then read she some poem, in French and English, likewise of her own composition, and which she keep lock in her own book--her own dear little book; it be bind in blue velvet, with a gilt lock, and on it be print in gold the title of 'Mes Larmes.'
"Mes larmes!--isn't it a pretty name?" the young lady continue, who be pleased with everything that she do, and do everything very well. Laura own that it be. she have never see anything like it before; anything so lovely, so accomplished, so fragile and pretty; warble so prettily, and trip about such a pretty room, with such a number of pretty book, picture, flower, round about she. the honest and generous country girl forget even jealousy in her admiration. "indeed, Blanche," she say, "everything in the room be pretty; and you be the prettiest of all." the other smile, look in the glass, go up and take both of Laura's hand, and kiss they, and sit down to the piano, and shake out a little song, as if she have be a nightingale.
this be the first visit pay by fairoak to Clavering Park, in return for Clavering Park's visit to Fairoaks, in reply to Fairoaks's card leave a few day after the arrival of Sir Francis's family. the intimacy between the young lady spring up like Jack's bean-stalk to the sky in a single night. the large footman be perpetually walk with little rose-colour pink note to Fairoaks; where there be a pretty house-maid in the kitchen, who might possibly tempt those gentleman to so humble a place. Miss Amory send music, or Miss Amory send a new novel, or a picture from the 'Journal des Modes,' to Laura; or my lady's compliment arrive with flower and fruit; or Miss Amory beg and pray Miss Bell to come to dinner; and dear Mrs. Pendennis, if she be strong enough; and Mr. Arthur, if a humdrum party be not too stupid for he; and would send a pony-carriage for Mrs. Pendennis; and would take no denial.
neither Arthur nor Laura wish to refuse. and Helen, who be, indeed, somewhat ailing, be glad that the two should have their pleasure; and would look at they fondly as they set forth, and ask in her heart that she might not be call away until those two being whom she love well in the world should be join together. as they go out and cross over the bridge, she remember summer evening five-and-twenty year ago, when she, too, have bloom in her brief prime of love and happiness. it be all over now. the moon be look from the purpling sky, and the star glitter there, just as they use in the early, well-remember evening. he be lie dead far away, with the billow roll between they. good God! how well she remember the last look of his face as they part. it look out at she through the vista of long year, as sad and as clear as then.
so Mr. Pen and Miss Laura find the society at Clavering Park an uncommonly agreeable resort of summer evening. Blanche vow that she raffole of Laura; and, very likely, Mr. Pen be pleased with Blanche. his spirit come back: he laugh and rattle till Laura wonder to hear he. it be not the same Pen, yawn in a shooting jacket, in the Fairoaks parlour, who appear alert and brisk, and smile and well dress, in Lady Clavering's drawing-room. sometimes they have music. Laura have a sweet contralto voice, and sing with Blanche, who have have the good continental instruction, and be charm to be her friend's mistress. sometimes Mr. Pen join in these concert, or oftener look sweet upon Miss Blanche as she sing. sometimes they have glee, when Captain Strong's chest be of vast service, and he boom out in a prodigious bass, of which he be not a little proud.
"good fellow, strong--ain't he, Miss Bell?" Sir Francis would say to she. "play at ecarte with Lady Clavering--play anything, pitch-and-toss, pianoforty, cwibbage if you like. how long do you think hebe be stay with I? he come for a week with a carpet-bag, and Gad, hebe be stay here thwee year. good fellow, benot he? donot know how he get a shillin' though, begad I donot, Miss Lauwa."
and yet the Chevalier, if he lose his money to Lady Clavering, always pay it; and if he live with his friend for three year, pay for that too--in good-humour, in kindness and joviality, in a thousand little service by which he make himself agreeable. what gentleman could want a well friend than a man who be always in spirit, never in the way or out of it, and be ready to execute any commission for his patron, whether it be to sing a song or meet a lawyer, to fight a duel or to carve a capon?
although Laura and Pen commonly go to Clavering Park together, yet sometimes Mr. Pen take walk there unattended by she, and about which he do not tell she. he take to fish the Brawl, which run through the Park, and pass not very far from the garden-wall. and by the oddest coincidence, Miss Amory would walk out (have be to look at her flower), and would be quite surprised to see Mr. Pendennis fishing.
I wonder what trout Pen catch while the young lady be look on? or whether Miss Blanche be the pretty little fish which play round his fly, and which Mr. Pen be endeavour to hook? it must be own, he become very fond of that healthful and invigorate pursuit of angling, and be whip the brawl continually with his fly.
as for Miss Blanche she have a kind heart; and have, as she own, herself "suffer" a good deal in the course of her brief life and experience--why, she could compassionate other susceptible being like Pen, who have suffer too. her love for Laura and that dear Mrs. Pendennis redouble: if they be not at the Park, she be not easy unless she herself be at Fairoaks. she play with Laura; she read french and german with Laura; and Mr. Pen read french and german along with they. he turn sentimental ballad of Schiller and Goethe into english verse for the lady, and Blanche unlock 'Mes Larmes' for he, and impart to he some of the plaintive outpouring of her own tender Muse.
it appear from these poem that this young creature have indeed suffer prodigiously. she be familiar with the idea of suicide. death she repeatedly long for. a fade rose inspire she with such grief that you would have think she must die in pain of it. it be a wonder how a young creature (who have have a snug home or be at a comfortable boarding-school, and have no outward grief or hardship to complain of) should have suffer so much--should have find the mean of get at such an ocean of despair and passion (as a runaway boy who will get to sea), and have embark on it should survive it. what a talent she must have have for weep to be able to pour out so many of Mes Larmes!
they be not particularly briny, Miss Blanche's tear, that be the truth; but Pen, who read she verse, think they very well for a lady-- and write some verse himself for she. his be very violent and passionate, very hot, sweet and strong: and he not only write verse; but--o the villain! o, the deceiver! he alter and adapt former poem in his possession, and which have be compose for a certain Emily Fotheringay, for the use and to the christian name of Miss Blanche Amory.
CHAPTER XXIV
a little Innocent
every house have its skeleton in it somewhere, and it may be a comfort to some unhappy folk to think that the lucky and most wealthy of their neighbour have their misery and cause of disquiet. our little innocent Muse of Blanche, who sing so nicely and talk so sweetly, you would have think she must have make sunshine where ever she go, be the skeleton, or the misery, or the bore, or the Nemesis of Clavering House, and of most of the inhabitant thereof. as one little stone in your own shoe or your horse's, suffice to put either to torture and to make your journey miserable, so in life a little obstacle be sufficient to obstruct your entire progress, and subject you to endless annoyance and disquiet. who would have guess that such a smile little fairy as Blanche Amory could be the cause of discord in any family?
"I say, strong," one day the Baronet say, as the pair be converse after dinner over the billiard-table, and that great unbosomer of secret, a cigar; "I say, Strong, I wish to the doose your wife be dead."
"so do I. thatbe a cannon, by Jove. but she willnot; shewill live for ever-- you see if she donot. why do you wish she off the hook, Frank, my boy?" ask Captain Strong.
"because then you might marry Missy. she benot bad-look. shewill have ten thousand, and thatbe a good bit of money for such a poor old devil as you," drawl out the other gentleman.
"and gad, Strong, I hate she bad and bad every day. I cannot stand she, Strong, by gad, I cannot."
"I wouldnot take she at twice the figure," Captain Strong say, laugh. "I never see such a little devil in my life."
"I should like to poison she," say the sententious Baronet; "by Jove I should,"
"why, what have she be at now?" ask his friend.
"nothing particular," answer Sir Francis; "only her old trick. that girl have such a knack of make everybody miserable that, hang I, itbe quite surprising. last night she send the governess cry away from the dinner-table. afterwards, as I be pass Frank's room, I hear the poor little beggar howl in the dark, and find his sister have be frightening his soul out of his body, by tell he story about the ghost thatbe in the house. at lunch she give my lady a turn; and though my wife's a fool, shebe a good soul--i'm hang if she benot."
"what do Missy do to she?" Strong ask.
"why, hang I, if she donot begin talk about the late Amory, my predecessor," the Baronet say, with a grin. "she get some picture out of the Keepsake, and say she be sure it be like her dear father, she want to know where her father's grave be. hang her father! whenever Miss Amory talk about he, Lady Clavering always burst out cry: and the little devil will talk about he in order to spite her mother. today when she begin, I get in a confounded rage; say I be her father; and--and that sort of thing, and then, sir, she take a shy at I."
"and what do she say about you, Frank?" Mr. Strong, still laugh, inquire of his friend and patron.
"Gad, she say I benot her father; that I benot fit to comprehend she; that her father must have be a man of genius, and fine feeling, and that sort of thing: whereas I have marry her mother for money."
"well, donot you?" ask Strong.
"it donot make it any the pleasanter to hear because itbe true, donot you know," Sir Francis Clavering answer. "I benot a literary man and that; but I benot such a fool as she make I out. I donot know how it be, but she always manage to put I in the hole, donot you understand. she turn all the house round she in her quiet way, and with her confound sentimental air. I wish she be dead, Ned."
"it be my wife whom you want dead just now," Strong say, always in perfect good-humour; upon which the Baron with his accustomed candour, say, "well; when people bear my life out, I do wish they be dead, and I wish Missy be down a well, with all my heart."
thus it will be see from the above report of this candid conversation that our accomplished little friend have some peculiarity or defect of character which render she not very popular. she be a young lady of some genius, exquisite sympathy and considerable literary attainment, living, like many another genius, with relative who could not comprehend she. neither her mother nor her stepfather be person of a literary turn. Bell's Life and the Racing Calendar be the extent of the Baronet's reading, and Lady Clavering still write like a schoolgirl of thirteen, and with an extraordinary disregard to grammar and spelling. and as Miss Amory feel very keenly that she be not appreciate, and that she live with person who be not she equal in intellect or conversational power, she lose no opportunity to acquaint her family circle with their inferiority to herself, and not only be a martyr, but take care to let everybody know that she be so. if she suffer, as she say and think she did, severely, be we to wonder that a young creature of such delicate sensibility should shriek and cry out a good deal? without sympathy life be nothing; and would it not have be a want of candour on her part to affect a cheerfulness which she do not feel, or pretend a respect for those towards whom it be quite impossible she should entertain any reverence? if a poetess may not bemoan her lot, of what earthly use be her lyre? Blanche strike hers only to the sad of tune; and sing elegy over her dead hope, dirge over her early frost-nipt bud of affection, as become such a melancholy fate and Muse.
her actual distress, as we have say, have not be up to the present time very considerable: but her grief lay; like those of most of we, in her own soul--that be sad and habitually dissatisfied, what wonder that she should weep? so Mes Larmes dribble out of her eye any day at command: she could furnish an unlimited supply of tear, and her faculty of shed they increase by practice. for sentiment be like another complaint mention by Horace, as increase by self-indulgence (I be sorry to say, lady, that the complaint in question be call the dropsy), and the more you cry, the more you will be able and desirous to do so.
Missy have begin to gush at a very early age. Lamartine be her favourite bard from the period when she first could feel: and she have subsequently improve her mind by a sedulous study of novel of the great modern author of the french language. there be not a romance of Balzac and George Sand which the indefatigable little creature have not devour--by the time she be sixteen: and, however little she sympathise with her relative at home, she have friend, as she say, in the spirit-world, mean the tender Indiana, the passionate and poetic Lelia, the amiable Trenmor, that high-souled convict, that angel of the galleys,--the fiery Stenio,--and the other numberless hero of the french romance. she have be in love with Prince Rodolph and Prince Djalma while she be yet at school, and have settle the divorce question, and the right of woman, with Indiana, before she have leave off pinafore. the impetuous little lady play at love with these imaginary worthy as a little while before she have play at maternity with her doll. pretty little poetical spirit! it be curious to watch they with those plaything. to-day the blue-eyed one be the favourite, and the black-eyed one be push behind the drawer. to-morrow blue-eye may take its turn of neglect and it may be an odious little wretch with a burn nose, or tear bead of hair, and no eye at all, that take the first place in Miss's affection, and be dandle and caress in her arm.
as novelist be suppose to know everything, even the secret of female heart, which the owner themselves do not perhaps know, we may state that at eleven year of age Mademoiselle Betsi, as Miss Amory be then call, have feel tender emotion towards a young Savoyard organ-grinder at Paris, whom she persist in believe to be a prince carry off from his parent; that at twelve an old and hideous drawing-master (but, ah, what age or personal defect be proof against woman's love?) have agitate her young heart; and that, at thirteen, be at Madame de Caramel's boarding-school, in the Champs Elysees, which, as everybody know, be next door to Monsieur Rogron's (Chevalier of the Legion of Honour) pension for young gentleman, a correspondence by letter take place between the seduisante Miss Betsi and two young gentleman of the College of Charlemagne, who be pensioner of the Chevalier Rogron.
in the above paragraph our young friend have be call by a christian name different to that under which we be lately present to she. the fact be, that Miss Amory, call Missy at home, have really at the first be christen Betsy--but assume the name of Blanche of her own will and fantasy, and crown herself with it; and the weapon which the Baronet, her stepfather, hold in terror over she, be the threat to call she publicly by her name of Betsy, by which menace he sometimes manage to keep the young rebel in order.
we have speak just now of child's doll, and of the manner in which those little people take up and neglect their darling toy, and very likely this history will show that Miss Blanche assume and put away her live doll with a similar girlish inconstancy. she have have host of dear, dear, darling, friend ere now, and have quite a little museum of lock of hair in her treasure-chest, which she have gather in the course of her sentimental progress. some dear friend have marry: some have go to other school: one beloved sister she have lose from the pension, and find again, o, horror! her darling, her Leocadie keep the book in her father's shop, a grocer in the Rue du Bac: in fact, she have meet with a number of disappointment, estrangement, disillusionment, as she call they in her pretty french jargon, and have see and suffer a great deal for so young a woman. but it be the lot of sensibility to suffer, and of confide tenderness to be deceive, and she feel that she be only undergo the penalty of genius in these pang and disappointment of her young career.
meanwhile, she manage to make the honest lady, her mother, as uncomfortable as circumstance would permit; and cause she worthy stepfather to wish she be dead. with the exception of Captain Strong, whose invincible good-humour be proof against her sarcasm, the little lady rule the whole house with he tongue. if Lady Clavering talk about Sparrowgrass instead of Asparagus, or call an object a hobject, as this unfortunate lady would sometimes do, Missy calmly correct she, and frighten the good soul, her mother, into error only the more frequent as she grow more nervous under her daughter's eye.
it be not to be suppose, consider the vast interest which the arrival of the family at Clavering Park inspire in the inhabitant of the little town, that Madame Fribsby alone, of all the folk in Clavering, should have remain unmoved and incurious. at the first appearance of the Park family in church, Madame note every article of toilette which the lady wear, from their bonnet to their brodequin, and take a survey of the attire of the lady' maid in the pew allot to they. we fear that Doctor Portman's sermon, though it be one of his old and most value composition, have little effect upon Madame Fribsby on that day.
in a very few day afterwards, she have manage for herself an interview with Lady Clavering's confidential attendant in the housekeeper's room at the Park; and her card in French and English, state that she receive the new fashion from Paris from her correspondent Madame Victorine, and that she be in the custom of make court and ball dress for the nobility and gentry of the shire, be in the possession of Lady Clavering and Miss Amory, and favourably receive, as she be happy to hear, by those lady.
Mrs. Bonner, Lady Clavering's lady, become soon a great frequenter of Madame Fribsby's drawing-room, and partook of many entertainment at the milliner's expense. a meal of green tea, scandal, hot Sally-Lunn cake, and a little novel reading, be always at the service of Mrs. Bonner, whenever she be free to pass an evening in the town. and she find much more time for these pleasure than her junior officer, Miss Amory's maid, who seldom could be spar for a holiday, and be work as hard as any factory-girl by that inexorable little Muse, her mistress.
the Muse love to be dress becomingly, and, have a lively fancy and a poetic desire for change, be for alter her attire every day. she maid have a taste in dressmaking--to which art she have be an apprentice at Paris, before she enter into Miss Blanche's service there--be keep from morning till night alter and remodelle Miss Amory's habiliment; and rise very early and go to bed very late, in obedience to the untiring caprice of her little taskmistress. the girl be of respectable english parent. there be many of our people, colonist of Paris, who have see well day, who be not quite ruin, who do not quite live upon charity, and yet cannot get on without it; and as her father be a cripple incapable of work, and her return home would only increase the burthen and add to the misery of the family, poor Pincott be fain to stay where she could maintain herself, and spare a little relief to her parent.
our Muse, with the candour which distinguish she, never fail to remind her attendant of the real state of matter. "I should send you away, Pincott, for you be a great deal too weak, and your eye be fail you, and you be always cry and snivel and want the doctor; but I wish that your parent at home should be support, and I go on endure you for their sake, mind," the dear Blanche would say to her timid little attendant. or, "Pincott, your wretched appearance and slavish manner, and red eye, positively give I the migraine; and I think I shall make you wear rouge, so that you may look a little cheerful;" or, "Pincott, I cannot bear, even for the sake of your starve parent, that you should tear my hair out of my head in that manner; and I will thank you to write to they and say that I dispense with your service." after which sort of speech, and after keep she for an hour tremble over her hair, which the young lady love to have comb, as she peruse one of her favourite french novel, she would go to bed at one o'clock, and say, "Pincott, you may kiss I. good night. I should like you to have the pink dress ready for the morning." and so with blessing upon her attendant, she would turn round and go to sleep.
the Muse might lie in bed as long as she choose of a morning, and avail herself of that privilege; but Pincott have to rise very early indeed to get her mistress's task do; and have to appear next day with the same red eye and the same wan face, which displease Miss Amory by their want of gaiety, and cause the mistress to be so angry, because the servant persist in be and look unwell and unhappy. not that Blanche ever think she be a hard mistress. indeed, she make quite a friend of Pincott, at time, and write some very pretty verse about the lonely little tiring-maid, whose heart be far away. our beloved Blanche be a superior being, and expect to be wait upon as such. and I do not know whether there be any other lady in this world who treat their servant or dependant so, but it may be that there be such, and that the tyranny which they exercise over their subordinate, and the pang which they can manage to inflict with a soft voice, and a well-breed simper, be as cruel as those which a slave-driver administer with an oath and a whip.
but Blanche be a Muse--a delicate little creature, quite tremulous with excitability, whose eye fill with tear at the small emotion; and who know, but that it be the very fineness of her feeling which cause they to be froisse so easily? you crush a butterfly by merely touch it. vulgar people have no idea of the sensibility of a Muse.
so little Pincott be occupy all day and night in stitch, hemming, rip, comb, ironing, crimping, for her mistress; read to she when in bed,--for the girl be mistress of the two language, and have a sweet voice and manner--could take no share in Madame Fribsby's soiree, nor indeed be she much miss, or consider of sufficient consequence to appear at their entertainment.
but there be another person connect with the Clavering establishment, who become a constant guest of our friend, the milliner. this be the chief of the kitchen, Monsieur Mirobolant, with whom Madame Fribsby soon form an intimacy.
not have be accustom to the appearance or society of person of the french nation, the rustic inhabitant of Clavering be not so favourably impress by Monsieur Alcide's manner and appearance, as that gentleman might have desire that they should be. he walk among they quite unsuspiciously upon the afternoon of a summer day, when his service be not require at the House, in his usual favourite costume, namely, his light green frock or paletot, his crimson velvet waistcoat, with blue glass button, his pantalon Ecossais, of a very large and decide check pattern, his orange satin neckcloth, and his jean-boot, with tip of shiny leather,--these, with a gold-embroider cap, and a richly gilt cane, or other variety of ornament of a similar tendency, form his usual holiday costume, in which he flatter himself there be nothing remarkable (unless, indeed, the beauty of his person should attract observation), and in which he consider that he exhibit the appearance of a gentleman of good parisian ton.
he walk then down the street, grin and ogle every woman he meet with glance, which he mean should kill they outright, and peer over the railing, and in at the window, where female be, in the tranquil summer evening. but Betsy, Mrs. Pybus's maid, shrink back with a Lor bless we, as Alcide ogle she over the laurel-bush; the Miss Bakers, and their mamma, star with wonder; and presently a crowd begin to follow the interesting foreigner, of ragged urchin and child, who leave their dirt-pie in the street to pursue he.
for some time he think that admiration be the cause which lead these person in his wake, and walk on, please himself that he could so easily confer on other so much harmless pleasure. but the little child and dirt-pie manufacturer be presently succeed by follower of a large growth, and a number of lad and girl from the factory be let loose at this hour, join the mob, and begin laugh, jeering, hoot, and call opprobrious name at the Frenchman. some cry out "Frenchy! Frenchy!" some exclaim "Frogs!" one ask for a lock of his hair, which be long and in richly-flow ringlet; and at length the poor artist begin to perceive that he be an object of derision rather than of respect to the rude grinning mob.
it be at this juncture that Madame Fribsby spy the unlucky gentleman with the train at his heel, and hear the scornful shout with which they assail he. she run out of her room, and across the street to the persecute foreigner; she hold out her hand, and, address he in his own language, invite he into her abode; and when she have house he fairly within her door, she stand bravely at the threshold before the gibe factory girl and boy, and say they be a pack of coward to insult a poor man who could not speak their language, and be alone and without protection. the little crowd, with some ironical cheer and hooting, nevertheless feel the force of Madame Fribsby's vigorous allocution, and retreat before she; for the old lady be rather respected in the place, and her oddity and her kindness have make she many friend there.
Poor Mirobolant be grateful indeed to hear the language of his country ever so ill speak. frenchman pardon our fault in their language much more readily than we excuse their bad English; and will face our blunder throughout a long conversation, without the least propensity to grin. the rescue artist vow that Madame Fribsby be his guardian angel, and that he have not as yet meet with such suavity and politeness among les Anglaises. he be as courteous and complimentary to she as if it be the fairest and noblest of lady whom he be address for Alcide Mirobolant pay homage after his fashion to all womankind, and never dream of a distinction of rank in the realm of beauty, as his phrase be.
a cream, flavour with pineapple--a mayonnaise of lobster, which he flatter himself be not unworthy of his hand, or of she to whom he have the honour to offer it as an homage, and a box of preserve fruit of Provence, be bring by one of the chef's aide-de-camp, in a basket, the next day to the milliner's, and be accompany with a gallant note to the amiable Madame Fribsbi. "her kindness," Alcide say, "have make a green place in the desert of his existence,--h suavity would ever contrast in memory with the grossierete of the rustic population, who be not worthy to possess such a jewel." an intimacy of the most confidential nature thus spring up between the milliner and the chief of the kitchen; but I do not know whether it be with pleasure or mortification that Madame receive the declaration of friendship which the young Alcides proffer to she, for he persist in call she "La respectable Fribsbi," "La vertueuse Fribsbi,"--and in state that he should consider she as his mother, while he hop she would regard he as her son. ah! it be not very long ago, Fribsby think, that word have be address to she in that dear french language, indicate a different sort of attachment. and she sigh as she look up at the picture of her Carabineer. for it be surprising how young some people's heart remain when their head have need of a front or a little hair-dye,--and, at this moment, Madame Fribsby, as she tell young Alcide, feel as romantic as a girl of eighteen.
when the conversation take this turn--and at their first intimacy Madame Fribsby be rather inclined so to lead it--Alcide always politely diverge to another subject: it be as his mother that he persist in consider the good milliner. he would recognise she in no other capacity, and with that relationship the gentle lady be force to content herself, when she find how deeply the artist's heart be engage elsewhere.
he be not long before he describe to she the subject and origin of his passion.
"I declare myself to she," say Alcide, lay his hand on his heart, "in a manner which be as novel as I be charm to think it be agreeable. where cannot love penetrate, respectable Madame Fribsbi? Cupid be the father of invention!--I inquire of the domestic what be the plat of which Mademoiselle partook with most pleasure; and build up my little battery accordingly. on a day when her parent have go to dine in the world (and I be grieve to say that a grossi dinner at a restaurateur, in the Boulevard, or in the Palais Royal seem to form the delight of these unrefined person), the charming Miss entertain some comrade of the pension; and I advise myself to send up a little repast suitable to so delicate young palate. her lovely name be Blanche. the name of the maiden be white; the wreath of rose which she wear be white. I determine that my dinner should be as spotless as the snow. at her accustomed hour, and instead of the rude gigot a l'eau, which be ordinarily serve at her too simple table, I send she up a little potage a la Reine--a la Reine Blanche I call it,--as white as her own tint-- and confection with the most fragrant cream and almond. I then offer up at her shrine a filet de merlan a l'gnes, and a delicate plat which I designate as Eperlan a la Sainte-Therese, and of which my charming Miss partook with pleasure. I follow this by two little entree of sweetbread and chicken; and the only brown thing which I permit myself in the entertainment be a little roast of lamb, which I lay in a meadow of spinach, surround with croustillon, represent sheep, and ornament with daisy and other savage flower. after this come my second service: a pudding a la Reine Elizabeth (who, Madame Fribsbi know, be a maiden princess); a dish of opal-coloured plover's egg which I call Nid de tourtereaux a la Roucoule; place in the midst of they two of those tender volatile, bill each other, and confection with butter; a basket contain little gateaux of apricot, which, I know, all young lady adore; and a jelly of marasquin, bland insinuating, intoxicate as the glance of beauty. this I designate Ambroisie de Calypso a la Souveraine de mon Coeur. and when the ice be bring in--an ice of plombiere and cherry--how do you think I have shape they, Madame Fribsbi? in the form of two heart unite with an arrow, on which I have lay, before it enter, a bridal veil in cut-paper, surmount by a wreath of virginal orange-flower. I stand at the door to watch the effect of this entry. it be but one cry of admiration. the three young lady fill their glass with the sparkle Ay, and carry I in a toast. I hear it--I hear Miss speak of I--I hear she say, 'tell Monsieur Mirobolant that we thank he--we admire he--we love he!' my foot almost fail I as she speak.
"since that, can I have any reason to doubt that the young artist have make some progress in the heart of the English Miss? I be modest, but my glass inform I that I be not ill-looking. other victory have convince I of the fact."
"dangerous man!" cry the milliner.
"the blond miss of Albion see nothing in the dull inhabitant of their brumous isle, which can compare with the ardour and vivacity of the child of the South. we bring our sunshine with we; we be Frenchmen, and accustom to conquer. be it not for this affair of the heart, and my determination to marry an anglaise, do you think I would stop in this island (which be not altogether ungrateful, since I have find here a tender mother in the respectable Madame Fribsbi), in this island, in this family? my genius would use itself in the company of these rustic--the poesy of my art cannot be understand by these carnivorous insularie. no--the man be odious, but the woman--the woman! I own, dear Fribsbi, be seducing! I have vow to marry one; and as I cannot go into your market and purchase, accord to the custom of the country, I be resolve to adopt another custom, and fly with one to Gretna Grin. the blonde Miss will go. she be fascinated. her eye have tell I so. the white dove want but the signal to fly."
"have you any correspondence with she?" ask Fribsby, in amazement, and not know whether the young lady or the lover might be labour under a romantic delusion.
"I correspond with she by mean of my art. she partake of dish which I make expressly for she. I insinuate to she thus a thousand hint which as she be perfectly spiritual, she receive. but I want other intelligence near she."
"there be Pincott, her maid," say Madame Fribsby, who, by aptitude or education, seem to have some knowledge of affair of the heart, but the great artist's brow darken at this suggestion.
"Madame," he say, "there be point upon which a gallant man ought to silence himself; though, if he break the secret, he may do so with the least impropriety to his good friend--his adopt mother. know then, that there be a cause why Miss Pincott should be hostile to I--a cause not uncommon with your sex--jealousy."
"perfidious monster!" say the confidante.
"ah, no," say the artist, with a deep bass voice, and a tragic accent worthy of the Port St Martin and his favourite melodrame, "not perfidious, but fatal. yes, I be a fatal man, Madame Fribsbi. to inspire hopeless passion be my destiny. I cannot help it that woman love I. be it my fault that that young woman deperishe and languish to the view of the eye, consume by a flame which I cannot return? listen! there be other in this family who be similarly unhappy. the governess of the young Milor have encounter I in my walk, and look at I in a way which can bear but one interpretation. and Milady herself, who be of mature age, but who have oriental blood, have once or twice address compliment to the lonely artist which can admit of no mistake. I avoid the household, I seek solitude, I undergo my destiny. I can marry but one, and be resolve it shall be to a lady of your nation. and, if her fortune be sufficient I think Miss would be the person who would be most suitable. I wish to ascertain what her mean be before I lead she to Gretna Grin."
whether Alcides be as irresistible a conqueror as his namesake, or whether he be simply crazy, be a point which must be leave to the reader's judgment. but the latter if he have have the benefit of much french acquaintance, have perhaps meet with man amongst they who fancy themselves almost as invincible; and who, if you credit they, have make equal havoc in the heart of les Anglaises.
CHAPTER XXV
contain both Love and Jealousy
our reader have already hear Sir Francis Clavering's candid opinion of the lady who have give he her fortune and restore he to his native country and home, and it must be own that the Baronet be not far wrong in his estimate of his wife, and that Lady Clavering be not the wise or the well educate of woman. she have have a couple of year' education in Europe, in a suburb of London, which she persist in call Ackney to her die day, whence she have be summon to join her father at Calcutta at the age of fifteen. and it be on her voyage thither, on board the Ramchunder East Indiaman, Captain Bragg, in which ship she have two year previously make her journey to Europe, that she form the acquaintance of her first husband, Mr. Amory, who be third mate of the vessel in question.
we be not go to enter into the early part of Lady Clavering's history, but Captain Bragg, under whose charge Miss Snell go out to her father, who be one of the Captain's consignee, and part owner of the ramchunder and many other vessel, find reason to put the rebellious rascal of a mate in iron, until they reach the Cape, where the Captain leave his officer behind; and finally deliver his ward to her father at Calcutta, after a stormy and perilous voyage in which the ramchunder and the cargo and passenger incur no small danger and damage.
some month afterwards Amory make his appearance at Calcutta, have work his way out before the mast from the Cape--marry the rich Attorney's daughter in spite of that old speculator--set up as indigo-planter and fail--set up as agent and fail again--set up as editor of the Sunderbund Pilot and fail again--quarrel ceaselessly with his father-in-law and his wife during the progress of all these mercantile transaction and disaster, and end his career finally with a crash which compel he to leave Calcutta and go to New South Wales. it be in the course of these luckless proceeding, that Mr. Amory probably make the acquaintance of Sir Jasper Rogers, the respected Judge of the Supreme Court of Calcutta, who have be mention before: and, as the truth must out, it be by make an improper use of his father-in-law's name, who could write perfectly well, and have no need of an amanuensis, that fortune finally forsook Mr. Amory and cause he to abandon all further struggle with she.
not be in the habit of read the Calcutta law-report very assiduously, the european public do not know of these fact as well as people do in Bengal, and Mrs. Amory and her father find her residence in India not a comfortable one, it be agree that the lady should return to Europe, whither she come with her little daughter Betsy or Blanche, then four year old. they be accompany by Betsy's nurse, who have be present to the reader in the last chapter as the confidential maid of Lady Clavering, Mrs. Bonner: and Captain Bragg take a house for they in the near neighbourhood of his residence in Pocklington Street.
it be a very hard bitter summer, and the rain it rain every day for some time after Mrs. Amory's arrival. Bragg be very pompous and disagreeable, perhaps ashamed, perhaps anxious, to get rid of the indian lady. she believe that all the world in London be talk about her husband's disaster, and that the King and Queen and the Court of Directors be aware of her unlucky history. she have a good allowance from her father; she have no call to live in England; and she determine to go abroad. away she go, then, glad to escape the gloomy surveillance of the odious bully, Captain Bragg. People have no objection to receive she at the continental town where she stop, and at the various boarding-house, where she royally pay her way. she call Hackney Ackney, to be sure (though otherwise she speak English with a little foreign twang, very curious and not unpleasant); she dress amazingly; she be conspicuous for her love of eat and drinking, and prepare curry and pillaw at every boarding-house which she frequent; but her singularity of language and behaviour only give a zest to her society, and Mrs. Amory be deservedly popular. she be the most good-nature, jovial, and generous of woman. she be up to any party of pleasure by whomsoever propose. she bring three time more champagne and fowl and ham to the picnic than anyone else. she take endless box for the play, and ticket for the mask ball, and give they away to everybody. she pay the boarding-house people month beforehand; she help poor shabby mustachiod buck and dowager whose remittance have not arrive, with constant supply from her purse; and in this way she tramp through Europe, and appear at Brussels, at Paris, at Milan, at Naples, at Rome, as her fancy lead she. news of Amory's death reach she at the latter place, where Captain Clavering be then stay, unable to pay his hotel bill, as, indeed, be his friend, the Chevalier Strong; and the good-natured widow marry the descendant of the ancient house of Clavering--professing, indeed, no particular grief for the scapegrace of a husband whom she have lose. we have bring she thus up to the present time when she be mistress of Clavering Park, in the midst of which Mr. Pinckney, the celebrated painter, pourtraye she with her little boy by her side.
Missy follow her mamma in most of her peregrination, and so learn a deal of life. she have a governess for some time; and after her mother's second marriage, the benefit of Madame de Caramel's select pension in the Champs Elysees. when the Claverings come to England, she of course come with they. it be only within a few year, after the death of her grandfather, and the birth of her little brother, that she begin to understand that her position in life be alter, and that Miss Amory, nobody's daughter, be a very small personage in a house compare with Master Francis Clavering, heir to an ancient baronetcy and a noble estate. but for little Frank, she would have be an heiress, in spite of her father: and though she know, and care not much about money, of which she never have any stint, and though she be a romantic little Muse, as we have see, yet she could not reasonably be grateful to the person who have so contribute to change her condition: nor, indeed, do she understand what the latter really be, until she have make some further progress, and acquire more accurate knowledge in the world.
but this be clear, that her stepfather be dull and weak: that mamma drop her H's, and be not refine in manner or appearance; and that little Frank be a spoiled quarrelsome urchin, always have his way, always tread upon her foot, always upset his dinner on her dress, and keep she out of her inheritance. none of these, as she feel, could comprehend she: and her solitary heart naturally pin for other attachment, and she seek around she where to bestow the precious boon of her unoccupied affection.
this dear girl, then, from want of sympathy, or other cause, make herself so disagreeable at home, and frighten her mother and bore she stepfather so much, that they be quite as anxious as she could be that she should settle for herself in life; and hence Sir Francis Clavering's desire express to his friend, in the last chapter, that Mrs. Strong should die, and that he would take Blanche to himself as a second Mrs. Strong.
but as this could not be, any other person be welcome to win she: and a smart young fellow, well-look and well educate like our friend Arthur Pendennis, be quite free to propose for she if he have a mind, and would have be receive with open arm by Lady Clavering as a son-in-law, have he have the courage to come forward as a competitor for Miss Amory's hand.
Mr. Pen, however, besides other drawback, choose to entertain an extreme diffidence about himself. he be ashamed of his late failure, of his idle and nameless condition, of the poverty which he have bring on his mother by his folly, and there be as much of vanity as remorse in his present state of doubt and distrust. how could he ever hope for such a prize as this brilliant Blanche Amory, who live in a fine park and mansion, and be wait on by a score of grand domestic, whilst a maid-servant bring in their meagre meal at Fairoaks, and his mother be oblige to pinch and manage to make both end meet? obstacle seem for he insurmountable, which would have vanish have he march manfully upon they: and he prefer despairing, or dally with his wishes,--or perhaps he have not positively shape they as yet,--to attempt to win gallantly the object of his desire. many a young man fail by that species of vanity call shyness, who might, for the asking have his will.
but we do not pretend to say that Pen have, as yet, ascertain his: or that he be do much more than think about fall in love. Miss Amory be charming and lively. she fascinate and cajole he by a thousand art or natural grace or flattery. but there be lurk reason and doubt, besides shyness and vanity, withhold he. in spite of her cleverness, and her protestation, and her fascination, Pen's mother have divine the girl, and do not trust she. Mrs. Pendennis see Blanche light-minded and frivolous, detect many want in she which offend the pure and pious-minded lady; a want of reverence for her parent, and for thing more sacred, Helen think: worldliness and selfishness couch under pretty word and tender expression. Laura and Pen battle these point strongly at first with the widow--Laura be as yet enthusiastic about her new friend, and Pen not far-go enough in love to attempt any concealment of his feeling. he would laugh at these objection of Helen's, and say, "Psha, mother! you be jealous about Laura--all woman be jealous."
but when, in the course of a month or two, and by watch the pair with that anxiety with which brood woman watch over their son' affection --and in acknowledge which, I have no doubt there be a sexual jealousy on the mother's part, and a secret pang--when Helen see that the intimacy appear to make progress, that the two young people be perpetually find pretext to meet, and that Miss Blanche be at Fairoaks or Mr. Pen at the Park every day, the poor widow's heart begin to fail she--her darling project seem to vanish before she; and, give way to her weakness, she fairly tell Pen one day what her view and longing be; that she feel herself break, and not long for this world, and that she hop and pray before she go, that she might see her two child one. the late event, Pen's life and career and former passion for the actress, have break the spirit of this tender lady. she feel that he have escape she, and be in the maternal nest no more; and she clung with a sickening fondness to Laura, Laura who have be leave to she by Francis in Heaven.
Pen kiss and soothe she in his grand patronising way. he have see something of this, he have long think his mother want to make this marriage--do Laura know anything of it? (not she,--mrs. Pendennis said-- not for world would she have breathe a word of it to laura)--"well, well, there be time enough, his mother wouldnot die," Pen say, laughingly: "he wouldnot hear of any such thing, and as for the Muse, she be too grand a lady to think about poor little I--and as for Laura, who know that she would have I? she would do anything you tell she, to be sure. but be I worthy of she?"
"O, Pen, you might be," be the widow's reply; not that Mr. Pen ever doubt that he be; and a feeling of indefinable pleasure and self-complacency come over he as he think over this proposal, and image Laura to himself, as his memory remember she for year past, always fair and open, kindly and pious, cheerful, tender and true. he look at she with brighten eye as she come in from the garden at the end of this talk, her cheek rather flushed, her look frank and smile --a basket of rose in her hand.
she take the fine of they and bring it to Mrs. Pendennis, who be refresh by the odour and colour of these flower; and hang over she fondly and give it to she.
"and I might have this prize for the asking!" Pen think with a thrill of triumph, as he look at the kindly girl. "why, she be as beautiful and as generous as her rose." the image of the two woman remain for ever after in his mind, and he never recall it but the tear come into his eye.
before very many week' intimacy with her new acquaintance, however, Miss Laura be oblige to give in to Helen's opinion, and own that the Muse be selfish, unkind, and inconstant. of course Blanche confide to her bosom friend all the little grief and domestic annoyance; how the family could not comprehend she and she move among they an isolated being; how her poor mamma's education have be neglect, and she be force to blush for her blunder; how Sir Francis be a weak person deplorably unintellectual, and only happy when smoke his odious cigar; how, since the birth of her little brother, she have see her mother's precious affection, which she value more than anything in life, estrange from her once darling daughter; how she be alone, alone, alone in the world.
but these grief, real and heart-rending though they might be to a young lady of exquisite sensibility, do not convince Laura of the propriety of Blanche's conduct in many small incident of Little Frank, for instance, life might be very provoking, and might have deprive Blanche of her mamma's affection, but this be no reason why Blanche should box the child's ear because he upset a glass of water over her drawing, and why she should call he many opprobrious name in the English and french language; and the preference accord to little Frank be certainly no reason why Blanche should give herself imperial air of command towards the boy's governess, and send that young lady upon message through the house to bring her book or to fetch her pocket-handkerchief. when a domestic perform an errand for honest Laura, she be always thankful and please; whereas she could not but perceive that the little Muse have not the slight scruple in give her command to all the world round about she, and in disturb anybody's ease or comfort, in order to administer to her own. it be Laura's first experience in friendship; and it pain the kind creature's heart to be oblige to give up as delusion, one by one, those charm and brilliant quality in which her fancy have dress her new friend, and to find that the fascinating little fairy be but a mortal, and not a very amiable mortal after all. what generous person be there that have not be so deceive in his time?--what person, perhaps, that have not so disappointed other in his turn?
after the scene with little Frank, in which that refractory son and heir of the house of Clavering have receive the compliment in French and English, and the accompany box on the ear from his sister, Miss Laura who have plenty of humour, could not help call to mind some very touching and tender verse which the Muse have read to she out of Mes Larmes, and which begin, "my pretty baby brother, may angel guard thy rest," in which the Muse, after compliment the baby upon the station in life which it be about to occupy, and contrast it with her own lonely condition, vow nevertheless that the angel boy would never enjoy such affection as hers be, or find in the false world before he anything so constant and tender as a sister's heart. "it may be," the forlorn one say, "it may be, you will slight it, my pretty baby sweet, you will spurn I from your bosom, Iwill cling around your foot! o let I, let I, love you! the world will prove to you as false as 'tis to other, but I be ever true." and behold the Muse be box the darling brother's ear instead of kneel at his foot, and give Miss Laura her first lesson in the Cynical philosophy--not quite her first, however,-- something like this selfishness and waywardness, something like this contrast between practice and poetry, between grand versify aspiration and everyday life, she have witness at home in the person of our young friend Mr. Pen.
but then Pen be different. Pen be a man. it seem natural somehow that he should be self-willed and should have his own way. and under his waywardness and selfishness, indeed there be a kind and generous heart. o it be hard that such a diamond should be change away against such a false stone as this. in a word, Laura begin to be tired of her admired Blanche. she have assay she and find she not true; and her former admiration and delight, which she have express with her accustomed generous artlessness, give way to a feeling, which we shall not call contempt, but which be very near it; and which cause Laura to adopt towards Miss Amory a grave and tranquil tone of superiority, which be at first by no means to the Muse's liking. nobody like to be find out, or, have hold a high place, to submit to step down.
the consciousness that this event be impend do not serve to increase Miss Blanche's good-humour, and as it make her peevish and dissatisfied with herself, it probably render she even less agreeable to the person round about she. so there arise, one fatal day, a battle-royal between dear Blanche and dear Laura, in which the friendship between they be all but slay outright. Dearest Blanche have be unusually capricious and wicked on this day. she have be insolent to her mother; savage with little Frank; odiously impertinent in her behaviour to the boy's governess; and intolerably cruel to Pincott, her attendant. not venture to attack her friend (for the little tyrant be of a timid feline nature, and only use her claw upon those who be weak than herself), she maltreat all these, and especially poor Pincott, who be menial, confidante, companion (slave always), accord to the caprice of her young mistress.
this girl, who have be sit in the room with the young lady, be drive thence in tear, occasion by the cruelty of her mistress, and rake with a parting sarcasm as she go sob from the door, Laura fairly break out into a loud and indignant invective--wonder how one so young could forget the deference owe to her elder as well as to her inferior in station; and profess so much sensibility of her own, could torture the feeling of other so wantonly. Laura tell her friend that her conduct be absolutely wicked, and that she ought to ask pardon of Heaven on her knee for it. and have deliver herself of a hot and voluble speech whereof the delivery astonish the speaker as much almost as her auditor, she run to her bonnet and shawl, and go home across the park in a great flurry and perturbation, and to the surprise of Mrs. Pendennis, who have not expect she until night.
alone with Helen, Laura give an account of the scene, and give up her friend henceforth. "o Mamma," she say, "you be right; Blanche, who seem so soft and so kind, be, as you have say, selfish and cruel. she who be always speak of her affection can have no heart. no honest girl would afflict a mother so, or torture a dependant; and--and, I give she up from this day, and I will have no other friend but you."
on this the two lady go through the osculatory ceremony which they be in the habit of perform, and Mrs. Pendennis get a great secret comfort from the little quarrel--for Laura's confession seem to say, "that girl can never be a wife for Pen, for she be light-minded and heartless, and quite unworthy of our noble hero. he will be sure to find out her unworthiness for his own part, and then he will be save from this flighty creature, and awake out of his delusion."
but Miss Laura do not tell Mrs. Pendennis, perhaps do not acknowledge to herself, what have be the real cause of the day's quarrel. be in a very wicked mood, and bend upon mischief everywhere, the little wicked Muse of a Blanche have very soon begin her trick. her darling Laura have come to pass a long day; and as they be sit in her own room together, have choose to bring the conversation round to the subject of Mr. Pen.
"I be afraid he be sadly fickle," Miss Blanche observe; "Mrs. Pybus, and many more clavering people, have tell we all about the actress."
"I be quite a child when it happen, and I donot know anything about it," Laura answer, blush very much.
"he use she very ill," Blanche say, wag her little head. "he be false to she."
"I be sure he be not," Laura cry out; "he act most generously by she; he want to give up everything to marry she. it be she that be false to he. he nearly break his heart about it: he----"
"I think you donot know anything about the story, dearest," interpose Miss Blanche.
"Mamma have say so," say Laura.
"well, he be very clever," continue the other little dear, "what a sweet poet he be! have you ever read his poem?"
"only the 'Fisherman and the Diver,' which he translate for we, and his Prize Poem, which donot get the prize; and, indeed, I think it very pompous and prosy," Laura say, laugh.
"have he never write you any poem, then, love?" ask Miss Amory.
"no, my dear," say Miss Bell.
Blanche run up to her friend, kiss she fondly, call she my dear Laura at least three time, look she archly in the face, nod her head, and say, "promise to tell no-o-body, and I will show you something."
and trip across the room daintily to a little mother-of-pearl inlaid desk, she open it with a silver key, and take out two or three paper crumple and rather stain with green, which she submit to her friend. Laura take they and read they. they be love-verse sure enough --somethe about Undine--about a Naiad--about a river. she look at they for a long time; but in truth the line be not very distinct before her eye.
"and you have answer they, Blanche?" she ask, put they back.
"o no! not for world, dearest," the other say: and when her dear Laura have quite do with the verse, she trip back and pop they again into the pretty desk.
then she go to her piano, and sing two or three song of Rossini, whose flourish of music her flexible little voice could execute to perfection, and Laura sate by, vaguely listen as she perform these piece. what be Miss Bell think about the while? she hardly know; but sate there silent as the song roll by. after this concert the young lady be summon to the room where luncheon be serve; and whither they of course go with their arm round each other's waist.
and it could not have be jealousy or anger on Laura's part which have make she silent; for, after they have trip along the corridor and descend the step, and be about to open the door which lead into the hall, Laura pause, and look her friend kindly and frankly in the face, kiss she with a sisterly warmth.
something occur after this--Master Frank's manner of eating, probably, or mamma's blunder, or Sir Francis smell of cigar--which vex Miss Blanche, and she give way to that series of naughtiness whereof we have speak, and which end in the above little quarrel.
CHAPTER XXVI
a House full of visitor
the difference between the girl do not last long. Laura be always too eager to forgive and be forgive, and as for Miss Blanche, her hostility, never very long or durable, have not be provoke by the above scene. nobody care about be accuse of wickedness. no vanity be hurt by that sort of charge: Blanche be rather pleased than provoke by her friend's indignation, which never would have be raise but for a cause which both know, though neither speak of.
and so Laura, with a sigh, be oblige to confess that the romantic part of her first friendship be at an end, and that the object of it be only worthy of a very ordinary sort of regard.
as for Blanche, she instantly compose a copy of touching verse, set forth her desertion and disenchantment. it be only the old story, she write, of love meet with coldness, and fidelity return by neglect; and some new neighbour arrive from London about this time, in whose family there be daughter, Miss Amory have the advantage of select an eternal friend from one of these young lady, and impart her sorrow and disappointment to this new sister. the tall footman come but seldom now with note to the sweet Laura; the pony-carriage be but rarely despatch to Fairoaks to be at the order of the lady there. Blanche adopt a sweet look of suffer martyrdom when Laura come to see she. the other laugh at her friend's sentimental mood, and treat it with a good-humour that be by no mean respectful.
but if Miss Blanche find new female friend to console she, the faithful historian be also bind to say, that she discover some acquaintance of the other sex who seem to give her consolation too. if ever this artless young creature meet a young man, and have ten minute' conversation with he in a garden walk, in a drawing-room window, or in the interval of a waltz, she confide in he, so to speak--make play with her beautiful eye--speak in a tone of tender interest, and simple and touching appeal, and leave he, to perform the same pretty little drama in behalf of his successor.
when the Claverings first come down to the Park, there be very few audience before whom Miss Blanche could perform: hence Pen have all the benefit of her glance and confidence, and the drawing-room window or the garden walk all to himself. in the town of Clavering, it have be say, there be actually young man: in the near surround country, only a curate or two or a rustic young squire, with large foot and ill-make clothe. to the dragoon quarter at Chatteris the Baronet make no overture: it be unluckily his own regiment: he have leave it on bad term with some officer of the corps--an ugly business about a horse bargain--a disputed play account--blind-Hookey--a white feather--who need ask?--it be not our business to inquire too closely into the bygone of our character, except in so far as their previous history appertain to the development of this present story.
but the autumn, and the end of the Parliamentary Session and the London season, bring one or two county family down to their house, and fill tolerably the neighbouring little watering-place of Baymouth, and open our friend Mr. Bingley's Theatre Royal at Chatteris, and collect the usual company at the Assizes and race-ball there. up to this time, the old county family have be rather shy of our friend of Clavering Park. the Fogeys of Drummington; the square of Tozely Park; the Welbores of the Barrow, etc.: all sort of story be current among these folk regard the family at Clavering;--indeed, nobody ought to say that people in the country have no imagination who hear they talk about new neighbour. about Sir Francis and his Lady, and her birth and parentage, about Miss Amory, about Captain Strong, there have be endless history which need not be recapitulate; and the family of the Park have be three month in the county before the great people around begin to call.
but at the end of the season, the Earl of Trehawk, Lord Lieutenant of the County, come to Eyrie Castle, and the Countess Dowager of Rockminster, whose son be also a magnate of the land, to occupy a mansion on the Marine Parade at Baymouth--these great folk come publicly, immediately, and in state, to call upon the family of Clavering Park; and the carriage of the county family speedily follow in the track which have be leave in the avenue by their lordly wheel.
it be then that Mirobolant begin to have an opportunity of exercise that skill which he possess, and of forget, in the occupation of his art, the pang of love. it be then that the large footman be too much employ at Clavering Park to be able to bring message, or dally over the cup of small beer with the poor little maid at Fairoaks. it be then that Blanche find other dear friend than Laura, and other place to walk in besides the river-side, where Pen be fish. he come day after day, and whip the stream, but the "fish, fish!" wouldnot do their duty, nor the Peri appear. and here, though in strict confidence, and with a request that the matter go no far, we may as well allude to a delicate business, of which previous hint have be give. Mention have be make, in a former page, of a certain hollow tree, at which Pen use to take his station when engage in his passion for Miss Fotheringay, and the cavity of which he afterwards use for other purpose than to insert his bait and fishing-can in. the truth be, be convert this tree into a post-office. under a piece of moss and a stone, he use to put little poem, or letter equally poetical, which be address to a certain Undine, or Naiad who frequent the stream, and which, once or twice, be replace by a receipt in the shape of a flower, or by a modest little word or two of acknowledgment, write in a delicate hand, in French or English, and on pink scented paper. certainly, Miss Amory use to walk by this stream, as we have see; and it be a fact that she use pink scented paper for her correspondence. but after the great folk have invade Clavering Park, and the family coach pass out of the lodge-gate, evening after evening, on their way to the other great country house, nobody come to fetch Pen's letter at the post-office; the white paper be not exchange for the pink, but lie undisturbed under its stone and its moss, whilst the tree be reflect into the stream, and the Brawl go roll by. there be not much in the letter certainly; in the pink note scarcely anything--merely a little word or two, half jocular, half sympathetic, such as might be write by any young lady. but oh, you silly Pendennis, if you want this one, why do you not speak? perhaps neither party be in earnest. you be only play at be in love, and the sportive little Undine be humour you at the same play.
but if a man be baulk at this game, he not unfrequently lose his temper; and when nobody come any more for Pen's poem, he begin to look upon those composition in a very serious light. he feel almost tragical and romantic again, as in his first affair of the heart:--at any rate he be bent upon have an explanation. one day he go to the Hall and there be a roomful of visitor: on another, Miss Amory be not to be see; she be go to a ball that night, and be lie down to take a little sleep. Pen curse ball, and the narrowness of his mean, and the humility of his position in the country that cause he to be pass over by the giver of these entertainment. on a third occasion, Miss Amory be in the garden, and he run thither; she be walk there in state with no less personage than the Bishop and Bishopess of Chatteris and the episcopal family, who scowl at he, and draw up in great dignity when he be present to they, and they hear his name. the Right Reverend Prelate have hear it before, and also of the little transaction in the Dean's garden.
"the Bishop say yoube a sad young man," good-natured Lady Clavering whisper to he. "what have you be a doing of? nothink, I hope, to vex such a dear Mar as your? how be your dear mar? why donot she come and I? we an't see she this ever such a time. webe a goin about a gaddin, so that we donot see no neighbour now. give my love to she and Laurar, and come all to dinner to-morrow."
Mrs. Pendennis be too unwell to come out but Laura and Pen come, and there be a great party, and Pen only get an opportunity of a hurried word with Miss Amory. "you never come to the river now" he say.
"I cannot," say Blanche, "the house be full of people."
"Undine have leave the stream," Mr. Pen go on, choose to be poetical.
"she never ought to have go there," Miss Amory answer. "she willnot go again. it be very foolish: very wrong: it be only play. besides, you have other consolation at home," she add, look he full in the face an instant, and drop her eye.
if he want she, why do he not speak then? she might have say "yes" even then. but as she speak of other consolation at home, he think of Laura, so affectionate and so pure, and of his mother at home, who have bend her fond heart upon unite he with her adopt daughter. "Blanche!" he begin, in a vex tone,--"miss Amory!"
"Laura be look at we, Mr. Pendennis," the young lady say. "I must go back to the company," and she run off, leave Mr. Pendennis to bite his nail in perplexity, and to look out into the moonlight in the garden.
Laura indeed be look at Pen. she be talk with, or appear to listen to the talk of, Mr. Pynsent, Lord Rockminster's son, and grandson of the Dowager Lady, who be seat in state in the place of honour, gravely receive Lady Clavering's bad grammar, and patronise the vacuous Sir Francis, whose interest in the county she be desirous to secure. Pynsent and Pen have be at Oxbridge together, where the latter, during his heyday of good fortune and fashion, have be the superior of the young patrician, and perhaps rather supercilious towards he. they have meet for the first time, since they part at the University, at the table to-day, and give each other that exceedingly impertinent and amusing demi-nod of recognition which be practise in England only, and only to perfection by University men,--and which seem to say, "confound you--what do you do here?"
"I know that man at Oxbridge," Mr. Pynsent say to Miss Bell--"a Mr. Pendennis, I think."
"yes," say Miss Bell.
"he seem rather sweet upon Miss Amory," the gentleman go on. Laura look at they, and perhaps think so too, but say nothing.
"a man of large property in the county, benot he? he use to talk about represent it. he use to speak at the Union. whereabout do his estate lie?"
Laura smile. "his estate lie on the other side of the river, near the lodge-gate. he be my cousin, and I live there."
"where?" ask Mr. Pynsent, with a laugh.
"why, on the other side of the river, at Fairoaks," answer Miss Bell.
"many pheasant there? Cover look rather good," say the simple gentleman.
Laura smile again. "we have nine hen and a cock, a pig, and an old pointer."
"Pendennis donot preserve, then?" continue Mr. Pynsent.
"you should come and see he," the girl say, laugh, and greatly amuse at the notion that her Pen be a great county gentleman, and perhaps have give himself out to be such.
"indeed, I quite long to renew our acquaintance," Mr. Pynsent say, gallantly, and with a look which fairly say, "it be you that I would like to come and see"--to which look and speech Miss Laura vouchsafe a smile, and make a little bow.
here Blanche come step up with her most fascinating smile and ogle, and beg dear Laura to come and take the second in a song. Laura be ready to do anything good-natured, and go to the piano; by which Mr. Pynsent listen as long as the duet last, and until Miss Amory begin for herself, when he stride away.
"what a nice, frank, amiable, well-breed girl that be, Wagg," say Mr. Pynsent to a gentleman who have come over with he from Baymouth--"the tall one, I mean, with the ringlet and red lip--monstrous red, benot they?"
"what do you think of the girl of the house?" ask Wagg.
"I think shebe a lean, scraggy humbug," say Mr. Pynsent, with great candour. "she drag her shoulder out of her dress, she never let her eye alone: and she go simper and ogle about like a French waiting-maid.
"Pynsent, be civil," cry the other, "somebody can hear."
"oh, itbe Pendennis of Boniface," Mr. Pynsent say. "fine evening, Mr. Pendennis; we be just talk of your charming cousin."
"any relation to my old friend, Major Pendennis?" ask Mr. Wagg.
"his nephew. have the pleasure of meet you at Gaunt House," Mr. Pen say with his very good air--the acquaintance between the gentleman be make in an instant.
in the afternoon of the next day, the two gentleman who be stay at Clavering Park be find by Mr. Pen on his return from a fishing excursion, in which he have no sport, seat in his mother's drawing-room in comfortable conversation with the widow and her ward. Mr. Pynsent, tall and gaunt, with large red whisker and an imposing tuft to his chin, be stride over a chair in the intimate neighbourhood of Miss Laura. she be amuse by his talk, which be simple, straightforward, rather humorous and keen, and intersperse with homely expression of a style which be sometimes call slang. it be the first speciman of a young London dandy that Laura have see or hear: for she have be but a chit at the time of Mr. Foker's introduction at Fairoaks, nor indeed be that ingenuous gentleman much more than a boy, and his refinement be only that of a school and college.
Mr. Wagg, as he enter the Fairoaks premise with his companion, eyed and note everything. "old gardener," he say, see Mr. John at the lodge--"old red livery waistcoat--clothe hang out to dry on the gooseberry-bush--blue apron, white duck--gad, they must be young Pendennis's white duck--nobody else wear them in the family. rather a shy place for a sucking county member, ay, Pynsent?"
"snug little crib," say Mr. Pynsent, "pretty cosy little lawn."
"Mr. Pendennis at home, old gentleman?" Mr. Wagg say to the old domestic. John answer, "no, Master Pendennis be agone out."
"be the lady at home?" ask the young visitor. Mr. John answer, "yes, they be;" and as the pair walk over the trim gravel, and by the neat shrubbery, up the step to the hall-door, which old John open, Mr. Wagg note everything that he see; the barometer and the letter-bag, the umbrella and the lady' clog, Pen's hat and tartan wrapper, and old John open the drawing-room door, to introduce the new-comer. such minutia attract Wagg instinctively; he seize they in spite of himself.
"old fellow do all the work," he whisper to Pynsent. "Caleb Balderstone. shouldnot wonder if hebe the housemaid." the next minute the pair be in the presence of the Fairoaks lady; in whom Pynsent could not help recognise two perfectly well-breed lady, and to whom Mr. Wagg make his obeisance, with florid bow, and extra courtesy, accompany with an occasional knowing leer at his companion. Mr. Pynsent do not choose to acknowledge these signal, except by extreme haughtiness towards Mr. Wagg, and particular deference to the lady. if there be one thing laughable in Mr. Wagg's eye, it be poverty. he have the soul of a butler who have be bring from his pantry to make fun in the drawing-room. his joke be plenty, and his good-nature thoroughly genuine, but he do not seem to understand that a gentleman could wear an old coat, or that a lady could be respectable unless she have her carriage, or employ a french milliner.
"charming place, ma'am," say he, bow to the widow; "noble prospect-- delightful to we Cocknies, who seldom see anything but Pall Mall." the widow say simply, she have never be in London but once in her life-- before her son be bear.
"fine village, ma'am, fine village," say Mr. Wagg, "and increase every day. itwill be quite a large town soon. itbe not a bad place to live in for those who cannot get the country, and will repay a visit when you honour it."
"my brother, Major Pendennis, have often mention your name to we," the widow say, "and we have be very much amuse by some of your droll book, sir," Helen continue, who never could be bring to like Mr. Wagg's book, and detest their tone most thoroughly. "he be my very good friend," Mr. Wagg say, with a low bow, "and one of the well know man about town, and where know, ma'am, appreciate--I assure you appreciate. he be with our friend Steyne, at Aix-la-Chapelle. Steyne have a touch of the gout and so, between ourselves, have your brother. I be go to Stillbrook for the pheasant-shooting, and afterwards to Bareacres, where Pendennis and I shall probably meet;" and he pour out a flood of fashionable talk, introduce the name of a score of peer, and rattle on with breathless spirit, whilst the simple widow listen in silent wonder. what a man, she think; be all the man of fashion in London like this? I be sure Pen will never like he.
Mr. Pynsent be in the meanwhile engage with Miss Laura. he name some of the house in the neighbourhood whither he be go, and hop very much that he should see Miss Bell at some of they. he hop that her aunt would give she a season in London. he say, that in the next parliament it be probable that he should canvass the county, and he hop to get Pendennis's interest here. he speak of Pen's triumph as an orator at Oxbridge, and ask be he come into parliament too? he talk on very pleasantly, and greatly to Laura's satisfaction, until Pen himself appear, and, as have be say, find these gentleman.
Pen behave very courteously to the pair, now that they have find their way into his quarter; and though he recollect with some twinge a conversation at Oxbridge, when Pynsent be present, and in which after a great debate at the Union, and in the midst of considerable excitement produce by a supper and champagne-cup,--he have announce his intention of come in for his native county, and have absolutely return thank in a fine speech as the future member; yet Mr. Pynsent's manner be so frank and cordial, that Pen hop Pynsent might have forget his little fanfaronnade, and any other braggadocio speech or action which he might have make. he suit himself to the tone of the visitor, then, and talk about Plinlimmon and Magnus Charters, and the old set at Oxbridge, with careless familiarity and high-breed ease, as if he live with marquis every day, and a duke be no more to he than a village curate.
but at this juncture, and it be then six o'clock in the evening, Betsy, the maid, who do not know of the advent of stranger, walk into the room without any preliminary but that of fling the door wide open before she, and bear in her arm a tray, contain three tea-cup, a tea-pot, and a plate of thick bread-and-butter. all Pen's splendour and magnificence vanish away at this--and he falter and become quite abash. "what will they think of we?" he think: and, indeed, Wagg thrust his tongue in his cheek, think the tea infinitely contemptible, and leer and wink at Pynsent to that effect.
but to Mr. Pynsent the transaction appear perfectly simple--there be no reason present to his mind why people should not drink tea at six if they be minded, as well as at any other hour; and he ask of Mr. Wagg, when they go away, "what the devil he be grin and wink at, and what amuse he?"
