"donot you see how the cub be ashamed of the thick bread-and-butter? I dare say theybe go to have treacle if they be good. Iwill take an opportunity of tell old Pendennis when we get back to town," Mr. Wagg chuckle out.
"donot see the fun," say Mr. Pynsent.
"never think you do," growl Wagg between his tooth; they walk home rather sulkily.
Wagg tell the story at dinner very smartly, with wonderful accuracy of observation. he describe old John, the clothe that be dry, the clog in the hall, the drawing-room, and its furniture and pictures;-- "old man with a beak and bald head--feu Pendennis I bet two to one; stick-plaster full-length of a youth in a cap and gown--the present Marquis of Fairoaks, of course; the widow when young in a miniature, Mrs. Mee; she have the gown on when we come, or a dress make the year after, and the tip cut off the finger of her glove which she stitch her son's collar with; and then the sarving maid come in with their tea so we leave the Earl and the Countess to their bread-and-butter."
Blanche, near whom he sate as he tell this story, and who adore les hommes desprit, burst out laugh, and call he such an odd, droll creature. but Pynsent, who begin to be utterly disgusted with he, break out in a loud voice, and say, "I donot know, Mr. Wagg, what sort of lady you be accustom to meet in your own family, but by gad, as far as a first acquaintance can show, I never meet two well-breed woman in my life, and I hope, ma'am, youwill call upon them," he add, address Lady Rockminster, who be seat at Sir Francis Clavering's right hand.
Sir Francis turn to the guest on his left, and whisper. "thatbe what I call a sticker for Wagg." and Lady Clavering, give the young gentleman a delighted tap with her fan, wink her black eye at he, and say, "Mr. Pynsent, yoube a good feller."
after the affair with Blanche, a difference ever so slight, a tone of melancholy, perhaps a little bitter, might be perceive in Laura's converse with her cousin. she seem to weigh he and find he want too; the widow see the girl's clear and honest eye watch the young man at time, and a look of almost scorn pass over her face, as he lounge in the room with the woman, or lazily saunter smoking upon the lawn, or loll under a tree there over a book which he be too listless to read.
"what have happen between you?" eager-sighted Helen ask of the girl. "something have happen. have that wicked little Blanche be make mischief? tell I, Laura."
"nothing have happen at all," Laura say.
"then why do you look at Pen so?" ask his mother quickly.
"look at he, dear mother!" say the girl. "we two woman be no society for he: we donot interest he; we be not clever enough for such a genius as Pen. he waste his life and energy away among we, tie to our apron-string. he interest himself in nothing: he scarcely care to go beyond the garden-gate. even Captain Glanders and Captain Strong pall upon he," she add with a bitter laugh; "and they be man, you know, and our superior. he will never be happy while he be here. why, be he not face the world, and without a profession?"
"we have get enough, with great economy," say the widow, her heart begin to beat violently. "Pen have spend nothing for month. Ibe sure he be very good. I be sure he might be very happy with we."
"donot agitate yourself so, dear mother," the girl answer. "I donot like to see you so. you should not be sad because Pen be unhappy here. all man be so. they must work. they must make themselves name and a place in the world. look, the two captain have fight and see battle; that Mr. Pynsent, who come here, and who will be very rich, be in a public office; he work very hard, he aspire to a name and a reputation. he say Pen be one of the good speaker at Oxbridge, and have as great a character for talent as any of the young gentleman there. Pen himself laugh at Mr. Wagg's celebrity (and indeed he be a horrid person), and say he be a dunce, and that anybody could write his book."
"I be sure they be odious and vulgar," interpose the widow.
"yet he have a reputation.--you see the County Chronicle say, 'the celebrate Mr. Wagg have be sojourn at Baymouth--let our fashionable and eccentric look out for something from his caustic pen.' if Pen can write well than this gentleman, and speak well than Mr. Pynsent, why donot he? Mamma, he cannot make speech to we; or distinguish himself here. he ought to go away, indeed he ought."
"Dear Laura," say Helen, take the girl's hand. "be it kind of you to hurry he so? I have be wait. I have be save up money these many month--to--to pay back your advance to we."
"hush, mother!" Laura cry, embrace her friend hastily. "it be your money, not mine. never speak about that again. how much money have you save?"
Helen say there be more than two hundred pound at the bank, and that she would be enable to pay off all Laura's money by the end of the next year.
"give it he--let he have the two hundred pound. let he go to London and be a lawyer: be something, be worthy of his mother--and of mine, dear mamma," say the good girl; upon which, and with her usual tenderness and emotion, the fond widow declare that Laura be a blessing to she and the good of girl--and I hope no one in this instance will be dispose to contradict she.
the widow and her daughter have more than one conversation on this subject; and the elder give way to the superior reason of the honest and strong-minded girl; and indeed, whenever there be a sacrifice to be make on her part, this kind lady be only too eager to make it. but she take her own way, and do not loose sight of the end she have in view, in impart these new plan to Pen. one day she tell he of these project, and it who that have form they; how it be Laura who insist upon his go to London and study; how it be Laura who would not hear of the--the money arrangement when he come back from Oxbridge--be settle just then: how it be Laura whom he have to thank, if indeed he think that he have to go.
at that news Pen's countenance blaze up with pleasure, and he hug his mother to his heart with an ardour that I fear disappoint the fond lady; but she rally when he say, "by Heaven! she be a noble girl, and may God Almighty bless her mother! I have be wear myself away for month here, long to work, and not know how. Ihave be fret over the thought of my shame, and my debt, and my past cursed extravagance and folly. Ihave suffer infernally. my heart have be half break--never mind about that. if I can get a chance to redeem the past, and to do my duty to myself and the good mother in the world, indeed, indeed, I will. Iwill be worthy of you yet. Heaven bless you! God bles Laura! why benot she here, that I may go and thank she?" Pen go on with more incoherent phrase; pace up and down the room, drink glass of water, jump about his mother with a thousand embrace--begin to laugh--begin to sing--be happy than she have see he since he be a boy--since he have taste of the fruit of that awful Tree of Life, which, from the beginning, have tempt all mankind.
Laura be not at home. Laura be on a visit to the stately Lady Rockminster, daughter to my Lord Bareacres, sister to the late Lady Pontypool, and by consequence a distant kinswoman of Helen's, as her ladyship, who be deeply verse in genealogy, be graciously to point out to the modest country lady. Mr. Pen be greatly delighted at the relationship be acknowledge; though perhaps not over well pleased that Lady Rockminster take Miss Bell home with she for a couple of day to Baymouth, and do not make the slight invitation to Mr. Arthur Pendennis. there be to be a ball at Baymouth, and it be to be Miss Laura's first appearance. the dowager come to fetch she in her carriage, and she go off with a white dress in her box, happy and blushing, like the rose to which Pen compare she.
this be the night of the ball--a public entertainment at the Baymouth Hotel. "by Jove!" say Pen, "Iwill ride over--no, I willnot ride, but Iwill go too." his mother be charm that he should do so; and, as he be debate about the conveyance in which he should start for Baymouth, Captain Strong call opportunely, say he be go himself, and that he would put his horse, the Butcher Boy, into the gig, and drive Pen over.
when the grand company begin to fill the house at Clavering Park, the Chevalier Strong, who, as his patron say, be never in the way or out of it, seldom intrude himself upon its society, but go elsewhere to seek his relaxation. "Ihave see plenty of grand dinner in my time," he say, "and din, by Jove, in a company where there be a king and royal duke at top and bottom, and every man along the table have six star on his coat; but dammy, Glanders, this finery donot suit I; and the english lady with their confound buckram air, and the squire with their politic after dinner, send I to sleep--sink I dead if they donot. I like a place where I can blow my cigar when the cloth be remove, and when Ibe thirsty, have my beer in its native pewter." so on a gala-day at Clavering Park, the Chevalier would content himself with superintend the arrangement of the table, and drill the major-domo and servant; and have look over the bill-of-fare with Monsieur Mirobolant, would not care to take the least part in the banquet. "send I up a cutlet and a bottle of claret to my room," this philosopher would say, and from the window of that apartment, which command the terrace and avenue, he would survey the company as they arrive in their carriage, or take a peep at the lady in the hall through an oeil-de-boeuf which command it from his corridor. and the guest be seat, Strong would cross the park to Captain Glanders's cottage at Clavering, or to pay the landlady a visit at the Clavering Arms, or to drop in upon Madame Fribsby over her novel and tea. wherever the Chevalier go he be welcome, and whenever he come away a smell of hot brandy-and-water linger behind he.
the Butcher Boy--not the bad horse in Sir Francis's stable--be appropriate to Captain Strong's express use; and the old Campaigner saddle he or bring he home at all hour of the day or night, and drive or ride he up and down the country. where there be a public-house with a good tap of beer--where there be a tenant with a pretty daughter who play on the piano--to Chatteris, to the play, or the barrack--to Baymouth, if any fun be on foot there; to the rural fair or race, the Chevalier and his brown horse make their way continually; and this worthy gentleman live at free quarter in a friendly country. the Butcher Boy soon take Pen and the Chevalier to Baymouth. the latter be as familiar with the hotel and landlord there as with every other inn round about; and have be accommodate with a bedroom to dress, they enter the ballroom. the Chevalier be splendid. he wear three little gold cross in a brochette on the portly breast of his blue coat, and look like a foreign field-marshal.
the ball be public and all sort of person be admit and encourage to come, young Pynsent have view upon the county and Lady Rockminster be patroness of the ball. there be a quadrille for the aristocracy at one end, and select bench for the people of fashion. towards this end the Chevalier do not care to penetrate far (as he say he do not care for the nob); but in the other part of the room he know everybody--the wine-merchant', innkeeper', tradesman's, solicitor', squire-farmer' daughter, their sire and brother, and plunge about shake hand.
"who be that man with the blue ribbon and the three-point star?" ask Pen. a gentleman in black with ringlet and a tuft stand gaze fiercely about he, with one hand in the arm-hole of his waistcoat and the other hold his claque.
"By Jupiter, itbe mirobolant!" cry Strong, burst out laugh. "Bon jour, Chef!--Bon jour, Chevalier!"
"De la croix de Juillet, Chevalier!" say the Chef, lay his hand on his decoration.
"by Jove, herebe some more ribbon!" say Pen, amuse.
a man with very black hair and whisker, dye evidently with the purple of Tyre, with twinkle eye and white eyelash, and a thousand wrinkle in his face, which be of a strange red colour, with two under-vest, and large glove and hand, and a profusion of diamond and jewel in his waistcoat and stock, with coarse foot crumple into immense shiny boot, and a piece of parti-colour ribbon in his button-hole, here come up and nod familiarly to the Chevalier.
the Chevalier shake hand. "my friend Mr. Pendennis," Strong say. "Colonel Altamont, of the bodyguard of his Highness the Nawaub of Lucknow." that officer bow to the salute of Pen; who be now look out eagerly to see if the person want have enter the room.
not yet. but the band begin presently perform 'see the Conquering Hero come,' and a host of fashionable--Dowager Countess of Rockminster, Mr. Pynsent and Miss Bell, Sir Francis Clavering, Bart., of Clavering Park, Lady Clavering and Miss Amory, Sir Horace Fogey, Bart., Lady Fogey, Colonel and Mrs. Higgs Wagg, Esq. (as the county paper afterwards describe they), enter the room.
Pen rush by Blanche, run up to Laura, and seize her hand. "God bless you!" he say, "I want to speak to you--I must speak to you--let I dance with you." "not for three dance, dear Pen," she say, smile: and he fall back, bite his nail with vexation, and forget to salute Pynsent.
after Lady Rockminster's party, Lady Clavering's follow in the procession.
Colonel Altamont eye it hard, hold a most musky pocket-handkerchief up to his face, and burst with laughter behind it.
"whobe the gal in green along with them, Cap'n?" he ask of Strong.
"thatbe Miss Amory, Lady Clavering's daughter," reply the Chevalier.
the Colonel could hardly contain himself for laugh.
CHAPTER XXVII
contain some ball-practise
under some calico drapery in the shady embrasure of a window, Arthur Pendennis choose to assume a very gloomy and frown countenance, and to watch Miss Bell dance her first quadrille with Mr. Pynsent for a partner. that gentleman be as solemn and severe as englishman be upon such occasion, and walk through the dance as he would have walk up to his pew in church, without a smile upon his face, or allow any outward circumstance to interfere with his attention to the grave duty in which he be engage. but Miss Laura's face be beam with pleasure and good-nature. the light and the crowd and music excite she. as she spread out her white robe, and perform her part of the dance, smile and happy, her brown ringlet flow back over her fair shoulder from her honest rosy face, more than one gentleman in the room admire and look after she; and Lady Fogey, who have a house in London and give herself no small air of fashion when in the country, ask of Lady Rockminster who the young person be, mention a reign beauty in London whom, in her ladyship's opinion, Laura be rather like, and pronounce that she would "do."
Lady Rockminster would have be very much surprised if any protegee of hers would not "do," and wonder at Lady Fogey's impudence in judge upon the point at all. she survey Laura with majestic glance through her eyeglass. she be pleased with the girl's artless look, and gay innocent manner. her manner be very good, her ladyship think. her arm be rather red, but that be a defect of her youth. her tone be far well than that of the little pert Miss Amory, who be dance opposite to she.
Miss Blanche be, indeed, the vis-a-vis of Miss Laura, and smile most killingly upon her dear friend, and nod to she and talk to she, when they meet during the quadrille evolution, and patronise she a great deal. her shoulder be the whitest in the whole room: and they be never easy in her frock for one single instant: nor be her eye, which roll about incessantly: nor be her little figure:--it seem to say to all the people, "come and look at I--not at that pink, healthy, bounce country lass, Miss Bell, who scarcely know how to dance till I teach she. this be the true parisian manner--this be the prettiest little foot in the room, and the pretty little chaussure too. look at it, Mr. Pynsent. look at it, Mr. Pendennis, you who be scowl behind the curtain--I know you be long to dance with I."
Laura go on dancing, and keep an attentive eye upon Mr. Pen in the embrasure of the window. he do not quit that retirement during the first quadrille, nor until the second, when the good-natured Lady Clavering beckon to he to come up to she to the dai or place of honour where the dowager were,--and whither Pen go blush and exceedingly awkward, as most conceited young fellow be. he perform a haughty salutation to Lady Rockminster, who hardly acknowledge his bow, and then go and pay his respect to the widow of the late Amory, who be splendid in diamond, velvet, lace, feather, and all sort of millinery and goldsmith's ware.
young Mr. Fogey, then in the fifth form at Eton, and ardently expect his beard and his commission in a dragoon regiment, be the second partner who be honour with Miss Bell's hand. he be rapt in admiration of that young lady. he think he have never see so charming a creature. "I like you much well than the french girl" (for this young gentleman have be dance with Miss Amory before), he candidly say to she. Laura laugh, and look more good-humour than ever; and in the midst of her laughter catch a sight of Pen, and continue to laugh as he, on his side, continue to look absurdly pompous and sulky. the next dance be a waltz, and young Fogey thought, with a sigh, that he do not know how to waltz, and vow he would have a master the next holiday.
Mr. Pynsent again claim Miss Bell's hand for this dance; and Pen behold she, in a fury, twirl round the room, her waist encircle by the arm of that gentleman. he never use to be angry before when, on summer evening, the chair and table be remove, and the governess call downstairs to play the piano, he and the Chevalier Strong (who be a splendid performer, and could dance a british hornpipe, a german waltz, or a spanish fandango, if need be), and the two young lady, Blanche and Laura, improvise little ball at Clavering Park. Laura enjoy this dancing so much, and be so animated, that she even animate Mr. Pynsent. Blanche, who could dance beautifully, have an unlucky partner, Captain Broadfoot, of the Dragoons, then station at Chatteris. for Captain Broadfoot, though devote himself with great energy to the object in view, could not get round in time: and, not have the least ear for music, be unaware that his movement be too slow.
so, in the waltz as in the quadrille, Miss Blanche see that her dear friend Laura have the honour of the dance, and be by no mean pleased with the latter's success. after a couple of turn with the heavy dragoon, she plead fatigue, and request to be lead back to her place, near her mamma, to whom Pen be talk; and she ask he why he have not ask she to waltz, and have leave she for the mercy of that great odious man in spur and a red coat?
"I think spur and scarlet be the most fascinating object in the world to young lady," Pen answer. "I never should have dare to put my black coat in competition with that splendid red jacket."
"you be very unkind and cruel and sulky and naughty," say Miss Amory, with another shrug of the shoulder. "you have well go away. your cousin be look at we over Mr. Pynsent's shoulder."
"will you waltz with I?" say Pen.
"not this waltz. I cannot, have just send away that good Captain Broadfoot. look at Mr. Pynsent, do you ever see such a creature? but I will dance the next waltz with you, and the quadrille too. I be promise, but I will tell Mr. Poole that I have forget my engagement to you."
"woman forget very readily," Pendennis say.
"but they always come back, and be very repentant and sorry for what theyhave do," Blanche say. "see, here come the Foker, and dear Laura lean on he. how pretty she look!"
Laura come up, and put out her hand to Pen, to whom Pynsent make a sort of bow, appear to be not much more graceful than that domestic instrument to which Miss Amory compare he.
but Laura's face be full of kindness. "I be so glad to have come, dear Pen," she say. "I can speak to you now. how be mamma? the three dance be over, and I be engaged to you for the next, Pen."
"I have just engage myself to Miss Amory," say Pen; and Miss Amory nod her head, and make her usual little curtsey. "I donot intend to give he up, dear Laura," she say.
"well, then, hewill waltz with I, dear Blanche," say the other. "willnot you, Pen?"
"I promise to waltz with Miss Amory."
"provoke!" say Laura, and make a curtsey in her turn she go and place herself under the ample wing of Lady Rockminster.
Pen be delighted with his mischief. the two prettiest girl in the room be quarrel about he. he flatter himself he have punish Miss Laura. he lean in a dandified air, with his elbow over the wall, and talk to Blanche: he quiz unmercifully all the man in the room--the heavy dragoon in their tight jacket--the country dandy in their queer attire--the strange toilette of the lady. one seem to have a bird's nest in her head; another have six pound of grape in her hair, besides her false pearl. "itbe a coiffure of almond and raisin," say Pen "and might be serve up for dessert." in a word, he be exceedingly satirical and amusing.
during the quadrille he carry on this kind of conversation with unflinche bitterness and vivacity, and keep Blanche continually laugh, both at his wickedness and joke, which be good, and also because Laura be again their vis-a-vis, and could see and hear how merry and confidential they be.
"Arthur be charming to-night," she whisper to Laura, across Cornet Perch's shell-jacket, as Pen be perform cavalier seul before they, drawl through that figure with a thumb in the pocket of each waistcoat.
"who?" say Laura.
"Arthur," answer Blanche, in French. "oh, itbe such a pretty name!" and now the young lady go over to Pen's side, and Cornet Perch perform a pas seul in his turn. he have no waistcoat pocket to put his hand into, and they look large and swollen as they hang before he depend from the tight arm in the jacket.
during the interval between the quadrille and the succeed waltz, Pen do not take any notice of Laura, except to ask she whether her partner, Cornet Perch, be an amusing youth, and whether she like he so well as her other partner, Mr. Pynsent. have plant which two dagger in Laura's gentle bosom, Mr. Pendennis proceed to rattle on with Blanche Amory, and to make joke good or bad, but which be always loud. Laura be at a loss to account for her cousin's sulky behaviour, and ignorant in what she have offend he; however, she be not angry in her turn at Pen's splenetic mood, for she be the most good-natured and forgiving of woman, and besides, an exhibition of jealousy on a man's part be not always disagreeable to a lady.
as Pen would not dance with she, she be glad to take up with the active Chevalier Strong, who be a still well performer than Pen; and be very fond of dancing, as every brisk and innocent young girl should be, when the waltz music begin she set off, and choose to enjoy herself with all her heart. captain Broadfoot on this occasion occupy the floor in conjunction with a lady of proportion scarcely inferior to his own; Miss Roundle, a large young woman in a strawberry-ice coloured crape dress, the daughter of the lady with the grape in her head, whose bunch Pen have admire.
and now take his time, and with his fair partner Blanche hang lovingly on the arm which encircle she, Mr. Arthur Pendennis set out upon his waltzing career, and feel, as he whirl round to the music, that he and Blanche be perform very brilliantly indeed. very likely he look to see if Miss Bell think so too; but she do not or would not see he, and be always engage with her partner Captain Strong. but Pen's triumph be not destine to last long; and it be doom that poor Blanche be to have yet another discomfiture on that unfortunate night. while she and Pen be whirl round as light and brisk as a couple of opera-dancer, honest Captain Broadfoot and the lady round whose large waist he be cling, be twist round very leisurely accord to their nature, and indeed be in everybody's way. but they be more in Pendennis's way than in anybody's else, for he and Blanche, whilst execute their rapid gyration, come bolt up against the heavy dragoon and his lady, and with such force that the centre of gravity be lose by all four of the circumvolve body; Captain Broadfoot and Miss Roundle be fairly upset, as be Pen himself, who be less lucky than his partner Miss Amory, who be only throw upon a bench against a wall.
but Pendennis come fairly down upon the floor, sprawl in the general ruin with broadfoot and Miss Roundle. the Captain, though heavy, be good-natured, and be the first to burst out into a loud laugh at his own misfortune, which nobody therefore heed. but Miss Amory be savage at her mishap; Miss Roundle place on her seant, and look pitifully round, present an object which very few people could see without laugh; and Pen be furious when he hear the people giggle about he. he be one of those sarcastic young fellow that do not bear a laugh at his own expense, and of all thing in the world fear ridicule most.
as he get up Laura and Strong be laugh at he; everybody be laugh; Pynsent and his partner be laugh; and Pen boil with wrath against the pair, and could have stab they both on the spot. he turn away in a fury from they, and begin blunder out apology to Miss Amory. it be the other couple's fault--the woman in pink have do it--Pen hop Miss Amory be not hurt--would she not have the courage to take another turn?
Miss Amory in a pet say she be very much hurt indeed, and she would not take another turn; and she accept with great thank a glass of water which a cavalier, who wear a blue ribbon and a three-point star, rush to fetch for she when he have see the deplorable accident. she drink the water, smile upon the bringer gracefully, and turn her white shoulder at Mr. Pen in the most marked and haughty manner, besought the gentleman with the star to conduct she to her mamma; and she hold out her hand in order to take his arm.
the man with the star tremble with delight at this mark of her favour; he bow over her hand, press it to his coat fervidly, and look round he with triumph.
it be no other than the happy Mirobolant whom Blanche have select as an escort. but the truth be, that the young lady have never fairly look in the artist's face since he have be employ in her mother's family, and have no idea but it be a foreign nobleman on whose arm she be lean. as she go off, Pen forget his humiliation in his surprise, and cry out, "by Jove, itbe the cook!"
the instant he have utter the word, he be sorry for have speak they--for it be Blanche who have herself invite Mirobolant to escort she, nor could the artist do otherwise than comply with a lady's command. blanche in her flutter do not hear what Arthur say; but Mirobolant hear he, and cast a furious glance at he over his shoulder, which rather amuse Mr. Pen. he be in a mischievous and sulky humour; want perhaps to pick a quarrel with somebody; but the idea of have insult a cook, or that such an individual should have any feeling of honour at all, do not much enter into the mind of this lofty young aristocrat, the apothecary's son.
it have never enter that poor artist's head, that he as a man be not equal to any other mortal, or that there be anything in his position so degrade as to prevent he from give his arm to a lady who ask for it. he have see in the fete in his own country fine lady, not certainly demoiselle (but the demoiselle Anglaise he know be a great deal more free than the spinster in France), join in the dance with Blaise or Pierre; and he would have take Blanche up to Lady Clavering, and possibly have ask she to dance too, but he hear Pen's exclamation, which strike he as if it have shoot he, and cruelly humiliate and anger he. she do not know what cause he to start, and to grind a Gascon oath between his tooth.
but Strong, who be acquaint with the poor fellow's state of mind, have have the interesting information from our friend Madame Fribsby, be luckily in the way when want, and say something rapidly in Spanish, which the other understood, the Chevalier beg Miss Amory to come and take an ice before she go back to Lady Clavering. upon which the unhappy Mirobolant relinquish the arm which he have hold for a minute, and with a most profound and piteous bow, fall back. "donot you know who it be?" Strong ask of Miss Amory, as he lead she away. "it be the chef Mirobolant."
"how should I know?" ask Blanche. "he have a croix; he be very distingue; he have beautiful eye."
"the poor fellow be mad for your beaux yeux, I believe," Strong say. "he be a very good cook, but he be not quite right in the head."
"what do you say to he in the unknown tongue?" ask Miss Blanche.
"he be a Gascon, and come from the border of Spain," Strong answer. "I tell he he would lose his place if he walk with you."
"Poor Monsieur Mirobolant!" say Blanche.
"do you see the look he give pendennis?"--strong ask, enjoy the idea of the mischief--"i think he would like to run little Pen through with one of his spit."
"he be an odious, conceited, clumsy creature, that Mr. Pen," say Blanche.
"Broadfoot look as if he would like to kill he too, so do Pynsent," Strong say. "what ice will you have--water ice or cream ice?"
"water ice. who be that odd man star at I--he be decore too."
"that be my friend Colonel Altamont, a very queer character, in the service of the Nawaub of Lucknow. Hallo! whatbe that noise? Iwill be back in an instant," say the Chevalier, and spring out of the room to the ballroom, where a scuffle and a noise of high voice be hear.
the refreshment-room, in which Miss Amory now find herself, be a room set apart for the purpose of supper, which Mr. Rincer the landlord have provide for those who choose to partake, at the rate of five shilling per head. also, refreshment of a superior class be here ready for the lady and gentleman of the county family who come to the ball; but the common sort of person be keep out of the room by a waiter who stand at the portal, and who say that be a select room for Lady Clavering and Lady Rockminster's party, and not to be open to the public till supper-time, which be not to be until past midnight. Pynsent, who dance with his constituent' daughter, take they and their mamma in for their refreshment there. strong, who be manager and master of the revel wherever he go, have of course the entree--and the only person who be now occupy the room be the gentleman with the black wig and the order in his button--hole; the officer in the service of his Highness the Nawaub of Lucknow.
this gentleman have establish himself very early in the evening in this apartment, where, say he be confoundedly thirsty, he call for a bottle of champagne. at this order the waiter instantly suppose that he have to do with a grandee, and the Colonel sate down and begin to eat his supper and absorb his drink, and enter affably into conversation with anybody who enter the room.
Sir Francis Clavering and Mr. Wagg find he there, when they leave the ballroom, which they do pretty early--Sir Francis to go and smoke a cigar, and look at the people gather outside the ballroom on the shore, which he declare be much well fun than to remain within; Mr. Wagg to hang on to a Baronet's arm, as he be always pleased to do on the arm of the great man in the company. Colonel Altamont have star at these gentleman in so odd a manner, as they pass through the 'Select' room, that Clavering make inquiry of the landlord who he be, and hint a strong opinion that the officer of the Nawaub's service be drunk.
Mr. Pynsent, too, have have the honour of a conversation with the servant of the indian potentate. it be Pynsent's cue to speak to everybody (which he do, to do he justice, in the most ungracious manner); and he take the gentleman in the black wig for some constituent, some merchant captain, or other outlandish man of the place. Mr. Pynsent, then, come into the refreshment-room with a lady, the wife of a constituent, on his arm, the Colonel ask he if he would try a glass of Sham? Pynsent take it with great gravity, bow, taste the wine, and pronounce it excellent, and with the utmost politeness retreat before Colonel Altamont. this gravity and decorum rout and surprise the Colonel more than any other kind of behaviour probably would: he star after Pynsent stupidly, and pronounce to the landlord over the counter that he be a rum one. Mr. Rincer blush, and hardly know what to say. Mr. Pynsent be a county Earl's grandson, go to set up as a Parliament man. Colonel Altamont on the other hand, wear order and diamond, jingle sovereign constantly in his pocket, and pay his way like a man; so not know what to say, Mr. Rincer say, "yes, Colonel--yes, ma'am, do you say tea? Cup a tea for Mr. Jones, Mrs. R.," and so get off that discussion regard Mr. Pynsent's quality, into which the Nizam's officer appear inclined to enter.
in fact, if the truth must be tell, Mr. Altamont, have remain at the buffet almost all night, and employ himself very actively whilst there, have considerably flush his brain by drinking, and he be still go on drinking, when Mr. Strong and Miss Amory enter the room.
when the Chevalier run out of the apartment, attract by the noise in the dancing-room, the Colonel rise from his chair with his little red eye glow like coal, and, with rather an unsteady gait advanced towards Blanche, who be sip her ice. she be absorb in absorb it, for it be very fresh and good; or she be not curious to know what be go on in the adjoining room, although the waiter be, who run after Chevalier Strong. so that when she look up from her glass, she behold this strange man star at she out of his little red eye. "who be he? it be quite exciting."
"and so yoube Betsy Amory," say he, after gaze at she. "Betsy Amory, by Jove!"
"who--who speak to I?" say Betsy, alias Blanche.
but the noise in the ballroom be really become so loud, that we must rush back thither, and see what be the cause of the disturbance.
CHAPTER XXVIII
which be both Quarrelsome and Sentimental
civil war be rag, high word pass, people push and squeeze together in an unseemly manner, round a window in the corner of the ballroom, close by the door through which the Chevalier Strong shoulder his way. through the open window, the crowd in the street below be send up sarcastic remark, such as "pitch into he!" "wherebe the police?" and the like; and a ring of individual, amongst whom Madame Fribsby be conspicuous, be gather round Monsieur Alcide Mirobolant on the one side; whilst several gentleman and lady surround our friend Arthur Pendennis on the other. Strong penetrate into this assembly, elbow by Madame Fribsby, who be charm at the Chevalier's appearance, and cry, "save he, save he!" in frantic and pathetic accent.
the cause of the disturbance, it appear, be the angry little chef of Sir Francis Clavering's culinary establishment. shortly after Strong have quit the room, and whilst Mr. Pen, greatly irate at his downfall in the waltz, which have make he look ridiculous in the eye of the nation, and by Miss Amory's behaviour to he, which have still far insult his dignity, be endeavour to get some coolness of body and temper, by look out of window towards the sea, which be sparkle in the distance, and murmur in a wonderful calm--whilst he be really try to compose himself, and own to himself, perhaps, that he have act in a very absurd and peevish manner during the night--he feel a hand upon his shoulder; and, on look round, beheld, to his utter surprise and horror, that the hand in question belong to Monsieur Mirobolant, whose eye be glare out of his pale face and ringlet at Mr. Pen. to be tap on the shoulder by a french cook be a piece of familiarity which make the blood of the Pendennises to boil up in the vein of their descendant, and he be astounded, almost more than enraged, at such an indignity.
"you speak French?" Mirobolant say in his own language to Pen.
"what be that to you, pray?" say Pen, in English.
"at any rate, you understand it?" continue the other, with a bow.
"yes, sir," say Pen, with a stamp of his foot; "I understand it pretty well."
"Vous I comprendrez alors, Monsieur Pendennis," reply the other, roll out his r with Gascon force, "quand je vous dis que vous ete un lache. Monsieur Pendennis--un lache, entendez-vous?"
"what?" say Pen, start round on he.
"you understand the meaning of the word and its consequence among man of honour?" the artist say, put his hand on his hip, and star at Pen.
"the consequence be, that I will fling you out of window, you impudent scoundrel," bawl out Mr. Pen; and dart upon the Frenchman, he would very likely have put his threat into execution, for the window be at hand, and the artist by no mean a match for the young gentleman--have not Captain Broadfoot and another heavy officer fling themselves between the combatants,--had not the lady begin to scream,--had not the fiddle stop, have not the crowd of people come run in that direction,--had not Laura, with a face of great alarm, look over their head and ask for Heaven's sake what be wrong,--had not the opportune Strong make his appearance from the refreshment-room, and find Alcides grind his tooth and jabber oath in his Galleon French, and Pen look uncommonly wicked, although try to appear as calm as possible, when the lady and the crowd come up.
"what have happen?" Strong ask of the chef, in Spanish.
"I be Chevalier de Juillet," say the other, slap his breast, "and he have insult I."
"what have he say to you?" ask Strong.
"Il m'a appele--Cuisinier," hiss out the little Frenchman.
Strong could hardly help laugh. "come away with I, poor Chevalier," he say. "we must not quarrel before lady. come away; I will carry your message to Mr. pendennis.--the poor fellow be not right in his head," he whisper to one or two people about him;--and other, and anxious Laura's face visible amongst these, gather round Pen and ask the cause of the disturbance.
Pen do not know. "the man be go to give his arm to a young lady, on which I say that he be a cook, and the man call I a coward and challenge I to fight. I own I be so surprised and indignant, that if you gentleman have not stop I, I should have throw he out of window," Pen say.
"d--- he, serve he right, too,--the impudent foreign scoundrel," the gentleman say.
"I--i'm very sorry if I hurt his feeling, though," Pen add and Laura be glad to hear he say that; although some of the young buck say, "no, hang the fellow,--hang those impudent foreigner--little thrashing would do they good."
"you will go and shake hand with he before you go to sleep--won't you, Pen?" say Laura, come up to he. "foreigner may be more susceptible than we be, and have different manner. if you hurt a poor man's feeling, I be sure you would be the first to ask his pardon. wouldnot you, dear Pen?"
she look all forgiveness and gentleness, like an angel, as she speak; and Pen take both her hand, and look into her kind face, and say indeed he would.
"how fond that girl be of I!" he think, as she stand gaze at he. "shall I speak to she now? no--not now. I must have this absurd business with the Frenchman over."
Laura ask--wouldn't he stop and dance with she? she be as anxious to keep he in the room, as he to quit it. "willnot you stop and waltz with I, Pen? Ibe not afraid to waltz with you."
this be an affectionate, but an unlucky speech. Pen see himself prostrate on the ground, have tumble over Miss Roundle and the dragoon, and fling Blanche up against the wall--see himself on the ground, and all the people laugh at he, Laura and Pynsent amongst they.
"I shall never dance again," he reply, with a dark and determined face. "never. Ibe surprised you should ask I."
"be it because you cannot get Blanche for a partner?" ask Laura, with a wicked, unlucky captiousness.
"because I donot wish to make a fool of myself, for other people to laugh at I," Pen answered--"for you to laugh at I, Laura. I see you and Pynsent. by Jove! no man shall laugh at I."
"Pen, Pen, donot be so wicked!" cry out the poor girl, hurt at the morbid perverseness and savage vanity of Pen. he be glare round in the direction of Mr. Pynsent as if he would have like to engage that gentleman as he have do the cook. "who think the bad of you for stumble in a waltz?" if Laura do, we donot. "why be you so sensitive, and ready to think evil?"
here again, by ill luck, Mr. Pynsent come up to Laura, and say "I have it in command from Lady Rockminster to ask whether I may take you in to supper?"
"I--I be go in with my cousin," Laura say.
"o--pray, no!" say Pen. "you be in such good hand, that I cannot do well than leave you: and Ibe go home."
"good-night, Mr. Pendennis," Pynsent say, drily--to which speech (which, in fact, mean, "go to the deuce for an insolent, jealous, impertinent jackanape, whose ear I should like to box") Mr. Pendennis do not vouchsafe any reply, except a bow: and in spite of Laura's implore look, he leave the room.
"how beautifully calm and bright the night outside be!" say Mr. Pynsent; "and what a murmur the sea be make! it would be pleasanter to be walk on the beach, than in this hot room."
"very," say Laura.
"what a strange congregation of people," continue Pynsent. "I have have to go up and perform the agreeable to most of they--the attorney's daughter--the apothecary's wife--I scarcely know whom. there be a man in the refreshment-room, who insist upon treat I to champagne--a seafaring-look man--extraordinarily dress, and seem half tipsy. as a public man one be bind to conciliate all these people, but it be a hard task--especially when one would so very much like to be elsewhere"-- and he blush rather as he speak.
"I beg your pardon," say laura--"i--I be not listen. indeed--I be frightened about that quarrel between my cousin and that--that--french person."
"your cousin have be rather unlucky to-night," Pynsent say. "there be three or four person whom he have not succeed in pleasing--captain Broadwood; what be his name--the officer--and the young lady in red with whom he dance--and Miss Blanche--and the poor chef--and I donot think he seem to be particularly pleased with I."
"donot he leave I in charge to you?" Laura say, look up into Mr. Pynsent's face, and drop her eye instantly, like a guilty little story-tell coquette.
"indeed, I can forgive he a good deal for that," Pynsent eagerly cry out, and she take his arm, and he lead off his little prize in the direction of the supper-room.
she have no great desire for that repast, though it be serve in Rincer's well-know style, as the county paper say, give an account of the entertainment afterwards; indeed, she be very distraite; and exceedingly pained and unhappy about Pen. captious and quarrelsome; jealous and selfish; fickle and violent and unjust when his anger lead he astray; how could her mother (as indeed Helen have by a thousand word and hint) ask she to give her heart to such a man? and suppose she be to do so, would it make he happy?
but she get some relief at length, when, at the end of half an hour--a long half-hour it have seem to she--a waiter bring she a little note in pencil from Pen, who say, "I meet Cooky below ready to fight I; and I ask his pardon. Ibe glad I do it. I want to speak to you to-night, but will keep what I have to say till you come home. God bless you. dance away all night with Pynsent, and be very happy.--pen." Laura be very thankful for this letter, and to think that there be goodness and forgiveness still in her mother's boy.
Pen go downstairs, his heart reproach he for his absurd behaviour to Laura, whose gentle and imploring look follow and rebuke he; and he be scarcely out of the ballroom door but he long to turn back and ask her pardon. but he remember that he have leave she with that confound Pynsent. he could not apologise before he. he would compromise and forget his wrath, and make his peace with the Frenchman.
the Chevalier be pace down below in the hall of the inn when Pen descend from the ballroom; and he come up to Pen, with all sort of fun and mischief light up his jolly face.
"I have get he in the coffee-room," he say, "with a brace of pistol and a candle. or would you like sword on the beach? mirobolant be a dead hand with the foil, and kill four garde-du-corps with his own point in the barricade of July."
"confound it," say Pen, in a fury, "I cannot fight a cook!"
"he be a Chevalier of July," reply the other. "they present arm to he in his own country."
"and do you ask I, Captain Strong, to go out with a servant?" Pen ask fiercely; "Iwill call a policeman he but--but----"
"youwill invite I to hair trigger?" cry Strong, with a laugh. "thank you for nothing; I be but joke. I come to settle quarrel, not to fight they. I have be soothe down Mirobolant; I have tell he that you do not apply the word 'Cook' to he in an offensive sense: that it be contrary to all the custom of the country that a hire officer of a household, as I call it, should give his arm to the daughter of the house." and then he tell Pen the grand secret which he have have from Madame Fribsby of the violent passion under which the poor artist be labour.
when Arthur hear this tale, he break out into a hearty laugh, in which Strong join, and his rage against the poor cook vanish at once. he have be absurdly jealous himself all the evening, and have long for a pretext to insult Pynsent. he remember how jealous he have be of oak in his first affair; he be ready to pardon anything to a man under a passion like that: and he go into the coffee-room where Mirobolant be wait, with an outstretched hand, and make he a speech in French, in which he declare that he be "sincerement fache d'avoir use une expression qui avoit pu blesser Monsieur Mirobolant, et qu'il donnoit sa parole comme un gentilhomme qu'il ne l'avoit jamais, jamais--intende," say Pen, who make a shot at a french word for "intended," and be secretly much pleased with his own fluency and correctness in speak that language.
"Bravo, bravo!" cry Strong, as much amuse with Pen's speech as pleased by his kind manner. and the Chevalier Mirobolant of course withdraw, and sincerely regret the expression of which he make use.
"Monsieur Pendennis have disprove my word himself," say Alcide with great politeness; "he have show that he be a galant homme."
and so they shake hand and part, Arthur in the first place despatch his note to Laura before he and Strong commit themselves to the Butcher Boy.
as they drive along, Strong compliment Pen upon his behaviour, as well as upon his skill in French. "yoube a good fellow, Pendennis, and you speak French like Chateaubriand, by Jove."
"Ihave be accustom to it from my youth upwards," say Pen; and Strong have the grace not to laugh for five minute, when he explode into fit of hilarity which Pendennis have never perhaps understand up to this day.
it be daybreak when they get to the Brawl, where they separate. by that time the ball at Baymouth be over too. Madame Fribsby and Mirobolant be on their way home in the Clavering fly; Laura be in bed with an easy heart and asleep at Lady Rockminster's; and the clavering at rest at the inn at Baymouth, where they have quarter for the night. a short time after the disturbance between Pen and the chef, Blanche have come out of the refreshment-room, look as pale as a lemon-ice. she tell she maid, have no other confidante at hand, that she have meet with the most romantic adventure--the most singular man--one who have know the author of her be--her persecute--her unhappy--her heroic--her murder father; and she begin a sonnet to his mane before she go to sleep.
so Pen return to Fairoaks, in company with his friend the Chevalier, without have utter a word of the message which he have be so anxious to deliver to Laura at Baymouth. he could wait, however, until her return home, which be to take place on the succeed day. he be not seriously jealous of the progress make by Mr. Pynsent in her favour; and he feel pretty certain that in this, as in any other family arrangement, he have but to ask and have, and Laura, like his mother, could refuse he nothing.
when Helen's anxious look inquire of he what have happen at Baymouth, and whether her darling project be fulfil, Pen, in a gay tone, tell of the calamity which have befall; laughingly say, that no man could think about declaration under such a mishap, and make light of the matter. "there will be plenty of time for sentiment, dear mother, when Laura come back," he say, and he look in the glass with a kill air, and his mother put his hair off his forehead and kiss he, and of course think, for her part, that no woman could resist he: and be exceedingly happy that day.
when he be not with she, Mr. Pen occupy himself in pack book and portmanteau, burn and arrange paper, clean his gun and put it into its case: in fact, in make disposition for departure. for though he be ready to marry, this gentleman be eager to go to London too, rightly consider that at three-and-twenty it be quite time for he to begin upon the serious business of life, and to set about make a fortune as quickly as possible.
the mean to this end he have already shape out for himself. "I shall take chamber," he say, "and enter myself at an Inn of Court. with a couple of hundred pound I shall be able to carry through the first year very well; after that I have little doubt my pen will support I, as it be do with several Oxbridge man now in town. I have a tragedy, a comedy, and a novel, all nearly finish, and for which I cannot fail to get a price. and so I shall be able to live pretty well, without draw upon my poor mother, until I have make my way at the bar. then, some day I will come back and make her dear soul happy by marry Laura. she be as good and as sweet-temper a girl as ever live, besides be really very good-looking, and the engagement will serve to steady me,--won't it, Ponto?" thus, smoke his pipe, and talk to his dog as he saunter through the garden and orchard of the little domain of Fairoaks, this young day-dreamer build castle in the air for himself: "yes, shewill steady I, willnot she? and youwill miss I when Ihave go, willnot you, old boy?" he ask of Ponto, who quiver his tail and thrust his brown nose into his master's fist. Ponto lick his hand and shoe, as they all do in that house, and Mr. Pen receive their homage as other folk do the flattery which they get.
Laura come home rather late in the evening of the second day; and Mr. Pynsent, as ill luck would have it, drive she from Clavering. the poor girl could not refuse his offer, but his appearance bring a dark cloud upon the brow of Arthur Pendennis. Laura see this, and be pain by it: the eager widow, however, be aware of nothing, and be anxious, doubtless, that the delicate question should be ask at once, be for go to bed very soon after Laura's arrival, and rise for that purpose to leave the sofa where she now generally lay, and where Laura would come and sit and work or read by she. but when Helen rise, Laura say, with a blush and rather an alarmed voice, that she be also very tired and want to go to bed: so that the widow be disappoint in her scheme for that night at least, and Mr. Pen be leave another day in suspense regard his fate.
his dignity be offend at be thus oblige to remain in the ante-chamber when he want an audience. such a sultan as he, could not afford to be keep wait. however, he go to bed and sleep upon his disappointment pretty comfortably, and do not wake until the early morning, when he look up and see his mother stand in his room.
"Dear Pen, rouse up," say this lady. "do not be lazy. it be the most beautiful morning in the world. I have not be able to sleep since daybreak; and Laura have be out for an hour. she be in the garden. everybody ought to be in the garden and out on such a morning as this."
Pen laugh. he see what thought be uppermost in the simple woman's heart. his good-natured laughter cheer the widow. "oh you profound dissembler," he say, kiss his mother. "oh you artful creature! can nobody escape from your wicked trick? and will you make your only son your victim?" Helen too laugh, she blush, she flutter, and be agitated. she be as happy as she could be--a good tender, matchmaking woman, the dear project of whose heart be about to be accomplish.
so, after exchange some know look and hasty word, Helen leave Arthur; and this young hero, rise from his bed, proceed to decorate his beautiful person, and shave his ambrosial chin; and in half an hour he issue out from his apartment into the garden in quest of Laura. his reflection as he make his toilette be rather dismal. "I be go to tie myself for life," he think, "to please my mother. Laura be the good of woman, and--and she have give I her money. I wish to Heaven I have not receive it; I wish I have not this duty to perform just yet. but as both the woman have set their heart on the match, why I suppose I must satisfy they--and now for it. a man may do bad than make happy two of the good creature in the world." so Pen, now he be actually come to the point, feel very grave, and by no mean elated, and, indeed, think it be a great sacrifice he be go to perform.
it be Miss Laura's custom, upon her garden excursion, to wear a sort of uniform, which, though homely, be think by many people to be not unbecoming. she have a large straw hat, with a streamer of broad ribbon, which be useless probably, but the hat sufficiently protect the owner's pretty face from the sun. over her accustomed gown she wear a blouse or pinafore, which, be fasten round her little waist by a smart belt, look extremely well, and her band be guarantee from the thorn of her favourite rose-bush by a pair of gauntlet, which give this young lady a military and resolute air.
somehow she have the very same smile with which she have laugh at he on the night previous, and the recollection of his disaster again offend Pen. but Laura, though she see he come down the walk look so gloomy and full of care, accord to he a smile of the most perfect and provoke good-humour, and go to meet he, hold one of the gauntlet to he, so that he might shake it if he like--and Mr. Pen condescend to do so. his face, however, do not lose its tragic expression in consequence of this favour, and he continue to regard she with a dismal and solemn air.
"excuse my glove," say Laura, with a laugh, press Pen's hand kindly with it. "we be not angry again, be we, Pen?"
"why do you laugh at I?" say Pen. "you do the other night, and make a fool of I to the people at Baymouth."
"my dear Arthur, I mean you no wrong," the girl answer. "you and Miss Roundle look so droll as you--as you meet with your little accident, that I could not make a tragedy of it. Dear Pen, it benot a serious fall. and, besides, it be Miss Roundle who be the most unfortunate."
"confound Miss Roundle," bellow out Pen.
"Ibe sure she look so," say Laura, archly. "you be up in an instant; but that poor lady sit on the ground in her red crape dress, and look about she with that piteous face--can I ever forget her?"--and Laura begin to make a face in imitation of Miss Roundle's under the disaster, but she check herself repentantly, say, "well, we must not laugh at she, but I be sure we ought to laugh at you, Pen, if you be angry about such a trifle."
"you should not laugh at I, Laura," say Pen, with some bitterness; "not you, of all people."
"and why not? be you such a great man?" ask Laura,
"ah no, Laura, Ibe such a poor one," Pen answer. "havenot you bait I enough already?"
"my dear Pen, and how?" cry Laura. "indeed, indeed, I donot think to vex you by such a trifle. I think such a clever man as you could bear a harmless little joke from his sister," she say, hold her hand out again. "Dear Arthur, if I have hurt you, I beg your pardon."
"it be your kindness that humiliate I more even than your laughter, Laura," Pen say. "you be always my superior."
"what! superior to the great Arthur Pendennis? how can it be possible?" say Miss Laura, who may have have a little wickedness as well as a great deal of kindness in her composition. "you cannot mean that any woman be your equal?"
"those who confer benefit should not sneer," say Pen. "I donot like my benefactor to laugh at I, Laura; it make the obligation very hard to bear. you scorn I because I have take your money, and I be worthy to be scorn; but the blow be hard come from you."
"money! Obligation! for shame, Pen; this be ungenerous," Laura say, flush red. "may not our mother claim everything that belong to we? donot I owe she all my happiness in this world, Arthur? what matter about a few paltry guineas, if we can set her tender heart at rest, and ease her mind regard you? I would dig in the field, I would go out and be a servant--I would die for she. you know I would," say Miss Laura, kindle up; "and you call this paltry money an obligation? oh, Pen, itbe cruel--itbe unworthy of you to take it so! if my brother may not share with I my superfluity, who may?--Mine?--I tell you it be not mine; it be all mamma's to do with as she choose, and so be everything I have," say Laura; "my life be her." and the enthusiastic girl look towards the window of the widow's room, and bless in her heart the kind creature within.
Helen be look, unseen, out of that window towards which Laura's eye and heart be turn as she speak, and be watch she two child with the deep interest and emotion, longing and hop that the prayer of her life might be fulfil; and if Laura have speak as Helen hop, who know what temptation Arthur Pendennis might have be spar, or what different trial he would have have to undergo? he might have remain at Fairoaks all his day, and die a country gentleman. but would he have escape then? temptation be an obsequious servant that have no objection to the country, and we know that it take up its lodging in hermitage as well as in city; and that in the most remote and inaccessible desert it keep company with the fugitive solitary.
"be your life my mother's?" say Pen, begin to tremble, and speak in a very agitated manner. "you know, Laura, what the great object of hers be?" and he take her hand once more.
"what, Arthur?" she say, drop it, and look at he, at the window again, and then drop her eye to the ground, so that they avoid Pen's gaze. she, too, tremble, for she feel that the crisis for which she have be secretly prepare be come.
"our mother have one wish above all other in the world, Laura," Pen say; "and I think you know it. I own to you that she have speak to I of it; and if you will fulfil it, dear sister, I be ready. I be but very young as yet; but I have have so many pain and disappointment, that I be old and weary. I think I have hardly get a heart to offer. before I have almost begin the race in life, I be a tired man. my career have be a failure; I have be protect by those whom I by right should have protect. I own that your nobleness and generosity, dear Laura, shame I, whilst they render I grateful. when I hear from our mother what you have do for I; that it be you who arm I and bid I go out for one struggle more; I long to go and throw myself at your foot, and say, 'Laura, will you come and share the contest with I?' your sympathy will cheer I while it last. I shall have one of the tender and most generous creature under heaven to aid and bear I company. will you take I, dear Laura, and make our mother happy?"
"do you think mamma would be happy if you be otherwise, Arthur?" Laura say in a low sad voice.
"and why should I not be," ask Pen eagerly, "with so dear a creature as you by my side? I have not my first love to give you. I be a broken man. but indeed I would love you fondly and truly. I have lose many an illusion and ambition, but I be not without hope still. talent I know I have, wretchedly as I have misapply they: they may serve I yet: they would, have I a motive for action. let I go away and think that I be pledge to return to you. let I go and work, and hope, that you will share my success if I gain it. you have give I so much, Laura dear, will you take from I nothing?"
"what have you get to give, Arthur?" Laura say, with a grave sadness of tone, which make Pen start, and see that his word have commit he. indeed, his declaration have not be such as he would have make it two day early, when, full of hope and gratitude, he have run over to Laura, his liberatress, to thank she for his recovered freedom. have he be permit to speak then, he have speak, and she, perhaps, have listen differently. it would have be a grateful heart ask for her; not a weary one offer to she, to take or to leave. Laura be offend with the term in which Pen offer himself to she. he have, in fact, say that he have no love, and yet would take no denial. "I give myself to you to please my mother," he have say: "take I, as she wish that I should make this sacrifice." the girl's spirit would brook a husband under no such condition: she be not minded to run forward because Pen choose to hold out the handkerchief, and her tone, in reply to Arthur, show her determination to be independent.
"no, Arthur," she say, "our marriage would not make mamma happy, as she fancy; for it would not content you very long. I, too, have know what her wish be; for she be too open to conceal anything she have at heart: and once, perhaps, I think--but that be over now--that I could have make you--that it might have be as she wish."
"you have see somebody else," say Pen, angry at her tone, and recall the incident of the past day.
"that allusion might have be spar," Laura reply, fling up her head. "a heart which have wear out love at three-and-twenty, as yours have, you say, should have survive jealousy too. I do not condescend to say whether I have see or encourage any other person. I shall neither admit the charge, nor deny it: and beg you also to allude to it no more."
"I ask your pardon, Laura, if I have offend you: but if I be jealous, do it not prove that I have a heart?"
"not for I, Arthur. perhaps you think you love I now but it be only for an instant, and because you be foil. be there no obstacle, you would feel no ardour to overcome it. no, Arthur, you donot love I. you would weary of I in three month, as--as you do of most thing; and mamma, see you tired of I, would be more unhappy than at my refusal to be yours. let we be brother and sister, Arthur, as heretofore--but no more. you will get over this little disappointment."
"I will try," say Arthur, in a great indignation.
"have you not try before?" Laura say, with some anger, for she have be angry with Arthur for a very long time, and be now determine, I suppose, to speak her mind. "and the next time, Arthur, when you offer yourself to a woman, do not say as you have do to I, 'I have no heart --I do not love you; but I be ready to marry you because my mother wish for the match.' we require more than this in return for our love--that be, I think so. I have have no experience hitherto, and have not have the-- the practice which you suppose I to have, when you speak but now of my have see somebody else. do you tell your first love that you have no heart, Arthur? or your second that you do not love she, but that she might have you if she like?"
"what--what do you mean?" ask Arthur, blushing, and still in great wrath.
"I mean Blanche Amory, Arthur Pendennis," Laura say, proudly. "it be but two month since you be sigh at her foot--make poem to her-- place they in hollow tree by the river-side. I know all. I watch you--that is, she show they to I. neither one nor the other be in earnest perhaps; but it be too soon now, Arthur, to begin a new attachment. go through the time of your--your widowhood at least, and do not think of marry until you be out of mourning"--(Here the girl's eye fill with tear, and she pass her hand across they.) "I be angry and hurt, and I have no right to be so, and I ask your pardon in my turn now, dear Arthur. you have a right to love Blanche. she be a thousand time pretty and more accomplished than--than any girl near we here; and you not could know that she have no heart; and so you be right to leave she too. I ought not to rebuke you about Blanche Amory, and because she deceive you. pardon I, pen,"--and she hold the kind hand out to Pen once more.
"we be both jealous," say Pen. "Dear Laura, let we both forgive"--and he seize her band and would have draw she towards he. he think that she be relent, and already assume the air of a victor.
but she shrink back, and her tear pass away; and she fix on he a look so melancholy and severe, that the young man in his turn shrank before it. "do not mistake I, Arthur," she say, "it cannot be. you do not know what you ask, and do not be too angry with I for say that I think you do not deserve it. what do you offer in exchange to a woman for her love, honour, and obedience? if ever I say these word, dear Pen, I hope to say they in earnest, and by the blessing of God to keep my vow. but you--what tie bind you? you do not care about many thing which we poor woman hold sacred, I do not like to think or ask how far your incredulity lead you. you offer to marry to please our mother, and own that you have no heart to give away. oh, Arthur, what be it you offer I? what a rash compact would you enter into so lightly? a month ago, and you would have give yourself to another. I pray you do not trifle with your own or other' heart so recklessly. go and work; go and mend, dear Arthur, for I see your fault, and dare speak of they now: go and get fame, as you say that you can, and I will pray for my brother, and watch our dear mother at home."
"be that your final decision, Laura?" Arthur cry.
"yes," say Laura, bow her head; and once more give he her hand, she go away. he see her pass under the creeper of the little porch, and disappear into the house. the curtain of his mother's window fall at the same minute, but he do not mark that, or suspect that Helen have be witness the scene.
be he please, or be he angry at its termination? he have ask she, and a secret triumph fill his heart to think that he be still free. she have refuse he, but do she not love he? that avowal of jealousy make he still think that her heart be his own, whatever her lip might utter.
and now we ought, perhaps, to describe another scene which take place at Fairoaks, between the widow and Laura, when the latter have to tell Helen that she have refuse Arthur Pendennis. perhaps it be the hard task of all which Laura have to go through in this matter: and the one which give she the most pain. but as we do not like to see a good woman unjust, we shall not say a word more of the quarrel which now befell between Helen and her adopt daughter, or of the bitter tear which the poor girl be make to shed. it be the only difference which she and the widow have ever have as yet, and the more cruel from this cause. Pen leave home whilst it be as yet pending--and Helen, who could pardon almost everything, could not pardon an act of justice in Laura.
CHAPTER XXIX
Babylon
our reader must now please to quit the wood and sea-shore of the west, and the gossip of Clavering, and the humdrum life of poor little fairoak, and transport himself with Arthur Pendennis, on the 'Alacrity' coach, to London, whither he go once for all to face the world and to make his fortune. as the coach whirl through the night away from the friendly gate of home, many a plan do the young man cast in his mind of future life and conduct, prudence, and peradventure success and fame. he know he be a well man than many who have hitherto be ahead of he in the race: his first failure have cause he remorse, and bring with it reflection; it have not take away his courage, or, let we add, his good opinion of himself. a hundred eager fancy and busy hope keep he awake. how much old his mishap and a year's thought and self-communion have make he, than when, twelve month since, he pass on this road on his way to and from Oxbridge! his thought turn in the night with inexpressible fondness and tenderness towards the fond mother who bless he when parting, and who, in spite of all his past fault and folly, trust he and love he still. blessing be on she! he pray, as he look up to the star overhead. o Heaven! give he strength to work, to endure, to be honest, to avoid temptation, to be worthy of the love soul who love he so entirely! very likely she be awake, too, at that moment, and send up to the same Father purer prayer than his for the welfare of her boy. that woman's love be a talisman by which he hold and hop to get his safety. and Laura's--he would have fain carry her affection with he too, but she have deny it, as he be not worthy of it. he own as much with shame and remorse; confess how much well and lofty her nature be than his own--confess it, and yet be glad to be free. "I be not good enough for such a creature," he own to himself. he draw back before her spotless beauty and innocence, as from something that scar he. he feel he be not fit for such a mate as that; as many a wild prodigal who have be pious and guiltless in early day, keep away from a church which he use to frequent once--shun it, but not hostile to it--only feel that he have no right in that pure place.
with these thought to occupy he, Pen do not fall asleep until the nipping dawn of an October morning, and wake considerably refresh when the coach stop at the old breakfast place at B----, where he have have a score of merry meal on his way to and from school and college many time since he be a boy. as they leave that place, the sun break out brightly, the pace be rapid, the horn blow, the milestone fly by, Pen smoke and joke with guard and fellow-passenger and people along the familiar road; it grow more busy and animate at every instant; the last team of grey come out at H----, and the coach drive into London. what young fellow have not feel a thrill as he enter the vast place? hundred of other carriage, crowd with their thousand of man, be hasten to the great city. "here be my place," think Pen; "here be my battle beginning, in which I must fight and conquer, or fall. I have be a boy and a dawdler as yet. oh, I long, I long to show that I can be a man." and from his place on the coach-roof the eager young fellow look down upon the city, with the sort of longing desire which young soldier feel on the eve of a campaign.
as they come along the road, Pen have form acquaintance with a cheery fellow-passenger in a shabby cloak, who talk a great deal about man of letter with whom he be very familiar, and who be, in fact, the reporter of a London newspaper, as whose representative he have be to attend a great wrestling-match in the west. this gentleman know intimately, as it appear, all the lead man of letter of his day, and talk about Tom Campbell, and Tom Hood, and Sydney Smith, and this and the other, as if he have be their most intimate friend. as they pass by Brompton, this gentleman point out to Pen Mr. Hurtle, the reviewer, walk with his umbrella. Pen crane over the coach to have a long look at the great Hurtle. he be a Boniface man, say Pen. and Mr. Doolan, of the Star newspaper (for such be the gentleman's name and address upon the card which he hand to Pen), say "faith he be, and he know he very well." Pen think it be quite an honour to have see the great Mr. Hurtle, whose work he admire. he believe fondly, as yet, in author, reviewer, and editor of newspaper. even Wagg, whose book do not appear to he to be masterpiece of human intellect, he yet secretly revere as a successful writer. he mention that he have meet Wagg in the country, and Doolan tell he how that famous novelist receive three hundther pound a volume for every one of his novel. Pen begin to calculate instantly whether he might not make five thousand a year.
the very first acquaintance of his own whom Arthur meet, as the coach pull up at the Gloster Coffee-house, be his old friend Harry Foker, who come prance down Arlington Street behind an enormous cab-horse. he have white kid glove and white rein, and nature have by this time decorate he with a considerable tuft on the chin. a very small cab-boy, vice Stoopid retire, swing on behind Foker's vehicle; knock-kneed and in the tight leather breech. Foker look at the dusty coach, and the smoking horse of the 'Alacrity' by which he have make journey in former time. "what, Foker!" cry out Pendennis-- "Hullo! Pen, my boy!" say the other, and he wave his whip by way of amity and salute to Arthur, who be very glad to see his queer friend's kind old face. Mr. Doolan have a great respect for Pen who have an acquaintance in such a grand cab; and Pen be greatly excited and pleased to be at liberty and in London. he ask Doolan to come and dine with he at the Covent Garden Coffee-house, where he put up: he call a cab and rattle away thither in the high spirit. he be glad to see the bustling waiter and polite bow landlord again; and ask for the landlady, and miss the old Boots and would have like to shake hand with everybody. he have a hundred pound in his pocket. he dress himself in his very well; din in the coffee-room with a modest pint of sherry (for he be determined to be very economical), and go to the theatre adjoining.
the light and the music, the crowd and the gaiety, charm and exhilarate Pen, as those sight will do young fellow from college and the country, to whom they be tolerably new. he laugh at the joke; he applaud the song, to the delight of some of the dreary old habitue of the box, who have cease long ago to find the least excitement in their place of nightly resort, and be pleased to see any one so fresh, and so much amuse. at the end of the first piece, he go and strut about the lobby of the theatre, as if he be in a resort of the high fashion. what tired frequenter of the London pave be there that cannot remember have have similar early delusion, and would not call they back again? here be young Foker again, like an ardent votary of pleasure as he be. he be walk with Grandy Tiptoff, of the Household Brigade, Lord Tiptoff's brother, and Lord Colchicum, Captain Tiptoff's uncle, a venerable peer, who have be a man of pleasure since the first French Revolution. Foker rush upon Pen with eagerness, and insist that the latter should come into his private box, where a lady with the long ringlet and the fair shoulder, be seat. this be Miss Blenkinsop, the eminent actress of high comedy; and in the back of the box snooze in a wig, sate old Blenkinsop, her papa. he be describe in the theatrical print as the "veteran blenkinsop"--"the useful Blenkinsop"-- "that old favourite of the public, Blenkinsop"--those part in the drama, which be call the heavy father, be usually assign to this veteran, who, indeed, act the heavy father in public, as in private life.
at this time, it be about eleven o'clock, Mrs. Pendennis be go to bed at Fairoaks, and wonder whether her dear Arthur be at rest after his journey. at this time Laura, too, be awake. and at this time yesterday night, as the coach roll over silent common, where cottage window twinkle, and by darkle wood under calm starlit sky, Pen be vow to reform and to resist temptation, and his heart be at home. meanwhile the farce be go on very successfully, and Mrs. Leary, in a hussar jacket and braided pantaloon, be enchant the audience with her archness, her lovely figure, and her delightful ballad.
Pen, be new to the town, would have like to listen to Mrs. Leary; but the other people in the box do not care about her song or her pantaloon, and keep up an incessant chattering. Tiptoff know where her maillot come from. Colchicum see she when she come out in '14. Miss Blenkinsop say she sing out of all tune, to the pain and astonishment of Pen, who think that she be as beautiful as an angel, and that she sing like a nightingale; and when Hoppus come on as Sir Harcourt Featherby, the young man of the piece, the gentleman in the box declare that Hoppus be get too stale, and Tiptoff be for fling Miss Blenkinsop's bouquet to he.
"not for the world," cry the daughter of the veteran Blenkinsop; "Lord Colchicum give it to I."
Pen remember that nobleman's name, and with a bow and a blush say he believe he have to thank Lord Colchicum for have propose he at the Megatherium Club, at the request of his uncle, Major Pendennis.
"what, yoube Wigsby's nephew, be you?" say the peer. "I beg your pardon, we always call he Wigsby." Pen blush to hear his venerable uncle call by such a familiar name. "we ballot you in last week, donot we? yes, last Wednesday night. your uncle benot there."
here be delightful news for Pen! he profess himself very much oblige indeed to Lord Colchicum, and make he a handsome speech of thank, to which the other listen with his double opera-glass up to his eye. Pen be full of excitement at the idea of be a member of this polite Club.
"donot be always look at that box, you naughty creature," cry Miss Blenkinsop.
"shebe a dev'lish fine woman, that Mirabel," say Tiptoff; "though Mirabel be a d----d fool to marry she."
"a stupid old spooney," say the peer. "Mirabel!" cry out Pendennis.
"ha! ha!" laugh out Harry Foker. "wehave hear of she before, havenot we, Pen?"
it be Pen's first love. it be Miss Fotheringay. the year before she have be lead to the altar by Sir Charles Mirabel, G.C.B., and formerly envoy to the Court of Pumpernickel, who have take so active a part in the negotiation before the Congress of Swammerdam, and sign, on behalf of H.B.M., the Peace of Pultusk.
"Emily be always as stupid as an owl," say Miss Blenkinsop.
"eh! Eh! pas si bete," the old Peer say.
"oh, for shame!" cry the actress, who do not in the least know what he mean.
and Pen look out and beheld his first love once again--and wonder how he ever could have love she.
thus on the very first night of his arrival in London, Mr. Arthur Pendennis find himself introduce to a Club, to an actress of genteel comedy and a heavy father of the Stage, and to a dash society of jovial blade, old and young; for my Lord Colchicum, though stricken in year, bald of head and enfeeble in person, be still indefatigable in the pursuit of enjoyment, and it be the venerable Viscount's boast that he could drink as much claret as the young member of the society which he frequent. he live with the youth about town: he give they countless dinner at Richmond and Greenwich: an enlightened patron of the drama in all language and of the terpsichorean art, he receive dramatic professor of all nation at his banquet--English from the Covent Garden and Strand house, Italians from the haymarket, French from their own pretty little theatre, or the board of the Opera where they dance. and at his villa on the Thames, this pillar of the State give sumptuous entertainment to score of young man of fashion, who very affably consort with the lady and gentleman of the greenroom--with the former chiefly, for Viscount Colchicum prefer their society as more polished and gay than that of their male brother.
