Pen go the next day and pay his entrance-money at the Club, which operation carry off exactly one-third of his hundred pound; and take possession of the edifice, and eat his luncheon there with immense satisfaction. he plunge into an easy-chair in the library, and try to read all the magazine. he wonder whether the member be look at he, and that they could dare to keep on their hat in such fine room. he sate down and write a letter to Fairoaks on the Club paper, and say, what a comfort this place would be to he after his day's work be over. he go over to his uncle's lodging in Bury Street with some considerable tremor, and in compliance with his mother's earnest desire, that he should instantly call on major Pendennis; and be not a little relieved to find that the Major have not yet return to town. his apartment be blank. brown holland cover his library-table, and bill and letter lie on the mantelpiece, grimly await the return of their owner. the Major be on the Continent, the landlady of the house say, at Badnbadn, with the Marcus of Steyne. Pen leave his card upon the shelf with the rest. Fairoaks be write on it still.
when the Major return to London, which he do in time for the fog of November, after enjoy which he propose to spend Christmas with some friend in the country, he find another card of Arthur's, on which Lamb Court, Temple, be engrave, and a note from that young gentleman and from his mother, state that he be come to town, be enter a member of the Upper Temple, and be read hard for the bar.
Lamb Court, Temple:--where be it? major Pendennis remember that some lady of fashion use to talk of dining with Mr. Ayliffe, the barrister, who be "in society," and who live there in the King's Bench, of which prison there be probably a branch in the Temple, and Ayliffe be very likely an officer. Mr. Deuceace, Lord Crabs's son, have also live there, he recollect. he despatch Morgan to find out where Lamb Court be, and to report upon the lodging select by Mr. Arthur. that alert messenger have little difficulty in discover Mr. Pen's abode. Discreet Morgan have in his time trace people far more difficult to find than Arthur.
"what sort of a place be it, Morgan?" ask the Major, out of the bed-curtain in Bury Street the next morning, as the valet be arrange his toilette in the deep yellow London fog.
"I should say rayther a shy place," say Mr. Morgan. "the lawyer live there, and have their name on the door. Mr. Harthur live three pair high, sir. Mr. Warrington live there too, sir."
"Suffolk Warringtons! I shouldnot wonder: a good family," think the Major. "the cadet of many of our good family follow the robe as a profession. comfortable room, eh?"
"honly see the outside of the door, sir, with Mr. Warrington's name and Mr. Arthur's paint up, and a piece of paper with 'back at 6;' but I couldnot see no servant, sir."
"economical at any rate," say the Major.
"very, sir. three pair, sir. nasty black staircase as ever I see. wonder how a gentleman can live in such a place."
"Pray, who teach you where gentleman should or should not live, Morgan? Mr. Arthur, sir, be go to study for the bar, sir," the Major say with much dignity; and close the conversation and begin to array himself in the yellow fog.
"boy will be boy," the mollified uncle think to himself. "he have write to I a devilish good letter. colchicum say he have have he to dine, and think he a gentlemanlike lad. his mother be one of the good creature in the world. if he have sow his wild oat, and will stick to his business, he may do well yet. think of Charley Mirabel, the old fool, marry that flame of his! that Fotheringay! he donot like to come here until I give he leave, and put it in a very manly nice way. I be deuce angry with he, after his Oxbridge escapade--and show it too when he be here before--Gad, Iwill go and see he, hang I if I donot."
and have ascertain from Morgan that he could reach the Temple without much difficulty, and that a city omnibus would put he down at the gate, the major one day after breakfast at his Club--not the Polyanthus, whereof Mr. Pen be just elect a member, but another Club: for the Major be too wise to have a nephew as a constant inmate of any house where he be in the habit of pass his time--the Major one day enter one of those public vehicle, and bade the conductor to put he down at the gate of the Upper Temple.
when Major Pendennis reach that dingy portal it be about twelve o'clock in the day; and he be direct by a civil personage with a badge and a white apron, through some dark alley, and under various melancholy archway into court each more dismal than the other, until finally he reach Lamb Court. if it be dark in Pall Mail, what be it in Lamb Court? candle be burn in many of the room there--in the pupil-room of Mr. Hodgeman, the special pleader, where six pupil be scribble declaration under the tallow; in Sir Hokey Walker's clerk's room, where the clerk, a person far more gentlemanlike and cheerful in appearance than the celebrate counsel, his master, be converse in a patronising manner with the manage clerk of an attorney at the door; and in curl the wigmaker's melancholy shop, where, from behind the feeble glimmer of a couple of light, large serpent' and judge' wig be loom drearily, with the blank block look at the lamp-post in the court. two little clerk be play at toss-halfpenny under that lamp. a laundress in patten pass in at one door, a newspaper boy issue from another. a porter, whose white apron be faintly visible, pace up and down. it would be impossible to conceive a place more dismal, and the Major shudder to think that any one should select such a residence. "Good Ged!" he say, "the poor boy mustnot live on here."
the feeble and filthy oil-lamp, with which the staircase of the Upper Temple be light of night, be of course not illuminate the stair by day, and Major Pendennis, have read with difficulty his nephew's name under Mr. Warringtonbe on the wall of No. 6, find still great difficulty in climb the abominable black stair, up the banister of which, which contribute their damp exudation to his glove, he grope painfully until he come to the third story. a candle be in the passage of one of the two set of room; the door be open, and the name of Mr. Warrington and Mr. A. Pendennis be very clearly visible to the Major as he go in. an irish charwoman, with a pail and broom, open the door for the Major.
"be that the beer?" cry out a great voice: "give we hold of it."
the gentleman who be speak be seat on a table, unshorn and smoke a short pipe; in a far chair sate Pen, with a cigar, and his leg near the fire. a little boy, who act as the clerk of these gentleman, be grin in the Major's face, at the idea of his be mistaken for beer. here, upon the third floor, the room be somewhat light, and the Major could see place.
"Pen, my boy, itbe I--itbe your uncle," he say, choke with the smoke. but as most young man of fashion use the weed, he pardon the practice easily enough.
Mr. Warrington get up from the table, and Pen, in a very perturb manner, from his chair. "beg your pardon for mistake you," say Warrington, in a frank, loud voice. "will you take a cigar, sir? clear those thing off the chair, Pidgeon, and pull it round to the fire."
Pen fling his cigar into the grate; and be pleased with the cordiality with which his uncle shake he by the hand. as soon as be could speak for the stair and the smoke, the Major begin to ask Pen very kindly about himself and about his mother; for blood be blood, and he be pleased once more to see the boy.
Pen give his news, and then introduce Mr. Warrington--an old Boniface man--whose chamber he share.
the Major be quite satisfied when he hear that Mr. Warrington be a young son of Sir Miles Warrington of Suffolk. he have serve with an uncle of his in India and in New South Wales, year ago.
"took a sheep-farm there, sir, make a fortune--well thing than law or soldiering," Warrington say. "think I shall go there too." and here the expect beer come in, in a tankard with a glass bottom, Mr. Warrington, with a laugh, say he suppose the Major would not have any, and take a long, deep draught himself, after which he wipe his wrist across his beard with great satisfaction. the young man be perfectly easy and unembarrassed. he be dress in a ragged old shooting jacket, and have a bristly blue beard. he be drink beer like a coalheaver, and yet you couldnot but perceive that he be a gentleman.
when he have sate for a minute or two after his draught he go out of the room, leave it to Pen and his uncle, that they might talk over family affair be they so inclined.
"rough and ready, your chum seem," the Major say. "somewhat different from your dandy friend at Oxbridge."
"Times be alter," Arthur reply, with a blush. "Warrington be only just call, and have no business, but he know law pretty well; and until I can afford to read with a pleader, I use his book, and get his help."
"be that one of the book?" the Major ask, with a smile. a french novel be lie at the foot of Pen's chair.
"this be not a work day, sir," the lad say. "we be out very late at a party last night--at Lady Whiston's," Pen add, know his uncle's weakness. "everybody in town be there except you, sir; count, Ambassadors, Turks, Stars and Garters--I donot know who--itbe all in the paper--and my name, too," say Pen, with great glee. "I meet an old flame of mine there, sir," he add, with a laugh. "you know whom I mean, sir, --Lady Mirabel--to whom I be introduce over again. she shake hand, and be gracious enough. I may thank you for be out of that scrape, sir. she present I to the husband, too--an old beau in a star and a blonde wig. he do not seem very wise. she have ask I to call on she, sir: and I may go now without any fear of lose my heart."
"what, we have have some new love, have we?" the Major ask in high good-humour.
"some two or three," Mr. Pen say, laugh. "but I donot put on my grand serieux any more, sir. that go off after the first flame."
"very right, my dear boy. flame and dart and passion, and that sort of thing, do very well for a lad: and you be but a lad when that affair with the Fotheringill--Fotheringay--(what's her name?) come off. but a man of the world give up those folly. you still may do very well. you have be bite, but you may recover. you be heir to a little independence; which everybody fancy be a doosid deal more. you have a good name, good wit, good manner, and a good person--and, begad! I donot see why you shouldnot marry a woman with money--get into Parliament--distinguish yourself, and--and, in fact, that sort of thing. remember, itbe as easy to marry a rich woman as a poor woman: and a devilish deal pleasanter to sit down to a good dinner, than to a scrag of mutton in lodging. make up your mind to that. a woman with a good jointure be a doosid deal easy a profession than the law, let I tell you that. look out; I shall be on the watch for you: and I shall die content, my boy, if I can see you with a good ladylike wife, and a good carriage, and a good pair of horse, live in society, and see your friend, like a gentleman. would you like to vegetate like your dear good mother at Fairoaks? Dammy, sir! life, without money and the good society benot worth have." it be thus this affectionate uncle speak, and expound to Pen his simple philosophy.
"what would my mother and Laura say to this, I wonder?" think the lad. indeed old Pendennis's moral be not their moral, nor be his wisdom theirs.
this affect conversation between uncle and nephew have scarcely conclude, when Warrington come out of his bedroom, no long in rag, but dress like a gentleman, straight and tall and perfectly frank and good-humour. he do the honour of his ragged sitting-room with as much ease as if it have be the fine apartment in London. and queer room they be in which the Major find his nephew. the carpet be full of hole--the table stain with many circle of Warrington's previous ale-pot. there be a small library of law-book, book of poetry, and of mathematic, of which he be very fond. (he have be one of the hard liver and hard reader of his time at Oxbridge, where the name of Stunning Warrington be yet famous for beat bargeman, pull match, win prize, and drink milk-punch.) a print of the old college hang up over the mantelpiece, and some batter volume of Plato, bear its well-know arm, be on the book-shelf. there be two easy-chair; a stand reading-desk pile with bill; a couple of very meagre brief on a break-legged study-table. indeed, there be scarcely any article of furniture that have not be in the war, and be not wound. "look here, sir, here be Pen's room. he be a dandy, and have get curtain to his bed, and wear shiny boot, and a silver dressing-case." indeed, Pen's room be rather coquettishly arrange, and a couple of neat print of opera-dancer, besides a drawing of Fairoaks, hang on the wall. in Warrington's room there be scarcely any article of furniture, save a great shower-bath, and a heap of book by the bedside: where he lie upon straw like Margery Daw, and smoke his pipe, and read half through the night his favourite poetry or mathematic.
when he have complete his simple toilette, Mr. Warrington come out of this room, and proceed to the cupboard to search for his breakfast.
"might I offer you a mutton-chop, sir? we cook them ourselves hot and hot: and I be teach Pen the first principle of law, cooking, and morality at the same time. hebe a lazy beggar, sir, and too much of a dandy."
and so say, Mr. Warrington wipe a gridiron with a piece of paper, put it on the fire, and on it two mutton-chop, and take from the cupboard a couple of plate and some knife and silver fork, and castor.
"say but a word, Major Pendennis," he say; "therebe another chop in the cupboard, or Pidgeon shall go out and get you anything you like."
Major Pendennis sate in wonder and amusement, but he say he have just breakfast, and wouldnot have any lunch. so Warrington cook the chop, and pop they hiss hot upon the plate.
Pen fall to at his chop with a good appetite, after look up at his uncle, and see that gentleman be still in good-humour.
"you see, sir," Warrington say, "Mrs. Flanagan benot here to do them, and we cannot employ the boy, for the little beggar be all day occupy clean Pen's boot. and now for another swig at the beer. Pen drink tea; itbe only fit for old woman."
"and so you be at Lady Whiston's last night," the Major say, not in truth know what observation to make to this rough diamond.
"I at Lady Whiston's! not such a flat, sir. I donot care for female society. in fact it bore I. I spend my evening philosophically at the Back Kitchen."
"the Back Kitchen? indeed!" say the Major.
"I see you donot know what it mean," Warrington say. "ask Pen. he be there after Lady Whiston's. tell Major Pendennis about the Back Kitchen, Pen--don't be ashamed of yourself."
so Pen say it be a little eccentric society of man of letter and man about town, to which he have be present; and the Major begin to think that the young fellow have see a good deal of the world since his arrival in London.
CHAPTER XXX
the Knights of the Temple
Colleges, school, and inn of court still have some respect for antiquity, and maintain a great number of the custom and institution of our ancestor, with which those person who do not particularly regard their forefather, or perhaps be not very well acquainted with they; have long since do away. a well-ordain workhouse or prison si much well provide with the appliance of health, comfort, and cleanliness, than a respectable Foundation School a venerable College, or a learn Inn. in the latter place of residence man be content to sleep in dingy closet, and to pay for the sitting-room and the cupboard which be their dormitory, the price of a good villa and garden in the suburb, or of a roomy house in the neglect square of the town. the poor mechanic in Spitalfields have a cistern and an unbounded suppy of water at his command; but the gentleman of the inn of court, and the gentleman of the university, have their supply of this cosmetic fetched in jug by laundress and bedmaker, and live in abode which be erect long before the custom of cleanliness and decency obtain among we. there be individual still alive who sneer at the people and speak of they with epithet of scorn. gentleman, there can be but little doubt that your ancestor be the great Unwashed: and in the Temple especially, it be pretty certain, that only under the great difficulty and restriction the virtue which have be pronounce to be next to godliness could have be practise at all.
Old Grump, of the Norfolk Circuit, who have live for more than thirty year in the chamber under those occupy by Warrington and Pendennis, and who use to be awaken by the roaring of the shower-bath which those gentleman have erect in their apartment--a part of the content of which occasionally trickle through the roof into Mr. Grump's room,-- declare that the practice be an absurd, newfangle, dandify folly, and daily curse the laundress who slop the staircase by which he have to pass. Grump, now much more than half a century old, have indeed never use the luxury in question. he have do without water very well, and so have our father before he. of all those knight and baronet, lord and gentleman, bear arm, whose escutcheon be paint upon the wall of the famous hall of the Upper Temple, be there no philanthropist good-natured enough to devise a set of Hummums for the benefit of the lawyer, his fellow and successor? the Temple historian make no mention of such a scheme. there be Pump Court and Fountain Court, with their hydraulic apparatus, but one never hear of a bencher disporting in the fountain; and cannot but think how many a counsel learn in the law of old day might have benefit by the pump.
nevertheless, those venerable Inns which have the Lamb and Flag and the Winged Horse for their ensign, have attraction for person who inhabit they, and a share of rough comfort and freedom which man always remember with pleasure. I donot know whether the student of law permit himself the refreshment of enthusiasm, or indulge in poetical reminiscence as he pass by historical chamber, and say, "Yonder Eldon live--upon this site Coke muse upon Littleton--here Chitty toil--here Barnewall and Alderson join in their famous labour--here Byles compose his great work upon bill, and Smith compile his immortal lead case--here Gustavus still toil, with Solomon to aid he:" but the man of letter cannot but love the place which have be inhabit by so many of his brother, or people by their creation as real to we at this day as the author whose child they be--and Sir Roger de Coverley walk in the Temple Garden, and discourse with Mr. Spectator about the beauty in hoop and patch who be saunter over the grass, be just as lively a figure to I as old Samuel Johnson roll through the fog with the Scotch gentleman at his heel on their way to Dr. Goldsmith's chamber in Brick Court; or Harry Fielding, with ink ruffle and a wet towel round his head, dash off article at midnight for the Covent Garden Journal, while the printer's boy be asleep in the passage.
if we could but get the history of a single day as it pass in any one of those four-story house in the dingy court where our friend Pen and Warrington dwelt, some Temple Asmodeus might furnish we with a queer volume. there may be a great parliamentary counsel on the ground floor, who drive off to Belgravia at dinner-time, when his clerk, too, become a gentleman, and go away to entertain his friend, and to take his pleasure. but a short time since he be hungry and briefless in some garret of the Inn; live by stealthy literature; hop, and wait, and sicken, and no client come; exhaust his own mean and his friend' kindness; have to remonstrate humbly with dun, and to implore the patience of poor creditor. Ruin seem to be star he in the face, when, behold, a turn of the wheel of fortune, and the lucky wretch in possession of one of those prodigious prize which be sometimes draw in the great lottery of the Bar. many a well lawyer than himself do not make a fifth part of the income of his clerk, who, a few month since, could scarcely get credit for black for his master's unpaid boot. on the first floor, perhaps, you will have a venerable man whose name be famous, who have live for half a century in the Inn, whose brain be full of book, and whose shelf be store with classical and legal lore. he have live alone all these fifty year, alone and for himself, amass learn, and compile a fortune. he come home now at night alone from the club, where he have be din freely, to the lonely chamber where he live a godless old recluse. when he die, his Inn will erect a tablet to his honour, and his heir burn a part of his library. would you like to have such a prospect for your old age, to store up learning and money, and end so? but we must not linger too long by Mr. Doomsday's door. Worthy Mr. Grump live over he, who be also an ancient inhabitant of the Inn, and who, when Doomsday come home to read Catullus, be sit down with three steady senior of his standing, to a steady rubber at whist, after a dinner at which they have consume their three steady bottle of Port. you may see the old boy asleep at the Temple Church of a Sunday. attorney seldom trouble they, and they have small fortune of their own. on the other side of the third landing, where Pen and Warrington live, till long after midnight, sit Mr. Paley, who take the high honour, and who be a fellow of his college, who will sit and read and note case until two o'clock in the morning; who will rise at seven and be at the pleader's chamber as soon as they be open, where he will work until an hour before dinner-time; who will come home from Hall and read and note case again until dawn next day, when perhaps Mr. Arthur Pendennis and his friend Mr. Warrington be return from some of their wild expedition. how differently employ Mr. Paley have be! he have not be throw himself away: he have only be bring a great intellect laboriously down to the comprehension of a mean subject, and in his fierce grasp of that, resolutely exclude from his mind all high thought, all well thing, all the wisdom of philosopher and historian, all the thought of poet; all wit, fancy, reflection, art, love, truth altogether--so that he may master that enormous legend of the law, which he propose to gain his livelihood by expound. Warrington and Paley have be competitor for university honour in former day, and have run each other hard; and everybody say now that the former be waste his time and energy, whilst all people praise Paley for his industry. there may be doubt, however, as to which be use his time well. the one could afford time to think, and the other never could. the one could have sympathy and do kindness; and the other must need be always selfish. he could not cultivate a friendship or do a charity, or admire a work of genius, or kindle at the sight of beauty or the sound of a sweet song--he have no time, and no eye for anything but his law-book. all be dark outside his reading-lamp. love, and Nature, and art (which be the expression of our praise and sense of the beautiful world of God) be shut out from he. and as he turn off his lonely lamp at night, he never think but that he have spend the day profitably, and go to sleep alike thankless and remorseless. but he shudder when he meet his old companion Warrington on the stair, and shun he as one that be doom to perdition.
it may have be the sight of that cadaverous ambition and self-complacent meanness, which show itself in Paley's yellow face, and twinkle in his narrow eye, or it may have be a natural appetite for pleasure and joviality, of which it must be confess Mr. Pen be exceedingly fond, which deter that luckless youth from pursue his design upon the Bench or the Woolsack with the ardour, or rather steadiness, which be requisite in gentleman who would climb to those seat of honour. he enjoy the Temple life with a great deal of relish: his worthy relative think he be read as become a regular student; and his uncle write home congratulatory letter to the kind widow at Fairoaks, announce that the lad have sow his wild oat, and be become quite steady. the truth be, that it be a new sort of excitement to Pen, the life in which he be now engage, and have give up some of the dandified pretension, and fine-gentleman air which he have contract among his aristocratic college acquaintance, of whom he now see but little, the rough pleasure and amusement of a London bachelor be very novel and agreeable to he, and he enjoy they all. time be he would have envy the dandy their fine horse in Rotten row, but he be content now to walk in the Park and look at they. he be too young to succeed in London society without a well name and a large fortune than he have, and too lazy to get on without these adjunct. Old Pendennis fondly think he be busy with law because he neglect the social advantage present to he, and, have be at half a dozen ball and evening party, retreat before their dulness and sameness; and whenever anybody make inquiry of the worthy Major about his nephew the old gentleman say the young rascal be reform, and could not be get away from his book. but the Major would have be almost as much horrify as Mr. Paley be, have he know what be Mr. Pen's real course of life, and how much pleasure enter into his law study.
a long morning's reading, a walk in the park, a pull on the river, a stretch up the hill to Hampstead, and a modest tavern dinner; a bachelor night pass here or there, in joviality, not vice (for Arthur Pendennis admire woman so heartily that he never could bear the society of any of they that be not, in his fancy at least, good and pure); a quiet evening at home, alone with a friend and a pipe or two, and a humble potation of british spirit, whereof Mrs. Flanagan, the laundress, invariably test the quality;--these be our young gentleman's pursuit, and it must be own that his life be not unpleasant. in term-time, Mr. Pen show a most praiseworthy regularity in perform one part of the law-student's course of duty, and eat his dinner in Hall. indeed, that Hall of the Upper Temple be a sight not uninteresting, and with the exception of some trifling improvement and anachronism which have be introduce into the practice there, a man may sit down and fancy that he join in a meal of the seventeenth century. the bar have their mess, the student their table apart; the bencher sit at the high table on the raise platform surround by picture of judge of the law and portrait of royal personage who have honour its festivity with their presence and patronage. Pen look about, on his first introduction, not a little amuse with the scene which he witness. among his comrade of the student class there be gentleman of all age, from sixty to seventeen; stout grey-head attorney who be proceed to take the superior dignity,--dandies and man--about town who wish for some reason to be barrister of seven year' standing,--swarthy, black-eyed native of the Colonies, who come to be call here before they practise in their own islands,--and many gentleman of the irish nation, who make a sojourn in Middle Temple Lane before they return to the green country of their birth. there be little squad of read student who talk law all dinner-time; there be row man, whose discourse be of scull match, the Red House, Vauxhall and the Opera; there be other great in politic, and orator of the student' debating club; with all of which set, except the first, whose talk be an almost unknown and a quite unintereste language to he, Mr. Pen make a gradual acquaintance, and have many point of sympathy.
the ancient and liberal Inn of the Upper Temple provide in its Hall, and for a most moderate price, an excellent wholesome dinner of soup, meat, tart, and port wine or sherry, for the barrister and student who attend that place of refection. the party be arrange in mess of four, each of which quartet have its piece of beef or leg of mutton, its sufficient apple-pie and its bottle of wine. but the honest habitue of the hall, amongst the low rank of student, who have a taste for good living, have many harmless art by which they improve their banquet, and innocent 'dodge' (if we may be permit to use an excellent phrase that have become vernacular since the appearance of the last dictionary) by which they strive to attain for themselves more delicate food than the common every-day roast meat of the student' table.
"wait a bit," say Mr. Lowton, one of these Temple gourmand. "wait a bit," say Mr. Lowton, tug at Pen's gown--"the side-table be very full, and therebe only three bencher to eat ten dish--if we wait, perhaps we shall get something from their table." and Pen look with some amusement, as do Mr. Lowton with eye of fond desire, towards the bencher' high table, where three old gentleman be stand up before a dozen silver dish-cover, while the clerk be quaver out a grace.
Lowton be great in the conduct of the dinner. his aim be to manage so as to be the first, a captain of the mess, and to secure for himself the thirteenth glass of the bottle of port wine. thus he would have the command of the joint on which he operate his favourite cut, and make rapid dexterous appropriation of gravy, which amuse Pen infinitely. poor Jack Lowton! thy pleasure in life be very harmless; an eager epicure, thy desire do not go beyond eighteen penny.
Pen be somewhat old than many of his fellow-student, and there be that about his style and appearance, which, as we have say, be rather haughty and impertinent, that stamp he as a man of ton--very unlike those pale student who be talk law to one another, and those ferocious dandy, in row shirt and astonishing pin and waistcoat, who represent the idle part of the little community. the humble and good-natured Lowton have feel attract by Pen's superior look and presence--and have make acquaintance with he at the mess by open the conversation.
"this be boil-beef day, I believe, sir," say Lowton to Pen.
"upon my word, sir, Ibe not aware," say Pen, hardly able to contain his laughter, but add, "Ibe a stranger; this be my first term;" on which Lowton begin to point out to he the notability in the Hall.
"thatbe Boosey the bencher, the bald one sit under the picture and aving soup; I wonder whether itbe turtle? they often ave turtle. Next be Balls, the King's Counsel, and Swettenham--Hodge and Swettenham, you know. thatbe old Grump, the senior of the bar; they say hebe din here forty year. they often send them down their fish from the bencher to the senior table. do you see those four fellow seat opposite we? those be regular swell--tip-top fellow, I can tell you--Mr. Trail, the Bishop of Ealing's son, Honourable Fred. Ringwood, Lord Cinqbar's brother, you know. hewill have a good place, I bet any money; and Bob Suckling, whobe always with he--a high fellow too. ha! ha!" here Lowton burst into a laugh,
"what be it?" say Pen, still amuse.
"I say, I like to mess with those chap," Lowton say, wink his eye knowingly, and pour out his glass of wine.
"and why?" ask Pen.
"why! they donot come down here to dine, you know, they only make believe to dine. they dine here, Law bless you! they go to some of the swell club, or else to some grand dinner-party. you see their name in the Morning Post at all the fine party in London. why, I bet anything that Ringwood have his cab, or Trail his Brougham (hebe a devil of a fellow, and make the bishop's money spin, I can tell you) at the corner of Essex Street at this minute. they dine! they willnot dine these two hour, I dare say."
"but why should you like to mess with they, if they donot eat any dinner?" Pen ask, still puzzle. "therebe plenty, benot there?"
"how green you be," say Lowton. "excuse I, but you be green. they donot drink any wine, donot you see, and a fellow get the bottle to himself if he like it when he mess with those three chap. thatbe why Corkoran get in with them."
"ah, Mr. Lowton, I see you be a sly fellow," Pen say, delight with his acquaintance: on which the other modestly reply, that he have live in London the well part of his life, and of course have his eye about he; and go on with his catalogue to Pen.
"therebe a lot of Irish here," he say; "that Corkoran's one, and I cannot say I like he. you see that handsome chap with the blue neck-cloth, and pink shirt, and yellow waistcoat, thatbe another; thatbe Molloy Maloney of Ballymaloney, and nephew to Major-General Sir Hector O'Dowd, he, he," Lowton say, try to imitate the Hibernian accent. "hebe always brag about his uncle; and come into Hall in silver-strip trouser the day he have be present. that other near he, with the long black hair, be a tremendous rebel. by Jove, sir, to hear he at the Forum it make your blood freeze; and the next be an Irishman, too, Jack Finucane, reporter of a newspaper. they all stick together, those irish. itbe your turn to fill your glass. what? you willnot have any port? donot like port with your dinner? herebe your health." and this worthy man find himself not the less attached to Pendennis because the latter dislike port wine at dinner.
it be while Pen be take his share of one of these dinner with his acquaintance Lowton as the captain of his mess, that there come to join they a gentleman in a barrister's gown, who could not find a seat, as it appear, amongst the person of his own degree, and who stride over the table and take his place on the bench where Pen sate. he be dress in old clothe and a fade gown, which hang behind he, and he wear a shirt which, though clean, be extremely ragged, and very different to the magnificent pink raiment of Mr. Molloy Maloney, who occupy a commanding position in the next mess. in order to notify their appearance at dinner, it be the custom of the gentleman who eat in the Upper Temple Hall to write down their name upon slip of paper, which be provide for that purpose, with a pencil for each mess. Lowton write his name first, then come Arthur Pendennis, and the next be that of the gentleman in the old clothe. he smile when he see Pen's name, and look at he. "we ought to know each other," he say. "webe both Boniface man; my name's Warrington."
"be you st---- Warrington?" Pen say, delight to see this hero.
Warrington laughed--"Stunning Warrington--yes," he say, "I recollect you in your freshman's term. but you appear to have quite cut I out."
"the college talk about you still," say Pen, who have a generous admiration for talent and pluck. "the bargeman you thrash, Bill Simes, donot you remember, want you up again at Oxbridge. the Miss Notleys, the haberdashers----"
"hush!" say Warrington--"glad to make your acquaintance, Pendennis. hear a good deal about you."
the young man be friend immediately, and at once deep in college-talk. and Pen, who have be act rather the fine gentleman on a previous day, when he pretend to Lowton that he could not drink port wine at dinner, see Warrington take his share with a great deal of gusto, do not scruple about help himself any more, rather to the disappointment of honest Lowton. when the dinner be over, Warrington ask Arthur where he be go.
"I think of go home to dress, and hear Grisi in Norma," Pen say.
"be you go to meet anybody there?" he ask.
Pen say, "no--only to hear the music," of which he be fond.
"you have much well come home and smoke a pipe with I," say Warrington,--"a very short one. come, I live close by in Lamb Court, and wewill talk over Boniface and old time."
they go away; Lowton sigh after they. he know Warrington be a baronet's son, and he look up with simple reverence to all the aristocracy. Pen and Warrington become swear friend from that night. Warrington's cheerfulness and jovial temper, his good sense, his rough welcome, and his never-fail pipe of tobacco, charm Pen, who find it more pleasant to dive into shilling tavern with he, than to dine in solitary state amongst the silent and polite frequenter of the Polyanthus.
Ere long Pen give up the lodging in St. James's, to which he have migrate on quit his hotel, and find it be much more economical to take up his abode with Warrington in Lamb Court, and furnish and occupy his friend's vacant room there. for it must be say of Pen, that no man be more easily lead than he to do a thing, when it be a novelty, or when he have a mind to it. and Pidgeon, the youth, and Flanagan, the laundress, divide their allegiance now between Warrington and Pen.
CHAPTER XXXI
Old and new Acquaintances
elate with the idea of see life, Pen go into a hundred queer London haunt. he like to think he be consort with all sort of man--so he behold coalheaver in their tap-room; boxer in their inn-parlour; honest citizen disport in the suburb or on the river; and he would have like to hob and nob with celebrate pickpocket, or drink a pot of ale with a company of burglar and cracksman, have chance afford he an opportunity of make the acquaintance of this class of society. it be good to see the gravity with which Warrington listen to the Tutbury Pet or the Brighton Stunner at the Champion's arm, and behold the interest which he take in the coalheaving company assemble at the Fox-under-the-Hill. his acquaintance with the public-house of the metropolis and its neighbourhood, and with the frequenter of their various parlour, be prodigious. he be the personal friend of the landlord and landlady, and welcome to the bar as to the clubroom. he like their society, he say, well than that of his own class, whose manner annoy he, and whose conversation bore he. "in society," he use to say, "everybody be the same, wear the same dress, eat and drink, and say the same thing; one young dandy at the club talk and look just like another, one Miss at a ball exactly resemble another, whereas therebe character here. I like to talk with the strong man in England, or the man who can drink the most beer in England, or with that tremendous republican of a hatter, who think Thistlewood be the great character in history. I like well gin-and-water than claret. I like a sanded floor in Carnaby Market well than a chalk one in Mayfair. I prefer snob, I own it." indeed, this gentleman be a social republican; and it never enter his head while converse with Jack and Tom that he be in any respect their well; although, perhaps, the deference which they pay he might secretly please he.
Pen follow he then to these various resort of man with great glee and assiduity. but he be considerably young, and therefore much more pompous and stately than Warrington, in fact a young prince in disguise, visit the poor of his father's kingdom. they respect he as a high chap, a fine fellow, a regular young swell. he have somehow about he an air of imperious good-humour, and a royal frankness and majesty, although he be only heir-apparent to twopence-halfpenny, and but one in descent from a gallypot. if these position be make for we, we acquiesce in they very easily; and be always pretty ready to assume a superiority over those who be as good as ourselves. Pen's condescension at this time of his life be a fine thing to witness. amongst man of ability this assumption and impertinence pass off with extreme youth: but it be curious to watch the conceit of a generous and clever lad--there be something almost touch in that early exhibition of simplicity and folly.,
so, after read pretty hard of a morning, and, I fear, not law merely, but politic and general history and literature, which be as necessary for the advancement and instruction of a young man as mere dry law, after apply with tolerable assiduity to letter, to review, to elemental book of law, and, above all, to the newspaper, until the hour of dinner be draw nigh, these young gentleman would sally out upon the town with great spirit and appetite, and bent upon enjoy a merry night as they have pass a pleasant forenoon. it be a jovial time, that of four-and-twenty, when every muscle of mind and body be in healthy action, when the world be new as yet, and one move over it spur onwards by good spirit and the delightful capability to enjoy. if ever we feel young afterwards, it be with the comrade of that time: the tune we hum in our old age, be those we learn then. sometimes, perhaps, the festivity of that period revive in our memory; but how dingy the pleasure-garden have grow, how tattere the garland look, how scant and old the company, and what a number of the light have go out since that day! Grey hair have come on like daylight stream in--daylight and a headache with it. pleasure have go to bed with the rouge on her cheek. well, friend, let we walk through the day, sober and sad, but friendly.
I wonder what Laura and Helen would have say, could they have see, as they might not unfrequently have do have they be up and in London, in the very early morning when the bridge begin to blush in the sunrise, and the tranquil street of the city to shine in the dawn, Mr. Pen and Mr. Warrington rattle over the echo flag towards the Temple, after one of their wild night of carouse--night wild, but not so wicked as such night sometimes be, for Warrington be a woman-hater; and Pen, as we have say, too lofty to stoop to a vulgar intrigue. our young Prince of Fairoaks never could speak to one of the sex but with respectful courtesy, and shrink from a coarse word or gesture with instinctive delicacy--for though we have see he fall in love with a fool, as his better and inferior have do, and as it be probable that he do more than once in his life, yet for the time of the delusion it be always as a Goddess that he consider she, and choose to wait upon she. man serve woman kneeling--when they get on their foot, they go away.
that be what an acquaintance of Pen's say to he in his hard homely way;--an old friend with whom he have fall in again in London--no other than honest Mr. Bows of the Chatteris Theatre, who be now employ as pianoforte player, to accompany the eminent lyrical talent which nightly delight the public at the Fielding's Head in Covent Garden: and where be hold the little club call the Back Kitchen.
number of Pen's friend frequent this very merry meeting. the Fielding's Head have be a house of entertainment, almost since the time when the famous author of 'Tom Jones' preside as magistrate in the neighbouring Bow Street; his place be point out, and the chair say to have be his, still occupy by the president of the night's entertainment. the worthy Cutts, the landlord of the Fielding's head, generally occupy this post when not disable by gout or other illness. his jolly appearance and fine voice may be remember by some of my male reader: he use to sing profusely in the course of the harmonic meeting, and his song be of what may be call the British Brandy-and-Water School of Song--such as 'the Good Old English Gentleman,' 'dear Tom, this Brown Jug,' and so forth--song in which pathos and hospitality be blend, and the praise of good liquor and the social affection be chant in a baritone voice. the charm of our woman, the heroic deed of our naval and military commander, be often sing in the ballad of this school; and many a time in my youth have I admire how Cutts the singer, after he have work we all up to patriotic enthusiasm, by describe the way in which the brave Abercrombie receive his death-wound, or make we join he in tear, which he shed liberally himself, as in falter accent he tell how autumn's fall leaf "proclaim the old man he must die"--how Cutts the singer become at once Cutts the landlord, and, before the applause which we be make with our fist on his table, in compliment to his heart-stir melody, have die away,--wa call, "now, gentleman, give your order, the waiter's in the room--John, a champagne cup for Mr. Green. I think, sir, you say sausage and mashed potato? John, attend on the gentleman."
"and Iwill thank ye give I a glass of punch too, John, and take care the wather boil," a voice would cry not unfrequently, a well-know voice to Pen, which make the lad blush and start when he hear it first--that of the venerable Captain Costigan; who be now establish in London, and one of the great pillar of the harmonic meeting at the Fielding's Head.
the Captain's manner and conversation bring very many young man to the place. he be a character, and his fame have begin to spread soon after his arrival in the metropolis, and especially after his daughter's marriage. he be great in his conversation to the friend for the time be (who be the neighbour drink by his side), about "I daughther." he tell of her marriage, and of the event previous and subsequent to that ceremony; of the carriage she keep; of Mirabel's adoration for she and for he; of the hundther pound which he be at perfect liberty to draw from his son-in-law, whenever necessity urge he. and have state that it be his firm intention to "dthraw next Sathurday, I give ye I secre word and honour next Sathurday, the fourteenth, when ye'll see the money will be hand over to I at Coutts's, the very instant I present the cheque," the Captain would not unfrequently propose to borrow a half-crown of his friend until the arrival of that day of Greek Calends, when, on the honour of an officer and gentleman, he would repee the thrifle obligetion.
Sir Charles Mirabel have not that enthusiastic attachment to his father-in-law, of which the latter sometimes boast (although in other stage of emotion cos would inveigh, with tear in his eye, against the ingratitude of the child of his bosom, and the stinginess of the wealthy old man who have marry she); but the pair have act not unkindly towards Costigan; have settle a small pension on he, which be pay regularly, and forestall with even more regularity by poor cos; and the period of the payment be always well know by his friend at the Fielding's Head, whither the honest Captain take care to repair, bank-note in hand, call loudly for change in the midst of the full harmonic meeting. "I think ye'll find that note willnot be refuse at the Bank of England, Cutts, my boy," Captain Costigan would say. "bow, have a glass? ye neednot stint yourself to-night, anyhow; and a glass of punch will make ye play con spirito." for he be lavishly free with his money when it come to he, and be scarcely know to button his breech pocket, except when the coin be go, or sometimes, indeed, when a creditor come by.
it be in one of these moment of exultation that Pen find his old friend swagger at the singer' table at the Back Kitchen of the Fielding's Head, and order glass of brandy-and-water for any of his acquaintance who make their appearance in the apartment. Warrington, who be on confidential term with the bass singer, make his way up to this quarter of the room, and Pen walk at his friend's heel.
Pen start and blush to see Costigan. he have just come from Lady Whiston's party, where he have meet and speak with the Captain's daughter again for the first time after very old old day. he come up with outstretched hand, very kindly and warmly to greet the old man; still retain a strong remembrance of the time when Costigan's daughter have be everything in the world to he. for though this young gentleman may have be somewhat capricious in his attachment, and occasionally have transfer his affection from one woman to another, yet he always respect the place where Love have dwelt, and, like the Sultan of Turkey, desire that honour should be pay to the lady towards whom he have once throw the royal pocket-handkerchief. the tipsy Captain return the clasp of Pen's hand with all the strength of a palm which have become very shaky by the constant lifting up of weight of brandy-and-water, look hard in Pen's face, and say, "Grecious Heavens, be it possible? I dear boy, I dear fellow, I dear friend;" and then with a look of muddle curiosity, fairly break down with, "I know your face, I dear dear friend, but, bedad, Ihave forget your name." five year of constant punch have pass since Pen and Costigan meet. Arthur be a good deal change, and the Captain may surly be excuse for forget he; when a man at the actual moment see thing double, we may expect that his view of the past will be rather muzzy.
Pen see his condition and laugh, although, perhaps, he be somewhat mortified. "donot you remember I, Captain?" he say. "I be Pendennis-- Arthur Pendennis, of Chatteris."
the sound of the young man's friendly voice recall and steadie Cos's tipsy remembrance, and he salute Arthur, as soon as he know he, with a loud volley of friendly greeting. Pen be his dear boy, his gallant young friend, his noble collagian, whom he have hold in his inmost heart ever since they have part--how be his fawther, no, his mother, and his guardian, the General, the Major? "I preshoom, from your apparance, youhave come into your prawpertee; and, bedad, yee'll spend it like a man of spirit--i'll go bail for that. no? not yet come into your estete? if ye want any thrifle, heark ye, therebe poor old Jack Costigan have get a guinea or two in his pocket--and, be heaven! you shall never want, Awthur, I dear boy. whatwill ye have? John, come hither, and look aloive; give this gentleman a glass of punch, and Iwill pay for't.--Your friend? Ihave see he before. permit I to have the honour of make meself know to ye, sir, and request ye'll take a glass of punch."
"I donot envy Sir Charles Mirabel his father-in-law," think Pendennis. "and how be my old friend, Mr. Bows, Captain? have you any news of he, and do you see he still?"
"no doubt hebe very well," say the Captain, jingle his money, and whistle the air of a song--'the little doodeen'--for the singing of which he be celebrate at the Fielding's Head. "I dear boy--I've forget your name again--but my name's Costigan, Jack Costigan, and Iwould loike ye to take as many tumbler of punch in my name as ever ye loike. ye know my name; Ibe not ashamed of it." and so the captain go maunder on.
"itbe pay-day with the General," say Mr. Hodgen, the bass singer, with whom Warrington be in deep conversation: "and hebe a precious deal more than half sea over. he have already try that 'Little Doodeen' of his, and break it, too, just before I sing 'King Death.' have you hear my new song, 'the Body Snatcher,' Mr. Warrington?--angcored at Saint Bartholomew's the other night--compose expressly for I. Per'aps you or your friend would like a copy of the song, sir? John, just 'ave the kyndness to 'and over a 'Body Snatcher' 'ere, will yer?--therebe a portrait of I, sir, as I sing it--as the Snatcher--consider rather like."
"thank you," say Warrington; "hear it nine time--know it by heart, Hodgen."
here the gentleman who preside at the pianoforte begin to play upon his instrument, and Pen, look in the direction of the music, behold that very Mr. Bows, for whom he have be ask but now, and whose existence Costigan have momentarily forget. the little old man sate before the battered piano (which have injure its constitution wofully by sit up so many night, and speak with a voice, as it be, at once hoarse and faint), and accompany the singer, or play with taste and grace in the interval of the song.
bow have see and recollect Pen at once when the latter come into the room, and have remark the eager warmth of the young man's recognition of Costigan. he now begin to play an air, which Pen instantly remember as one which use to be sing by the chorus of villager in 'the Stranger,' just before Mrs. Haller come in. it shake Pen as he hear it. he remember how his heart use to beat as that air be play, and before the divine Emily make her entry. nobody, save Arthur, too any notice of old Bows's playing: it be scarcely hear amidst the clatter of knife and fork, the call for poached egg and kidney, and the tramp of guest and waiter.
Pen go up and kindly shake the player by the hand at the end of his performance; and Bows greet Arthur with great respect and cordiality. "what, you havenot forget the old tune, Mr. Pendennis?" he say; "I think youwould remember it. I take it, it be the first tune of that sort you ever hear play--wasn't it, sir? you be quite a young chap then. I fear the Captain's very bad to-night. he break out on a pay-day; and I shall have the deuce's own trouble in get home. we live together. we still hang on, sir, in partnership, though Miss Em--though my lady Mirabel have leave the firm.--And so you remember old time, do you? benot she a beauty, sir?--your health and my service to you,"--and he take a sip at the pewter measure of porter which stand by his side as he play.
Pen have many opportunity of see his early acquaintance afterwards, and of renew his relation with Costigan and the old musician.
as they sate thus in friendly colloquy, man of all sort and condition enter and quit the house of entertainment; and Pen have the pleasure of see as many different person of his race, as the most eager observer need desire to inspect. healthy country tradesman and farmer, in London for their business, come and recreate themselves with the jolly singing and supper of the Back kitchen,--squad of young apprentice and assistant, the shutter be close over the scene of their labour, come hither for fresh air doubtless,--rakish young medical student, gallant, dashing, what be call "loudly" dress, and (must it be own?) somewhat dirty,--were here smoking and drinking, and vociferously applaud the song; young university buck be to be find here, too, with that indescribable genteel simper which be only learn at the knee of Alma Mater;--and handsome young guardsman, and florid buck from the St. James's Street Clubs--nay, senator English and irish; and even member of the House of Peers.
the bass singer have make an immense hit with his song of 'the Body Snatcher,' and the town rush to listen to it. the curtain draw aside, and Mr. Hodgen appear in the character of the Snatcher, sit on a coffin, with a flask of gin before he, with a spade, and a candle stick in a skull. the song be sing with a really admirable terrific humour. the singer's voice go down so low, that its grumble rumble into the hearer's awe-stricken soul; and in the chorus he clamp with his spade, and give a demoniac "ha! ha!" which cause the very glass to quiver on the table, as with terror. none of the other singer, not even Cutts himself, as that high-minded man own, could stand up before the Snatcher, and he commonly use to retire to Mrs. Cutts's private apartment, or into the bar, before that fatal song extinguish he. poor cos's ditty, 'the little Doodeen,' which bow accompany charmingly on the piano, be sing but to a few admirer, who might choose to remain after the tremendous resurrectionist chant. the room be commonly empty after that, or only leave in possession of a very few and persevere votary of pleasure.
Whilst Pen and his friend be sit here together one night, or rather morning, two habitue of the house enter almost together. "Mr. Hoolan and Mr. Doolan," whisper Warrington to Pen, salute these gentleman, and in the latter Pen recognise his friend of the Alacrity coach, who could not dine with Pen on the day on which the latter have invite he, be compel by his professional duty to decline dinner-engagement on Fridays, he have state, with his compliment to Mr. Pendennis.
Doolan's paper, the Dawn, be lie on the table much bestaine by porter, and cheek-by-jowl with Hoolan's paper, which we shall call the day; the Dawn be liberal--the day be ultra-conservative. many of our journal be officer by irish gentleman, and their gallant brigade do the penning among we, as their ancestor use to transact the fighting in Europe; and engage under many a flag, to be good friend when the battle be over.
"Kidneys, John, and a glass of stout," say Hoolan. "how be you, Morgan? howbe Mrs. Doolan?"
"do pretty well, thank ye, Mick, my boy--faith shebe accustomed to it," say Doolan. "howbe the lady that own ye? maybe Iwill step down Sunday, and have a glass of punch, Kilburn way."
"donot bring Patsey with you, Mick, for our Georgy's get the measle," say the friendly Morgan, and they straightway fall to talk about matter connect with their trade--about the foreign mail--about who be correspondent at Paris, and who write from Madrid--about the expense the Morning Journal be at in send courier, about the circulation of the Evening Star, and so forth.
Warrington, laughing, take the Dawn which be lie before he, and point to one of the lead article in that journal, which commence thus--
"as rogue of note in former day who have some wicked work to perform,-- an enemy to be put out of the way, a quantity of false coin to be pass, a lie to be tell or a murder to be do--employ a professional perjurer or assassin to do the work, which they be themselves too notorious or too cowardly to execute: our notorious contemporary, the day, engage smasher out of door to utter forgery against individual, and call in auxiliary cut-throat to murder the reputation of those who offend he. a black-vizarde ruffian (whom we will unmask), who sign the forge name of Trefoil, be at present one of the chief bravo and bully in our contemporary's establishment. he be the eunuch who bring the bowstre, and strangle at the order of the day. we can convict this cowardly slave, and propose to do so. the charge which he have bring against Lord Bangbanagher, because he be a liberal irish peer, and against the Board of Poor Law Guardians of the Bangbanagher Union, be," etc.
"how do they like the article at your place, Mick?" ask Morgan; "when the Captain put his hand to it hebe a tremendous hand at a smasher. he write the article in two hour--in--whew--you know where, while the boy be wait."
"our governor think the public donot mind a straw about these newspaper row, and have tell the Docthor to stop answer," say the other. "they two talk it out together in my room. the Docthor would have like a turn, for he say itbe such easy writing, and require no reading up of a subject: but the governor put a stopper on he."
"the taste for eloquence be go out, Mick," say Morgan.
"'deed then it be, Morgan," say Mick. "that be fine writing when the Docthor write in the Phaynix, and he and Condy Roony blaze away at each other day after day."
"and with powder and shot, too, as well as paper," say Morgan, "Faith, the Docthor be out twice, and Condy Roony wing his man."
"they be talk about Doctor Boyne and Captain Shandon," Warrington say, "who be the two irish controversialist of the Dawn and the day, Dr. Boyne be the protestant champion and Captain Shandon the liberal orator. they be the good friend in the world, I believe, in spite of their newspaper controversy; and though they cry out against the English for abuse their country, by Jove they abuse it themselves more in a single article than we should take the pain to do in a dozen volume. how be you, Doolan?"
"your servant, Mr. Warrington--Mr. Pendennis, I be delighted to have the honour of see ye again. the night's journey on the top of the Alacrity be one of the most agreeable I ever enjoy in my life, and it be your liveliness and urbanity that make the trip so charming. I have often think over that happy night, sir, and talk over it to Mrs. Doolan. I have see your elegant young friend, Mr. Foker, too, here, sir, not unfrequently. he be an occasional frequenter of this hostelry, and a right good one it be. Mr. Pendennis, when I see you I be on the Tom and Jerry Weekly Paper; I have now the honour to be sub-editor of the Dawn, one of the well-write paper of the empire"--and he bow very slightly to Mr. Warrington. his speech be unctuous and measure, his courtesy oriental, his tone, when talk with the two Englishmen, quite different to that with which he speak to his comrade.
"why the devil will the fellow compliment so?" growl Warrington, with a sneer which he hardly take the pain to suppress. "Psha--who come here? --all Parnassus be abroad to-night: herebe Archer. we shall have some fun. well, Archer, House up?"
"havenot be there. I have be," say Archer, with an air of mystery, "where I be want. get I some supper, John--something substantial. I hate your grandee who give you nothing to eat. if it have be at Apsley House, it would have be quite different. the Duke know what I like, and say to the Groom of the Chambers, 'Martin, you will have some cold beef, not too much do, and a pint bottle of pale ale, and some brown sherry, ready in my study as usual;--Archer be come here this evening.' the Duke donot eat supper himself, but he like to see a man enjoy a hearty meal, and he know that I dine early. a man cannot live upon air, be hang to he."
"let I introduce you to my friend, Mr. Pendennis," Warrington say, with great gravity. "Pen, this be Mr Archer, whom you have hear I talk about. you must know Pen's uncle, the Major, Archer, you who know everybody?"
"din with he the day before yesterday at Gaunt House," Archer say. "we be four--the french Ambassador, Steyne, and we two commoner."
"why, my uncle be in Scot----" Pen be go to break out, but Warrington press his foot under the table as a signal for he to be quiet.
"it be about the same business that I have be to the palace to-night," Archer go on simply, "and where Ihave be keep four hour, in an anteroom, with nothing but yesterday's time, which I know by heart, as I write three of the lead article myself; and though the Lord Chamberlain come in four time, and once hold the royal teacup and saucer in his hand, he do not so much as say to I, 'Archer, will you have a cup of tea?'"
"indeed! what be in the wind now?" ask Warrington--and turn to Pen, add, "you know, I suppose, that when there be anything wrong at Court they always send for Archer."
"there be something wrong," say Mr. Archer, "and as the story will be all over the town in a day or two I donot mind tell it. at the last Chantilly race, where I ride Brian Boru for my old friend the Duke de Saint Cloud--the old King say to I, Archer, Ibe uneasy about Saint Cloud. I have arrange his marriage with the Princess Marie Cunegonde; the peace of Europe depend upon it--for Russia will declare war if the marriage do not take place, and the young fool be so mad about Madame Massena, Marshal Massena's wife, that he actually refuse to be a party to the marriage. well, Sir, I speak to Saint Cloud, and have get he into pretty good humour by win the race, and a good bit of money into the bargain, he say to I, 'Archer, tell the Governor Iwill think of it.'"
"how do you say Governor in French?" ask Pen, who pique himself on know that language.
"oh, we speak in English--I teach he when we be boy, and I save his life at Twickenham, when he fall out of a punt," Archer say. "I shall never forget the Queen's look as I bring he out of the water. she give I this diamond ring, and always call I Charles to this day."
"Madame Massena must be rather an old woman, Archer," Warrington say.
"dev'lish old--old enough to be his grandmother; I tell he so," Archer answer at once. "but those attachment for old woman be the deuce and all. thatbe what the King feel: thatbe what shock the poor queen so much. they go away from Paris last Tuesday night, and be live at this present moment at Jaunay's Hotel."
"have there be a private marriage, Archer?" ask Warrington.
"whether there have or not I donot know," Mr. Archer reply, "all I know be that I be keep wait for four hour at the palace; that I never see a man in such a state of agitation as the King of Belgium when he come out to speak to I, and that Ibe devilish hungry--and here come some supper."
"he have be pretty well to-night," say Warrington, as the pair go home together: "but I have know he in much great force, and keep a whole room in a state of wonder. put aside his archery practice, that man be both able and honest--a good man of business, an excellent friend, admirable to his family as husband, father, and son."
"what be it make he pull the long bow in that wonderful manner?"
"an amiable insanity," answer Warrington. "he never do anybody harm by his talk, or say evil of anybody. he be a stout politician too, and would never write a word or do an act against his party, as many of we do."
"of we! who be we?" ask Pen. "of what profession be Mr. Archer?"
"of the Corporation of the Goosequill--of the Press, my boy," say Warrington; "of the fourth estate."
"be you, too, of the craft, then?" Pendennis say.
"we will talk about that another time," answer the other. they be pass through the Strand as they talk, and by a newspaper office, which be all light up and bright. reporter be come out of the place, or rush up to it in cab; there be lamp burn in the editor' room, and above where the compositor be at work: the window of the building be in a blaze of gas.
"look at that, Pen," Warrington say. "there she be--the great engine-- she never sleep. she have her ambassador in every quarter of the world-- her courier upon every road. her officer march along with army, and her envoy walk into statesman's cabinet. they be ubiquitous. Yonder journal have an agent, at this minute, give bribe at Madrid; and another inspect the price of potato in Covent Garden. look! here come the Foreign Express gallop in. they will be able to give news to Downing Street to-morrow: fund will rise or fall, fortune be make or lose; Lord B. will get up, and, hold the paper in his hand, and see the noble marquis in his place, will make a great speech; and--and Mr. Doolan will be call away from his supper at the Back Kitchen; for he be foreign sub-editor, and see the mail on the newspaper sheet before he go to his own."
and so talk, the friend turn into their chamber, as the dawn be begin to peep.
CHAPTER XXXII
in which the Printer's Devil come to the Door
Pen, in the midst of his revel and enjoyment, humble as they be, and moderate in cost if not in kind, see an awful sword hang over he which must drop down before long and put an end to his frolic and feasting. his money be very nearly spend. his club subscription have carry away a third part of it. he have pay for the chief article of furniture with which he have supply his little bedroom: in fine, he be come to the last five-pound note in his pocket-book, and could think of no method of provide a successor: for our friend have be breed up like a young prince as yet, or as a child in arm whom his mother feed when it cry out. Warrington do not know what his comrade's mean be. an only child, with a mother at her country house, and an old dandy of an uncle who din with a great man every day, Pen might have a large bank at his command for anything that the other know. he have gold chain and a dressing-case fit for a lord. his habit be those of an aristocrat,-- not that he be expensive upon any particular point, for he din and laugh over the pint of porter and the plate of beef from the cook's shop with perfect content and good appetite,--but he could not adopt the penny-wise precaution of life. he could not give twopence to a waiter; he could not refrain from take a cab if he have a mind to do so, or if it rain, and as surely as he take the cab he overpay the driver. he have a scorn for clean glove and minor economy. have he be breed to ten thousand a year he could scarcely have be more free-handed; and for a beggar, with a sad story, or a couple of pretty piteous-face child, he never could resist put his hand into his pocket. it be a sumptuous nature, perhaps, that could not be bring to regard money; a natural generosity and kindness; and possibly a petty vanity that be pleased with praise, even with the praise of waiter and cabman. I doubt whether the wise of we know what our own motif be, and whether some of the action of which we be the very proud will not surprise we when we trace they, as we shall one day, to their source.
Warrington then do not know, and Pen have not think proper to confide to his friend, his pecuniary history. that Pen have be wild and wickedly extravagant at college, the other be aware; everybody at college be extravagant and wild; but how great the son's expense have be, and how small the mother's mean, be point which have not be as yet submit to Mr. Warrington's examination.
at last the story come out, while Pen be grimly survey the change for the last five-pound note, as it lie upon the tray from the public-house by Mr. Warrington's pot of ale.
"it be the last rose of summer," say Pen; "its bloom companion have go long ago; and behold the last one of the garland have shed its leaf;" and he tell Warrington the whole story which we know of his mother's mean, of his own folly, of Laura's generosity; during which time Warrington smoke his pipe and listened intent.
"impecuniosity will do you good," Pen's friend say, knock out the ash at the end of the narration; "I donot know anything more wholesome for a man--for an honest man, mind you--for another, the medicine lose its effect--than a state of tick. it be an alterative and a tonic; it keep your moral man in a perpetual state of excitement: as a man who be rid at a fence, or have his opponent's single-stick before he, be force to look his obstacle steadily in the face, and brace himself to repulse or overcome it; a little necessity bring out your pluck if you have any, and nerve you to grapple with fortune. you will discover what a number of thing you can do without when you have no money to buy they. you willnot want new glove and varnish boot, eau de Cologne and cab to ride in. you have be breed up as a molly-coddle, Pen, and spoilt by the woman. a single man who have health and brain, and cannot find a livelihood in the world, donot deserve to stay there. let he pay his last halfpenny and jump over Waterloo Bridge. let he steal a leg of mutton and be transport and get out of the country--he be not fit to live in it. dixi; I have speak. give we another pull at the pale ale.
"you have certainly speak; but how be one to live?" say Pen. "there be beef and bread in plenty in England, but you must pay for it with work or money. and who will take my work? and what work can I do?"
Warrington burst out laugh. "suppose we advertise in the Times," he say, "for an usher's place at a classical and commercial academy--a gentleman, B.A. of St. Boniface College, and who be pluck for his degree--"
"confound you," cry Pen.
"--wishe to give lesson in classic and mathematic, and the rudiment of the french language; he can cut hair, attend to the young pupil, and play a second on the piano with the daughter of the principal. Address A. P., Lamb Court, Temple."
"go on," say Pen, growling.
"man take to all sort of profession. why, there be your friend Bloundell-Bloundell be a professional blackleg, and travel the Continent, where he pick up young gentleman of fashion and fleece they. there be Bob O'Toole, with whom I be at school, who drive the Ballynafad mail now, and carry honest Jack Finucane's own correspondence to that city. I know a man, sir, a, doctor's son, like-- well, donot be angry, I mean nothing offensive--a doctor's son, I say, who be walk the hospital here, and quarrel with his governor on question of finance, and what do he do when he come to his last five-pound note? he let his mustachio grow, go into a provincial town, where he announce himself as Professor Spineto, chiropodist to the Emperor of all the Russians, and by a happy operation on the editor of the country newspaper, establish himself in practice, and live reputably for three year. he have be reconcile to his family, and have succeed to his father's gallypot."
"hang gallypot," cry Pen. "I cannot drive a coach, cut corn, or cheat at card. therebe nothing else you propose."
"yes; therebe our own correspondent," Warrington say. "every man have his secret, look you. before you tell I the story of your money-matter, I have no idea but that you be a gentleman of fortune, for, with your confounded air and appearance, anybody would suppose you to be so. from what you tell I about your mother's income, it be clear that you must not lay any more hand on it. you cannot go on spunge upon the woman. you must pay off that trump of a girl. Laura be her name?--here be your health, Laura!--and carry a hod rather than ask for a shilling from home."
"but how earn one?" ask Pen.
"how do I live, think you?" say the other. "on my young brother's allowance, Pendennis? I have secret of my own, my boy;" and here Warrington's countenance fall. "I make away with that allowance five year ago: if I have make away with myself a little time before, it would have be well. I have play off my own bat, ever since. I donot want much money. when my purse be out, I go to work and fill it, and then lie idle like a serpent or an Indian, until I have digest the mass. look, I begin to feel empty," Warrington say, and show Pen a long lean purse, with but a few sovereign at one end of it.
"but how do you fill it?" say Pen.
"I write," say Warrington. "I donot tell the world that I do so," he add, with a blush. "I do not choose that question should be ask: or, perhaps, I be an ass, and donot wish it to be say that George Warrington write for bread. but I write in the Law Reviews: look here, these article be mine." and he turn over some sheet. "I write in a newspaper now and then, of which a friend of mine be editor." and Warrington, go with Pendennis to the club one day, call for a file of the Dawn, and point with his finger silently to one or two article, which Pen read with delight. he have no difficulty in recognise the style afterwards--the strong thought and curt period, the sense, the satire, and the scholarship.
"I be not up to this," say Pen, with a genuine admiration of his friend's power. "I know very little about politic or history, Warrington; and have but a smattering of letter. I cannot fly upon such a wing as your."
"but you can on your own, my boy, which be light, and soar higher, perhaps," the other say, good-naturedly. "those little scrap and verse which I have see of yours show I, what be rare in these day, a natural gift, sir. you neednot blush, you conceite young jackanape. you have think so yourself any time these ten year. you have get the sacred flame--a little of the real poetical fire, sir, I think; and all our oil-lamp be nothing compare to that, though ever so well trim. you be a poet, Pen, my boy," and so speak, Warrington stretch out his broad hand, and clap Pen on the shoulder.
Arthur be so delighted that the tear come into his eye. "how kind you be to I, Warrington!" he say.
"I like you, old boy," say the other. "I be dev'lish lonely in chamber, and want somebody, and the sight of your honest face somehow please I. I like the way you laugh at Lowton--that poor good little snob. and, in fine, the reason why I cannot tell--but so it be, young 'un. Ibe alone in the world, sir; and I want some one to keep I company;" and a glance of extreme kindness and melancholy pass out of Warrington's dark eye.
Pen be too much pleased with his own thought to perceive the sadness of the friend who be compliment he. "thank you, Warrington," he say, "thank you for your friendship to I, and--and what you say about I. I have often think I be a poet. I will be one--I think I be one, as you say so, though the world maynot. be it--be it the Ariadne in Naxos which you like (I be only eighteen when I write it), or the Prize Poem?"
Warrington burst into a roar of laughter. "why, young goose," he yell out--"of all the miserable weak rubbish I ever try, Ariadne in Naxos be the most mawkish and disgusting. the Prize Poem be so pompous and feeble, that Ibe positively surprised, sir, it donot get the medal. you donot suppose that you be a serious poet, do you, and be go to cut out Milton and Aeschylus? be you set up to be a Pindar, you absurd little tom-tit, and fancy you have the strength and pinion which the Theban eagle bear, sail with supreme dominion through the azure field of air? no, my boy, I think you can write a magazine article, and turn a pretty copy of verse; thatbe what I think of you."
"by Jove!" say Pen, bounce up and stamp his foot, "Iwill show you that I be a well man than you think for."
Warrington only laugh the more, and blow twenty-four puff rapidly out of his pipe by way of reply to Pen.
an opportunity for show his skill present itself before very long. that eminent publisher, Mr. Bacon (formerly Bacon and Bungay) of Paternoster Row, besides be the proprietor of the legal Review, in which Mr. Warrington write, and of other periodical of note and gravity, use to present to the world every year a beautiful gilt volume call the Spring Annual, edit by the Lady Violet Lebas, and number amongst its contributor not only the most eminent, but the most fashionable, poet of our time. young Lord Dodo's poem first appear in this miscellany--the honourable Percy Popjoy, whose chivalrous ballad have obtain he such a reputation--Bedwin Sands's Eastern Ghazuls, and many more of the work of our young noble, be fast give to the world in the Spring Annual, which have since share the fate of other vernal blossom, and perish out of the world. the book be daintily illustrate with picture of reign beauty, or other print of a tender and voluptuous character; and, as these plate be prepare long beforehand, require much time in engraving, it be the eminent poet who have to write to the plate, and not the painter who illustrate the poem.
one day, just when this volume be on the eve of publication, it chance that Mr. Warrington call in Paternoster Row to talk with Mr. Hack, Mr. Bacon's reader and general manager of publication--for Mr. Bacon, not have the least taste in poetry or in literature of any kind, wisely employ the service of a professional gentleman. Warrington, then, go into Mr. Hack's room on business of his own, find that gentleman with a bundle of proof plate and sheet of the Spring Annual before he, and glance at some of they.
Percy Popjoy have write some verse to illustrate one of the picture, which be call the Church Porch. a spanish damsel be hasten to church with a large prayer-book; a youth in a cloak be hide in a niche watch this young woman. the picture be pretty: but the great genius of Percy Popjoy have desert he, for he have make the most execrable verse which ever be perpetrate by a young nobleman.
Warrington burst out laugh as he read the poem: and Mr. Hack laugh too but with rather a rueful face.--"It willnot do," he say, "the public willnot stand it. Bungay's people be go to bring out a very good book, and have set up Miss Bunyan against Lady Violet. we have most title to be sure--but the verse be too bad. Lady Violet herself own it; shebe busy with her own poem; whatbe to be do? we cannot lose the plate. the governor give sixty pound for it."
"I know a fellow who would do some verse, I think," say Warrington. "let I take the plate home in my pocket: and send to my chamber in the morning for the verse. youwill pay well, of course."
"of course," say Mr. Hack; and Warrington, have despatch his own business, go home to Mr. Pen, plate in hand.
"now, boy, herebe a chance for you. turn I off a copy of verse to this."
"whatbe this? a Church Porch--a lady enter it, and a youth out of a wine-shop window ogling her.--what the deuce be I to do with it?"
"try," say Warrington. "earn your livelihood for once, you who long so to do it."
"well, I will try," say Pen.
"and Iwill go out to dinner," say Warrington, and leave Mr. Pen in a brown study.
when Warrington come home that night, at a very late hour, the verse be do. "there they be," say Pen. "Ihave screw them out at last. I think theywill do."
"I think, they will," say Warrington, after read they; they run as follows:--
the Church Porch
although I enter not, yet round about the spot sometimes I hover, and at the sacred gate, with long eye I wait, Expectant of she.
