the Minster bell toll out above the city's rout and noise and hum theyhave stopp'd the chime bell, I hear the organ's swell shebe come, shebe come!
my lady come at last, Timid and stepping fast, and hasten hither, with modest eye downcast. she come--shebe here--shebe past. may Heaven go with she!
Kneel undisturb'd, fair saint, pour out your praise or plaint Meekly and duly. I will not enter there, to sully your pure prayer with thought unruly.
but suffer I to pace Round the forbidden place, linger a minute, like outcast spirit, who wait and see through Heaven's gate Angels within it.
"have you get any more, young fellow?" ask Warrington. "we must make they give you a couple of guineas a page; and if the verse be like, why, youwill get an entree into Bacon's magazine, and may turn a decent penny."
Pen examine his portfolio and find another ballad which he think might figure with advantage in the Spring Annual, and consign these two precious document to Warrington, the pair walk from the Temple to the famous haunt of the Muses and their master, Paternoster Row. Bacon's shop be an ancient low-browed building, with a few of the book publish by the firm display in the window, under a bust of my Lord of Verulam, and the name of Mr. Bacon in brass on the private door. exactly opposite to Bacon's house be that of Mr. Bungay, which be newly paint and elaborately decorate in the style of the seventeenth century, so that you might have fancy stately Mr. Evelyn pass over the threshold, or curious Mr. Pepys examine the book in the window. Warrington go into the shop of Mr. Bacon, but Pen stay without. it be agree that his ambassador should act for he entirely; and the young fellow pace up and down the street in a very nervous condition, until he should learn the result of the negotiation. many a poor devil before he have tread those flag, with similar care and anxiety at his heel, his bread and his fame dependent upon the sentence of his magnanimous patron of the row. Pen look at all the wonder of all the shop, and the strange variety of literature which they exhibit. in this be display black-letter volume and book in the clear pale type of Aldus and Elzevir: in the next, you might see the Penny Horrific Register; the Halfpenny Annals of Crime and history of the most celebrated murderer of all country, the Raff's Magazine, the Larky Swell, and other publication of the penny press; whilst at the next window, portrait of ill-favour individual, with fac-simile of the venerate signature of the Reverend Grimes Wapshot, the Reverend Elias Howle, and the work write and the sermon preach by they, show the British Dissenter where he could find mental pabulum. hard by would be a little casement hang with emblem, with medal and rosary with little paltry print of saint gilt and paint, and book of controversial theology, by which the faithful of the roman opinion might learn a short way to deal with Protestants, at a penny apiece, or ninepence the dozen for distribution; whilst in the very next window you might see 'come out of Rome,' a sermon preach at the opening of the Shepherd's Bush College, by John Thomas Lord Bishop of Ealing. Scarce an opinion but have its expositor and its place of exhibition in this peaceful old Paternoster row, under the toll of the bell of Saint Paul.
Pen look in at all the window and shop, as a gentleman who be go to have an interview with the dentist examine the book on the waiting-room table. he remember they afterwards. it seem to he that Warrington would never come out; and indeed the latter be engage for some time in plead his friend's cause.
Pen's natural conceit would have swell immensely if he could but have hear the report which Warrington give of he. it happen that Mr. Bacon himself have occasion to descend to Mr. Hack's room whilst Warrington be talk there, and Warrington, know Bacon's weakness, act upon they with great adroitness in his friend's behalf. in the first place, he put on his hat to speak to Bacon, and address he from the table on which he seat himself. Bacon like to be treat with rudeness by a gentleman, and use to pass it on to his inferior as boy pass the mark. "what! not know Mr. Pendennis, Mr. Bacon?" Warrington say. "you cannot live much in the world, or you would know he. a man of property in the West, of one of the most ancient family in England, relate to half the nobility in the empire--hebe cousin to Lord Pontypool--he be one of the most distinguished man at Oxbridge; he din at Gaunt House every week."
"Law bless I, you donot say so, sir. well--really--Law bless I now," say Mr. Bacon.
"I have just be show Mr. Hack some of his verse, which he sit up last night, at my request, to write; and hack talk about give he a copy of the book--the what-d'-you-call-'em."
"Law bless I now, do he? the what-d'-you-call-'em. indeed!"
"'the Spring Annual' be its name,--as payment for those verse. you donot suppose that such a man as Mr. Arthur Pendennis give up a dinner at Gaunt House for nothing? you know as well as anybody, that the man of fashion want to be pay."
"that they do, Mr. Warrington, sir," say the publisher.
"I tell you hebe a star; hewill make a name, sir. hebe a new man, sir."
"theyhave say that of so many of those young swell, Mr. Warrington," the publisher interpose, with a sigh. "there be Lord Viscount Dodo, now; I give his lordship a good bit of money for his poem, and only sell eighty copy. Mr. Popjoy's Hadgincourt, sir, fall dead."
"well, then, Iwill take my man over to Bungay," Warrington say, and rise from the table. this threat be too much for Mr. Bacon, who be instantly ready to accede to any reasonable proposal of Mr. Warrington's, and finally ask his manager what those proposal be? when he hear that the negotiation only relate as yet to a couple of ballad, which Mr. Warrington offer for the Spring Annual, Mr. Bacon say, "Law bless you, give he a check directly;" and with this paper Warrington go out to his friend, and place it, grin, in Pen's hand. Pen be as elated as if somebody have leave he a fortune. he offer Warrington a dinner at Richmond instantly. "what should he go and buy for Laura and his mother? he must buy something for they."
"theywill like the book well than anything else," say Warrington, "with the young one's name to the verse, print among the swell."
"thank God! thank God!" cry Arthur, "I neednot be a charge upon the old mother. I can pay off Laura now. I can get my own living. I can make my own way."
"I can marry the grand vizier's daughter: I can purchase a house in Belgrave Square; I can build a fine castle in the air!" say Warrington, please with the other's exultation. "well, you may get bread and cheese, Pen: and I own it taste well, the bread which you earn yourself."
they have a magnum of claret at dinner at the club that day, at Pen's charge. it be long since he have indulge in such a luxury, but Warrington would not baulk he: and they drink together to the health of the Spring Annual.
it never rain but it pour, accord to the proverb; so very speedily another chance occur, by which Mr. Pen be to be help in his scheme of make a livelihood. Warrington one day throw he a letter across the table, which be bring by a printer's boy, "from Captain Shandon, sir" --the little emissary say: and then go and fall asleep on his accustomed bench in the passage. he pay many a subsequent visit there, and bring many a message to Pen.
F. P. Tuesday Morning.
"MY DEAR SIR,--Bungay will be here to-day, about the Pall Mall Gazette. you would be the very man to help we with a genuine west-end article,-- you understand--dash, trenchant, and d---- aristocratic. Lady Hipshaw will write; but shebe not much you know, and wehave two lord; but the less they do the well. we must have you. wewill give you your own term, and wewill make a hit with the Gazette.
"Shall B. come and see you, or can you look in upon I here?--ever your, C. S."
"some more opposition," Warrington say, when Pen have read the note. "Bungay and Bacon be at dagger draw; each marry the sister of the other, and they be for some time the close friend and partner. Hack say it be Mrs. Bungay who cause all the mischief between the two; whereas Shandon, who read for Bungay a good deal, say Mrs. Bacon do the business; but I donot know which be right, Peachum or Lockit. but since they have separate, it be a furious war between the two publisher; and no soon do one bring out a book of travel, or poem, a magazine or periodical, quarterly, or monthly, or weekly, or annual, but the rival be in the field with something similar. I have hear poor Shandon tell with great glee how he make Bungay give a grand dinner at Blackwall to all his writer, by say that Bacon have invite his corp to an entertainment at Greenwich. when Bungay engage your celebrated friend Mr. Wagg to edit the 'Londoner,' Bacon straightway rush off and secure Mr. Grindle to give his name to the 'Westminster Magazine.' when Bacon bring out his comic irish novel of 'Barney Brallaghan,' off go Bungay to Dublin, and produce his rollick hibernian story of 'Looney MacTwolter.' when Doctor Hicks bring out his 'Wanderings in Mesopotamia' under Bacon's auspex, Bungay produce Professor Sandiman's 'research in Zahara;' and Bungay be publish his 'Pall Mall Gazette' as a counterpoise to Bacon's 'Whitehall Review.' let we go and hear about the 'Gazette.' there may be a place for you in it, Pen, my boy. we will go and see Shandon. we be sure to find he at home."
"where do he live?" ask Pen.
"in the Fleet Prison," Warrington say. "and very much at home he be there, too. he be the king of the place."
Pen have never see this scene of London life, and walk with no small interest in at the grim gate of that dismal edifice. they go through the anteroom, where the officer and janitor of the place be seat, and pass in at the wicket, enter the prison. the noise and the crowd, the life and the shouting, the shabby bustle of the place, strike and excite Pen. People move about ceaselessly and restless, like cage animal in a menagerie. man be play at five. other pace and tramping: this one in colloquy with his lawyer in dingy black--that one walk sadly, with his wife by his side, and a child on his arm. some be array in tattered dressing-gown, and have a look of rakish fashion. everybody seem to be busy, hum, and on the move. Pen feel as if he choke in the place, and as if the door be lock upon he they never would let he out.
they go through a court up a stone staircase, and through passage full of people, and noise, and cross light, and black door clapping and banging;--pen feel as one do in a feverish morning dream. at last the same little runner who have bring Shandon's note, and have follow they down Fleet Street munching apple, and who show the way to the two gentleman through the prison, say, "this be the Captain's door," and Mr. Shandon's voice from within bade they enter.
the room, though bare, be not uncheerful. the sun be shin in at the window--near which sate a lady at work, who have be gay and beautiful once, but in whose faded face kindness and tenderness still beam. through all his error and reckless mishap and misfortune, this faithful creature adore her husband, and think he the good and cleverest, as indeed he be one of the kind of man. nothing ever seem to disturb the sweetness of his temper; not debt: not dun: not misery: not the bottle, not his wife's unhappy position, or his child's ruin chance. he be perfectly fond of wife and child after his fashion: he always have the kind word and smile for they, and ruin they with the utmost sweetness of temper. he never could refuse himself or any man any enjoyment which his money could purchase; he would share his last guinea with Jack and Tom, and we may be sure he have a score of such retainer. he would sign his name at the back of any man's bill, and never pay any debt of his own. he would write on any side, and attack himself or another man with equal indifference. he be one of the wittiest, the most amiable, and the most incorrigible of Irishmen. nobody could help like Charley Shandon who see he once, and those whom he ruin could scarcely be angry with he.
when Pen and Warrington arrive, the Captain (he have be in an irish militia regiment once, and the title remain with he) be sit on his bed in a torn dressing-gown, with a desk on his knee, at which he be scribble as fast as his rapid pen could write. slip after slip of paper fall off the desk wet on to the ground. a picture of his child be hang up over his bed, and the young of they be pattering about the room.
opposite the Captain sate Mr. Bungay, a portly man of stolid countenance, with whom the little child have be try a conversation.
"Papabe a very clever man," say she; "mamma say so."
"oh, very," say Mr. Bungay.
"and yoube a very rich man, Mr. Bundy," cry the child, who could hardly speak plain.
"Mary!" say Mamma, from her work.
"oh, never mind," Bungay roar out with a great laugh; no harm in say Ibe rich--he, he--I be pretty well off, my little dear."
"if yoube rich, why donot you take papa out of piz'n?" ask the child.
Mamma at this begin to wipe her eye with the work on which she be employ. (the poor lady have hang curtain up in the room, have bring the child's picture and place it there, and have make one or two attempt to ornament it.) Mamma begin to cry; Mr. Bungay turn red, and look fiercely out of his bloodshot little eye; Shandon's pen go on, and Pen and Warrington arrive with their knock.
Captain Shandon look up from his work. "how do you do, Mr. Warrington," he say. "Iwill speak to you in a minute. please sit down, gentleman, if you can find place," and away go the pen again.
Warrington pull forward an old portmanteau--the only available seat-- and sate down on it, with a bow to Mrs. Shandon and a nod to Bungay: the child come and look at Pen solemnly and in a couple of minute the swift scribbling cease; and Shandon, turn the desk over on the bed, stoop and pick up the paper.
"I think this will do," say he. "itbe the prospectus for the Pall Mall Gazette."
"and herebe the money for it," Mr. Bungay say, lay down a five-pound note. "Ibe as good as my word, I be. when I say Iwill pay, I pay."
"faith thatbe more than some of we can say," say Shandon, and he eagerly clap the note into his pocket.
CHAPTER XXXIII
which be pass in the Neighbourhood of Ludgate Hill
our imprisoned Captain announce, in smart and emphatic language in his prospectus, that the time have come at last when it be necessary for the gentleman of England to band together in defence of their common right and their glorious order, menace on all side by foreign revolution, by intestine radicalism, by the artful calumny of mill-owner and cotton-lord, and the stupid hostility of the mass whom they gull and lead. "the ancient monarchy be insult," the Captain say, "by a ferocious republican rabble. the Church be desert by envious dissent, and undermine by stealthy infidelity. the good institution, which have make our country glorious, and the name of English Gentleman the proud in the world, be leave without defence, and expose to assault and contumely from man to whom no sanctuary be sacred, for they believe in nothing holy; no history venerable, for they be too ignorant to have hear of the past; and no law be bind which they be strong enough to break, when their leader give the signal for plunder. it be because the king of France mistrust their gentleman," Mr. Shandon remark, "that the monarchy of Saint Louis go down: it be because the people of England still believe in their gentleman, that this country encounter and overcome the great enemy a nation ever meet: it be because we be head by gentleman, that the Eagles retreat before we from the Donro to the Garonne: it be a gentleman who break the line at Trafalgar, and sweep the plain of Waterloo."
Bungay nod his head in a knowing manner, and wink his eye when the Captain come to the Waterloo passage: and Warrington burst out laugh.
"you see how our venerable friend Bungay be affect," Shandon say, slily look up from his papers--"that's your true sort of test. I have use the Duke of Wellington and the battle of Waterloo a hundred time, and I never know the Duke to fail."
the Captain then go on to confess, with much candour, that up to the present time the gentleman of England, confident of their right, and careless of those who question it, have leave the political interest of their order as they do the management of their estate, or the settlement of their legal affair, to person affect to each peculiar service, and have permit their interest to be represent in the press by professional proctor and advocate. that time Shandon profess to consider be now go by: the gentleman of England must be their own champion: the declare enemy of their order be brave, strong, numerous, and uncompromising. they must meet their foe in the field: they must not be belie and misrepresent by hireling advocate: they must not have Grub Street publish gazette from Whitehall; "thatbe a dig at Bacon's people, Mr. Bungay," say Shandon, turn round to the publisher. Bungay clap his stick on the floor. "hang he, pitch into he, Capting," he say with exultation: and turn to Warrington, wag his dull head more vehemently than ever, and say, "for a slashing article, sir, therebe nobody like the Capting--no-obody like he."
the prospectus-writer go on to say that some gentleman, whose name be, for obvious reason, not bring before the public (at which Mr. Warrington begin to laugh again), have determine to bring forward a journal, of which the principle be so-and-so. "these man be proud of their order, and anxious to uphold it," cry out Captain Shandon, flourish his paper with a grin. "they be loyal to their Sovereign, by faithful conviction and ancestral allegiance; they love their Church, where they would have their child worship, and for which their forefather bleed; they love their country, and would keep it what the gentleman of England--yes, the gentleman of England (wewill have that in large cap, Bungay, my boy) have make it--the great and free in the world: and as the name of some of they be append to the deed which secure our liberty at runnymede--"
"whatbe that?" ask Mr. Bungay.
"an ancestor of mine seal it with his sword-hilt," Pen say, with great gravity.
"itbe the Habeas Corpus, Mr. Bungay," Warrington say, on which the publisher answer, "all right, I dare say," and yawn, though he say, "go on, Capting."
"--at runnymede; they be ready to defend that freedom to-day with sword and pen, and now, as then, to rally round the old law and liberty of England."
"Bravo!" cry Warrington. the little child stand wonder; the lady be work silently, and look with fond admiration. "come here, little Mary," say Warrington, and pat the child's fair curl with his large hand. but she shrink back from his rough caress, and prefer to go and take refuge at Pen's knee, and play with his fine watch-chain: and Pen be very much pleased that she come to he; for he be very soft-hearted and simple, though he conceal his gentleness under a shy and pompous demeanour. so she clamber up on his lap, whilst her father continue to read his programme.
"you be laugh," the Captain say to Warrington, "about 'the obvious reason' which I mention. now, Iwill show ye what they be, ye unbelieve heathen. 'we have say,'" he go on, "'that we cannot give the name of the party engage in this undertaking, and that there be obvious reason for that concealment. we number influential friend in both Houses of the Senate, and have secure ally in every diplomatic circle in Europe. our source of intelligence be such as cannot, by any possibility, be make public--and, indeed, such as no other London or european journal could, by any chance, acquire. but this we be free to say, that the very early information connect with the movement of English and Continental politic will be find only in the column of the Pall Mall Gazette, the Statesman and the Capitalist, the Country Gentleman and the Divine, will be amongst our reader, because our writer be amongst they. we address ourselves to the high circle of society: we care not to disown it--the Pall Mall Gazette be write by gentleman for gentleman; its conductor speak to the class in which they live and be bear. the field-preacher have his journal, the radical free-thinker have his journal: why should the Gentlemen of England be unrepresented in the Press?'"
Mr. Shandon then go on with much modesty to descant upon the literary and fashionable department of the Pall Mall Gazette, which be to be conduct by gentleman of acknowledge reputation; man famous at the Universities (at which Mr Pendennis could scarcely help laugh and blushing), know at the Clubs, and of the Society which they describe. he point out delicately to advertiser that there would be no such medium as the Pall Mall Gazette for give publicity to their sale; and he eloquently call upon the nobility of England, the baronetage of England, the revered clergy of England, the bar of England, the matron, the daughter, the home and hearth of England, to rally round the good old cause; and Bungay at the conclusion of the reading wake up from a second snooze in which he have indulge himself, and again say it be all right.
the reading of the prospectus conclude, the gentleman present enter into some detail regard the political and literary management of the paper, and Mr. Bungay sate by listen and nod his head, as if he understand what be the subject of their conversation, and approve of their opinion. Bungay's opinion, in truth, be pretty simple. he think the Captain could write the good smashing article in England. he want the opposition house of Bacon smash, and it be his opinion that the Captain could do that business. if the Captain have write a letter of Junius on a sheet of paper, or copy a part of the Church Catechism, Mr. Bungay would have be perfectly content, and have consider that the article be a smash article. and he pocket the paper with the great satisfaction: and he not only pay for the MS., as we have see, but he call little Mary to he, and give she a penny as he go away.
the reading of the manuscript over, the party engage in general conversation, Shandon lead with a jaunty fashionable air in compliment to the two guest who sate with he and, and who, by their appearance and manner, he presume to be person of the beau monde. he know very little indeed of the great world, but he have see it, and make the most of what he have see. he speak of the character of the day, and great personage of the fashion, with easy familiarity and jocular allusion, as if it have be his habit to live amongst they. he tell anecdote of their private life, and of conversation he have have, and entertainment at which he have be present, and at which such and such a thing occur. Pen be amuse to hear the shabby prisoner in a tattered dressing-gown talk glibly about the great of the land. Mrs. Shandon be always delighted when her husband tell these tale, and believe in they fondly every one. she do not want to mingle in the fashionable world herself, she be not clever enough; but the great Society be the very place for her Charles: he shine in it: he be respect in it. indeed, Shandon have once be ask to dinner by the Earl of X; his wife treasure the invitation-card in her workbox at that very day.
Mr. Bungay presently have enough of this talk and get up to take leave, whereupon Warrington and Pen rise to depart with the publisher, though the latter would have like to stay to make a further acquaintance with this family, who interest he and touch he. he say something about hop for permission to repeat his visit, upon which Shandon, with a rueful grin, say he be always to be find at home, and should be delighted to see Mr. Pennington.
"Iwill see you to my park-gate, gentleman," say Captain Shandon, seize his hat, in spite of a deprecatory look and a faint cry of "Charles" from Mrs. Shandon. and the Captain, in shabby slipper, shuffle out before his guest, lead the way through the dismal passage of the prison. his hand be already fiddle with his waistcoat pocket, where Bungay's five-pound note be, as he take leave of the three gentleman at the wicket; one of they, Mr. Arthur Pendennis, be greatly relieve when he be out of the horrid place, and again freely tread the flag of Farringdon Street.
Mrs. Shandon sadly go on with her work at the window look into the court. she see Shandon with a couple of man at his heel run rapidly in the direction of the prison tavern. she have hop to have have he to dinner herself that day: there be a piece of meat, and some salad in a basin, on the ledge outside of the window of their room which she have expect that she and little Mary be to share with the child's father. but there be no chance of that now. he would be in that tavern until the hour for close it; then he would go and play at card or drink in some other man's room and come back silent, with glaze eye, reel a little on his walk, that his wife might nurse he. oh, what variety of pain do we not make our woman suffer!
so Mrs. Shandon go to the cupboard, and, in lieu of a dinner, make herself some tea. and in those variety of pain of which we speak anon, what a part of confidante have that poor tea-pot play ever since the kindly plant be introduce among we! what myriad of woman have cry over it, to be sure! what sick-bed it have smoke by! what fevere lip have receive refreshment from out of it! nature mean very gently by woman when she make that tea-plant; and with a little thought what a series of picture and group the fancy may conjure up and assemble round the tea-pot and cup! Melissa and Sacharissa be talk love-secret over it. poor Polly have it and her lover's letter upon the table; his letter who be her lover yesterday, and when it be with pleasure, not despair, she weep over they. Mary tripping noiselessly come into her mother's bedroom, bear a cup of the consoler to the widow who will take no other food, Ruth be busy concoct it for her husband, who be come home from the harvest-field--one could fill a page with hint for such pictures;--finally, Mrs. Shandon and little Mary sit down and drink their tea together, while the Captain go out and take his pleasure. she care for nothing else but that, when her husband be away.
a gentleman with whom we be already slightly acquaint, Mr. Jack Finucane, a townsman of Captain Shandon's, find the Captain's wife and little Mary (for whom Jack always bring a sweetmeat in his pocket) over this meal. Jack think Shandon the great of create genius, have have one or two help from the good-natured prodigal, who have always a kind word, and sometimes a guinea for any friend in need; and never miss a day in see his patron. he be ready to run Shandon's errand and transact his money-business with publisher and newspaper editor, dun, creditor, holder of Shandon's acceptance, gentleman dispose to speculate in those security, and to transact the thousand little affair of an embarrassed irish gentleman. I never know an embarrassed irish gentleman yet, but he have an aide-de-camp of his own nation, likewise in circumstance of pecuniary discomfort. that aide-de-camp have subordinate of his own, who again may have other insolvent dependents-- all through his life our captain march at the head of a ragged staff, who share in the rough fortune of their chieftain.
"he willnot have that five-pound note very long, I bet a guinea," Mr. Bungay say of the Captain, as he and his two companion walk away from the prison; and the publisher judge rightly, for when Mrs. Shandon come to empty her husband's pocket, she find but a couple of shilling, and a few halfpence out of the morning's remittance. Shandon have give a pound to one follower; have send a leg of mutton and potato and beer to an acquaintance in the poor side of the prison; have pay an outstanding bill at the tavern where he have change his five-pound note; have have a dinner with two friend there, to whom he lose sundry half-crown at card afterwards; so that the night leave he as poor as the morning have find he.
the publisher and the two gentleman have have some talk together after quit Shandon, and Warrington reiterate to Bungay what he have say to his rival, Bacon, viz., that Pen be a high fellow, of great genius, and what be more, well with the great world, and relate to "no end" of the peerage. Bungay reply that he should be happy to have dealing with Mr. Pendennis, and hop to have the pleasure of see both gent to cut mutton with he before long, and so, with mutual politeness and protestation, they part.
"it be hard to see such a man as Shandon," Pen say, musing, and talk that night over the sight which he have witness, "of accomplishment so multifarious, and of such an undoubted talent and humour, an inmate of a gaol for half his time, and a bookseller's hanger-on when out of prison."
"I be a bookseller's hanger-on--you be go to try your pace as a hack," Warrington say with a laugh. "we be all hack upon some road or other. I would rather be myself, than Paley our neighbour in chamber: who have as much enjoyment of his life as a mole. a deuced deal of undeserved compassion have be throw away upon what you call your bookseller's drudge."
"much solitary pipe and ale make a cynic of you," say Pen "you be a Diogenes by a beer-barrel, Warrington. no man shall tell I that a man of genius, as Shandon be, ought to be drive by such a vulgar slave-driver, as yonder Mr. Bungay, whom we have just leave, who fatten on the profit of the other's brain, and enrich himself out of his journeyman's labour. it make I indignant to see a gentleman the serf of such a creature as that, of a man who cannot speak the language that he live by, who be not fit to black Shandon's boot."
"so you have begin already to gird at the publisher, and to take your side amongst our order. Bravo, Pen, my be boy!" Warrington answer, laugh still. "what have you get to say against Bungay's relation with Shandon? be it the publisher, think you, who send the author to prison? be it Bungay who be tipple away the five-pound note which we see just now, or Shandon?"
"misfortune drive a man into bad company," Pen say. "it be easy to cry 'fie!' against a poor fellow who have no society but such as he find in a prison; and no resource except forgetfulness and the bottle. we must deal kindly with the eccentricity of genius, and remember that the very ardour and enthusiasm of temperament which make the author delightful often lead the man astray."
"a fiddlestick about man of genius!" Warrington cry out, who be a very severe moralist upon some point, though possibly a very had practitioner. "I deny that there be so many genius as people who whimper about the fate of man of letter assert there be. there be thousand of clever fellow in the world who could, if they would, turn verse, write article, read book, and deliver a judgment upon they; the talk of professional critic and writer be not a whit more brilliant, or profound, or amusing, than that of any other society of educated people. if a lawyer, or a soldier, or a parson, outrun his income, and do not pay his bill, he must go to gaol; and an author must go, too. if an author fuddle himself, I donot know why he should be let off a headache the next morning,--if he order a coat from the tailor's, why he shouldnot pay for it."
"I would give he more money to buy coat," say Pen, smile. I suppose I should like to belong to a well-dress profession. I protest against that wretch of a middle-man whom I see between Genius and his great landlord, the Public, and who stop more than half of the labourer's earning and fame."
"I be a prose labourer," Warrington say; "you, my boy, be a poet in a small way, and so, I suppose, consider you be authorise to be flighty. what be it you want? do you want a body of capitalist that shall be force to purchase the work of all author, who may present themselves, manuscript in hand? everybody who write his epic, every driveller who can or cannot spell, and produce his novel or his tragedy,--are they all to come and find a bag of sovereign in exchange for their worthless ream of paper? who be to settle what be good or bad, saleable or otherwise? will you give the buyer leave, in fine, to purchase or not? why, sir, when Johnson sate behind the screen at Saint John's Gate, and take his dinner apart, because he be too shabby and poor to join the literary bigwig who be regale themselves, round Mr. Cave's good table-cloth, the tradesman be do he no wrong. you couldnot force the publisher to recognise the man of genius in the young man who present himself before he, ragged, gaunt, and hungry. rag be not a proof of genius; whereas capital be absolute, as time go, and be perforce the bargain-master. it have a right to deal with the literary inventor as with any other;--if I produce a novelty in the book trade, I must do the good I can with it; but I can no more force Mr. Murray to purchase my book of travel or sermon, than I can compel Mr. Tattersall to give I a hundred guineas for my horse. I may have my own idea of the value of my Pegasus, and think he the most wonderful of animal; but the dealer have a right to his opinion, too, and may want a lady's horse, or a cob for a heavy timid rider, or a sound hack for the road, and my beast willnot suit he."
"you deal in metaphor, Warrington," Pen say; "but you rightly say that you be very prosaic. poor Shandon! there be something about the kindness of that man, and the gentleness of that sweet creature of a wife, which touch I profoundly. I like he, I be afraid, well than a well man"
"and so do I," Warrington say. "let we give he the benefit of our sympathy, and the pity that be due to his weakness: though I fear that sort of kindness would be resent as contempt by a more high-minded man. you see he take his consolation along with his misfortune, and one generate the other or balance ii, as the way of the world. he be a prisoner, but he be not unhappy."
"his genius sing within his prison bar," Pen say.
"yes," Warrington say, bitterly; "Shandon accommodate himself to a cage pretty well. he ought to be wretche, but he have Jack and Tom to drink with, and that console he: he might have a high place, but, as he cannot, why, he can drink with Tom and Jack;--he might be provide for his wife and child, but Thomas and John have get a bottle of brandy which they want he to taste;--he might pay poor Snip, the tailor, the twenty pound which the poor devil want for his landlord, but John and Thomas lie their hand upon his purse;--and so he drink whilst his tradesman go to gaol and his family to ruin. let we pity the misfortune of genius, and conspire against the publishing tyrant who oppress man of letter."
"what! be you go to have another glass of brandy-and-water?" Pen say, with a humorous look. it be at the Black Kitchen that the above philosophical conversation take place between the two young man.
Warrington begin to laugh as usual. "video meliora proboque--I mean, bring it I hot, with sugar, John," he say to waiter.
"I would have some more, too, only I donot want it," say Pen. "it do not seem to I, Warrington, that we be much well than our neighbour." and Warrington's last glass have be despatch, the pair return to their chamber.
they find a couple of note in the letter-box, on their return, which have be send by their acquaintance of the morning, Mr. Bungay. that hospitable gentleman present his compliment to each of the gentleman, and request their pleasure of company at dinner on an early day, to meet a few literary friend.
"we shall have a grand spread, Warrington. we shall meet all Bungay's corps."
"all except poor Shandon," say Pen, nod a good-night to his friend, and he go into his own little room. the event and acquaintance of the day have excite he a good deal, and he lie for some time awake thinking over they, as Warrington's vigorous and regular snore from the neighbouring apartment pronounce that that gentleman be engage in deep slumber.
be it true, think Pendennis, lie on his bed and gaze at a bright moon without, that light up a corner of his dressing-table, and the frame of a little sketch of fairoak draw by Laura, and hang over his drawer--be it true that I be go to earn my bread at last, and with my pen? that I shall impoverish the dear mother no long; and that I may gain a name and reputation in the world, perhaps? these be welcome if they come, think the young visionary, laugh and blush to himself, though alone and in the night, as he think how dearly he would relish honour and fame if they could be his. if fortune favour I, I laud she; if she frown, I resign she. I pray Heaven I may be honest if I fail, or if I succeed. I pray Heaven I may tell the truth as far as I know it: that I maynot swerve from it through flattery, or interest, or personal enmity, or party prejudice. Dearest old mother, what a pride will you have, if I can do anything worthy of our name I and you, Laura, you willnot scorn I as the worthless idler and spendthrift, when you see that I-- when I have achieve a--psha! what an Alnaschar I be because I have make five pound by my poem, and be engage to write half a dozen article for a newspaper. he go on with these musing, more happy and hopeful, and in a humbler frame of mind, than he have feel to be for many a day. he think over the error and idleness, the passion, extravagance, disappointment, of his wayward youth: he get up from the bed: throw open the window, and look out into the night: and then, by some impulse, which we hope be a good one, he go up and kiss the picture of Fairoaks, and fling himself down on his knee by the bed, remain for some time in that posture of hope and submission. when he rise, it be with streaming eye. he have find himself repeat, mechanically, some little word which he have be accustom to repeat as a child at his mother's side, after the saying of which she would softly take he to his bed and close the curtain round he, hush he with a benediction.
the next day, Mr. Pidgeon, their attendant, bring in a large brown-paper parcel, direct to G. Warrington, Esq., with Mr. Trotter's compliment, and a note which Warrington read.
"Pen, you beggar!" roar Warrington to Pen, who be in his own room.
"Hullo!" sing out Pen.
"come here, yoube want," cry the other, and Pen come out.
"what be it?" say he.
"catch!" cry Warrington, and fling the parcel at Pen's head, who would have be knock down have he not catch it.
"itbe book for review for the Pall Mall Gazette: pitch into them," Warrington say. as for Pen, he never have be so delighted in his life: his hand tremble as he cut the string of the packet, and beheld within a smart set of new neat calico-bind book--travel, and novel, and poem.
"Sport the oak, Pidgeon," say he. "Ibe not at home to anybody to-day." and he fling into his easy-chair, and hardly give himself time to drink his tea, so eager be he to begin to read and to review.
CHAPTER XXXIV
in which the history still hover about Fleet Street
Captain Shandon, urge on by his wife, who seldom meddle in business matter, have stipulate that John Finucane, Esquire, of the Upper Temple, should be appoint sub-editor of forthcoming Pall Mall Gazette, and this post be accordingly confer upon Mr. Finucane by the spirited proprietor of the Journal. indeed he deserve any kindness at the hand of Shandon, so fondly attach be he, as we have say, to the Captain and his family, and so eager to do he a service. it be in Finucane's chamber that Shandon in former day use to hide when danger be near and bailiff abroad: until at length his hiding-place be know, and the sheriff's officer come as regularly to wait for the Captain on Finucane's staircase as at his own door. it be to Finucane's chamber that poor Mrs. Shandon come often and often to explain her trouble and grief, and devise mean of rescue for her adore Captain. many a meal do Finucane furnish for she and the child there. it be an honour to his little room to be visit by such a lady; and as she go down the staircase with her veil over her face, Fin would lean over the balustrade look after she, to see that no Temple Lovelace assail she upon the road, perhaps hop that some rogue might be induce to waylay she, so that he, Fin, might have the pleasure of rush to her rescue, and break the rascal's bone. it be a sincere pleasure to Mrs. Shandon when the arrangement be make by which her kind honest champion be appoint her husband's aide-de-camp in the newspaper.
he would have sate with Mrs. Shandon as late as the prison hour permit, and have indeed many a time witness the putting to bed of little Mary, who occupy a crib in the room; and to whose evening prayer that God might bless papa, Finucane, although of the romish faith himself, have say amen with a great deal of sympathy--but he have an appointment with Mr. Bungay regard the affair of the paper which they be to discuss over a quiet dinner. so he go away at six o'clock from Mrs. Shandon, but make his accustomed appearance at the Fleet Prison next morning, have array himself in his good clothe and ornament, which, though cheap as to cost, be very brilliant as to colour and appearance, and have in his pocket four pound two shilling, be the amount of his week's salary at the Daily Journal, minus two shilling expend by he in the purchase of a pair of glove on his way to the prison.
he have cut his mutton with Mr. Bungay, as the latter gentleman phrase it, and Mr. Trotter, Bungay's reader and literary man of business, at Dick's Coffee-house on the previous day, and enter at large into his view respect the conduct of the Pall Mall Gazette. in a masterly manner he have point out what should be the sub-editorial arrangement of the paper: what should be the type for the various article: who should report the market; who the turf and ring; who the Church intelligence; and who the fashionable chit-chat. he be acquaint with gentleman engage in cultivate these various department of knowledge, and in communicate they afterwards to the public--in fine, Jack Finucane be, as Shandon have say of he, and as he proudly own himself to be, one of the good sub-editor of a paper in London. he know the weekly earning of every man connect with the Press, and be up to a thousand dodge, or ingenious economic contrivance, by which money could be save to spirited capitalist, who be go to set up a paper. he at once dazzle and mystify Mr. Bungay, who be slow of comprehension, by the rapidity of the calculation which he exhibit on paper, as they sate in the box. and Bungay afterwards own to his subordinate Mr. Trotter, that that Irishman seem a clever fellow.
and now have succeed in make this impression upon Mr. Bungay, the faithful fellow work round to the point which he have very near at heart, viz., the liberation from prison of his admire friend and chief, Captain Shandon. he know to a shill the amount of the detainer which be against the Captain at the porter's lodge of the Fleet; and, indeed, profess to know all his debt, though this be impossible, for no man in England, certainly not the Captain himself, be acquaint with they. he point out what Shandon's engagement already be; and how much well he would work if remove from confinement (though this Mr. Bungay deny, for, "when the Captain's lock up," he say, "we be sure to find he at home; whereas, when hebe free, you can never catch hold of he"); finally, he so work on Mr. Bungay's feeling, by describe Mrs. Shandon pin away in the prison, and the child sicken there, that the publisher be induce to promise that, if Mrs. Shandon would come to he in the morning, he would see what could be do. and the colloquy end at this time with the second round of brandy-and-water, although Finucane, who have four guineas in his pocket, would have discharge the tavern reckoning with delight, Bungay say, "no, sir,--this be my affair, sir, if you please. James, take the bill, and eighteenpence for yourself," and he hand over the necessary fund to the waiter. thus it be that Finucane, who go to bed at the Temple after the dinner at Dick's, find himself actually with his week's salary intact upon Saturday morning.
he give Mrs. Shandon a wink so know and joyful, that that kind creature know some good news be in store for she, and hasten to get her bonnet and shawl, when Fin ask if he might have the honour of take she a walk, and give she a little fresh air. and little Mary jump for joy at the idea of this holiday, for Finucane never neglect to give she a toy, or to take she to a show, and bring newspaper order in his pocket for all sort of London diversion to amuse the child. indeed, he love they with all his heart, and would cheerfully have dash out his ramble brain to do they, or his adore Captain, a service.
"may I go, Charley? or shall I stay with you, for yoube poorly, dear, this morning? hebe get a headache, Mr. Finucane. he suffer from headache, and I persuade he to stay in bed," Mrs. Shandon say.
"go along with you, and Polly. Jack, take care of them. hand I over the Burton's Anatomy, and leave I to my abominable device," Shandon say, with perfect good-humour. he be write, and not uncommonly take his greek and latin quotation (of which he know the use as a public writer) from that wonderful repertory of learn.
so Fin give his arm to Mrs. Shandon, and Mary go skip down the passage of the prison, and through the gate into the free air. from Fleet Street to Paternoster Row be not very far. as the three reach Mr. Bungay's shop, Mrs. Bungay be also enter at the private door, hold in her hand a paper parcel and a manuscript volume bind in red, and, indeed, contain an account of her transaction with the butcher in the neighbouring market. Mrs. Bungay be in a gorgeous shot-silk dress, which flame with red and purple; she wear a yellow shawl, and have red flower inside her bonnet, and a brilliant light blue parasol.
Mrs. Shandon be in an old black water silk; her bonnet have never see very brilliant day of prosperity any more than its owner, but she could not help look like a lady whatever her attire be. the two woman curtsy to each other, each accord to her fashion.
"I hope yoube pretty well, mum?" say Mrs. Bungay.
"itbe a very fine day," say Mrs. Shandon.
"willnot you step in, mum?" say Mrs. Bungay, look so hard at the child as almost to frighten she.
"I--I come about business with Mr. Bungay--i--I hope hebe pretty well?" say timid Mrs. Shandon.
"if you go to see he in the counting-house, couldnot you, couldnot you leave your little gurl with I?" say Mrs. Bungay, in a deep voice, and with a tragic look, as she hold out one finger towards the child.
"I want to stay with mamma," cry little Mary, bury her face in her mother's dress.
"go with this lady, Mary, my dear," say the mother.
"Iwill show you some pretty picture," say Mrs. Bungay, with the voice of an ogress, "and some nice thing besides; look here,"--and open her brown-paper parcel, Mrs. Bungay display some choice sweet buscuit, such as her Bungay love after his wine. little Mary follow after this attraction, the whole party enter at the private entrance, from which a side door lead into Mr. Bungay's commercial apartment. here, however, as the child be about to part from her mother, her courage again fail she, and again she run to the maternal petticoat; upon which the kind and gentle Mrs. Shandon, see the look of disappointment in Mrs. Bungay's face, good-naturedly say, "if you will let I, I will come up too, and sit for a few minute," and so the three female ascend the stair together. a second biscuit charm little Mary into perfect confidence, and in a minute or two she prattle away without the least restraint.
faithful Finucane meanwhile find Mr. Bungay in a severer mood than he have be on the night previous, when two-third of a bottle of port, and two large glass of brandy-and-water, have warm his soul into enthusiasm, and make he generous in his promise towards Captain Shandon. his impetuous wife have rebuke he on his return home. she have order that he should give no relief to the captain; he be a good-for-nothing fellow, whom no money would help; she disapprove of the plan of the Pall Mall Gazette, and expect that Bungay would only lose his money in it as they be lose over the way (she always call her brother's establishment "over the way") by the Whitehall Journal. let Shandon stop in prison and do his work; it be the good place for he. in vain Finucane plead and promise and implore, for his friend Bungay have have an hour's lecture in the morning and be inexorable.
but what honest Jack fail to do below-stair in the counting-house, the pretty face and manner of the mother and child be effecting in the drawing-room, where they be melt the fierce but really soft Mrs. Bungay. there be an artless sweetness in Mrs. Shandon's voice, and a win frankness of manner, which make most people fond of she, and pity she: and take courage by the rugged kindness with which her hostess receive she, the Captain's lady tell her story, and describe her husband's goodness and virtue, and her child's fail health (she be oblige to part with two of they, she say, and send they to school, for she could not have they in that horrid place)--that Mrs. Bungay, though as grim as Lady Macbeth, melt under the influence of the simple tale, and say she would go down and speak to Bungay. now in this household to speak be to command, with Mrs. Bungay; and with Bungay, to hear be to obey.
it be just when poor finucane be in despair about his negotiation, that the majestic Mrs. Bungay descend upon her spouse, politely request Mr. Finucane to step up to his friend in her drawing-room, while she hold a few minute' conversation with Mr. B., and when the pair be alone the publisher's well half inform he of her intention towards the Captain's lady.
"whatbe in the wind now, my dear?" Maecenas ask, surprised at his wife's altered tone. "you wouldnot hear of my do anything for the Captain this morning: I wonder what have be a changing of you.
"the Capting be an Irishman," Mrs. Bungay reply; "and those irish I have always say I couldnot abide. but his wife be a lady, as any one can see; and a good woman, and a clergyman's daughter, and a West of England woman, B., which I be myself, by my mother's side--and, O Marmaduke! donot you remark the little gurl?"
"yes, Mrs. B., I see the little girl."
"and donot you see how like she be to our angel, Bessy, Mr. B.?"--and Mrs. Bungay's thought fly back to a period eighteen year back, when Bacon and Bungay have just set up in business as small bookseller in a country town, and when she have have a child, name Bessy, something like the little Mary who have move her compassion.
"well, well, my dear," Mr. Bungay say, see the little eye of his wife begin to twinkle and grow red; "the Captain benot in for much. therebe only a hundred and thirty pound against he. half the money will take he out of the Fleet, Finucane say, and wewill pay he half salary till he have make the account square. when the little 'un say, 'why donot you take Par out of prizn?' I do feel it, Flora, upon my honour I do, now." and the upshot of this conversation be, that Mr. and Mrs. Bungay both ascend to the drawing-room, and Mr. Bungay make a heavy and clumsy speech, in which he announce to Mrs. Shandon, that, hear sixty-five pound would set her husband free, he be ready to advance that sum of money, deduct it from the Captain's salary, and that he would give it to she on condition that she would personally settle with the creditor regard her husband's liberation.
I think this be the happy day that Mrs. Shandon and Mr. Finucane have have for a long time. "Bedad, Bungay, yoube a trump!" roar out Fin, in an overpowering brogue and emotion. "give we your fist, old boy: and willnot we send the Pall Mall Gazette up to ten thousand a week, thatbe all!" and he jump about the room, and toss up little Mary, with a hundred frantic antic.
"if I could drive you anywhere in my carriage, Mrs. Shandon--I'm sure itbe quite at your service," Mrs. Bungay say, look out at a one-horse vehicle which have just drive up, and in which this lady take the air considerably--and the two lady, with little Mary between they (whose tiny hand Maecenas's wife keep fix in her great grasp), with the delighted Mr. Finucane on the back seat, drive away from Paternoster Row, as the owner of the vehicle throw triumphant glance at the opposite window at Bacon's.
"it willnot do the Captain any good," think Bungay, go back to his desk and account, "but Mrs. B. become reglar upset when she think about her misfortune. the child would have be of age yesterday, if shehave live. Flora tell I so:" and he wonder how woman do remember thing.
we be happy to say that Mrs. Shandon speed with very good success upon her errand. she who have have to mollify creditor when she have no money at all, and only tear and entreaty wherewith to soothe they, find no difficulty in make they relent by mean of a bribe of ten shilling in the pound; and the next Sunday be the last, for some time at least, which the Captain spend in prison.
CHAPTER XXXV Dinner in the row
upon the appoint day our two friend make their appearance at Mr. Bungay's door in Paternoster row; not the public entrance through which bookseller' boy issue with their sack full of Bungay's volume, and around which timid aspirant linger with their virgin manuscript ready for sale to Sultan Bungay, but at the private door of the house, whence the splendid Mrs. Bungay would come forth to step into her chaise and take her drive, settle herself on the cushion, and cast look of defiance at Mrs. Bacon's opposite window--at Mrs. Bacon, who be as yet a chaiseless woman.
on such occasion, when very much wroth at her sister-in-law's splendour Mrs. Bacon would fling up the sash of her drawing-room window, and look out with her four child at the chaise, as much as to say, "look at these four darling. Flora Bungay! this be why I cannot drive in my carriage; you would give a coach-and-four to have the same reason." and it be with these arrow out of her quiver that Emma Bacon shoot Flora Bungay as she sate in her chariot envious and childless.
as Pen and Warrington come to Bungay's door, a carriage and a cab drive up to Bacon's. Old Dr. Slocum descend heavily from the first; the Doctor's equipage be as ponderous as his style, but both have a fine sonorous effect upon the publisher in the row. a couple of dazzle white waistcoat step out of the cab.
Warrington laugh. "you see Bacon have his dinner-party too. that be Dr. Slocum, author of 'Memoirs of the Poisoners.' you would hardly have recognise our friend Hoolan in that gallant white waistcoat. Doolan be one of Bungay's man, and faith, here he come." indeed, Messrs. Hoolan and Doolan have come from the Strand in the same cab, toss up by the way which should pay the shilling; and Mr. D. step from the other side of the way, array in black, with a large pair of white glove which be spread out on his hand, and which the owner could not help regard with pleasure.
the house porter in an evening coat, and gentleman with glove as large as Doolan's, but of the famous Berlin web, be on the passage of Mr. Bungay's house to receive the guest' hat and coat, and bawl their name up the stair. some of the latter have arrive when the three new visitor make their appearance; but there be only Mrs. Bungay in red satin and a turban to represent her own charming sex. she make curtsy to each new-comer as he enter the drawing-room, but her mind be evidently pre-occupy by extraneous thought. the fact be, Mrs. Bacon's dinner-party be disturb she, and as soon as she have receive each individual of her own company, Flora Bungay fly back to the embrasure of the window, whence she could rake the carriage of Emma Bacon's friend as they come rattle up the row. the sight of Dr. Slocum's large carriage, with the gaunt job-horse, crush Flora: none but hack cab have drive up to her own door on that day.
they be all literary gentleman, though unknown as yet to Pen. there be Mr. Bole, the real editor of the magazine, of which Mr. Wagg be the nominal chief; Mr. Trotter, who, from have break out on the world as a poet of a tragic and suicidial cast, have now subside into one of Mr. Bungay's back shop as reader for that gentleman; and Captain Sumph, an ex-beau reader about town, and relate in some indistinct manner to Literature and the Peerage. he be say to have write a book once, to have be a friend of Lord Byron, to be relate to Lord Sumphington; in fact, anecdote of Byron form his staple, and he seldom speak but with the name of that poet or some of his contemporary in his mouth, as thus: "I remember poor Shelley, at school be send up for good for a copy of verse, every line of which I write, by Jove;" or, "I recollect, when I be at Missolonghi with Byron, offer to bet gamba," and so forth. this gentleman, Pen remark, be listen to with great attention by Mrs. Bungay; his anecdote of the aristocracy, of which he be a middle-aged member, delight the publisher's lady; and he be almost a great man than the great Mr. Wagg himself in her eye. have he but come in his own carriage, Mrs. Bungay would have make her Bungay purchase any give volume from his pen.
Mr. Bungay go about to his guest as they arrive, and do the honour of his house with much cordiality. "how be you, sir? fine day, sir. glad to see you year, sir. Flora, my love, let I ave the honour of introduce Mr. Warrington to you. Mr. Warrington, Mrs. Bungay; Mr. Pendennis, Mrs. Bungay. hope youhave bring good appetite with you, gentleman. you, Doolan, I know ave, for youhave always ad a deuce of a twist."
"Lor, Bungay!" say Mrs. Bungay.
"Faith, a man must be hard to please, Bungay, who cannot eat a good dinner in this house," Doolan say, and he wink and stroke his lean chop with his large glove; and make appeal of friendship to Mrs. Bungay, which that honest woman refuse with scorn from the timid man. "she couldnot abide that Doolan," she say in confidence to her friend. indeed, all his flattery fail to win she.
as they talk, Mrs. Bungay survey mankind from her window, a magnificent vision of an enormous grey cab-horse appear, and near rapidly. a pair of white rein, hold by small white glove, be visible behind it; a face pale, but richly decorate with a chin-tuft, the head of an exiguous groom bob over the cab-head--these bright thing be reveal to the delighted Mrs. Bungay. "the honourable Percy Popjoy's quite punctual, I declare," she say, and sail to the door to be in wait at the nobleman's arrival.
"itbe Percy Popjoy," say Pen, look out of window, and see an individual, in extremely lacquered boot, descend from the swing cab: and, in fact, it be that young nobleman Lord Falconet's eld son, as we all very well know, who be come to dine with the publisher--his publisher of the row.
"he be my fag at Eton," Warrington say. "I ought to have lick he a little more." he and Pen have have some bout at the Oxbridge Union debate, in which Pen have have very much the well of Percy: who presently appear, with his hat under his arm, and a look of indescribable good-humour and fatuity in his round dimpled face: upon which Nature have burst out with a chin-tuft, but, exhaust with the effort, have leave the rest of the countenance bare of hair.
the temporary groom of the chamber bawl out, "the honourable Percy Popjoy," much to that gentleman's discomposure at hear his title announce.
"what do the man want to take away my hat for, Bungay?" he ask of the publisher. "cannot do without my hat--want it to make my bow to Mrs. Bungay. how well you look. Mrs. Bungay, to-day. havenot see your carriage in the Park: why havenot you be there? I miss you; indeed, I do."
"Ibe afraid yoube a sad quiz," say Mrs. Bungay.
"Quiz! never make a joke in my--hullo! whobe here? how d'ye do, Pendennis? how d'ye do, Warrington? these be old friend of mine, Mrs. Bungay. I say, how the doose do you come here?" he ask of the two young man, turnip his lacquered heel upon Mrs. Bungay, who respect her husband's two young guest, now that she find they be intimate with a lord's son.
"what! do they know he?" she ask rapidly of Mr. B.
"high feller, I tell you--the young one relate to all the nobility," say the publisher; and both run forward, smile and bow, to greet almost as great personage as the young lord--no less character, indeed, than the great Mr. Wenham and the great Mr. Wagg, who be now announce.
Mr. Wenham enter, wear the usual demure look and stealthy smile with which he commonly survey the tip of his neat little shin boot, and which he but seldom bring to bear upon the person who address he. Wagg's white waistcoat spread out, on the contrary, with profuse brilliancy; his burly, red face shine resplendent over it, light up with the thought of good joke and a good dinner. he like to make his entree into a drawing-room with a laugh, and, when he go away at night, to leave a joke explode behind he. no personal calamity or distress (of which that humourist have his share in common with the unjocular part of mankind) could altogether keep his humour down. whatever his grief might be, the thought of a dinner rally his great soul; and when he see a lord, he salute he with a pun.
Wenham go up, then, with a smug smile and whisper, to Mrs. Bungay, and look at she from under his eye, and show she the tip of his shoe. Wagg say she look charming, and push on straight at the young nobleman, whom he call Pop, and to whom he instantly relate a funny story, season with what the french call gro sel. he be delighted to see Pen, too, and shake hand with he, and slap he on the back cordially; for he be full of spirit and good-humour. and he talk in a loud voice about their last place and occasion of meeting at Baymouth; and ask how their friend of Clavering Park be, and whether Sir Francis be not come to London for the season; and whether Pen have be to see Lady Rockminster, who have arrive--fine old lady, Lady Rockminster! these remark Wagg make not for Pen's ear so much as for the edification of the company, whom he be glad to inform that he pay visit to gentleman's country seat, and be on intimate term with the nobility.
Wenham also shake hand with our young friend--all of which scene Mrs. Bungay remark with respectful pleasure, and communicate her idea to Bungay, afterwards, regard the importance of Mr. Pendennis--idea by which Pen profit much more than he be aware.
Pen, who have read, and rather admire some of her work (and expect to find in Miss Bunion a person somewhat resemble her own description of herself in the 'Passion-Flower,' in which she state that her youth resembled--
"a violet, shrink meanly when blow the March wind keenly; a timid fawn, on wild-wood lawn, where oak-boughs rustle greenly,--"
and that her mature beauty be something very different, certainly, to the artless loveliness of her prime, but still exceedingly captivate and striking), beheld, rather to his surprise and amusement, a large and bony woman in a crumpled satin dress, who come creak into the room with a step as heavy as a grenadier's. Wagg instantly note the straw which she bring in at the rumpled skirt of her dress, and would have stoop to pick it up: but Miss Bunion disarm all criticism by observe this ornament herself, and, put her own large foot upon it, so as to separate it from her robe, she stoop and pick up the straw, say to Mrs. Bungay, that she be very sorry to be a little late, but that the omnibus be very slow, and what a comfort it be to get a ride all the way from Brompton for sixpence. nobody laugh at the poetess's speech, it be utter so simply. indeed, the worthy woman have not the least notion of be ashamed of an action incidental upon her poverty.
"be that 'passion-flower?'" Pen say to Wenham, by whom he be stand. "why, her picture in the volume represent she as a very well-look young woman."
"you know passion-flower, like all other, will run to seed," Wenham say; "Miss Bunion's portrait be probably paint some year ago."
"well, I like she for not be ashamed of her poverty."
"so do I," say Mr. Wenham, who would have starve rather than have come to dinner in an omnibus, "but I donot think that she need flourish the straw about, do you, Mr. Pendennis? my dear Miss Bunion, how do you do? I be in a great lady's drawing-room this morning, and everybody be charm with your new volume. those line on the christening of Lady Fanny Fantail bring tear into the Duchess's eye. I say that I think I should have the pleasure of meet you to-day, and she beg I to thank you, and say how greatly she be pleased."
this history, tell in a bland smile manner, of a Duchess whom Wenham have meet that very morning, too, quite put poor Wagg's dowager and baronet out of court, and place Wenham beyond Wagg as a man of fashion. Wenham keep this inestimable advantage, and have the conversation to himself, run on with a number of anecdote regard the aristocracy. he try to bring Mr. Popjoy into the conversation by make appeal to he, and say, "I be tell your father this morning," or, "I think you be present at W. house the other night when the Duke say so-and-so," but Mr. Popjoy would not gratify he by join in the talk, prefer to fall back into the window recess with Mrs. Bungay, and watch the cab that drive up to the opposite door. at least, if he would not talk, the hostess hop that those odious Bacons would see how she have secure the noble Percy Popjoy for her party.
and now the bell of Saint Paul's toll half an hour later than that for which Mr. Bungay have invite his party, and it be complete with the exception of two guest, who at last make their appearance, and in whom Pen be pleased to recognise Captain and Mrs. Shandon.
when these two have make their greeting to the master and mistress of the house, and exchange nod of more or less recognition with most of the people present, Pen and Warrington go up, and shake hand very warmly with Mrs. Shandon, who, perhaps, be affect to meet they, and think where it be she have see they but a few day before. Shandon be brush up, and look pretty smart, in a red velvet waistcoat, and a frill, into which his wife have stick her good brooch. in spite of Mrs. Bungay's kindness, perhaps in consequence of it, Mrs. Shandon feel great terror and timidity in approach she: indeed, she be more awful than ever in her red satin and bird of paradise, and it be not until she have ask in her great voice about the dear little gurl, that the latter be somewhat encouraged, and venture to speak.
"nice-look woman," Popjoy whisper to Warrington. "do introduce I to Captain Shandon, Warrington. Ibe tell hebe a tremendous clever fellow; and, dammy, I adore intellect, by Jove I do!" this be the truth: Heaven have not endow young Mr. Popjoy with much intellect of his own, but have give he a generous faculty for admire, if not for appreciate, the intellect of other. "and introduce I to Miss Bunion. Ibe tell shebe very clever too. shebe rum to look at, certainly, but that donot matter. Dammy, I consider myself a literary man, and I wish to know all the clever fellow." so Mr. Popjoy and Mr. Shandon have the pleasure of become acquaint with one another; and now the door of the adjoining dining-room be fling open, the party enter and take their seat at table. Pen find himself next to bunion on one side, and to Mr. Wagg--the truth be, Wagg flee alarmed from the vacant place by the poetess, and Pen be compel to take it.
the gifted being do not talk much during dinner, but Pen remark that she eat with a vast appetite, and never refuse any of the supply of wine which be offer to she by the butler. indeed, Miss Bunion have consider Mr. Pendennis for a minute, who give himself rather grand air, and who be attire in an extremely fashionable style, with his very good chain, shirt stud, and cambric front, he be set down, and not without reason, as a prig by the poetess; who think it be much well to attend to her dinner than to take any notice of he. she tell he as much in after day with her usual candour. "I take you for one of the little Mayfair dandy," she say to Pen. "you look as solemn as a little undertaker; and as I dislike, beyond measure, the odious creature who be on the other side of I, I think it be good to eat my dinner and hold my tongue."
"and you do both very well, my dear Miss Bunion," Pen say with a laugh.
"well, so I do, but I intend to talk to you the next time a great deal: for you be neither so solemn, nor so stupid, nor so pert as you look."
"ah, Miss Bunion, how I pine for that 'next time' to come," Pen say with an air of comical gallantry:--but we must return to the day, and the dinner at Paternoster Row.
the repast be of the rich description--"What I call of the florid Gothic style," Wagg whisper to Penn, who sate beside the humourist, in his side-wing voice. the man in creak shoe and Berlin glove be numerous and solemn, carry on rapid conversation behind the guest, as they move to and fro with the dish. Doolan call out, "Waither," to one of they, and blush when he think of his blunder. Mrs. Bungay's footboy be lose amidst those large and black-coat attendant.
"look at that very bow-windowe man," Wagg say. "hebe an undertaker in Amen Corner, and attend funeral and dinner. cold meat and hot, donot you perceive? hebe the sham butler here, and I observe, my dear Mr. Pendennis, as you will through life, that wherever there be a sham butler at a London dinner there be sham wine--this sherry be filthy. Bungay, my boy, where do you get this delicious brown sherry?"
"Ibe glad you like it, Mr. Wagg; glass with you," say the publisher. "itbe some I get from Alderman Benning's store, and give a good figure for it, I can tell you. Mr. Pendennis, will you join we? your 'ealth, gentleman."
"the old rogue, where do he expect to go to? it come from the public-house," Wagg say. "it require two man to carry off that sherry, 'tis so uncommonly strong. I wish I have a bottle of old Steyne's wine here, Pendennis: your uncle and I have have many a one. he send it about to people where he be in the habit of dining. I remember at poor Rawdon Crawley's, Sir Pitt Crawley's brother--he be Governor of Coventry Island--Steyne's chef always come in the morning, and the butler arrive wit the champagne from Gaunt House, in the ice-pail ready."
"how good this be!" say Popjoy, good-naturedly. "you must have a cordon bleu in your kitchen."
"o yes," Mrs. Bungay say, think he speak of a jack-chain very likely.
"I mean a french chef," say the polite guest.
"o yes, your lordship," again say the lady.
"do your artist say hebe a Frenchman, Mrs. B.?" call out Wagg.
"well, Ibe sure I donot know," answer the publisher's lady.
"because, if he do, hebe a quizzin yer," cry Mr. Wagg; but nobody see the pun, which disconcert somewhat the bashful punster. "the dinner be from Griggs, in St. Paul's Churchyard; so be Bacon's," he whisper Pen. "Bungay write to give half-a-crown a head more than Bacon, so do Bacon. they would poison each other's ice if they could get near they; and as for the make-dish--they be poison. this--hum--ha--this Brimborion a la Sevigne be delicious, Mrs. B.," he say, help himself to a dish which the undertaker hand to he.
"well, Ibe glad you like it," Mrs. Bungay answer, blush and not know whether the name of the dish be actually that which Wagg give to it, but dimly conscious that that individual be quiz she. accordingly she hat Mr. Wagg with female ardour; and would have depose he from his command over Mr. Bungay's periodical, but that his name be great in the trade, and his reputation in the land considerable.
by the displacement of person, Warrington have find himself on the right hand of Mrs. Shandon, who sate in plain black silk and fade ornament by the side of the florid publisher. the sad smile of the lady move his rough heart to pity. nobody seem to interest himself about she: she sate look at her husband, who himself seem rather abash in the presence of some of the company. Wenham and Wagg both know he and his circumstance. he have work with the latter, and be immeasurably his superior in wit, genius, and acquirement; but Wagg's star be brilliant in the world, and poor Shandon be unknown there. he could not speak before the noisy talk of the coarser and more successful man; but drink his wine in silence, and as much of it as the people would give he. he be under surveillance. Bungay have warn the undertaker not to fill the Captain's glass too often or too full. it be a melancholy precaution that, and the more melancholy that it be necessary. Mrs. Shandon, too, cast alarmed glance across the table to see that her husband do not exceed.
abash by the failure of his first pun, for he be impudent and easily disconcert, Wagg keep his conversation pretty much to Pen during the rest of dinner, and of course chiefly speak about their neighbour. "this be one of Bungay's grand field-day," he say. "we be all Bungavians here.--Did you read Popjoy's novel? it be an old magazine story write by poor Buzzard year ago, and forget here until Mr. Trotter (that be Trotter with the large shirt collar) fish it out and bethink he that it be applicable to the late elopement; so Bob write a few chapter a propos--Popjoy permit the use of his name, and I dare say supply a page here and there--and 'desperation, or the Fugitive Duchess' make its appearance. the great fun be to examine Popjoy about his own work, of which he donot know a word.--i say, Popjoy, what a capital passage that be in Volume three,--where the Cardinal in disguise, after be convert by the Bishop of London, propose marriage to the Duchess's daughter."
"Glad you like it," Popjoy answer; "itbe a favourite bit of my own."
"therebe no such thing in the whole book," whisper Wagg to Pen. "invent it myself. Gad! it wouldnot be a bad plot for a high-church novel."
"I remember poor Byron, Hobhouse, Trelawney, and myself, din with Cardinal Mezzocaldo at Rome," Captain Sumph begin, "and we have some Orvieto wine for dinner, which Byron like very much. and I remember how the Cardinal regret that he be a single man. we go to Civita Vecchia two day afterwards, where Byron's yacht be--and, by Jove, the Cardinal die within three week; and Byron be very sorry, for he rather like he."
"a devilish interesting story, Sumph, indeed," Wagg say.
"you should publish some of those story, Captain Sumph, you really should. such a volume would make our friend Bungay's fortune," Shandon say.
"why donot you ask Sumph to publish them in your new paper--the what-d'ye-call-'em--hay, Shandon?" bawl out Wagg.
"why donot you ask he to publish them in your old magazine, the Thingumbob?" Shandon reply.
"be there go to be a new paper?" ask Wenham, who know perfectly well, but be ashamed of his connection with the press.
"Bungay go to bring out a paper?" cry Popjoy, who, on the contrary, be proud of his literary reputation and acquaintance. "you must employ I. Mrs. Bungay, use your influence with he, and make he employ I. prose or verse--what shall it be? novel, poem, travel, or lead article, begad. anything or everything--only let Bungay pay I, and Ibe ready--I be now my dear Mrs. Bungay, begad now."
"itbe to be call the Small Beer Chronicle," growl Wagg, "and little Popjoy be to be engage for the infantine department."
"it be to be call the Pall Mall Gazette, sir, and we shall be very happy to have you with we," Shandon say.
"Pall Mall Gazette--why Pall Mall Gazette?" ask Wagg.
"because the editor be bear at Dublin, the sub-editor at Cork, because the proprietor live in Paternoster Row;--and the paper be publish in Catherine Street, Strand. willnot that reason suffice you, Wagg?" Shandon say; he be get rather angry. "everything must have a name. my dog Ponto have get a namee. youhave get a name, and a name which you deserve, more or less, indeed. why d'ye grudge the name to our paper?"
"by any other name it would smell as sweet," say Wagg.
"Iwill have ye remember its namebe not what-d'ye-call-'em, Mr. Wagg," say Shandon. "you know its name well enough, and--and you know mine."
"and I know your address too," say Wagg; but this be speak in an undertone, and the good-natured Irishman be appease almost in an instant after his ebullition of spleen, and ask Wagg to drink wine with he in a friendly voice.
when the lady retire from the table, the talk grow louder still; and presently Wenham, in a courtly speech, propose that everybody should drink to the health of the new Journal, eulogise highly the talent, wit, and learning of its editor, Captain Shandon. it be his maxim never to lose the support of a newspaper man, and in the course of that evening he go round and salute every literary gentleman present with a privy compliment specially address to he; inform this one how great an impression have be make in Downing Street by his last article, and tell that one how profoundly his good friend, the Duke of so-and-so, have be strike by the ability of the late number.
the evening come to a close, and in spite of all the precaution to the contrary, poor Shandon reel in his walk, and go home to his new lodging, with his faithful wife by his side, and the cabman on his box jeering at he. Wenham have a chariot of his own, which he put at Popjoy's seat; and the timid Miss Bunion see Mr. Wagg, who be her neighbour, about to depart, insist upon a seat in his carriage, much to that gentleman's discomfiture.
Pen and Warrington walk home together in the moonlight. "and now," Warrington say, "that you have see the man of letter, tell I, be I far wrong in say that there be thousand of people in this town, who donot write book, who be, to the full, as clever and intellectual as people who do?"
Pen be force to confess that the literary personage with whom he have become acquainted have not say much, in the course of the night's conversation, that be worthy to be remember or quote. in fact not one word about literature have be say during the whole course of the night:--and it may be whisper to those uninitiated people who be anxious to know the habit and make the acquaintance of man of letter, that there be no race of people who talk about book, or, perhaps, who read book, so little as literary man.
CHAPTER xxxvi
the Pall Mall Gazette
considerable success at first attend the new journal. it be generally state, that an influential political party support the paper; and great name be cite amongst the contributor to its column. be there any foundation for these rumour? we be not at liberty to say whether they be ill-found; but this much we may divulge, that an article upon foreign policy, which be generally attribute to a noble Lord, whose connexion with the Foreign Office be very well know, be in reality compose by Captain Shandon, in the parlour of the Bear and Staff public-house near Whitehall Stairs, whither the printer's boy have track he, and where a literary ally of his, Mr. Bludyer, have a temporary residence; and that a series of paper on finance question, which be universally suppose to be write by a great Statesman of the House of Commons, be in reality compose by Mr. George Warrington of the Upper Temple.
that there may have be some dealing between the Pall Mall Gazette and this influential party, be very possible, Percy Popjoy (whose father, Lord Falconet, be a member of the party) might be see not unfrequently ascend the stair to Warrington's chamber; and some information appear in the paper which it give a character, and could only be get from very peculiar source. several poem, feeble in thought, but loud and vigorous in expression, appear in the Pall Mall Gazette, with the signature of "P. P."; and it must be own that his novel be praise in the new journal in a very outrageous manner.
