in the political department of the paper Mr. Pen do not take any share; but he be a most active literary contributor. the Pall Mall Gazette have its office, as we have hear, in Catherine Street, in the Strand, and hither Pen often come with his manuscript in his pocket, and with a great deal of bustle and pleasure; such as a man feel at the outset of his literary career, when to see himself in print be still a novel sensation, and he yet please himself to think that his writing be create some noise in the world.
here it be that Mr. Jack Finucane, the sub-editor, compile with paste and scissor the Journal of which he be supervisor. with an eagle eye he scan all the paragraph of all the newspaper which have anything to do with the world of fashion over which he preside. he donot let a death or a dinner-party of the aristocracy pass without have the event record in the column of his Journal; and from the most recondite provincial print, and distant Scotch and irish newspaper, he fish out astonishing paragraph and intelligence regard the upper class of society. it be a grand, nay, a touching sight, for a philosopher, to see Jack Finucane, Esquire, with a plate of meat from the cookshop and glass of porter from the public-house, for his meal, recount the feast of the great as if h have be present at they; and in tattered trouser and dingy shirt-sleeve, cheerfully describe and arrange the most brilliant fete of the world of fashion. the incongruity of Finucane's avocation, and his manner and appearance amuse his new friend Pen. since he leave his own native village, where his rank probably be not very, lofty Jack have seldom see any society but such as use the parlour of the tavern which he frequent, whereas from his writing you would have suppose that he din with ambassador, and that his common lounge be the bow-window of White's. error of description, it be true, occasionally slip from his pen; but the Ballinafad Sentinel, of which he be own correspondent, suffer by these, not the Pall Mall Gazette, in which Jack be not permit to write much, his London chief think that the scissor and the paste be well wield by he than the pen.
Pen take a great deal of pain with the writing of his review, and have a pretty fair share of desultory reading, acquire in the early year of his life an eager fancy and a keen sense of fun, his article please his chief and the public, and he be proud to think that he deserve the money which he earn. we may be sure that the Pall Mall Gazette be take in regularly at Fairoaks, and read with delight by the two lady there. it be receive at Clavering Park, too, where we know there be a young lady of great literary taste; and old Doctor Portman himself, to whom the widow send her paper after she have get her son's article by heart, signify his approval of Pen's production, say that the lad have spirit, taste, and fancy, and write, if not like a scholar, at any rate like a gentleman.
and what be the astonishment and delight of our friend Major Pendennis, on walk into one of his club, the Regent, where Wenham, Lord Falconet, and some other gentleman of good reputation and fashion be assemble, to hear they one day talk over a number of the Pall Mall Gazette, and of an article which appear in its column, make some bitter fun of the book recently publish by the wife of a celebrated member of the opposition party. the book in question be a Book of Travels in Spain and Italy, by the Countess of Muffborough, in which it be difficult to say which be the most wonderful, the French or the English, in which language her ladyship write indifferently, and upon the blunder of which the critic pounce with delightful mischief. the critic be no other than Pen: he jump and dance round about his subject with the great jocularity and high spirit: he show up the noble lady's fault with admirable mock gravity and decorum. there be not a word in the article which be not polite and gentlemanlike; and the unfortunate subject of the criticism be scarify and laugh at during the operation. Wenham's bilious countenance be pucker up with malign pleasure as he read the critique. Lady Muffborough have not ask he to her party during the last year. Lord Falconet giggle and laugh with all his heart; Lord Muffborough and he have be rival ever since they begin life; and these compliment Major Pendennis, who until now have scarcely pay any attention to some hint which his Fairoaks correspondence throw out of "dear Arthur's constant and severe literary occupation, which I fear may undermine the poor boy's health," and have think any notice of Mr. Pen and his newspaper connexion quite below his dignity as a Major and a gentleman.
but when the oracular Wenham praise the boy's production; when Lord Falconet, who have have the news from Percy Popjoy, approve of the genius of young Pen; when the great Lord Steyne himself, to whom the Major refer the article, laugh and snigger over it, swear it be capital, and that the Muffborough would writhe under it, like a whale under a harpoon, the Major, as in duty bind, begin to admire his nephew very much, say, "by gad, the young rascal have some stuff in he, and would do something; he have always say he would do something;" and with a hand quite tremulous with pleasure, the old gentleman sate down to write to the widow at Fairoaks all that the great folk have say in praise of Pen; and he write to the young rascal, too, ask when he would come and eat a chop with his old uncle, and say that he be commission to take he to dinner at Gaunt House, for Lord Steyne like anybody who could entertain he, whether by his folly, wit, or by his dulness, by his oddity, affectation, good spirit, or any other quality. Pen fling his letter across the table to Warrington: perhaps he be disappoint that the other do not seem to be much affect by it.
the courage of young critic be prodigious: they clamber up to the judgment-seat, and, with scarce a hesitation, give their opinion upon work the most intricate or profound. have Macaulay's History or Herschel's astronomy be put before Pen at this period, he would have look through the volume, meditate his opinion over a cigar, and signify his august approval of either author, as if the critic have be their bear superior and indulgent master and patron. by the help of the Biographie Universelle or the British Museum, he would be able to take a rapid resume of a historical period, and allude to name, date, and fact, in such a masterly, easy way, as to astonish his mamma at home, who wonder where her boy could have acquire such a prodigious store of reading and himself, too, when he come to read over his article two or three month after they have be compose, and when he have forget the subject and the book which he have consult. at that period of his life, Mr. Pen own that he would not have hesitate, at twenty-four hour' notice, to pass his opinion upon the great scholar, or to give a judgment upon the Encyclopaedia. luckily he have Warrington to laugh at he and to keep down his impertinence by a constant and wholesome ridicule, or he might have become conceited beyond all sufferance; for Shandon like the dash and flippancy of his young aide-de-camp, and be, indeed, well pleased with Pen's light and brilliant flash, than with the heavy metal which his eld coadjutor bring to bear.
but though he might justly be blame on the score of impertinence and a certain prematurity of judgment, Mr. Pen be a perfectly honest critic; a great deal too candid for Mr. Bungay's purpose, indeed, who grumble sadly at his impartiality. Pen and his chief, the Captain, have a dispute upon this subject one day. "in the name of common-sense, Mr. Pendennis," Shandon ask, "what have you be do--praise one of Mr. Bacon's book? Bungay have be with I in a fury this morning at see a laudatory article upon one of the work of the odious firm over the way."
Pen's eye open with wide astonishment. "do you mean to say," he ask, "that we be to praise no book that Bacon publish: or that, if the book be good, we be to say they be bad?"
"my good young friend--for what do you suppose a benevolent publisher undertake a critical journal, to benefit his rival?" Shandon inquire.
"to benefit himself certainly, but to tell the truth too," Pen say, "ruat coelum, to tell the truth."
"and my prospectus," say Shandon, with a laugh and a sner; "do you consider that be a work of mathematical accuracy of statement?"
"pardon I, that be not the question," Pen say "and I donot think you very much care to argue it. I have some qualm of conscience about that same prospectus, and debate the matter with my friend Warrington. we agree, however," Pen say, laugh "that because the prospectus be rather declamatory and poetical, and the giant be paint upon the show-board rather large than the original, who be inside the caravan; we need not be too scrupulous about this trifle inaccuracy, but might do our part of the show, without loss of character or remorse of conscience. we be the fiddler, and play our tune only; you be the showman."
"and leader of the van," say Shandon. "well, I be glad that your conscience give you leave to play for we."
"yes, but," say Pen, with a fine sense of the dignity of his position, "we be all party man in England, and I will stick to my party like a Briton. I will be as good-natured as you like to our own side, he be a fool who quarrel with his own nest; and I will hit the enemy as hard as you like--but with fair play, Captain, if you please. one cannot tell all the truth, I suppose; but one can tell nothing but the truth; and I would rather starve, by Jove, and never earn another penny by my pen" (this redoubte instrument have now be in use for some six week, and Pen speak of it with vast enthusiasm and respect) "than strike an opponent an unfair blow, or, if call upon to place he, rank he below his honest desert."
"well, Mr. Pendennis, when we want Bacon smash, we must get some other hammer to do it," Shandon say, with fatal good-nature; and very likely thought within himself, "a few year hence perhaps the young gentleman willnot be so squeamish." the veteran Condottiere himself be no long so scrupulous. he have fight and kill on so many a side for many a year past, that remorse have long leave he. "Gad," say he, "youhave a tender conscience, Mr. Pendennis. itbe the luxury of all novice, and I may have have one once myself; but that sort of bloom wear off with the rubbing of the world, and Ibe not go to the trouble myself of put on an artificial complexion, like our pious friend Wenham, or our model of virtue, Wagg."
"I donot know whether some people's hypocrisy be not well, Captain, than other's cynicism."
"itbe more profitable, at any rate," say the Captain, bite his nail. "that Wenham be as dull a quack as ever quack: and you see the carriage in which he drive to dinner. faith, itwill be a long time before Mrs. Shandon will take a drive in her own chariot. God help she, poor thing!" and Pen go away from his chief, after their little dispute and colloquy, point his own moral to the Captain's tale, and think to himself, "behold this man, store with genius, wit, learning, and a hundred good natural gift: see how he have wreck they, by palter with his honesty, and forget to respect himself. wilt thou remember thyself, o Pen? thou art conceite enough! wilt thou sell thy honour for a bottle? no, by heaven's grace, we will be honest, whatever befall, and our mouth shall only speak the truth when they open."
a punishment, or, at least, a trial, be in store for Mr. Pen. in the very next number of the Pall Mall Gazette, Warrington read out, with roar of laughter, an article which by no mean amuse Arthur Pendennis, who be himself at work with a criticism for the next week's number of the same journal; and in which the Spring Annual be ferociously maltreat by some unknown writer. the person of all most cruelly maul be Pen himself. his verse have not appear with his own name in the Spring Annual, but under an assumed signature. as he have refuse to review the book, Shandon have hand it over to Mr. Bludyer, with direction to that author to dispose of it. and he have do so effectually. Mr. Bludyer, who be a man of very considerable talent, and of a race which, I believe, be quite extinct in the press of our time, have a certain notoriety in his profession, and reputation for savage humour. he smash and trample down the poor spring flower with no more mercy than a bull would have on a parterre; and have cut up the volume to his heart's content, go and sell it at a bookstall, and purchase a pint of brandy with the proceed of the volume.
CHAPTER XXXVII
where Pen appear in Town and Country
let we be allow to pass over a few month of the history of Mr. Arthur Pendennis's lifetime, during the which, many event may have occur which be more interesting and exciting to himself, than they would be likely to prove to the reader of his present memoir. we leave he, in his last chapter, regularly enter upon his business as a professional writer, or literary hack, as Mr. Warrington choose to style himself and his friend; and we know how the life of any hack, legal or literary, in a curacy, or in a march regiment, or at a merchant's desk, be dull of routine, and tedious of description. one day's labour resemble another much too closely. a literary man have often to work for his bread against time, or against his will, or in spite of his health, or of his indolence, or of his repugnance to the subject on which he be call to exert himself, just like any other daily toiler. when you want to make money by Pegasus (as he must, perhaps, who have no other saleable property), farewell poetry and aerial flight: Pegasus only rise now like Mr. Green's balloon, at period advertise beforehand, and when the spectator's money have be pay. pegasus trot in harness, over the stony pavement, and pull a cart or a cab behind he. often Pegasus do his work with pant side and tremble knee, and not seldom get a cut of the whip from his driver.
do not let we, however, be too prodigal of our pity upon Pegasus. there be no reason why this animal should be exempt from labour, or illness, or decay, any more than any of the other creature of God's world. if he get the whip, Pegasus often deserve it, and I for one be quite ready to protest my friend, George Warrington, against the doctrine which poetical sympathiser be inclined to put forward, viz., that of letter, and what be call genius, be to be exempt from prose duty of this daily, bread-wanting, tax-pay life, and not to be make to work and pay like their neighbour.
well, then, the Pall Mall Gazette be duly establish and Arthur Pendennis's merit recognise as a flippant, witty, and amusing critic, he work away hard every week, prepare review of such work as come into his department, and write his review with flippancy certainly, but with honesty, and to the good of his power. it might be that a historian of threescore, who have spend a quarter of a century in compose a work of which our young gentleman dispose in the course of a couple of day' reading at the British Museum, be not altogether fairly treat by such a facile critic; or that a poet who have be elaborate sublime sonnet and ode until he think they fit for the public and for fame, be annoy by two or three dozen pert line in Mr. Pen's review, in which the poet's claim be settle by the critic, as if the latter be my lord on the bench and the author a miserable little suitor tremble before he. the actor at the theatre complain of he wofully, too, and very likely he be too hard upon they. but there be not much harm do after all. it be different now, as we know; but there be so few great historian, or great poet, or great actor, in Pen's time, that scarce any at all come up for judgment his critical desk. those who get a little whipping, get what in the main be good for they; not that the judge be any well or wise than the person whom he sentence, or indeed ever fancy himself so. Pen have a strong sense of humour and justice, and have not therefore an overweening respect for his own work; besides, he have his friend Warrington at his elbow--a terrible critic if the young man be dispose to be conceite, and more savage over Pen than ever he be to those whom he try at his literary assize.
by these critical labour, and by occasional contribution to lead article of the journal, when, without wound his paper, this eminent publicist could conscientiously speak his mind, Mr. Arthur Pendennis gain the sum of four pound four shilling weekly, and with no small pain and labour. likewise be furnish Magazines and Reviews with article of his composition, and be believe to have be (though on this score he never choose to speak) London correspondent of the Chatteris Champion, which at that time contain some very brilliant and eloquent letter from the metropolis. by these labour the fortunate youth be enable to earn a sum very nearly equal to four hundred pound a year; and on the second Christmas after his arrival in London, he actually bring a hundred pound to his mother, as a dividend upon the debt which he owe to Laura. that Mrs. Pendennis read every word of her son's work, and consider he to be the profound thinker and most elegant writer of the day; that she think his retribution of the hundred pound an act of angelic virtue; that she fear he be ruin his health by his labour, and be delighted when he tell she of the society which he meet, and of the great man of letter and fashion whom he see, will be imagine by all reader who have see son-worship amongst mother, and that charming simplicity of love with which woman in the country watch the career of their darling in London. if John have hold such and such a brief; if Tom have be invite to such and such a ball; or George have meet this or that great and famous man at dinner; what a delight there be in the heart of mother and sister at home in Somersetshire! how young Hopeful's letter be read and remember! what a theme for village talk they give, and friendly congratulation! in the second winter, Pen come for a very brief space, and cheer the widow's heart, and lighten up the lonely house at Fairoaks. Helen have her son all to herself; Laura be away on a visit to old Lady Rockminster; the folk of Clavering Park be absent; the very few old friend of the house, Doctor Portman at their head, call upon Mr. Pen, and treat he with marked respect; between mother and son, it be all fondness, confidence, and affection. it be the happy fortnight of the widow's whole life; perhaps in the life of both of they. the holiday be go only too quickly; and Pen be back in the busy world, and the gentle widow alone again. she send Arthur's money to Laura: I donot know why this young lady take the opportunity of leave home when Pen be come thither, or whether he be the more pique or relieve by her absence.
he be by this time, by his own merit and his uncle's introduction, pretty well introduce into London, and know both in literary and polite circle. amongst the former his fashionable reputation stand he in no little stead; he be consider to be a gentleman of good present mean and well expectation, who write for his pleasure, than which there cannot be a great recommendation to a young literary aspirant. Bacon, Bungay and Co. be proud to accept his article; Mr. Wenham ask he to dinner; Mr. Wagg look upon he with a favourable eye; and they report how they meet he at the house of person of fashion, amongst whom he be pretty welcome, as they do not trouble themselves about his mean, present or future; as his appearance and address be good; and as he have get a character for be a clever fellow. finally, he be ask to one house, because he be see at another house: and thus no small variety of London life be present to the young man: he be make familiar with all sort of people from Paternoster Row to Pimlico, and be as much at home at Mayfair dining-table as at those tavern board where some of his companion of the pen be accustom to assemble.
full of high spirit and curiosity, easily adapt himself to all whom he meet, the young fellow please himself in this strange variety and jumble of man, and make himself welcome, or at ease at least, wherever he go. he would breakfast, for instance, at Mr. Plover's of a morning, in company with a peer, a Bishop, a parliamentary orator, two blue lady of fashion, a popular preacher, the author of the last new novel, and the very late lion import from Egypt or from America: and would quit this distinguished society for the back room at the newspaper office, where pen and ink and the wet proof-sheet be await he. here would be Finucane, the sub-editor, with the last news from the row: and Shandon would come in presently, and give a nod to Pen, would begin scribble his lead article at the other end of the table, flank by the pint of sherry, which, when the attendant boy behold he, be always silently bring for the Captain: or Mr. Bludyer's roar voice would be hear in the front room, where that truculent critic would impound the book on the counter in spite of the timid remonstrance of Mr. Midge, the publisher, and after look through the volume would sell they at his accustomed bookstall, and have drunken and din upon the produce of the sale in a tavern box, would call for ink and paper, and proceed to "smash" the author of his dinner and the novel. towards evening Mr. Pen would stroll in the direction of his club, and take up Warrington there for a constitutional walk. this exercise free the lung, and give an appetite for dinner, after which Pen have the privilege to make his bow at some very pleasant house which be open to he; or the town before he for amusement. there be the Opera; or the Eagle Tavern; or a ball to go to in Mayfair; or a quiet night with a cigar and a book and a long talk with Warrington; or a wonderful new song at the Back Kitchen;--at this time of his life Mr. Pen beheld all sort of place and man; and very likely do not know how much he enjoy himself until long after, when ball give he no pleasure, neither do farce make he laugh; nor do the tavern joke produce the least excitement in he; nor do the lovely dancer that ever show her ankle cause he to stir from his chair after dinner. at his present mature age all these pleasure be over: and the time have pass away too. it be but a very very few year since--but the time be go, and most of the man. Bludyer will no more bully author or cheat landlord of their score. Shandon, the learn and thriftless, the witty and unwise, sleep his last sleep. they bury honest Doolan the other day: never will he cringe or flatter, never pull long-bow or empty whisky-noggin any more.
the London season be now bloom in its full vigour, and the fashionable newspaper abound with information regard the grand banquet, rout, and ball which be enliven the polite world. our gracious Sovereign be hold levee and drawing-room at St. James's: the bow-window of the club be crowd with the head of respectable red-faced newspaper-read gentleman: along the Serpentine trail thousand of carriage: squadron of dandy horseman trample over Rotten row, everybody be in town, in a word; and of course Major Arthur Pendennis, who be somebody, be not absent.
with his head tie up in a smart bandana handkerchief and his meagre carcass envelop in a brilliant turkish dressing-gown, the worthy gentleman sate on a certain morning by his fireside let his foot gently simmer in a bath, whilst he take his early cup of tea, and peruse his Morning Post. he could not have face the day without his two hour' toilet, without his early cup of tea, without his Morning Post. I suppose nobody in the world except Morgan, not even Morgan's master himself, know how feeble and ancient the Major be grow, and what numberless little comfort he require.
if man sneer, as our habit be, at the artifice of an old beauty, at her paint, perfume, ringlet; at those innumerable, and to we unknown, stratagem with which she be say to remedy the ravage of time and reconstruct the charm whereof year have bereft she; the lady, it be to be presume, be not on their side altogether ignorant that man be vain as well as they, and that the toilet of old buck be to the full as elaborate as their own. how be it that old Blushington keep that constant little rose-tint on his cheek; and where do old Blondel get the preparation which make his silver hair pass for golden? have you ever see Lord Hotspur get off his horse when he think nobody be look? take out of his stirrup, his shiny boot can hardly totter up the step of Hotspur House. he be a dash young nobleman still as you see the back of he in Rotten row; when you behold he on foot, what an old, old fellow! do you ever form to yourself any idea of Dick Lacy (Dick have be Dick these sixty year) in a natural state, and without his stay? all these man be object whom the observer of human life and manner may contemplate with as much profit as the most elderly Belgravian Venus, or inveterate Mayfair Jezebel. an old reprobate daddy-longleg, who have never say his prayer (except perhaps in public) these fifty year: an old buck who still cling to as many of the habit of youth as his feeble grasp of health can hold by: who have give up the bottle, but sit with young fellow over it, and tell naughty story upon toast-and-water--who have give up beauty, but still talk about it as wickedly as the young roue in company--such an old fellow, I say, if any parson in Pimlico or St. James's be to order the beadle to bring he into the middle aisle, and there set he in an armchair, and make a text of he, and preach about he to the congregation, could be turn to a wholesome use for once in his life, and might be surprised to find that some good thought come out of he. but we be wander from our text, the honest Major, who sit all this while with his foot cool in the bath: Morgan take they out of that place of purification, and dry they daintily, and proceed to set the old gentleman on his leg, with waistband and wig, starched cravat, and spotless boot and glove.
it be during these hour of the toilet that Morgan and his employer have their confidential conversation, for they do not meet much at other time of the day--the major abhor the society of his own chair and table in his lodging; and Morgan, his master's toilet over and letter deliver, have his time very much on his own hand.
this spare time the active and well-mannered gentleman bestow among the valet and butler of the nobility, his acquaintance; and Morgan Pendennis, as he be style, for, by such compound name, gentleman's gentleman be call in their private circle, be a frequent and welcome guest at some of the very high table in this town. he be a member of two influential club in Mayfair and Pimlico; and he be thus enable to know the whole gossip of the town, and entertain his master very agreeably during the two hour' toilet conversation. he know a hundred tale and legend regard person of the very high ton, whose valet canvass their august secret, just, my dear Madam, as our own parlour-maid and dependant in the kitchen discuss our character, our stinginess and generosity, our pecuniary mean or embarrassment, and our little domestic or connubial tiff and quarrel. if I leave this manuscript open on my table, I have not the slight doubt Betty will read it, and they will talk it over in the low region to-night; and to-morrow she will bring in my breakfast with a face of such entire imperturbable innocence, that no mortal could suppose she guilty of play the spy. if you and the Captain have high word upon any subject, which be just possible, the circumstance of the quarrel, and the character of both of you, will be discuss with impartial eloquence over the kitchen tea-table; and if Mrs. Smith's maid should by chance be take a dish of tea with your, her presence will not undoubtedly act as a restraint upon the discussion in question; her opinion will be give with candour; and the next day her mistress will probably know that Captain and Mrs. Jones have be a quarrelling as usual. nothing be secret. take it as a rule that John know everything: and as in our humble world so in the great: a duke be no more a hero to his valet-de-chambre than you or I; and his grace's Man at his club, in company doubtless with other Men of equal social rank, talk over his master's character and affair with the ingenuous truthfulness which befit gentleman who be meet together in confidence. who be a niggard and screw up his money-box: who be in the hand of the money-lender, and be put his noble name on the back of bill of exchange: who be intimate with whose wife: who want whom to marry her daughter, and which he willnot, no not at any price:--all these fact gentleman's confidential gentleman discuss confidentially, and be know and examine by every person who have any claim to rank in genteel society. in a word, if old Pendennis himself be say to know everything, and be at once admirably scandalous and delightfully discreet; it be but justice to Morgan to say, that a great deal of his master's information be supply to that worthy man by his valet, who go out and forage knowledge for he. indeed, what more effectual plan be there to get a knowledge of London society, than to begin at the foundation--that be, at the kitchen floor?
so Mr. Morgan and his employer converse as the latter's toilet proceed. there have be a drawing-room on the previous day, and the major read among the presentation that of Lady Clavering by Lady Rockminster, and of Miss Amory by her mother Lady Clavering,--and in a further part of the paper their dress be describe, with a precision and in a jargon which will puzzle and amuse the antiquary of future generation. the sight of these name carry Pendennis back to the country. "how long have the Claverings be in London?" he ask; "pray, Morgan, have you see any of their people?"
"Sir Francis have send away his fore man, sir," Mr. Morgan reply; "and have take a friend of mine as own man, sir. indeed he apply on my reckmendation. you may recklect Towler, sir,--tall red-air man--but dye his air. be groom of the chamber in Lord Levant's family till his Lordship break hup. itbe a fall for Towler, sir; but pore man cannot be particklar," say the valet, with a pathetic voice.
"devilish hard on Towler, by gad!" say the Major, amuse, "and not pleasant for Lord Levant--he, he!"
"always know it be come, sir. I speak to you of it Michaelmas be four year: when her ladyship put the diamond in pawn. it be Towler, sir, take them in two cab to Dobree's--and a good deal of the plate go the same way. donot you remember see of it at Blackwall, with the levant arm and coronick, and Lord Levant settn oppsit to it at the Marquis of Steyne's dinner? beg your pardon; do I cut you, sir?" Morgan be now operate upon the Major's chin--he continue the theme while strap the skilful razor. "theyhave take a house in Grosvenor Place, and be come out strong, sir. her ladyshipbe go to give three party, besides a dinner a week, sir. her fortune willnot stand it--can't stand it."
"Gad, she have a devilish good cook when I be at Fairoaks," the Major say, with very little compassion for the widow Amory's fortune.
"Marobblan be his name, sir; Marobblan's go away, sir," Morgan said,-- and the Major, this time, with hearty sympathy, say, "he be devilish sorry to lose he."
"therebe be a tremenjuous row about that Mosseer Marobblan," Morgan continue "at a ball at Baymouth, sir, bless his impadence, he challenge Mr. Harthur to fight a jewel, sir, which Mr. Arthur be very near knock he down, and pitchin' him outawind, and serve he right; but Chevalier Strong, sir, come up and stop the shindy--I beg pardon, the holtercation, sir--they french cook have as much pride and hinsolence as if they be real gentleman."
"I hear something of that quarrel," say the Major; "but Mirobolant be not turn off for that?"
"no, sir--that affair, sir, which Mr. Harthur forgive it he and beayve most handsome, be hush hup: it be about Miss Hamory, sir, that he ad be dismissial. those french feller, they fancy everybody be in love with them; and he climb up the large grape vine to her winder, sir, and be a try to get in, when he be catch, sir; and Mr. Strong come out, and they get the garden-engine and play on he, and there be no end of a row, sir."
"confound his impudence! you donot mean to say Miss Amory encourage he," cry the Major, amazed at a peculiar expression in Mr. Morgan's countenance.
Morgan resume his imperturbable demeanour. "know nothing about it, sir. servant donot know they kind of thing the least. most probbly there be nothing in it--so many lie be tell about family--Marobblan go away, bag and baggage, saucepan, and pianna, and all--the feller ad a pianna, and write potry in French, and he take a lodging at Clavering, and he hanker about the primise, and it be say that Madam Fribsy, the milliner, bring letter to Miss Hamory, though I donot believe a word about it; nor that he try to pison hisself with charcoal, which it be all a humbug betwigst he and Madam Fribsy; and he be nearly shoot by the keeper in the park."
in the course of that very day, it chance that the Major have station himself in the great window of Bays's Club in Saint James's Street, at the hour in the afternoon when you see a half-score of respectable old buck similarly recreate themselves (Bays's be rather an old-fashioned place of resort now, and many of its member more than middle-aged; but in the time of the Prince Regent, these old fellow occupy the same window, and be some of the very great dandy in this empire)--major Pendennis be look from the great window, and spy his nephew Arthur walk down the street in company with his friend Mr. Popjoy.
"look!" say Popjoy to Pen, as they pass, "do you ever pass Bays's at four o'clock, without see that collection of old fogy? itbe a regular museum. they ought to be cast in wax, and set up at Madame Tussaud's--"
"--in a chamber of old horror by themselves," Pen say, laugh.
"--in the chamber of horror! Gad, doosid good!" pop cry. they be old rogue, most of them, and no mistake. therebe old Blondel; therebe my Uncle Colchicum, the most confound old sinner in Europe; there's-- hullo! therebe somebody rap the window and nod at we."
"itbe my uncle, the Major," say Pen. "be he an old sinner too?"
"notorious old rogue," pop say, wag his head. ("notowious old wogue," he pronounce the word, thereby render they much more emphatic.)--"he's beckoning you in; he want to speak to you."
"come in too," Pen say.
"--can't," reply the other. "Cut uncle Col. two year ago, about Mademoiselle Frangipane--Ta, ta," and the young sinner take leave of Pen, and the club of the eld criminal, and saunter into Blacquiere's, an adjacent establishment, frequent by reprobate of his own age.
Colchicum, Blondel, and the senior buck have just be converse about the Clavering family, whose appearance in London have form the subject of Major Pendennis's morning conversation with his valet. Mr. Blondel's house be next to that of Sir Francis Clavering, in Grosvenor Place: give very good dinner himself, he have remark some activity in his neighbour's kitchen. Sir Francis, indeed, have a new chef, who have come in more than once and dress Mr. Blondel's dinner for he; that gentleman have only a remarkably expert female artist permanently engage in his establishment, and employ such chief of note as happen to be free on the occasion of his grand banquet. "they go to a devilish expense and see devilish bad company as yet, I hear," Mr. Blondel say, "they scour the street, by gad, to get people to dine with them. Champignon say it break his heart to serve up a dinner to their society. what a shame it be that those low people should have money at all," cry Mr. Blondel, whose grandfather have be a reputable leather-breech maker, and whose father have lend money to the Princes.
"I wish I have fall in with the widow myself" sigh Lord Colchicum, "and not be lay up with that confound gout at Leghorn--I would have marry the woman myself.--I'm tell she have six hundred thousand pound in the Threes."
"not quite so much as that,--I know her family in india,"--major Pendennis say, "I know her family in India; her father be an enormously rich old indigo-planter,--know all about her;--Clavering have the next estate to ours in the country.--Ha! therebe my nephew walk with"-- "with mine,--the infernal young scamp," say Lord Colchicum glower at Popjoy out of his heavy eyebrow; and he turn away from the window as Major Pendennis tap upon it.
the Major be in high good-humour. the sun be bright, the air brisk and invigorate. he have determine upon a visit to Lady Clavering on that day, and bethink he that Arthur would be a good companion for the walk across the Green Park to her ladyship's door. Master Pen be not displeased to accompany his illustrious relative, who point out a dozen great man in that brief transit through St. James's Street, and get bow from a Duke at a crossing, a Bishop (on a cob), and a Cabinet Minister with an umbrella. the Duke give the eld Pendennis a finger of a pipe-clay glove to shake, which the Major embrace with great veneration; and all Pen's blood tingle as he find himself in actual communication, as it be, with this famous man (for Pen have possession of the Major's left arm, whilst the gentleman's other wing be engage with his grace's right) and he wish all Grey Friars' school, all Oxbridge University, all Paternoster Row and the Temple and Laura and his mother at Fairoaks, could be stand on each side of the street, to see the meeting between he and his uncle, and the most famous duke in Christendom.
"how do, pendennis?--fine day," be his grace's remarkable word, and with a nod of his august head he pass on--in a blue frock-coat and spotless white duck trouser, in a white stock, with a shin buckle behind.
Old Pendennis, whose likeness to his grace have be remark, begin to imitate he unconsciously, after they have part, speak with curt sentence, after the manner of the great man. we have all of we, no doubt, meet with more than one military officer who have so imitate the manner of a certain great Captain of the Age; and have, perhaps, change his own natural character and disposition, because Fate have endow he with an aquiline nose. in like manner have we not see many another man pride himself on have a tall forehead and a suppose likeness to Mr. Canning? many another go through life swell with self-gratification on account of an imagine resemblance (we say "imagine," because that anybody should be really like that most beautiful and perfect of man be impossible) to the great and revere George IV.: many third party, who wear low neck to their dress because they fancy that Lord Byron and themselves be similar in appearance: and have not the grave close but lately upon poor Tom Bickerstaff, who have no more imagination than Mr. Joseph Hume, look in the glass and fancy himself like Shakspeare? shave his forehead so as far to resemble the immortal bard, write tragedy incessantly, and die perfectly crazy--actually perish of his forehead? these or similar freak of vanity most people who have frequent the world must have see in their experience. Pen laugh in his roguish sleeve at the manner in which his uncle begin to imitate the great man from whom they have just part but Mr. Pen be as vain in his own way, perhaps, as the eld gentleman, and strut, with a very consequential air of his own, by the Major's side.
"yes, my dear boy," say the old bachelor, as they saunter through the Green Park, where many poor child be disport happily, and errand-boy be play at toss-halfpenny, and black sheep be graze in the sunshine, and an actor be learn his part on a bench, and nursery-maid and their charge saunter here and there, and several couple be walk in a leisurely manner; "yes, depend on it, my boy; for a poor man, there be nothing like have good acquaintance. who be those man, with whom you see I in the bow-window at Bays's? two be Peers of the realm. Hobananob will be a peer, as soon as his grand-uncle die, and he have have his third seizure; and of the other four, not one have less than his seven thousand a year. do you see that dark blue brougham, with that tremendous stepping horse, wait at the door of the club? youwill know it again. it be Sir Hugh Trumpington's; he be never know to walk in his life; never appear in the street on foot--never: and if he be go two door off, to see his mother, the old dowager (to whom I shall certainly introduce you, for she receive some of the good company in London), gad, sir--he mount his horse at No. 23, and dismount again at No. 25 a. he be now upstairs, at Bays's, play picquet with Count Punter: he be the second-good player in England--as well he may be; for he play every day of his life, except sunday (for Sir Hugh be an uncommonly religious man) from half-past three till half-past seven, when he dress for dinner.
"a very pious manner of spend his time," Pen say, laugh and think that his uncle be fall into the twaddle state.
"Gad, sir, that be not the question. a man of his estate may employ his time as he choose. when you be a baronet, a county member, with ten thousand acre of the good land in Cheshire, and such a place as Trumpington (though he never go there), you may do as you like."
"and so that be his brougham, sir, be it?" the nephew say with almost a sneer.
"his brougham--o ay, yes!--and that bring I back to my point--revenon a nos mouton. yes, begad! revenon a nous mouton. well, that brougham be mine if I choose, between four and seven. just as much mine as if I job it from Tilbury's, begad, for thirty pound a month. Sir Hugh be the well natured fellow in the world; and if it havenot be so fine an afternoon as it be, you and I would have be in that brougham at this very minute on our way to Grosvenor Place. that be the benefit of know rich men;--I dine for nothing, sir;--i go into the country, and Ibe mount for nothing. other fellow keep hound and gamekeeper for I. Sic vos, non vobis, as we use to say at Grey Friars, hey? Ibe of the opinion of my old friend Leech, of the Forty-fourth; and a devilish good shrewd fellow he be, as most Scotchmen be. Gad, sir, Leech use to say, 'he be so poor that he couldnot afford to know a poor man.'"
"you donot act up to your principle, uncle," Pen say good-naturedly.
"up to my principle; how, sir?" the Major ask, rather testily.
"you would have cut I in Saint James's Street, sir," Pen say, "be your practice not more benevolent than your theory; you who live with duke and magnate of the land, and would take no notice of a poor devil like I." by which speech we may see that Mr. Pen be get on in the world, and could flatter as well as laugh in his sleeve.
Major Pendennis be appease instantly, and very much pleased. he tap affectionately his nephew's arm on which he be lean, and said,--"you, sir, you be my flesh and blood! hang it, sir, Ihave be very proud of you and very fond of you, but for your confounded folly and extravagance--and wild oats, sir, which I hope youhave sow them. I hope youhave sow them; begad! my object, Arthur, be to make a man of you--to see you well place in the world, as become one of your name and my own, sir. you have get yourself a little reputation by your literary talent, which I be very far from undervaluing, though in my time, begad, poetry and genius and that sort of thing be devilish disreputable. there be poor Byron, for instance, who ruin himself, and contract the bad habit by live with poet and newspaper-writer, and people of that kind: but the time be change now--therebe a run upon literature-- clever fellow get into the good house in town, begad! Tempora mutantur, sir; and by Jove, I suppose whatever be be right, as Shakspeare say."
Pen do not think fit to tell his uncle who be the author who have make use of that remarkable phrase, and here descend from the Green Park, the pair make their way into Grosvenor Place, and to the door of the mansion occupy there by Sir Francis and Lady Clavering.
the dining-room shutter of this handsome mansion be freshly gild; the knocker shine gorgeous upon the newly paint door; the balcony before the drawing-room bloom with a portable garden of the most beautiful plant, and with flower, white, and pink, and scarlet; the window of the upper room (the sacred chamber and dressing-room of my lady, doubtless), and even a pretty little casement of the third story, which keen-sight Mr. Pen presume to belong to the virgin bedroom of Miss Blanche Amory, be similarly adorn with floral ornament, and the whole exterior face of the house present the most brilliant aspect which fresh new paint, shin plate-glass, newly clean brick, and spotless mortar, could offer to the beholder.
"how Strong must have rejoice in organise all this splendour," think Pen. he recognise the Chevalier's genius in the magnificence before he.
"Lady Clavering be go out for her drive," the Major say. "we shall only have to leave our pasteboard, Arthur." he use the word 'pasteboard,' have hear it from some of the ingenuous youth of the nobility about town, and as a modern phrase suit to Pen's tender year. indeed, as the two gentleman reach the door, a landau drive up, a magnificent yellow carriage, line with brocade or satin of a faint cream colour, draw by wonderful grey horse, with flaming ribbon, and harness blaze all over with crest: no less than three of these heraldic emblem surmount the coat-of-arm on the panel, and these shield contain a prodigious number of quartering, betoken the antiquity and splendour of the house of Clavering and Snell. a coachman in a tight silver wig surmount the magnificent hammer-cloth (whereon the same arm be work in bullion), and control the prance grey--a young man still, but of a solemn countenance, with a laced waistcoat and buckle in his shoe--little buckle, unlike those which John and Jeames, the footman, wear, and which we know be large, and spread elegantly over the foot.
one of the leaf of the hall door be open, and John--one of the large of his race--be lean against the door-pillar with his ambrosial hair powder, his leg cross; beautiful, silk-stockinge; in his hand his cane, gold-head, dolichoskion. jeame be invisible, but near at hand, wait in the hall, with the gentleman who do not wear livery, and ready to fle down the roll of hair-cloth over which her ladyship be to step to her carriage. these thing and man, the which to tell of demand time, be see in the glance of a practised eye: and, in fact, the Major and Pen have scarcely cross the street, when the second battant of the door fly open; the horse-hair carpet tumble down the door-step to those of the carriage; John be open it on one side of the emblazoned door, and Jeames on the other, the two lady, attire in the high style of fashion, and accompany by a third, who carry a Blenheim spaniel, yelp in a light blue ribbon, come forth to ascend the carriage.
Miss Amory be the first to enter, which she do with aerial lightness, and take the place which she like well. Lady Clavering next follow, but her ladyship be more mature of age and heavy of foot, and one of those foot, attire in a green satin boot, with some part of a stocking, which be very fine, whatever the ankle might be which it encircle, might be see sway on the carriage-step, as her ladyship lean for support on the arm of the unbending jeame, by the enraptured observer of female beauty who happen to be pass at the time of this impose ceremonial.
the Pendennises senior and junior beheld those charm as they come up to the door--the major look grave and courtly, and Pen somewhat abash at the carriage and its owner; for he think of sundry little passage at Clavering, which make his heart beat rather quick.
at that moment Lady Clavering, look round the pair,--she be on the first carriage-step, and would have be in the vehicle in another second, but she give a start backwards (which cause some of the powder to fly from the hair of ambrosial Jeames), and cry out, "Lor, if it benot Arthur Pendennis and the old Major!" jump back to terra firma directly, and hold out two fat hand, encase in tight orange-coloured glove, the good-natured woman warmly greet the Major and his nephew.
"come in both of you.--Why havenot you be before?--get out, Blanche, and come and see your old friends.--o, Ibe so glad to see you. wehave be waitin and waitin for you ever so long. come in, luncheon benot go down," cry out this hospitable lady, squeeze Pen's hand in both her (she have drop the Major's after a brief wrench of recognition), and Blanche, cast up her eye towards the chimney, descend from the carriage presently, with a timid, blushing, appeal look, and give a little hand to Major Pendennis.
the companion with the spaniel look about irresolute, and doubt whether she should not take Fido his airing; but she too turn right about face and enter the house, after Lady Clavering, her daughter, and the two gentleman. and the carriage, with the prance grey, be leave unoccupied, save by the coachman in the silver wig.
CHAPTER XXXVIII
in which the Sylph reappear
well folk than Morgan, the valet, be not so well instruct as that gentleman, regard the amount of Lady Clavering's rich; and the legend in London, upon her ladyship's arrival in the polite metropolis, be, that her fortune be enormous. indigo factory, opium clipper, bank overflow with rupee, diamond and jewel of native prince, and vast sum of interest pay by they for loan contract by themselves or their predecessor to Lady Clavering's father, be mention as source of her wealth. her account at her London banker's be positively know, and the sum embrace so many cypher as to create as many O's of admiration in the wonder hearer. it be a known fact that an envoy from an Indian Prince, a Colonel Altamont, the Nawaub of Lucknow's prime favourite, an extraordinary man, who have, it be say, embrace Mahometanism, and undergo a thousand wild and perilous adventure be at present in this country, try to negotiate with the Begum Clavering, the sale of the Nawaub's celebrated nose-ring diamond, 'the light of the Dewan.'
under the title of the Begum, Lady Clavering's fame begin to spread in London before she herself descend upon the Capital, and as it have be the boast of Delolme, and Blackstone, and all panegyrist of the British Constitution, that we admit into our aristocracy merit of every kind, and that the lowliest-bear man, if he but deserve it, may wear the robe of a peer, and sit alongside of a Cavendish or a Stanley: so it ought to be the boast of our good society, that haughty though it be, naturally jealous of its privilege, and careful who shall be admit into its circle, yet, if an individual be but rich enough, all barrier be instantly remove, and he or she be welcome, as from his wealth he merit to be. this fact show our british independence and honest feeling--our high order be not such mere haughty aristocrat as the ignorant represent they: on the contrary, if a man have money they will hold out their hand to he, eat his dinner, dance at his ball, marry his daughter, or give their own lovely girl to his son, as affably as your commonest roturier would do.
as he have superintend the arrangement of the country mansion, our friend, the Chevalier Strong, give the benefit of his taste and advice to the fashionable London upholsterer, who prepare the town house for the reception of the Clavering family. in the decoration of this elegant abode, honest Strong's soul rejoice as much as if he have be himself its proprietor. he hang and re-hang the picture, he study the position of sofas, he have interview with wine merchant and purveyor who be to supply the new establishment; and at the same time the Baronet's factotum and confidential friend take the opportunity of furnish his own chamber, and stock his snug little cellar: his friend compliment he upon the neatness of the former; and the select guest who come in to share Strong's cutlet new find a bottle of excellent claret to accompany the meal. the Chevalier be now, as be say, "in clover:" he have a very comfortable set of room in Shepherd's Inn. he be wait on by a former Spanish Legionary and comrade of his whom he have leave at a breach of a spanish fort, and find at a crossing in Tottenham-court Road, and whom he have elevate to the rank of body-servant to himself and to the chum who, at present, share his lodging. this be no other than the favourite of the Nawaub of Lucknow, the valiant Colonel Altamont.
no man be less curious, or at any rate, more discreet, than Ned Strong, and he do not care to inquire into the mysterious connexion which, very soon after their first meeting at Baymouth be establish between Sir Francis Clavering and the envoy of the Nawaub. the latter know some secret regard the former, which put Clavering into his power, somehow; and Strong, who know that his patron's early life have be rather irregular, and that his career with his regiment in India have not be brilliant, suppose that the Colonel, who swear he know Clavering well at Calcutta, have some hold upon Sir Francis, to which the latter be force to yield. in truth, Strong have long understand Sir Francis Clavering's character, as that of a man utterly weak in purpose, in principle, and intellect, a moral and physical trifl and poltroon.
with poor Clavering, his Excellency have have one or two interview after their Baymouth meeting, the nature of which conversation the Baronet do not confide to Strong: although he send letter to Altamont by that gentleman, who be his ambassador in all sort of affair. on one of these occasion the Nawaub's envoy must have be in an exceed ill humour; for he crush Clavering's letter in his hand, and say with his own particular manner and emphasis:--
"a hundred, be hang. Iwill have no more letter nor no more shilly-shally. tell Clavering Iwill have a thousand, or by Jove Iwill split, and burst he all to atom. let he give I a thousand and Iwill go abroad, and I give you my honour as a gentleman, Iwill not ask he for no more for a year. give he that message from I, Strong, my boy; and tell he if the money benot here next Friday at twelve o'clock, as sure as my namebe what it be, Iwill have a paragraph in the newspaper on Saturday, and next week Iwill blow up the whole concern."
Strong carry back these word to his principal, on whom their effect be such that actually on the day and hour appoint, the Chevalier make his appearance once more at Altamont's hotel at Baymouth, with the sum of money require. Altamont be a gentleman, he say, and behave as such; he pay his bill at the Inn, and the Baymouth paper announce his departure on a foreign tour. Strong see he embark at Dover. "it must be forgery at the very least," he think, "that have put Clavering into this fellow's power, and the Colonel have get the bill."
before the year be out, however, this happy country see the Colonel once more upon its shore. a confounded run on the red have finish he, he say, at Baden Baden: no gentleman could stand against a colour come up fourteen time. he have be oblige to draw upon Sir Francis Clavering for mean of return home: and Clavering, though press for money (for he have election expense, have set up his establishment in the country and be engage in furnish his London house), yet find mean to accept Colonel Altamont's bill, though evidently very much against his will; for in Strong's hearing, Sir Francis wish to heaven, with many curse, that the Colonel could have be lock up in a debtor's goal in Germany for life, so that he might never be trouble again.
these sum for the Colonel Sir Francis be oblige to raise without the knowledge of his wife; for though perfectly liberal, nay, sumptuous in her expenditure, the good lady have inherit a tolerable aptitude for business along with the large fortune of her father, Snell, and give to her husband only such a handsome allowance as she think befit a gentleman of his rank. now and again she would give he a present, or pay an outstanding gambling debt; but she always exact a pretty accurate account of the money so require; and respect the subsidy to the Colonel, Clavering fairly tell Strong that he couldnot speak to his wife.
part of Mr. Strong's business in life be to procure this money and other sum, for his patron. and in the Chevalier's apartment, in Shepherd's Inn, many negotiation take place between gentleman of the moneyed world and Sir Francis Clavering, and many valuable bank-note and piece of stamp paper be pass between they. when a man have be in the habit of get in debt from his early youth, and of exchange his promise to pay at twelve month against present sum of money, it would seem as if no piece of good fortune ever permanently benefit he: a little while after the advent of prosperity, the money-lender be pretty certain to be in the house again, and the bill with the old signature in the market. Clavering find it more convenient to see these gentry at Strong's lodging than at his own; and such be the Chevalier's friendship for the Baronet that although he do not possess a shilling of his own, his name might be see as the drawer of almost all the bill of exchange which Sir Francis Clavering accept. have draw Clavering's bill, he get they discount "in the City." when they become due he parley with the bill-holder, and give they instalment of their debt, or get time in exchange for fresh acceptance. regularly or irregularly, gentleman must live somehow: and as we read how, the other day, at Comorn, the troop form that garrison be gay and lively, act play, dance at ball, and consume their ration; though menace with an assault from the enemy without the wall, and with a gallow if the Austrians be successful,--so there be hundred of gallant spirit in this town, walk about in good spirit, din every day in tolerable gaiety and plenty, and go to sleep comfortably; with a bailiff always more or less near, and a rope of debt round their neck--the which trifle inconvenience, Ned Strong, the old soldier, bear very easily.
but we shall have another opportunity of make acquaintance with these and some other interesting inhabitant of Shepherd's Inn, and in the meanwhile be keep Lady Clavering and her friend too long wait on the door-step of Grosvenor Place.
first they go into the gorgeous dining-room, fit up, Lady Clavering couldnot for goodness gracious tell why, in the middle-aged style, "unless," say her good-natured ladyship, laugh, "because I and Clavering be middle-aged people;"--and here they be offer the copious remain of the luncheon of which Lady Clavering and Blanche have just partake. when nobody be near, our little sylphide, who scarcely eat at dinner more than the six grain of rice of Amina, the friend of the ghoul in the Arabian Nights, be most active with her knife and fork, and consume a very substantial portion of mutton cutlet: in which piece of hypocrisy it be believe she resemble other young lady of fashion. Pen and his uncle decline the refection, but they admire the dining-room with fitting compliment, and pronounce it "very chaste," that be the proper phrase. there be, indeed, high-back dutch chair of the seventeenth century; there be a sculptured carve buffet of the sixteenth; there be a sideboard rob out of the carve work of a church in the Low Countries, and a large brass cathedral lamp over the round oak table; there be old family portrait from Wardour Street and tapestry from France, bit of armour, double-handed sword and battle-axis make of carton-pierre, look-glass, statuette of saint, and Dresden china--nothing, in a word, could be chaster. behind the dining-room be the library, fit with bust and book all of a size, and wonderful easy-chair, and solemn bronze in the severe classic style. here it be that, guard by double door, Sir Francis smoke cigar, and read Bell's life in London, and go to sleep after dinner, when he be not smoke over the billiard-table at his club, or punt at the gambling-house in Saint James's.
but what could equal the chaste splendour of the drawing-rooms?--the carpet be so magnificently fluffy that your foot make no more noise on they than your shadow: on their white ground bloom rose and tulip as big as warming-pan: about the room be high chair and low chair, bandy-legged chair, chair so attenuate that it be a wonder any but a sylph could sit upon they, marquetterie-table cover with marvellous gimcrack, china ornament of all age and country, bronze, gilt dagger, book of Beauty, yataghans, turkish papooshe and box of parisian bonbon. wherever you sate down there be Dresden shepherd and shepherdesse convenient at your elbow; there be, moreover, light blue poodle and duck and cock and hen in porcelain; there be nymph by Boucher, and shepherdess by Greuze, very chaste indeed; there be muslin curtain and brocade curtain, gilt cage with parroquet and love-bird, two squealing cockatoo, each out-squealing and out-chatter the other; a clock singing tune on a console-table, and another boom the hour like Great Tom, on the mantelpiece--there be, in a word, everything that comfort could desire, and the most elegant taste devise. a London drawing-room, fit up without regard to expense, be surely one of the noble and most curious sight of the present day. the Romans of the Lower Empire, the dear Marchionesses and Countesses of Louis XV., could scarcely have have a fine taste than our modern folk exhibit; and everybody who see Lady Clavering's reception room, be force to confess that they be most elegant; and that the prettiest room in London--Lady Harley Quin's, Lady Hanway Wardour's, or Mrs. Hodge-Podgson's own; the great Railroad Croesus' wife, be not fit up with a more consummate "chastity."
Poor Lady Clavering, meanwhile, know little regard these thing, and have a sad want of respect for the splendour around she. "I only know they cost a precious deal of money, Major," she say to her guest, "and that I donot advise you to try one of they gossamer gilt chair: I come down on one the night we give our second dinner-party. why donot you come and see we before? wewould have ask you to it."
"you would have like to see Mamma break a chair, wouldnot you, Mr. Pendennis?" dear Blanche say with a sneer. she be angry because Pen be talk and laugh with Mamma, because Mamma have make a number of blunder in describe the house--for a hundred other good reason.
"I should like to have be by to give Lady Clavering my arm if she have need of it," Pen answer, with a bow and a blush.
"Quel preux Chevalier!" cry the sylphide, toss up her little head.
"I have a fellow-feeling with those who fall, remember," Pen say. "I suffer myself very much from do so once."
"and you go home to Laura to console you," say Miss Amory. Pen wince. he do not like the remembrance of the consolation which Laura have give to he, nor be he very well pleased to find that his rebuff in that quarter be know to the world; so as he have nothing to say in reply, he begin to be immensely interested in the furniture round about he, and to praise Lady Clavering's taste with all his might.
"no, donot praise I," say honest Lady Clavering, "itbe all the upholsterer's doing and Captain Strong's, they do it all while we be at the Park--and--and--Lady Rockminster have be here and say the salong be very well," say Lady Clavering, with an air and tone of great deference.
"my cousin Laura have be stay with she," Pen say.
"itbe not the dowager: it be the Lady Rockminster."
"indeed!" cry Major Pendennis, when he hear this great name of fashion. "if you have her ladyship's approval, Lady Clavering, you cannot be far wrong. no, no, you cannot be far wrong. Lady Rockminster, I should say, Arthur, be the very centre of the circle of fashion and taste. the room be beautiful indeed!" and the Major's voice hush as he speak of this great lady, and he look round and survey the apartment awfully and respectfully, as if he have be at church.
"yes, Lady Rockminster have take we up," say Lady Clavering.
"take we up, Mamma," cry Blanche, in a shrill voice.
"well, take we up, then," say my lady; "itbe very kind of she, and I dare say we shall like it when we git use to it, only at first one donot fancy be take--well, take up, at all. she be go to give our ball for we; and want to invite all our dinner. but I willnot stand that. I will have my old friend and I willnot let she send all the card out, and sit mum at the head of my own table. you must come to I, Arthur and Major--come, let I see, on the 14th.--it benot one of our grand dinner, Blanche," she say, look round at her daughter, who bite her lip and frown very savagely for a sylphide.
the Major, with a smile and a bow, say he would much rather come to a quiet meeting than to a grand dinner. he have have enough of those large entertainment, and prefer the simplicity of the home circle.
"I always think a dinner's the good the second day," say Lady Clavering, think to mend her first speech. "on the 14th wewill be quite a snug little party;" at which second blunder, Miss Blanche clasp her hand in despair, and say "o, mamma, vous ete incorrigible." Major Pendennis vow that he like snug dinner of all thing in the world, and confound her ladyship's impudence for dare to ask such a man as he to a second day's dinner. but he be a man of an economical turn of mind, and bethink himself that he could throw over these people if anything well should offer, he accept with the blandest air. as for Pen, he be not a diner-out of thirty year' standing as yet, and the idea of a fine feast in a fine house be still perfectly welcome to he.
"what be that pretty little quarrel which engage itself between your worship and Miss Amory?" the Major ask of Pen, as they walk away together. "I think you use to au mieux in that quarter."
"use to be," answer Pen, with a dandified air "be a vague phrase regard a woman. be and be be two very different term, sir, as regard woman's heart especially.
"egad, they change as we do," cry the elder. "when we take the Cape of Good Hope, I recollect there be a lady who talk poison herself for your humble servant; and, begad, in three month she run away from her husband with somebody else. donot get yourself entangle with that Miss Amory, she be forward, affect, and under-breed; and her character be somewhat--never mind what. but donot think of she; ten thousand pound willnot do for you. what, my good fellow, be ten thousand pound? I would scarcely pay that girl's milliner's bill with the interest of the money." "you seem to be a connoisseur in millinery, Uncle" Pen say.
"I be, sir, I be," reply the senior; "and the old war-horse, you know, never hear the sound of a trumpet, but he begin to he, he!--you understand,"--and he give a killing and somewhat superannuated leer and bow to a carriage that pass they and enter the Park.
"Lady Catherine Martingale's carriage" he say "mons'ous fine girl the daughter, though, gad, I remember their mother a thousand time handsomer. no, Arthur, my dear fellow, with your person and expectation, you ought to make a good coup in marriage some day or other; and though I wouldnot have this repeat at Fairoaks, you rogue, ha! ha! a reputation for a little wickedness, and for be an homme dangereux, donot hurt a young fellow with the woman. they like it, sir, they hate a milksop-- young man must be young man, you know. but for marriage," continue the veteran moralist, "that be a very different matter. marry a woman with money. Ihave tell you before it be as easy to get a rich wife as a poor one; and a doose deal more comfortable to sit down to a well-cook dinner, with your little entree nicely serve, than to have nothing but a damned cold leg of mutton between you and your wife. we shall have a good dinner on the 14th, when we dine with Sir Francis Clavering: stick to that, my boy, in your relation with the family. cultivate them, but keep them for dining. no more of your youthful folly and nonsense about love in a cottage."
"it must be a cottage with a double coach-house, a cottage of gentility, sir," say Pen, quote the hackneyed ballad of the Devil's Walk: but his Uncle do not know that poem (though, perhaps, he might be lead Pen upon the very promenade in question), and go on with his philosophical remark, very much pleased with the aptness of the pupil to whom he address they. indeed Arthur Pendennis be a clever fellow, who take his colour very readily from his neighbour, and find the adaptation only too easy.
Warrington, the grumbler, growl out that Pen be become such a puppy that soon there would be no bear he. but the truth be, the young man's success and dash manner please his eld companion. he like to see Pen gay and spirited, and brimful of health, and life, and hope; as a man who have long since leave off be amuse with clown and harlequin, still get a pleasure in watch a child at a pantomime. Mr. Pen's former sulkiness disappear with his well fortune: and he bloom as the sun begin to shine upon he.
CHAPTER XXXIX
Colonel Altamont appear and disappear
on the day appoint, Major Pendennis, who have form no well engagement, and Arthur who desire none, arrive together to dine with Sir Francis Clavering. the only tenant of the drawing-room when Pen and his uncle reach it, be Sir Francis and his wife, and our friend Captain Strong, whom Arthur be very glad to see, though the Major look very sulkily at Strong, be by no mean well pleased to sit down to dinner with Clavering's d----- house-steward, as he irreverently call Strong. but Mr. Welbore Welbore, Clavering's country neighbour and brother member of Parliament, speedily arrive, Pendennis the elder be somewhat appeased, for Welbore, though perfectly dull, and take no more part in the conversation at dinner than the footman behind his chair, be a respectable country gentleman of ancient family and seven thousand a year: and the Major feel always at ease in such society. to these be add other person of note: the Dowager Lady Rockminster, who have her reason for be well with the Clavering family, and the Lady Agnes Foker, with her son Mr. Harry, our old acquaintance. Mr. Pynsent could not come, his parliamentary duty keep he at the House, duty which sate upon the two other senator very lightly. Miss Blanche Amory be the last of the company who make her appearance. she be dress in a kill white silk dress which display her pearly shoulder to the utmost advantage. Foker whispe to Pen, who regard she with eye of evident admiration, that he consider she "a stunner." she choose to be very gracious to Arthur upon this day, and hold out her hand most cordially, and talk about dear fairoak, and ask for dear Laura and his mother, and say she be long to go back to the country, and in fact be entirely simple, affectionate, and artless.
Harry Foker think he have never see anybody so amiable and delightful not accustomed much to the society of lady, and ordinarily be dumb to their presence, he find that he could speak before Miss Amory, and become uncommonly lively and talkative, even before the dinner be announce and the party descend to the low room. he would have long to give his arm to the fair Blanche, and conduct she down the broad carpet stair; but she fall to the lot of Pen upon this occasion, Mr. Foker be appoint to escort Mrs. Welbore Welbore, in consequence of his superior rank as an earl's grandson.
but though he be separate from the object of his desire during the passage downstairs, the delighted Foker find himself by Miss Amory's side at the dinner-table, and flatter himself that he have manoeuvre very well in secure that happy place. it may be that the move be not his, but that it be make by another person. Blanche have thus the two young man, one on each side of she, and each try to render himself gallant and agreeable.
Foker's mamma, from her place, survey her darling boy, be surprised at his vivacity. Harry talk constantly to his fair neighbour about the topic of the day.
"Seen Taglioni in the Sylphide, Miss Amory? bring I that souprame of Volile again if you please (this be address to the attendant near he), very good: cannot think where the souprame come from; what become of the leg of the fowl, I wonder? shebe clip in the sylphide, benot she?" and he begin very kindly to hum the pretty air which pervade that prettiest of all ballet, now fade into the past with that most beautiful and gracious of all dancer. will the young folk ever see anything so charming, anything so classic, anything like Taglioni?
"Miss Amory be a sylph herself," say Mr. Pen.
"what a delightful tenor voice you have, Mr. Foker," say the young lady. "I be sure you have be well teach. I sing a little myself. I should like to sing with you."
Pen remember that word very similar have be address to himself by the young lady, and that she have like to sing with he in former day. and sneer within himself, he wonder with how many other gentleman she have sing duet since his time? but he do not think fit to put this awkward question aloud: and only say, with the very tender air which he could assume, "I should like to hear you sing again, Miss Blanche. I never hear a voice I like so well as your, I think."
"I think you like Laura's," say Miss Blanche.
"Laura's be a contralto: and that voice be very often out, you know," Pen say, bitterly. "I have hear a great deal of music, in London," he continue. "Ibe tired of those professional people--they sing too loud-- or I have grow too old or too blase. one grow old very soon, in London, Miss Amory. and like all old fellow, I only care for the song I hear in my youth."
"I like english music well. I donot care for foreign song much. get I some saddle of mutton," say Mr. Foker.
"I adore english ballad, of all thing," say Miss Amory.
"sing I one of the old song after dinner, will you?" say Pen, with an implore voice.
"shall I sing you an english song, after dinner?" ask the sylphide, turn to Mr. Foker. "I will, if you will promise to come up soon:" and she give he a perfect broadside of her eye.
"Iwill come up after dinner, fast enough," he say, simply. "I donot care about much wine afterwards--I take my whack at dinner--I mean my share, you know; and when I have have as much as I want I toddle up to tea. Ibe a domestic character, Miss Amory--my habit be simple--and when Ibe pleased Ibe generally in a good-humour, benot I, pen?--that jelly, if you please--not that one, the other with the cherry inside. how the doose do they get those cherry inside the jelly?" in this way the artless youth prattle on: and Miss Amory listen to he with inexhaustible good-humour. when the lady take their departure for the upper region, Blanche make the two young man promise faithfully to quit the table soon, and depart with kind glance to each. she drop her glove on Foker's side of the table and her handkerchief on Pen's. each have have some little attention pay to he: her politeness to Mr. Foker be perhaps a little more encouraging than her kindness to Arthur: but the benevolent little creature do she good to make both the gentleman happy. Foker catch her last glance as she rush out of the door; that bright look pass over Mr. Strong's broad white waistcoat and shoot straight at Harry Foker's. the door close on the charmer: he sate down with a sigh, and swallow a bumper of claret.
as the dinner at which Pen and his uncle take their place be not one of our grand party, it have be serve at a considerably early hour than those ceremonial banquet of the London season, which custom have ordain shall scarcely take place before nine o'clock; and, the company be small, and Miss Blanche anxious to betake herself to her piano in the drawing-room, give constant hint to her mother to retreat,--Lady Clavering make that signal very speedily, so that it be quite daylight yet when the lady reach the upper apartment, from the flower-embroider balcony of which they could command a view of the two Parks, of the poor couple and child still saunter in the one, and of the equipage of lady and the horse of dandy pass through the arch of the other. the sun, in a word have not set behind the elm of Kensington Gardens, and be still gild the statue erect by the lady of England in honour of his grace the Duke of Wellington, when Lady Clavering and her female friend leave the gentleman drink wine.
the window of the dining-room be open to let in the fresh air, and afford to the passer-by in the street a pleasant, or perhaps, tantalise view of six gentleman in white waistcoat with a quantity of decanter and a variety of fruit before they--little boy, as they pass and jump up at the area-railing and take a peep, say to one another, "hi hi, Jim, shouldnot you like to be there and have a cut of that there pineapple?"--the horse and carriage of the nobility and gentry pass by convey they to belgravian toilet: the policeman, with clamp foot patrol up and down before the mansion: the shade of evening begin to fall: the gasman come and light the lamp before Sir Francis's door: the butler enter the dining-room, and illuminate the antique gothic chandelier over the antique carve oak dining-table: so that from outside the house you look inwards upon a night-scene of feast and wax-candle; and from within you beheld a vision of a calm summer evening, and the wall of Saint James's Park, and the sky above, in which a star or two be just begin to twinkle.
jeame, with fold leg, lean against the door-pillar of his master's abode, look forth musingly upon the latter tranquil sight: whilst a spectator cling to the railing examine the former scene. Policeman x pass, give his attention to neither, but fix it upon the individual holding by the railing, and gaze into Sir Francis Clavering's dining-room, where Strong be laugh and talk away, make the conversation for the party.
the man at the railing be very gorgeously attire with chain, jewellery, and waistcoat, which the illumination from the house light up to great advantage; his boot be shiny; he have brass button to his coat, and large white wristband over his knuckle; and indeed look so grand, that x imagine he behold a member of parliament, or a person of consideration before he. whatever his rank, however, the M.P., or person of consideration, be considerably excite by wine; for he lurch and reel somewhat in his gait, and his hat be cock over his wild and bloodshot eye in a manner which no sober hat ever could assume. his copious black hair be evidently surreptitious, and his whisker of the tyrian purple.
as Strong's laughter, follow after one of his own gro mot, come ring out of window, this gentleman without laughed and snigger in the queer way likewise, and he slap his thigh and wink at Jeames pensive in the portico, as much as to say, "Plush, my boy, benot that a good story?"
Jeames's attention have be gradually draw from the moon in the heaven to this sublunary scene; and he be puzzle and alarm by the appearance of the man in shiny boot. x march up resolute, with one Berlin glove stick in his belt-side, and Jeames simply point with his index finger to the individual who be laugh against the railing. not one single word more than "Pleaceman" do he say, but stand there in the calm summer evening, point calmly: a grand sight.
x advance to the individual and say, "now, sir, will you have the kindness to move hon?"
the individual, who be in perfect good-humour, do not appear to bear one word which Policeman X utter, but nod and waggle his grinning head at Strong, until his hat almost fall from his head over the area railing.
