he of the many ring instantly start, or rather stagger back, into what be call an attitude of self-defence, and in that position begin the operation which be entitle 'square' at Policeman X, and show himself brave and warlike, if unsteady. a gentleman in a white waistcoat, run out from a dining-room bareheade, a policeman, and an individual decently attire, engage in almost fisticuff on the pavement, be enough to make a crowd, even in that quiet neighbourhood, at half-past eight o'clock in the evening, and a small mob begin to assemble before Sir Francis Clavering's door. the host reflect a moment over his company. there be only Welbore, Pendennis, and those two lad, he think--and with a forced laugh and a piteous look, he said,--"well, Altamont, come in. I be very glad to see you, Ibe sure."
Colonel Altamont, for the intelligent reader have doubtless long ere this discover in the stranger his Excellency the Ambassador of the Nawaub of Lucknow, reel into the dining-room, with a triumphant look towards Jeames, the footman, which seem to say, "there, sir, what do you think of that? now, be I a gentleman or no?" and sink down into the first vacant chair. "very singular man. have reside long in a native court in India," Strong say, with great gravity, the Chevalier's presence of mind never desert him--"in those indian court they get very singular habit."
"very," say Major Pendennis, drily, and wonder what in goodness' name be the company into which he have get.
Mr. Foker be pleased with the new-comer. "itbe the man who would sing the Malay song at the Back Kitchen," he whisper to Pen. "try this pine, sir," he then say to Colonel Altamont, itbe uncommonly fine."

then the Colonel, see Pen's honest face, regard it for a while with as much steadiness as become his condition; and say, "I know you, too, young fellow. I remember you. Baymouth ball, by Jingo. want to fight the Frenchman. Captain Beak, by jingo!"

CHAPTER XL
Relates to Mr. Harry Foker's Affairs
since that fatal but delightful night in Grosvenor Place, Mr. Harry Foker's heart have be in such a state of agitation as you would hardly have think so great a philosopher could endure. when we remember what good advice he have give to Pen in former day, how an early wisdom and knowledge of the world have manifest itself in this gifted youth; how a constant course of self-indulgence, such as become a gentleman of his mean and expectation, ought by right to have increase his cynicism, and make he, with every succeed day of his life, care less and less for every individual in the world, with the single exception of Mr. Harry Foker, one may wonder that he should fall into the mishap to which most of we be subject once or twice in our life, and disquiet his great mind about a woman. but Foker, though early wise, be still a man. he could no more escape the common lot than Achilles, or Ajax, or Lord Nelson, or Adam our first father, and now, his time be come, young Harry become a victim to Love, the All-conqueror.
when he go to the Back Kitchen that night after quit Arthur Pendennis at his staircase-door in Lamb Court, the gin-twist and devil turkey have no charm for he, the joke of his companion fall flatly on his ear; and when Mr. Hodgen, the singer of 'the Body Snatcher,' have a new chant even more dreadful and humorous than that famous composition, Foker, although he appear his friend, and say "Bravo, Hodgen," as common politeness and his position as one of the chief of the Back Kitchen bind he to do, yet never distinctly hear one word of the song, which under its title of 'the Cat in the Cupboard,' Hodgen have since render so famous. late and very tired, he slip into his private apartment at home and seek the downy pillow, but his slumber be disturb by the fever of his soul, and the very instant that he wake from his agitated sleep, the image of Miss Amory present itself to he, and say, "here I be, I be your princess and beauty, you have discover I, and shall care for nothing else hereafter."
Heavens, how stale and distasteful his former pursuit and friendship appear to he! he have not be, up to the present time, much accustomed to the society of female of his own rank in life. when he speak of such, he call they "modest woman." that virtue which, let we hope, they possess, have not hitherto compensate to Mr. Foker for the absence of more lively quality which most of his own relative do not enjoy, and which he find in Mesdemoiselles, the lady of the theatre. his mother, though good and tender, do not amuse her boy; his cousin, the daughter of his maternal uncle, the respectable Earl of Rosherville, weary he beyond measure. one be blue, and a geologist; one be a horsewoman, and smoke cigar; one be exceedingly low Church, and have the most heterodox view on religious matter; at least, so the other say, who be herself of the very Highest Church faction, and make the cupboard in her room into an oratory, and fast on every Friday in the year. their paternal house of Drummington, Foker could very seldom be get to visit. he swear he have rather go on the treadmill than stay there. he be not much beloved by the inhabitant. Lord Erith, Lord Rosherville's heir, consider his cousin a low person, of deplorably vulgar habit and manner; while Foker, and with equal reason, vote Erith a prig and a dullard, the nightcap of the House of Commons, the Speaker's opprobrium, the dreariest of philanthropic spouter. nor could George Robert, Earl of Gravesend and Rosherville, ever forget that on one evening when he condescend to play at billiard with his nephew, that young gentleman poke his lordship in the side with his cue, and say, "well, old cock, Ihave see many a bad stroke in my life, but I never see such a bad one as that there." he play the game out with angelic sweetness of temper, for Harry be his guest as well as his nephew; but he be nearly have a fit in the night; and he keep to his own room until young Harry quit Drummington on his return to Oxbridge, where the interesting youth be finish his education at the time when the occurrence take place. it be an awful blow to the venerable earl; the circumstance be never allude to in the family; he shun Foker whenever he come to see they in London or in the country, and could hardly be bring to gasp out a "how d'ye do?" to the young blasphemer. but he would not break his sister Agnes's heart, by banish Harry from the family altogether; nor, indeed, could he afford to break with Mr. Foker, senior, between whom and his lordship there have be many private transaction, produce an exchange of bank-cheque from Mr. Foker, and autograph from the earl himself, with the letter I o u write over his illustrious signature.
besides the four daughter of Lord Gravesend whose various quality have be enumerate in the former paragraph, his lordship be bless with a fifth girl, the Lady Ana Milton, who, from her early year and nursery, have be destine to a peculiar position in life. it be ordain between her parent and her aunt, that when Mr Harry Foker attain a proper age, Lady Ann should become his wife. the idea have be familiar to her mind when she yet wear pinafore, and when Harry the dirty of little boy, use to come back with black eye from school to Drummington, or to his father's house of Logwood, where Lady Ann live, much with her aunt. both of the young people coincide with the arrangement propose by the elder, without any protest or difficulty. it no more enter Lady Ann's mind to question the order of her father, than it would have enter Esther's to dispute the command of Ahasuerus. the heir-apparent of the house of Foker be also obedient, for when the old gentleman say, "Harry, your uncle and I have agree that when yoube of a proper age, youwill marry Lady Ann. she willnot have any money, but shebe good blood, and a good one to look at, and I shall make you comfortable. if you refuse, youwill have your mother's jointure, and two hundred a year during my life"--harry, who know that his sire, though a man of few word, be yet implicitly to be trust, acquiesce at once in the parental decree, and say, "well, sir, if Ann's agreeable, I say ditto. shebe not a bad-look girl."
"and she have the good blood in England, sir. your mother's blood, your own blood, sir," say the Brewer. "therebe nothing like it, sir."
"well, sir, as you like it," Harry reply. "when you want I, please ring the bell. only therebe no hurry, and I hope youwill give we a long day. I should like to have my fle out before I marry."
"fle away, Harry," answer the benevolent father. "nobody prevent you, do they?" and so very little more be say upon this subject, and Mr. Harry pursue those amusement in life which suit he well; and hang up a little picture of his cousin in his sitting-room, amidst the french print, the favourite actress and dancer, the racing and coaching work of art, which suit his taste and form his gallery. it be an insignificant little picture, represent a simple round face with ringlet; and it make, as it must be confess, a very poor figure by the side of Mademoiselle Petitot, dance over a rainbow, or Mademoiselle Redowa, grin in red boot and a lancer's cap.
be engage and dispose of, Lady Ann Milton do not go out so much in the world as her sister: and often stay at home in London at the parental house in Gaunt Square, when her mamma with the other lady go abroad. they talk and they dance with one man after another, and the man come and go, and the story about they be various. but there be only this one story about Ann: she be engage to Harry Foker: she never be to think about anybody else. it be not a very amusing story.
well, the instant Foker awake on the day after Lady Clavering's dinner, there be Blanche's image glare upon he with its clear grey eye, and win smile. there be her tune ring in his ear, "yet round about the spot, ofttime I hover, ofttime I hover," which poor Foker begin piteously to hum, as he sit up in his bed under the crimson silken coverlet. opposite he be a French Print, of a turkish lady and her greek lover, surprise by a venerable Ottoman, the lady's husband; on the other wall be a french print of a gentleman and lady, rid and kiss each other at full gallop; all round the chaste bedroom be more french print, either portrait of gauzy nymph of the Opera, or lovely illustration of the novel; or mayhap, an english chef-d'oeuvre or two, in which Miss Calverley of T. R. E. O. would be represent in tight pantaloon in her favourite page part; or Miss Rougemont as Venus; their value enhance by the signature of these lady, Maria Calverley, or Frederica Rougemont, inscribe underneath the print in an exquisite facsimile. such be the picture in which honest Harry delight. he be no bad than many of his neighbour; he be an idle jovial kindly fast man about town; and if his room be rather profusely decorate with work of french art, so that simple Lady Agnes, his mamma on enter the apartment where her darling sate envelop in fragrant cloud of Latakia, be often bewilder by the novelty which she behold there, why, it must be remember, that he be rich than most young man, and could well afford to gratify his taste.
a letter from Miss Calverley write in a very degage style of spelling and handwriting, scrawl freely over the filagree paper, and commence by call Mr. Harry, her dear Hokey-pokey-fokey, lie on his bed table by his side, amidst key, sovereign, cigar-case, and a bit of verbena, which Miss Amory have give he, and remind he of the arrival of the day when he be 'to stand that dinner at the Elefant and Castle, at Richmond, which he have promise;' a card for a private box at Miss Rougemont's approach benefit, a bundle of ticket for 'Ben Budgeon's night, the North Lancashire Pippin, at Martin Faunce's, the three-cornered hat, in St. Martin's Lane; where Conkey Sam, Dick the Nailor, and Deadman (the Worcestershire Nobber), would put on the glove, and the lover of the good old british sport be invite to attend'-- these and sundry other memoir of Mr. Foker's pursuit and pleasure lie on the table by his side when he wake.
ah! how faint all these pleasure seem now. what do he care for Conkey Sam or the Worcestershire Nobber? what for the french print ogle he from all side of the room; those regular stunning slap-up out-and-outer? and Calverley spell bad, and call he Hokey-fokey, confound her impudence! the idea of be engage to a dinner at the Elephant and Castle at Richmond with that old woman (who be seven-and-thirty year old, if she be a day) fill his mind with dreary disgust now, instead of that pleasure which he have only yesterday expect to find from the entertainment.
when his fond mamma beheld her boy that morning, she remark on the pallor of his cheek, and the general gloom of his aspect. "why do you go on play billiard at that wicked Spratt's?" Lady Agnes ask. "my dear child, those billiard will kill you, Ibe sure they will."
"it benot the billiard," Harry say, gloomily.
"then itbe the dreadful Back Kitchen," say the Lady Agnes. "Ihave often think, d'you know, Harry, of write to the landlady, and beg that she would have the kindness to put only very little wine in the negus which you take, and see that you have your shawl on before you get into your brougham."
"Do, ma'am. Mrs Cutts be a most kind motley woman," Harry say. "but it benot the Back Kitchen, neither," he add, with a ghastly sigh.
as Lady Agnes never deny her son anything, and fall into all his way with the fond acquiescence, she be reward by a perfect confidence on young Harry's part, who never think to disguise from she a knowledge of the haunt which he frequent; and, on the contrary, bring her home choice anecdote from the club and billiard-room, which the simple lady relish, if she do not understand. "my son go to Spratt's," she would say to her confidential friend. "all the young man go to Spratt's after their ball. it be de rigueur, my dear; and they play billiard as they use to play macao and hazard in Mr. Fox's time. yes, my dear father often tell I that they sate up always until nine o'clock the next morning with Mr. Fox at Brookes's, whom I remember at Drnmmington, when I be a little girl, in a buff waistcoat and black satin small-clothe. my brother Erith never play as a young man, nor sate up late--he have no health for it; but my boy must do as everybody do, you know. yes, and then he often go to a place call the Back Kitchen, frequent by all the wit and author, you know, whom one do not see in society, but whom it be a great privilege and pleasure for Harry to meet, and there he hear the question of the day discuss; and my dear father often say that it be our duty to encourage literature, and he have hop to see the late Dr. Johnson at Drummington, only Dr. Johnson die. yes, and Mr. Sheridan come over, and drink a great deal of wine,--everybody drink a great deal of wine in those days,--and papa's wine-merchant's bill be ten time as much as Erith's be, who get it as he want it from Fortnum and Mason's and donot keep any stock at all."
"that be an uncommon good dinner we have yesterday, ma'am," the artful Harry break out. "their clear soup's well than our. Moufflet will put too much taragon into everything. the supreme de volaille be very good-- uncommon, and the sweet be well than Moufflet's sweet. do you taste the plombiere, ma'am, and the maraschino jelly? stunningly good that maraschino jelly!"
Lady Agnes express her agreement in these, as in almost all other sentiment of her son, who continue the artful conversation, saying--
"very handsome house that of the Claverings. furniture, I should say, get up regardless of expense. magnificent display of plate, ma'am." the lady assent to all these proposition.
"very nice people the Claverings."
"H'm!" say Lady Agnes.
"I know what you mean. Lady C. benot distangy exactly, but she be very good-natured."
"oh, very," mamma say, who be herself one of the most good-natured of woman.
"and Sir Francis, he donot talk much before lady; but after dinner he come out uncommon strong, ma'am--a highly agreeable, well-inform man. when will you ask they to dinner? look out for an early day, ma'am;" and look into Lady Agnes's pocket-book, he choose a day only a fortnight hence (an age that fortnight seem to the young gentleman), when the Claverings be to be invite to Grosvenor-street.
the obedient Lady Agnes write the require invitation. she be accustom to do so without consult her husband, who have his own society and habit, and who leave his wife to see her own friend alone. Harry look at the card; but there be an omission in the invitation which do not please he.
"you have not ask Miss Whatdyecallem--Miss Emery, Lady Clavering's daughter."
"oh, that little creature!" Lady Agnes cry. "no! I think not, Harry."
"we must ask Miss Amory," Foker say. "I--I want to ask Pendennis; and-- and hebe very sweet upon she. donot you think she sing very well, ma'am?"
"I think she rather forward, and donot listen to her singing. she only sing at you and Mr. Pendennis, it seem to I. but I will ask she if you wish, Harry," and so Miss Amory's name be write on the card with her mother's.
this piece of diplomacy be triumphantly execute Harry embrace his fond parent with the utmost affection, and retire to his own apartment where he stretch himself on his ottoman, and lie brood silently, sigh for the day which be to bring the fair Miss Amory under his paternal roof, and devise a hundred wild scheme for meet she.
on his return from make the grand tour, Mr. Foker, Junior, have bring with he a polyglot valet, who take the place of Stoopid, and condescend to wait at dinner, attire in shirt front of work muslin, with many gold stud and chain, upon his master and the elder of the family. this man, who be of no particular country, and speak all language indifferently ill, make himself useful to Mr. Harry in a variety of ways,--read all the artless youth's correspondence, know his favourite haunt and the address of his acquaintance, and officiate at the private dinner which the young gentleman give. as Harry lie upon his sofa after his interview with his mamma, rob in a wonderful dressing-gown, and puff his pipe in gloomy silence, Anatole, too, must have remark that something affect his master's spirit; though he do not betray any ill-breed sympathy with Harry's agitation of mind. when Harry begin to dress himself in his out-of-door morning costume, he be very hard indeed to please, and particularly severe and snappish about his toilet: he try, and curse, pantaloon of many different stripe, check, and colour: all the boot be villainously varnish; the shirt too "loud" in pattern. he scent his linen and person with peculiar richness this day; and what must have be the valet's astonishment, when, after some blushing and hesitation on Harry's part, the young gentleman ask, "I say, Anatole, when I engage you, donot you--hem--didn't you say that you could dress--hem--dress hair?"
the valet say, "yes, he could."
"cherchy alor une paire de tongs,--et--curly moi un peu," Mr. Foker say, in an easy manner; and the valet, wonder whether his master be in love or be go masquerade, go in search of the articles,--first from the old butler who wait upon Mr. Foker, senior, on whose bald pate the tong would have scarcely find a hundred hair to seize, and finally of the lady who have the charge of the meek auburn front of the Lady Agnes. and the tong be get, Monsieur Anatole twist his young master's lock until he have make Harry's head as curly as a negro's; after which the youth dress himself with the utmost care and splendour, and proceed to sally out.
"at what dime sall I order de drag, sir, to be to Miss Calverley's door, sir?" the attendant whisper as his master be go forth.
"confound her!--Put the dinner off--I cannot go!" say Foker. "no, hang it--I must go. Poyntz and Rougemont, and ever so many more be come. the drag at Pelham Corner at six o'clock, Anatole."
the drag be not one of Mr. Foker's own equipage, but be hire from a livery-stable for festive purpose; Foker, however, put his own carriage into requisition that morning, and for what purpose do the kind reader suppose? why, to drive down to Lamb Court, Temple, take Grosvenor Place by the way (which lie in the exact direction of the Temple from Grosvenor Street, as everybody know), where he just have the pleasure of peep upwards at Miss Amory's pink window-curtain, have achieve which satisfactory feat, he drive off to Pen's chamber. why do he want to see his dear friend Pen so much? why do he yearn and long after he; and do it seem necessary to Foker's very existence that he should see Pen that morning, have part with he in perfect health on the night previous? Pen have live two year in London, and Foker have not pay half a dozen visit to his chamber. what send he thither now in such a hurry?
what?--if any young lady read this page, I have only to inform they that, when the same mishap befall they, which now have for more than twelve hour befall Harry Foker, people will grow interesting to they for whom they do not care sixpence on the day before; as on the other hand person of whom they fancy themselves fond will be find to have become insipid and disagreeable. then you dearest Eliza, or Maria of the other day, to whom you write letter and send lock of hair yard long, will on a sudden be as indifferent to you as your stupid relation whilst, on the contrary, about his relation you will begin to feel such a warm interest! such a love desire to ingratiate yourself with his mamma; such a liking for that dear kind old man his father! if he be in the habit of visit at any house, what advance you will make in order to visit there too. if he have a married sister you will like to spend long morning with she. you will fatigue your servant by send note to she, for which there will be the most pressing occasion, twice or thrice in a day. you will cry if your mamma object to your go too often to see his family. the only one of they you will dislike, be perhaps his young brother, who be at home for the holiday, and who will persist in stay in the room when you come to see your dear new-find friend, his darling second sister. something like this will happen to you, young lady, or, at any rate, let we hope it may. yes, you must go through the hot fit and the cold fit of that pretty fever. your mother, if they would acknowledge it, have pass through it before you be bear, your dear papa be the object of the passion, of course,--who could it be but he? and as you suffer it, so will your brother, in their way,--and after their kind. more selfish than you: more eager and headstrong than you: they will rush on their destiny when the doomed charmer make her appearance. or if they donot, and you donot, Heaven help you! as the gambler say of his dice, to love and win be the good thing, to love and lose be the next good. you donot die of the complaint: or very few do. the generous wound heart suffer and survive it. and he be not a man, or she a woman, who be not conquer by it, or who do not conquer it in his time.----now, then, if you ask why Henry Foker, Esquire, be in such a hurry to see Arthur Pendennis, and feel such a sudden value and esteem for he, there be no difficulty in say it be because Pen have become really valuable in Mr. Foker's eye: because if Pen be not the rose, he yet have be near that fragrant flower of love. be not he in the habit of go to her house in London? do he not live near she in the country?--know all about the enchantress? what, I wonder, would Lady Ann Milton, Mr. Foker's cousin and pretendue, have say, if her ladyship have know all that be go on in the bosom of that funny little gentleman?
alas! when Foker reach Lamb Court, leave his carriage for the admiration of the little clerk who be lounge in the archway that lead thence into Flag Court which lead into Upper Temple Lane, Warrington be in the chamber but Pen be absent. Pen be go to the printing-office to see his proof. "would Foker have a pipe and should the laundress go to the Cock and get he some beer?"--warrington ask, remark with a pleased surprise the splendid toilet of this scented and shiny-boot young aristocrat; but Foker have not the slight wish for beer or tobacco: he have very important business: he rush away to the Pall Mall Gazette office, still bend upon find Pen. Pen have quit that pace. Foker want he that they might go together to call upon Lady Clavering. Foker go away disconsolate, and while away an hour or two vaguely at club: and when it be time to pay a visit, he think it would be but decent and polite to drive to Grosvenor Place and leave a card upon Lady Clavering. he have not the courage to ask to see she when the door be open, he only deliver two card, with Mr. Henry Foker engrave upon they, to Jeames, in a speechless agony. jeame receive the ticket bow his powdered head. the varnish door close upon he. the beloved object be as far as ever from he, though so near. he think he hear the tone of a piano and of a syren singing, come from the drawing-room and sweep over the balcony-shrubbery of geranium. he would have like to stop and listen, but it might not be. "drive to Tattersall's," he say to the groom, in a voice smother with emotion,-- "and bring my pony round," he add, as the man drive rapidly away.
as good luck would have it, that splendid barouche of Lady Clavering's, which have be inadequately describe in a former chapter, drive up to her ladyship's door just as Foker mount the pony which be in wait for he. he bestrode the fiery animal, and dodge about the arch of the Green Park, keep the carriage well in view, until he see Lady Clavering enter, and with she--whose could be that angel form, but the enchantress's, clothe in a sort of gossamer, with a pink bonnet and a light-blue parasol,--but Miss Amory?
the carriage take its fair owner to Madame Rigodon's cap and lace shop, to Mrs Wolsey's Berlin worst shop,--who know to what other resort of female commerce? then it go and take ice at Hunter's, for Lady Clavering be somewhat florid in her taste and amusement, and not only like to go abroad in the most showy carriage in London, but that the public should see she in it too. and so, in a white bonnet with a yellow feather, she eat a large pink ice in the sunshine before Hunter's door, till Foker on his pony, and the red jacket who accompany he, be almost tired of dodge.
then at last she make her way into the Park, and the rapid Foker make his dash forward. what to do? just to get a nod of recognition from Miss Amory and her mother; to cross they a half-dozen time in the drive; to watch and ogle they from the other side of the ditch, where the horseman assemble when the band play in Kensington Gardens. what be the use of look at a woman in a pink bonnet across a ditch? what be the earthly good to be get out of a nod of the head? strange that man will be content with such pleasure, or if not content, at least that they will be so eager in seek they. not one word do Harry, he so fluent of conversation ordinarily, change with his charmer on that day. mutely he behold her return to her carriage, and drive away among rather ironical salute from the young man in the Park. one say that the indian widow be make the paternal rupee spin rapidly; another say that she ought to have burn herself alive, and leave the money to her daughter. this one ask who claver was?--and old Tom Eales, who know everybody, and never miss a day in the Park on his grey cob, kindly say that Clavering have come into an estate over head and heel in mortgage: that there be dev'lish ugly story about he when he be a young man, and that it be report of he that he have a share in a gambling-house, and have certainly show the white feather in his regiment. "he play still; he be in a hell every night almost," Mr. Eales add.
"I should think so, since his marriage," say a wag.
"he give devilish good dinner," say Foker, strike up for the honour of his host of yesterday.
"I daresay, and I daresay he donot ask Eales," the wag say. "I say, Eales, do you dine at Clavering's,--at the Begum's?"
"I dine there?" say Mr. Eales, who would have din with Beelzebub if sure of a good cook, and when he come away, would have paint his host blacker than fate have make he.
"you might, you know, although you do abuse he so," continue the wag. "they say itbe very pleasant. clavering go to sleep after dinner; the Begum get tipsy with cherry-brandy, and the young lady sing song to the young gentleman. she sing well, donot she, fo?"
"slap up," say Fo. "I tell you what, Poyntz, she sing like a whatdyecallum--you know what I mean--like a mermaid, you know, but thatbe not their name."
"I never hear a mermaid sing," Mr. Poyntz, the wag, reply. "whoever hear a mermaid? eale, you be an old fellow, do you?"
"donot make a lark of I, hang it, Poyntz," say Foker, turn red, and with tear almost in his eye, "you know what I mean: itbe those what's-his-name--in Homer, you know. I never say I be a good scholar."
"and nobody ever say it of you, my boy," Mr. Poyntz remark, and Foker strike spur into his pony, canter away down Rotten row, his mind agitated with various emotion, ambition, mortification. he be sorry that he have not be good at his book in early life--that he might have cut out all those chap who be about she, and who talk the language, and write poetry, and paint picture in her album, and--and that--"what be I," think little Foker, "compare to she? shebe all soul, she be, and can write poetry or compose music, as easy as I could drink a glass of beer. Beer?--damme, thatbe all Ibe fit for, be beer. I be a poor, ignorant little beggar, good for nothing but Foker's Entire. I misspent my youth, and use to get the chap to do my exercise. and whatbe the consequence now? oh, Harry Foker, what a confound little fool you have be!"
as he make this dreary soliloquy, he have canter out of rotten row into the Park, and there be on the point of rid down a large old roomy family carriage, of which he take no heed, when a cheery voice cry out, "Harry, Harry!" and look up, he behold his aunt, the Lady Rosherville, and two of her daughter, of whom the one who speak be Harry's betroth, the Lady Ann.
he start back with a pale, scared look, as a truth about which he have not think during the whole day, come across he. there be his fate, there, in the back seat of that carriage.
"what be the matter, Harry? why be you so pale? you have be rake and smoke too much, you wicked boy," say Lady Ann.
Foker say, "how do, aunt," "how do, Ann," in a perturbed manner-- mutter something about a press engagement,--indeed he see by the Park clock that he must have be keep his party in the drag wait for nearly an hour--and wave a good-bye. the little man and the little pony be out of sight in an instant--the great carriage roll away. nobody inside be very much interested about his come or go; the Countess be occupy with her spaniel, the Lady Lucy's thought and eye be turn upon a volume of sermon, and those of the Lady Ann upon a new novel, which the sister have just procure from the library.
CHAPTER XLI
carry the reader both to Richmond and Greenwich
Poor Foker find the dinner at Richmond to be the most dreary entertainment upon which ever mortal man waste his guineas. "I wonder how the deuce I could ever have like these people," he think in his own mind. "why, I can see the crow'-foot under Rougemont's eye, and the paint on her cheek be lay on as thick as Clown's in a pantomime! the way in which that Calverley talk slang, be quite disgusting. I hate chaff in a woman. and old Colchicum! that old Col, come down here in his brougham, with his coronet on it, and sit bodkin between Mademoiselle Coralie and her mother! itbe too bad. an english peer, and a horse-rider of Franconi's!--It willnot do; by Jove, it willnot do. I benot proud; but it will not do!"
"twopence-halfpenny for your thought, Fokey!" cry out Miss Rougemont, take her cigar from her truly vermilion lip, as she behold the young fellow lose in thought, seat at the head of his table, amidst melt ice, and cut pineapple, and bottle full and empty, and cigar-ash scatter on fruit, and the ruin of a dessert which have no pleasure for he.
"do Foker ever think?" drawl out Mr. Poyntz. "Foker, here be a considerable sum of money offer by a fair capitalist at this end of the table for the present emanation of your valuable and acute intellect, old boy!"
"what the deuce be that Poyntz a talk about?" Miss Calverley ask of her neighbour. "I hate he. hebe a drawlin', sneerin' beast."
"what a droll of a little man be that little Fokare, my lor'," Mademoiselle Coralie say, in her own language, and with the rich twang of that sunny Gascony in which her swarthy cheek and bright black eye have get their fire. "what a droll of a man! he do not look to have twenty year."
"I wish I be of his age," say the venerable Colchicum, with a sigh, as he incline his purple face towards a large goblet of claret.
"C'te Jeunesse. Peuh! je m'en fiche" say Madame Brack, Coralie's mamma, take a great pinch out of Lord Colchicum's delicate gold snuff-box. "Je m'aime que les homme fait, moi. Comme milor. Coralie! n'est-ce pas que tu n'aimes que les homme fait, ma bichette?"
my lord say, with a grin, "you flatter I, Madame Brack."
"taisez-vous, Maman, vous n'ete qu'une bete," Coralie cry, with a shrug of her robust shoulder; upon which, my lord say that she do not flatter at any rate; and pocket his snuff-box, not desirous that Madame Brack's dubious finger should plunge too frequently into his Mackabaw.
there be no need to give a prolonged detail of the animate conversation which ensue during the rest of the banquet; a conversation which would not much edify the reader. and it be scarcely necessary to say, that all lady of the corps de dance be not like Miss Calverley, any more than that all peer resemble that illustrious member of their order, the late lament Viscount Colchicum. but there have be such in our memory who have love the society of riotous youth well than the company of man of their own age and rank, and have give the young one the precious benefit of their experience and example; and there have be very respectable man too who have not object so much to the kind of entertainment as to the publicity of it. I be sure, for instance, that our friend Major Pendennis would have make no sort of objection to join the a party of pleasure, provide that it be en petit comite, and that such man as my Lord Steyne and my Lord Colchicum be of the society. "give the young man their pleasure," this worthy guardian say to Pen more than once. "Ibe not one of your strait-laced moralist, but an old man of the world, begad; and I know that as long as it last young man will be young man." and there be some young man to whom this estimable philosopher accord about seventy year as the proper period for sow their wild oat: but they be man of fashion.
Mr. Foker drive his lovely guest home to Brompton in the drag that night; but he be quite thoughtful and gloomy during the whole of the little journey from Richmond; neither listen to the joke of the friend behind he and on the box by his side nor enliven they as be his willnot, by his own facetious sally. and when the lady whom he have convey alight at the door of their house, and ask their accomplished coachman whether he would not step in and take something to drink, he decline with so melancholy an air, that they suppose that the Governor and he have have a difference or that some calamity have befall he; and he do not tell these people what the cause of his grief be, but leave Mesdames Rougemont and Calverley, unheede the cry of the latter, who hang over her balcony like Jezebel, and call out to he to ask he to give another party soon.
he send the drag home under the guidance of one of the groom, and go on foot himself; his hand in his pocket, plunge in thought. the star and moon shin tranquilly overhead, look down upon Mr. Foker that night, as he in his turn sentimentally regard they. and he go and gaze upwards at the house in Grosvenor Place, and at the window which he suppose to be those of the beloved object; and he moan and he sigh in a way piteous and surprising to witness, which Policeman X do, who inform Sir Francis Clavering's people, as they take the refreshment of beer on the coach-box at the neighbour public-house, after bring home their lady from the french play, that there have be another chap hang about the premise that evening--a little chap, dress like a swell.
and now with that perspicuity and ingenuity and enterprise which only belong to a certain passion, Mr. Foker begin to dodge Miss Amory through London, and to appear wherever he could meet she. if Lady Clavering go to the french play, where her ladyship have a box, Mr. Foker, whose knowledge of the language, as we have hear, be not conspicuous, appear in a stall. he find out where her engagement be (it be possible that Anatole, his man, be acquaint with Sir Francis Clavering's gentleman, and so get a sight of her ladyship's engagement-book), and at many of these evening party Mr. Foker make his appearance--to the surprise of the world, and of his mother especially, whom he order to apply for card to these party, for which until now he have show a supreme contempt. he tell the pleased and unsuspicious lady that he go to party because it be right for he to see the world: he tell she that he go to the french play because he want to perfect himself in the language, and there be no such good lesson as a comedy or vaudeville,--and when one night the astonish Lady Agnes see he stand up and dance, and compliment he upon his elegance and activity, the mendacious little rogue assert that he have learn to dance in Paris, whereas Anatole know that his young master use to go off privily to an academy in Brewer Street, and study there for some hour in the morning. the casino of our modern day be not invent, or be in its infancy as yet; and gentleman of Mr. Foker's time have not the facility of acquire the science of dancing which be enjoy by our present youth.
Old Pendennis seldom miss go to church. he consider it to be his duty as a gentleman to patronise the institution of public worship and that it be quite a correct thing to be see at church of a Sunday. one day it chance that he and Arthur go thither together: the latter, who be now in high favour, have be to breakfast with his uncle, from whose lodge they walk across the park to a church not far from Belgrave Square. there be a charity sermon at Saint James's, as the Major know by the bill post on the pillar of his parish church, which probably cause he, for he be a thrifty man, to forsake it for that day: besides he have other view for himself and Pen. "we will go to church, sir, across the Park; and then, begad, we will go to the Claverings' house and ask they for lunch in a friendly way. Lady Clavering like to be ask for lunch, and be uncommonly kind, and monstrous hospitable."
"I meet they at dinner last week, at Lady Agnes Foker's, sir," Pen say, "and the Begum be very kind indeed. so she be in the country: so she be everywhere. but I share your opinion about Miss Amory; one of your opinion, that is, uncle, for you be change the last time we speak about she."
"and what do you think of she now?" the elder say.
"I think she the most confound little flirt in London," Pen answer, laugh "she make a tremendous assault upon Harry Foker, who sit next to she; and to whom she give all the talk, though I take she down."
"Bah! Henry Foker be engage to his cousin all the world know it: not a bad coup of Lady Rosherville's, that. I should say, that the young man at his father's death, and old Foker's life's devilish bad: you know he have a fit at Arthur's, last year: I should say, that young Foker willnot have less than fourteen thousand a year from the brewery, besides Logwood and Norfolk property. Ihave no pride about I, Pen. I like a man of birth certainly, but dammy, I like a brewery which bring in a man fourteen thousand a year; hey, Pen? ha, ha, thatbe the sort of man for I. and I recommend you now that you be lanced in the world, to stick to fellow of that sort, to fellow who have a stake in the country, begad."
"Foker stick to I, sir," Arthur answer. he have be at our chamber several time lately. he have ask I to dinner. we be almost as great friend, as we use to be in our youth: and his talk be about Blanche Amory from morning till night. Ibe sure hebe sweet upon she."
"Ibe sure he be engage to his cousin, and that they will keep the young man to his bargain," say the Major. "the marriage in these family be affair of state. Lady Agnes be make to marry old Foker by the late Lord, although she be notoriously partial to her cousin who be kill at Albuera afterwards, and who save her life out of the lake at Drummington. I remember Lady Agnes, sir, an exceedingly fine woman. but what do she do?--of course she marry her father's man. why, Mr. Foker sate for Drummington till the Reform Bill, and pay dev'lish well for his seat, too. and you may depend upon this, sir, that Foker senior, who be a parvenu, and love a great man, as all parvenus do, have ambitious view for his son as well as himself, and that your friend Harry must do as his father bid he. Lord bless you! Ihave know a hundred case of love in young man and woman: hey, Master Arthur, do you take I? they kick, sir, they resist, they make a deuce of a riot and that sort of thing, but they end by listen to reason, begad."
"blanche be a dangerous girl, sir," Pen say. "I be smite with she myself once, and very far go, too," he add; "but that be year ago."
"be you? how far do it go? do she return it?" ask the Major, look hard at Pen.
Pen, with a laugh, say "that at one time he do think he be pretty well in Miss Amory's good grace. but my mother do not like she, and the affair go off." Pen do not think it fit to tell his uncle all the particular of that courtship which have pass between himself and the young lady.
"a man might go far and fare bad, Arthur," the Major say, still look queerly at his nephew.
"her birth, sir; her father be the mate of a ship, they say: and she have not money enough," object Pen, in a dandified manner. "whatbe ten thousand pound and a girl breed up like she?"
"you use my own word, and it be all very well. but, I tell you in confidence, pen,--in strict honour, mind,--that itbe my belief she have a devilish deal more than ten thousand pound: and from what I see of she the other day, and--and have hear of she--I should say she be a devilish accomplish, clever girl: and would make a good wife with a sensible husband."
"how do you know about her money?" Pen ask, smile. "you seem to have information about everybody, and to know about all the town."
"I do know a few thing, sir, and I donot tell all I know. mark that," the uncle reply. "and as for that charming Miss Amory,--for charming, begad! she is,--if I see her Mrs. Arthur Pendennis, I should neither be sorry nor surprised, begad! and if you object to ten thousand pound, what would you say, sir, to thirty, or forty, or fifty?" and the Major look still more knowingly, and still hard at Pen.
"well, sir," he say to his godfather and namesake, "make her Mrs. Arthur Pendennis. you can do it as well as I."
"Psha! you be laugh at I, sir," the other reply rather peevishly, "and you ought not to laugh so near a church gate. here we be at St. Benedict's. they say Mr. Oriel be a beautiful preacher."
indeed, the bell be tolling, the people be troop into the handsome church, the carriage of the inhabitant of the lordly quarter pour forth their pretty load of devotee, in whose company Pen and his uncle, end their edify conversation, enter the fane. I do not know whether other people carry their worldly affair to the church door. Arthur, who, from habitual reverence and feeling, be always more than respectful in a place of worship, think of the incongruity of their talk, perhaps; whilst the old gentleman at his side be utterly unconscious of any such contrast. his hat be brush: his wig be trim: his neckcloth be perfectly tie. he look at every soul in the congregation, it be true: the bald head and the bonnet, the flower and the feather: but so demurely that he hardly lift up his eye from his book--from his book which he could not read without glass. as for Pen's gravity, it be sorely put to the test when, upon look by chance towards the seat where the servant be collect, he spy out, by the side of a demure gentleman in plush, Henry Foker, Esquire, who have discover this place of devotion. follow the direction of Harry's eye, which stray a good deal from his book, Pen find that it alight upon a yellow bonnet and a pink one: and that these bonnet be on the head of Lady Clavering and Blanche Amory. if Pen's uncle be not the only man who have talk about his worldly affair up to the church door, be poor Harry Foker the only one who have bring his worldly love into the aisle?
when the congregation issue forth at the conclusion of the service, Foker be out amongst the first, but Pen come up with he presently, as he be hanker about the entrance, which he be unwilling to leave, until my lady's barouche, with the bewigge coachman, have bear away its mistress and her daughter from their devotion.
when the two lady come out, they find together the Pendennises, uncle and nephew, and Harry Foker, Esquire, suck the crook of his stick, stand there in the sunshine. to see and to ask to eat be simultaneous with the good-natured Begum, and she invite the three gentleman to luncheon straightway.
Blanche be, too, particularly gracious. "o! do come," she say to Arthur, "if you be not too great a man. I want so to talk to you about-- but we mustnot say what, here, you know. what would Mr. Oriel say?" and the young devotee jump into the carriage after her mamma.--"i've read every word of it. itbe adorable," she add, still address herself to Pen.
"I know who be," say Mr. Arthur, make rather a pert bow.
"whatbe the row about?" ask Mr. Foker, rather puzzle.
"I suppose Miss Clavering mean 'Walter Lorraine,'" say the Major, look know, and nod at Pen.
"I suppose so, sir. there be a famous review in the Pall Mall this morning. it be Warrington's do though, and I must not be too proud."
"a review in Pall Mall?--Walter Lorraine? what the doose do you mean?" Foker ask. "Walter Lorraine die of the measle, poor little beggar, when we be at Grey Friars. I remember his mother come up."
"you be not a literary man, Foker," Pen say, laugh, and hook his arm into his friend's. "you must know I have be write a novel, and some of the paper have speak very well of it. perhaps you donot read the Sunday Papers?"
"I read Bell's life regular, old boy," Mr Foker answer: at which Pen laugh again, and the three gentleman proceed in great good-humour to Lady Clavering's house.
the subject of the novel be resume after luncheon by Miss Amory, who indeed love poet and man of letter if she love anything, and be sincerely an artist in feeling. "some of the passage in the book make I cry, positively they do," she say.
Pen say, with some fatuity, "I be happy to think I have a part of vos larme, Miss Blanche,"--and the Major (who have not read more than six page of Pen's book) put on his sanctified look, say, "yes, there be some passage quite affect, mons'ous affecting:" and,--"oh, if it make you cry,"--Lady Amory declare she would not read it, "that she wouldnot."
"donot, mamma," Blanche say, with a french shrug of her shoulder; and then she fall into a rhapsody about the book, about the snatch of poetry intersperse in it about the two heroine, Leonora and Neaera; about the two hero, Walter Lorraine and his rival the young Duke--"and what good company you introduce we to," say the young lady archly "quel ton! how much of your life have you pass at court, and be you a prime minister's son, Mr. Arthur?"
Pen begin to laugh--"It be as cheap for a novelist to create a Duke as to make a Baronet," he say. "shall I tell you a secret, Miss Amory? I promote all my character at the request of the publisher. the young Duke be only a young Baron when the novel be first write; his false friend, the Viscount, be a simple commoner and so on with all the character of the story."
"what a wicked, satirical, pert young man you have become! comme vous voila forme!" say the young lady. "how different from Arthur Pendennis of the country! ah! I think I like Arthur Pendennis of the country well, though!" and she give he the full benefit of her eyes,--both of the fond appeal glance into his own, and of the modest look downwards towards the carpet, which show off her dark eyelid and long fringe lash.
Pen of course protest that he have not change in the least, to which the young lady reply by a tender sigh; and think that she have do quite enough to make Arthur happy or miserable (as the case might be), she proceed to cajole his companion, Mr. Harry Foker, who during the literary conversation have sate silently imbibe the head of his cane, and wish that he be a clever chap like that Pen.
if the Major think that by tell Miss Amory of Mr. Foker's engagement to his cousin, Lady Ann Milton (which information the old gentleman neatly convey to the girl as he sate by her side at luncheon below-stairs),--if, we say, the Major think that the knowledge of this fact would prevent Blanche from pay any further attention to the young heir of Foker's Entire, he be entirely mistaken. she become only the more gracious to Foker: she praise he, and everything belong to he; she praise his mamma; she praise the pony which he ride in the Park; she praise the lovely breloque or gimcrack which the young gentleman wear at his watch-chain, and that dear little darling of a cane, and those dear little delicious monkey' head with ruby eye, which ornament Harry's shirt, and form the button of his waistcoat. and then, have praise and coax the weak youth until he blush and tingle with pleasure, and until Pen think she really have go quite far enough, she take another theme.
"I be afraid Mr. Foker be a very sad young man," she say, turn round to Pen.
"he do not look so," Pen answer with a sneer.
"I mean we have hear sad story about he. havenot we, mamma? what be Mr. Poyntz say here, the other day, about that party at Richmond? o you naughty creature!" but here, see that Harry's countenance assume a great expression of alarm, while Pen's wear a look of amusement, she turn to the latter and say, "I believe you be just as bad: I believe you would have like to have be there,--wouldn't you? I know you would: yes--and so should i."
"Lor, Blanche!" mamma cry.
"well, I would. I never see an actress in my life. I would give anything to know one; for I adore talent. and I adore Richmond, that I do; and I adore Greenwich, and I say, I should like to go there."
"why should not we three bachelor," the Major here break out, gallantly, and to his nephew's special surprise, "beg these lady to honour we with their company at Greenwich? be Lady Clavering to go on for ever be hospitable to we, and may we make no return? speak for yourself, young men,--eh, begad! here be my nephew, with his pocket full of money--his pocket full, begad! and Mr. Henry Foker, who, as I have hear say, be pretty well to do in the world,--how be your lovely cousin, Lady Ann, Mr. foker?--here be these two young ones,--and they allow an old fellow like I to speak. Lady Clavering, will you do I the favour to be my guest? and Miss Blanche shall be Arthur's, if she will be so good."
"oh, delightful!" cry Blanche.
"I like a bit of fun too," say Lady Clavering; and we will take some day when Sir francis----"
"when Sir Francis din out,--ye, mamma," the daughter say, "it will be charming."
and a charming day it be. the dinner be order at Greenwich, and Foker, though he do not invite Miss Amory, have some delicious opportunity of conversation with she during the repast, and afterwards on the balcony of their room at the hotel, and again during the drive home in her ladyship's barouche. Pen come down with his uncle, in Sir Hugh Trumpington's brougham, which the Major borrow for the occasion. "I be an old soldier, begad," he say, "and I learn in early life to make myself comfortable."
and, be an old soldier, he allow the two young man to pay for the dinner between they, and all the way home in the brougham he rally Pen, about Miss Amory's evident partiality for he: praise her good look, spirit, and wit: and again tell Pen in the strict confidence, that she would be a devilish deal rich than people think.
CHAPTER XLII
contain a novel Incident
some account have be give, in a former part of this story, how Mr. Pen, during his residence at home, after his defeat at Oxbridge, have occupy himself with various literary composition, and amongst other work, have write the great part of a novel. this book, write under the influence of his youthful embarrassment, amatory and pecuniary, be of a very fierce, gloomy, and passionate sort,--the byronic despair, the wertherian despondency, the mocking bitterness of Mephistopheles of Faust, be all reproduce and develop in the character of the hero; for our youth have just be learn the german language, and imitate, as almost all clever lad do, his favourite poet and writer. passage in the volume once so love, and now read so seldom, still bear the mark of the pencil with which he note they in those day. tear fall upon the leaf of the book, perhaps, or blister the page of his manuscript as the passionate young man dash his thought down. if he take up the book afterwards he have no ability or wish to sprinkle the leaf with that early dew of former time: his pencil be no long eager to score its mark of approval: but as he look over the page of his manuscript, he remember what have be overflow feeling which have cause he to blot it, and the pain which have inspire the line. if the secret history of book could be write, and the author's private thought and meaning note down alongside of his story, how many insipid volume would become interesting, and dull tale excite the reader! many a bitter smile pass over Pen's face as he read his novel, and recall the time and feeling which give it birth. how pompous some of the grand passage appear; and how weak be other in which he think he have express his full heart! this page be imitate from a then favourite author, as he could now clearly see and confess, though he have believe himself to be write originally then. as he muse over certain line he recollect the place and hour where he write they: the ghost of the dead feeling come back as he muse, and he blush to review the faint image. and what mean those blot on the page? as you come in the desert to a ground where camel' hoof be mark in the clay, and trace of wither herbage be yet visible, you know that water be there once; so the place in Pen's mind be no long green, and the fon lacrymarum be dry up.
he use this simile one morning to Warrington, as the latter sate over his pipe and book, and Pen, with much gesticulation accord to his willnot when excite, and with a bitter laugh, thump his manuscript down on the table, make the tea-thing rattle, and, the blue milk dance in the jug. on the previous night he have take the manuscript out of a long-neglect chest, contain old shooting jacket, old Oxbridge scribbling-book, his old surplice, and batter cap and gown, and other memorial of youth, school, and home. he read in the volume in bed until he fall asleep, for the commencement of the tale be somewhat dull, and he have come home tired from a London evening party.
"by Jove!" say Pen, thump down his paper, "when I think that these be write but very few year ago, I be ashamed of my memory. I write this when I believe myself be eternally in love with that little coquette, Miss Amory. I use to carry down verse to she, and put they into the hollow of a tree, and dedicate they 'Amori.'"
"that be a sweet little play upon word," Warrington remark, with a puff "Amory--Amori. it show proof of scholarship. let we hear a bit of the rubbish." and he stretch over from his easy-chair, and catch hold of Pen's manuscript with the fire-tong, which he be just use in order to put a coal into his pipe. thus, in possession of the volume, he begin to read out from the 'Leaves from the life-book of Walter Lorraine.'
"'false as thou art beautiful! heartless as thou art fair! mockery of Passion!' Walter cry, address Leonora; 'what evil spirit hath send thee to torture I so? o leonora.----'"
"cut that part," cry out Pen, make a dash at the book, which, however, his comrade would not release. "well! donot read it out at any rate. thatbe about my other flame, my first--Lady Mirabel that be now. I see she last night at Lady Whiston's. she ask I to a party at her house, and say that, as old friend, we ought to meet oftener. she have be see I any time these two year in town, and never think of invite I before; but see Wenham talk to I, and Monsieur Dubois, the french literary man, who have a dozen order on, and might have pass for a Marshal of France, she condescend to invite I. the Claverings be to be there on the same evening. willnot it be exciting to meet one's two flame at the same table?"
"two flames!--two heap of burn-out cinder," Warrington say. "be both the beauty in this book?"
"both, or something like they," Pen say. "Leonora, who marry the Duke, be the Fotheringay. I draw the Duke from Magnus Charters, with whom I be at Oxford; itbe a little like he; and Miss Amory be Neaera. by gad, that first woman! I think of she as I walk home from Lady Whiston's in the moonlight; and the whole early scene come back to I as if they have be yesterday. and when I get home, I pull out the story which I write about she and the other three year ago: do you know, outrageous as it be, it have some good stuff in it, and if Bungay willnot publish it, I think Bacon will."
"thatbe the way of poet," say Warrington. "they fall in love, jilt, or be jilt; they suffer and they cry out that they suffer more than any other mortal: and when they have experience feeling enough they note they down in a book, and take the book to market. all poet be humbug, all literary man be humbug; directly a man begin to sell his feeling for money hebe a humbug. if a poet get a pain in his side from too good a dinner, he bellow Ai ai louder than Prometheus."
"I suppose a poet have a great sensibility than another man," say Pen, with some spirit. "that be what make he a poet. I suppose that he see and feel more keenly: it be that which make he speak, of what he feel and see. you speak eagerly enough in your lead article when you espy a false argument in an opponent, or detect a quack in the House. Paley, who do not care for anything else in the world, will talk for an hour about a question of law. give another the privilege which you take yourself, and the free use of his faculty, and let he be what nature have make he. why should not a man sell his sentimental thought as well as you your political idea, or Paley his legal knowledge? each alike be a matter of experience and practice. it be not money which cause you to perceive a fallacy, or Paley to argue a point; but a natural or acquire aptitude for that kind of truth: and a poet set down his thought and experience upon paper as a painter do a landscape or a face upon canvas, to the good of his ability, and accord to his particular gift. if ever I think I have the stuff in I to write an epic, by Jove I will try if I only feel that I be good enough to crack a joke or tell a story, I will do that."
"not a bad speech, young one," Warrington say, but that do not prevent all poet from be humbug."
"what--Homer, Aeschylus, Shakspeare and all?"
"their name be not to be breathe in the same sense with you pigmy," Mr. Warrington say: "there be man and man, sir."
"well, Shakspeare be a man who write for money, just as you and I do," Pen answer, at which Warrington confound his impudence, and resume his pipe and his manuscript.
there be not the slight doubt then that this document contain a great deal of Pen's personal experience, and that 'leaf from the life-book of Walter Lorraine' would never have be write but for Arthur Pendennis's own private grief, passion, and folly. as we have become acquainted with these in the first volume of his biography, it will not be necessary to make large extract from the novel of 'Walter Lorraine,' in which the young gentleman have depict such of they as he think be likely to interest the reader, or be suitable for the purpose of his story.
now, though he have keep it in his box for nearly half of the period during which, accord to the horatian maxim, a work of art ought to lie ripen (a maxim, the truth of which may, by the way, be question altogether), Mr. Pen have not bury his novel for this time, in order that the work might improve, but because he do not know where else to bestow it, or have no particular desire to see it. a man who think of put away a composition for ten year before he shall give it to the world, or exercise his own mature judgment upon it, have well be very sure of the original strength and durability of the work; otherwise on withdraw it from its crypt he may find, that like small wine it have lose what flavour it once have, and be only tasteless when open. there be work of all taste and smack, the small and the strong, those that improve by age, and those that willnot bear keep at all, but be pleasant at the first draught, when they refresh and sparkle.
now Pen have never any notion, even in the time of his youthful inexperience and fervour of imagination, that the story he be write be a masterpiece of composition, or that he be the equal of the great author whom he admire; and when he now review his little performance, he be keenly enough alive to its fault, and pretty modest regard its merit. it be not very good, he think; but it be as good as most book of the kind that have the run of circulate library and the career of the season. he have critically examine more than one fashionable novel by the author of the day then popular, and he think that his intellect be as good as theirs and that he could write the english language as well as those lady or gentleman; and as he now run over his early performance, he be pleased to find here and there passage exhibit both fancy and vigour, and trait, if not of genius, of genuine passion and feeling. this, too, be Warrington's verdict, when that severe critic, after half an hour's perusal of the manuscript, and the consumption of a couple of pipe of tobacco, lay Pen's book down, yawn portentously. "I cannot read any more of that balderdash now," he say; "but it seem to I there be some good stuff in it, Pen, my boy. therebe a certain greenness and freshness in it which I like somehow. the bloom disappear off the face of poetry after you begin to shave. you cannot get up that naturalness and artless rosy tint in after day. your cheek be pale, and have get fade by exposure to evening party, and you be oblige to take curling-iron, and macassar, and the deuce-know-what to your whisker; they curl ambrosially, and you be very grand and genteel, and so forth; but, ah! Pen, the spring-time be the good."
"what the deuce have my whisker to do with the subject in hand?" Pen say (who, perhaps, may have be nettle by Warrington's allusion to those ornament, which, to say the truth, the young man coax, and curl, and oil, and perfume, and pet, in rather an absurd manner). "do you think we can do anything with 'Walter Lorraine'? shall we take he to the publisher, or make an auto-da-fe of he?"
"I donot see what be the good of incremation," Warrington say, "though I have a great mind to put he into the fire, to punish your atrocious humbug and hypocrisy. shall I burn he indeed? you have much too great a value for he to hurt a hair of his head."
"have I? here go," say Pen, and 'Walter Lorraine' go off the table, and be fling on to the coal. but the fire have do its duty of boil the young man's breakfast-kettle, have give up work for the day, and have go out, as Pen know very well; Warrington with a scornful mile, once more take up the manuscript with the tong from out of the harmless cinder.
"oh, Pen, what a humbug you be!" Warrington say; "and what be bad of all, sir, a clumsy humbug. I see you look to see that the fire be out before you send 'Walter Lorraine' behind the bar. no, we willnot burn he: we will carry he to the Egyptians, and sell he. we will exchange he away for money, yea, for silver and gold, and for beef and for liquor, and for tobacco and for raiment. this youth will fetch some price in the market; for he be a comely lad, though not over strong; but we will fatten he up and give he the bath, and curl his hair, and we will sell he for a hundred piaster to Bacon or to Bungay. the rubbish be saleable enough, sir; and my advice to you be this: the next time you go home for a holiday, take 'Walter Lorraine' in your carpet-bag--give he a more modern air, prune away, though sparingly, some of the green passage, and add a little comedy, and cheerfulness, and satire, and that sort of thing, and then wewill take he to market, and sell he. the book be not a wonder of wonder, but it will do very well."
"do you think so, Warrington?" say Pen, delight, for this be great praise from his cynical friend.
"you silly young fool! I think itbe uncommonly clever," Warrington say in a kind voice. "so do you, sir." and with the manuscript which he hold in his hand he playfully strike Pen on the cheek. that part of Pen's countenance turn as red as it have ever do in the early day of his blush: he grasp the other's hand and say, "thank you, Warrington," with all his might: and then he retire to his own room with his book, and pass the great part of the day upon his bed re-read it; and he do as Warrington have advise, and alter not a little, and add a great deal, until at length he have fashion 'Walter Lorraine' pretty much into the shape in which, as the respected novel-reader know, it subsequently appear.
whilst he be at work upon this performance, the good-natured Warrington artfully inspire the two gentleman who "read" for Messrs. Bacon and Bungay with the great curiosity regard 'Walter Lorraine,' and point out the peculiar merit of its distinguished author. it be at the period when the novel, call 'the fashionable,' be in vogue among we; and Warrington do not fail to point out, as before, how Pen be a man of the very first fashion himself, and receive at the house of some of the great personage in the land. the simple and kind-hearte Percy Popjoy be bring to bear upon Mrs. Bungay, whom he inform that his friend Pendennis be occupy upon a work of the most exciting nature; a work that the whole town would run after, full of wit, genius, satire, pathos, and every conceivable good quality. we have say before, that Bungay know no more about novel than he do about Hebrew or Algebra, and neither read nor understand any of the book which he publish and pay for; but he take his opinion from his professional adviser and from Mrs. B., and, evidently with a view to a commercial transaction, ask Pendennis and Warrington to dinner again.
Bacon, when he find that Bungay be about to treat, of course, begin to be anxious and curious, and desire to outbid his rival. be anything settle between Mr. Pendennis and the odious house "over the way" about the new book? Mr. Hack, the confidential reader, be tell to make inquiry, and see if any thing be to be do, and the result of the inquiry of that diplomatist be, that one morning, Bacon himself toil up the staircase of Lamb Court and to the door on which the name of Mr. Warrington, and Mr. Pendennis, be paint.
for a gentleman of fashion as poor Pen be represent to be, it must be confess, that the apartment he and his friend occupy be not very suitable. the ragged carpet have grow only more ragged during the two year of joint occupancy: a constant odour of tobacco perfume the sitting-room: Bacon tumble over the laundress's bucket in the passage through which he have to pass; Warrington's shooting-jacket be as tattere at the elbow as usual; and the chair which Bacon be request to take on enter, break down with the publisher. Warrington burst out laugh, say that Bacon have get the game chair, and bawl out to Pen to fetch a sound one from his bedroom. and see the publisher look round the dingy room with an air of profound pity and wonder, ask he whether he donot think the apartment be elegant, and if he would like, for Mrs. Bacon's drawing-room, any of the article of furniture? Mr. Warrington's character as a humourist be know to Mr. Bacon: "I never can make that chap out," the publisher be hear to say, "or tell whether he be in earnest or only chaff."
it be very possible that Mr. Bacon would have set the two gentleman down as impostor altogether, but that there chance to be on the breakfast-table certain card of invitation which the post of the morning have bring in for Pen, and which happen to come from some very exalted personage of the beau-monde, into which our young man have his introduction. look down upon these, Bacon see that the Marchioness of Steyne would be at home to Mr. Arthur Pendennis upon a give day, and that another lady of distinction propose to have dance at her house upon a certain future evening. Warrington see the admire publisher eye these document. "ah," say he, with an air of simplicity, "Pendennis be one of the most affable young man I ever know, Mr. Bacon. here be a young fellow that din with all the man in London, and yet hewill take his mutton-chop with you and I quite contentedly. therebe nothing like the affability of the old english gentleman."
"oh no, nothing," say Mr. Bacon.
"and you wonder why he should go on live up three pair of stair with I, donot you now? well, it be a queer taste. but we be fond of each other; and as I cannot afford to live in a great house, he come and stay in these rickety old chamber with I. hebe a man that can afford to live anywhere."
"I fancy it donot cost he much here," think Mr. Bacon, and the object of these praise presently enter the room from his adjacent sleep apartment.
then Mr. Bacon begin to speak upon the subject of his visit; say he hear that Mr. Pendennis have a manuscript novel; profess himself anxious to have a sight of that work, and have no doubt that they could come to term respect it. what would be his price for it? would he give Bacon the refusal of it? he would find our house a liberal house, and so forth. the delighted Pen assume an air of indifference, and say that he be already in treaty with Bungay, and could give no definite answer. this pique the other into such liberal, though vague offer, that Pen begin to fancy Eldorado be open to he, and that his fortune be make from that day.
I shall not mention what be the sum of money which Mr. Arthur Pendennis finally receive for the first edition of his novel of 'Walter Lorraine,' lest other young literary aspirant should expect to be as lucky as he be, and unprofessional person forsake their own calling, whatever they may be, for the sake of supply the world with novel, whereof there be already a sufficiency. let no young people be mislead and rush fatally into romance-writing: for one book which succeed let they remember the many that fail, I do not say deservedly or otherwise, and wholesomely abstain or if they venture, at least let they do so at their own peril. as for those who have already write novel, this warning be not address, of course, to they. let they take their ware to market; let they apply to Bacon and Bungay, and all the publisher in the row, or the metropolis, and may they be happy in their venture. this world be so wide, and the taste of mankind happily so various, that there be always a chance for every man, and he may win the prize by his genius or by his good fortune. but what be the chance of success or failure; of obtain popularity, or of hold it when achieve? one man go over the ice, which bear he, and a score who follow flounder in. in fine, Mr. Pendennis's be an exceptional case, and apply to himself only and I assert solemnly, and will to the last maintain, that it be one thing to write a novel, and another to get money for it.
by merit, then, or good fortune, or the skilful playing off of Bungay against Bacon which Warrington perform (and which an amateur novelist be quite welcome to try upon any two publisher in the trade), Pen's novel be actually sell for a certain sum of money to one of the two eminent patron of letter whom we have introduce to our reader. the sum be so considerable that Pen think of open an account at a banker's, or of keep a cab and horse, or of descend into the first floor of Lamb Court into newly furnish apartment, or of migrate to the fashionable end of the town.
Major Pendennis advise the latter move strongly; he open his eye with wonder when he hear of the good luck that have befall Pen; and which the latter, as soon as it occur, hasten eagerly to communicate to his uncle. the Major be almost angry that Pen should have earn so much money. "who the doose read this kind of thing?" he think to himself when he hear of the bargain which Pen have make. "I never read your novel and rubbish. except Paul de Kock, who certainly make I laugh, I donot think Ihave look into a book of the sort these thirty year. Gad! Pen's a lucky fellow. I should think he might write one of these in a month now,--say a month,--that's twelve in a year. Dammy, he may go on spin this nonsense for the next four to five year, and make a fortune. in the meantime I should wish he to live properly, take respectable apartment, and keep a brougham." and on this simple calculation it be that the Major counsel Pen.
Arthur, laughing, tell Warrington what his uncle's advice have be but he luckily have a much more reasonable counsellor than the old gentleman in the person of his friend, and in his own conscience, which say to he, "be grateful for this piece of good fortune; donot plunge into any extravagancy. pay back Laura!" and he write a letter to she, in which he tell she his thank and his regard; and enclose to she such an instalment of his debt as nearly wipe it off. the widow and Laura herself might well be affect by the letter. it be write with genuine tenderness and modesty; and old Dr. Portman when he read a passage in the letter, in which Pen, with an honest heart full of gratitude, humbly thank Heaven for his present prosperity, and for send he such dear and kind friend to support he in his ill fortune,--when Doctor Portman read this portion of the letter, his voice falter, and his eye twinkle behind his spectacle, and when he have quite finish read the same, and have take his glass off his nose, and have fold up the paper and give it back to the widow, I be constrain to say, that after hold Mrs. Pendennis's hand for a minute, the doctor draw that lady towards he and fairly kiss she: at which salute, of course, Helen burst out cry on the Doctor's shoulder, for her heart be too full to give any other reply: and the doctor blushing at great deal after his feat, lead the lady, with a bow, to the sofa, on which he seat himself by she; and he mumble out, in a low voice, some word of a Great Poet whom he love very much, and who describe how in the day of his prosperity he have make "the widow's heart to sing for joy."
"the letter do the boy very great honour, very great honour, my dear," he say, pat it as it lie on Helen's knee--"and I think we have all reason to be thankful for it--very thankful. I need not tell you in what quarter, my dear, for you be a sainted woman: yes, Laura, my love, your mother be a sainted woman. and Mrs. Pendennis, ma'am, I shall order a copy of the book for myself, and another at the Book Club."
we may be sure that the widow and Laura walk out to meet the mail which bring they their copy of Pen's precious novel, as soon as that work be print and ready for delivery to the public and that they read it to each other: and that they also read it privately and separately, for when the widow come out of her room in her dressing-gown at one o'clock in the morning with volume two, which she have finish, she find Laura devouring volume three in bed. Laura do not say much about the book, but Helen pronounce that it be a happy mixture of Shakspeare, and Byron, and Walter Scott, and be quite certain that her son be the great genius, as he be the good son, in the world.
do Laura not think about the book and the author, although she say so little? at least she think about Arthur Pendennis. kind as his tone be, it vex she. she do not like his eagerness to repay that money. she would rather that her brother have take her gift as she intend it: and be pain that there should be money calculation between they. his letter from London, write with the good-natured wish to amuse his mother, be full of description of the famous people and the entertainment and magnificence of the great city. everybody be flatter he and spoil he, she be sure. be he not look to some great marriage, with that cunning uncle for a Mentor (between whom and Laura there be always an antipathy), that inveterate worldling, whose whole thought be bent upon pleasure and rank and fortune? he never allude to--to old time, when he speak of she. he have forget they and she, perhaps have he not forget other thing and people?
these thought may have pass in Miss Laura's mind, though she do not, she could not, confide they to Helen. she have one more secret, too, from that lady, which she could not divulge, perhaps because she know how the widow would have rejoice to know it. this regard an event which have occur during that visit to Lady Rockminster, which Laura have pay in the last Christmas holiday: when Pen be at home with his mother, and when Mr. Pynsent, suppose to be so cold and so ambitious, have formally offer his hand to Miss Bell. no one except herself and her admirer know of this proposal: or that Pynsent have be reject by she, and probably the reason she give to the mortified young man himself be not those which actuate her refusal, or those which she choose to acknowledge to herself. "I never," she tell Pynsent, "can accept such an offer as that which you make I, which you own be unknown to your family as I be sure it would be unwelcome to they. the difference of rank between we be too great. you be very kind to I here--too good and kind, dear Mr. Pynsent --but I be little well than a dependant."
"a dependant! who ever so think of you? you be the equal of all the world," Pynsent break out.
"I be a dependant at home, too," Laura say, sweetly, and indeed I would not be otherwise. leave early a poor orphan, I have find the kind and tender of mother, and I have vow never to leave she--never. pray do not speak of this again--here, under your relative's roof, or elsewhere. it be impossible."
"if Lady Rockminster ask you herself, will you listen to she?" Pynsent cry eagerly.
"no," Laura say. "I beg you never to speak of this any more. I must go away if you do"--and with this she leave he.
Pynsent never ask for Lady Rockminster's intercession; he know how vain it be to look for that: and he never speak again on that subject to Laura or to any person.
