when at length the famous novel appear it not only meet with applause from more impartial critic than Mrs. Pendennis, but, luckily for Pen it suit the taste of the public, and obtain a quick and considerable popularity before two month be over, Pen have the satisfaction and surprise of see the second edition of 'Walter Lorraine' advertise in the newspaper; and enjoy the pleasure of read and send home the critique of various literary journal and reviewer upon his book. their censure do not much affect he; for the good-natured young man be dispose to accept with considerable humility the dispraise of other. nor do their praise elate he over much; for, like most honest person he have his own opinion about his own performance, and when a critic praise he in the wrong place he be rather hurt than pleased by the compliment. but if a review of his work be very laudatory, it be a great pleasure to he to send it home to his mother at Fairoaks, and to think of the joy which it would give there. there be some nature, and perhaps, as we have say, Pendennis's be one, which be improve and soften by prosperity and kindness, as there be man of other disposition, who become arrogant and graceless under good fortune. happy he, who can endure one or the other with modesty and good-humour! lucky he who have be educate to bear his fate, whatsoever it may be, by an early example of uprightness, and a childish training in honour!
CHAPTER XLIII
Alsatia
breed up, like a bailiff or a shabby attorney, about the purlieus of the Inns of Court, Shepherd's Inn be always to be find in the close neighbourhood of Lincoln's-Inn Fields, and the Temple. some where behind the black gable and smutty chimney-stack of Wych Street, Holywell Street, Chancery Lane, the quadrangle lie, hide from the outer world; and it be approach by curious passage and ambiguous smoky alley, on which the sun have forget to shine. slop-seller, brandy-ball and hard-bake vendor, purveyor of theatrical print for youth, dealer in dingy furniture and bedding suggestive of anything but sleep, line the narrow wall and dark casement with their ware. the door be many-bell: and crowd of dirty child form endless group about the step: or around the shell-fish dealer' tray in these court; whereof the damp pavement resound with patten, and be drabble with a never-fail mud. ballad-singer come and chant here, in deadly guttural tone, satirical song against the Whig administration, against the bishop and dignified clergy, against the german relative of an august royal family: Punch set up his theatre, sure of an audience, and occasionally of a halfpenny from the swarm occupant of the house: woman scream after their child for loiter in the gutter, or, bad still, against the husband who come reel from the gin-shop;--there be a ceaseless din and life in these court out of which you pass into the tranquil, old-fashioned quadrangle of Shepherd's Inn. in a mangy little grass-plat in the centre rise up the statue of Shepherd, defend by iron railing from the assault of boy. the hall of the Inn, on which the founder's arm be paint, occupy one side of the square, the tall and ancient chamber be carry round other two side, and over the central archway, which lead into Oldcastle Street, and so into the great London thoroughfare.
the Inn may have be occupy by lawyer once: but the laity have long since be admit into its precinct, and I do not know that any of the principal legal firm have their chamber here. the office of the Polwheedle and Tredyddlum Copper Mines occupy one set of the ground-floor chamber; the Registry of Patent Inventions and Union of Genius and Capital Company, another;--the only gentleman whose name figure here, and in the "Law List," be Mr. Campion, who wear mustachio, and who come in his cab twice or thrice in a week; and whose West End office be in Curzon Street, Mayfair, where Mrs. Campion entertain the nobility and gentry to whom her husband lend money. there, and on his glaze card, he be Mr. Somerset Campion; here he be Campion and Co.; and the same tuft which ornament his chin, sprout from the under lip of the rest of the firm. it be splendid to see his cab-horse harness blaze with heraldic bearing, as the vehicle stop at the door lead to his chamber: the horse fling froth off his nostril as he chafe and toss under the shin bit. the rein and the breech of the groom be glitter white,--the lustre of that equipage make a sunshine in that shady place.
our old friend, Captain Costigan, have examine Campion's cab and horse many an afternoon, as he trail about the court in his carpet slipper and dressing-gown, with his old hat cock over his eye. he sun himself there after his breakfast when the day be suitable; and go and pay a visit to the porter's lodge, where he pat the head of the child, and talk to Mrs. Bolton about the thayatre and I daughther Leedy Mirabel. Mrs. Bolton be herself in the profession once, and dance at the Wells in early day as the thirteenth of Mr. Serle's forty pupil.
Costigan live in the third floor at No. 4, in the room which be Mr. Podmore's, and whose name be still on the door--(somebody else's name, by the way, be on almost all the door in Shepherd's Inn). when Charley Podmore (the pleasing tenor singer, T.R.D.L., and at the Back Kitchen Concert Rooms) marry, and go to live at Lambeth, he cede his chamber to Mr. Bows and Captain Costigan, who occupy they in common now, and you may often hear the tone of Mr. Bows's piano of fine day when the window be open, acid when he be practise for amusement, or for the instruction of a theatrical pupil, of whom he have one or two. Fanny Bolton be one, the porteress's daughter, who have hear tell of her mother's theatrical glory, which she long to emulate. she have a good voice and a pretty face and figure for the stage; and she prepare the room and make the bed and breakfast for Messrs. Costigan and Bows, in return for which the latter instruct she in music and singing. but for his unfortunate propensity to liquor (and in that excess she suppose that all man of fashion indulge), she think the Captain the fine gentleman in the world, and believe in all the version of all his story, and she be very fond of Mr. Bows too, and very grateful to he, and this shy queer old gentleman have a fatherly fondness for she too, for in truth his heart be full of kindness, and he be never easy unless he love somebody.
Costigan have have the carriage of visitor of distinction before his humble door in Shepherd's Inn: and to hear he talk of a morning (for his evening song be of a much more melancholy nature) you would fancy that Sir Charles and Lady Mirabel be in the constant habit of call at his chamber, and bring with they the select nobility to visit the "old man, the honest old half-pay Captain, poor old Jack Costigan," as Cos call himself.
the truth be, that Lady Mirabel have leave her husband's card (which have be stick in the little look-glass over the mantelpiece of the sitting-room at No. 4, for these many month past), and have come in person to see her father, but not of late day. a kind person, dispose to discharge her duty gravely, upon her marriage with Sir Charles she settle a little pension upon her father, who occasionally be admit to the table of his daughter and son-in-law. at first poor cos's behaviour "in the hoight of poloit societee," as he denominate Lady Mirabel's drawing-room table, be harmless, if it be absurd. as he clothe his person in his good attire, so he select the long and rich word in his vocabulary to deck his conversation, and adopt a solemnity of demeanour which strike with astonishment all those person in whose company he happen to be.--"was your Leedyship in the Pork to dee?" he would demand of his daughter. "I look for your equipage in veen:--the poor old man be not gratify by the soight of his daughther's choriot. Sir Chorlus, I see your neem at the Levee; manybe the Levee at the Castle at Dublin that poor old Jack Costigan have attend in his time. do the Juke look pretty well? Bedad, Iwill call at Apsley House and lave I cyard upon 'um. I thank ye, James, a little dthrop more champeane." indeed, he be magnificent in his courtesy to all, and address his observation not only to the master and the guest, but to the domestic who wait at the table, and who have some difficulty in maintain their professional gravity while they wait on Captain Costigan.
on the first two or three visit to his son-in-law, Costigan maintain a strict sobriety, content to make up for his lose time when he get to the Back Kitchen, where he brag about his son-in-law's dart and burgundee, until his own utterance begin to fail he, over his sixth tumbler of whisky-punch. but with familiarity his caution vanish, and poor cos lamentably disgrace himself at Sir Charles Mirabel's table, by premature inebriation. a carriage be call for he: the hospitable door be shut upon he. often and sadly do he speak to his friend at the Kitchen of his resemblance to King Lear in the plee--of his have a thankless choild, bedad--of his be a pore wear-out lonely old man, dthriven to dthrinke by ingratitude, and seek to dthrown his sorrow in punch. it be painful to be oblige to record the weakness of father, but it must be furthermore tell of Costigan, that when his credit be exhausted and his money go, he would not unfrequently beg money from his daughter, and make statement to she not altogether consistent with strict truth. on one day a bailiff be about to lead he to prison, he write, "unless the--to you insignificant--sum of three pound five can be forthcoming to liberate a poor man's grey hair from gaol." and the good-natured Lady Mirabel despatch the money necessary for her father's liberation, with a caution to he to be more economical for the future. on a second occasion the Captain meet with a frightful accident, and break a plate-glass window in the Strand, for which the proprietor of the shop hold he liable. the money be forthcoming on this time too, to repair her papa's disaster, and be carry down by Lady Mirabel's servant to the slipshod messenger and aide-de-camp of the Captain, who bring the letter announce his mishap. if the servant have follow the Captain's aide-de-camp who carry the remittance, he would have see that gentleman, a person of Costigan's country too (for have we not say, that however poor an irish gentleman be, he always have a poor irish gentleman to run on his errand and transact his pecuniary affair?), call a cab from the near stand, and rattle down to the Roscius Head, Harlequin Yard, Drury Lane, where the Captain be indeed in pawn, and for several glass contain rum-and-water, or other spirituous refreshment, of which he and his staff have partake. on a third melancholy occasion he write that he be attack by illness, and want money to pay the physician whom he be compel to call in; and this time Lady Mirabel, alarmed about her father's safety, and perhaps reproach herself that she have of late lose sight of her father, call for her carriage and drive to Shepherd's Inn, at the gate of which she alight, whence she find the way to her father's chamber, "no. 4, third floor, name of Podmore over the door," the porteress say, with many curtsy, point towards the door of the house, into which the affectionate daughter enter and mount the dingy stair. alas! the door, surmount by the name of Podmore, be open to she by poor cos in his shirt-sleeve, and prepare with the gridiron to receive the mutton-chop which Mrs. Bolton have go to purchase.
also, it be not pleasant for Sir Charles Mirabel to have letter constantly address to he at Brookes's, with the information that Captain Costigan be in the hall, wait for an answer; or when he go to play his rubber at the Travellers', to be oblige to shoot out of his brougham and run up the step rapidly, lest his father-in-law should seize upon he; and to think that while he read his paper or play his whist, the Captain be walk on the opposite side of Pall Mall, with that dreadful cocked hat, and the eye beneath it fix steadily upon the window of the club. Sir Charles be a weak man; he be old, and have many infirmity: he cry about his father-in-law to his wife, whom he adore with senile infatuation: he say he must go abroad,--he must go and live in the country--he should die or have another fit if he see that man again--he know he should. and it be only by pay a second visit to Captain Costigan, and represent to he, that if he plague Sir Charles by letter or address he in the street, or make any further application for loan, his allowance would be withdraw altogether, that Lady Mirabel be enable to keep she papa in order, and to restore tranquillity to her husband. and on occasion of this visit, she sternly rebuke bow for not keep a well watch over the captain; desire that he should not be allow to drink in that shameful way; and that the people at the horrid tavern which he frequent should be tell, upon no account to give he credit. "Papa's conduct be bring I to the grave," she say (though she look perfectly healthy), "and you, as an old man, Mr. Bows, and one that pretend to have a regard for we, ought to be ashamed of abet he in it." those be the thank which honest bow get for his friendship and his life's devotion. and I do not suppose that the old philosopher be much bad off than many other man, or have great reason to grumble.
on the second floor of the next house to Bows's, in Shepherd's Inn, at No. 3, live two other acquaintance of our: Colonel Altamont, agent to the Nawaub of Lucknow, and Captain Chevalier Edward Strong. no name at all be over their door. the Captain do not choose to let all the world know where he live and his card bear the address of a Jermyn Street hotel; and as for the Ambassador Plenipotentiary of the indian potentate, he be not an envoy accredit to the Courts of St. James's or Leadenhall Street but be here on a confidential mission quite independent of the East India Company or the Board of Control. "in fact," Strong say, "Colonel Altamont's object be financial, and to effectuate a sale of some of the principal diamond and ruby of the Lucknow crown, his wish be not to report himself at the India House or in Cannon row, but rather to negotiate with private capitalist--with whom he have have important transaction both in this country and on the continent."
we have say that these anonymous chamber of Strong's have be very comfortably furnish since the arrival of Sir Francis Clavering in London, and the Chevalier might boast with reason to the friend who visit he, that few retire captain be more snugly quarter than he, in his crib in Shepherd's Inn. there be three room below: the office where Strong transact his business--whatever that might be--and where still remain the desk and railing of the depart official who have precede he, and the Chevalier's own bedroom and sitting-room; and a private stair lead out of the office to two upper apartment, the one occupy by Colonel Altamont, and the other serve as the kitchen of the establishment, and the bedroom of Mr. Grady, the attendant. these room be on a level with the apartment of our friend Bows and Costigan next door at No. 4; and by reach over the communicating lead, Grady could command the mignonette-box which bloom in Bows's window.
from Grady's kitchen casement often come odour still more fragrant. the three old soldier who form the garrison of no. 3 be all skilled in the culinary art. Grady be great at an irish stew; the Colonel be famous for pillaus and curry; and as for Strong he could cook anything. he make french dish and spanish dish, stew, fricassee, and omelette, to perfection; nor be there any man in England more hospitable than he when his purse be full or his credit be good. at those happy period, he could give a friend, as he say, a good dinner, a good glass of wine, and a good song afterwards; and poor cos often hear with envy the roar of Strong's chorus, and the musical clinking of the glass, as he sate in his own room, so far removed and yet so near to those festivity. it be not expedient to invite Mr. Costigan always: his practice of inebriation be lamentable; and he bore Strong's guest with his story when sober, and with his maudlin tear when drunk.
a strange and motley set they be, these friend of the Chevalier; and though Major Pendennis would not much have relish their company, Arthur and Warrington like it not a little, and Pen think it as amusing as the society of the fine gentleman in the fine house which he have the honour to frequent. there be a history about every man of the set: they seem all to have have their tide of luck and bad fortune. Most of they have wonderful scheme and speculation in their pocket, and plenty for make rapid and extraordinary fortune. Jack Holt have be in Don Carlos's army, when Ned Strong have fight on the other side; and be now organise a little scheme for smuggle tobacco into London, which must bring thirty thousand a year to any man who would advance fifteen hundred, just to bribe the last officer of the Excise who hold out, and have wind of the scheme. Tom Diver, who have be in the mexican navy, know of a specie-ship which have be sink in the first year of the war, with three hundred and eighty thousand dollar on board, and a hundred and eighty thousand pound in bar and doubloon. "give I eighteen hundred pound," Tom say, "and Ibe off tomorrow. I take out four man, and a diving-bell with I; and I return in ten month to take my seat in Parliament, by Jove! and to buy back my family estate." Keightley, the manager of the Tredyddlum and Polwheedle Copper Mines (which be as yet under water), besides sing as good a second as any professional man, and besides the Tredyddlum Office, have a Smyrna Sponge Company, and a little quicksilver operation in view, which would set he straight with the world yet. Filby have be everything a corporal of dragoon, a field-preacher, and missionary-agent for convert the Irish; an actor at a Greenwich fair-booth, in front of which his father's attorney find he when the old gentleman die and leave he that famous property, from which he get no rent now, and of which nobody exactly know the situation. add to these be Sir Francis Clavering, Bart., who like their society, though he do not much add to its amusement by his convivial power. but he be make much of by the company now, on account of his wealth and position in the world. he tell his little story and sing his little song or two with great affability; and he have have his own history, too, before his accession to good fortune; and have see the inside of more prison than one, and write his name on many a stamp paper.
when Altamont first return from Paris, and after he have communicate with Sir Francis Clavering from the hotel at which he have take up his quarter (and which he have reach in a very denude state, consider the wealth of diamond and ruby with which this honest man be entrust), Strong be send to his patron by the Baronet; pay his little bill at the inn, and invite he to come and sleep for a night or two at the chamber, where he subsequently take up his residence. to negotiate with this man be very well, but to have such a person settle in his room, and to be constantly burthen with such society, do not suit the Chevalier's taste much; and he grumble not a little to his principal.
"I wish you would put this bear into somebody else's cage," he say to Clavering. "the fellow's no gentleman. I donot like walk with he. he dress himself like a nigger on a holiday. I take he to the play the other night; and, by Jove, sir, he abuse the actor who be do the part of villain in the play, and swear at he so, that the people in the box want to turn he out. the after-piece be the 'Brigand,' where Wallack come in wounded, you know, and die. when he die, Altamont begin to cry like a child, and say it be a d----d shame, and cry and swear so, that there be another row, and everybody laugh. then I have to take he away, because he want to take his coat off to one fellow who laugh at he; and bellow to he to stand up like a man.--who be he? where the deuce do he come from? you have well tell I the whole story. Frank; you must one day. you and he have rob a church together, thatbe my belief. you have well get it off your mind at once, Clavering, and tell I what this Altamont be, and what hold he have over you."
"hang he! I wish he be dead!" be the Baronet's only reply; and his countenance become so gloomy, that Strong do not think fit to question his patron any far at that time; but resolve, if need be, to try and discover for himself what be the secret tie between Altamont and Clavering.
CHAPTER XLIV
in which the Colonel narrate some of his adventure
early in the forenoon of the day after the dinner in Grosvenor Place, at which Colonel Altamont have choose to appear, the Colonel emerge from his chamber in the upper story at Shepherd's Inn, and enter into Strong's sitting-room, where the Chevalier sate in his easy-chair with the newspaper and his cigar. he be a man who make his tent comfortable wherever he pitch it, and long before Altamont's arrival, have do justice to a copious breakfast of fried egg and broiled rasher, which Mr. Grady have prepare secundum artem. good-humour and talkative, he prefer any company rather than none; and though he have not the least like for his fellow-lodger, and would not have grieve to hear that the accident have befall he which Sir Francis Clavering desire so fervently, yet keep on fair term with he. he have see Altamont to bed with great friendliness on the night previous, and take away his candle for fear of accident; and find a spirit-bottle empty, upon which he have count for his nocturnal refreshment, have drink a glass of water with perfect contentment over his pipe, before he turn into his own crib and to sleep. that enjoyment never fail he: he have always an easy temper, a faultless digestion, and a rosy cheek; and whether he be go into action the next morning or to prison (and both have be his lot), in the camp or the Fleet, the worthy Captain snore healthfully through the night, and wake with a good heart and appetite, for the struggle or difficulty or pleasure of the day.
the first act of Colonel Altamont be to bellow to Grady for a pint of pale ale, the which he first pour into a pewter flagon, whence he transfer it to his own lip. he put down the tankard empty, draw a great breath, wipe his mouth in his dressing-gown (the difference of the colour of his beard from his dye whisker have long strike Captain Strong, who have see too that his hair be fair under his black wig, but make no remark upon these circumstances)--the Colonel draw a great breath, and profess himself immensely refresh by his draught. "nothing like that beer," he remark, "when the copper be hot. many a day Ihave drink a dozen of Bass at Calcutta, and--and----"
"and at Lucknow, I suppose," Strong say with a laugh. "I get the beer for you on purpose: know youwould want it after last night." and the Colonel begin to talk about his adventure of the precede evening.
"I cannot help myself," the Colonel say, beat his head with his big hand. "Ibe a madman when I get the liquor on board I; and benot fit to be trust with a spirit-bottle. when I once begin I cannot stop till Ihave empty it; and when Ihave swallow it, Lord know what I say or what I donot say. I din at home here quite quiet. Grady give I just my two tumbler, and I intend to pass the evening at the Black and Red as sober as a parson. why do you leave that confound sample-bottle of holland out of the cupboard, strong? Grady must go out too, and leave I the kettle a-boiling for tea. it be of no use, I couldnot keep away from it. wash it all down, sir, by Jove. and itbe my belief I have some more, too, afterwards at that infernal little thief' den."
"what, be you there too?" Strong ask, "and before you come to Grosvenor Place? that be begin betime."
"early hour to be drunk and clear out before nine o'clock, eh? but so it be. yes, like a great big fool, I must go there; and find the fellow dining, Blackland and young Moss, and two or three more of the thief. if wehave go to Rouge et Noir, I must have win. but we donot try the black and red. no, hang them, they know'd Iwould have beat them at that--I must have beat them--I cannot help beat them, I tell you. but they be too cunnin for I. that rascal Blackland get the bone out, and we play hazard on the dining-table. and I drop all the money I have from you in the morning, be hang to my luck. it be that that set I wild, and I suppose I must have be very hot about the head, for I go off think to get some more money from Clavering, I recollect; and then--and then I donot much remember what happen till I wake this morning, and hear old bow at no. 4 play on his pianner."
Strong muse for a while as he light his cigar with a coal, "I should like to know how you always draw money from Clavering, Colonel," he say.
the Colonel burst out with a laugh--"ha, ha! he owe it I," he say.
"I donot know that thatbe a reason with Frank for pay," Strong answer. "he owe plenty besides you."
"well, he give it I because he be so fond of I," the other say with the same grinning sneer. "he love I like a brother; you know he do, Captain.--No?--He don't?--Well, perhaps he donot; and if you ask I no question, perhaps Iwill tell you no lie, Captain Strong--put that in your pipe and smoke it, my boy." "but Iwill give up that confound brandy-bottle," the Colonel continue, after a pause. "I must give it up, or itwill be the ruin of I."
"it make you say queer thing," say the Captain, look Altamont hard in the face. "remember what you say last night, at Clavering's table."
"say? what do I say?" ask the other hastily. "do I split anything? Dammy, Strong, do I split anything?"
"ask I no question, and I will tell you no lie," the Chevalier reply on his part. strong thought of the word Mr. Altamont have use, and his abrupt departure from the Baronet's dining-table and house as soon as he recognise Major Pendennis, or Captain Beak, as he call the Major. but Strong resolve to seek an explanation of these word otherwise than from Colonel Altamont, and do not choose to recall they to the other's memory. "no," he say then, "you donot split as you call it, Colonel; it be only a trap of mine to see if I could make you speak; but you donot say a word that anybody could comprehend--you be too far go for that."
so much the well, Altamont think; and heave a great sigh, as if relieve. Strong remark the emotion, but take no notice, and the other be in a communicative mood, go on speak.
"yes, I own to my fault," continue the Colonel. "there be some thing I cannot, do what I will, resist: a bottle of brandy, a box of dice, and a beautiful woman. no man of pluck and spirit, no man as be worth his salt ever could, as I know of. therebe hardly p'raps a country in the world in which they three benot get I into trouble."
"indeed?" say Strong.
"yes, from the age of fifteen, when I run away from home, and go cabin-boy on board an Indiaman, till now, when Ibe fifty year old, pretty nigh, they woman have always be my ruin. why, it be one of them, and with such black eye and jewel on her neck, and Battens and ermine like a duchess, I tell you--it be one of them at Paris that sweep off the good part of the thousand pound as I go off with. donot I ever tell you of it? well, I donot mind. at first I be very cautious and have such a lot of money keep it close and live like a gentleman--Colonel Altamont, Meurice's hotel, and that sort of thing--never play, except at the public table, and win more than I lose. well, sir, there be a chap that I see at the hotel and the Palace Royal too, a regular swell fellow, with white kid glove and a tuft to his chin, Bloundell-Bloundell his name be, as I make acquaintance with somehow, and he ask I to dinner, and take I to Madame the Countess de Foljambe's soiree--such a woman, strong!--such an eye! such a hand at the pianner. Lor bless you, shewould sit down and sing to you, and gaze at you, until she warble your soul out of your body a'most. she ask I to go to her evening party every toosday; and donot I take opera-box and give her dinner at the restauranteur's, thatbe all? but I have a run of luck at the table, and it be not in the dinner and opera-box that poor Clavering's money go. no, be hang to it, it be sweep off in another way. one night, at the Countess's, there be several of we at supper--Mr. Bloundell-Bloundell, the honourable Deuceace, the Marky de la Tour de Force--all tip-top nob, sir, and the height of fashion, when we have supper, and champagne you may be sure in plenty, and then some of that confound brandy. I would have it--I would it go on at it--the Countess mix the tumbler of punch for I, and we have card as well as grog after supper, and I play and drank until I donot know what I do. I be like I be last night. I be take away and put to bed somehow, and never wake until the next day, to a roar headache, and to see my servant, who say the Honourable Deuceace want to see I, and be wait in the sitting-room. 'how be you, Colonel?' say he, a come into my bedroom. 'how long do you stay last night after I go away? the play be get too high for I, and Iwould lose enough to you for one night.'"
"'to I,' say I, 'howbe that, my dear feller? (for though he be an Earl's son, we be as familiar as you and I). howbe that, my dear feller?' say I, and he tell I, that he have borrow thirty louis of I at vingt-et-un, that he give I an I.O.U. for it the night before, which I put into my pocket-book before he leave the room. I take out my card-case--it be the Countess as work it for I--and there be the I.O.U. sure enough, and he pay I thirty louis in gold down upon the table at my bedside. so I say he be a gentleman, and ask he if he would like to take anything, when my servant should get it for he; but the Honourable Deuceace donot drink of a morning, and he go away to some business which he say he have.
"presently therebe another ring at my outer door; and this time itbe Bloundell-Bloundell and the Marky that come in. 'Bong jour, Marky,' say I. 'good morning--no headache?' say he. so I say I have one; and how I must have be uncommon queer the night afore; but they both declare I donot show no sign of have have too much, but take my liquor as grave as a judge.
"'so,' say the Marky, 'deuceace have be with you; we meet he in the Palais Royal as we be come from breakfast. have he settle with you? get it while you can: hebe a slippery card; and as he win three pony of Bloundell, I recommend you to get your money while he have some.'
"'he have pay I,' say I; 'but I know no more than the dead that he owe I anything, and donot remember a bit about lend he thirty louis.'
"the Marky and Bloundell look and smile at each other at this; and Bloundell say, 'Colonel, you be a queer feller. no man could have suppose, from your manner, that you have taste anything strong than tea all night, and yet you forget thing in the morning. Come, come,-- tell that to the marine, my friend,--we willnot have it at any price.'
"'en efet,' say the Marky, twiddle his little black mustachio in the chimney-glass, and make a lunge or two as he use to do at the fencing-school. (he be a wonder at the fencing-school, and Ihave see he knock down the image fourteen time run, at Lepage's.) 'let we speak of affair. Colonel, you understand that affair of honour be well settle at once: perhaps it willnot be inconvenient to you to arrange our little matter of last night.'
"'what little matter?' say I. 'do you owe I any money, Marky?'
"'bah!' say he; 'do not let we have any more jesting. I have your note of hand for three hundred and forty louis. La voia!' say he, take out a paper from his pocket-book.
"'and mine for two hundred and ten,' say Bloundell-Bloundell, and he pull out his bit of paper.
"I be in such a rage of wonder at this, that I spring out of bed, and wrap my dressing-gown round I. 'be you come here to make a fool of I?' say I. 'I donot owe you two hundred, or two thousand, or two louis; and I willnot pay you a farthing. do you suppose you can catch I with your note of hand? I laugh at them and at you; and I believe you to be a couple----.'
"'a couple of what?' say Mr. Bloundell. 'you, of course, be aware that we be a couple of man of honour, Colonel Altamont, and not come here to trifle or to listen to abuse from you. you will either pay we or we will expose you as a cheat, and chastise you as a cheat, too,' say Bloundell.
"'Oui, parbleu,' say the Marky,--but I donot mind he, for I could have throw the little fellow out of the window; but it be different with Bloundell,--he be a large man, that weigh three stone more than I, and stand six inch high, and I think he could have do for I.
"'Monsieur will pay, or Monsieur will give I the reason why. I believe yoube little well than a polisson, Colonel altamont,'--that be the phrase he use--Altamont say with a grin--and I get plenty more of this language from the two fellow, and be in the thick of the row with they, when another of our party come in. this be a friend of mine--a gent I have meet at Boulogne, and have take to the Countess's myself. and as he havenot play at all on the previous night, and have actually warn I against Bloundell and the other, I tell the story to he, and so do the other two.
"'I be very sorry,' say he. 'you would go on play: the Countess entreat you to discontinue. these gentleman offer repeatedly to stop. it be you that insist on the large stake, not they.' in fact he charge dead against I: and when the two other go away, he tell I how the Marky would shoot I as sure as my name be--be what it be. 'I leave the Countess crying, too,' say he. 'she hat these two man; she have warn you repeatedly against they'( which she actually have do, and often tell I never to play with they), 'and now, Colonel, I have leave she in hysteric almost, lest there should be any quarrel between you, and that confound Marky should put a bullet through your head. its my belief,' say my friend, 'that that woman be distractedly in love with you.'
"'do you think so?' say I; upon which my friend tell I how she have actually go down on her knee to he and 'save Colonel Altamont!'
"as soon as I be dress, I go and call upon that lovely woman. she give a shriek and pretty near faint when she see I. she call I Ferdinand,--I'm blest if she donot."
"I think your name be Jack," say Strong, with a laugh; at which the Colonel blush very much behind his dye whisker.
"a man may have more name than one, maynot he, strong?" Altamont ask. "when Ibe with a lady, I like to take a good one. she call I by my christian name. she cry fit to break your heart. I cannot stand see a woman cry--never could--not whilst Ibe fond of she. she say she could bear not to think of my lose so much money in her house. wouldnot I take her diamond and necklace, and pay part?
"I swear I wouldnot touch a farthing's worth of her jewellery, which perhaps I do not think be worth a great deal,--but what can a woman do more than give you she all? thatbe the sort I like, and I know therebe plenty of them. and I tell she to be easy about the money, for I would not pay one single farthing.
"'then theywill shoot you,' say she; 'theywill kill my ferdinand.'"
"theywill kill my Jack wouldnot have sound well in French," Strong say, laugh.
"never mind about name," say the other, sulkily; "a man of honour may take any name he choose, I suppose."
"well, go on with your story," say Strong. "she say they would kill you."
"'no,' say I, 'they willnot: for I will not let that scamp of a Marquis send I out of the world; and if he lay a hand on I, Iwill brain he, Marquis as he be.'
"at this the Countess shrink back from I as if I have say something very shocking. 'do I understand Colonel Altamont aright?' say she: 'and that a british officer refuse to meet any person who provoke he to the field of honour?'
"'Field of honour be hang, Countess,' say I. 'you would not have I be a target for that little scoundrel's pistol practice.'
"'Colonel Altamont,' say the Countess, 'I think you be a man of honour--I think, I--but no matter. good-bye, sir.'--and she be sweep out of the room, her voice regular choke in her pocket-handkerchief.
"'countess!' say I, rush after she and seize her hand.
"'leave I, Monsieur le Colonel,' say she, shake I off, 'my father be a General of the Grand Army. a soldier should know how to pay all his debt of honour.'
"what could I do? everybody be against I. Caroline say I have lose the money: though I donot remember a syllable about the business. I have take Deuceace's money too; but then it be because he offer it to I you know, and thatbe a different thing. every one of these chap be a man of fashion and honour; and the Marky and the Countess of the first family in France. and, by Jove, sir, rather than offend she, I pay the money up five hundred and sixty gold napoleon, by Jove: besides three hundred which I lose when I have my revenge.
"and I cannot tell you at this minute whether I be do or not," conclude the Colonel, musing. "sometimes I think I be: but then Caroline be so fond of I. that woman would never have see I do: never, Ibe sure she wouldnot: at least, if she would, Ibe deceive in woman."
any further revelation of his past life which Altamont might have be dispose to confide to his honest comrade the Chevalier, be interrupt by a knocking at the outer door of their chamber; which, when open by Grady the servant, admit no less a person than Sir Francis Clavering into the presence of the two worthy.
"the Governor, by Jove," cry Strong, regard the arrival of his patron with surprise. "whatbe bring you here?" growl Altamont, look sternly from under his heavy eyebrow at the Baronet. "itbe no good, I warrant." and indeed, good very seldom bring Sir Francis Clavering into that or any other place.
whenever he come into Shepherd's inn it be money that bring the unlucky baronet into those precinct; and there be commonly a gentleman of the money-deal world in wait for he at Strong's chamber, or at Campion's below; and a question of bill to negotiate or to renew. Claverng be a man who have never look his debt fairly in the face, familiar as he have be with they all his life; as long as he could renew a bill, his mind be easy regard it; and he would sign almost anything for to-morrow, provide to-day could be leave unmoleste. he be a man whom scarcely any amount of fortune could have benefit permanently, and who be make to be ruin to cheat small tradesman, to be the victim of astuter sharper: to be niggardly and reckless, and as destitute of honesty as the people who cheat he, and a dupe, chiefly because he be too mean to be a successful knave. he have tell more lie in his time, and undergone more baseness of stratagem in order to stave off a small debt, or to swindle a poor creditor, than would have suffice to make a fortune for a braver rogue. he be abject and a shuffler in the very height of his prosperity. have he be a Crown Prince--he could not have be more weak, useless, dissolute or ungrateful. he could not move through life except lean on the arm of somebody: and yet he never have an agent but he mistrust he; and mar any plan which might be arrange for his benefit, and secretly act against the people whom he employ. Strong know Clavering and judge he quite correctly. it be not as friend that this pair meet: but the Chevalier work for his principal, as he would when in the army have pursue a harass march, or undergone his part in the danger and privation of a siege; because it be his duty, and because he have agree to it. "what be it he want?" think the officer of the Shepherd's Inn garrison when the Baronet come among they.
his pale face express extreme anger and irritation. "so sir," he say, address Altamont, "youhave be at your old trick."
"which of 'um?" ask Altamont, with a sneer.
"you have be at the Rouge et Noir: you be there last night," cry the Baronet.
"how do you know,--were you there?" the other say. "I be at the Club but it benot on the colour I played,--ask the Captain,--I've be tell he of it. it be with the bone. it be at hazard, Sir Francis, upon my word and honour it be;" and he look at the Baronet with a know humorous mock humility, which only seem to make the other more angry.
"what the deuce do I care, sir, how a man like you lose his money, and whether it be at hazard or roulette?" scream the Baronet, with a multiplicity of oath, and at the top of his voice. "what I will not have, sir, be that you should use my name, or couple it with yours.--damn he, Strong, why donot you keep he in well order? I tell you he have go and use my name again, sir,--drawn a bill upon I, and lose the money on the table--I cannot stand it--I willnot stand it. flesh and blood willnot bear it--do you know how much I have pay for you, sir?"
"this be only a very little 'un, Sir Francis--only fifteen pound, Captain Strong, they wouldnot stand another: and it oughtnot to anger you, Governor. why, itbe so trifle I do not even mention it to Strong,--did I now, Captain? I protest it have quite slip my memory, and all on account of that confound liquor I take."
"Liquor or no liquor, sir, it be no business of mine. I donot care what you drink, or where you drink it--only it shallnot be in my house. and I will not have you break into my house of a night, and a fellow like you intrude himself on my company: how dare you show yourself in Grosvenor Place last night, sir,--and--and what do you suppose my friend must think of I when they see a man of your sort walk into my dining-room uninvited, and drunk, and call for liquor as if you be the master of the house?"
"theywill think you know some very queer sort of people, I dare say," Altamont say with impenetrable good-humour. "look here, Baronet, I apologise; on my honour I do, and benot an apology enough between two gentleman? it be a strong measure I own, walk into your cuddy, and call for drink as if I be the Captain: but I have have too much before, you see, thatbe why I want some more; nothing can be more simple--and it be because they wouldnot give I no more money upon your name at the Black and Red, that I think I would come down and speak to you about it. to refuse I be nothing: but to refuse a bill draw on you that have be such a friend to the shop, and be a baronet and a member of parliament, and a gentleman and no mistake--Damme, its ungrateful."
"by heaven, if ever you do it again--if ever you dare show to yourself in my house; or give my name at a gambling-house or at any other house, by Jove--at any other house--or give any reference at all to I, or speak to I in the street, by God, or anywhere else until I speak to you--I disclaim you altogether--I willnot give you another shilling."
"Governor, donot be provoke," Altamont say surlily. "donot talk to I about dare to do this thing or t'other, or when my dander be up itbe the very thing to urge I on. I oughtnot to have come last night, I know I oughtnot: but I tell you I be drunk, and that ought to be sufficient between gentleman and gentleman."
"you a gentleman! dammy, sir," say the Baronet, "how dare a fellow like you to call himself a gentleman?"
"I benot a baronet, I know," growl the other; and Ihave forget how to be a gentleman almost now, but--but I be one, once, and my father be one, and Iwill not have this sort of talk from you, Sir F. Clavering, thatbe flat. I want to go abroad again. why donot you come down with the money, and let I go? why the devil be you to be roll in rich, and I to have none? why should have a house and a table cover with plate, and I be in a garret here in this beggarly Shepherd's Inn? webe partner, benot we? Iwould as good a right to be rich as you have, havenot I? tell the story to Strong here, if you like; and ask he to be umpire between we. I donot mind let my secret out to a man that willnot split. look here, Strong--perhaps you guess the story already--the fact be, I and the governor----"
"d---, hold your tongue," shriek out the Baronet in a fury. "you shall have the money as soon as I can get it. I benot make of money. Ibe so pressed and badger, I donot know where to turn. I shall go mad; by Jove, I shall. I wish I be dead, for Ibe the most miserable brute alive. I say, Mr. Altamont, donot mind I. when Ibe out of health--and Ibe devilish bilious this morning--hang I, I abuse everybody, and donot know what I say. excuse I if Ihave offend you. I--i'll try and get that little business do. strong shall try. upon my word he shall. and I say, Strong, my boy, I want to speak to you. come into the office for a minute."
almost all Clavering's assault end in this ignominious way, and in a shameful retreat. altamont sneer after the Baronet as he leave the room, and enter into the office, to talk privately with his factotum.
"what be the matter now?" the latter ask of he. "itbe the old story, I suppose."
"d--- it, yes," the Baronet say. "I drop two hundred in ready money at the Little Coventry last night, and give a cheque for three hundred more. on her ladyship's banker, too, for to-morrow; and I must meet it, for therewill be the deuce to pay else. the last time she pay my play-debt, I swear I would not touch a dice-box again, and shewill keep her word, strong, and dissolve partnership, if I go on. I wish I have three hundred a year, and be away. at a german watering-place you can do devilish well with three hundred a year. but my habit be so d----- reckless: I wish I be in the Serpentine. I wish I be dead, by Gad I wish I be. I wish I have never touch those confound bone. I have such a run of luck last night, with five for the main, and seven to five all night, until those ruffian want to pay I with Altamont's bill upon I. the luck turn from that minute. never hold the box again for three main, and come away clear out, leave that infernal cheque behind I. how shall I pay it? Blackland willnot hold it over. Hulker and Bullock will write about it directly to her ladyship. by Jove, Ned, Ibe the most miserable brute in all England."
it be necessary for Ned to devise some plan to console the Baronet under this pressure of grief; and no doubt he find the mean of procure a loan for his patron, for he be closet at Mr. Campion's office that day for some time. Altamont have once more a guinea or two in his pocket, with a promise of a further settlement; and the Baronet have no need to wish himself dead for the next two or three month at least. and Strong, put together what he have learn from the Colonel and Sir Francis, begin to form in his own mind a pretty accurate opinion as to the nature of the tie which bind the two man together.
CHAPTER XLV
a chapter of conversation
every day, after the entertainment at Grosvenor Place and Greenwich, of which we have see Major Pendennis partake, the worthy gentleman's friendship and cordiality for the Clavering family seem to increase. his call be frequent; his attention to the lady of the house unremitting. an old man about town, he have the good fortune to be receive in many house, at which a lady of Lady Clavering's distinction ought to be see. would her ladyship not like to be present at the grand entertainment at Gaunt House? there be to be a very pretty breakfast ball at Viscount Marrowfat's, at Fulham. everybody be to be there (include august personage of the high rank), and there be to be a Watteau quadrille, in which Miss Amory would surely look charming. to these and other amusement the obsequious old gentleman kindly offer to conduct Lady Clavering, and be also ready to make himself useful to the Baronet in any way agreeable to the latter.
in spite of his present station and fortune, the world persist in look rather coldly upon Clavering, and strange suspicious rumour follow he about. he be blackball at two club in succession. in the House of Commons, he only converse with a few of the most disreputable member of that famous body, have a happy knack of choose bad society, and adapt himself naturally to it, as other people do to the company of their better. the name all the senator with whom Clavering consort, would be invidious. we may mention only a few. there be Captain Raff, the honourable member for Epsom, who retire after the last Goodwood race, have accept, as Mr. Hotspur, the whip of the party, say, a mission to the Levant; there be Hustingson, the patriotic member for Islington, whose voice be never hear now denunciate corruption, since his appointment to the Governorship of Coventry Island; there be Bob Freeny, of the Booterstown Freenys, who be a dead shot, and of whom we therefore wish to speak with every respect; and of all these gentleman, with whom in the course of his professional duty Mr. Hotspur have to confer, there be none for whom he have a more thorough contempt and dislike than for Sir Francis Clavering, the representative of an ancient race, who have sit for their own borough of Clavering time out of mind in the House. "if that man be want for a division," Hotspur say, "ten to one he be to be find in a hell. he be educate in the Fleet, and he have not hear the end of newgate yet, take my word for it. hewill muddle away the Begum's fortune at thimble-rig, be catch pick pocket, and finish on board the hulk." and if the high-bear Hotspur, with such an opinion of Clavering, could yet from professional reason be civil to he, why should not Major Pendennis also have reason of his own for be attentive to this unlucky gentleman?
"he have a very good cellar and a very good cook," the Major say; "as long as he be silent he be not offensive, and he very seldom speak. if he choose to frequent gambling-table, and lose his money to blackleg, what matter to I? donot look too curiously into any man's affair, Pen, my boy; every fellow have some cupboard in his house, begad, which he would not like you and I to peep into. why should we try, when the rest of the house be open to we? and a devilish good house, too, as you and I know. and if the man of the family be not all one could wish, the woman be excellent. the Begum be not over-refine, but as kind a woman as ever live, and devilish clever too; and as for the little Blanche, you know my opinion about she, you rogue; you know my belief be that she be sweet on you, and would have you for the asking. but you be grow such a great man, that I suppose you willnot be content under a Duke's daughter-- Hey, sir? I recommend you to ask one of they, and try."
perhaps Pen be somewhat intoxicate by his success in the world; and it may also have enter into the young man's mind (his uncle's perpetual hint serve not a little to encourage the notion) that Miss Amory be tolerably well disposed to renew the little flirtation which have be carry on in the early day of both of they, by the bank of the rural Brawl. but he be little disposed to marriage, he say, at that moment, and, adopt some of his uncle's worldly tone, speak rather contemptuously of the institution, and in favour of a bachelor life.
"you be very happy, sir," say he, "and you get on very well alone, and so do I. with a wife at my side, I should lose my place in society; and I donot, for my part, much fancy retire into the country with a Mrs. Pendennis; or take my wife into lodging to be wait upon by the servant-of-all-work. the period of my little illusion be over. you cure I of my first love who, certainly be a fool, and would have have a fool for her husband, and a very sulky discontent husband too if she have take I. we young fellow live fast, sir; and I feel as old at five-and-twenty as many of the old fo--the old bachelor--whom I see in the bow-window at Bays's. donot look offend, I only mean that I be blase about love matter, and that I could no more fan myself into a flame for Miss Amory now, than I could adore Lady Mirabel over again. I wish I could; I rather like old Mirabel for his infatuation about she, and think his passion be the most respectable part of his life."
"Sir Charles Mirabel be always a theatrical man, sir," the Major say, annoy that his nephew should speak flippantly of any person of Sir Charles's rank and station. "he have be occupy with theatrical since his early day. he act at Carlton House when he be Page to the Prince; he have be mix up with that sort of thing: he could afford to marry whom he choose; and Lady Mirabel be a most respectable woman, receive everywhere--everywhere, mind. the Duchess of Connaught receive she, Lady Rockminster receive she--it donot become young fellow to speak lightly of people in that station. therebe not a more respectable woman in England than Lady Mirabel:--and the old fogy, as you call they, at Bays's, be some of the first gentleman in England, of whom you youngster have well learn a little manner, and a little breeding, and a little modesty." and the Major begin to think that Pen be grow exceedingly pert and conceited, and that the world make a great deal too much of he.
the Major's anger amuse Pen. he study his uncle's peculiarity with a constant relish, and be always in a good humour with his worldly old mentor. "I be a youngster of fifteen year' standing, sir," he say, adroitly, "and if you think that we be disrespectful, you should see those of the present generation. a protege of yours come to breakfast with I the other day. you tell I to ask he, and I do it to please you. we have a day's sight together, and din at the club, and go to the play. he say the wine at the Polyanthus be not so good as Ellis's wine at Richmond, smoke Warrington's cavendish after breakfast, and when I give he a sovereign as a farewell token, say he have plenty of they, but would take it to show he benot proud."
"do he?--did you ask young Clavering?" cry the Major, appease at once--"fine boy, rather wild, but a fine boy--parent like that sort of attention, and you cannot do well than pay it to our worthy friend of Grosvenor Place. and so you take he to the play and tip he? that be right, sir, that be right:" with which mentor quit Telemachus, think that the young man be not so very bad, and that he should make something of that fellow yet.
as Blaster Clavering grow into year and stature, he become too strong for the authority of his fond parent and governess; and rather govern they than permit himself to be lead by their order. with his papa he be silent and sulky, seldom make his appearance, however, in the neighbourhood of that gentleman; with his mamma be roar and fight when any contest between they arise as to the gratification of his appetite, or other wish of his heart; and in his dispute with his governess over his book, he kick that quiet creature's shin so fiercely, that she be entirely overmaster and subdue by he. and he would have so treat his sister Blanche, too, and do on one or two occasion attempt to prevail over she; but she show an immense resolution and spirit on her part, and box his ear so soundly, that he forbore from molest Miss Amory, as he do the governess and his mamma, and his mamma's maid.
at length, when the family come to London, Sir Francis give forth his opinion, that "the little beggar have well be send to school." accordingly the young son and heir of the house of Clavering be despatch to the Rev. Otto Rose's establishment at Twickenham, where young nobleman and gentleman be receive preparatory to their introduction to the great english public school.
it be not our intention to follow Master Clavering in his scholastic career; the path to the Temple of Learning be make more easy to he than they be to some of we of early generation. he advance towards that fane in a carriage-and-four, so to speak, and might halt and take refreshment almost whenever he please. he wear varnish boot from the early period of youth, and have cambric handkerchief and lemon-colour kid glove, of the small size ever manufacture by Privat. they dress regularly at Mr. Rose's to come down to dinner; the young gentleman have shawl dressing-gown, fire in their bedroom, horse and carriage exercise occasionally, and oil for their hair. corporal punishment be altogether dispense with by the Principal, who think that moral discipline be entirely sufficient to lead youth; and the boy be so rapidly advanced in many branch of learn, that they acquire the art of drink spirit and smoking cigar, even before they be old enough to enter a public school. Young Frank Clavering steal his father's Havannahs, and convey they to school, or smoke they in the stable, at a surprisingly early period of life, and at ten year old drink his champagne almost as stoutly as any whiskered cornet of dragoon could do.
when this interesting youth come home for his vacation Major Pendennis be as laboriously civil and gracious to he as he be to the rest of the family; although the boy have rather a contempt for old Wigsby, as the Major be denominate, mimic he behind his back, as the polite Major bow and smirk with Lady Clavering or Miss Amory; and draw rude caricature, such as be design by ingenious youth, in which the Major's wig, his nose, his tie, etc., be represent with artless exaggeration. untire in his effort to be agreeable, the Major wish that Pen, too, should take particular notice of this child; incite Arthur to invite he to his chamber, to give he a dinner at the club, to take he to Madame Tussaud's, the Tower, the play, and so forth, and to tip he, as the phrase be, at the end of the day's pleastre. Arthur, who be good-natured and fond of child, go through all these ceremony one day; have the boy to breakfast at the Temple, where he make the most contemptuous remark regard the furniture, the crockery, and the tattered state of Warrington's dressing-gown; and smoke a short pipe, and recount the history of a fight between Tuffy and Long Biggings, at Rose's, greatly to the edification of the two gentleman his host.
as the Major rightly predict, Lady Clavering be very grateful for Arthur's attention to the boy; more grateful than the lad himself, who take attention as a matter of course, and very likely have more sovereign in his pocket than poor Pen, who generously give he one of his own slender stock of those coin.
the Major, with the sharp eye with which nature endow he, and with the glass of age and experience, watch this boy, and survey his position in the family without seem to be rudely curious about their affair. but, as a country neighbour, one who have many family obligation to the Claverings, an old man of the world, he take occasion to find out what Lady Clavering's mean be, how her capital be dispose, and what the boy be to inherit. and set himself to work,--for what purpose will appear, no doubt, ulteriorly,--he soon have get a pretty accurate knowledge of Lady Clavering's affair and fortune, and of the prospect of her daughter and son. the daughter be to have but a slender provision; the bulk of the property be, as before have be say, to go to the son,--his father do not care for he or anybody else,--his mother be dotingly fond of he as the child of her latter days,--his sister dislike he. such may be state in round number, to be the result of the information which Major Pendennis get. "ah! my dear madam," he would say, pat the head of the boy, "this boy may wear a baron's coronet on his head on some future coronation, if matter be but manage rightly, and if Sir Francis Clavering would but play his card well,"
at this the widow Amory heave a deep sigh. "he play only much of his card, Major, Ibe afraid," she say. the Major own that he know as much; do not disguise that he have hear of Sir Francis Clavering's unfortunate propensity to play; pity Lady Clavering sincerely; but speak with such genuine sentiment and sense, that her ladyship, glad to find a person of experience to whom she could confide her grief and her condition, talk about they pretty unreservedly to major Pendennis, and be eager to have his advice and consolation. major Pendennis become the Begum's confidante and house-friend, and as a mother, a wife, and a capitalist, she consult he.
he give she to understand (show at the same time a great deal of respectful sympathy) that he be acquaint with some of the circumstance of her first unfortunate marriage, and with even the person of her late husband, whom he remember in Calcutta--when she be live in seclusion with her father. the poor lady, with tear of shame more than of grief in her eye, tell her version of her story. go back a child to India after two year at a european school, she have meet Amory, and foolishly marry he. "oh, you donot know how miserable that man, make I," she say, "or what a life I pass betwixt he and my father. before I see he I have never see a man except my father's clerk and native servant. you know we donot go into society in India on account of----" ("I know," say Major Pendennis, with a bow) "I be a wild romantic child, my head be full of novel which Iwould read at school--I listen to his wild story and adventure, for he be a daring fellow, and I think he talk beautifully of those calm night on the passage out, when he use to----. well, I marry he, and I be wretched from that day--wretche with my father, whose character you know, Major Pendennis, and I willnot speak of: but he benot a good man, sir,--neither to my poor mother, nor to I, except that he leave I his money,--nor to no one else that I ever hear of: and he donot do many kind action in his lifetime, Ibe afraid. and as for Amory, he be almost bad; he be a spendthrift when my father be close: he drink dreadfully, and be furious when in that way. he benot in any way a good or a faithful husband to I, Major Pendennis, and if hehave die in the gaol before this trial, instead of afterwards he would have save I a deal of shame and of unhappiness since, sir." Lady Clavering add: "for perhaps I should not have marry at all if I have not be so anxious to change his horrid name, and I have not be happy in my second husband, as I suppose you know, sir. ah, Major Pendennis, Ihave get money to be sure, and Ibe a lady, and people fancy Ibe very happy, but I benot. we all have our care, and grief, and trouble: and manybe the day that I sit down to one of my grand dinner with an aching heart, and many a night do I lay awake on my fine bed a great deal more unhappy than the maid that make for it. Ibe not a happy woman, Major, for all the world say; and envy the Begum her diamond, and carriage, and the great company that come to my house. Ibe not happy in my husband; Ibe not in my daughter. she benot a good girl like that dear Laura Bell at Fairoaks. shebe cost I many a tear though you donot see them; and she sneer at her mother because I havenot have learn and that. how should I? I be bring up amongst native till I be twelve, and go back to India when I be fourteen. ah, Major, I should have be a good woman if I have have a good husband. and now I must go upstairs and wipe my eye, for theybe red with cryin. and Lady Rockminster's a comin, and webe goin to ave a drive in the Park. and when Lady Rockminster make her appearance, there be not a trace of tear or vexation on Lady Clavering's face, but she be full of spirit, and bounce out with her blunder and talk, and murder the king's English with the utmost liveliness and good-humour.
"Begad, she be not such a bad woman!" the Major thought within himself. "she be not refine, certainly, and call 'Apollo' 'Apoller;' but she have some heart, and I like that sort of thing, and a devilish deal of money, too. three star in India Stock to her name, begad! which that young cub be to have--be he?" and he think how he should like to see a little of the money transfer to Miss Blanche, and, well still, one of those star shin in the name of Mr. Arthur Pendennis.
still bent upon pursue his scheme, whatsoever they might be, the old negotiator take the privilege of his intimacy and age, to talk in a kindly and fatherly manner to Miss Blanche, when he find occasion to see she alone. he come in so frequently at luncheon-time, and become so familiar with the lady, that they do not even hesitate to quarrel before he; and Lady Clavering, whose tongue be loud, and temper brusque, have many a battle with the Sylphide in the family friend's presence. Blanche's wit seldom fail to have the mastery in these encounter, and the keen barb of her arrow drive her adversary discomfit away. "I be an old fellow," the Major say; "I have nothing to do in life. I have my eye open. I keep good counsel. I be the friend of both of you; and if you choose to quarrel before I, why, I shallnot tell any one. but you be two good people, and I intend to make it up between you. I have between lot of people--husband and wife, father and son, daughter and mamma, before this. I like it; Ihave nothing else to do."
one day, then, the old diplomatist enter Lady Clavering's drawing-room, just as the latter quit it, evidently in a high state of indignation, and run past he up the stair to her own apartment. "she couldnot speak to he now," she say; "she be a great deal too angry with that--that-- that little, wicked"--anger choke the rest of the word, or prevent their utterance until Lady Clavering have pass out of hearing.
"my dear, good Miss Amory," the Major say, enter the drawing-room, "I see what be happen. you and mamma have be disagree. mother and daughter disagree in the good family. it be but last week that I heal up a quarrel between Lady Clapperton and her daughter Lady Claudia. Lady Lear and her eld daughter have not speak for fourteen year. Kinder and more worthy people than these I never know in the whole course of my life; for everybody but each other admirable. but they cannot live together: they oughtnot to live together: and I wish, my dear creature, with all my soul, that I could see you with an establishment of your own--for there be no woman in London who could conduct one better-- with your own establishment, make your own home happy."
"I be not very happy in this one," say the sylphide; "and the stupidity of mamma be enough to provoke a saint."
"precisely so; you be not suit to one another. your mother commit one fault in early life--or be it Nature, my dear, in your case?--she ought not to have educate you. you ought not to have be breed up to become the refined and intellectual be you be, surround, as I own you be, by those who have not your genius or your refinement. your place would be to lead in the most brilliant circle, not to follow, and take a second place in any society. I have watch you, Miss Amory: you be ambitious; and your proper sphere be command. you ought to shine; and you never can in this house, I know it. I hope I shall see you in another and a happy one, some day, and the mistress of it."
the Sylphide shrug her lily shoulder with a look of scorn. "where be the Prince, and where be the palace, Major Pendennis?" she say. "I be ready. but there be no romance in the world now, no real affection."
"no, indeed," say the Major, with the most sentimental and simple air which he could muster.
"not that I know anything about it," say Blanche, cast her eye down "except what I have read in novel."
"of course not," Major Pendennis cry; "how should you, my dear young lady? and novel benot true, as you remark admirably, and there be no romance leave in the world. Begad, I wish I be a young fellow like my nephew."
"and what," continue Miss Amory, musing, "what be the man whom we see about at the ball every night--dancing guardsman, penniless treasury clerk--booby! if I have my brother's fortune, I might have such an establishment as you promise I--but with my name, and with my little mean, what be I to look to! a country parson, or a barrister in a street near Russell Square, or a captain in a dragoon regiment, who will take lodging for I, and come home from the mess tipsy and smell of smoke like Sir Francis Clavering. that be how we girl be destine to end life. o Major Pendennis, I be sick of London, and of ball, and of young dandy with their chin-tip, and of the insolent great lady who know we one day and cut we the next--and of the world altogether. I should like to leave it and to go into a convent, that I should. I shall never find anybody to understand I. and I live here as much alone in my family and in the world, as if I be in a cell lock up for ever. I wish there be Sisters of Charity here, and that I could be one and catch the plague, and die of it--I wish to quit the world. I be not very old: but I be tired, I have suffer so much--I've be so disillusionate--I'm weary, Ibe weary--o that the Angel of Death would come and beckon I away!"
this speech may be interpret as follow. a few night since a great lady, Lady Flamingo, have cut Miss Amory and Lady Clavering. she be quite mad because she could not get an invitation to Lady Drum's ball: it be the end of the season and nobody have propose to she: she have make no sensation at all, she who be so much cleverer than any girl of the year, and of the young lady form her special circle. Dora who have but five thousand pound, Flora who have nothing, and Leonora who have red hair, be go to be marry, and nobody have come for Blanche Amory!
"you judge wisely about the world, and about your position, my dear Miss Blanche," the Major say. "the Prince donot marry nowadays, as you say: unless the Princess have a doosid deal of money in the fund, or be a lady of his own rank.--the young folk of the great family marry into the great family: if they havenot fortune they have each other's shoulder, to push on in the world, which be pretty nearly as good.--A girl with your fortune can scarcely hope for a great match: but a girl with your genius and your admirable tact and fine manner, with a clever husband by her side, may make any place for herself in the world.--we be grow doosid republican. talent rank with birth and wealth now, begad: and a clever man with a clever wife, may take any place they please."
Miss Amory do not of course in the least understand what Major Pendennis meant.--perhaps she think over circumstance in her mind and ask herself, could he be a negotiator for a former suitor of her, and could he mean Pen? no, it be impossible--he have be civil, but nothing more. --so she say laugh, "who be the clever man, and when will you bring he to I, Major Pendennis? I be die to see he."
at this moment a servant throw open the door, and announce Mr. Henry Foker: at which name, and at the appearance of our friend, both the lady and the gentleman burst out laugh.
"that be not the man," Major Pendennis say. "he be engage to his cousin, Lord Gravesend's daughter.--good-bye, my dear Miss Amory."
* * * * *
be Pen grow worldly, and should a man not get the experience of the world and lie it to his account? "he feel, for his part," as he say, "that he be grow very old very soon." "how this town form and change we," he say once to Warrington. each have come in from his night's amusement; and Pen be smoke his pipe, and recounting, as his habit be, to his friend the observation and adventure of the evening just past. "how I be change," he say, "from the simpleton boy at Fairoaks, who be fit to break his heart about his first love! Lady Mirabel have a reception to-night, and be as grave and collect as if she have be bear a duchess, and have never see a trap-door in her life. she give I the honour of a conversation, and patronise I about 'Walter Lorraine,' quite kindly."
"what condescension!" break in Warrington.
"benot it?" Pen say, simply--at which the other burst out laugh accord to his willnot. "be it possible," he say, "that anybody should think of patronise the eminent author of 'Walter Lorraine?'"
"you laugh at both of we," Pen say, blush a little--"i be come to that myself. she tell I that she have not read the book (as indeed I believe she never read a book in her life), but that Lady Rockminster have, and that the Duchess of Connaught pronounce it to be very clever. in that case, I say, I should die happy, for that to please those two lady be in fact the great aim of my existence, and have their approbation, of course I need look for no other. Lady Mirabel look at I solemnly out of her fine eye, and say, 'oh, indeed,' as if she understand I, and then she ask I whether I go to the Duchess's Thursdays, and when I say no, hop she should see I there, and that I must try and get there, everybody go there--everybody who be in society: and then we talk of the new ambassador from timbuctoo, and how he be well than the old one; and how Lady Mary Billington be go to marry a clergyman quite below she in rank; and how Lord and Lady Ringdove have fall out three month after their marriage about Tom Pouter of the Blues, Lady Ringdove's cousin--and so forth. from the gravity of that woman you would have fancy she have be bear in a palace, and live all the season of her life in Belgrave Square."
"and you, I suppose you take your part in the conversation pretty well, as the descendant of the Earl your father, and the heir of Fairoaks Castle?" Warrington say. "yes, I remember reading of the festivity which occur when you come of age. the Countess give a brilliant tea soiree to the neighbouring nobility; and the tenantry be regale in the kitchen with a leg of mutton and a quart of ale. the remain of the banquet be distribute amongst the poor of the village, and the entrance to the park be illuminate until old John put the candle out on retire to rest at his usual hour."
"my mother be not a countess," say Pen, "though she have very good blood in her vein too--but commoner as she be, I have never meet a peeress who be more than her peer, Mr. George; and if you will come to Fairoaks Castle you shall judge for yourself of she and of my cousin too. they be not so witty as the London woman, but they certainly be as well breed. the thought of woman in the country be turn to other object than those which occupy your London lady. in the country a woman have her household and her poor, her long calm day and long calm evening."
"devilish long," Warrington say, "and a great deal too calm; Ihave try them."
"the monotony of that existence must be to a certain degree melancholy-- like the tune of a long ballad; and its harmony grave and gentle, sad and tender: it would be unendurable else. the loneliness of woman in the country make they of necessity soft and sentimental. lead a life of calm duty, constant routine, mystic reverie,--a sort of nun at large-- too much gaiety or laughter would jar upon their almost sacred quiet, and would be as out of place there as in a church."
"where you go to sleep over the sermon," Warrington say.
"you be a profess misogynist, and hate the sex because, I suspect, you know very little about they," Mr. Pen continue, with an air of considerable self-complacency. "if you dislike the woman in the country for be too slow, surely the London woman ought to be fast enough for you. the pace of London life be enormous: how do people last at it, I wonder,--male and female? take a woman of the world: follow she course through the season; one ask how she can survive it? or if she tumble into a sleep at the end of August, and lie torpid until the spring? she go into the world every night, and sit watch her marriageable daughter dance till long after dawn. she have a nursery of little one, very likely, at home, to whom she administer example and affection; have an eye likewise to bread-and-milk, catechism, music and French, and roast leg of mutton at one o'clock; she have to call upon lady of her own station, either domestically or in her public character, in which she sit upon Charity Committees, or Ball Committees, or Emigration Committees, or Queen's College Committees, and discharge I donot know what more duty of british stateswomanship. she very likely keep a poor-visit list; have conversation with the clergyman about soup or flannel, or proper religious teaching for the parish; and (if she live in certain district) probably attend early church. she have the newspaper to read, and, at least, must know what her husband's party be about, so as to be able to talk to her neighbour at dinner; and it be a fact that she read every new book that come out; for she can talk, and very smartly and well, about they all, and you see they all upon her drawing-room table. she have the care of her household besides--to make both end meet; to make the girl' milliner's bill appear not too dreadful to the father and paymaster of the family; to snip off, in secret, a little extra article of expenditure here and there, and convey it, in the shape of a bank-note, to the boy at college or at sea; to check the encroachment of tradesman and housekeeper' financial fallacy; to keep upper and low servant from jangle with one another, and the household in order. add to this, that she have a secret taste for some art or science, model in clay, make experiment in chemistry, or play in private on the violoncello,--and I say, without exaggeration, many London lady be do this,--and you have a character before you such as our ancestor never hear of, and such as belong entirely to our era and period of civilisation. ye god! how rapidly we live and grow! in nine month, Mr. Paxton grow you a pineapple as large as a portmanteau, whereas a little one, no big than a dutch cheese, take three year to attain his majority in old time; and as the race of pineapple so be the race of man. Hoiaper--whatbe the Greek for a pineapple, Warrington?"
"stop, for mercy's sake, stop with the English and before you come to the Greek," Warrington cry out, laugh. "I never hear you make such a long speech, or be aware that you have penetrate so deeply into the female mystery. who teach you all this, and into whose boudoir and nursery have you be peep, whilst I be smoke my pipe, and read my book, lie on my straw bed?"
"you be on the bank; old boy, content to watch the wave toss in the wind, and the struggle of other at sea," Pen say. "I be in the stream now, and by Jove I like it. how rapidly we go down it, hey? strong and feeble, old and young--the metal pitcher and the earthen pitcher--the pretty little china boat swim gaily till the big bruised brazen one bump he and send he down--eh, vogue la galere!--you see a man sink in the race, and say good-bye to he--look, he have only dive under the other fellow's leg, and come up shake his pole, and strike out ever so far ahead. eh, vogue la galere, I say. itbe good sport, warrington-- not win merely, but play."
"well, go in and win, young 'un. Iwill sit and mark the game," Warrington say, survey the ardent young fellow with an almost fatherly pleasure. "a generous fellow play for the play, a sordid one for the stake; an old fogy sit by and smoke the pipe of tranquillity, while Jack and Tom be pummel each other in the ring."
"why donot you come in, George, and have a turn with the glove? you be big enough and strong enough," Pen say. "dear old boy, you be worth ten of I."
"you be not quite as tall as Goliath, certainly," the other answer, with a laugh that be rough and yet tender. "as for I, I be disabled. I have a fatal hit in early life. I will tell you about it some day. you may, too, meet with your master. donot be too eager, or too confident, or too worldly, my boy."
be Pendennis become worldly, or only see the worldly, or both? and be a man very wrong for be after all only a man? which be the most reasonable, and do his duty well: he who stand aloof from the struggle of life, calmly contemplating, or he who descend to the ground, and take his part in the contest?
