"that philosopher," Pen say, "have hold a great place amongst the leader of the world, and enjoy to the full what it have to give of rank and rich, renown and pleasure, who come, weary-hearted, out of it, and say that all be vanity and vexation of spirit. many a teacher of those whom we reverence, and who step out of his carriage up to his carved cathedral place, shake his lawn ruffle over the velvet cushion, and cry out, that the whole struggle be an accursed one, and the work of the world be evil. many a conscience-stricken mystic fly from it altogether, and shut himself out from it within convent wall (real or spiritual), whence he can only look up to the sky, and contemplate the heaven out of which there be no rest, and no good.
"but the earth, where our foot be, be the work of the same power as the immeasurable blue yonder, in which the future lie into which we would peer. who order toil as the condition of life, order weariness, order sickness, ordered poverty, failure, success--to this man a foremost place, to the other a nameless struggle with the crowd--to that a shameful fall, or paralysed limb, or sudden accident--to each some work upon the ground he stand on, until he be lay beneath it." while they be talk, the dawn come shin through the window of the room, and Pen throw they open to receive the fresh morning air. "look, George," say he; "look and see the sun rise: he see the labourer on his way a-field; the work-girl ply her poor needle; the lawyer at his desk, perhaps; the beauty smile asleep upon her pillow of down; or the jade reveller reel to bed; or the fevere patient tossing on it; or the doctor watch by it, over the throe of the mother for the child that be to be bear into the world;--to be bear and to take his part in the suffering and struggle, the tear and laughter, the crime, remorse, love, folly, sorrow, rest."
CHAPTER XLVII
Miss Amory's Partners
the noble Henry Foker, of whom we have lose sight for a few page, have be in the meanwhile occupy, as we might suppose a man of his constancy would be, in the pursuit and indulgence of his all-absorb passion of love.
I wish that a few of my youthful reader who be inclined to that amusement would take the trouble to calculate the time which be spend in the pursuit, when they would find it to be one of the most costly occupation in which a man can possibly indulge. what donot you sacrifice to it, indeed, young gentleman and young lady of ill-regulate mind? many hour of your precious sleep in the first place, in which you lie toss and think about the adore object, whence you come down late to breakfast, when noon be advance and all the family be long since away to its daily occupation. then when you at length get to these occupation you pay no attention to they, and engage in they with no ardour--all your thought and power of mind be fix elsewhere. then the day's work be slur over, you neglect your friend and relative, your natural companion and usual associate in life, that you may go and have a glance at the dear personage, or a look up at her window, or a peep at her carriage in the Park. then at night the artless blandishment of home bore you; mamma's conversation pall upon you; the dish which that good soul prepare for the dinner of her favourite be send away untasted,--the whole meal of life, indeed, except one particular plat, have no relish. life, business, family tie, home, all thing useful and dear once, become intolerable, and you be never easy except when you be in pursuit of your flame.
such I believe to be not unfrequently the state of mind amongst ill-regulate young gentleman, and such indeed be Mr. H. Foker's condition, who, have be breed up to indulge in every propensity towards which he be incline, abandon himself to this one with his usual selfish enthusiasm. nor because he have give his friend Arthur Pendennis a great deal of good advice on a former occasion, need man of the world wonder that Mr. Foker become passion's slave in his turn. who among we have not give a plenty of the very good advice to his friend? who have not preach, and who have practise? to be sure, you, madam, be perhaps a perfect being, and never have a wrong thought in the whole course of your frigid and irreproachable existence: or sir, you be a great deal too strong-minded to allow any foolish passion to interfere with your equanimity in chamber or your attendance on 'Change; you be so strong that you donot want any sympathy. we donot give you any, then; we keep ours for the humble and weak, that struggle and stumble and get up again, and so march with the rest of mortal. what need have you of a hand who never fall? your serene virtue be never shade by passion, or ruffle by temptation, or darken by remorse; compassion would be impertinence for such an angel: but then with such a one companionship become intolerable; you be, from the elevation of your very virtue and high attribute, of necessity lonely; we cannot reach up and talk familiarly with such potentatess good-bye, then; our way lie with humble folk, and not with serene highness like you; and we give notice that there be no perfect character in this history, except, perhaps, one little one, and that one be not perfect either, for she never know to this day that she be perfect, and with a deplorable misapprehension and perverseness of humility, believe herself to be as great a sinner as need be.
this young person do not happen to be in London at the present period of our story, and it be by no mean for the like of she that Mr. Henry Foker's mind be agitated. but what matter a few failing? need we be angel, male or female, in order to be worship as such? let we admire the diversity of the taste of mankind; and the old, the ugly, the stupid and most pompous, the silly and most vapid, the great criminal, tyrant booby, Bluebeard, Catherine Hayes, George Barnwell, amongst we, we need never despair. I have read of the passion of a transport pickpocket for a female convict (each of they advanced in age, be repulsive in person, ignorant, quarrelsome, and give to drink), that be as magnificent as the love of Cleopatra and Antony, or Lancelot and Guinever. the passion which Count Borulawski, the polish dwarf, inspire in the bosom of the most beautiful Baroness at the Court of Dresden, be a matter with which we be all of we acquaint: the flame which burn in the heart of young Cornet Tozer but the other day, and cause he to run off and espouse Mrs. Battersby, who be old enough to be his mamma,--all these instance be tell in the page of history or the newspaper column. be we to be ashamed or pleased to think that our heart be form so that the big and highest-place Ajax among we may some day find himself prostrate before the patten of his kitchen-maid; as that there be no poverty or shame or crime, which will not be support, hug even with delight, and cherish more closely than virtue would be, by the perverse fidelity and admirable constant folly of a woman?
so then Henry Foker, Esquire, long after his love, and curse the fate which separate he from she. when Lord Gravesend's family retire to the country (his lordship leave his proxy with the venerable Lord Bagwig), Harry still remain linger on in London, certainly not much to the sorrow of Lady Ann, to whom he be affiance, and who do not in the least miss he. wherever Miss Clavering go, this infatuate young fellow continue to follow she; and be aware that his engagement to his cousin be know in the world, he be force to make a mystery of his passion, and confine it to his own breast, so that it be so pent in there and press down, that it be a wonder he do not explode some day with the stormy secret, and perish collapse after the outburst.
there have be a grand entertainment at Gaunt House on one beautiful evening in June, and the next day's journal contain almost two column of the name of the most closely print nobility and gentry who have be honour with invitation to the ball. among the guest be Sir Francis and Lady Clavering and Miss Amory, for whom the indefatigable Major Pendennis have procure an invitation, and our two young friend Arthur and Harry. each exert himself, and dance a great deal with Miss Blanche. as for the worthy Major, he assume the charge of Lady Clavering, and take care to introduce she to that department of the mansion where her ladyship specially distinguished herself, namely, the refreshment-room, where, amongst picture of Titian and Giorgione, and regal portrait of Vandyke and Reynolds, and enormous salver of gold and silver, and pyramid of large flower, and constellation of wax candle --in a manner perfectly regardless of expense, in a word--a supper be go on all night. of how many cream, jelly, salad, peach, white soup, grape, pate, galantine, cup of tea, champagne, and so forth, Lady Clavering partook, it do not become we to say. how much the Major suffer as he follow the honest woman about, call to the solemn male attendant and lovely servant-maid, and administer to Lady Clavering's various want with admirable patience, nobody knows;--he never confess. he never allow his agony to appear on his countenance in the least; but with a constant kindness bring plate after plate to the Begum.
Mr. Wagg count up all the dish of which Lady Clavering partook as long as he could count (but as he partook very freely himself of champagne during the evening, his power of calculation be not to be trust at the close of the entertainment), and he recommend Mr. Honeyman, Lady Steyne's medical man, to look carefully after the Begum, and to call and get news of her ladyship the next day.
Sir Francis Clavering make his appearance, and skulk for a while about the magnificent room; but the company and the splendour which he meet there be not to the Baronet's taste, and after toss off a tumbler of wine or two at the buffet, he quit Gaunt House for the neighbourhood of Jermyn Street, where his friend Loder, Punter, little Moss Abramns, and Captain Skewball be assemble at the familiar green table. in the rattle of the box, and of their agreeable conversation, Sir Francis's spirit rise to their accustomed point of feeble hilarity.
Mr. Pynsent, who have ask Miss Amory to dance, come up on one occasion to claim her hand, but scowl of recognition have already pass between he and Mr. Arthur Pendennis in the dancing-room, Arthur suddenly rise up and claim Miss Amory as his partner for the present dance, on which Mr. Pynsent, bite his lip and scowl yet more savagely, withdraw with a profound bow, say that he give up his claim. there be some man who be always fall in one's way in life. Pynsent and Pen have this view of each other; and each regard other accordingly.
"what a confound conceited provincial fool that be!" think the one. "because he have write a twopenny novel, his absurd head be turn, and a kicking would take his conceit out of he."
"what an impertinent idiot that man be!" remark the other to his partner. "his soul be in Downing Street; his neckcloth be foolscap; his hair be sand; his leg be ruler; his vital be tape and sealing-wax; he be a prig in his cradle; and never laugh since he be bear, except three time at the same joke of his chief. I have the same liking for that man, Miss Amory, I have for that cold boil veal." upon which Blanche of course remark, that Mr. Pendennis be wicked, mechant, perfectly abominable, and wonder what he would say when her back be turn.
"say!--say that you have the most beautiful figure, and the slim waist in the world, Blanche--Miss Amory, I mean. I beg your pardon. another turn; this music would make an alderman dance."
"and you have leave off tumble when you waltz now?" Blanche ask, archly look up at her partner's face.
"one fall and one get up again in life, Blanche; you know I use to call you so in old time, and it be the prettiest name in the world. besides, I have practise since then."
"and with a great number of partner, Ibe afraid," Blanche say, with a little sham sigh, and a shrug of the shoulder. and so in truth Mr. Pen have practise a good deal in this life; and have undoubtedly arrive at be able to dance well.
if Pendennis be impertinent in his talk, Foker, on the other hand, so bland and communicative on most occasion, be entirely mum and melancholy when he dance with Miss Amory. to clasp her slender waist be a rapture, to whirl round the room with she be a delirium; but to speak to she, what could he say that be worthy of she? what pearl of conversation could he bring that be fit for the acceptance of such a queen of love and wit as Blanche? it be she who make the talk when she be in the company of this love-stricken partner. it be she who ask he bow that dear little pony be, and look at he and thank he with such a tender kindness and regret, and refuse the dear little pony with such a delicate sigh when he offer it. "I have nobody to ride with in London," she say. "Mamma be timid, and her figure be not pretty on horseback. Sir Francis never go out with I. he love I like--like a stepdaughter. oh, how delightful it must be to have a father--a father, Mr. Foker!"
"oh, uncommon," say Mr. Harry, who enjoy that blessing very calmly, upon which, and forget the sentimental air which she have just before assume, Blanche's grey eye gaze at Foker with such an arch twinkle that both of they burst out laugh, and Harry enrapture and at his ease begin to entertain she with a variety of innocent prattle--good kind simple Foker talk, flavour with many expression by no mean to be discover in dictionary, and relate to the personal history of himself or horse, or other thing dear and important to he, or to person in the ballroom then pass before they, and about whose appearance or character Mr. Harry speak with artless freedom, and a considerable dash of humour.
and it be Blanche who, when the conversation flag, and the youth's modesty come rush back and overpower he, know how to reanimate her companion: ask he question about Logwood, and whether it be a pretty place? whether he be a hunt man, and whether he like woman to hunt? (in which case she be prepared to say that she adore hunting)--but Mr. Foker express his opinion against sporting female, and point out Lady Bullfinch, who happen to pass by, as a horse-godmother, whom he have see at cover with a cigar in her face, Blanche too express her detestation of the sport of the field, and say it would make her shudder to think of a dear sweet little fox be kill, on which Foker laugh and waltz with renew vigour and grace.
and at the end of the waltz,--the last waltz they have on that night,-- Blanche ask he about Drummington, and whether it be a fine house. his cousin, she have hear, be very accomplish: Lord Erith she have meet, and which of his cousin be his favourite? be it not Lady Ann? yes, she be sure it be she; sure by his look and his blush. she be tired of dancing; it be get very late; she must go to mamma;--and, without another word, she spring away from Harry Foker's arm, and seize upon Pen's, who be swagger about the dancing-room, and again say, "Mamma, mamma!--take I to mamma, dear, Mr. Pendennis!" transfix Harry with a parthian shot, as she flee from he.
my Lord Steyne, with garter and ribbon, with a bald head and shin eye, and a collar of red whisker round his face, always look grand upon an occasion of state; and make a great effect upon Lady Clavering, when he introduce himself to she at the request of the obsequious Major Pendennis. with his own white and royal hand, he hand to her ladyship a glass of wine, say he have hear of her charming daughter, and beg to be present to she; and, at this very juncture, Mr. Arthur Pendennis come up with the young lady on his arm.
the peer make a profound bow, and Blanche the deep curtesy that ever be see. his lordship give Mr. Arthur Pendennis his hand to shake; say he have read his book, which be very wicked and clever; ask Miss Blanche if she have read it,--at which Pen blush and wince. why, Blanche be one of the heroine of the novel. Blanche, in black ringlet and a little alter, be the Neaera of 'Walter Lorraine.'
Blanche have read it: the language of the eye express her admiration and rapture at the performance. this little play be achieve, the Marquis of Steyne make other two profound bow to Lady Clavering and her daughter, and pass on to some other of his guest at the splendid entertainment.
Mamma and daughter be loud in their expression of admiration of the noble Marquis so soon as his broad back be turn upon they. "he say they make a very nice couple," whisper major Pendennis to Lady Clavering. do he now, really? Mamma think they would; Mamma be so flustered with the honour which have just be show to she, and with other intoxicate event of the evening, that her good-humour know no bound. she laugh, she wink, and nod knowingly at Pen; she tap he on the arm with her fan; she tap Blanche; she tap the major;-- her contentment be boundless, and her method of show her joy equally expansive.
as the party go down the great staircase of Gaunt House, the morning have rise stark and clear over the black tree of the square; the sky be ting with pink; and the cheek of some of the people at the ball, --ah, how ghastly they look! that admirable and devoted Major above all,--who have be for hour by Lady Clavering's side, minister to she and feed her body with everything that be nice, and her ear with everything that be sweet and flattering,--oh! what an object he be! the ring round his eye be of the colour of bistre; those orb themselves be like the plover' egg whereof Lady Clavering and Blanche have each taste; the wrinkle in his old face be furrow in deep gash; and a silver stubble, like an elderly morning dew be glitter on his chin, and alongside the dye whisker now limp and out of curl.
there he stand, with admirable patience, endure, uncomplainingly, a silent agony; know that people could see the state of his face (for could he not himself perceive the condition of other, male and female, of his own age?)--longing to go to rest for hour past; aware that supper disagree with he, and yet have eat a little so as to keep his friend, Lady Clavering, in good-humour; with twinge of rheumatism in the back and knee; with weary foot burn in his varnished boots,--so tired, oh, so tired and longing for bed! if a man, struggle with hardship and bravely overcome it, be an object of admiration for the god, that power in whose chapel the old Major be a faithful worshipper must have look upwards approvingly upon the constancy of Pendennis's martyrdom. there be sufferer in that cause as in the other: the negro in the service of Mumbo Jumbo tattoo and drill themselves with burn skewer with great fortitude; and we read that the priest in the service of Baal gash themselves and bleed freely. you who can smash the idol, do so with a good courage; but do not be too fierce with the idolaters,-- they worship the good thing they know.
the Pendennises, the elder and the young, wait with Lady Clavering and her daughter until her ladyship's carriage be announce, when the elder's martyrdom may be say to have come to an end, for the good-natured Begum insist upon leave he at his door in Bury Street; so he take the back seat of the carriage after a feeble bow or two, and speech of thank, polite to the last, and resolute in do his duty. the Begum wave her dumpy little hand by way of farewell to Arthur and Foker, and Blanche smile languidly out upon the young man, think whether she look very wan and green under her rose-colour hood, and whether it be the mirror at Gaunt House, or the fatigue and fever of her own eye, which make her fancy herself so pale.
Arthur, perhaps, see quite well how yellow Blanche look, but do not attribute that peculiarity of her complexion to the effect of the look-glass, or to any error in his sight or her own. our young man of the world could use his eye very keenly, and could see Blanche's face pretty much as nature have make it. but for poor Foker it have a radiance which dazzle and blind he: he could see no more fault in it than in the sun, which be now flare over the house-top.
amongst other wicked London habit which Pen have acquire, the moralist will remark that he have get to keep very bad hour; and often be go to bed at the time when sober country-people be think of leave it. man get use to one hour as to another. editor of newspaper, Covent Garden market-people, night cabman and coffee-seller, chimney-sweep, and gentleman and lady of fashion who frequent ball, be often quite lively at three or four o'clock of a morning, when ordinary mortal be snore. we have show in the last chapter how Pen be in a brisk condition of mind at this period, incline to smoke his cigar at ease, and to speak freely.
Foker and Pen walk away from Gaunt House, then, indulge in both the above amusement: or rather Pen talk, and Foker look as if he want to say something. Pen be sarcastic and dandified when he have be in the company of great folk; he could not help imitate some of their air and tone, and have a most lively imagination, mistook himself for a person of importance very easily. he rattle away, and attack this person and that; sneer at Lady John Turnbull's bad French, which her ladyship will introduce into all conversation in spite of the sneer of everybody; at Mrs. Slack Roper's extraordinary costume and sham jewel; at the old dandy and the young ones;--at whom donot he sneer and laugh?
"you fire at everybody, Pen--you're grow awful, that you be," Foker say. "now youhave pull about Blondel's yellow wig, and Colchicum's black one, why donot you have a shy at a brown one, hay? you know whose I mean. it get into Lady Clavering's carriage."
"under my uncle's hat? my uncle be a martyr, Foker, my boy. my uncle have be do excruciating duty all night. he like to go to bed rather early. he have a dreadful headache if he sit up and touch supper. he always have the gout if he walk or stand much at a ball. he have be sit up, and stand up, and sup. he have go home to the gout and the headache, and for my sake. shall I make fun of the old boy? no, not for Venice!"
"how do you mean that he have be do it for your sake?" Foker ask, look rather alarmed.
"boy! canst thou keep a secret if I impart it to thee?" Pen cry out, in high spirit. "art thou of good counsel? wilt thou swear? wilt thou be mum, or wilt thou preach? wilt thou be silent and hear, or wilt thou speak and die?" and as he speak, fling himself into an absurd theatrical attitude, the man in the cabstand in Piccadilly wonder and grin at the antic of the two young swell.
"what the doose be you drive at?" Foker ask, look very much agitated.
Pen, however, do not remark this agitation much, but continue in the same bantering and excited vein. "Henry, friend of my youth," he say, "and witness of my early folly, though dull at thy book, yet thou art not altogether deprive of sense,--nay, blush not, Henrico, thou hast a good portion of that, and of courage and kindness too, at the service of thy friend. be I in a strait of poverty, I would come to my Foker's purse. be I in grief, I would discharge my grief upon his sympathise bosom----"
"Gammon, Pen--go on," Foker say.
"I would, Henrico, upon thy stud, and upon thy cambric work by the hand of beauty, to adorn the breast of valour! know then, friend of my boyhood's day, that Arthur Pendennis of the Upper Temple, student-at-law, feel that he be grow lonely and old Care be furrow his temple, and Baldness be busy with his crown. shall we stop and have a drop of coffee at this stall, it look very hot and nice? look how that cabman be blow at his saucer. no, you willnot? Aristocrat! I resume my tale. I be get on in life. I have get devilish little money. I want some. I be think of get some, and settle in life. Ibe think of settle. Ibe think of marry, old boy. Ibe think of become a moral man; a steady port and sherry character: with a good reputation in my quarti, and a moderate establishment of two maid and a man--with an occasional brougham to drive out Mrs. Pendennis, and a house near the Parks for the accommodation of the child. ha! what sayest thou? answer thy friend, thou worthy child of beer. speak, I adjure thee by all thy vat.
"but you benot get any money, Pen," say the other, still look alarmed.
"I benot? no, but she ave. I tell thee there be gold in store for I--not what you call money, nurse in the lap of luxury, and cradle on grain, and drink in wealth from a thousand mash-tub. what do you know about money? what be poverty to you, be splendour to the hardy son of the humble apothecary. you cannot live without an establishment, and your house in town and country. a snug little house somewhere off Belgravia, a brougham for my wife, a decent cook, and a fair bottle of wine for my friend at home sometimes; these simple necessary suffice for I, my Foker." and here Pendennis begin to look more serious. without banter far, Pen continue, "Ihave rather serious thought of settle and marrying. no man can get on in the world without some money at his back. you must have a certain stake to begin with, before you can go in and play the great game. who know that Ibe not go to try, old fellow? bad man than I have win at it. and as I have not get enough capital from my father, I must get some by my wife--thatbe all."
they be walk down Grosvenor Street, as they talk, or rather as Pen talk, in the selfish fulness of his heart; and Mr. Pen must have be too much occupy with his own affair to remark the concern and agitation of his neighbour, for he continue: "we be no long child, you know, you and I, Harry. Bah! the time of our romance have pass away. we donot marry for passion, but for prudence and for establishment. what do you take your cousin for? because she be a nice girl, and an Earl's daughter, and the old folk wish it, and that sort of thing."
"and you, Pendennis," ask Foker, "you benot very fond of the girl-- yoube go to marry?"
Pen shrug his shoulder. "Comme can," say he; "I like she well enough. shebe pretty enough; shebe clever enough. I think shewill do very well. and she have get money enough--thatbe the great point. Psha! you know who she be, donot you? I think you be sweet on her yourself one night when we din with her mamma. itbe little amory."
"I--I think so," Foker say; "and have she accept you!"
"not quite," Arthur reply, with a confident smile, which seem to say, I have but to ask, and she come to I that instant.
"oh, not quite," say Foker; and he break out with such a dreadful laugh, that Pen, for the first time, turn his thought from himself towards his companion, and be strike by the other's ghastly pale face.
"my dear fellow, Fo! whatbe the matter? yoube ill," Pen say, in a tone of real concern.
"you think it be the champagne at Gaunt House, donot you? it benot that. come in; let I talk to you for a minute. Iwill tell you what it be. d--- it, let I tell somebody," Foker say.
they be at Mr. Foker's door by this time, and, open it, Harry walk with his friend into his apartment, which be situate in the back part of the house, and behind the family dining-room where the eld Foker receive his guest, surround by picture of himself, his wife, his infant son on a donkey, and the late Earl of Gravesend in his robe as a Peer. Foker and Pen pass by this chamber, now close with death-like shutter, and enter into the young man's own quarter. Dusky stream of sunbeam be play into that room, and light up poor Harry's gallery of dancing-girl and opera nymph with flickering illumination.
"look here! I cannot help tell you, Pen," he say. ever since the night we din there, Ibe so fond of that girl, that I think I shall die if I donot get she. I feel as if I should go mad sometimes. I cannot stand it, Pen. I couldnot bear to hear you talk about she, just now, about marry she only because shebe money. ah, Pen! that benot the question in marry. Iwould bet anything it benot. talk about money and such a girl as that, it's--it's--what-d'ye-call-'em--you know what I mean--I benot good at talk--sacrilege, then. if shewould have I, Iwould take and sweep a crossing, that I would!"
"poor fo! I donot think that would tempt she," Pen say, eye his friend with a great deal of real good-nature and pity. "she be not a girl for love and a cottage."
"she ought to be a duchess, I know that very well, and I know she wouldnot take I unless I could make she a great place in the world--for I benot good for anything myself much--I benot clever and that sort of thing," Foker say sadly. "if I have all the diamond that all the duchess and marchioness have on to-night, wouldnot I put them in her lap? but whatbe the use of talk? Ibe book for another race. itbe that kill I, Pen. I cannot get out of it; though I die, I cannot get out of it. and though my cousinbe a nice girl, and I like she very well, and that, yet I havenot see this one when our governor settle that matter between we. and when you talk, just now, about she do very well, and about she have money enough for both of you, I think to myself it benot money or mere like a girl, that ought to be enough to make a fellow marry. he may marry, and find he like somebody else well. all the money in the world willnot make you happy then. look at I; Ihave plenty of money, or shall have out of the mash-tub, as you call them. my Governor think hewould make it all right for I in settle my marriage with my cousin. I tell you it willnot do; and when Lady Ann have get her husband, it willnot be happy for either of we, and shewill have the most miserable beggar in town."
"poor old fellow!" Pen say, with rather a cheap magnanimity, "I wish I could help you. I have no idea of this, and that you be so wild about the girl. do you think she would have you without your money? no. do you think your father would agree to break off your engagement with your cousin? you know he very well, and that he would cast you off rather than do so."
the unhappy Foker only groan a reply, fling himself prostrate on a sofa, face forwards, his head in his hand.
"as for my affair," Pen go on, "my dear fellow, if I have think matter be so critical with you, at least I would not have pain you by choose you as my confidant. and my business be not serious, at least not as yet. I have not speak a word about it to Miss Amory. very likely she would not have I if I ask she. only I have have a great deal of talk about it with my uncle, who say that the match might be an eligible one for I. Ibe ambitious and Ibe poor. and it appear Lady Clavering will give she a good deal of money, and Sir Francis might be get to never mind the rest. nothing be settle, Harry. they be go out of town directly. I promise you I willnot ask she before she go. therebe no hurry: therebe time for everybody. but, suppose you get she, Foker. remember what you say about marriage just now, and the misery of a man who donot care for his wife; and what sort of a wife would you have who donot care for her husband?"
"but she would care for I," say Foker, from his sofa--"that be, I think she would. last night only, as we be dance, she said----"
"what do she say?" Pen cry, start up in great wrath. but he see his own meaning more clearly than Foker, and break off with a laugh--"Well, never mind what she say, Harry. Miss Amory be a clever girl, and say number of civil thing--to you--to I, perhaps--and who the deuce know to whom besides? nothing's settle, old boy. at least, my heart willnot break if I donot get she. win she if you can, and I wish you joy of she. good-bye! donot think about what I say to you. I be excited, and confoundedly thirsty in those hot room, and donot, I suppose, put enough Seltzer-water into the champagne. good night! Iwill keep your counsel too. 'Mum' be the word between we; and 'let there be a fair fight, and let the good man win,' as Peter Crawley say."
so say, Mr. Arthur Pendennis, give a very queer and rather dangerous look at his companion, shake he by the hand, with something of that sort of cordiality which befit his just repeat simile of the boxing-match, and which Mr. Bendigo display when he shake hand with Mr. Gaunt before they fight each other for the champion's belt and two hundred pound a side. Foker return his friend's salute with an implore look, and a piteous squeeze of the hand, sink back on his cushion again, and Pen, put on his hat, stride forth into the air, and almost over the body of the matutinal housemaid, who be rub the step at the door.
"and so he want she too, do be?" think Pen as he march along--and note within himself with a fatal keenness of perception and almost an infernal mischief, that the very pain and torture which that honest heart of Foker's be suffer give a zest and an impetus to his own pursuit of Blanche: if pursuit might be call which have be no pursuit as yet, but mere sport and idle dallying. "she say something to he, do she? perhaps she give he the fellow flower to this;" and he take out of his coat and twiddle in his thumb and finger a poor little shrivelled crumpled bud that have fade and blacken with the heat and flare of the night--"i wonder to how many more she have give her artless token of affection--the little flirt"--and he fling his into the gutter, where the water may have refresh it, and where any amateur of rosebud may have pick it up. and then bethink he that the day be quite bright, and that the passer-by by might be star at his beard and white neckcloth, our modest young gentleman take a cab and drive to the Temple.
ah! be this the boy that pray at his mother's knee but a few year since, and for whom very likely at this hour of morning she be pray? be this jade and selfish worldle the lad who, a short while back, be ready to fling away his worldly all, his hope, his ambition, his chance of life, for his love? this be the man you be proud of, old Pendennis. you boast of have form he: and of have reason he out of his absurd romance and folly--and groan in your bed over your pain and rheumatism, satisfy yourself still by think, that, at last, that lad will do something to better himself in life, and that the Pendennises will take a good place in the world. and be he the only one, who in his progress through this dark life go wilfully or fatally astray, whilst the natural truth and love which should illumine he grow dim in the poison air, and suffice to light he no more?
when Pen be go away, poor Harry Foker get up from the sofa, and take out from his waistcoat--the splendidly button, but the gorgeously embroider, the work of his mamma--a little white rosebud, he draw from his dressing-case, also the maternal present, a pair of scissor, with which he nip carefully the stalk of the flower, and place it in a glass of water opposite his bed, he seek refuge there from care and bitter remembrance.
it be to be presume that Miss Blanche Amory have more than one rise in her bouquet, and why should not the kind young creature give out of her superfluity, and make as many partner as possible happy?
CHAPTER XLVII
Monseigneur s'amuse
the exertion of that last night at Gaunt House have prove almost too much for Major Pendennis; and as soon as he could move his weary old body with safety, he transport himself groan to Buxton, and seek relief in the healing water of that place. Parliament break up. Sir Francis Clavering and family leave town, and the affair which we have just mention to the reader be not advanced, in the brief interval of a few day or week which have occur between this and the last chapter. the town be, however, empty since then.
the season be now come to a conclusion: Pen's neighbour, the lawyer, be go upon circuit: and his more fashionable friend have take their passport for the continent, or have flee for health or excitement to the Scotch moor. Scarce a man be to be see in the bow-window of the Clubs, or on the solitary Pall Mall pavement. the red jacket have disappear from before the Palace-gate: the tradesman of St. James's be abroad take their pleasure: the tailor have grow mustachio and be go up the rhine: the bootmaker be at Ems or Baden, blush when they meet their customer at those place of recreation, or punt beside their creditor at the gambling-table: the clergyman of St. James's only preach to half a congregation, in which there be not a single sinner of distinction: the band in Kensington Gardens have shut up their instrument of brass and trumpet of silver: only two or three old fly and chaise crawl by the bank of the Serpentine; and Clarence Bulbul, who be retain in town by his arduous duty as a Treasury clerk, when he take his afternoon ride in Rotten row, compare its loneliness to the vastness of the arabian desert and himself to a Bedouin wend his way through that dusty solitude. Warrington stow away a quantity of cavendish tobacco in his carpet-bag, and betook himself, as his custom be in the vacation, to his brother's house in Norfolk. Pen be leave alone in chamber for a while, for this man of fashion could not quit the metropolis when he choose always: and be at present detain by the affair of his newspaper, the Pall Mall Gazette, of, which he act as the editor and charge d'affaires during the temporary absence of the chief, Captain Shandon, who be with his family at the salutary watering-place of Boulogne-sur-Mer.
although, as we have see, Mr. Pen have pronounce himself for year past to be a man perfectly blase and weary of life, yet the truth be that he be an exceedingly healthy young fellow still: with a fine appetite, which he satisfy with the great relish and satisfaction at least once a day; and a constant desire for society, which show he to be anything but misanthropical. if he could not get a good dinner he sate down to a bad one with perfect contentment; if he could not procure the company of witty or great or beautiful person, he put up with any society that come to hand; and be perfectly satisfied in a tavern-parlour or on board a Greenwich steamboat, or in a jaunt to Hampstead with Mr. Finucane, his colleague at the Pall Mall Gazette; or in a visit to the summer theatre across the river; or to the Royal Gardens of Vauxhall, where he be on term of friendship with the great Simpson, and where he shake the principal comic singer of the lovely equestrian of the arena by the hand. and while he could watch the grimace or the grace of these with a satiric humour that be not deprive of sympathy, he could look on with an eye of kindness at the looker-on too; at the roystere youth bent upon enjoyment, and here take it: at the honest parent, with their delighted child laugh and clap their hand at the show: at the poor outcast, whose laughter be less innocent though perhaps louder, and who bring their shame and their youth here, to dance and be merry till the dawn at least; and to get bread and drown care. of this sympathy with all condition of man Arthur often boast: say he be pleased to possess it: and that he hop thus to the last he should retain it. as another man have an ardour for art or music, or natural science, Mr. Pen say that anthropology be his favourite pursuit; and have his eye always eagerly open to its infinite variety and beauty: contemplate with an unfailing delight all specimen of it in all place to which he resort, whether it be the coquetting of a wrinkle dowager in a ballroom, or a high-breed young beauty blushing in her prime there; whether it be a hulking guardsman coax a servant-girl in the Park--or innocent little Tommy that be feed the duck whilst the nurse listen. and indeed a man whose heart be pretty clean, can indulge in this pursuit with an enjoyment that never cease, and be only perhaps the more keen because it be secret and have a touch of sadness in it: because he be of his mood and humour lonely, and apart although not alone.
yes, Pen use to brag and talk in his impetuous way to Warrington. "I be in love so fiercely in my youth, that I have burn out that flame for ever, I think, and if ever I marry, it will be a marriage of reason that I will make, with a well-breed, good-temper, good-look person who have a little money, and so forth, that will cushion our carriage in its course through life. as for romance, it be all do; I have spend that out, and be old before my time--I'm proud of it."
"Stuff!" growl the other, "you fancy you be get bald the other day, and brag about it as you do about everything. but you begin to use the bear's-grease pot directly the hairdresser tell you; and be scented like a barber ever since."
"you be diogene," the other answer, "and you want every man to live in a tub like yourself. violet smell well than stale tobacco, you grizzly old cynic." but Mr. Pen be blush whilst he make this reply to his unromantical friend, and indeed care a great deal more about himself still than such a philosopher perhaps should have do. indeed, consider that he be careless about the world, Mr. Pen ornament his person with no small pain in order to make himself agreeable to it, and for a weary pilgrim as he be, wear very tight boot and bright varnish.
it be in this dull season of the year, then, of a shin Friday night in autumn, that Mr. Pendennis, have complete at his newspaper office a brilliant lead article--such as Captain Shandon himself might have write, have the Captain be in good-humour, and incline to work, which he never would do except under compulsion--that Mr. Arthur Pendennis have write his article, and review it approvingly as it lie before he in its wet proof-sheet at the office of the paper, bethink he that he would cross the water, and regale himself with the firework and other amusement of Vauxhall. so he affably put in his pocket the order which admit "Editor of Pall Mall Gazette and friend" to that place of recreation, and pay with the coin of the realm a sufficient sum to enable he to cross Waterloo Bridge. the walk thence to the Gardens be pleasant, the star be shin in the sky above, look down upon the royal property, whence the rocket and roman candle have not yet ascend to outshine the star.
before you enter the enchant ground, where twenty thousand additional lamp be burn every night as usual, most of we have pass through the black and dreary passage and wicket which hide the splendour of Vauxhall from uninitiated man. in the wall of this passage be two hole strongly illuminate, in the midst of which you see two gentleman at desk, where they will take either your money as a private individual, or your order of admission if you be provide with that passport to the Gardens. Pen go to exhibit his ticket at the last-name orifice, where, however, a gentleman and two lady be already in parley before he.
the gentleman, whose hat be very much on one side, and who wear a short and shabby cloak in an excessively smart manner, be cry out in a voice which Pen at once recognise.
"Bedad, sir, if ye doubt I honour, will ye obleege I by stippe out of that box, and----"
"Lor, Capting!" cry the eld lady.
"donot bother I," say the man in the box.
and ask Mr. Hodgen himself, whobe in the gyardens, to let these leedie pass. donot be froightene, I dear madam, Ibe not go to quarl with this gintleman, at anyreet before leedie. will ye go, sir, and desoire Mr. Hodgen (whose orther I keem in with, and hebe I most intemate friend, and I know hebe goan to sing the 'Body Snatcher' here to-noight), with Captain Costigan's compliment, to stip out and let in the leedies-- for meself, sir, Ihave see Vauxhall, and I scawrun any interfayrance on moi account: but for these leedie, one of they have never be there, and of should think ye'd harly take advantage of I misfartune in lose the ticket, to deproive she of her pleasure."
"it benot no use, Captain. I cannot go about your business," the check-taker say; on which the Captain swear an oath, and the eld lady say, "Lor, ow provokin!"
as for the young one, she look up at the Captain and say, "never mind, Captain Costigan, Ibe sure I donot want to go at all. come away, mamma." and with this, although she do not want to go at all, her feeling overcome she, and she begin to cry.
"I poor child!" the Captain say. "can ye see that, sir, and will ye not let this innocent creature in?"
"it benot my business," cry the doorkeeper, peevishly, out of the illuminate box. and at this minute Arthur come up, and recognise Costigan, say, "donot you know I, Captain? Pendennis!" and he take off his hat and make a bow to the two lady. "I dear boy! I dear friend!" cry the Captain, extend towards Pendennis the grasp of friendship; and he rapidly explain to the other what he call "a most unluckee conthratong." he have an order for Vauxhall, admit two, from Mr. Hodgen, then within the Gardens, and singing (as he do at the Back Kitchen and the nobility's concert, the 'Body Snatcher,' the 'death of General Wolfe,' the 'Banner of Blood,' and other favourite melody); and, have this order for the admission of two person, he think that it would admit three, and have come accordingly to the Gardens with his friend. but, on his way, Captain Costigan have lose the paper of admission--it be not forthcoming at all; and the leedie must go back again, to the great disappointment of one of they, as Pendennis see.
Arthur have a great deal of good-nature for everybody, and sympathise with the misfortune of all sort of people: how could he refuse his sympathy in such a case as this? he have see the innocent face as it look up to the Captain, the appealing look of the girl, the piteous quiver of the mouth, and the final outburst of tear. if it have be his last guinea in the world, he must have pay it to have give the poor little thing pleasure. she turn the sad implore eye away directly they light upon a stranger, and begin to wipe they with her handkerchief. Arthur look very handsome and kind as he stand before the woman, with his hat off, blushing, bowing, generous, a gentleman. "who be they?" he ask of himself. he think he have see the eld lady before.
"if I can be of any service to you, Captain Costigan," the young man say, "I hope you will command I; be there any difficulty about take these lady into the garden? will you kindly make use of my purse? And-- and I have a ticket myself which will admit two--I hope, ma'am, you will permit I?"
the first impulse of the Prince of Fairoaks be to pay for the whole party, and to make away with his newspaper order as poor Costigan have do with his own ticket. but his instinct, and the appearance of the two woman, tell he that they would be well pleased if he do not give himself the air of a grand seigneur, and he hand his purse to Costigan, and laughingly pull out his ticket with one hand, as he offer the other to the elder of the lady--lady be not the word-- they have bonnet and shawl, and collar and ribbon, and the young show a pretty little foot and boot under her modest grey gown, but his Highness of Fairoaks be courteous to every person who wear a petticoat whatever its texture be, and the humbler the wearer, only the more stately and polite in his demeanour.
"Fanny, take the gentleman's arm," the elder say; "since you will be so very kind--i've see you often come in at our gate, sir, and go in to Captain Strong's at no. 3."
Fanny make a little curtsey, and put her hand under Arthur's arm. it have on a shabby little glove, but it be pretty and small. she be not a child, but she be scarcely a woman as yet; her tear have dry up, and her cheek mantle with youthful blush, and her eye glisten with pleasure and gratitude, as she look up into Arthur's kind face.
Arthur, in a protect way, put his other hand upon the little one rest on his arm. "Fannybe a very pretty little name," he say, "and so you know I, do you?"
"we keep the lodge, sir, at Shepherd's Inn," Fanny say with a curtsey; "and Ihave never be at Vauxhall, sir, and Papa donot like I to go-- and--and--o--o--law, how beautiful!" she shrink back as she speak, start with wonder and delight as she see the Royal Gardens blaze before she with a hundred million of lamp, with a splendour such as the fine fairy tale, the fine pantomime she have ever witness at the theatre, have never realise. Pen be pleased with her pleasure, and press to his side the little hand which clung so kindly to he. "what would I not give for a little of this pleasure?" say the blase young man.
"your purse, Pendennis, I dear boy," say the Captain's voice behind he. "will ye count it? itbe all roight--no--ye thrust in old Jack Costigan (he thrust I, ye see, madam). Ye've be I preserver, Pen (Ihave know um since choildhood, Mrs. Bolton; hebe the proproietor of Fairoaks Castle, and manybe the cooper of clart Ihave dthrunk there with the first nobilitee of his neetive countee),--mr. Pendennis, ye've be I preserver, and of thank ye; I daughtther will thank ye;--Mr. Simpson, your humble servant sir."
if Pen be magnificent in his courtesy to the lady, what be his splendour in comparison to Captain Costigan's bow here and there, and cry bravo to the singer?
a man, descend like Costigan, from a long line of hibernian king, chieftain, and other magnate and sheriff of the county, have of course too much dignity and self-respect to walk arrum-in-arrum (as the Captain phrase it) with a lady who occasionally sweep his room out, and cook his mutton-chop. in the course of their journey from Shepherd's Inn to Vauxhall Gardens, Captain Costigan have walk by the side of the two lady, in a patronising and affable manner point out to they the edifice worthy of note, and discoorsing, accord to his willnot, about other city and country which he have visit, and the people of rank and fashion with whom he have the honour of an acquaintance. nor could it be expect, nor, indeed, do Mrs. Bolton expect, that, arrive in the Royal property, and strongly illuminate by the flare of the twenty thousand additional lamp, the Captain could relax from his dignity, and give an arm to a lady who be, in fact, little well than a housekeeper or charwoman.
but Pen, on his part, have no such scruple. Miss Fanny Bolton do not make his bed nor sweep his chamber; and he do not choose to let go his pretty little partner. as for Fanny, her colour heighten, and her bright eye shine the brighter with pleasure, as she lean for protection on the arm of such a fine gentleman as Mr. Pen. and she look at number of other lady in the place, and at score of other gentleman under whose protection they be walk here and there; and she think that her gentleman be handsomer and grander-look than any other gent in the place. of course there be votary of pleasure of all rank there--rakish young surgeon, fast young clerk and commercialist, occasional dandy of the Guard regiment, and the rest. Old Lord Colchicum be there in attendance upon Mademoiselle Caracoline, who have be rid in the ring; and who talk her native French very loud, and use idiomatic expression of exceed strength as she walk about, lean on the arm of his lordship.
Colchicum be in attendance upon Mademoiselle Carandine, little Tom Tufthunt be in attendance upon Lord Colchicum; and rather pleased, too, with his position. when Don Juan scalle the wall, therebe never a want of a Leporello to hold the ladder. Tom Tufthunt be quite happy to act as friend to the elderly viscount, and to carve the fowl, and to make the salad at supper. when Pen and his young lady meet the Viscount's party, that noble poor only give Arthur a pass leer of recognition as his lordship's eye pass from Pen's face under the bonnet of Pen's companion. but Tom Tufthunt wag his head very good-naturedly at Mr. Arthur, and say, "how be you, old boy?" and look extremely know at the godfather of this history.
"that be the great rider at Astley's; I have see she there," Miss Bolton say, look after Mademoiselle Caracoline; "and who be that old man? be it not the gentleman in the ring!"
"that be Lord Viscount Colchicum, Miss Fanny," say Pen with an air of protection. he mean no harm; he be pleased to patronise the young girl, and he be not displeased that she should be so pretty, and that she should be hang upon his arm, and that yonder elderly Don Juan should have see she there.
Fanny be very pretty; her eye be dark and brilliant, her tooth be like little pearl; her mouth be almost as red as Mademoiselle Caracoline's when the latter have put on her vermilion. and what a difference there be between the one's voice and the other's, between the girl's laugh and the woman's! it be only very lately, indeed, that Fanny, when look in the little glass over the Bows-Costigan mantelpiece as she be dust it have begin to suspect that she be a beauty. but a year ago, she be a clumsy, gawky girl, at whom her father sneer, and of whom the girl at the day-school (Miss Minifer's, Newcastle Street, Strand; Miss M., the young sister, take the lead business at the Norwich circuit in 182--; and she herself have play for two season with some credit T. R. E. O., T. R. S. W., until she fall down a trap-door and break her leg); the girl at Fanny's school, we say, take no account of she, and think she a dowdy little creature as long as she remain under Miss Minifer's instruction. and it be unremarked and almost unseen in the porter's dark lodge of Shepherd's Inn, that this little flower bloom into beauty.
so this young person hang upon Mr. Pen's arm, and they pace the garden together, Empty as London be, there be still some two million of people leave linger about it, and amongst they, one or two of the acquaintance of Mr. Arthur Pendennis.
amongst they, silent and alone, pale, with his hand in his pocket, and a rueful nod of the head to Arthur as they meet, pass Henry Foker, Esq. Young Henry be try to ease his mind by move from place to place, and from excitement to excitement. but he think about Blanche as he saunter in the dark walk; he think about Blanche as he look at the device of the lamp. he consult the fortune-teller about she, and be disappoint when that gipsy tell he that he be in love with a dark lady who would make he happy; and at the concert, though Mr. Momus sing his most stunning comic song, and ask his most astonishing riddle, never do a kind smile come to visit Foker's lip. in fact, he never hear Mr. Momus at all.
Pen and Miss Bolton be hard by listen to the same concert, and the latter remark, and Pen laugh at Mr. Fokei's woebegone face.
Fanny ask what it be that make that odd-look little man so dismal? "I think he be cross in love!" Pen, say. "benot that enough to make any man dismal, Fanny?" and he look down at she, splendidly protect she, like Egmont at Clara in Goethe's play, or Leicester at Amy in Scott's novel.
"cross in love be he? poor gentleman," say Fanny with a sigh, and her eye turn round towards he with no little kindness and pity--but Harry do not see the beautiful dark eye.
"how dy do, Mr. Pendennis!"--a voice break in here--it be that of a young man in a large white coat with a red neckcloth, over which a dingy shirt-collar be turn so as to exhibit a dubious neck--with a large pin of bullion or other metal, and an imaginative waistcoat with exceedingly fanciful glass button, and trouser that cry with a loud voice, "come look at I and see how cheap and tawdry I be; my master, what a dirty buck!" and a little stick in one pocket of his coat, and a lady in pink satin on the other arm--"How dy do--forget I, I dare say? huxter,-- Clavering."
"how do you do, Mr. Huxter," the Prince of Fairoaks say in his most princely manner--"I hope you be very well."
"pretty bobbish, thanky."--And Mr. Huxter wag his head. "I say, Pendennis, youhave be come it uncommon strong since we have the row at Wapshot's, donot you remember. great author, hay? go about with the swell. see your name in the Morning Post. I suppose yoube too much of a swell to come and have a bit of supper with an old friend?--charterhouse Lane to-morrow night,--some devilish good fellow from Bartholomew's, and some stunning gin-punch. herebe my card." and with this Mr. Huxter release his hand from the pocket where his cane be, and pull off the top of his card-case with his tooth produce thence a visit ticket, which he hand to Pen.
"you be exceedingly kind, I be sure," say Pen: "but I regret that I have an engagement which will take I out of town to-morrow night." and the Marquis of Fairoaks, wonder that such a creature as this could have the audacity to give he a card, put Mr. Huxter's card into his waistcoat pocket with a lofty courtesy. possibly Mr. Samuel Huxter be not aware that there be any great social difference between Mr. Arthur Pendennis and himself. Mr. Huxter's father be a surgeon and apothecary at Clavering just as Mr. Pendennis's papa have be a surgeon and apothecary at Bath. but the impudence of some man be beyond all calculation.
"well, old fellow, never mind," say Mr. Huxter, who, always frank and familiar, be from vinous excitement even more affable than usual. "if ever you be pass, look up our place, Ibe mostly at home Saturdays; and therebe generally a cheese cupboard. Ta, ta.--therebe the bell for the firework ring. come along, Mary." and he set off run with the rest of the crowd in the direction of the firework.
so do Pen presently, when this agreeable youth be out of sight, begin to run with his little companion; Mrs. Bolton follow after they, with Captain Costigan at her side. but the Captain be too majestic and dignified in his movement to run for friend or enemy, and he pursue his course with the usual jaunty swagger which distinguish his step, so that he and his companion be speedily distance by Pen and Miss Fanny.
perhaps Arthur forgot, or perhaps he do not choose to remember, that the eld couple have no money in their pocket, as have be prove by their adventure at the entrance of the Gardens; howbeit, Pen pay a couple of shilling for himself and his partner, and with she hang close on his arm, scale the staircase which lead to the firework gallery. the Captain and mamma might have follow they if they like, but Arthur and Fanny be too busy to look back. People be push and squeeze there beside and behind they. one eager individual rush by Fanny, and elbow she so, that she fall back with a little cry, upon which, of course, Arthur catch she adroitly in his arm, and, just for protection, keep she so defended, until they mount the stair, and take their place.
Poor Foker sate alone on one of the high bench, his face illuminate by the firework, or in their absence by the moon. Arthur see he, and laugh, but do not occupy himself about his friend much. he be engage with Fanny. how she wonder! how happy she be! how she cry O, O, O, as the rocket soar into the air, and shower down in azure, and emerald, and vermilion! as these wonder blaze and disappear before she, the little girl thrill and tremble with delight at Arthur's side --her hand be under his arm still, he feel it press he as she look up delighted.
"how beautiful they be, sir!" she cry.
"donot call I sir, Fanny," Arthur say.
a quick blush rush up into the girl's face. "what shall I call you?" she say, in a low voice, sweet and tremulous. "what would you wish I to say, sir?"
"again, Fanny! well, I forget; it be good so, my dear," Pendennis say, very kindly and gently. "I may call you Fanny?"
"oh yes!" she say, and the little hand press his arm once more very eagerly, and the girl clung to he so that he could feel her heart beat on his shoulder.
"I may call you Fanny, because you be a young girl, and a good girl, Fanny, and I be an old gentleman. but you mustnot call I anything but sir, or Mr. Pendennis, if you like; for we live in very different station, Fanny; and donot think I speak unkindly; and--and why do you take your hand away, Fanny? be you afraid of I? do you think I would hurt you? not for all the world, my dear little girl. and--and look how beautiful the moon and star be, and how calmly they shine when the rocket have go out, and the noisy wheel have do hiss and blaze. when I come here to-night I do not think I should have have such a pretty little companion to sit by my side, and see these fine firework. you must know I live by myself, and work very hard. I write in book and newspaper, Fanny; and I quite tired out, and be expect to sit alone all night; and--don't cry, my dear, dear, little girl." here Pen break out, rapidly put an end to the calm oration which he have begin to deliver; for the sight of a woman's tear always put his nerve in a quiver, and he begin forthwith to coax she and soothe she, and to utter a hundred and twenty little ejaculation of pity and sympathy, which need not be repeat here, because they would be absurd in print. so would a mother's talk to a child be absurd in print; so would a lover's to his bride. that sweet artless poetry bear no translation; and be too subtle for grammarian' clumsy definition. you have but the same four letter to describe the salute which you perform on your grandmother's forehead, and that which you bestow on the sacred cheek of your mistress; but the same four letter, and not one of they a labial. do we mean to hint that r. Arthur Pendennis make any use of the monosyllable in question? not so. in the first place, it be dark: the firework be over, and nobody could see he; secondly, he be not a man to have this kind of secret, and tell it; thirdly and lastly, let the honest fellow who have kiss a pretty girl, say what would have be his own conduct in such a delicate juncture?
well, the truth be, that however you may suspect he, and whatever you would have do under the circumstance, or Mr. Pen would have like to do, he behave honestly, and like a man. "I will not play with this little girl's heart," he say within himself, "and forget my own or her honour. she seem to have a great deal of dangerous and rather contagious sensibility, and I be very glad the firework be over, and that I can take she back to her mother. come along, Fanny; mind the step, and lean on I. donot stumble, you heedless little thing; this be the way, and there be your mamma at the door."
and there, indeed, Mrs. Bolton be, unquiet in spirit, and grasp her umbrella. she seize Fanny with maternal fierceness and eagerness, and utter some rapid abuse to the girl in an undertone. the expression in Captain Costigan's eye--stand behind the matron and wink at Pendennis from under his hat--be, I be bind to say, indefinably humorous.
it be so much so, that Pen could not refrain from burst into a laugh. "you should have take my arm, Mrs. Bolton," he say, offer it. "I be very glad to bring Miss Fanny back quite safe to you. we think you would have follow we up into the gallery. we enjoy the firework, donot we?"
"oh yes!" say Miss Fanny, with rather a demure look.
"and the bouquet be magnificent," say Pen. "and it be ten hour since I have anything to eat, lady; and I wish you would permit I to invite you to supper."
"Dad," say Costigan, "Iwould loike a snack to; only I forgawt I purse, or I should have invoite these leedie to a collection."
Mrs. Bolton with considerable asperity say, she ad an eadache, and would much rather go ome.
"a lobster salad be the good thing in the world for a headache," Pen say gallantly, "and a glass of wine Ibe sure will do you good. come, Mrs. Bolton, be kind to I and oblige I. I shallnot have the heart to sup without you, and upon my word I have have no dinner. give I your arm: give I the umbrella. Costigan, Ibe sure youwill take care of Miss Fanny; and I shall think Mrs. Bolton angry with I, unless she will favour I with her society. and we will all sup quietly, and go back in a cab together."
the cab, the lobster salad, the frank and good-humour look of Pendennis, as he smilingly invite the worthy matron, subdue her suspicion and her anger. since he would be so obliging, she think she could take a little bit of lobster, and so they all march away to a box; and Costigan call for a waither with such a loud and belligerent voice, as cause one of those official instantly to run to he.
the carte be examine on the wall, and Fanny be ask to choose her favourite dish; upon which the young creature say she be fond of lobster, too, but also own to a partiality for raspberry tart. this delicacy be provide by Pen, and a bottle of the most frisky champagne be moreover order for the delight of the lady. little Fanny drink this;--what other sweet intoxication have she not drunk in the course of the night?
when the supper, which be very brisk and gay, be over, and Captain Costigan and Mrs. Bolton have partake of some of the rack-punch that be so fragrant at Vauxhall, the bill be call and discharge by Pen with great generosity,--"loike a foin young english gentleman of th' olden toime, be Jove," Costigan enthusiastically remark. and as, when they go out of the box, he step forward and give Mrs. Bolton his arm, Fanny fall to Pen's lot, and the young people walk away in high good-humour together, in the wake of their senior.
the champagne and the rack-punch, though take in moderation by all person, except perhaps poor cos, who lurch ever so little in his gait, have set they in high spirit and good-humour, so that Fanny begin to skip and move her brisk little foot in time to the band, which be play waltz and galop for the dancer. as they come up to the dancing, the music and Fanny's foot seem to go quick together--she seem to spring, as if naturally, from the ground, and as if she require repression to keep she there.
"shouldnot you like a turn?" say the Prince of Fairoaks. "what fun it would be! Mrs. Bolton, ma'am, do let I take she once round." upon which Mr. Costigan say, "Off wid you!" and Mrs. Bolton not refuse (indeed, she be an old war-horse, and would have like, at the trumpet's sound, to have enter the arena herself), Fanny's shawl be off she back in a minute, and she and Arthur be whirl round in a waltz in the midst of a great deal of queer, but exceedingly joyful company.
Pen have no mishap this time with little Fanny, as he have with Miss Blanche in old days,--at least, there be no mishap of his making. the pair dance away with great agility and contentment,--first a waltz, then a galop, then a waltz again, until, in the second waltz, they be bump by another couple who have join the terpsichorean choir. this be Mr. Huxter and his pink satin young friend, of whom we have already have a glimpse.
Mr. Huxter very probably have be also partake of supper, for he be even more excited now than at the time when he have previously claim Pen's acquaintance; and, have run against Arthur and his partner, and nearly knock they down, this amiable gentleman of course begin to abuse the people whom he have injure, and break out into a volley of slang against the unoffending couple. "now then, stoopid! donot keep the ground if you cannot dance, old Slow Coach!" the young surgeon roar out (use, at the same time, other expression far more emphatic), and be join in his abuse by the shrill language and laughter of his partner; to the interruption of the ball, the terror of poor little fanny, and the immense indignation of Pen.
Arthur be furious; and not so angry at the quarrel as at the shame attend it. a battle with a fellow like that! a row in a public garden, and with a porter's daughter on his arm! what a position for Arthur Pendennis! he draw poor little Fanny hastily away from the dancer to her mother, and wish that lady, and Costigan, and poor Fanny underground, rather than there, in his companionship, and under his protection.
when Huxter commence his attack, that free-speak young gentleman have not see who be his opponent; and directly he be aware that it be Arthur whom he have insult, he begin to make apology. "hold your stoopid tongue, Mary," he say to his partner. "itbe an old friend and crony at home. I beg pardon, Pendennis; benot aware it be you, old boy." Mr. Huxter have be one of the boy of the Clavering School, who have be present at a combat which have be mention in the early part of this story, when young Pen knock down the big champion of the academy, and Huxter know that it be dangerous to quarrel with Arthur.
his apology be as odious to the other as his abuse have be. Pen stop his tipsy remonstrance, by tell he to hold his tongue, and desire he not to use his (Pendennis's) name in that place or any other; and he walk out of the garden with a titter behind he from the crowd, every one of whom he would have like to massacre for have be witness to the degrade broil. he walk out of the garden, quite forget poor little Fanny, who come tremble behind he with her mother and the stately Costigan.
he be bring back to himself by a word from the Captain, who touch he on the shoulder just as they be pass the inner gate.
"therebe no ray-admittance except ye pay again," the Captain say. "hadnot I well go back and take the fellow your message?" Pen burst out laugh. "take he a message! do you think I would fight with such a fellow as that?" he ask.
"no, no! donot, donot?" cry out little Fanny. "how can you be so wicked, Captain Costigan?" the Captain mutter something about honour, and wink knowingly at Pen, but Arthur say gallantly, "no, Fanny, donot be frightened. it be my fault to have dance in such a place,--I beg your padon to have ask you to dance there." and he give she his arm once more, and call a cab, and put his three friend into it.
he be about to pay the driver, and to take another carriage for himself, when little Fanny, still alarmed, put she little hand out, and catch he by the coat, and implore he and besought he to come in.
"will nothing satisfy you," say Pen, in great good-humour, "that I be not go back to fight he? well, I will come home with you. drive to Shepherd's Inn, cab." the cab drive to its destination. Arthur be immensely pleased by the girl's solicitude about he: her tender terror quite make he forget his previous annoyance.
Pen put the lady into their lodge, have shake hand kindly with both of they; and the Captain again whisper to he that he would see um in the morning if he be inclined, and take his message to that "scounthrel." but the Captain be in his usual condition when he make the proposal; and Pen be perfectly sure that neither he nor Mr. Huxter, when they awake, would remember anything about the dispute.
CHAPTYER xlviii
a Visit of Politeness
Costigan never rouse Pen from his slumber; there be no hostile message from Mr. Huxter to disturb he; and when Pen wake, it be with a brisker and more lively feeling than ordinarily attend that moment in the day of the tired and blase London man. a City man wake up to care and consol, and the thought of 'Change and the counting-house take possession of he as soon as sleep fly from under his night-cap; a lawyer rouse himself with the early morning to think of the case that will take he all his day to work upon, and the inevitable attorney to whom he have promise his paper ere night. which of we have not his anxiety instantly present when his eye be open, to it and to the world, after his night's sleep? kind strengthener that enable we to face the day's task with renewed heart! beautiful ordinance of Providence that create rest as it award labour!
Mr. Pendennis's labour, or rather his disposition, be of that sort that his daily occupation do not much interest he, for the excitement of literary composition pretty soon subside with the hire labourer, and the delight of see one's-self in print only extend to the first two or three appearance in the magazine or newspaper page. Pegasus put into harness, and oblige to run a stage every day, be as prosaic as any other hack, and willnot work without his whip or his feed of corn. so, indeed, Mr. Arthur perform his work at the Pall Mall Gazette (and since his success as a novelist with an increase salary), but without the least enthusiasm, do his good or pretty nearly, and sometimes write ill and sometimes well. he be a literary hack, naturally fast in pace, and brilliant in action.
neither do society, or that portion which he see, excite or amuse he over much. in spite of his brag and boast to the contrary, he be too young as yet for woman's society, which probably can only be have in perfection when a man have cease to think about his own person, and have give up all design of be a conqueror of lady; he be too young to be admit as an equal amongst man who have make their mark in the world, and of whose conversation he could scarcely as yet expect to be more than a listener. and he be too old for the man of pleasure of his own age; too much a man of pleasure for the man of business; destinie in a word to be a good deal alone. fate award this lot of solitude to many a man; and many like it from taste, as many without difficulty bear it. Pendennis, in reality, suffer it very equanimously; but in word, and accord to his willnot, grumble over it not a little.
"what a nice little artless creature that be," Mr. Pen think at the very instant of wake after the Vauxhall affair; "what a pretty natural manner she have; how much pleasanter than the minauderie of the young lady in the ballroom" (and here he recall to himself some instance of what he could not help see be the artful simplicity of Miss Blanche, and some of the stupid grace of other young lady in the polite world); "who could have think that such a pretty rose could grow in a porter's lodge, or bloom in that dismal old flower-pot of a Shepherd's Inn? so she learn to sing from old bow? if her singing voice be as sweet as her speak voice, it must be pretty. I like those low voilee voice. 'what would you like I to call you?' indeed, poor little fanny! it go to my heart to adopt the grand air with she and tell she to call I, 'Sir.' but wewill have no nonsense of that sort--no Faust and Margaret business for I. that old bow! so he teach she to sing, do he? hebe a dear old fellow, old bow: a gentleman in those old clothe: a philosopher, and with a kind heart, too. how good he be to I in the Fotheringay business. he, too, have have his grief and his sorrow. I must cultivate old bow. a man ought to see people of all sort. I be get tired of genteel society. besides, therebe nobody in town. yes, Iwill go and see bow, and Costigan too; what a rich character! begad, Iwill study he, and put he into a book." in this way our young anthropologist talk with himself, and as Saturday be the holiday of the week, the Pall Mall Gazette make its appearance upon that day, and the contributor to that journal have no further call upon their brain or ink-bottle, Mr. Pendennis determine he would take advantage of his leisure, and pay a visit to Shepherd's Inn--of course to see old bow.
the truth be, that if Arthur have be the most determined roue and artful Lovelace who ever set about deceive a young girl, he could hardly have adopt well mean for fascinating and overcome poor little Fanny Bolton than those which he have employ on the previous night. his dandified protect air, his conceit, generosity, and good-humour, the very sense of good and honesty which have enable he to check the tremulous advance of the young creature, and not to take advantage of that little flutter sensibility,--his fault and his virtue at once contribute to make her admire he; and if we could peep into Fanny's bed (which she share in a cupboard, along with those two little sister to whom we have see Mr. Costigan administer gingerbread and apple), we should find the poor little maid toss upon her mattress, to the great disturbance of its other two occupant, and think over all the delight and event of that delightful, eventful night, and all the word, look, and action of Arthur, its splendid hero. many novel have Fanny read, in secret and at home, in three volume and in number. Periodical literature have not reach the height which it have attain subsequently, and the girl of Fanny's generation be not enable to purchase sixteen page of excitement for a penny, rich with history of crime, murder, oppress virtue, and the heartless seduction of the aristocracy; but she have have the benefit of the circulate library which, in conjunction with her school and a small brandy-ball and millinery business, Miss Minifer kept,--and Arthur appear to she at once as the type and realisation of all the hero of all those darling greasy volume which the young girl have devour. Mr. Pen, we have see, be rather a dandy about shirt and haberdashery in general. Fanny have look with delight at the fineness of his linen, at the brilliancy of his shirt-stud, at his elegant cambric pocket-handkerchief and white glove, and at the jetty brightness of his charming boot. the Prince have appear and subjugate the poor little handmaid. his image traverse constantly her restless slumber; the tone of his voice, the blue light of his eye, the generous look, half love, half pity,--the manly protect smile, the frank, win laughter,--all these be repeat in the girl's fond memory. she feel still his arm encircle she, and see he smile so grand as he fill up that delicious glass of champagne. and then she think of the girl, her friend, who use to sneer at her --of Emma Baker, who be so proud, forsooth, because she be engage to a cheesemonger, in a white apron, near Clare Market; and of Betsy Rodgers, who make such a to-do about her young man--an attorney's clerk, indeed, that go about with a bag!
so that, at about two o'clock in the afternoon--the Bolton family have conclude their dinner (and Mr. B., who besides his place of porter of the Inn, be in the employ of Messrs. Tressler, the eminent undertaker of the Strand, be absent in the country with the Countess of Estrich's hearse), when a gentleman in a white hat and white trouser make his appearance under the Inn archway, and stop at the porter's wicket, Fanny be not in the least surprised, only delightful, only happy, and blush beyond all measure. she know it could be no other than he. she know hewould come. there he be; there be his Royal Highness beam upon she from the gate. she call to her mother, who be busy in the upper apartment, "Mamma, mamma," and run to the wicket at once, and open it, push aside the other child. how she blush as she give her hand to he! how affably he take off his white hat as he come in; the child star up at he! he ask Mrs. Bolton if she have sleep well, after the fatigue of the night, and hop she have no headache; and he say that as he be go that way, he could not pass the door without ask news of his little partner.
Mrs. Bolton be perhaps rather shy and suspicious about these advance; but Mr. Pen's good-humour be inexhaustible, he could not see that he be unwelcome. he look about the premise for a seat, and none be disengage, for a dish-cover be on one, a workbox on the other, and so forth, he take one of the child's chair, and perch himself upon that uncomfortable eminence. at this, the child begin laugh, the child Fanny louder than all--at least, she be more amused than any of they, and amazed at his Royal Highness's condescension. he to sit down in that chair--that little child's chair!--many and many a time after, she regard it: havenot we almost all, such furniture in our room, that our fancy people with dear figure, that our memory fill with sweet smile face, which may never look on we more?
