so Pen sate down and talk away with great volubility to Mrs. Bolton. he ask about the undertaking business, and how many mute go down with Lady Estrich's remain; and about the Inn, and who live there. he seem very much interested about Mr. Campion's cab and horse, and have meet that gentleman in society. he think he should like share in the Polwheedle and Tredyddlum; do Mrs. Bolton do for those chamber? be there any chamber to let in the Inn? it be well than the Temple: he should like to come to live in Shepherd's Inn. as for Captain Strong, and--Colonel Altamont--be his name? he be deeply interested in they too. the Captain be an old friend at home. he have din with he at chamber here, before the Colonel come to live with he. what sort of man be the Colonel? benot he a stout man, with a large quantity of jewellery, and a wig and large black whisker--very black (here Pen be immensely waggish, and cause hysteric giggle of delight from the ladies)--very black indeed; in fact, blue black; that be to say, a rich greenish purple? that be the man; he have meet he, too, at Sir fr---- in Society.
"oh, we know," say the lady, "Sir F---- be Sir F. Clavering hebe often here: two or three time a week with the Captain. my little boy have be out for bill-stamp for he. o Lor! Fanny love her mother for that speech. she cast up her dark eye to the low ceiling and say, "oh, that he have, Ibe sure, Ma," with a voice full of feeling.

these question answer, and Pen be at a loss for more, luckily recollect his privilege as a member of the Press, and ask the lady whether they would like any order for the play? the play be their delight, as it be almost always the delight of every theatrical person. when Bolton be away professionally (it appear that of late the porter of Shepherd's Inn have take a serious turn, drink a good deal, and otherwise make himself unpleasant to the lady of his family), they would like of all thing to slip out and go to the theatre--little Barney, their son, keep the lodge; and Mr. Pendennis's most generous and most genteel compliment of order be receive with boundless gratitude by both mother and daughter.
Fanny clap her hand with pleasure: her face beam with it. she look and nod, and laugh at har mamma, who nod and laugh in her turn. Mrs. Bolton be not superannuate for pleasure yet, or by any mean too old for admiration, she think. and very likely Mr. Pendennis, in his conversation with she, have insinuate some compliment, or shape his talk so as to please she. at first against Pen, and suspicious of he, she be his partisan now, and almost as enthusiastic about he as her daughter. when two woman get together to like a man, they help each other on--each push the other forward--and the second, out of sheer sympathy, become as eager as the principal:--at least, so it be say by philosopher who have examine this science.
so the offer of the play-ticket, and other pleasantry; put all party into perfect good-humour, except for one brief moment, when one of the young child, hear the name of 'Astley's' pronounce, come forward and state that she should like very much to go, too; on which, Fanny say, "donot bother!" rather sharply; and Mamma say, "Git-long, Betsy-Jane, do now, and play in the court:" so that the two little one, namely, Betsy-Jane and Ameliar--Ann, go away in their little innocent pinafore, and disport in the courtyard on the smooth gravel, round about the statue of Shepherd the Great.
and here, as they be play, they very possibly communicate with an old friend of theirs and dweller in the Inn; for while Pen be make himself agreeable to the lady at the lodge, who be laugh delighted at his sally, an old gentleman pass under the archway from the Inn-square, and come and look in at the door of the lodge.
he make a very blank and rueful face when he see Mr. Arthur seat upon a table, like Macheath in the play, in easy discourse with Mrs. Bolton and her daughter.

in Shepherd's Inn

"you be come to see I, sir," Mr. Bows say. "willnot you have the kindness to walk up to my chamber with I? you do they a great honour, I be sure. they be rather high up; but----"
"oh! I live in a garret myself, and Shepherd's Inn be twice as cheerful as Lamb Court," Mr. Pendennis break in.
"I know that you have third-floor apartment," Mr. Bows say; "and be go to say--you will please not take my remark as discourteous--that the air up three pair of stair be wholesomer for gentleman, than the air of a porter's lodge."
"Sir!" say Pen, whose candle flame up again in his wrath, and who be disposed to be as quarrelsome as man be when they be in the wrong. "will you permit I to choose my society without----?
"you be so polite as to say that you be about to honour my umble domicile with a visit," Mr. Bows say, with his sad voice. "shall I show you the way? Mr. Pendennis and I be old friend, Mrs. Bolton--very old acquaintance; and at the early dawn of his life we cross each other."
the old man point towards the door with a trembling finger, and a hat in the other hand, and in an attitude slightly theatrical; so be his word when he speak somewhat artificial, and choose from the vocabulary which he have hear all his life from the paint lip of the orator before the stage-lamp. but he be not act or masquerade, as Pen know very well, though he be disposed to pooh-pooh the old fellow's melodramatic air. although Costigan have once before be make pretty accurately to understand what Pen's pecuniary mean and expectation be, I suppose cos have forget the information acquire at Chatteris year ago, or have be induce by his natural enthusiasm to exaggerate his friend's income. he have describe Fairoaks Park in the most glowing term to Mrs. Bolton, on the precede evening, as he be walk about with she during Pen's little escapade with Fanny, have dilate upon the enormous wealth of Pen's famous uncle, the Major, and show an intimate acquaintance with Arthur's funded and land property. very likely Mrs. Bolton, in her wisdom, have speculate upon these matter during the night; and have have vision of Fanny drive in her carriage, like Mrs. Bolton's old comrade, the dancer of Sadler's Wells.

the interview between bow and his guest, when those two mount to the apartment occupy by the former in common with the descendant of the milesian king, be not particularly satisfactory to either party. Horseman's travesty seem to he a horrid and hideous degradation; Mrs. Leary's glance and ankle have not the least effect. he laugh again, and bitterly, to himself, as he think of the effect which she have produce upon he, on the first night of his arrival in London, a short time--what a long long time ago!
CHAPTER L
or near the Temple Garden
Fashion have long desert the green and pretty Temple Garden, which in Shakespeare make York and Lancaster to pluck the innocent white and red rose which become the badge of their bloody war; and the learn and pleasant writer of the Handbook of London tell we that "the commonest and hardy kind of rose have long cease to put forth a bud" in that smoky air. not many of the present occupier of the building round about the quarter know or care, very likely, whether or not rose grow there, or pass the old gate, except on their way to chamber. the attorney' clerk donot carry flower in their bag, or posy under their arm, as they run to the counsel's chamber--the few lawyer who take constitutional walk think very little about York and Lancaster, especially since the railroad business be over. only antiquarian and literary amateur care to look at the garden with much interest, and fancy good Sir Roger de Coverley and Mr. Spectator with his short face pace up and down the road; or dear Oliver Goldsmith in the summer-house, perhaps meditate about the next 'Citizen of the world,' or the new suit that Mr. Filby, the tailor, be fashion for he, or the dunning letter that Mr. Newbery have send. tread heavily on the gravel, and roll majestically along in a snuff-colour suit, and a wig that sadly want the barber's powder and iron, one see the great Doctor step up to he (his Scotch lackey follow at the lexicographer's heel, a little the bad for port wine that they have be take at the Mitre), and Mr. Johnson ask Mr. Goldsmith to come home and take a dish of tea with Miss Williams. kind faith of Fancy! Sir Roger and Mr. Spectator be as real to we now as the two doctor and the boozy and faithful Scotchman. the poetical figure live in our memory just as much as the real personages,--and as Mr. Arthur Pendennis be of a romantic and literary turn, by no mean addict to the legal pursuit common in the neighbourhood of the place, we may presume that he be cherish some such poetical reflection as these, when, upon the evening after the event record in the last chapter, the young gentleman choose the Temple Gardens as a place for exercise and meditation.
on the Sunday evening the Temple be commonly calm. the chamber be for the most part vacant: the great lawyer be give grand dinner-party at their house in the belgravian or tyburnian district; the agreeable young barrister be absent, attend those party, and pay their respect to Mr. Kewsy's excellent claret, or Mr. Justice Ermine's accomplished daughter the uninvited be partake of the economic joint and the modest half-pint of wine at the Club, entertain themselves, and the rest of the company in the Club-room, with Circuit joke and point of wit and law. nobody be in chamber at all, except poor Mr. Cockle, who be ill, and whose laundress be make he gruel; or Mr. Toodle, who be an amateur of the flute, and whom you may hear pip solitary from his chamber in the second floor; or young Tiger, the student, from whose open window come a great gush of cigar smoke, and at whose door be a quantity of dish and cover, bear the insignia of Dicks' or the Cock. but stop! Whither do Fancy lead we? it be vacation time; and with the exception of Pendennis, nobody be in Chambers at all.
perhaps it be solitude, then, which drive Pen into the garden; for although he have never before pass the gate, and have look rather carelessly at the pretty flower-bed, and the group of pleased citizen saunter over the trim lawn and the broad gravel-walk by the river, on this evening it happen, as we have say, that the young gentleman, who have din alone at a tavern in the neighbourhood of the Temple, take a fancy, as he be return home to his chamber, to take a little walk in the garden, and enjoy the fresh evening air, and the sight of the shin Thames. after walk for a brief space, and look at the many peaceful and happy group round about he, he grow tired of the exercise, and betook himself to one of the summer-house which flank either end of the main walk, and there modestly seat himself. what be his cogitation? the evening be delightfully bright and calm; the sky be cloudless; the chimney on the opposite bank be not smoke; the wharfs warehouse look rosy in the sunshine, and as clear as if they, too, have wash for the holiday. the steamer rush rapidly up and down the stream, laden with holiday passenger. the bell of the multitudinous city church be ring to evening prayer--such peaceful Sabbath evening as this Pen may have remember in his early day, as he pace, with his arm round his mother's waist, on the terrace before the lawn at home. the sun be light up the little Brawl, too, as well as the broad thame, and sink downward majestically behind the Clavering elm, and the tower of the familiar village church. be it thought of these, or the sunset merely, that cause the blush in the young man's face? he beat time on the bench, to the chorus of the bell without; flick the dust off his shin boot with his pocket-handkerchief, and start up, stamp with his foot and say, "no, by Jove, Iwill go home." and with this resolution, which indicate that some struggle as to the propriety of remain where he be, or of quit the garden, have be go on in his mind, he step out of the summer-house.
he nearly knock down two little child, who do not indeed reach much high than his knee, and be trot along the gravel-walk, with their long blue shadow slant towards the east.
one cry out "oh!" the other begin to laugh; and with a knowing little infantile chuckle, say, "Missa Pendennis!" and Arthur, look down, see his two little friend of the day before, Mesdemoiselles Ameliar-Ann and Betsy-Jane. he blush more than ever at see they, and seize the one whom he have nearly upset, jump she up into the air, and kiss she: at which sudden assault Ameliar-Ann begin to cry in great alarm.
this cry bring up instantly two lady in clean collar and new ribbon, and grand shawl, namely: Mrs. Bolton in a rich scarlet Caledonian Cashmere, and a black silk dress, and Miss F. Bolton with a yellow scarf and a sweet sprig muslin, and a parasol--quite the lady. Fanny do not say one single word: though; her eye flash a welcome, and shine as bright--as bright as the most blazing window in Paper Buildings. but Mrs. Bolton, after admonish Betsy-Jane, say, "Lor sir --how very odd that we should meet you year! I ope you ave your ealth well, sir.--ain't it odd, Fanny, that we should meet Mr. Pendennis?" what do you mean by sniggering, Mesdames? when young Croesus have be stay at a country-house, have you never, by any singular coincidence, be walk with your fanny in the shrubbery? have you and your Fanny never happen to be listen to the band of the Heavies at Brighton, when young De Boots and Captain Padmore come clink down the pier? have you and your darling Frances never chance to be visit old widow Wheezy at the cottage on the common, when the young curate have step in with a tract adapt to the rheumatism? do you suppose that, if singular coincidence occur at the Hall, they donot also happen at the Lodge?
it be a coincidence, no doubt: that be all. in the course of the conversation on the day previous, Mr. Pendennis have merely say, in the simple way imaginable, and in reply to a question of Miss Bolton, that although some of the court be gloomy, part of the Temple be very cheerful and agreeable, especially the chamber look on the river and around the garden, and that the garden be a very pleasant walk on Sunday evening and frequent by a great number of people--and here, by the mere chance, all our acquaintance meet together, just like so many people in genteel life. what could be more artless, good-nature, or natural?
Pen look very grave, pompous, and dandify. he be unusually smart and brilliant in his costume. his white duck trouser and white hat, his neckcloth of many colour, his light waistcoat, gold chain, and shirt-stud, give he the air of a prince of the blood at least. how his splendour become his figure! be anybody ever like he? some one thought. he blush--how his blush become he! the same individual say to herself. the child, on see he the day before, have be so strike with he, that after he have go away they have be play at he. and Ameliar-Ann, stick her little chubby finger into the arm-hole of her pinafore, as Pen be willnot to do with his waistcoat, have say, "now, Bessy-Jane, Iwill be Missa Pendennis." Fanny have laugh till she cry, and smother her sister with kiss for that feat. how happy, too, she be to see Arthur embrace the child!
if Arthur be red, Fanny, on the contrary, be very worn and pale. Arthur remark it, and ask kindly why she look so fatigued.
"I be awake all night," say Fanny, and begin to blush a little.
"I put out her candle, and hordere she to go to sleep and leave off readin," interpose the fond mother.
"you be read! and what be it that interested you so?" ask Pen, amuse.
"oh, itbe so beautiful!" say Fanny.
"what?"
"'Walter Lorraine,'" Fanny sigh out. "how I do hate that Neaera-- Neaera--I donot know the pronunciation. and I love Leonora, and Walter, oh, how dear he be!"
how have Fanny discover the novel of 'Walter Lorraine,' and that Pen be the author? this little person remember every single word which Mr. Pendennis have speak on the night previous, and how he write in book and newspaper. what book? she be so eager to know, that she have almost a mind to be civil to old Bows, who be suffer under her displeasure since yesterday, but she determine first to make application to Costigan. she begin by coax the Captain and smile upon he in her most win way, as she help to arrange his dinner and set his humble apartment in order. she be sure his linen want mending (and indeed the Captain's linen-closet contain some curious specimen of manufacture flax and cotton). she would mend his shirt--all his shirt. what horrid hole--what funny hole! she put she little face through one of they, and laugh at the old warrior in the most win manner. she would have make a funny little picture look through the hole. then she daintily remove Costigan's dinner thing, trip about the room as she have see the dancer do at the play; and she dance to the Captain's cupboard, and produce his whisky-bottle, and mix he a tumbler, and must taste a drop of it--a little drop; and the Captain must sing her one of his song, his dear song, and teach it to she. and when he have sing an irish melody in his rich quavering voice, fancy it be he who be fascinate the little siren, she put she little question about Arthur Pendennis and his novel, and have get an answer, care for nothing more, but leave the Captain at the piano about to sing she another song, and the dinner-tray on the passage, and the shirt on the chair, and run downstairs quicken her pace as she speed.
Captain Costigan, as he say, be not a litherary cyarkter, nor have he as yet find time to peruse his young friend's ellygant perfaurumance, though he intend to teak an early opporchunitee of purchase a cawpee of his work. but he know the name of Pen's novel from the fact that Messrs. Finucane, Bludyer, and other frequenter of the Back Kitchen, speak of Mr. Pendennis (and not all of they with great friendship; for Bludyer call he a confounded coxcomb, and Hoolan wonder that Doolan do not kick he etc.) by the sobriquet of Walter Lorraine,--and be hence enable to give Fanny the information which she require.
"and she go and ast for it at the libery," Mrs. Bolton said,--several liberie--and some ad it and it be bout, and some adn't it. and one of the liberie as ad it wouldnot let er ave it without a sovering: and she adn't one, and she come back a-cryin to I--didn't you, fanny?--and I give she a sovering."
"and, oh, I be in such a fright lest any one should have come to the libery and take it while I be away," Fanny say, her cheek and eye glow. "and, oh, I do like it so!"
Arthur be touch by this artless sympathy, immensely flatter and move by it. "do you like it?" he say. "if you will come up to my chamber I will--no, I will bring you one--no, I will send you one. good night. thank you, Fanny. God bless you. I mustnot stay with you. good-bye, good-bye." and, press her hand once, and nod to her mother and the other child, he stride out of the garden.
he quicken his pace as he go from they, and run out of the gate talk to himself. "dear, dear little thing," he said,--"darle little Fanny! you be worth they all. I wish to heaven Shandon be back. Iwould go home to my mother. I mustnot see she. I willnot. I willnot, so help me----"
as he be talk thus, and run, the passer-by turn to look at he, he run against a little old man, and perceive it be Mr. Bows.
"your very umble servant, sir," say Mr. Bows, make a sarcastic bow, and lift his old hat from his forehead.
"I wish you a good day," Arthur answer sulkily. "donot let I detain you, or give you the trouble to follow I again. I be in a hurry, sir. good evening."
bow think Pen have some reason for hurry to his room. "where be they?" exclaim the old gentleman. "you know whom I mean. theybe not in your room, sir, be they? they tell Bolton they be go to church at the Temple, they benot there. they be in your chamber: they mustnot stay in your chamber, Mr. Pendennis."
"damn it, sir!" cry out Pendennis, fiercely. "come and see if they be in my chamber: herebe the court and the door--come in and see." and bow, take off his hat and bow first, follow the young man.
they be not in Pen's chamber, as we know. but when the garden be close, the two woman, who have r have but a melancholy evening's amusement, walk away sadly with the child, and they enter into Lamb Court, and stand under the lamp-post which cheerfully ornament the centre of that quadrangle, and look up to the third floor of the house where Pendennis's chamber be, and where they see a light presently kindle. then this couple of fool go away, the child drag wearily after they, and return to Mr. Bolton, who be immerse in rum-and-water at his lodge in Shepherd's Inn.
Mr. Bows look round the blank room which the young man occupy, and which have receive but very few ornament or addition since the last time we see they. Warrington's old bookcase and battered library, Pen's writing-table with its litter of paper, present an aspect cheerless enough. "will you like to look in the bedroom, Mr. Bows, and see if my victim be there?" he say bitterly; "or whether I have make away with the little girl, and hide they in the coal-hole?"
"your word be sufficient, Mr. Pendennis," the other say in his sad tone. "you say they be not here, and I know they be not. and I hope they never have be here, and never will come."
"upon my word, sir, you be very good, to choose my acquaintance for I," Arthur say, in a haughty tone; "and to suppose that anybody would be the bad for my society. I remember you, and owe you kindness from old time, Mr. Bows; or I should speak more angrily than I do, about a very intolerable sort of persecution to which you seem inclined to subject I. you follow I out of your Inn yesterday, as if you want to watch that I shouldnot steal something." here Pen stammer and turn red, directly he have say the word; he feel he have give the other an opening, which bow instantly take.
"I do think you come to steal something, as you say the word, sir," bow say. "do you mean to say that you come to pay a visit to poor old bow, the fiddler; or to Mrs. Bolton, at the porter's lodge? o fie! such a fine gentleman as Arthur Pendennis, Esquire, donot condescend to walk up to my garret, or to sit in a laundress's kitchen, but for reason of his own. and my belief be that you come to steal a pretty girl's heart away, and to ruin it, and to spurn it afterwards, Mr. Arthur Pendennis. thatbe what the world make of you young dandy, you gentleman of fashion, you high and mighty aristocrat that trample upon the people. itbe sport to you, but what be it to the poor, think you; the toy of your pleasure, whom you play with and whom you fle into the street when you be tired? I know your order, sir. I know your selfishness, and your arrogance, and your pride. what do it matter to my lord, that the poor man's daughter be make miserable, and her family bring to shame? you must have your pleasure, and the people of course must pay for they. what be we make for, but for that? itbe the way with you all--the way with you all, sir."
bow be speak beside the question, and Pen have his advantage here, which he be not sorry to take--not sorry to put off the debate from the point upon which his adversary have first engage it. Arthur break out with a sort of laugh, for which he ask Bows's pardon. "yes, I be an aristocrat," he say, "in a palace up three pair of stair, with a carpet nearly as handsome as your, Mr. Bows. my life be pass in grind the people, be it?--in ruin virgin and rob the poor? my good sir, this be very well in a comedy, where Job Thornberry slap his breast, and ask my Lord how dare he trample on an honest man and poke out an Englishman's fireside; but in real life, Mr. Bows, to a man who have to work for his bread as much as you do--how can you talk about aristocrat tyrannise over the people? have I ever do you a wrong? or assume air of superiority over you? do you not have an early regard for I--in day when we be both of we romantic young fellow, Mr. Bows? come, donot be angry with I now, and let we be as good friend as we be before."
"those day be very different," Mr. Bows answer; "and Mr. Arthur Pendennis be an honest, impetuous young fellow then; rather selfish and conceited, perhaps, but honest. he like you then, because you be ready to ruin yourself for a woman."
"and now, sir?" Arthur ask.
"and now time be change, and you want a woman to ruin herself for you," bow answer. "I know this child, sir. Ihave always say this lot be hang over she. she have heat her little brain with novel, until her whole thought be about love and lover, and she scarcely see that she tread on a kitchen floor. I have teach the little thing. she be full of many talent and win way, I grant you. I be fond of the girl, sir. Ibe a lonely old man; I lead a life that I donot like, among boon companion, who make I melancholy. I have but this child that I care for. have pity upon I, and donot take she away from I, Mr. Pendennis--don't take she away."
the old man's voice break as he speak. its accent touch Pen, much more than the menacing or sarcastic tone which Bows have commence by adopt.
"indeed," say he, kindly, "you do I a wrong if you fancy I intend one to poor little Fanny. I never see she till Friday night. it be the mere chance that our friend Costigan throw she into my way. I have no intention regard she--that is----"
"that be, you know very well that she be a foolish girl, and her mother a foolish woman,--that be, you meet she in the Temple Gardens, and of course without previous concert,--that be, that when I find she yesterday read the book youhave write, she scorn I," bow say. "what be I good for but to be laugh at? a deformed old fellow like I; an old fiddler, that wear a threadbare coat, and get his bread by play tune at an ale-house? you be a fine gentleman, you be. you wear scent in your handkerchief, and a ring on your finger. you go to dine with great people. who ever give a crust to old bow? and yet I might have be as good a man as the good of you. I might have be a man of genius, if I have have the chance; ay, and have live with the master-spirit of the land. but everything had ail with I. Iwould ambition once, and write play, poem, music--nobody would give I a hearing. I never love a woman, but she laugh at I; and here I be in my old age alone--alone! donot take this girl from I, Mr. Pendennis, I say again. leave she with I a little long. she be like a child to I till yesterday. why do you step in, and make she to mock my deformity and old age?"
"I be guiltless of that, at least," Arthur say, with something of a sigh. "upon my word of honour, I wish I have never see the girl. my calling be not seduction, Mr. Bows. I do not imagine that I have make an impression on poor Fanny, until--until to-night. and then, sir, I be sorry, and be fly from my temptation, as you come upon I. and," he add, with a glow upon his cheek, which, in the gathering darkness, his companion could not see, and with an audible tremor in his voice, "I do not mind tell you, sir, that on this Sabbath evening, as the church bell be ring, I think of my own home, and of woman angelically pure and good, who dwell there; and I be run hither as I meet you, that I might avoid the danger which beset I, and ask strength of God Almighty to do my duty."
after these word from Arthur a silence ensue, and when the conversation be resume by his guest, the latter speak in a tone which be much more gentle and friendly. and on take farewell of Pen, Bows ask leave to shake hand with he, and with a very warm and affectionate greeting on both side, apologise to Arthur for have mistaken he, and pay he some compliment which cause the young man to squeeze his old friend's hand heartily again. and as they part at Pen's door, Arthur say he have give a promise, and he hop and trust that Mr. Bows might rely on it?
"amen to that prayer," say Mr. Bows, and go slowly down the stair.
CHAPTER LI
the happy village again
early in this history, we have have occasion to speak of the little town of Clavering, near which Pen's paternal home of Fairoaks stand, and of some of the people who inhabite the place; and as the society there be by no mean amusing or pleasant, our report concern it be not carry to any very great length. Mr. Samuel Huxter, the gentleman whose acquaintance we lately make at Vauxhall, be one of the choice spirit of the little town, when he visit it during his vacation, and enliven the table of his friend there, by the wit of Bartholomew's and the gossip of the fashionable London circle which he frequent.
Mr. Hobnell, the young gentleman whom Pen have thrash in consequence of the quarrel in the Fotheringay affair, be, whilst a pupil at the Grammar School at Clavering, make very welcome at the tea-table of Mrs. Huxter, Samuel's mother, and be free of the surgery, where he know the way to the tamarind-pot, and could scent his pocket-handkerchief with rose-water. and it be at this period of his life that he form an attachment for Miss Sophy Huxter, whom, on his father's demise, he marry, and take home to his house of the Warren, at a few mile from Clavering.
the family have possess and cultivate an estate there for many year, as yeoman and farmer. Mr. Hobnell's father pull down the old farmhouse; build a flaring new whitewashed mansion, with capacious stable; and a piano in the drawing-room; keep a pack of harrier; and assume the title of Squire Hobnell. when he die, and his son reign in his stead, the family might be fairly consider to be establish as county gentry. and Sam Huxter, at London, do no great wrong in boast about his brother-in-law's place, his hound, horse, and hospitality, to his admire comrade at Bartholomew's. every year, at a time commonly when Mrs. Hobnell could not leave the increase duty of her nursery, Hobnell come up to London for a lark, have room at the Tavistock, and he and Sam indulge in the pleasure of the town together. Ascot, the theatre, Vauxhall, and the convivial tavern in the joyous neighbourhood of Covent Garden, be visit by the vivacious squire, in company with his learn brother. when he be in London, as he say, he like to do as London do, and to "go it a bit," and when he return to the west, he take a new bonnet and shawl to Mrs. Hobnell, and relinquish, for country sport and occupation during the next eleven month, the elegant amusement of London life.
Sam Huxter keep up a correspondence with his relative, and supply he with choice news of the metropolis, in return for the basket of hare, partridge, and clout cream which the squire and his good-natured wife forward to Sam. a youth more brilliant and distinguish they do not know. he be the life and soul of their house, when he make his appearance in his native place. his song, joke, and fun keep the Warren in a roar. he have save their eld darling's life, by take a fish-bone out of her throat: in fine, he be the delight of their circle.
as ill-luck would have it, Pen again fall in with Mr. Huxter, only three day after the rencontre at Vauxhall. faithful to his vow, he have not be to see little Fanny. he be try to drive she from his mind by occupation, or other mental excitement. he labour, though not to much profit, incessantly in his room; and, in his capacity of critic for the Pall Mall Gazette, make woful and savage onslaught on a poem and a romance which come before he for judgment. these author slain, he go to dine alone at the lonely club of the Polyanthus, where the vast solitude frighten he, and make he only the more moody. he have be to more theatre for relaxation. the whole house be roar with laughter and applause, and he see only an ignoble farce that make he sad. it would have damp the spirit of the buffoon on the stage to have see Pen's dismal face. he hardly know what be happen; the scene and the drama pass before he like a dream or a fever. then he think he would go to the Back Kitchen, his old haunt with Warrington--he be not a bit sleepy yet. the day before he have walk twenty mile in search after rest, over Hampstead Common and Hendon lane, and have get no sleep at night. he would go to the Back Kitchen. it be a sort of comfort to he to think he should see bow. bow be there, very calm, preside at the old piano. some tremendous comic song be sing, which make the room crack with laughter. how strange they seem to Pen! he could only see bow. in an extinct volcano, such as he boast that his breast be, it be wonderful how he should feel such a flame! two day' indulgence have kindle it; two day' abstinence have set it burn in fury. so, muse upon this, and drink down one glass after another, as ill luck would have it, Arthur's eye light upon Mr. Huxter, who have be to the theatre, like himself, and, with two or three comrade, now enter the room. Huxter whisper to his companion, greatly to Pen's annoyance. Arthur feel that the other be talk about he. Huxter then work through the room, follow by his friend, and come and take a place opposite Pen, nod familiarly to he, and hold he out a dirty hand to shake.
Pen shake hand with his fellow-townsman. he think he have be needlessly savage to he on the last night when they have meet. as for Huxter, perfectly at good-humour with himself, and the world, it never enter his mind that he could be disagreeable to anybody; and the little dispute, or "chaff," as he style it, of Vauxhall, be a trifle which he do not in the least regard.
the disciple of Galen have call for "four stout," with which he and his party refresh themselves, begin to think what would be the most amusing topic of conversation with Pen, and hit upon that precise one which be most painful to our young gentleman.
"jolly night at Vauxhall--wasn't it?" he say, and wink in a very knowing way.
"Ibe glad you like it," poor Pen say, groan in spirit.
"I be dev'lish cut--uncommon--be din with some chap at Greenwich. that be a pretty bit of muslin hang on your arm--who be she?" ask the fascinating student.
the question be too much for Arthur. "have I ask you any question about yourself, Mr. Huxter?" he say.
"I donot mean any offence--beg pardon--hang it, you cut up quite savage," say Pen's astonished interlocutor.
"do you remember what take place between we the other night?" Pen ask, with gather wrath. "you forget? very probably. you be tipsy, as you observe just now, and very rude."
"hang it, sir, I ask your pardon," Huxter say, look red.
"you do certainly, and it be grant with all my heart. I be sure. but if you recollect, I beg that you would have the goodness to omit I from the list of your acquaintance for the future; and when we meet in public, that you would not take the trouble to recognise I. will you please to remember this, hereafter? and as the song be begin, permit I to leave you to the unrestrained enjoyment of the music."
he take his hat, and make a bow to the amazed Mr. Huxter leave the table, as Huxter's comrade, after a pause of wonder, set up such a roar of laughter at Huxter, as call for the intervention of the president of the room; who bawl out, "Silence, gentleman; do have silence for the Body Snatcher!" which popular song begin as Pen leave the Back Kitchen. he flatter himself that he have command his temper perfectly. he rather wish that Huxter have be pugnacious. he would have like to fight he or somebody. he go home. the day's work, the dinner, the play, the whisky-and-water, the quarrel,--nothing soothe he. he sleep no well than on the previous night.
a few day afterwards, Mr. Sam Huxter write home a letter to Mr. Hobnell in the country, of which Mr. Arthur Pendennis form the principal subject. Sam describe Arthur's pursuit in London, and his confounded insolence of behaviour to his old friend from home. he say he be an abandon criminal, a regular Don Juan, a fellow who, when he do come into the country, ought to be keep out of honest people's house. he have see he at Vauxhall, dance with an innocent girl in the low rank of life, of whom he be make a victim. he have find out from an irish gentleman (formerly in the army), who frequent a club of which he, Huxter, be member, who the girl be, on whom this conceited humbug be practise his infernal art; and he think he should warn her father, etc. etc.,--the letter then touch on general news, convey the writer's thank for the last parcel and the rabbit, and hint his extreme readiness for further favour.
about once a year, as we have state, there be occasion for a christening at the Warren, and it happen that this ceremony take place a day after Hobnell have receive the letter of his brother-in-law in town. the infant (a darling little girl) be christen Myra Lucretia, after its two godmother, Miss Portman and Mrs. Pybus of Clavering, and as of course Hobnell have communicate Sam's letter to his wife, Mrs. Hobnell impart its horrid content to her two gossip. a pretty story it be, and prettily it be tell throughout Clavering in the course of that day.
Myra do not--she be too much shocked to do so--speak on the matter to her mamma, but Mrs. Pybus have no such feeling of reserve. she talk over the matter not only with Mrs. Portman, but with Mr. and the Honourable Mrs. Simcoe, with Mrs. Glanders, her daughter be to that end order out of the room, with Madame Fribsby, and, in a word, with the whole of the Clavering society. Madame Fribsby look furtively up at her picture of the dragoon, and inward into her own wound memory, say that man would be man, and as long as they be man would be deceiver; and she pensively quote some line from Marmion, request to know where deceive lover should rest? Mrs. Pybus have no word of hatred, horror, contempt, strong enough for a villain who could be capable of conduct so base. this be what come of early indulgence, and insolence, and extravagance, and aristocratic air (it be certain that Pen have refuse to drink tea with Mrs. Pybus), and attend the corrupt and horrid party in the dreadful modern Babylon! Mrs. Portman be afraid that she must acknowledge that the mother's fatal partiality have spoil this boy, that his literary success have turn his head, and his horrid passion have make he forget the principle which Doctor Portman have instill into he in early life. glander, the atrocious Captain of Dragoons, when inform of the occurrence by Mrs. Glanders, whistle and make jocular allusion to it at dinner-time; on which Mrs. Glanders call he a brute, and order the girl again out of the room, as the horrid Captain burst out laugh. Mr. Simcoe be calm under the intelligence; but rather pleased than otherwise; it only serve to confirm the opinion which he have always have of that wretched young man: not that he know anything about he--not that he have read one line of his dangerous and poisonous work; Heaven forbid that he should: but what could be expect from such a youth, and such frightful, such lamentable, such deplorable want of seriousness? Pen form the subject for a second sermon at the Clavering chapel-of-ease: where the danger of London, and the crime of read or writing novel, be point out on a Sunday evening to a large and warm congregation. they do not wait to hear whether he be guilty or not. they take his wickedness for grant: and with these admirable moralist, it be who should fling the stone at poor Pen.
the next day Mrs. Pendennis, alone and almost faint with emotion and fatigue, walk or rather run to Dr. Portman's house to consult the good Doctor. she have have an anonymous letter;--some Christian have think it his or her duty to stab the good soul who have never do mortal a wrong-- an anonymous letter with reference to scripture, point out the doom of such sinner and a detailed account of Pen's crime. she be in a state of terror and excitement pitiable to witness. two or three hour of this pain have age she already. in her first moment of agitation she have drop the letter, and Laura have read it. Laura blush when she read it; her whole frame tremble, but it be with anger. "the coward," she said.--it benot true.--no, mother, it benot true."
"it be true, and youhave do it, Laura," cry out Helen fiercely. "why do you refuse he when he ask you? why do you break my heart and refuse he? it be you who lead he into crime. it be you who fling he into the arm of this--this woman.--don't speak to me.--don't answer I. I will never forgive you, never. Martha, bring I my bonnet and shawl. Iwill go out. I willnot have you come with I. go away. leave I, cruel girl; why have you bring this shame on I?" and bid her daughter and her servant keep away from she, she run down the road to Clavering.
Doctor Portman, glance over the letter, think he know the handwriting, and, of course, be already acquaint with the charge make against poor Pen. against his own conscience, perhaps (for the worthy Doctor, like most of we, have a considerable natural aptitude for receive any report unfavourable to his neighbour), he strive to console Helen; he point out that the slander come from an anonymous quarter, and therefore must be the work of a rascal; that the charge might not be true--be not true, most likely--at least, that Pen must be hear before he be condemn; that the son of such a mother be not likely to commit such a crime, etc. etc.
Helen at once see through his feint of objection and denial. "you think he have do it," she said,--"you know you think he have do it. oh, why do I ever leave he, Doctor Portman, or suffer he away from I? but he cannot be dishonest--pray God, not dishonest--you donot think that, do you? remember his conduct about that other--person--how madly he be attach to she. he be an honest boy then--he be now. and I thank God-- yes, I fall down on my knee and thank God he pay Laura. you say he be good--you do yourself. and now--if this woman love he--and you know they must--if he have take she from her home, or she tempt he, which be most likely--why still, she must be his wife and my daughter. and he must leave the dreadful world and come back to I--to his mother, Doctor Portman. let we go away and bring he back--yes--bring he back--and there shall be joy for the--the sinner that repenteth. let we go now, directly, dear friend--this very----"
Helen could say no more. she fall back and faint. she be carry to a bed in the house of the pitying Doctor, and the surgeon be call to attend she. she lie all night in an alarming state. Laura come to she, or to the rectory rather; for she would not see Laura. and Doctor Portman, still beseech she to be tranquil, and grow bold and more confident of Arthur's innocence as he witness the terrible grief of the poor mother, write a letter to Pen warn he of the rumour that be against he and earnestly pray that he would break off and repent of a connexion so fatal to his good interest and his soul's welfare.
and laura?--wa her heart not wring by the thought of Arthur's crime and Helen's estrangement? be it not a bitter blow for the innocent girl to think that at one stroke she should lose all the love which she care for in the world?
CHAPTER LII
which have very nearly be the last of the Story
Doctor Portman's letter be send off to its destination in London, and the worthy clergyman endeavour to soothe down Mrs. Pendennis into some state of composure until an answer should arrive, which the Doctor try to think, or at any rate persist in say, would be satisfactory as regard the morality of Mr. Pen. at least Helen's wisdom of move upon London and appear in person to warn her son of his wickedness, be impracticable for a day or two. the apothecary forbid she move even so far as fairoak for the first day, and it be not until the subsequent morning that she find herself again back on her sofa at home, with the faithful, though silent, Laura nursing at her side.
unluckily for himself and all party, Pen never read that homily which Doctor Portman address to he, until many week after the epistle have be compose; and day after day the widow wait for her son's reply to the charge against he; her own illness increase with every day's delay. it be a hard task for Laura to bear the anxiety; to witness her dear friend's suffering; bad of all, to support Helen's estrangement, and the pain cause to she by that avert affection. but it be the custom of this young lady to the utmost of her power, and by mean of that gracious assistance which Heaven award to her pure and constant prayer, to do her duty. and; as that duty be perform quite noiselessly,--while the supplication, which endow she with the requisite strength for fulfil it, also take place in her own chamber, away from all mortal sight,--we, too, must be perforce silent about these virtue of her, which no more bear public talk about, than a flower will bear to bloom in a ballroom. this only we will say--that a good woman be the lovely flower that bloom under heaven; and that we look with love and wonder upon its silent grace, its pure fragrance, its delicate bloom of beauty. sweet and beautiful!--the fairest and the most spotless!--is it not pity to see they bow down or devour by Grief or death inexorable--waste in disease--pin with long pain--or cut off by sudden fate in their prime? we may deserve grief--but why should these be unhappy?--except that we know that Heaven chasten those whom it love well; be please, by repeat trial, to make these pure spirit more pure.
so Pen never get the letter, although it be duly post and faithfully discharge by the postman into his letter-box in Lamb Court, and thence carry by the laundress to his writing-table with the rest of his lordship's correspondence; into which room, have we not see a picture of he, enter from his little bedroom adjoining, as Mrs. Flanagan, his laundress, be in the act of drink his gin?
those kind reader who have watch Mr. Arthur's career hitherto, and have make, as they naturally would do, observation upon the moral character and peculiarity of their acquaintance, have probably discover by this time what be the prevail fault in Mr. Pen's disposition, and who be that great enemy, artfully indicate in the title-page, with whom he have to contend. not a few of we, my beloved public, have the very same rascal to contend with: a scoundrel who take every opportunity of bring we into mischief, of plunge we into quarrel, of lead we into idleness and unprofitable company, and what not. in a word, Pen's great enemy be himself: and as he have be pamper, and coax, and indulge that individual all his life, the rogue grow insolent, as all spoil servant will be; and at the slight attempt to coerce he, or make he do that which be unpleasant to he, become frantically rude and unruly. a person who be use to make sacrifice--Laura, for instance, who have get such a habit of give up her own pleasure for other--can do the business quite easily; but Pen, unaccustomed as he be to any sort of self-denial, suffer woundily when call on to pay his share, and savagely grumble at be oblige to forgo anything he like.
he have resolve in his mighty mind then that he would not see Fanny; and he wouldnot. he try to drive the thought of that fascinating little person out of his head, by constant occupation, by exercise, by dissipation and society. he work then too much; he walk and ride too much; he eat, drank, and smoke too much: nor could all the cigar and the punch of which he partook drive little Fanny's image out of his inflame brain, and at the end of a week of this discipline and self-denial our young gentleman be in bed with a fever. let the reader who have never have a fever in chamber pity the wretch who be bind to undergo that calamity.
a committee of marriageable lady, or of any christian person interested in the propagation of the domestic virtue, should employ a Cruikshank or a Leech, or some other kindly expositor of the folly of the day, to make a series of design represent the horror of a bachelor's life in chamber, and lead the beholder to think of well thing, and a more wholesome condition. what can be more uncomfortable than the bachelor's lonely breakfast?--with the black kettle in the dreary fire in midsummer; or, bad still, with the fire go out at Christmas, half an hour after the laundress have quit the sitting-room? into this solitude the owner enter shiver, and have to commence his day by hunt for coal and wood; and before he begin the work of a student, have to discharge the duty of a housemaid, vice Mrs. Flanagan, who be absent without leave. or, again, what can form a fine subject for the classical designer than the bachelor's shirt--that garment which he want to assume just at dinner-time, and which he find without any button to fasten it? then there be the bachelor's return to chamber, after a merry Christmas holiday, spend in a cosy country-house, full of pretty face, and kind welcome and regret. he leave his portmanteau at the barber's in the court: he light his dismal old candle at the sputtering little lamp on the stair: he enter the blank familiar room, where the only token to greet he, that show any interest in his personal welfare, be the Christmas bill, which be lie in wait for he, amiably spread out on his reading-table. add to these scene an appalling picture of bachelor's illness, and the rent in the Temple will begin to fall from the day of the publication of the dismal diorama. to be well in chamber be melancholy, and lonely and selfish enough; but to be ill in chamber--to pass long night of pain and watchfulness--to long for the morning and the laundress--to serve yourself your own medicine by your own watch--to have no other companion for long hour but your own sickening fancy and fevere thought: no kind hand to give you drink if you be thirsty, or to smooth the hot pillow that crumple under you,-- this, indeed, be a fate so dismal and tragic, that we shall not enlarge upon its horror, and shall only heartily pity those bachelor in the Temple, who brave it every day.
this lot befell Arthur Pendennis after the various excess which we have mention, and to which he have subject his unfortunate brain. one night he go to bed ill, and the next day awake worse. his only visitor that day, besides the laundress, be the Printer's Devil, from the Pall Mall Gazette office, whom the writer endeavour, as good he could, to satisfy. his exertion to complete his work render his fever the great: he could only furnish a part of the quantity of "copy" usually supply by he; and Shandon be absent, and Warrington not in London to give a help, the political and editorial column of the Gazette look very blank indeed; nor do the sub-editor know how to fill they.
Mr. Finucane rush up to Pen's chamber, and find that gentleman so exceedingly unwell, that the good-natured Irishman set to work to supply his place, if possible, and produce a series of political and critical composition, such as no doubt greatly edify the reader of the periodical in which he and Pen be concern. allusion to the greatness of Ireland, and the genius and virtue of the inhabitant of that injure country, flow magnificently from Finucane's pen; and Shandon, the Chief of the paper, who be enjoy himself placidly at Boulogne-sur-Mer, look over the column of the journal, which be forward to he, instantly recognise the hand of the great sub-editor, and say, laugh, as he fling over the paper to his wife, "look here, Mary, my dear, here be Jack at work again." indeed, Jack be a warm friend, and a gallant partisan, and when he have the pen in hand, seldom let slip an opportunity of let the world know that Rafferty be the great painter in Europe, and wonder at the petty jealousy of the Academy, which refuse to make he an R.A.: of state that it be generally report at the West End, that Mr. Rooney, M.P., be appoint Governor of Barataria; or of introduce into the subject in hand, whatever it might be, a compliment to the Round Towers, or the Giant's Causeway. and besides do Pen's work for he, to the good of his ability, his kind-hearted comrade offer to forgo his Saturday's and Sunday's holiday, and pass those day of holiday and rest as nurse-tender to Arthur, who, however, insist, that the other should not forgo his pleasure, and thankfully assure he that he could bear good his malady alone.
take his supper at the Back Kitchen on the Friday night, after have achieve the work of the paper, Finucane inform Captain Costigan of the illness of their young friend in the Temple; and remember the fact two day afterwards, the Captain go to Lamb Court and pay a visit to the invalid on Sunday afternoon.
he find Mrs. Flanagan, the laundress, in tear in the sitting-room, and get a bad report of the poor dear young gentleman within. Pen's condition have so much alarmed she, that she be oblige to have recourse to the stimulus of brandy to enable she to support the grief which his illness occasion. as she hang about his bed, and endeavour to minister to he, her attention become intolerable to the invalid, and he beg she peevishly not to come near he. hence the laundress's tear and redouble grief, and renew application to the bottle, which she be accustomed to use as an anodyne. the Captain rat the woman soundly for her intemperance, and point out to she the fatal consequence which must ensue if she persist in her imprudent course.
Pen, who be by this time in a very fevered state, yet be greatly pleased to receive Costigan's visit. he hear the ell-know voice in his sitting-room, as he lie in the bedroom within, and call the Captain eagerly to he, and thank he for come, and beg he to take a chair and talk to he. the Captain feel the young man's pulse with great gravity--(his own tremulous and clammy hand grow steady for the instant while his finger press Arthur's throb vein)--the pulse be beat very fiercely--Pen's face be haggard and hot--his eye be bloodshot and gloomy; his "bird," as the Captain pronounce the word, afterwards give a description of his condition, have not be shave for nearly a week. Pen make his visitor sit down, and, toss and turn in his comfortless bed, begin to try and talk to the Captain in a lively manner, about the Back Kitchen, about Vauxhall and when they should go again, and about Fanny--how be little Fanny?
indeed how be she? we know how she go home very sadly on the previous Sunday evening, after she have see Arthur light his lamp in his chamber, whilst he be have his interview with bow. bow come back to his own room presently, pass by the lodge door, and look into Mrs. Bolton's, accord to his willnot, as he pass, but with a very melancholy face. she have another weary night that night. her restlessness waken her little bedfellow more than once. she darenot read more of 'Walter Lorraine:' Father be at home, and would suffer no light. she keep the book under her pillow, and feel for it in the night. she have only just get to sleep, when the child begin to stir with the morning, almost as early as the bird. though she be very angry with bow, she go to his room at her accustomed hour in the day, and there the good-hearted musician begin to talk to she.
"I see Mr. Pendennis last night, Fanny," he say.
"do you? I think you do," Fanny answer, look fiercely at the melancholy old gentleman.
"Ihave be fond of you ever since we come to live in this place," he continue. "you be a child when I come; and you use to like I, Fanny, until three or four day ago: until you see this gentleman."
"and now, I suppose, you be go to say ill of he," say Fanny. "do, Mr. Bows--that will make I like you well."
"indeed I shall do no such thing," bow answer; "I think he be a very good and honest young man."
"indeed! you know that if you say a word against he, I would never speak a word to you again--never!" cry Miss Fanny; and clench her little hand, and pace up and down the room. bow note, watch, and follow the ardent little creature with admiration and gloomy sympathy. her cheek flush, her frame tremble; her eye beam love, anger, defiance. "you would like to speak ill of he," she say; "but you daren't--you know you darenot!"
"I know he many year since," bow continue, "when he be almost as young as you be, and he have a romantic attachment for our friend the Captain's daughter--Lady Mirabel that be now."
Fanny laugh. "I suppose there be other people, too, that have romantic attachment for Miss Costigan," she say: "I donot want to hear about them."
"he want to marry she; but their age be quite disproportionate: and their rank in life. she would not have he because he have no money. she act very wisely in refuse he; for the two would have be very unhappy, and she benot a fit person to go and live with his family, or to make his home comfortable. Mr. Pendennis have his way to make in the world, and must marry a lady of his own rank. a woman who love a man will not ruin his prospect, cause he to quarrel with his family, and lead he into poverty and misery for her gratification. an honest girl willnot do that, for her own sake, or for the man's."
Fanny's emotion, which but now have be that of defiance and anger, here turn to dismay and supplication. "what do I know about marry, Bows?" she say. "when be there any talk of it? what have there be between this young gentleman and I thatbe to make people speak so cruel? it be not my doing; nor Arthur's--Mr. Pendennis's--that I meet he at Vauxhall. it be the Captain take I and Ma there. we never think of nothing wrong, Ibe sure. he come and rescue we, and he be so very kind. then he come to call and ask after we: and very, very good it be of a such grand gentleman to be so polite to humble folk like we! and yesterday Ma and I just go to walk in the Temple Gardens, and--and"--here she break out with that usual, unanswerable female argument of tear--and cry, "oh! I wish I be dead! I wish I be lay in my grave; and have never, never see he!"
"he say as much himself, Fanny," bow say; and Fanny ask through her sob, why, why should he wish he have never see she? have she ever do he any harm? oh, she would perish rather than do he any harm. whereupon the musician inform she of the conversation of the day previous, show she that Pen could not and must not think of she as a wife fit for he, and that she, as she value her honest reputation, must strive too to forget he. and Fanny, leave the musician, convince, but still of the same mind, and promise that she would avoid the danger which menace she, go back to the porter's lodge, and tell her mother all. she talk of her love for Arthur, and bewail, in her artless manner, the inequality of their condition, that set barrier between they. "therebe the 'Lady of Lyons,'" Fanny say; "oh, Ma! how I do love Mr. Macready when I see he do it; and Pauline, for be faithful to poor Claude, and always think of he; and he come back to she, an officer, through all his danger! and if everybody admire Pauline--and Ibe sure everybody do, for be so true to a poor man--why should a gentleman be ashamed of love a poor girl? not that Mr. Arthur love I --Oh no, no! I benot worthy of he; only a princess be worthy of such a gentleman as he. such a poet!--writing so beautifully, and look so grand! I be sure hebe a nobleman, and of ancient family, and kep' out of his estate. perhaps his uncle have it. ah, if I might, oh, how Iwould serve he, and work for he, and slave for he, that I would. I wouldnot ask for more than that, Ma, just to be allow to see he of a morning; and sometimes hewould say 'how d'you, Fanny?' or 'God bless you, Fanny!' as he say on Sunday. and Iwould work, and work; and Iwould sit up all night, and read, and learn, and make myself worthy of he. the Captain say his mother live in the country, and be a grand lady there. oh, how I wish I might go and be her servant, Ma! I can do plenty of thing, and work very neat; and--and sometimes hewould come home, and I should see he!"
the girl's head fall on her mother's shoulder, as she speak, and she give way to a plentiful outpouring of girlish tear, to which the matron, of course, join her own. "you mustnot think no more of he, Fanny," she say. "if he donot come to you, hebe a horrid, wicked man."
"donot call he so, Mother," Fanny reply. "hebe the good of man, the good and the kind. bow say he think he be unhappy at leave poor little fanny. it benot his fault, be it, that we met?--and it benot his that I mustnot see he again. he say I mustn't--and I mustnot, Mother. hewill forget I, but I shall never forget he. no! Iwill pray for he, and love he always--until I die--and I shall die, I know I shall--and then my spirit will always go and be with he."
"you forget your poor mother, Fanny, and youwill break my heart by goin on so," Mrs. Bolton say. "perhaps you will see he. Ibe sure youwill see he. Ibe sure hewill come to-day. if ever I see a man in love, that man be he. when Emily Budd's young man first come about she, he be send away by old Budd, a most respectable man, and violoncello in the orchestra at the Wells; and his own family wouldnot hear of it neither. but he come back. we all know he would. Emily always say so; and he marry she; and this one will come back too; and you mark a mother's word, and see if he donot, dear."
at this point of the conversation Mr. Bolton enter the lodge for his evening meal. at the father's appearance, the talk between mother and daughter cease instantly. Mrs. Bolton caress and cajole the surly undertaker's aide-de-camp, and say, "Lor, Mr. B. whowould have think to see you away from the Club of a Saturday night. Fanny, dear, get your pa some supper. what will you have, B.? the poor gurl's get a gathering in her eye, or somethink in it--I be lookin at it just now as you come in." and she squeeze her daughter's hand as a signal of prudence and secrecy; and Fanny's tear be dry up likewise; and by that wondrous hypocrisy and power of disguise which woman practise, and with which weapon of defence nature endow they, the trace of her emotion disappear; and she go and take her work, and sate in the corner so demure and quiet, that the careless male parent never suspect that anything ail she.
thus, as if fate seem determined to inflame and increase the poor child's malady and passion, all circumstance and all party round about she urge it on. her mother encourage and applaud it; and the very word which bow use in endeavour to repress her flame only augment this unlucky fever. Pen be not wicked and a seducer: Pen be high-minded in wish to avoid she. Pen love she: the good and the great, the magnificent youth, with the chain of gold and the scented auburn hair! and so he do: or so he would have love she five year back perhaps, before the world have harden the ardent and reckless boy--before he be ashamed of a foolish and imprudent passion, and strangle it as poor woman do their illicit child, not on account of the crime, but of the shame, and from dread that the finger of the world should point to they.
what respectable person in the world will not say he be quite right to avoid a marriage with an ill-educate person of low degree, whose relation a gentleman could not well acknowledge, and whose manner would not become her new station?--and what philosopher would not tell he that the good thing to do with these little passion if they spring up, be to get rid of they, and let they pass over and cure they: that no man die about a woman or vice versa: and that one or the other have find the impossibility of gratify his or her desire in the particular instance, must make the good of matter, forget each other, look out elsewhere, and choose again? and yet, perhaps, there may be something say on the other side. perhaps Bows be right in admire that passion of Pen's, blind and unreasoning as it be, that make he ready to stake his all for his love; perhaps if self-sacrifice be a laudable virtue, mere worldly self-sacrifice be not very much to be praised;--in fine, let this be a reserved point to be settle by the individual moralist who choose to debate it.
so much be certain, that with the experience of the world which Mr. Pen now have, he would have laugh at and scout the idea of marry a penniless girl out of a kitchen. and this point be fix in his mind, he be but do his duty as an honest man, in crush any unlucky fondness which he might feel towards poor little fanny.
so she wait and wait in hope that Arthur would come. she wait for a whole week, and it be at the end of that time that the poor little creature hear from Costigan of the illness under which Arthur be suffer.
it chance on that very evening after Costigan have visit Pen, that Arthur's uncle the excellent Major arrive in town from Buxton, where his health have be mend, and send his valet Morgan to make inquiry for Arthur, and to request that gentleman to breakfast with the Major the next morning. the Major be merely pass through London on his way to the Marquis of Steyne's house of Stillbrook, where he be engage to shoot partridge.
Morgan come back to his master with a very long face. he have see Mr. Arthur; Mr. Arthur be very bad indeed; Mr. Arthur be in bed with a fever. a doctor ought to be send to he; and Morgan think his case most alarming.
gracious goodness! this be sad news indeed. he have hop that Arthur could come down to Stillbrook: he have arrange that he should go, and procure an invitation for his nephew from Lord Steyne. he must go himself; he couldnot throw Lord Steyne over: the fever might be catch: it might be measle: he have never himself have the measle; they be dangerous when contract at his age. be anybody with Mr. Arthur?
Morgan say there be somebody a-nussing of Mr. Arthur.
the Major then ask, have his nephew take any advice? Morgan say he have ask that question, and have be tell that Mr. Pendennis have have no doctor.
Morgan's master be sincerely vex at hearing of Arthur's calamity. he would have go to he, but what good could it do Arthur that he, the Major, should catch a fever? his own ailment render it absolutely impossible that he should attend to anybody but himself. but the young man must have advice--the good advice; and Morgan be straightway despatch with a note from Major Pendennis to his friend Doctor Goodenough, who by good luck happen to be in London and at home, and who quit his dinner instantly, and whose carriage be in half an hour in Upper Temple Lane, near Pen's chamber. the Major have ask the kind-hearted physician to bring he news of his nephew at the Club where he himself be dining, and in the course of the night the Doctor make his appearance. the affair be very serious: the patient be in a high fever: he have have Pen bleed instantly: and would see he the first thing in the morning. the Major go disconsolate to bed with this unfortunate news. when Goodenough come to see he accord to his promise the next day, the Doctor have to listen for a quarter of an hour to an account of the Major's own malady, before the latter have leisure to hear about Arthur.
he have have a very bad night--his--his nurse say: at one hour he have be delirious. it might end badly: his mother have well be send for immediately. the Major write the letter to Mrs. Pendennis with the great alacrity, and at the same time with the most polite precaution. as for go himself to the lad, in his state it be impossible. "could I be of any use to he, my dear doctor?" he ask.
the Doctor, with a peculiar laugh, say, no: he donot think the Major could be of any use: that his own precious health require the most delicate treatment, and that he have well go into the country and stay: that he himself would take care to see the patient twice a day, and do all in his power for he.
the Major declare upon his honour, that if he could be of any use he would rush to Pen's chamber. as it be, Morgan should go and see that everything be right. the Doctor must write to he by every post to Stillbrook: it be but forty mile distant from London, and if anything happen he would come up at any sacrifice.
Major Pendennis transact his benevolence by deputy and by post. "what else could he do," as he say? "Gad, you know, in these case, itbe well not disturb a fellow. if a poor fellow go to the bad, why, Gad, you know hebe disposed of. but in order to get well (and in this, my dear doctor, Ibe sure that you will agree with I), the good way be to keep he quiet--perfectly quiet."
thus it be the old gentleman try to satisfy his conscience and he go his way that day to Stillbrook by railway (for railway have spring up in the course of this narrative, though they have not quite penetrate into Pen's country yet), and make his appearance in his usual trim order and curly wig, at the dinner-table of the Marquis of Steyne. but we must do the Major the justice to say, that he be very unhappy and gloomy in demeanour. Wagg and Wenham rally he about his low spirit; ask whether he be cross in love? and otherwise divert themselves at his expense. he lose his money at whist after dinner, and actually trump his partner's high spade. and the thought of the suffering boy, of whom he be proud, and whom he love after his manner, keep the old fellow awake half through the night, and make he feverish and uneasy.
on the morrow he receive a note in a handwriting which he do not know: it be that of Mr. Bows, indeed, say that Mr. Arthur Pendennis have have a tolerable night; and that as Dr. Goodenough have state that the Major desire to be inform of his nephew's health, he, R. B., have send he the news per rail.
the next day he be go out shooting, about noon, with some of the gentleman stay at Lord Steyne's house; and the company, wait for the carriage, be assemble on the terrace in front of the house, when a fly drive up from the neighbouring station, and a grey-head, rather shabby old gentleman jump out, and ask for Major Pendennis. it be Mr. Bows. he take the Major aside and speak to he; most of the gentleman round about see that something serious have happen, from the alarmed look of the Major's face.
Wagg say, "itbe a bailiff come down to nab the Major," but nobody laugh at the pleasantry.
"Hullo! whatbe the matter, Pendennis?" cry Lord Steyne, with his strident voice;--"anythe wrong?"
"it'--it's--my boy thatbe dead," say the Major, and burst into a sob-- the old man be quite overcome.
"not dead, my Lord; but very ill when I leave London," Mr. Bows say, in a low voice.
a britzka come up at this moment as the three man be speak. the Peer look at his watch. "youhave twenty minute to catch the mail-train. jump in, Pendennis; and drive like h---, sir, do you hear?"
the carriage drive off swiftly with Pendennis and his companion, and let we trust that the oath will be pardon to the Marquis of Steyne.
the Major drive rapidly from the station to the Temple, and find a travel carriage already before he, and block up the narrow Temple Lane. two lady get out of it, and be ask their way of the porter; the Major look by chance at the panel of the carriage, and see the wear-out crest of the Eagle look at the Sun, and the motto, "Nec tenui penna," paint beneath. it be his brother's old carriage, build many, many year ago. it be Helen and Laura that be ask their way to Pen's room.
he run up to they; hastily clasp his sister's arm and kiss her hand; and the three enter into Lamb Court, and mount the long gloomy stair.
they knock very gently at the door, on which Arthur's name be write, and it be open by Fanny Bolton.
CHAPTER LIII
a critical chapter
as Fanny see the two lady and the anxious countenance of the eider, who regard she with a look of inscrutable alarm and terror, the poor girl at once know that Pen's mother be before she; there be a resemblance between the widow's haggard eye and Arthur's as he toss in his bed in fever. Fanny look wistfully at Mrs. Pendennis and at Laura afterwards; there be no more expression in the latter's face than if it have be a mass of stone. hard-heartedness and gloom dwelt on the figure of both the new-comer; neither show any the faint gleam of mercy or sympathy for Fanny. she look desperately from they to the Major behind they. Old Pendennis drop his eyelid, look up ever so stealthily from under they at Arthur's poor little nurse.
"I--I write to you yesterday, if you please, ma'am," Fanny say, tremble in every limb as she speak; and as pale as Laura, whose sad menacing face look over Mrs. Pendennis's shoulder.
"do you, madam?" Mrs. Pendennis say. "I suppose I may now relieve you from nurse my son. I be his mother, you understand."
"yes, ma'am. I--this be the way to his--oh, wait a minute," cry out Fanny. "I must prepare you for his----"
the widow, whose face have be hopelessly cruel and ruthless, here start back with a gasp and a little cry, which she speedily stifle.
"hebe be so since yesterday," Fanny say, tremble very much, and with chatter tooth.
a horrid shriek of laughter come out of Pen's room, whereof the door be open; and, after several shout, the poor wretch begin to sing a college drinking-song, and then to hurray and to shout as if he be in the midst of a wine-party, and to thump with his fist against the wainscot. he be quite delirious.
"he do not know I, ma'am," Fanny say.
"indeed. perhaps he will know his mother; let I pass, if you please, and go in to he." and the widow hastily push by little Fanny, and through the dark passage which lead into Pen's sitting-room. Laura sail by Fanny, too, without a word; and Major Pendennis follow they. Fanny sit down on a bench in the passage, and cry, and pray as well as she could. she would have die for he; and they hat she. they have not a word of thank or kindness for she, the fine lady. she sate there in the passage, she do not know how long. they never come out to speak to she. she sate there until Doctor Goodenough come to pay his second visit that day; he find the poor little thing at the door.
"what, nurse? howbe your patient?" ask the good-natured doctor. "have he have any rest?"
"go and ask they. theybe inside," Fanny answer.
"who? his mother?"
Fanny nod her head and donot speak.
"you must go to bed yourself, my poor little maid," say the Doctor. "you will be ill, too, if you donot."
"oh, maynot I come and see he: maynot I come and see he! I--I--love he so," the little girl say; and as she speak she fall down on her knee and clasp hold of the Doctor's hand in such an agony that to see she melt the kind physician's heart, and cause a mist to come over his spectacle.
"Pooh, pooh! nonsense! Nurse, have he take his draught? have he have any rest? of course you must come and see he. so must i."
"theywill let I sit here, willnot they, sir? Iwill never make no noise. I only ask to stop here," Fanny say. on which the Doctor call she a stupid little thing; put she down upon the bench where Pen's printer's devil use to sit so many hour; tap her pale cheek with his finger, and bustle into the farth room.
Mrs. Pendennis be ensconce pale and solemn in a great chair by Pen's bedside. her watch be on the bed-table by Pen's medicine. her bonnet and cloak be lay in the window. she have her Bible in her lap, without which she never travel. her first movement, after see her son, have be to take Fanny's shawl and bonnet which be on his drawer, and bring they out and drop they down upon his study-table. she have close the door upon Major Pendennis, and Laura too; and take possession of her son.
she have have a great doubt and terror lest Arthur should not know she; but that pang be spar to she in part at least. Pen know his mother quite well, and familiarly smile and nod at she. when she come in, he instantly fancy that they be at home at Fairoaks; and begin to talk and chatter and laugh in a ramble wild way. Laura could hear he outside. his laughter shoot shaft of poison into her heart. it be true, then. he have be guilty--and with that creature!--an intrigue with a servant-maid, and she have love he--and he be die most likely rave and unrepentant. the Major now and then hum out a word of remark or consolation, which Laura scarce hear.
a dismal sitting it be for all party; and when Goodenough appear, he come like an angel into the room.
it be not only for the sick man, it be for the sick man's friend that the doctor come. his presence be often as good for they as for the patient, and they long for he yet more eagerly. how we have all watch after he! what an emotion the thrill of his carriage-wheel in the street, and at length at the door, have make we feel! how we hang upon his word, and what a comfort we get from a smile or two, if he can vouchsafe that sunshine to lighten our darkness! who havenot see the mother pry into his face, to know if there be hope for the sick infant that cannot speak, and that lie yonder, its little frame battle with fever? ah how she look into his eye! what thank if there be light there; what grief and pain if he cast they down, and dare not say "hope!" or it be the house-father who be stricken. the terrified wife look on, while the physician feel his patient's wrist, smother her agony, as the child have be call upon to stay their play and their talk. over the patient in the fever, the wife expectant, the child unconscious, the doctor stand as if he be Fate, the dispenser of life and death: he must let the patient off this time: the woman pray so for his respite! one can fancy how awful the responsibility must be to a conscientious man: how cruel the feeling that he have give the wrong remedy, or that it might have be possible to do well: how harass the sympathy with survivor, if the case be unfortunate--how immense the delight of victory!
have pass through a hasty ceremony of introduction to the new-comer, of whose arrival he have be make aware by the heartbroken little nurse in wait without, the doctor proceed to examine the patient, about whose condition of high fever there could be no mistake, and on whom he think it necessary to exercise the strong antiphlogistic remedy in his power. he console the unfortunate mother as good he might; and give she the most comfortable assurance on which he could venture, that there be no reason to despair yet, that everything might still be hop from his youth, the strength of his constitution, and so forth; and have do his utmost to allay the horror of the alarmed matron, he take the eld Pendennis aside into the vacant room (Warrington's bedroom), for the purpose of hold a little consultation.
the case be very critical. the fever, if not stop, might and would carry off the young fellow: he must be bleed forthwith: the mother must be inform of this necessity. why be that other young lady bring with she? she be out of place in a sick-room.
"and there be another woman still, be hang to it!" the Major say, "the--the little person who open the door." his sister-in-law have bring the poor little devil's bonnet and shawl out, fling they upon the study-table. do Goodenough know anything about the--the little person? "I just catch a glimpse of she as we pass in," the Major say, "and begad she be uncommonly nice-looking." the Doctor look queer: the doctor smile--in the very grave moment, with life and death pende, such strange contrast and occasion of humour will arise, and such smile will pass, to satirise the gloom, as it be, and to make it more gloomy!
"I have it," at last he say, re-enter the study; and he write a couple of note hastily at the table there, and seal one of they. then, take up poor Fanny's shawl and bonnet, and the note, he go out in the passage to that poor little messenger, and say, "quick, nurse; you must carry this to the surgeon, and bid he come instantly; and then go to my house, and ask for my servant Harbottle, and tell he to get this prescription prepare, and wait until I--until it be ready. it may take a little in preparation."
so poor Fanny trudge away with her two note, and find the apothecary, who live in the Strand hard by, and who come straightway, his lancet in his pocket, to operate on his patient; and then Fanny make for the Doctor's house, in Hanover Square.
