the Doctor be at home again before the prescription be make up, which take Harbottle, his servant, such a long time in compounding; and, during the remainder of Arthur's illness, poor Fanny never make her appearance in the quality of nurse at his chamber any more. but for that day and the next, a little figure might be see lurk about Pen's staircase,--a sad, sad little face look at and interrogate the apothecary, and the apothecary's boy, and the laundress, and the kind physician himself, as they pass out of the chamber of the sick man. and on the third day, the kind Doctor's chariot stop at Shepherd's Inn, and the good, and honest, and benevolent man go into the porter's lodge, and tend a little patient whom he have there, for the good remedy he find be on the day when he be enable to tell Fanny Bolton that the crisis be over, and that there be at length every hope for Arthur Pendennis.
J. Costigan, Esquire, late of her Majesty's service, see the Doctor's carriage, and criticise its horse and appointment. "green livery, bedad!" the General say, "and as foin a pair of high-step bee horse as ever a gentleman need sit behoind, let alone a docthor. therebe no ind to the proide and ar'gance of they docthor, nowadays--not but that be a good one, and a scoientific cyarkter, and a roight good fellow, bedad; and hebe bring the poor little girl well troo her faver, Bows, I boy;" and so pleased be Mr. Costigan with the Doctor's behaviour and skill, that, whenever he meet Dr. Goodenough's carriage in future, he make a point of salute it and the physician inside, in as courteous and magnificent a manner, as if Dr. Goodenough have be the Lord Liftenant himself, and Captain Costigan have be in his glory in Phaynix Park.
the widow's gratitude to the physician know no bound--or scarcely any bound, at least. the kind gentleman laugh at the idea of take a fee from a literary man, or the widow of a brother practitioner; and she determine when she get to Fairoaks that she would send Goodenough the silver-gilt vase, the jewel of the house, and the glory of the late John Pendennis, preserve in green baize, and present to he at Bath, by the Lady Elizabeth Firebrace, on the recovery of her son, the late Sir Anthony Firebrace, from the scarlet fever. hippocrate, Hygeia, King Bladud, and a wreath of serpent surmount the cup to this day; which be execute in their fine manner by Messrs. Abednego, of Milsom Street; and the inscription be by Mr. Birch, tutor to the young baronet.
this priceless gem of art the widow determine to devote to Goodenough, the preserver of her son; and there be scarcely any other favour which her gratitude would not have confer upon he, except one, which he desire most, and which be that she should think a little charitably and kindly of poor Fanny, of whose artless, sad story he have get something during his interview with she, and of whom he be induce to think very kindly,--not be dispose, indeed, to give much credit to Pen for his conduct in the affair, or not know what that conduct have be. he know enough, however, to be aware that the poor infatuate little girl be without stain as yet; that while she have be in Pen's room it be to see the last of he, as she think, and that Arthur be scarcely aware of her presence; and that she suffer under the deep and most pitiful grief, at the idea of lose he, dead or live.
but on the one or two occasion when Goodenough allude to Fanny, the widow's countenance, always soft and gentle, assume an expression so cruel and inexorable, that the Doctor see it be in vain to ask she for justice or pity, and he break off all entreaty, and cease make any further allusion regard his little client. there be a complaint which neither poppy, nor mandragora, nor all the drowsy syrup of the East could allay, in the man in his time, as we be inform by a popular poet of the day of Elizabeth; and which, when exhibit in woman, no medical discovery or practice subsequent--neither homoeopathy, nor hydropathy, nor mesmerism, nor Dr. Simpson, nor Dr. Locock can cure, and that be--we willnot call it jealousy, but rather gently denominate rivalry and emulation in lady.
some of those mischievous and prosaic people who carp and calculate at every detail of the romancer, and want to know, for instance, how, when the character in the 'Critic' be at a dead lock with their dagger at each other's throat, they be to be get out of that murderous complication of circumstance, may be induce to ask how it be possible in a set of chamber in the Temple, consist of three room, two cupboard, a passage, and a coal-box, Arthur a sick gentleman, Helen his mother, Laura her adopt daughter, Martha their country attendant, Mrs. Wheezer a nurse from St. Bartholomew's Hospital, Mrs. Flanagan an irish laundress, Major Pendennis a retired military officer, Morgan his valet, Pidgeon Mr. Arthur Pendennis's boy, and other could be accommodate--the answer be give at once, that almost everybody in the Temple be out of town, and that there be scarcely a single occupant of Pen's house in Lamb Court except those who be occupy round the sick-bed of the sick gentleman, about whose fever we have not give a lengthy account, neither enlarge we very much upon the more cheerful theme of his recovery.
everybody we have say be out of town, and of course such a fashionable man as young Mr. Sibwright, who occupy chamber on the second floor in Pen's staircase, could not be suppose to remain in London. Mrs. Flanagan, Mr. Pendennis's laundress be acquaint with Mrs. Rouncy who do for Mr. Sibwright; and that gentleman's bedroom be get ready for Miss Bell, or Mrs. Pendennis, when the latter should be incline to leave her son's sick-room, to try and seek for a little rest for herself.
if that young buck and flower of Baker Street, Percy Sibwright, could have know who be the occupant of his bedroom, how proud he would have be of that apartment:--what poem he would have write about Laura! (several of his thing have appear in the annual, and in manuscript in the nobility's albums)--he be a Camford man and very nearly get the English Prize Poem, it be say--Sibwright, however, be absent and his bed give up to Miss Bell. it be the prettiest little brass bed in the world, with chintz curtain line with pink--he have a mignonette-box in his bedroom window, and the mere sight of his little exhibition of shiny boot, arrange in trim row over his wardrobe, be a gratification to the beholder. he have a museum of scent, pomatum, and bear's-grease pot, quite curious to examine, too; and a choice selection of portrait of female, almost always in sadness and generally in disguise or deshabille, glitter round the neat wall of his elegant little bower of repose. Medora with dishevel hair be console herself over her banjo for the absence of her Conrad--the Princesse Fleur de Marie (of Rudolstein and the Mysteres de Paris) be sadly ogle out of the bar of her convent cage, in which, poor prisone bird, she be moult away,-- Dorothea of Don Quixote be wash her eternal feet:--in fine, it be such an elegant gallery as become a gallant lover of the sex. and in Sibwright's sitting-room, while there be quite an infantine law library clothe in skin of fresh new-bear calf, there be a tolerably large collection of classical book which he could not read, and of English and french work of poetry and fiction which he read a great deal too much. his invitation card of the past season still decorate his look-glass: and scarce anything tell of the lawyer but the wig-box beside the Venus upon the middle shelf of the bookcase, on which the name of P. Sibwright, Esquire, be gild.
with Sibwright in chamber be Mr. Bangham. Mr. Bangham be a sporting man marry to a rich widow. Mr. Bangham have no practice--do not come to chamber thrice in a term: go a circuit for those mysterious reason which make man go circuit,--and his room serve as a great convenience to Sibwright when that young gentleman give his little dinner. it must be confess that these two gentleman have nothing to do with our history, will never appear in it again probably, but we cannot help glance through their door as they happen to be open to we, and as we pass to Pen's room; as in the pursuit of our own business in life through the Strand, at the Club, nay at church itself, we cannot help peep at the shop on the way, or at our neighbour's dinner, or at the face under the bonnet in the next pew.
very many year after the circumstance about which we be at present occupy, Laura, with a blush and a laugh show much humour, own to have read a french novel once much in vogue, and when her husband ask she, wonder where on earth she could have get such a volume, she own that it be in the Temple, when she live in Mr. Percy Sibwright's chamber.
"and, also, I never confess," she say, "on that same occasion, what I must now own to: that I open the japanned box, and take out that strange-look wig inside it, and put it on and look at myself in the glass in it."
suppose Percy Sibwright have come in at such a moment as that? what would he have said,--the enraptured rogue? what would have be all the picture of disguised beauty in his room compare to that live one? ah, we be speak of old time, when Sibwright be a bachelor and before he get a county court,--when people be young--when most people be young. other people be young now; but we no more.
when Miss Laura play this prank with the wig, you cannot suppose that Pen could have be very ill upstairs; otherwise, though she have grow to care for he ever so little, common sense of feeling and decorum would have prevent she from perform any trick or try any disguise.
but all sort of event have occur in the course of the last few day which have contribute to increase or account for her gaiety, and a little colony of the reader's old friend and acquaintance be by this time establish in Lamb Court, Temple, and round Pen's sick-bed there. first, Martha, Mrs. Pendennis's servant, have arrive from Fairoaks, be summon thence by the Major who justly think her presence would be comfortable and useful to her mistress and her young master, for neither of whom the constant neighbourhood of Mrs. Flanagan (who during Pen's illness require more spirituous consolation than ever to support she) could be pleasant. Martha then make her appearance in due season to wait upon Mr. Pendennis, nor do that lady go once to bed until the faithful servant have reach she, when, with a heart full of maternal thankfulness she go and lie down upon Warrington's straw mattress, and among his mathematical book as have be already describe.
it be true that ere that day a great and delightful alteration in Pen's condition have take place. the fever, subjugate by Dr. Goodenough's blister, potion, and lancet, have leave the young man, or only return at interval of feeble intermittence; his wandering sens have settle in his weakened brain: he have have time to kiss and bless his mother for come to he, and call for Laura and his uncle (who be both affect accord to their different nature by his wan appearance, his lean shrunken hand, his hollow eye and voice, his thin bearded face) to press their hand and thank they affectionately; and after this greeting, and after they have be turn out of the room by his affectionate nurse, he have sink into a fine sleep which have last for about sixteen hour, at the end of which period he awake call out that he be very hungry. if it be hard to be ill and to loathe food, oh, how pleasant to be get well and to be feel hungry--how hungry! alas, the joy of convalescence become feebler with increase year, as other joy do--and then--and then come that illness when one do not convalesce at all.
on the day of this happy event, too, come another arrival in Lamb Court. this be introduce into the Pen-Warring sitting-room by large puff of tobacco smoke--the puff of be follow by an individual with a cigar in his mouth, and a carpet-bag under his arm--this be Warrington who have run back from Norfolk, when Mr. Bows thoughtfully write to inform he of his friend's calamity. but he have be from home when Bows's letter have reach his brother's house--the Eastern Counties do not then boast of a railway (for we beg the reader to understand that we only commit anachronism when we choose and when by a daring violation of those natural law some great ethical truth be to be advanced)--in fine, Warrington only appear with the rest of the good luck upon the lucky day after Pen's convalescence may have be say to have begin.
his surprise be, after all, not very great when he find the chamber of his sick friend occupy, and his old acquaintance the major seat demurely in an easy-chair (Warrington have let himself into the room with his own passkey), listen, or pretend to listen, to a young lady who be read to he a play of Shakspeare in a low sweet voice. the lady stop and start, and lay down her book, at the apparition of the tall traveller with the cigar and the carpet-bag. he blush, he fling the cigar into the passage: he take off his hat, and drop that too, and go up to the Major, seize that old gentleman's hand, and ask question about Arthur.
the Major answer in a tremulous, though cheery voice--it be curious how emotion seem to olden he--and return Warrington's pressure with a shake hand, tell he the news of Arthur's happy crisis, of his mother's arrival--with her young charge--with miss----"
"you need not tell I her name," Mr. Warrington say with great animation, for he be affect and elate with the thought of his friend's recovery--"you need not tell I your name. I know at once it be Laura." and he hold out his hand and take her. immense kindness and tenderness gleam from under his rough eyebrow, and shake his voice as he gaze at she and speak to she. "and this be Laura!" his look seem to say. "and this be Warrington!" the generous girl's heart beat back. "Arthur's hero--the brave and the kind--he have come hundred of mile to succour he, when he hear of his friend's misfortune!"
"thank you, Mr. Warrington," be all that Laura say, however; and as she return the pressure of his kind hand, she blush so, that she be glad the lamp be behind she to conceal her flush face.
as these two be stand in this attitude, the door of Pen's bedchamber be open stealthily as his mother be willnot to open it, and Warrington see another lady, who first look at he, and then turn round towards the bed, say, "Hsh!" and put up her hand.
it be to Pen Helen be turn, and give caution. he call out with a feeble, tremulous, but cheery voice, "come in, Stunner--come in, Warrington. I know it be you--by the--by the smoke, old boy," he say, as hold his worn hand out, and with tear at once of weakness and pleasure in his eye, he greet his friend.
"I--I beg pardon, ma'am, for smoking," Warrington say, who now almost for the first time blush for his wicked propensity.
Helen only say, "God bless you, Mr. Warrington." she be so happy, she would have like to kiss George. then, and after the friend have have a brief, very brief interview, the delighted and inexorable mother, give her hand to Warrington, send he out of the room, too, back to Laura and the Major, who have not resume their play of Cymbeline where they have leave it off at the arrival of the rightful owner of Pen's chamber.
CHAPTER LIV
Convalescence
our duty now be to record a fact concern Pendennis, which, however shameful and disgraceful, when tell regard the chief personage and godfather of a novel, must, nevertheless, be make known to the public who read his veritable memoir. have go to bed ill with fever, and suffer to a certain degree under the passion of love, after he have go through his physical malady, and have be bleed and have be blister, and have have his head shave, and have be treat and medicamente as the doctor ordained:--it be a fact, that, when he rally up from his bodily ailment, his mental malady have likewise quit he, and he be no more in love with Fanny Bolton than you or I, who be much too wise, or too moral, to allow our heart to go gad after porter' daughter.
he laugh at himself as he lie on his pillow, think of this second cure which have be effect upon he. he do not care the least about Fanny now: he wonder how he ever should have care: and accord to his custom make an autopsy of that dead passion, and anatomise his own defunct sensation for his poor little nurse. what could have make he so hot and eager about she but a few week back? not her wit, not her breeding, not her beauty--there be hundred of woman well-look than she. it be out of himself that the passion have go: it do not reside in she. she be the same; but the eye which see be change; and, alas, that it should be so! be not particularly eager to see she any more. he feel very well disposed towards the little thing, and so forth, but as for violent personal regard, such as he have but a few week ago, it have flee under the influence of the pill and lancet, which have destroy the fever in his frame. and an immense source of comfort and gratitude it be to Pendennis (though there be something selfish in that feeling, as in most other of our young man), that he have be enable to resist temptation at the time when the danger be great, and have no particular cause of self-reproach as he remember his conduct towards the young girl. as from a precipice down which he might have fall, so from the fever from which he have recover, he review the Fanny Bolton snare, now that he have escape out of it, but Ibe not sure that he be not ashamed of the very satisfaction which he experience. it be pleasant, perhaps, but it be humiliating to own that you love no more.
meanwhile the kind smile and tender watchfulness of the mother at his bedside, fill the young man with peace and security. to see that health be return, be all the unwearied nurse demand: to execute any caprice or order of her patient's, her chief joy and reward. he feel himself environ by her love, and think himself almost as grateful for it as he have be when weak and helpless in childhood.
some misty notion regard the first part of his illness, and that Fanny have nurse he, Pen may have have, but they be so dim that he could not realise they with accuracy, or distinguish they from what he know to be delusion which have occur and be remember during the delirium of his fever. so as he have not think proper on former occasion to make any allusion about Fanny Bolton to his mother, of course he could not now confide to she his sentiment regard Fanny, or make this worthy lady a confidante. it be on both side an unlucky precaution and want of confidence; and a word or two in time might have spar the good lady, and those connect with she, a deal of pain and anguish.
see Miss Bolton instal as nurse and tender to Pen, I be sorry to say Mrs. Pendennis have put the bad construction on the fact of the intimacy of these two unlucky young person, and have settle in her own mind that the accusation against Arthur be true. why not have stop to inquire?--there be story to a man's disadvantage that the woman who be fondest of he be always the most eager to believe. benot a man's wife often the first to be jealous of he? Poor Pen get a good stock of this suspicious kind of love from the nurse who be now watch over he; and the kind and pure creature think that her boy have go through a malady much more awful and debase than the mere physical fever, and be stain by crime as well as weaken by illness. the consciousness of this she have to bear perforce silently, and to try to put a mask of cheerfulness and confidence over her doubt and despair and inward horror.
when Captain Shandon, at Boulogne, read the next number of the Pall Mall Gazette, it be to remark to Mrs. Shandon that Jack Finucane's hand be no long visible in the lead article, and that Mr. Warrington must be at work there again. "I know the crack of his whip in a hundred, and the cut which the fellow's thong leaf. therebe Jack Bludyer, go to work like a butcher, and mangle a subject. Mr. Warrington finish a man, and lay his cut neat and regular, straight down the back, and draw blood every line;" at which dreadful metaphor, Mrs. Shandon say, "Law, Charles, how can you talk so! I always think Mr. Warrington very high, but a kind gentleman; and Ibe sure he be most kind to the child." upon which Shandon say, "yes; hebe kind to the child; but hebe savage to the man; and to be sure, my dear, you donot understand a word about what Ibe say; and itbe good you shouldnot; for itbe little good come out of writing for newspaper; and itbe well here, live easy at Boulogne, where the wine's plenty, and the brandy cost but two franc a bottle. mix we another tumbler, Mary, my dear; wewill go back into harness soon. 'Cras ingen iterabimus aequor' bad luck to it."
in a word, Warrington go to work with all his might, in place of his prostrate friend, and do Pen's portion of the Pall Mall Gazette "with a vengeance," as the saying be. he write occasional article and literary criticism; he attend theatre and musical performance, and discourse about they with his usual savage energy. his hand be too strong for such small subject, and it please he to tell Arthur's mother, and uncle, and Laura, that there be no hand in all the band of penman more graceful and light, more pleasant and more elegant, than Arthur's. "the people in this country, ma'am, donot understand what style be, or they would see the merit of our young one," he say to Mrs. Pendennis. "I call he our, ma'am, for I breed he; and I be as proud of he as you be; and, bat a little wilfulness, and a little selfishness, and a little dandification, I donot know a more honest, or loyal, or gentle creature. his pen be wicked sometimes, but he be as kind as a young lady--as Miss Laura here--and I believe he would not do any live mortal harm."
at this, Helen, though she heave a deep, deep sigh, and Laura, though she, too, be sadly wound, nevertheless be most thankful for Warrington's good opinion of Arthur, and love he for be so attach to their Pen. and Major Pendennis be loud in his praise of Mr. warrington,--more loud and enthusiastic than it be the Major's willnot to be. "he be a gentleman, my dear creature," he say to Helen, "every inch a gentleman, my good madam--the Suffolk Warringtons--Charles the First's baronets:--what could he be but a gentleman, come out of that family?-- father,--sir Miles Warrington; run away with--beg your pardon, Miss Bell. Sir Miles be a very well know man in London, and a friend of the Prince of Wales, this gentleman be a man of the great talent, the very high accomplishments,--sure to get on, if he have a motive to put his energy to work."
Laura blush for herself whilst the Major be talk and praise Arthur's hero. as she look at Warrington's manly face, and dark, melancholy eye, this young person have be speculate about he, and have settle in her mind that he must have be the victim of an unhappy attachment; and as she catch herself so speculate, why, Miss Bell blush.
Warrington get chamber hard by,--Grenier's chamber in Flag Court; and have execute Pen's task with great energy in the morning, his delight and pleasure of an afternoon be to come and sit with the sick man's company in the sunny autumn evening; and he have the honour more than once of give Miss Bell his arm for a walk in the Temple Gardens; to take which pastime, when the frank Laura ask of Helen permission, the Major eagerly say, "yes, yes, begad--of course you go out with he--itbe like the country, you know; everybody go out with everybody in the Gardens, and there be beadle, you know, and that sort of thing-- everybody walk in the Temple Gardens." if the great arbiter of moral do not object, why should simple Helen? she be glad that her girl should have such fresh air as the river could give, and to see her return with heighten colour and spirit from these harmless excursion.
Laura and Helen have come, you must know, to a little explanation. when the news arrive of Pen's alarming illness, Laura insist upon accompany the terrified mother to London, would not hear of the refusal which the still angry Helen give she, and, when refuse a second time yet more sternly, and when it seem that the poor lose lad's life be despair of, and when it be know that his conduct be such as to render all thought of union hopeless, Laura have, with many tear, tell her mother a secret with which every observant person who read this story be acquaint already. now she never could marry he, be she to be deny the consolation of own how fondly, how truly, how entirely she have love he? the mingle tear of the woman appease the agony of their grief somewhat; and the sorrow and terror of their journey be at least in so far mitigate that they share they together.
what could Fanny expect when suddenly bring up for sentence before a couple of such judge? nothing but swift condemnation, awful punishment, merciless dismissal! woman be cruel critic in case such as that in which poor Fanny be implicate; and we like they to be so; for, besides the guard which a man place round his own harem, and the defence which a woman have in her heart, her faith, and honour, havenot she all her own friend of her own sex to keep watch that she do not go astray, and to tear she to piece if she be find err? when our Mahmouds or Selims of Baker Street or Belgrave Square visit their Fatimas with condign punishment, their mother sew up Fatima's sack for she, and her sister and sister-in-law see she well under water. and this present writer do not say nay. he protest most solemnly he be a Turk, too. he wear a turban and a beard like another, and be all for the sack practice, Bismillah! but o you spotless, who have the right of capital punishment vest in you, at least be very cautious that you make away with the proper (if so she may be call) person. be very sure of the fact before you order the barge out: and donot pop your subject into the Bosphorus, until you be quite certain that she deserve it. this be all I would urge in poor Fatima's behalf--absolutely all--not a word more, by the beard of the Prophet. if shebe guilty, down with she--heave over the sack, away with it into the Golden Horn bubble and squeak, and justice be do, give way, man, and let we pull back to supper.
so the Major do not in any way object to Warrington's continue promenade with Miss Laura, but, like a benevolent old gentleman, encourage in every way the intimacy of that couple. be there any exhibition in town? he be for Warrington conduct she to they. if Warrington have propose to take she to Vauxhall itself, this most complaisant of man would have see no harm,--nor would Helen, if Pendennis the eld have so rule it,--nor would there have be any harm between two person whose honour be entirely spotless,--between Warrington, who see in intimacy a pure, and high-minded, and artless woman for the first time in his life,--and Laura, who too for the first time be throw into the constant society of a gentleman of great natural part and power of pleasing; who possess varied acquirement, enthusiasm, simplicity, humour, and that freshness of mind which his simple life and habit give he, and which contrast so much with Pen's dandy indifference of manner and fade sneer. in Warrington's very uncouthness there be a refinement, which the other's finery lack. in his energy, his respect, his desire to please, his hearty laughter, or simple confide pathos, what a difference to Sultan Pen's yawn sovereignty and languid acceptance of homage! what have make Pen at home such a dandy and such a despot? the woman have spoil he, as we like they and as they like to do. they have cloy he with obedience, and surfeit he with sweet respect and submission, until he grow weary of the slave who wait upon he, and their caress and cajolery excite he no more. abroad, he be brisk and lively, and eager and impassione enough--most man be so constituted and so nurtured.--Does this, like the former sentence, run a chance of be misinterpret, and do any one dare to suppose that the writer would incite the woman to revolt? Nevert, by the whisker of the Prophet again, he say. he wear a beard, and he like his woman to be slave. what man donot? what man would be henpecke, I say? we will cut off all the head in Christendom or Turkeydom rather than that.
well, then, Arthur be so languid, and indifferent, and careless about the favour bestow upon he, how come it that Laura should have such a love and rapturous regard for he, that a mere inadequate expression of it should have keep the girl talk all the way from Fairoaks to London, as she and Helen travel in the post-chaise? as soon as Helen have finish one story about the dear fellow, and narrate, with a hundred sob and ejaculation, and look up to heaven, some thrilling incident which occur about the period when the hero be breeche, Laura begin another equally interesting and equally ornament with tear, and tell how heroically he have a tooth out or wouldnot have it out, or how daringly he rob a bird's nest or how magnanimously he spar it; or how he give a shilling to the old woman on the common, or go without his bread-and-butter for the beggar-boy who come into the yard--and so on one to another the sob woman sing lament upon their hero, who, my worthy reader have long since perceive, be no more a hero than one of we. be as he be, why should a sensible girl be so fond of he?
this point have be argue before in a previous unfortunate sentence (which lately draw down all the wrath of Ireland upon the writer's head), and which say that the great rascal-cut-throat have have somebody to be fond of they, and if those monster, why not ordinary mortal? and with whom shall a young lady fall in love but with the person she see? she be not suppose to lose her heart in a dream, like a Princess in the Arabian Nights; or to plight her young affection to the portrait of a gentleman in the exhibition, or a sketch in the Illustrated London News. you have an instinct within you which incline you to attach yourself to some one: you meet somebody: you hear somebody constantly praise: you walk, or ride, or waltz, or talk or sit in the same pew at church with somebody: you meet again, and again, and--"marriage be make in Heave," your dear mamma say, pin your orange-flower wreath on, with her blessed eye dim with tear--and there be a wedding breakfast, and you take off your white satin and retire to your coach-and-four, and you and he be a happy pair.--or, the affair be break off, and then, poor wound heart! why, then you meet Somebody Else, and twine your young affection round number two. it be your nature so to do. do you suppose it be all for the man's sake that you love, and not a bit for your own? do you suppose you would drink if you be not thirsty, or eat if you be not hungry?
so then Laura like Pen because she see scarcely anybody else at Fairoaks except Doctor Portman and Captain Glanders, and because his mother constantly praise her Arthur, and because he be gentlemanlike, tolerably good-looking and witty, and because, above all, it be of her nature to like somebody. and have once receive this image into her heart, she there tenderly nurse it and clasp it--she there, in his long absence and her constant solitude, silently brood over it and fondle it--and when after this she come to London, and have an opportunity of become rather intimate with Mr. George Warrington, what on earth be to prevent she from think he a most odd, original, agreeable, and please person?
a long time afterwards, when these day be over, and fate in its own way have dispose of the various person now assemble in the dingy building in Lamb Court, perhaps some of they look back and think how happy the time be, and how pleasant have be their evening talk and little walk and simple recreation round the sofa of Pen the convalescent. the Major have a favourable opinion of September in London from that time forward, and declare at his club and in society that the dead season in town be often pleasant, doosid pleasant, begad. he use to go home to his lodging in Bury Street of a night, wonder that it be already so late, and that the evening have pass away so quickly. he make his appearance at the Temple pretty constantly in the afternoon, and tug up the long black staircase with quite a benevolent activity and perseverance. and he make interest with the chef at Bays's (that renowned cook, the superintendence of whose work upon Gastronomy compel the gifted author to stay in the metropolis), to prepare little jelly, delicate clear soup, aspic, and other trifle good for invalid, which Morgan the valet constantly bring down to the little Lamb Court colony. and the permission to drink a glass or two of pure sherry be accord to Pen by Doctor Goodenough, the Major tell with almost tear in his eye how his noble friend the Marquis of Steyne, pass through London on his way to the continent, have order any quantity of his precious, his priceless Amontillado, that have be a present from King Ferdinand to the noble Marquis, to be place at the disposal of Mr. Arthur Pendennis. the widow and Laura taste it with respect (though they donot in the least like the bitter flavour) but the invalid be greatly invigorate by it, and Warrington pronounce it superlatively good, and propose the Major's health in a mock speech after dinner on the first day when the wine be serve, and that of Lord Steyne and the aristocracy in general.
Major Pendennis return thank with the utmost gravity and in a speech in which he use the word, 'the present occasion,' at least the proper number of time. Pen cheer with his feeble voice from his armchair. Warrington teach Miss Laura to cry "Hear! hear!" and tap the table with his knuckle. Pidgeon the attendant grin, and honest Doctor Goodenough find the party so merrily engage, when he come in to pay his faithful gratuitous visit.
Warrington know Sibwright, who live below and that gallant gentleman, in reply to a letter inform he of the use to which his apartment have be put, write back the most polite and flowery letter of acquiescence he place his chamber at the service of their fair occupant, his bed at their disposal, his carpet at their foot. everybody be kindly disposed towards the sick man and his family. his heart (and his mother's too, as we may fancy) melt within he at the thought of so much good-feeling and good-nature. let Pen's biographer be pardon for allude to a time not far distant when a somewhat similar mishap bring he a providential friend, a kind physician, and a thousand proof of a most touching and surprising kindness and sympathy.
there be a piano in Mr. Sibwright's chamber (indeed, this gentleman, a lover of all the art, perform himself--and excellently ill too--upon the instrument; and have have a song dedicate to he, the word by himself, the air by his devoted friend Leopoldo Twankidillo), and at this music-box, as Mr. Warrington call it, Laura, at first with a great deal of tremor and blushing (which become her very much), play and sing, sometimes of an evening, simple air, and old song of home. her voice be a rich contralto, and Warrington, who scarcely know one tune from another and who have but one tune or bray in his repertoire,--a most discordant imitation of 'God save the King'--sat rapt in delight listen to these song. he could follow their rhythm if not their harmony; and he could watch, with a constant and daily grow enthusiasm, the pure and tender and generous creature who make the music.
I wonder how that poor pale little girl in the black bonnet, who use to stand at the lamp-post in Lamb Court sometimes of an evening, look up to the open window from which the music come, like to hear it? when Pen's bedtime come the song be hush. light appear in the upper room: his room, whither the widow use to conduct he; and then the Major and Mr. Warrington, and sometimes Miss Laura, would have a game at ecarte or backgammon; or she would sit by work a pair of slipper in worsted --a pair of gentleman's slipper--they might have be for Arthur or for George or for Major Pendennis: one of those three would have give anything for the slipper.
Whilst such business as this be go on within, a rather shabby old gentleman would come and lead away the pale girl in the black bonnet, who have no right to be abroad in the night air; and the Temple porter, the few laundress, and other amateur who have be listen to the concert, would also disappear.
just before ten o'clock there be another musical performance, namely that of the chime of St. Clement's clock in the Strand, which play the clear cheerful note of a psalm, before it proceed to ring its ten fatal stroke. as they be ring, Laura begin to fold up the slipper; Martha from Fairoaks appear with a bed-candle, and a constant smile on her face; the Major say, "God bless my soul, be it so late?" Warrington and he leave their unfinished game, and get up and shake hand with Miss Bell. Martha from Fairoaks light they out of the passage and down the stair, and, as they descend, they could hear her bolt and lock "the sporting door" after they, upon her young mistress and herself. if there have be any danger, grin Martha say she would have get down "that thar hooky soord which hang up in gantleman's room,"--meane the Damascus scimitar with the name of the prophet engrave on the blade and the red velvet scabbard, which Percy Sibwright, Esquire, bring back from his tour in the Levant, along with an albanian dress, and which he wear with such elegant effect at Lady Mullingar's fancy ball, Gloucester Square, Hyde Park. it entangle itself in Miss Kewsey's train, who appear in the dress in which she, with her mamma, have be present to their sovereign (the latter by the L--d ch-nc-ll-r's lady), and lead to event which have nothing to do with this history. be not Miss Kewsey now Mrs. Sibwright? have Sibwright not get a county court?--good night, Laura and Fairoaks Martha. sleep well and wake happy, pure and gentle lady.
sometimes after these evening Warrington would walk a little way with Major Pendennis--just a little way just as far as the Temple gate--as the Strand--as Charing Cross--as the Club--he be not go into the Club? well, as far as Bury Street, where he would laughingly shake hand on the Major's own door-step. they have be talk about Laura all the way. it be wonderful how enthusiastic the Major, who, as we know, use to dislike she, have grow to be regard the young lady--"dev'lish fine girl, begad. Dev'lish well-mannered girl--my sister-in-law have the manner of a duchess and would bring up any girl well. Miss Bell's a little countryfied. but the smell of the hawthorn be pleasant, demmy. how she blush! your London girl would give many a guinea for a bouquet like that--natural flower, begad! and shebe a little money too--nothing to speak of--but a pooty little bit of money." in all which opinion no doubt Mr. Warrington agree; and though he laugh as he shake hand with the Major, his face fall as he leave his veteran companion; and he stride back to chamber, and smoke pipe after pipe long into the night, and write article upon article, more and more savage, in lieu of friend Pen disable.
well, it be a happy time for almost all party concern. Pen mend daily. sleep and eating be his constant occupation. his appetite be something frightful. he be ashamed of exhibit it before Laura, and almost before his mother who laugh and applaud he. as the roast chicken of his dinner go away he eye the depart friend with sad longing, and begin to long for jelly, or tea, or what not. he be like an ogre in devouring. the doctor cry stop, but Pen would not. Nature call out to he more loudly than the Doctor, and that kind and friendly physician hand he over with a very good grace to the other healer.
and here let we speak very tenderly and in the strict confidence of an event which befell he, and to which he never like an allusion. during his delirium the ruthless Goodenough order ice to be put to his head, and all his lovely hair to be cut. it be do in the time of--of the other nurse, who leave every single hair of course in a paper for the widow to count and treasure up. she never believe but that the girl have take away some of it, but then woman be so suspicious upon these matter.
when this direful loss be make visible to Major Pendennis as of course it be the first time the elder see the poor young man's shorn pate, and when Pen be quite out of danger, and gain daily vigour, the Major, with something like blush and a queer wink of his eye, say he know of a--a person--a coiffeur, in fact--a good man, whom he would send down to the Temple, and who would--a--apply--a--a temporary remedy to that misfortune.
Laura look at Warrington with the arch sparkle in her eyes-- Warrington fairly burst out into a boohoo of laughter: even the widow be oblige to laugh: and the major erubescent confound the impudence of the young folk, and say when he have his hair cut he would keep a lock of it for Miss Laura.
Warrington vote that Pen should wear a barrister's wig. there be Sibwright's down below, which would become he hugely. Pen say "Stuff," and seem as confuse as his uncle; and the end be that a gentleman from Burlington Arcade wait next day upon Mr. Pendennis, and have a private interview with he in his bedroom; and a week afterwards the same individual appear with a box under his arm, and an ineffable grin of politeness on his face, and announce that he have bring 'ome Mr. Pendennis's 'ead of 'air.
it must have be a grand but melancholy sight to see Pen in the recess of his apartment, sadly contemplate his ravage beauty, and the artificial mean of hide its ruin. he appear at length in the 'ead of 'air; but Warrington laugh so, that Pen grow sulky, and go back for his velvet cap, a neat turban which the fond of mamma have work for he. then Mr. Warrington and Miss Bell get some flower off the lady' bonnet and make a wreath, with which they decorate the wig and bring it out in procession, and do homage before it. in fact they indulge in a hundred sport, jularities, waggery, and petit jeux innocen: so that the second and third floor of Number 6 Lamb Court, Temple, ring with more cheerfulness and laughter than have be know in those precinct for many a long day.
at last, after about ten day of this life, one evening when the little spy of the court come out to take her usual post of observation at the lamp, there be no music from the second-floor window, there be no light in the third-story chamber, the window of each be open, and the occupant be go. Mrs. Flanagan, the laundress, tell Fanny what have happen. the lady and all the party have go to Richmond for change of air. the antique travel chariot be bring out again and cushion with many pillow for Pen and his mother; and Miss Laura go in the most affable manner in the omnibus under the guardianship of Mr. George Warrington. he come back and take possession of his old bed that night in the vacant and cheerless chamber, and to his old book and his old pipe, but not perhaps to his old sleep.
the widow have leave a jar full of flower upon his table, prettily arrange, and when he enter they fill the solitary room with odour. they be memorial of the kind, gentle soul who have go away, and who have decorate for a little while that lonely cheerless place. he have have the happy day of his whole life George feel--he know it now they be just go: he go and take up the flower and put his face to they, and smell they--perhaps kiss they. as he put they down, he rub his rough hand across his eye with a bitter word and laugh. he would have give his whole life and soul to win that prize which Arthur reject. do she want fame? he would have win it for her:--devotion?--a great heart full of pent-up tenderness and manly love and gentleness be there for she, if she might take it. but it might not be. Fate have rule otherwise. "even if I could, she would not have I," George think. "what have an ugly, rough old fellow like I, to make any woman like he? Ibe get old, and Ihave make no mark in life. Ihave neither good look, nor youth, nor money, nor reputation. a man must be able to do something besides stare at she and offer on his knee his smooth devotion, to make a woman like he. what can I do? lot of young fellow have pass I in the race-- what they call the prize of life donot seem to I worth the trouble of the struggle. but for she. if she have be mine and like a diamond--ah! shouldnot she have wear it! Psha, what a fool I be to brag of what I would have do! we be the slave of destiny. our lot be shape for we, and mine be ordain long ago. come, let we have a pipe, and put the smell of these flower out of court, poor little silent flower! youwill be dead to-morrow. what business have you to show your red cheek in this dingy place?"
by his bedside George find a new Bible which the widow have place there, with a note inside say that she have not see the book amongst his collection in a room where she have spend a number of hour, and where God have vouchsafe to her prayer the life of her son, and that she give to Arthur's friend the good thing she could, and besought he to read in the volume sometimes, and to keep it as a token of a grateful mother's regard and affection. poor George mournfully kiss the book as he have do the flower; and the morning find he still read in its awful page, in which so many stricken heart, in which so many tender and faithful soul, have find comfort under calamity, and refuge and hope in affliction.
CHAPTER LV
Fanny's Occupation's go
Good Helen, ever since her son's illness, have take, as we have see, entire possession of the young man, of his drawer and closet and all which they contain: whether shirt that want button, or stocking that require mending, or, must it be own? letter that lie amongst those article of raiment, and which of course it be necessary that somebody should answer during Arthur's weakened and incapable condition. perhaps Mrs. Pendennis be laudably desirous to have some explanation about the dreadful Fanny Bolton mystery, regard which she have never breathe a word to her son, though it be present in her mind always, and occasion her inexpressible anxiety and disquiet. she have cause the brass knocker to be screw off the inner door of the chamber, where upon the postman's startling double rap would, as she justly argue, disturb the rest of her patient, and she do not allow he to see any letter which arrive, whether from bootmaker who importune he, or hatter who have a heavy account to make up against next Saturday, and would be very much oblige if Mr. Arthur Pendennis would have the kindness to settle, etc. of these document, Pen, who be always freehanded and careless, of course have his share, and though no great one, one quite enough to alarm his scrupulous and conscientious mother. she have some saving; Pen's magnificent self-denial, and her own economy, amount from her great simplicity and avoidance of show to parsimony almost, have enable she to put by a little sum of money, a part of which she delightedly consecrate to the pay off the young gentleman's obligation. at this price, many a worthy youth and respected reader would hand over his correspondence to his parent; and perhaps there be no great test of a man's regularity and easiness of conscience, than his readiness to face the postman. blessed be he who be make happy by the sound of the rat-tat! the good be eager for it: but the naughty tremble at the sound thereof. so it be very kind of Mrs. pendenni doubly to spare Pen the trouble of hear or answer letter during his illness.
there could have be nothing in the young man's chest of drawer and wardrobe which could be consider as inculpate he in any way, nor any satisfactory document regard the Fanny Bolton affair find there, for the widow have to ask her brother-in-law if he know anything about the odious transaction, and the dreadful intrigue about which her son be engage. when they be at Richmond one day, and Pen with Warrington have take a seat on a bench on the terrace, the widow keep Major Pendennis in consultation, and lay her terror and perplexity before he, such of they at least (for as be the willnot of man and woman, she do not make quite a clean confession, and I suppose no spendthrift ask for a schedule of his debt, no lady of fashion ask by her husband for her dressmaker's bill, ever send in the whole of they yet)--such, we say, of her perplexity, at least, as she choose to confide to her Director for the time be.
when, then, she ask the Major what course she ought to pursue, about this dreadful--this horrid affair, and whether he know anything regard it? the old gentleman pucker up his face, so that you could not tell whether he be smile or not; give the widow one queer look with his little eye; cast they down to the carpet again, and say, "my dear, good creature, I donot know anything about it; and I donot wish to know anything about it; and, as you ask I my opinion, I think you have well know nothing about it too. young man will be young man; begad, and, my good ma'am, if you think our boy be a jo----"
"pray, spare I this," Helen break in, look very stately.
"my dear creature, I do not commence the conversation, permit I to say," the Major say, bow very blandly.
"I cannot bear to hear such a sin--such a dreadful sin--speak of in such a way," the widow say, with tear of annoyance start from her eye. "I cannot bear to think that my boy should commit such a crime. I wish he have die, almost, before he have do it. I donot know how I survive it myself; for it be break my heart, Major Pendennis, to think that his father's son--my child--whom I remember so good--oh, so good, and full of honour!--should be fall so dreadfully low, as to--as to----"
"as to flirt with a little grisette, my dear creature?" say the Major. "egad, if all the mother in England be to break their heart because-- Nay, nay; upon my word and honour, now, donot agitate yourself--don't cry. I cannot bear to see a woman's tear--I never could--never. but how do we know that anything serious have happen? Has Arthur say anything?"
"his silence confirm it," sob Mrs. Pendennis, behind her pocket-handkerchief.
"not at all. there be subject, my dear, about which a young fellow cannot surely talk to his mamma," insinuate the brother-in-law.
"she have write to he," cry the lady, behind the cambric.
"what, before he be ill? nothing more likely."
"no, since," the mourner with the batiste mask gasp out; not before; that be, I donot think so--that is, I----"
"only since; and you have--yes, I understand. I suppose when he be too ill to read his own correspondence, you take charge of it, do you?"
"I be the most unhappy mother in the world," cry out the unfortunate Helen.
"the most unhappy mother in the world, because your son be a man and not a hermit! have a care, my dear sister. if you have suppress any letter to he, you may have do yourself a great injury; and, if I know anything of Arthur's spirit, may cause a difference between he and you, which youwill rue all your life--a difference thatbe a dev'lish deal more important, my good madam, than the little--little--trumpery cause which originate it."
"there be only one letter," break out helen,--"only a very little one-- only a few word. here it be--oh--how can you, how can you speak so?"
when the good soul say "only a very little one," the Major could not speak at all, so inclined be he to laugh, in spite of the agony of the poor soul before he, and for whom he have a hearty pity and like too. but each be look at the matter with his or her peculiar eye and view of moral, and the Major's moral, as the reader know, be not those of an ascetic.
"I recommend you," he gravely continue, "if you can, to seal it up-- those letter benot unfrequently seal with wafer--and to put it amongst Pen's other letter, and let he have they when he call for they or if wewill cannot seal it, we mistook it for a bill."
"I cannot tell my son a lie," say the widow. it have be put silently into the letter-box two day previous to their departure from the Temple, and have be bring to Mrs. Pendennis by Martha. she have never see Fanny's handwriting, of course; but when the letter be put into her hand she know the author at once. she have be on the watch for that letter every day since Pen have be ill. she have open some of his other letter because she want to get at that one. she have the horrid paper poison her bag at that moment. she take it out and offer it to her brother-in-law.
"Arther Pendennis, Esq.," he read in a timid little sprawl handwriting, and with a sneer on his face. "no, my dear, I willnot read any more. but you who have read it may tell I what the letter contain--only prayer for his health in bad spelling, you say--and a desire to see he? well--therebe no harm in that. and as you ask me--" here the Major begin to look a little queer for his own part, and put on his demure look--"as you ask I, my dear, for information, why, I donot mind tell you that --ah--that--Morgan, my man, have make some inquiry regard this affair, and that--my friend Doctor Goodenough also look into it--and it appear that this person be greatly smite with Arthur; that he pay for she and take she to Vauxhall Gardens, as Morgan hear from an old acquaintance of Pen's and our, an irish gentleman, who be very nearly once have the honour of be the--from an Irishman, in fact;--that the girl's father, a violent man of intoxicated habit, have beat her mother, who persist in declare her daughter's entire innocence to her husband on the one hand, while on the other she tell Goodenough, that Arthur have act like a brute to her child. and so you see the story remain in a mystery. will you have it clear up? I have but to ask Pen, and he will tell I at once--he be as honourable a man as ever live."
"honourable!" say the widow with bitter scorn. "oh, brother, what be this you call honour? if my boy have be guilty, he must marry she. I would go down on my knee and pray he to do so."
"Good God! be you mad?" scream out the Major; and remember former passage in Arthur's history and Helen's, the truth come across his mind that, be Helen to make this prayer to her son, he would marry the girl: he be wild enough and obstinate enough to commit any folly when a woman he love be in the case. "my dear sister, have you lose your sens?" he continue (after an agitated pause, during which the above dreary reflection cross he); and in a softened tone, "what right have we to suppose that anything have pass between this girl and he? letus see the letter. her heart be break; pray, pray, write to I--home unhappy-- unkind father--your nurse--poor little Fanny--spelt, as you say, in a manner to outrage all sense of decorum. but, good heaven! my dear, what be there in this? only that the little devil be make love to he still. why, she donot come into his chamber until he be so delirious that he donot know she. what-d'you-call-'em, Flanagan, the laundress, tell Morgan, my man, so. she come in company of an old fellow, an old Mr. Bows, who come most kindly down to Stillbrook and bring I away--by the way, I leave he in the cab, and never pay the fare; and dev'lish kind it be of he. no, therebe nothing in the story."
"do you think so? thank Heaven--thank God!" Helen cry. "Iwill take the letter to Arthur and ask he now. look at he there. hebe on the terrace with Mr. Warrington. they be talk to some child. my boy be always fond of child. hebe innocent, thank God--thank God! let I go to he."
Old Pendennis have his own opinion. when he briskly take the not guilty side of the case, but a moment before, very likely the old gentleman have a different view from that which he choose to advocate, and judge of Arthur by what he himself would have do. if she go to Arthur, and he speak the truth, as the rascal will, it spoil all, he think. and he try one more effort.
"my dear, good soul," he say, take Helen's hand and kiss it, "as your son have not acquaint you with this affair, think if you have any right to examine it. as you believe he to be a man of honour, what right have you to doubt his honour in this instance? who be his accuser? an anonymous scoundrel who have bring no specific charge against he. if there be any such, wouldnot the girl's parent have come forward? he be not call upon to rebut, nor you to entertain an anonymous accusation; and as for believe he guilty because a girl of that rank happen to be in his room act as nurse to he, begad you might as well insist upon his marrying that dem'd old irish gin-drinking laundress, Mrs. Flanagan."
the widow burst out laugh through her tear--the victory be gain by the old general.

"no. the boy have tell you nothing about it, and you know nothing about it. the boy be innocent--of course. and what, my good soul, be the course for we to pursue? suppose he be attach to this girl--don't look sad again, itbe merely a supposition--and begad a young fellow may have an attachment, maynot he?--directly he get well he will be at she again."
"he must come home! we must go off directly to Fairoaks," the widow cry out.
"my good creature, hewill bore himself to death at Fairoaks. hewill have nothing to do but to think about his passion there. therebe no place in the world for make a little passion into a big one, and where a fellow feed on his own thought, like a dem'd lonely country-house where therebe nothing to do. we must occupy he: amuse he: we must take he abroad: hebe never be abroad except to Paris for a lark. we must travel a little. he must have a nurse with he, to take great care of he, for Goodenough say he have a dev'lish narrow squeak of it (donot look frightened), and so you must come and watch: and I suppose youwill take Miss Bell, and I should like to ask Warrington to come. Arthur's dev'lish fond of Warrington. he cannot do without Warrington. Warrington's family be one of the old in England, and he be one of the good young fellow I ever meet in my life. I like he exceedingly."

"not a word--i--I've ask he about it. Ihave pump he. he never hear of the transaction, never; I pledge you my word," cry out the Major, in some alarm. "and, my dear, I think you have much good not talk to he about it--much good not--of course not: the subject be most delicate and painful."
the simple widow take her brother's hand and press it. "thank you, brother," she say. "you have be very, very kind to I. you have give I a great deal of comfort. have achieve this victory, the fatigued and happy warrior lay himself down on the sofa, and put his yellow silk pocket-handkerchief over his face, and indulge in a snug little nap, of which the dream, no doubt, be very pleasant, as he snore with refreshing regularity. the young man sate, meanwhile, dawdle away the sunshiny hour on the terrace, very happy, and Pen, at least, very talkative. he be narrate to Warrington a plan for a new novel, and a new tragedy. "Hm!" say Lovelace. "she drop her h's, but she be a dear little girl."
o clarissas of this life, o you poor little ignorant vain foolish maiden! if you do but know the way in which the Lovelaces speak of you: if you could but hear Jack talk to Tom across the coffee-room of a Club; or see Ned take your poor little letter out of his cigar-case, and hand they over to Charley, and Billy, and Harry across the messroom table, you would not be so eager to write, or so ready to listen! therebe a sort of crime which be not complete unless the lucky rogue boast of it afterwards; and the man who betray your honour in the first place, be pretty sure, remember that, to betray your secret too.

could Helen have suspect that, with Pen's return strength, his unhappy partiality for little Fanny would also reawaken? though she never speak a word regard that young person, after her conversation with the Major, and though, to all appearance, she utterly ignore Fanny's existence, yet Mrs. Pendennis keep a particularly close watch upon all Master Arthur's action; on the plea of ill-health would scarcely let he out of her sight; and be especially anxious that he should be spar the trouble of all correspondence for the present at least. very likely Arthur look at his own letter with some tremor; very likely, as he receive they at the family table, feel his mother's watch upon he (though the good soul's eye seem fix upon her teacup or her book), he expect daily to see a little handwriting, which he would have know, though he have never see it yet, and his heart beat as he receive the letter to his address. be he more pleased or annoyed, that, day after day, his expectation be not realise; and be his mind relieve, that there come no letter from Fanny? though, no doubt, in these matter, when Lovelace be tired of Clarissa (or the contrary) it be good for both party to break at once, and each, after the failure of the attempt at union, to go his own way, and pursue his course through life solitary; yet our self-love, or our pity, or our sense of decency, do not like that sudden bankruptcy. before we announce to the world that our firm of Lovelace and Co. cannot meet its engagement, we try to make compromise: we have mournful meeting of partner: we delay the putting up of the shutter, and the dreary announcement of the failure. it must come: but we pawn our jewel to keep thing go a little long. on the whole, I dare say, Pen be rather annoyed that he have no remonstrance from Fanny. what! could she part from he, and never so much as once look round? could she sink, and never once hold a little hand out, or cry, "Help, Arthur?" well, well: they donot all go down who venture on that voyage. some few drown when the vessel founder; but most be only duck, and scramble to shore. and the reader's experience of a. Pendennis, Esquire, of the Upper Temple, will enable he to state whether that gentleman belong to the class of person who be likely to sink or to swim.
though Pen be as yet too weak to walk half a mile; and might not, on account of his precious health, be trust to take a drive in a carriage by himself, and without a nurse in attendance; yet Helen could not keep watch over Mr. Warrington too, and have no authority to prevent that gentleman from go to London if business call he thither. indeed, if he have go and stay, perhaps the widow, from reason of her own, would have be glad; but she check these selfish wish as soon as she ascertain or own they; and, remember Warrington's great regard and service, and constant friendship for her boy, receive he as a member of her family almost, with her usual melancholy kindness and submissive acquiescence. yet somehow, one morning when his affair call he to town, she divine what Warrington's errand be, and that he be go to London to get news about Fanny for Pen.

we have show how Mr. Huxter, in write home to his Clavering friend, mention that there be a fashionable club in London of which he be an attendant, and that he be there in the habit of meet an irish officer of distinction, who, amongst other news, have give that intelligence regard Pendennis, which the young surgeon have transmit to Clavering. this club be no other than the Back Kitchen, where the disciple of Saint Bartholomew be accustom to meet the General, the peculiarity of whose brogue, appearance, disposition, and general conversation, greatly divert many young gentleman who use the Back Kitchen as a place of nightly entertainment and refreshment. Huxter, who have a fine natural genius for mimic everything, whether it be a favourite tragic or comic actor, or a cock on a dunghill, a corkscrew go into a bottle and a cork issue thence, or an irish officer of genteel connexion who offer himself as an object of imitation with only too much readiness, talk his talk, and twang his poor old long bow whenever drink, a hearer, and an opportunity occur, study our friend the General with peculiar gusto, and draw the honest fellow out many a night. a bait, consist of sixpennyworth of brandy-and-water, the worthy old man be sure to swallow: and under the influence of this liquor, who be more happy than he to tell his story of his daughter's triumph and his own, in love, war, drink, and polite society? thus Huxter be enable to present to his friend many picture of Costigan: of Costigan fight a jewel in the Phaynix--of Costigan and his interview with the Juke of York--of Costigan at his sonunlaw's teeble, surround by the nobilitee of his countree--of Costigan, when cry drunk, at which time he be in the habit of confidentially lament his daughter's ingratichewd, and state that his grey hair be hasten to a praymachure greeve. and thus our friend be the mean of bring a number of young fellow to the Back Kitchen, who consume the landlord's liquor whilst they relish the General's peculiarity, so that mine host pardon many of the latter's foible, in consideration of the good which they bring to his house. not the high position in life be this--certainly, or one which, if we have a reverence for an old man, we would be anxious that he should occupy: but of this aged buffoon it may be mention that he have no particular idea that his condition of life be not a high one, and that in his whiskie blood there be not a black drop, nor in his muddled brain a bitter feeling, against any mortal being. even his child, his cruel Emily, he would have take to his heart and forgive with tear; and what more can one say of the christian charity of a man than that he be actually ready to forgive those who have do he every kindness, and with whom he be wrong in a dispute!
there be some idea amongst the young man who frequent the Back Kitchen, and make themselves merry with the society of Captain Costigan, that the Captain make a mystery regard his lodging for fear of dun, or from a desire of privacy, and live in some wonderful place. nor would the landlord of the premise, when question upon this subject, answer any inquiry; his maxim be that he only know gentleman who frequent that room, in that room; that when they quit that room, have pay their score as gentleman, and behave as gentleman, his communication with they cease; and that, as a gentleman himself, he think it be only impertinent curiosity to ask where any other gentleman live. Costigan, in his most intoxicated and confidential moment, also evade any reply to question or hint address to he on this subject: there be no particular secret about it, as we have see, who have have more than once the honour of enter his apartment, but in the vicissitude of a long life he have be pretty often in the habit of reside in house where privacy be necessary to his comfort, and where the appearance of some visitor would have bring he anything but pleasure. hence all sort of legend be form by wag or credulous person respect his place of abode. it be state that he sleep habitually in a watch-box in the city: in a cab at a mews, where a cab-proprietor give he a shelter: in the Duke of York's Column etc, the wild of these theory be put abroad by the facetious and imaginative Huxter. for Huxey, when not silence by the company of "swell," and when in the society of his own friend, be a very different fellow to the youth whom we have see cow by Pen's impertinent air, and, adore by his family at home, be the life and soul of the circle whom he meet, either round the festive board or the dissect table. on one brilliant September morning, as Huxter be regale himself with a cup of coffee at a stall in Covent Garden, have spend a delicious night dance at Vauxhall, he spy the General reel down Henrietta Street, with a crowd of hoot blackguard boy at his heel, who have leave their bed under the arch of the river betime, and be prowl about already for breakfast, and the strange livelihood of the day. the poor old General be not in that condition when the sneer and joke of these young beggar have much effect upon he: the cabman and waterman at the cabstand know he and pass their comment upon he: the policeman gaze after he and warn the boy off he, with look of scorn and pity; what do the scorn and pity of man, the joke of ribald child, matter to the General? he reel along the street with glaze eye, have just sense enough to know whither he be bind, and to pursue his accustomed beat homeward. he go to bed not know how he have reach it, as often as any man in London. he wake and find himself there, and ask no question, and he be tack about on this daily though perilous voyage, when, from his station at the coffee-stall, Huxter spy he. to note his friend, to pay his twopence (indeed, he have but eightpence leave, or he would have have a cab from Vauxhall to take he home), be with the eager Huxter the work of an instant--Costigan dive down the alley by Drury Lane Theatre, where gin-shop, oyster-shop, and theatrical wardrobe abound, the proprietor of which be now asleep behind their shutter, as the pink morning light up their chimney; and through these court Huxter follow the General, until he reach Oldcastle Street, in which be the gate of Shepherd's Inn.
here, just as he be within sight of home, a luckless slice of orange-peel come between the General's heel and the pavement, and cause the poor old fellow to fall backwards.
Huxter run up to he instantly, and after a pause, during which the veteran, giddy with his fall and his previous whisky, gather, as he well might, his dizzy brain together, the young surgeon lift up the limping General, and very kindly and good-naturedly offer to conduct he to his home. for some time, and in reply to the query which the student of medicine put to he, the muzzy General refuse to say where his lodging be and declare that they be hard by, and that he could reach they without difficulty; and he disengage himself from Huxter's arm, and make a rush as if to get to his own home unattended: but he reel and lurch so, that the young surgeon insist upon accompany he, and, with many soothe expression and cheering and consolatory phrase, succeed in get the General's dirty old hand under what he call his own fin, and lead the old fellow, moan piteously, across the street. he stop when he come to the ancient gate, ornament with the armorial bearing of the venerable shepherd. "here 'tis," say he, draw up at the portal, and he make a successful pull at the gate bell, which presently bring out old Mr. Bolton, the porter, scowl fiercely, and grumble as he be use to do every morning when it become his turn to let in that early bird.
Costigan try to hold Bolton for a moment in genteel conversation, but the other surlily would not. "donot bother I," say he; "go to your hown bed Capting, and donot keep honest man out of theirs." so the Captain tack across the square and reach his own staircase, up which he stumble with the worthy Huxter at his heel. Costigan have a key of his own, which Huxter insert into the keyhole for he, so that there be no need to call up little Mr. bow from the sleep into which the old musician have not long since fall, and Huxter have aid to disrobe his tipsy patient, and ascertain that no bone be break, help he to bed and apply compress an water to one of his knee and shin, which, with the pair of trouser which encase they, Costigan have severely tear in his fall. at the General's age, and with his habit of body, such wound as he have inflict on himself be slow to heal: a good deal of inflammation ensue, and the old fellow lie ill for some day, suffer both pain and fever.
Mr. Huxter undertake the case of his interesting patient with great confidence and alacrity, and conduct it with become skill. he visit his friend day after day, and console he with lively rattle and conversation for the absence of the society which Costigan need, and of which he be an ornament; and he give special instruction to the invalid's nurse about the quantity of whisky which the patient be to take--instruction which, as the poor old fellow could not for many day get out of his bed or sofa himself, he could not by any mean infringe. bow, Mrs. Bolton, and our little friend Fanny, when able to do so, officiate at the General's bedside, and the old warrior be make as comfortable as possible under his calamity.
thus Huxter, whose affable manner and social turn make he quickly intimate with person in whose society he fall, and whose over-refinement do not lead they to repulse the familiarity of this young gentleman, become pretty soon intimate in Shepherd's Inn, both with our acquaintance in the garret and those in the porter's lodge. he think he have see Fanny somewhere: he feel certain that he have: but it be no wonder that he should not accurately remember she, for the poor little thing never choose to tell he where she have meet he: he himself have see she at a period, when his own view both of person and of right and wrong be cloud by the excitement of drinking and dancing, and also little Fanny be very much change and wear by the fever and agitation, and passion and despair, which the past three week have pour upon the head of that little victim. Borne down be the head now, and very pale and wan the face; and many and many a time the sad eye have look into the postman's, as he come to the Inn, and the sickened heart have sink as he pass away. when Mr. Costigan's accident occur, Fanny be rather glad to have an opportunity of be useful and do something kind-- something that would make she forget her own little sorrow perhaps: she feel she bear they well whilst she do her duty, though I dare say many a tear drop into the old Irishman's gruel. ah, I! stir the gruel well, and have courage, little Fanny! if everybody who have suffer from your complaint be to die of it straightway, what a fine year the undertaker would have!
whether from compassion for his only patient, or delight in his society, Mr. Huxter find now occasion to visit Costigan two or three time in the day at least, and if any of the member of the porter's lodge family be not in attendance on the General, the young doctor be sure to have some particular direction to address to those at their own place of habitation. he be a kind fellow; he make or purchase toy for the child; he bring they apple and brandy-ball; he bring a mask and frighten they with it, and cause a smile upon the face of pale Fanny. he call Mrs. Bolton Mrs. B., and be very intimate, familiar, and facetious with that lady, quite different from that "aughty, artless beast," as Mrs. Bolton now denominate a certain young gentleman of our acquaintance, and whom she now vow she never could abear.
it be from this lady, who be very free in her conversation, that Huxter presently learn what be the illness which be evidently prey upon little Fan, and what have be Pen's behaviour regard she. Mrs. Bolton's account of the transaction be not, it may be imagine, entirely an impartial narrative. one would have think from her story that the young gentleman have employ a course of the most persevering and flagitious artifice to win the girl's heart, have break the most solemn promise make to she and be a wretch to be hat and chastise by every champion of woman. Huxter, in his present frame of mind respect Arthur, and suffer under the latter's contumely, be ready, of course, to take all for grant that be say in the disfavour of this unfortunate convalescent. but why do he not write home to Clavering, as he have do previously, give an account of Pen's misconduct, and of the particular regard it, which have now come to his knowledge? he soon, in a letter to his brother-in-law, announce that that nice young man, Mr. Pendennis, have escape narrowly from a fever, and that no doubt all Clavering, where he be so popular, would be pleased at his recovery; and he mention that he have an interesting case of compound fracture, an officer of distinction, which keep he in town; but as for Fanny Bolton, he make no more mention of she in his letter--no more than Pen himself have make mention of she. o you mother at home, how much do you think you know about your lad? how much do you think you know?
but with bow, there be no reason why Huxter should not speak his mind, and so, a very short time after his conversation with Mrs. Bolton, Mr. Sam talk to the musician about his early acquaintance with Pendennis; describe he as a confound conceited blackguard, and express a determination to punch his impudent head as soon as ever he should be well enough to stand up like a man.
then it be that bow on his part speak and tell his version of the story, whereof Arthur and little Fan be the hero and heroine; how they have meet by no contrivance of the former, but by a blunder of the old Irishman, now in bed with a broken shin--how Pen have act with manliness and self-control in the business--how Mrs Bolton be an idiot; and he relate the conversation which he, Bows, have have with Pen, and the sentiment utter by the young man. perhaps Bow's story cause some twinge of conscience in the breast of Pen's accuser, and that gentleman frankly own that he have be wrong with regard to Arthur, and withdraw his project for punch Mr. Pendennis's head.
but the cessation of his hostility for Pen do not diminish Huxter's attention to Fanny, which unlucky Mr Bows mark with his usual jealousy and bitterness of spirit, "I have but to like anybody" the old fellow thought, "and somebody be sure to come and be prefer to I. it have be the same ill-luck with I since I be a lad, until now that I be sixty year old. what can such a man as I be expect well than to be laugh at? it be for the young to succeed, and to be happy, and not for old fool like I. Ihave play a second fiddle through life," he say, with a bitter laugh; "how can I suppose the luck be to change after it have go against I so long?" this be the selfish way in which bow look at the state of affair: though few person would have think there be any cause for his jealousy, who look at the pale and grief-stricken countenance of the hapless little girl, its object. Fanny receive Huxter's good-natured effort at consolation and kind attention kindly. she laugh now and again at his joke and game with her little sister, but relapse quickly into a dejection which ought to have satisfy Mr. Bows that the new-comer have no place in her heart as yet, have jealous Mr. Bows be enable to see with clear eye.
but bow do not. Fanny attribute Pen's silence somehow to Bows's interference. Fanny hat he. Fanny treat bow with constant cruelty and injustice. she turn from he when he speak--she loathe his attempt at consolation. a hard life have Mr. Bows, and a cruel return for his regard.
when Warrington come to Shepherd's Inn as Pen's ambassador, it be for Mr. Bows's apartment he inquire (no doubt upon a previous agreement with the principal for whom he act in this delicate negotiation), and he do not so much as catch a glimpse of Miss Fanny when he stop at the Inn-gate and make his inquiry. Warrington be, of course, direct to the musician's chamber, and find he tend the patient there, from whose chamber he come out to wait upon his guest. we have say that they have be previously know to one another, and the pair shake hand with sufficient cordiality. after a little preliminary talk, Warrington say that he have come from his friend Arthur Pendennis, and from his family, to thank bow for his attention at the commencement of Pen's illness, and for his kindness in hastening into the country to fetch the Major.
bow reply that it be but his duty: he have never think to have see the young gentleman alive again when he go in search of Pen's relative, and he be very glad of Mr. Pendennis's recovery, and that he have his friend with he. "Lucky be they who have friend, Mr. Warrington," say the musician. "I might be up in this garret and nobody would care for I, or mind whether I be alive or dead."
"what! not the General, Mr. Bows?" Warrington ask.
