"the General like his whisky-bottle more than anything in life," the other answer; "we live together from habit and convenience; and he care for I no more than you do. what be it you want to ask I, Mr. Warrington? you benot come to visit I, I know very well. nobody come to visit I. it be about Fanny, the porter's daughter, you be come--I see that--very well. be Mr. Pendennis, now he have get well, anxious to see she again? do his lordship the Sultan propose to throw his 'andkerchief to she? she have be very ill, sir, ever since the day when Mrs. Pendennis turn she out of door--kind of a lady, benot it? the poor girl and myself find the young gentleman rave in a fever, know nobody, with nobody to tend he but his drunken laundress--she watch day and night by he. I set off to fetch his uncle. Mamma come and turn Fanny to the right-about. Uncle come and leave I to pay the cab. carry my compliment to the lady and gentleman, and say we be both very thankful, very. why, a countess couldnot have behave well, and for an apothecary's lady, as Ibe give to understand Mrs. Pendennis be--i'm sure her behaviour be most uncommon aristocratic and genteel. she ought to have a double-gilt pestle and mortar to her coach."
it be from Mr. Huxter that Bows have learn Pen's parentage, no doubt, and if he take Pen's part against the young surgeon, and Fannybe against Mr. Pendennis, it be because the old gentleman be in so savage a mood, that his humour be to contradict everybody.
Warrington be curious, and not ill pleased at the musician's taunt and irascibility. "I never hear of these transaction," he say, "or get but a very imperfect account of they from major Pendennis. what be a lady to do? I think (I have never speak with she on the subject) she have some notion that the young woman and my friend Pen be on--on term of--of an intimacy which Mrs. Pendennis could not, of course, recognise----"
"oh, of course not, sir. speak out, sir; say what you mean at once, that the young gentleman of the Temple have make a victim of the girl of Shepherd's Inn, eh? and so she be turn to be out of door--or bray alive in the double-gilt pestle and mortar, by Jove! no, Mr. Warrington, there be no such thing: there be no victimising, or if there be, Mr. Arthur be the victim, not the girl. he be an honest fellow, he be, though he be conceited, and a puppy sometimes. he can feel like a man, and run away from temptation like a man. I own it, though I suffer by it, I own it. he have a heart, he have: but the girl havenot, sir. that girl will do anything to win a man, and fle he away without a pang, sir. if shebe fling away herself, sir, shewill feel it and cry. she have a fever when Mrs. Pendennis turn she out of door; and she make love to the Doctor, Doctor Goodenough, who come to cure she. now she have take on with another chap--another sawbone, ha, ha! d--- it, sir, she like the pestle and mortar, and hang round the pill-box, shebe so fond of them, and she have get a fellow from Saint Bartholomew's, who grin through a horse-collar for her sister, and charm away her melancholy. go and see, sir: very likely hebe in the lodge now. if you want news about Miss Fanny, you must ask at the Doctor's shop, sir, not of an old fiddler like I--good-bye, sir. therebe my patient calling."
and a voice be hear from the Captain's bedroom, a well-know voice, which say, "Iwould loike a dthrop of dthrink, Bows, Ibe thirstee." and not sorry, perhaps, to hear that such be the state of thing, and that Pen's forsaken be console herself, Warrington take his leave of the irascible musician.
as luck would have it, he pass the lodge door just as Mr. Huxter be in the act of frighten the child with the mask whereof we have speak, and Fanny be smile languidly at his farce. Warrington laugh bitterly. "be all woman like that?" he think. "I think therebe one thatbe not," he add, with a sigh.
at Piccadilly, wait for the Richmond omnibus, George fall in with Major Pendennis, bind in the same direction, and he tell the old gentleman of what he have see and hear respect Fanny.
Major Pendennis be highly delighted: and as might be expect of such a philosopher, make precisely the same observation as that which have escape from Warrington. "all woman be the same," he say. "La petite se console. Daymy, when I use to read 'Telemaque' at school, Calypso ne pouvait se consoler,--you know the rest, Warrington,--I use to say it be absard. absard, by Gad, and so it be. and so shebe get a new soupirant, have she, the little porteress? dayvlish nice little girl. how mad Pen will be--eh, Warrington? but we must break it to he gently, or hewill be in such a rage that he will be go after she again. we must menager the young fellow."
"I think Mrs. Pendennis ought to know that Pen act very well in the business. she evidently think he guilty, and accord to Mr. Bows, Arthur behave like a good fellow," Warrington say.
"my dear Warrington," say the Major, with a look of some alarm, "in Mrs. Pendennis's agitated state of health and that sort of thing, the good way, I think, be not to say a single word about the subject--or, stay, leave it to I: and Iwill talk to she--break it to she gently, you know, and that sort of thing. I give you my word I will. and so Calypso's console, be she," and he snigger over this gratifying truth, happy in the corner of the omnibus during the rest of the journey.
Pen be very anxious to hear from his envoy what have be the result of the latter's mission; and as soon as the two young man could be alone, the ambassador speak in reply to Arthur's eager query.
"you remember your poem, Pen, of Ariadne in Naxos," Warrington say; "devilish bad poetry it be, to be sure."
"apre?" ask Pen, in a great state of excitement.
"when Theseus leave Ariadne, do you remember what happen to she, young fellow?"
"itbe a lie, itbe a lie! you donot mean that!" cry out Pen, start up, his face turn red.
"sit down, stoopid," Warrington say, and with two finger push Pen back into his seat again. "itbe well for you as it be, young one," he say sadly, in reply to the savage flush in Arthur's face.
CHAPTER LVII
Foreign Ground
Worthy Major Pendennis fulfil his promise to Warrington so far as to satisfy his own conscience, and in so far to ease poor Helen with regard to her son, as to make she understand that all connexion between Arthur and the odious little gatekeeper be at an end, and that she need have no further anxiety with respect to an imprudent attachment or a degrading marriage on Pen's part. and that young fellow's mind be also relieve (after he have recover the shock to his vanity) by think that Miss Fanny be not go to die of love for he, and that no unpleasant consequence be to be apprehend from the luckless and brief connexion.
so the whole party be free to carry into effect their project Continental trip, and Arthur Pendennis, renti, voyageant avec Madame Pendennis and Mademoiselle Bell, and George Warrington, particuli, age de 32 an, taille 6 pied (Anglais), figure ordinaire, cheveux noir, barbe idem, etc., procure passport from the consul of H.M. the King of the Belgians at Dover, and pass over from that port to Ostend, whence the party take their way leisurely, visit bruge and Ghent on their way to Brussels and the Rhine. it be not our purpose to describe this oft-travelled tour, or Laura's delight at the tranquil and ancient city which she see for the first time, or Helen's wonder and interest at the Beguine convent which they visit, or the almost terror with which she see the black-veil nun with outstretche arm kneeling before the illuminate altar, and behold the strange pomp and ceremonial of the catholic worship. barefoote friar in the street; crown image of saint and Virgins in the church before which people be bow down and worship, in direct defiance, as she hold, of the write law; priest in gorgeous robe, or lurk in dark confessional; theatre open, and people dance on sundays,--all these new sight and manner shock and bewilder the simple country lady; and when the young man after their evening drive or walk return to the widow and her adopt daughter, they find their book of devotion on the table, and at their entrance Laura would commonly cease read some of the psalm or the sacred page which, of all other, Helen love. the late event connect with her son have cruelly shake she; Laura watch with intense, though hide anxiety, every movement of her dear friend; and poor Pen be most constant and affectionate in wait upon his mother, whose wound bosom yearn with love towards he, though there be a secret between they, and an anguish or rage almost on the mother's part, to think that she be dispossess somehow of her son's heart, or that there be recess in it which she must not or dare not enter. she sicken as she think of the sacred day of boyhood when it have not be so--when her Arthur's heart have no secret, and she be his all in all: when he pour his hope and pleasure, his childish grief, vanity, triumph into her willing and tender embrace; when her home be his nest still; and before fate, selfishness, nature, have drive he forth on wayward wing--to range his own flight--to sing his own song--and to seek his own home and his own mate. watch this devouring care and rack disappointment in her friend, Laura once say to Helen, "if Pen have love I as you wish, I should have gain he, but I should have lose you, mamma, I know I should; and I like you to love I well. man do not know what it be to love as we do, I think,"--and Helen, sighing, agree to this portion of the young lady's speech, though she protest against the former part. for my part I suppose Miss Laura be right in both statement, and with regard to the latter assertion especially, that it be an old and receive truism--love be an hour with we: it be all night and all day with a woman. Damon have taxis, sermon, parade, tailor' bill, parliamentary duty, and the deuce know what to think of; Delia have to think about Damon--Damon be the oak (or the post) and stand up, and Delia be the ivy or the honeysuckle whose arm twine about he. be it not so, Delia? be it not your nature to creep about his foot and kiss they, to twine round his trunk and hang there; and Damon's to stand like a british man with his hand in his breech pocket, while the pretty fond parasite cling round he?
Old Pendennis have only accompany our friend to the water's edge, and leave they on board the boat, give the chief charge of the little expedition to Warrington. he himself be bind on a brief visit to the house of a great man, a friend of his, after which sojourn he propose to join his sister-in-law at the german watering-place, whither the party be bind. the Major himself think that his long attention to his sick family have earn for he a little relaxation--and though the good of the partridge be thin off, the pheasant be still to be shoot at Stillbrook, where the noble owner still be; old Pendennis betook himself to that hospitable mansion and disport there with great comfort to himself. a royal Duke, some foreigner of note, some illustrious statesman, and some pleasant people visit it: it do the old fellow's heart good to see his name in the Morning Post amongst the list of the distinguished company which the Marquis of Steyne be entertaining at his country-house at Stillbrook. he be a very useful and pleasant personage in a country-house. he entertain the young man with queer little anecdote and grivoise story on their shooting-party or in their smoking-room, where they laugh at he and with he. he be obsequious with the lady of a morning, in the room dedicate to they. he walk the new arrival about the park and garden, and show they the carte du pay, and where there be the good view of the mansion, and where the most favourable point to look at the lake: he show, where the timber be to be fell, and where the old road go before the new bridge be build, and the hill cut down; and where the place in the wood be where old Lord Lynx discover Sir Phelim O'Neal on his knee before her ladyship, etc. etc.; he call the lodge-keeper and gardener by their name; he know the number of domestic that sit down in the housekeeper's room, and how many din in the servants'-hall; he have a word for everybody, and about everybody, and a little against everybody. he be invaluable in a country-house, in a word: and richly merit and enjoy his vacation after his labour. and perhaps whilst he be thus deservedly enjoy himself with his country friend, the Major be not ill pleased at transfer to Warrington the command of the family expedition to the continent, and thus perforce keep he in the service of the ladies,--a servitude which George be only too willing to undergo, for his friend's sake, and for that of a society which he find daily more delightful. Warrington be a good german scholar, and be willing to give Miss Laura lesson in the language, who be very glad to improve herself, though Pen, for his part, be too weak or lazy now to resume his german study. Warrington act as courier and interpreter; Warrington see the baggage in and out of ship, inn and carriage, manage the money matter, and put the little troop into marching order. Warrington find out where the english church be, and, if Mrs. Pendennis and Miss Laura be incline to go thither, walk with great decorum along with they. Warrington walk by Mrs. Pendennis's donkey, when that lady go out on her evening excursion; or take carriage for she; or get 'Galignani' for she; or devise comfortable seat under the lime-tree for she, when the guest parade after dinner, and the Kursaal band at the bath, where our tired friend stop, perform their pleasant music under the tree. many a fine whisker prussian or french dandy, come to the bath for the 'Trente-et-quarante,' cast glance of longing towards the pretty fresh-coloured english girl who accompany the pale widow, and would have long to take a turn with she at the galop or the waltz. but Laura do not appear in the ballroom, except once or twice, when Pen vouchsafe to walk with she; and as for Warrington, that rough diamond have not have the polish of a dancing-master, and he do not know how to waltz,--though he would have like to learn, if he could have have such a partner as laura.--such a partner! psha, what have a stiff bachelor to do with partner and waltzing? what be he about, dance attendance here? drink in sweet pleasure at a risk he know not of what after-sadness, and regret, and lonely longing? but yet he stay on. you would have say he be the widow's son, to watch his constant care and watchfulness of she; or that he be an adventurer, and want to marry her fortune, or, at any rate, that he want some very great treasure or benefit from her,--and very likely he did,--for ours, as the reader have possibly already discover, be a Selfish Story, and almost every person, accord to his nature, more or less generous than George, and accord to the way of the world as it seem to we, be occupy about Number one. so Warrington selfishly devote himself to Helen, who selfishly devote herself to Pen, who selfishly devote himself to himself at this present period, have no other personage or object to occupy he, except, indeed, his mother's health, which give he a serious and real disquiet; but though they, sate together, they do not talk much, and the cloud be always between they.
every day Laura look for Warrington, and receive he with more frank and eager welcome. he find himself talk to she as he donot know himself that he could talk. he find himself perform act of gallantry which astound he after the performance: he find himself look blankly in the glass at the crow's foot round his eye, and at some streak of white in his hair, and some intrusive silver bristle in his grim, blue beard. he find himself look at the young buck at the bath--at the bland, tight-waiste Germans--at the capering Frenchmen, with their lacquered mustachio and trim varnish boot--at the english dandy, Pen amongst they, with their calm domineering air, and insolent languor: and envy each one of these some excellence or quality of youth, or good look, which he possess, and of which Warrington feel the need. and every night, as the night come, he quit the little circle with great reluctance; and, retire to his own lodging in their neighbourhood, feel himself the more lonely and unhappy. the widow could not help see his attachment. she understand, now, why Major Pendennis (always a tacit enemy of her darling project) have be so eager that Warrington should be of their party. Laura frankly own her great, her enthusiastic, regard for he: and Arthur would make no movement. Arthur do not choose to see what be go on; or do not care to prevent, or actually encourage, it. she remember his often have say that he could not understand how a man propose to a woman twice. she be in torture--at secret feud with her son, of all object in the world the dear to she--in doubt, which she dare not express to herself, about Laura--averse to Warrington, the good and generous. no wonder that the healing water of Rosenbad do not do she good, or that Doctor von Glauber, the bath physician, when he come to visit she, find that the poor lady make no progress to recovery. meanwhile Pen get well rapidly; sleep with immense perseverance twelve hour out of the twenty-four; eat huge meal; and, at the end of a couple of month, have almost get back the bodily strength and weight which he have possess before his illness.
after they have pass some fifteen day at their place of rest and refreshment, a letter come from Major Pendennis announce his speedy arrival at Rosenbad, and, soon after the letter, the Major himself make his appearance accompany by Morgan his faithful valet, without whom the old gentleman could not move. when the Major travel he wear a jaunty and juvenile travel costume; to see his back still you would have take he for one of the young fellow whose slim waist and youthful appearance Warrington be begin to envy. it be not until the worthy man begin to move, that the observer remark that Time have weaken his ancient knee, and have unkindly interfere to impede the action of the natty little varnish boot in which the gay old traveller still pinch his toe. there be magnate both of our own country and of foreign nation present that autumn at Rosenbad. the elder Pendennis read over the stranger' list with great gratification on the night of his arrival, be pleased to find several of his acquaintance among the great folk, and would have the honour of present his nephew to a german Grand Duchess, a russian Princess, and an English Marquis, before many day be over: nor be Pen by any mean averse to make the acquaintance of these great personage, have a liking for polite life, and all the splendour and amenity belong to it. that very evening the resolute old gentleman, lean on his nephew's arm, make his appearance in the hall of the Kursaal, and lose or win a napoleon or two at the table of 'Trente-et-quarante.' he do not play to lose, he say, or to win, but he do as other folk do, and bet his napoleon and take his luck as it come. he point out the Russians and Spaniards gamble for heap of gold, and denounce their eagerness as something sordid and barbarous; an english gentleman should play where the fashion be play, but should not elate or depress himself at the sport; and he tell how he have see his friend the Marquis of Steyne, when Lord Gaunt, lose eighteen thousand at a sitting, and break the bank three night run at Paris, without ever show the least emotion at his defeat or victory. "and thatbe what I call be an english gentleman, Pen, my dear boy," the old gentleman say, warm as he prattle about his recollections--"what I call the great manner only remain with we and with a few family in France." and as Russian Princesses pass he, whose reputation have long cease to be doubtful, and damage english lady, who be constantly see in company of their faithful attendant for the time be in these gay haunt of dissipation, the old Major, with eager garrulity and mischievous relish, tell his nephew wonderful particular regard the life of these heroine; and divert the young man with a thousand scandal. egad, he feel himself quite young again, he remark to Pen, as, rouge and grinning, her enormous chasseur behind her bear her shawl, the Princess Obstropski smile and recognise and accost he. he remember she in '14 when she be an actress of the Paris Boulevard, and the Emperor Alexander's aide-de-camp Obstropski (a man of great talent, who know a good deal about the Emperor Paul's death, and be a devil to play) marry she. he most courteously and respectfully ask leave to call upon the Princess, and to present to her his nephew, Mr. Arthur Pendennis; and he point out to the latter a half-dozen of other personage whose name be as famous, and whose history be as satisfying. what would poor Helen have think, could she have hear those tale, or know to what kind of people her brother-in-law be present her son? only once, lean on Arthur's arm, she have pass through the room where the green table be prepare for play, and the croak croupier be call out their fatal word of Rouge gagne and Couleur perd. she have shrink terrify out of the pandemonium, implore Pen, extort from he a promise, on his word of honour, that he would never play at those table; and the scene which so frighten the simple widow, only amuse the worldly old veteran, and make he young again! he could breathe the air cheerfully which stifle she. her right be not his right: his food be her poison. human creature be constitute thus differently, and with this variety the marvellous world be people. to the credit of Mr. Pen, let it be say, that he keep honestly the promise make to his mother, and stoutly tell his uncle of his intention to abide by it.
when the Major arrive, his presence somehow cast a damp upon at least three of the person of our little party--upon Laura who have anything but respect for he; upon Warrington, whose manner towards he show an involuntary haughtiness and contempt; and upon the timid and alarmed widow, who dread lest he should interfere with her darling, though almost desperate, project for her boy. and, indeed, the Major, unknown to himself, be the bearer of tiding which be to bring about a catastrophe in the affair of all our friend.
Pen with his two lady have apartment in the town of Rosenbad; honest Warrington have lodging hard by; the Major, on arrival at Rosenbad, have, as befit his dignity, take his quarter at one of the great hotel, at the Roman Emperor or the Four Seasons, where two or three hundred gambler, pleasure-seeker, or invalid, sate down and over-eat themselves daily at the enormous table-d'hote. to this hotel Pen go on the morning after the Major's arrival, dutifully to pay his respect to his uncle, and find the latter's sit-room duly prepare and arrange by Mr. Morgan, with the Major's hat brush, and his coat lay out: his despatch-box and umbrella-case, his guidebook, passport, map, and other elaborate necessary of the english traveller, all as trim and ready as they could be in their master's own room in Jermyn Street. everything be ready, from the medicine-bottle fresh fill from the pharmacien's, down to the old fellow's prayer-book, without which he never travel, for he make a point of appear at the english church at every place which he honour with a stay "everybody do it," he say; "every english gentleman do it," and this pious man would as soon have think of not call upon the english ambassador in a Continental town, as of not show himself at the national place of worship.
the old gentleman have be to take one of the bath for which Rosenbad be famous, and which everybody take, and his after-bath toilet be not yet complete when Pen arrive. the elder call out to Arthur in a cheery voice from the inner apartment, in which he and Morgan be engage, and the valet presently come in, bear a little packet to Pen's address-- Mr. Arthur's letter and paper, Morgan say, which he have bring from Mr. Arthur's chamber in London, and which consist chiefly of number of the Pall Mall Gazette, which our friend Mr. Finucane think his collaborateur would like to see. the paper be tie together: the letter in an envelope, address to Pen, in the last-name gentleman's handwriting.
amongst the letter there be a little note address, as a former letter we have hear of have be, to "Arther Pendennis, Esquire," which Arthur open with a start and a blush, and read with a very keen pang of interest, and sorrow, and regard. she have come to Arthur's house, Fanny Bolton say--and find that he be go--go away to Germany without ever leave a word for she--or answer to her last letter, in which she pray but for one word of kindness--or the book which he have promise she in happy time, before he be ill, and which she should like to keep in remembrance of he. she say she would not reproach those who have find she at his bedside when he be in the fever, and know nobody, and who have turn the poor girl away without a word. she think she should have die, she say, of that, but Doctor Goodenough have kindly tend she, and keep her life, when, perhaps, the keeping of it be of no good, and she forgave everybody and as for Arthur, she would pray for he for ever. and when he be so ill, and they cut off his hair, she have make so free as to keep one little lock for herself, and that she own. and might she still keep it, or would his mamma order that that should be give up too? she be willing to obey he in all thing, and couldnot but remember that once he be so kind, oh! so good and kind! to his poor Fanny.
when Major Pendennis, fresh and smirk from his toilet, come out of his bedroom to his sitting-room, he find Arthur, with this note before he, and an expression of savage anger on his face, which surprise the eld gentleman. "what news from London, my boy?" he rather faintly ask; "be the dun at you that you look so glum?"
"do you know anything about this letter, sir?" Arthur ask.
"what letter, my good sir?" say the other dryly, at once perceive what have happen.
"you know what I mean--about, about Miss--about Fanny Bolton--the poor dear little girl," Arthur break out. "when she be in my room? be she there when I be delirious--I fancy she be--be she? who send she out of my chamber? who intercept her letter to I? who dare to do it? do you do it, uncle?"
"itbe not my practice to tamper with gentleman's letter, or to answer damned impertinent question," Major Pendennis cry out, in a great tremor of emotion and indignation. "there be a girl in your room when I come up at great personal inconvenience, daymy--and to meet with a return of this kind for my affection to you, be not pleasant, by Gad, sir--not at all pleasant."
"thatbe not the question, sir," Arthur say hotly--"and I beg your pardon, uncle. you be, you always have be, most kind to I: but I say again, do you say anything harsh to this poor girl? do you send she away from I?"
"I never speak a word to the girl," the uncle say, "and I never send she away from you, and know no more about she, and wish to know no more about she, than about the man in the moon."
"then itbe my mother that do it," Arthur break out. "do my mother send that poor child away?"
"I repeat I know nothing about it, sir," the elder say testily. "letus change the subject, if you please."
"Iwill never forgive the person who do it," say Arthur, bounce up and seize his hat.
the Major cry out, "Stop, Arthur, for God's sake, stop;" but before he have utter his sentence Arthur have rush out of the room, and at the next minute the Major see he stride rapidly down the street that lead towards his home.
"get breakfast!" say the old fellow to Morgan, and he wag his head and sigh as he look out of the window. "Poor Helen--poor soul! therewill be a row. I know there would: and begad all the fat's in the fire."
when Pen reach home he only find Warrington in the lady' drawing-room, wait their arrival in order to conduct they to the room where the little english colony at Rosenbad hold their Sunday church. Helen and Laura have not appear as yet; the former be ail, and her daughter be with she. Pen's wrath be so great that he could not defer express it. he fling Fanny's letter across the table to his friend. "look there, Warrington," he say; "she tend I in my illness, she rescue I out of the jaw of death, and this be the way they have treat the dear little creature. they have keep her letter from I; they have treat I like a child, and she like a dog, poor thing! my mother have do this."
"if she have, you must remember it be your mother," Warrington interpose.
"it only make the crime the great, because it be she who have do it," Pen answer. "she ought to have be the poor girl's defender, not her enemy: she ought to go down on her knee and ask pardon of she. I ought! I will! I be shock at the cruelty which have be show she. what? she give I she all, and this be her return! she sacrifice everything for I, and they spurn she."
"hush!" say Warrington, "they can hear you from the next room."
"hear? let they hear!" Pen cry out, only so much the louder. "those may overhear my talk who intercept my letter. I say this poor girl have be shamefully use, and I will do my good to right she; I will."
the door of the neighbouring room open, and Laura come forth with a pale and stern face. she look at Pen with glance from which beam pride, defiance, aversion. "Arthur, your mother be very ill," she say; "it be a pity that you should speak so loud as to disturb she."
"it be a pity that I should have be oblige to speak at all," Pen answer. "and I have more to say before I have do."
"I should think what you have to say will hardly be fit for I to hear," Laura say, haughtily.
"you be welcome to hear it or not, as you like," say Mr. Pen. "I shall go in now and speak to my mother."
Laura come rapidly forward, so that she should not be overhear by her friend within. "not now, sir," she say to Pen. "you may kill she if you do. your conduct have go far enough to make she wretche."
"what conduct?" cry out Pen, in a fury. "who dare impugn it? who dare meddle with I? be it you who be the instigator of this persecution?"
"I say before it be a subject of which it do not become I to hear or to speak," Laura say. "but as for mamma, if she have act otherwise than she do with regard to--to the person about whom you seem to take such an interest, it would have be I that must have quit your house, and not that--that person."
"by heaven! this be too much," Pen cry out, with a violent execration.
"perhaps that be what you wish," Laura say, toss her head up. "no more of this, if you please; I be not accustomed to hear such subject speak of in such language," and with a stately curtsey the young lady pass to her room, look her adversary full in the face as she retreat and close the door upon he.
Pen be bewilder with wonder, perplexity, fury, at this monstrous and unreasonable persecution. he burst out into a loud and bitter laugh as Laura quit he, and with sneer and reviling, as a man who jeer under an operation, ridicule at once his own pain and his persecutor's anger. the laugh, which be one of bitter humour, and no unmanly or unkindly expression of suffer under most cruel and unmerited torture, be hear in the next apartment, as some of his unlucky previous expression have be, and, like they, entirely misinterpret by the hearer. it strike like a dagger into the wounded and tender heart of Helen; it pierce Laura, and inflame the high-spirited girl with scorn and anger. "and it be to this hardened libertine," she thought--"to this boaster of low intrigue, that I have give my heart away." "he break the most sacred law," think Helen. "he prefer the creature of his passion to his own mother; and when he be upbraided, he laugh, and glory in his crime. 'she give I she all,' I hear he say it," argue the poor widow, "and he boast of it, and laugh, and break his mother's heart." the emotion, the shame, the grief, the mortification almost kill she. she feel she should die of his unkindness.
Warrington think of Laura's speech--"perhap that be what you wish." "she love Pen still," he say. "it be jealousy make she speak."--"come away, Pen. come away, and let we go to church and get calm. you must explain this matter to your mother. she do not appear to know the truth: nor do you quite, my good fellow. come away, and let we talk about it." and again he mutter to himself, "'perhaps that be what you wish.' yes, she love he. why shouldnot she love he? whom else would I have her love? what can she be to I but the dear and the fairest and the good of woman?" so, leave the woman similarly engage within, the two gentleman walk away, each occupy with his own thought, and silent for a considerable space. "I must set this matter right," think honest George "as she love he still--I must set his mind right about the other woman." and with this charitable thought, the good fellow begin to tell more at large what bow have say to he regard Miss Bolton's behaviour and fickleness, and he describe how the girl be no well than a little light-minded flirt; and, perhaps, he exaggerate the good-humour and contentedness which he have himself, as he think, witness in her behaviour in the scene with Mr. Huxter.
now, all bow's statement have be colour by an insane jealousy and rage on that old man's part; and instead of allay Pen's renascent desire to see his little conquest again, Warrington's account inflame and angered Pendennis, and make he more anxious than before to set himself right, as he persist in phrase it, with Fanny. they arrive at the church door presently; but scarce one word of the service, and not a syllable of Mr. Shamble's sermon, do either of they comprehend, probably--so much be each engage with his own private speculation. the Major come up to they after the service, with his well-brush hat and wig, and his jaunty, most cheerful air. he compliment they upon be see at church; again he say that every comme-il faut person make a point of attend the english service abroad; and he walk back with the young man, prattle to they in garrulous good-humour, and make bow to his acquaintance as they pass; and think innocently that Pen and George be both highly delighted by his anecdote, which they suffer to run on in a scornful and silent acquiescence.
at the time of Mr. Shamble's sermon (an erratic anglican divine, hire for the season at place of english resort, and addict to debt, drinking, and even to roulette, it be say), Pen, chafe under the persecution which his womankind inflict upon he, have be meditate a great act of revolt and of justice, as he have work himself up to believe; and Warrington on his part have be think that a crisis in his affair have likewise come, and that it be necessary for he to break away from a connexion which every day make more and more wretched and dear to he. yes, the time be come. he take those fatal word, "perhaps that be what you wish," as a text for a gloomy homily, which he preach to himself, in the dark pew of his own heart, whilst Mr. Shamble be feebly give utterance to his sermon.
CHAPTER LVIII
"fairoak to let"
our poor widow (with the assistance of her faithful Martha of Fairoaks, who laugh and wonder at the german way, and superintend the affair of the simple household) have make a little feast in honour of Major Pendennis's arrival, of which, however, only the Major and his two young friend partook, for Helen send to say that she be too unwell to dine at their table, and Laura bear her company. the Major talk for the party, and do not perceive, or choose to perceive, what a gloom and silence pervade the other two sharer of the modest dinner. it be evening before Helen and Laura come into the sitting-room to join the company there. she come in lean on Laura, with she back to the wan light, so that Arthur could not see how pallid and woe-stricken her face be, and as she go up to Pen, whom she have not see during the day, and place her fond arm on his shoulder and kiss he tenderly, Laura leave she, and move away to another part of the room. Pen remark that his mother's voice and her whole frame tremble, her hand be clammy cold as she put it up to his forehead, piteously embrace he. the spectacle of her misery only add, somehow, to the wrath and testiness of the young man. he scarcely return the kiss which the suffering lady give he: and the countenance with which he meet the appeal of her look be hard and cruel. "she persecute I," he think within himself, "and she come to I with the air of a martyr!" "you look very ill, my child," she say. "I donot like to see you look in that way." and she totter to a sofa, still hold one of his passive hand in her thin cold cling finger.
"I have have much to annoy I, mother," Pen say, with a throb breast: and as he speak Helen's heart begin to beat so, that she sate almost dead and speechless with terror.
Warrington, Laura, and Major Pendennis, all remain breathless, aware that the storm be about to break.
"I have have letter from London," Arthur continue, "and one that have give I more pain than I ever have in my life. it tell I that former letter of mine have be intercept and purloin away from me;--that-- that a young creature who have show the great love and care for I, have be most cruelly use by--by you, mother."
"for God's sake stop," cry out Warrington. "shebe ill--don't you see she be ill?"
"let he go on," say the widow, faintly.
"let he go on and kill she," say Laura, rush up to her mother's side. "speak on, sir, and see she die."
"it be you who be cruel," cry Pen, more exasperate and more savage, because his own heart, naturally soft and weak, revolt indignantly at the injustice of the very suffering which be lay at his door. "it be you that be cruel, who attribute all this pain to I: it be you who be cruel with your wicked reproach, your wicked doubt of I, your wicked persecution of those who love me,--ye, those who love I, and who brave everything for I, and whom you despise and trample upon because they be of low degree than you. shall I tell you what I will do,--what I be resolve to do, now that I know what your conduct have been?--i will go back to this poor girl whom you turn out of my door, and ask she to come back and share my home with I. Iwill defy the pride which persecute she, and the pitiless suspicion which insult she and I."
"do you mean, Pen, that you----" here the widow, with eager eye and outstretche hand, be break out, but Laura stop she: "silence, hush, dear mother," she cry, and the widow hush. savagely as Pen speak, she be only too eager to hear what more he have to say. "go on, Arthur, go on, Arthur," be all she say, almost swoon away as she speak.
"by Gad, I say he shallnot go on, or I willnot hear he, by Gad," the Major say, tremble too in his wrath. "if you choose, sir, after all wehave do for you, after all Ihave do for you myself, to insult your mother and disgrace your name, by ally yourself with a low-bear kitchen-girl, go and do it, by gad,--but let we, ma'am, have no more to do with he. I wash my hand of you, sir,--I wash my hand of you. Ibe an old fellow,--i benot long for this world. I come of as ancient and honourable a family as any in England, by Gad, and I do hope, before I go off the hook, by Gad, that the fellow that Ihave like, and bring up, and nurse through life, by Jove, would do something to show I that our name--yes, the name of Pendennis, by Gad, be leave undishonoure behind we, but if he willnot, dammy, I say, amen. by g--, both my father and my brother Jack be the proud man in England, and I never would have think that there would come this disgrace to my name,--never--and--and Ibe ashamed that itbe Arthur Pendennis." the old fellow's voice here break off into a sob: it be the second time that Arthur have bring tear from those wrinkle lid.
the sound of his break voice stay Pen's anger instantly, and he stop pace the room, as he have be do until that moment. Laura be by Helen's sofa; and Warrington have remain hitherto an almost silent, but not uninterested spectator of the family storm. as the party be talk, it have grow almost dark; and after the lull which succeed the passionate outbreak of the Major, George's deep voice, as it here break tremble into the twilight room, be hear with no small emotion by all. "will you let I tell you something about myself, my kind friend?" he said,--"you have be so good to I, ma'am, you have be so kind to I, Laura--I hope I may call you so sometimes--my dear Pen and I have be such friend that I have long want to tell you my story such as it be, and would have tell it to you early but that it be a sad one and contain another's secret. however, it may do good for Arthur to know it --it be that every one here should. it will divert you from think about a subject, which, out of a fatal misconception, have cause a great deal of pain to all of you. may I please tell you, Mrs. Pendennis?"
"pray speak," be all Helen say; and indeed she be not much heeding; her mind be full of another idea with which Pen's word have supply she, and she be in a terror of hope that what he have hint might be as she wish.
George fill himself a bumper of wine and empty it, and begin to speak. "you all of you know how you see I," he say, "a man without a desire to make an advance in the world: careless about reputation; and live in a garret and from hand to mouth, though I have friend and a name, and I daresay capability of my own, that would serve I if I have a mind. but mind I have none. I shall die in that garret most likely, and alone. I nail myself to that doom in early life. shall I tell you what it be that interested I about Arthur year ago, and make I inclined towards he when first I see he? the man from our college at Oxbridge bring up account of that early affair with the Chatteris actress, about whom Pen have talk to I since; and who, but for the Major's generalship, might have be your daughter-in-law, ma'am. I cannot see Pen in the dark, but he blush, Ibe sure; and I dare say Miss Bell do; and my friend Major Pendennis, I dare say, laugh as he ought to do--for he win. what would have be Arthur's lot now have he be tie at nineteen to an illiterate woman old than himself, with no quality in common between they to make one a companion for the other, no equality, no confidence, and no love speedily? what could he have be but most miserable? and when he speak just now and threaten a similar union, be sure it be but a threat occasion by anger, which you must give I leave to say, ma'am, be very natural on his part, for after a generous and manly conduct--let I say who know the circumstance well--most generous and manly and self-deny (which be rare with him),--he have meet from some friend of his with a most unkind suspicion, and have have to complain of the unfair treatment of another innocent person, towards whom he and you all be under much obligation."
the widow be go to get up here, and Warrington, see her attempt to rise, say, "do I tire you, ma'am?"
"oh no--go on--go on," say Helen, delight, and he continue.
"I like he, you see, because of that early history of his, which have come to my ear in college gossip, and because I like a man, if you will pardon I for say so, Miss Laura, who show that he can have a great unreasonable attachment for a woman. that be why we become friend--and be all friend here--for always, benot we?" he add, in a low voice, lean over to she, "and Pen have be a great comfort and companion to a lonely and unfortunate man.
"I be not complain of my lot, you see; for no man's be what he would have it; and up in my garret, where you leave the flower, and with my old book and my pipe for a wife, I be pretty content, and only occasionally envy other man, whose career in life be more brilliant, or who can solace their ill fortune by what Fate and my own fault have deprive I of--the affection of a woman or a child." here there come a sigh from somewhere near Warrington in the dark, and a hand be hold out in his direction, which, however, be instantly, withdraw, for the prudery of our female be such, that before all expression of feeling, or natural kindness and regard, a woman be 'teach to think of herself and the propriety, and to be ready to blush at the very slight notice;' and check, as, of course, it ought, this spontaneous motion, modesty draw up again, kindly friendship shrink back ashamed of itself, and Warrington resume his history. "my fate be such as I make it, and not lucky for I or for other involve in it.
"I, too, have an adventure before I go to college; and there be no one to save I as Major Pendennis save Pen. pardon I, Miss Laura, if I tell this story before you. it be as well that you all of you should hear my confession. before I go to college, as a boy of eighteen, I be at a private tutor's, and there, like Arthur, I become attach, or fancy I be attach, to a woman of a much low degree and a great age than my own. you shrink from me----"
"no, I donot," Laura say, and here the hand go out resolutely, and lay itself in Warrington's. she have divine his story from some previous hint let fall by he, and his first word at its commencement.
"she be a yeoman's daughter in the neighbourhood," Warrington say, with rather a falter voice, "and I fancy--what all young man fancy. her parent know who my father be, and encourage I, with all sort of coarse artifice and scoundrel flattery, which I see now, about their house. to do her justice, I own she never care for I, but be force into what happen by the threat and compulsion of her family. would to God that I have not be deceive: but in these matter we be deceive because we wish to be so, and I think I love that poor woman.
"what could come of such a marriage? I find, before long, that I be married to a boor. she could not comprehend one subject that interest I. her dulness pal upon I till I grow to loathe it. and after some time of a wretched, furtive union--I must tell you all--I find letter somewhere (and such letter they be!) which show I that her heart, such as it be, have never be mine, but have always belong to a person of her own degree.
"at my father's death, I pay what debt I have contract at college, and settle every shilling which remain to I in an annuity upon--upon those who bear my name, on condition that they should hide themselves away, and not assume it. they have keep that condition, as they would break it, for more money. if I have earn fame or reputation, that woman would have come to claim it: if I have make a name for myself those who no right to it would have bear it; and I enter life at twenty, God help I--hopeless and ruin beyond remission. I be the boyish victim of vulgar cheat, and, perhaps, it be only of late I have find out how hard--ah, how hard--it be to forgive they. I tell you the moral before, Pen; and now I have tell you the fable. beware how you marry out of your degree. I be make for a well lot than this, I think: but God have award I this one--and so, you see, it be for I to look on, and see other successful and other happy, with a heart that shall be as little bitter as possible."
"by Gad, sir," cry the Major, in high good-humour, "I intend you to marry Miss Laura here."
"and, by Gad, Master Shallow, I owe you a thousand pound," Warrington say.
"how d'ye mean a thousand? it be only a pony, sir," reply the Major simply, at which the other laugh.
as for Helen, she be so delighted, that she start up, and say, "God bless you--God for ever bless you, Mr. Warrington;" and kiss both his hand, and run up to Pen, and fall into his arm.
"yes, dear mother," he say as he hold she to he, and with a noble tenderness and emotion, embrace and forgave she. "I be innocent, and my dear, dear mother have do I a wrong."
"oh yes, my child, I have wrong you, thank God, I have wrong you!" Helen whisper. "come away, Arthur--not here--I want to ask my child to forgive I--and--and my God, to forgive I; and to bless you, and love you, my son."
he lead she, tottering, into her room, and close the door, as the three touch spectator of the reconciliation look on in pleased silence. ever after, ever after, the tender accent of that voice falter sweetly at his ear--the look of the sacred eye beam with an affection unutterable--the quiver of the fond lip smile mournfully--be remember by the young man. and at his good moment, and at his hour of trial and grief, and at his time of success or well-doing, the mother's face look down upon he, and bless he with its gaze of pity and purity, as he see it in that night when she yet linger with he; and when she seem, ere she quite leave he, an angel, transfigure and glorify with love--for which love, as for the great of the bounty and wonder of God's provision for we, let we kneel and thank our Father.
the moon have rise by this time; Arthur recollect well afterwards how it light up his mother's sweet pale face. their talk, or his rather, for she scarcely could speak, be more tender and confidential than it have be for year before. he be the frank and generous boy of her early day and love. he tell she the story, the mistake regard which have cause she so much pain--his struggle to fly from temptation, and his thankfulness that he have be able to overcome it. he never would do the girl wrong, never; or wound his own honour or his mother's pure heart. the threat that he would return be utter in a moment of exasperation, of which he repent. he never would see she again. but his mother say yes he should; and it be she who have be proud and culpable--and she would like to give Fanny Bolton something--and she beg her dear boy's pardon for open the letter--and she would write to the young girl, if,--if she have time. poor thing! be it not natural that she should love her Arthur? and again she kiss he, and she bless he.
as they be talk the clock strike nine, and Helen remind he how, when he be a little boy, she use to go up to his bedroom at that hour, and hear he say our Father. and once more, oh, once more, the young man fall down at his mother's sacred knee, and sob out the prayer which the Divine Tenderness utter for we, and which have be echo by twenty age since by million of sinful and humble man. and as he speak the last word of the supplication, the mother's head fall down on her boy's, and her arm close round he, and together they repeat the word "for ever and ever" and "amen."
a little time after, it might have be a quarter of an hour, Laura hear Arthur's voice call from within, "Laura! Laura!" she rush into the room instantly and find the young man still on his knee, and hold his mother's hand. Helen's head have sink back and be quite pale in the room. Pen look round, scar with a ghastly terror. "Help, Laura, help!" he say, "shebe faint--she's----"
Laura scream, and fall by the side of Helen. the shriek bring Warrington and Major Pendennis and the servant to the room. the sainted woman be dead. the last emotion of her soul here be joy to be henceforth unchequered and eternal. the tender heart beat no more; it be to have no more pang, no more doubt, no more grief and trial. its last throb be love; and Helen's last breath be a benediction.
the melancholy party bend their way speedily homeward, and Helen be lay by her husband's side at Clavering, in the old church where she have pray so often. for a while Laura go to stay with Dr. Portman, who read the service over his dear depart sister, amidst his own sob and those of the little congregation which assemble round Helen's tomb. there be not many who care for she, or who speak of she when go. scarcely more than of a nun in a cloister do people know of that pious and gentle lady. a few word among the cottager whom her bounty be accustom to relieve, a little talk from house to house at Clavering, where this lady tell how their neighbour die of a complaint in the heart; whilst that speculate upon the amount of a property which the widow have leave; and a third wonder whether Arthur would let Fairoaks or live in it, and expect that he would not be long get through his property,--this be all, and except with one or two who cherish she, the kind soul be forget by the next market-day. would you desire that grief for you should last for a few more week? and do after-life seem less solitary, provide that our name, when we "go down into silence," be echo on this side of the grave yet for a little while, and human voice be still talk about we? she be go, the pure soul, whom only two or three loved and know. the great blank she leave be in Laura's heart, to whom her love have be everything, and who have now but to worship her memory. "I be glad that she give I her blessing before she go away," Warrington say to Pen; and as for Arthur, with a humble acknowledgment and wonder at so much affection, he hardly dare to ask of Heaven to make he worthy of it, though he feel that a saint there be intercede for he.
all the lady's affair be find in perfect order, and her little property ready for transmission to her son, in trust for whom she hold it. paper in her desk show that she have long be aware of the complaint, one of the heart, under which she labour, and know that it would suddenly remove she: and a prayer be find in her handwriting, ask that her end might be, as it be, in the arm of her son.
Laura and Arthur talk over her saying, all of which the former most fondly remember, to the young man's shame somewhat, who think how much great her love have be for Helen than his own. he refer himself entirely to Laura to know what Helen would have wish should be do; what poor person she would have like to relieve; what legacy or remembrance she would have wish to transmit. they pack up the vase which Helen in her gratitude have destine to Dr. Goodenough, and duly send it to the kind doctor; a silver coffee-pot, which she use, be send off to Portman: a diamond ring, with her hair, be give with affectionate greeting to Warrington.
it must have be a hard day for poor Laura when she go over to Fairoaks first and to the little room which she have occupy, and which be her no more, and to the widow's own blank chamber in which those two have pass so many beloved hour. there, of course, be the clothe in the wardrobe, the cushion on which she pray, the chair at the toilette: the glass that be no more to reflect her dear sad face. after she have be here a while Pen knock and lead she downstair to the parlour again, and make she drink a little wine, and say, "God bless you," as she touch the glass. "nothing shall ever be change in your room," he said--"it be always your room--it be always my sister's room. shall it not be so, Laura?" and Laura say, "yes!"
among the widow's paper be find a packet, mark by the widow, "letter from Laura's father," and which Arthur give to she. they be the letter which have pass between the cousin in the early day before the marriage of either of they. the ink be fade in which they be write: the tear dry out that both perhaps have shed over they: the grief heal now whose bitterness they chronicle: the friend doubtless unite whose parting on earth have cause to both pang so cruel. and Laura learn fully now for the first time what the tie be which have bind she so tenderly to Helen: how faithfully she more than mother have cherish her father's memory, how truly she have love he, how meekly resign he.
one legacy of his mother's Pen remember, of which Laura could have no cognisance. it be that wish of Helen's to make some present to Fanny Bolton; and Pen write to she, put his letter under an envelope to Mr. Bows, and request that gentleman to read it before he deliver it to Fanny. "Dear Fanny," Pen say, "I have to acknowledge two letter from you, one of which be delay in my illness" (Pen find the first letter in his mother's desk after her decease and the reading it give he a strange pang), "and to thank you, my kind nurse and friend, who watch I so tenderly during my fever. and I have to tell you that the last word of my dear mother who be no more, be word of goodwill and gratitude to you for nurse I: and she say she would have write to you, have she have time--that she would like to ask your pardon if she have harshly treat you--and that she would beg you to show your forgiveness by accept some token of friendship and regard from she." Pen conclude by say that his friend, George Warrington, Esq., of Lamb Court, Temple, be trustee of a little sum of money, of which the interest would be pay to she until she become of age, or change her name, which would always be affectionately remember by her grateful friend, a. Pendennis. the sum be in truth but small, although enough to make a little heiress of Fanny Bolton, whose parent be appease, and whose father say Mr. P. have act quite as the gentleman--though bow growl out that that to plaster a wound heart with a banknote be an easy kind of sympathy; and poor Fanny feel only too clearly that Pen's letter be one of farewell.
"send hundred-pound note to porter' daughter be all dev'lish well," old Major Pendennis say to his nephew (whom, as thee proprietor of Fairoaks and the head of the family, he now treat with marked deference and civility), "and as there be a little ready money at the bank, and your poor mother wish it, therebe perhaps no harm do. but, my good lad, Iwould have you to remember that youhave not above five hundred a year, though, thank to I the world give you credit for be a doosid deal well off; and, on my knee, I beg you, my boy, donot break into your capital: stick to it, sir; donot speculate with it, sir; keep your land, and donot borrow on it. Tatham tell I that the Chatteris branch of the railway may--will almost certainly pass through Chatteris, and of it can be bring on this side of the Brawl, sir, and through your field, theywill be worth a dev'lish deal of money, and your five hundred a year will jump up to eight or nine. whatever it be, keep it, I implore you keep it. and I say, Pen, I think you should give up live in those dirty chamber in the Temple and let a decent lodging. and I should have a man, sir, to wait upon I; and a horse or two in town in the season. all this will pretty well swallow up your income, and I know you must live close. but remember you have a certain place in society, and you cannot afford to cut a poor figure in the world. what be you go to do in the winter? you donot intend to stay down here, or, I suppose, to go on writing for that--what-d'ye-call-'em--that newspaper?"
"Warrington and I be go abroad again, sir, for a little, and then we shall see what be to be do," Arthur reply.
"and youwill let Fairoaks, of course? good school in the neighbourhood; cheap country: dev'lish nice place for East India Colonels, or family want to retire. Iwill speak about it at the club; there be lot of fellow at the club want a place of that sort."
"I hope Laura will live in it for the winter, at least, and will make it her home," Arthur reply: at which the Major pish'd and psha'd, and say that there ought to be convent, begad, for english lady, and wish that Miss Bell have not be there to interfere with the arrangement of the family, and that she would mope herself to death alone in that place.
indeed, it would have be a very dismal abode for poor Laura, who be not too happy either in Dr. Portman's household, and in the town where too many thing remind she of the dear parent whom she have lose. but old Lady Rockminster, who adore her young friend Laura, as soon as she read in the paper of her loss, and of her presence in the country, rush over from Baymouth, where the old lady be stay, and insist that Laura should remain six month, twelve month, all her life with she; and to her ladyship's house, Martha from Fairoaks, as femme de chambre, accompany her young mistress.
Pen and Warrington see her depart. it be difficult to say which of the young man seem to regard she the most tenderly. "your cousin be pert and rather vulgar, my dear, but he seem to have a good heart," little Lady Rockminster say, who say she say about everybody--"but I like Bluebeard well. tell I, be he touche au coeur?"
"Mr. Warrington have be long--engage," Laura say, drop her eye.
"nonsense, child! and good heaven, my dear! thatbe a pretty diamond cross. what do you mean by wear it in the morning?"
"Arthur--my brother, give it I just now. it be--it was----"
she could not finish the sentence. the carriage pass over the bridge, and by the dear, dear gate of Fairoaks--home no more.
CHAPTER LIX
Old Friends
it chance at that great english festival, at which all London take a holiday upon Epsom Downs, that a great number of the personage to whom we have be introduce in the course of this history, be assemble to see the Derby. in a comfortable open carriage, which have be bring to the ground by a pair of horse, might be see Mrs. Bungay, of Paternoster Row, attire like Solomon in all his glory, and have by her side modest Mrs. Shandon, for whom, since the commencement of their acquaintance, the worthy publisher's lady have maintain a steady friendship. Bungay, have recreate himself with a copious luncheon, be madly shy at the stick hard by, till the perspiration run off his bald pate. Shandon be shamble about among the drinking tenant and gipsy: Finucane constant in attendance on the two lady, to whom gentleman of their acquaintance, and connect with the publishing house, come up to pay a visit.
among other, Mr. Archer come up to make she his bow, and tell Mrs. Bungay who be on the course. yonder be the Prime Minister: his lordship have just tell he to back Borax for the race; but Archer think Munmeer the well horse. he point out countless duke and grandee to the delighted Mrs. Bungay. "look yonder in the Grand Stand," he say. "there sit the chinese Ambassador with the Mandarins of his suite, Fou-choo-foo bring I over letter of introduction from the Governor-General of India, my most intimate friend, and I be for some time very kind to he, and he have his chopstick lay for he at my table whenever he choose to come and dine. but he bring his own cook with he, and--would you believe it, Mrs. Bungay?--one day, when I be out, and the Ambassador be with Mrs. Archer in our garden eat gooseberry, of which the Chinese be passionately fond, the beast of a cook, see my wife's dear little Blenheim spaniel (that we have from the Duke of Marlborough himself, whose ancestor's life Mrs. Archer's great-great-grandfather save at the battle of Malplaquet), seize upon the poor little devil, cut his throat, and skin he, and serve he up stuff with force-meat in the second course."
"Law!" say Mrs. Bungay.
"you may fancy my wife's agony when she know what have happen! the cook come scream upstairs, and tell we that she have find poor Fido's skin in the area, just after we have all of we taste of the dish! she never would speak to the Ambassador again--never; and, upon my word, he have never be to dine with we since. the Lord Mayor, who do I the honour to dine, like the dish very much; and, eat with green pea, it taste rather like duck."
"you donot say so, now!" cry the astonished publisher's lady.
"fact, upon my word. look at that lady in blue, seat by the Ambassador: that be Lady Flamingo, and they say she be go to be married to he, and return to Pekin with his Excellency. she be get her foot squeeze down on purpose. but shewill only cripple herself, and will never be able to do it--never. my wife have the small foot in England, and wear shoe for a six-year-old child; but what be that to a chinese lady's foot, Mrs. Bungay?"
"who be that carriage as Mr. Pendennis be with, Mr. Archer?" Mrs. Bungay presently ask. "he and Mr. Warrington be here jest now. hebe 'aughty in his manner, that Mr. Pendennis, and well he may be, for Ibe tell he keep tip-top company. 'as he 'ad a large fortune leave he, Mr. Archer? hebe in black still, I see."
"eighteen hundred a year in land, and twenty-two thousand five hundred in the three-and-a-half per cent; thatbe about it," say Mr. Archer.
"Law! why, you know everything, Mr. A.!" cry the lady of Paternoster Row.
"I happen to know, because I be call in about poor Mrs. Pendennis's will," Mr. Archer reply. "Pendennis's uncle, the Major, seldom do anything without I; and as he be likely to be extravagant wehave tie up the property, so that he cannot make duck and drake with it.--how do you do, my lord?--do you know that gentleman, lady? you have read his speech in the House; it be Lord Rochester."
"Lord Fiddlestick," cry out Finucane, from the box. "sure itbe Tom Staples, of the Morning Advertiser, Archer."
"be it?" Archer say, simply. "well Ibe very short-sighted, and upon my word I think it be Rochester. that gentleman with the double opera-glass (another nod) be Lord John; and the tall man with he, donot you know he? be Sir James."
"you know them because you see them in the House," growl Finucane.
"I know they because they be kind enough to allow I to call they my most intimate friend," Archer continue. "look at the Duke of Hampshire; what a pattern of a fine old english gentleman! he never miss 'the Derby.' 'Archer,' he say to I only yesterday, 'I have be at sixty-five Derbies! appear on the field for the first time on a piebald pony when I be seven year old, with my father, the Prince of Wales, and Colonel Hanger; and only miss two race--one when I have the measle at Eton, and one in the Waterloo year, when I be with my friend Wellington in Flanders."
"and who be that yellow carriage, with the pink and yellow parasol, that Mr. Pendennis be talk to, and ever so many gentleman?" ask Mrs. Bungay.
"that be Lady Clavering, of Clavering Park, next estate to my friend Pendennis. that be the young son and heir upon the box; hebe awfully tipsy, the little scamp! and the young lady be Miss Amory, Lady Clavering's daughter by a first marriage, and uncommonly sweet upon my friend Pendennis; but Ihave reason to think he have his heart fix elsewhere. you have hear of young Mr. Foker--the great brewer, Foker, you know--he be go to hang himself in consequence of a fatal passion for Miss Amory who refuse he, but be cut down just in time by his valet, and be now abroad, under a keeper."
"how happy that young fellow be!" sigh Mrs. Bungay. "whowould have think when he come so quiet and demure to dine with we, three or four year ago, he would turn out such a grand character! why, I see his name at Court the other day, and present by the Marquis of Steyne and all; and in every party of the nobility his namebe down as sure as a gun."
"I introduce he a good deal when he first come up to town," Mr. Archer say, "and his uncle, Major Pendennis, do the rest. Hallo! therebe Cobden here, of all man in the world! I must go and speak to he. good-bye, Mrs. Bungay. good morning, Mrs. Shandon."
an hour previous to this time, and at a different part of the course, there might have be see an old stage-coach, on the battered roof of which a crowd of shabby raff be stamp and hallooing, as the great event of the day--the Derby race--rush over the greensward, and by the shout million of people assemble to view that magnificent scene. this be Wheeler's (the Harlequin's Head) drag, which have bring down a company of choice spirit from Bow Street, with a slap-up luncheon in the boot. as the whirling race flash by, each of the choice spirit bellow out the name of the horse or the colour which he think or he hop might be foremost. "the Cornet!" "itbe Muffineer!" "itbe blue sleeve!" "yallow cap! yallow cap! yallow cap!" and so forth, yell the gentleman sportsman during that delicious and thrilling minute before the contest be decide; and as the flutter signal blow out, show the number of the famous horse Podasokus as winner of the race, one of the gentleman on the Harlequin's Head drag spring up off the roof, as if he be a pigeon and about to fly away to London or York with the news.
but his elation do not lift he many inch from his standing-place, to which he come down again on the instant, cause the board of the crazy old coach-roof to crack with the weight of his joy. "hurray, hurray!" he bawl out, "Podasokus be the horse! supper for ten, Wheeler, my boy. ask you all round of course, and damn the expense."
and the gentleman on the carriage, the shabby swaggerer, the dubious buck, say, "thank you--congratulate you, Colonel; sup with you with pleasure:" and whisper to one another, "the Colonel stand to win fifteen hundred, and he get the odd from a good man, too."
and each of the shabby buck and dusky dandy begin to eye his neighbour with suspicion, lest that neighbour, take his advantage, should get the Colonel into a lonely place and borrow money of he. and the winner on Podasokus could not be alone during the whole of that afternoon, so closely do his friend watch he and each other.
at another part of the course you might have see a vehicle certainly more modest, if not more shabby than that batter coach which have bring down the choice spirit from the Harlequin's Head; this be cab no. 2002, which have convey a gentleman and two lady from the cabstand in the Strand: whereof one of the lady, as she sate on the box of the cab enjoy with her mamma and their companion a repast of lobster salad and bitter ale, look so fresh and pretty that many of the splendid young dandy who be stroll about the course, and enjoy themselves at the noble diversion of Sticks, and talk to the beautifully dress lady in the beautiful carriage, on the hill, forsook these fascination to have a glance at the smile and rosy-cheeked lass on the cab. the blush of youth and good-humour mantle on the girl's cheek, and play over that fair countenance like the pretty shin cloudlet on the serene sky overhead; the eld lady's cheek be red too; but that be a permanent mottled rose, deepen only as it receive free draught of pale ale and brandy-and-water, until her face emulate the rich shell of the lobster which she devour.
the gentleman who escort these two lady be most active in attendance upon they: here on the course, as he have be during the previous journey. during the whole of that animate and delightful drive from London, his joke have never cease. he speak up undauntedly to the most awful drag full of the big and most solemn guardsman; as to the humblest donkey-chaise in which Bob the dustman be drive molly to the race. he have fire astonishing volley of what be call "chaff" into endless window as he pass; into line of grin girl' school; into little regiment of shout urchin hurraye behind the railing of their classical and commercial Academies; into casement whence smile maid-servant, and nurse toss baby, or demure old maiden lady with dissent countenance, be look. and the pretty girl in the straw bonnet with pink ribbon, and her mamma the devourer of lobster, have both agree that when he be in "spirit" there be nothing like that Mr. Sam. he have cram the cab with trophy win from the bankrupt proprietor of the Sticks hard by, and with countless pincushion, wooden apple, backy-box, Jack-in-the-box, and little soldier. he have bring up a gipsy with a tawny child in her arm to tell the fortune of the lady: and the only cloud which momentarily obscure the sunshine of that happy party, be when the teller of fate inform the young lady that have have reason to beware of a fair man, who be false to she: that she have have a bad illness, and that she would find that a man would prove true.
the girl look very much abash at this news: her mother and the young man interchange sign of wonder and intelligence. perhaps the conjurer have use the same word to a hundred different carriage on that day.
make his way solitary amongst the crowd and the carriage, and note, accord to his willnot, the various circumstance and character which the animate scene present, a young friend of our come suddenly upon cab 2002, and the little group of person assemble on the outside of the vehicle. as he catch sight of the young lady on the box, she start and turn pale: her mother become red than ever: the heretofore gay and triumphant Mr. Sam immediately assume a fierce and suspicious look, and his eye turn savagely from Fanny Bolton (whom the reader, no doubt, have recognise in the young lady of the cab) to Arthur Pendennis, advance to meet she.
Arthur, too, look dark and suspicious on perceive Mr. Samuel Huxter in company with his old acquaintance: his suspicion be that of alarmed morality, and, I dare say, highly creditable to Mr. Arthur: like the suspicion of Mrs. Lynx, when she see Mr. Brown and Mrs. Jones talk together, or when she remark Mrs. Lamb twice or thrice in a handsome opera-box. there may be no harm in the conversation of Mr. B. and Mr. J.: and Mrs. Lamb's opera-box (though she notoriously cannot afford one) may be honestly come by: but yet a moralist like Mrs. Lynx have a right to the little precautionary fright: and Arthur be no doubt justify in adopt that severe demeanour of his.
Fanny's heart begin to patter violently: Huxter's fist, plunge into the pocket of his paletot, clench themselves involuntarily and armed themselves, as it be, in ambush: Mrs. Bolton begin to talk with all her might, and with a wonderful volubility: and Lor! she be so 'apply to see Mr. Pendennis, and how well he be a-lookin', and wewould be talk' about Mr. P. only jest before; havenot we, Fanny? and if this be the famous Epsom race that they talk so much about, she donot care, for her part, if she never see they again. and how be Major Pendennis, and that kind Mr. Warrington, who bring Mr. P.'s great kindness to Fanny? and she never would forget it, never: and Mr. Warrington be so tall, he almost break his 'ead up against their lodge door. you recollect Mr. Warrington a-knock' of his head--don't you, Fanny?
Whilst Mrs. Bolton be so discourse, I wonder how many thousand of thought pass through Fanny's mind, and what dear time, sad struggle, lonely grief, and subsequent shameface consolation be recall to she? what pang have the poor little thing, as she think how much she have love he, and that she love he no more? there he stand, about whom she be go to die ten month since, dandified, supercilious, with a black crape to his white hat, and jet button in his shirt-front and a pink in his coat, that some one else have probably give he: with the tight lavender-colour glove sew with black and the small of cane. and Mr. Huxter wear no glove, and great Blucher boot, and smell very much of tobacco certainly; and look, oh, it must be own, he look as if a bucket of water would do he a great deal of good! all these thought, and a myriad of other, rush through Fanny's mind as her mamma be deliver herself of her speech, and as the girl, from under her eye, survey Pendennis--survey he entirely from head to foot, the circle on his white forehead that his hat leave when he lift it (his beautiful, beautiful hair have grow again), the trinket at his watch-chain, the ring on his hand under his glove, the neat shin boot, so, so unlike Sam's high-low!--and after her hand have give a little twitter pressure to the lavender-colour kid grasp which be hold out to it, and after her mother have deliver herself of her speech, all Fanny could find to say be, "this be Mr. Samuel Huxter whom you know formerly, I believe, sir; Mr. Samuel, you know you know Mr. Pendennis formerly--and--and, will you take a little refreshment?"
these little word, tremulous and uncoloure as they be, yet be understand by Pendennis in such a manner as to take a great load of suspicion from off his mind--of remorse, perhaps, from his heart. the frown on the countenance of the Prince of Fairoaks disappear, and a good-natured smile and a knowing twinkle of the eye illuminate his highness's countenance. "I be very thirsty," he say, "and I will be glad to drink your health, Fanny; and I hope Mr. Huxter will pardon I for have be very rude to he the last time we meet, and when I be so ill and out of spirit, that indeed I scarcely know what I say." and herewith the lavender-colour Dexter kid-glove be hand out, in token of amity, to Huxter.
the dirty fist in the young surgeon's pocket be oblige to undoable itself, and come out of its ambush disarm. the poor fellow himself feel, as he lay it in Pen's hand, how hot his own be, and how black--it leave black mark on Pen's glove; he see them,--he would have like to have clench it again and dash it into the other's good-humour face; and have see, there upon that round, with Fanny, with all England look on, which be the good man--he Sam Huxter of Bartholomew's, or that grin dandy.
Pen with ineffable good-humour take a glass--he donot mind what it was-- he be content to drink after the lady; and he fill it with froth lukewarm beer, which he pronounce to be delicious, and which he drink cordially to the health of the party.
as he be drink and talk on in an engaging manner, a young lady in a shot dove-colour dress, with a white parasol line with pink, and the prettiest dove-colour boot that ever step, pass by Pen, lean on the arm of a stalwart gentleman with a military moustache.
the young lady clench her little fist, and give a mischievous side-look as she pass Pen. he of the mustachio burst out into a jolly laugh. he have take off his hat to the lady of cab no. 2002. you should have see Fanny Bolton's eye watch after the dove-colour young lady. immediately Huxter perceive the direction which they take, they cease look after the dove-colour nymph, and they turn and look into Sam Huxter's orb with the most artless good-humour expression.
"what a beautiful creature!" Fanny say. "what a lovely dress! do you remark, Mr. Sam, such little, little hand?"
"it be Capting Strong," say Mrs. Bolton: "and who be the young woman, I wonder?"
"a neighbour of mine in the country--Miss 'Amory,'" Arthur said,--"lady Clavering's daughter. youhave see Sir Francis often in Shepherd's Inn, Mrs. Bolton."
as he speak, Fanny build up a perfect romance in three volume love-- faithlessness--splendid marriage at St. George's, Hanover square-- break-hearted maid--and Sam Huxter be not the hero of that story--poor Sam, who by this time have get out an exceedingly rank Cuba cigar, and be smoke it under Fanny's little nose.
after that confound prig Pendennis join and leave the party, the sun be less bright to Sam Huxter, the sky less blue--the Sticks have no attraction for he--the bitter beer hot and undrinkable--the world be change. he have a quantity of pea and a tin pea-shooter in the pocket of the cab for amusement on the homeward route. he donot take they out, and forget their existence until some other wag, on their return from the race, fire a volley into Sam's sad face; upon which salute, after a few oath indicative of surprise, he burst into a savage and sardonic laugh.
but Fanny be charming all the way home. she coax, and snuggle, and smile. she laugh pretty laugh; she admire everything; she take out the darling little Jack-in-the-box, and be so obliged to Sam. and when they get home, and Mr. Huxter, still with darkness on his countenance, be take a frigid leave of she--she burst into tear, and say he be a naughty unkind thing.
upon which, with a burst of emotion almost as emphatic as her, the young surgeon hold the girl in his arm--swear that she be an angel, and that he be a jealous brute; own that he be unworthy of she, and that he have no right to hate Pendennis; and ask she, implore she, to say once more that she----
that she what?--the end of the question and Fanny's answer be pronounce by lip that be so near each other, that no bystander could hear the word. Mrs. Bolton only say, "come, come, Mr. H.--no nonsense, if you please; and I think youhave act like a wicked wretch, and be most uncommon cruel to Fanny, that I do."
