when Arthur leave no. 2002, he go to pay his respect to the carriage to which, and to the side of her mamma, the dove-colour author of Mes Larmes have by this time return. indefatigable old Major Pendennis be in wait upon Lady Clavering, and have occupy the back seat in her carriage; the box be in possession of young Hopeful, under the care of Captain Strong.
a number of dandy, and man of a certain fashion--of military buck, of young rake of the public office, of those who may be style man's man rather than ladies'--had come about the carriage during its station on the hill--and have exchange a word or two with Lady Clavering, and a little talk (a little "chaff," some of the most elegant of the man style their conversation) with Miss Amory. they have offer her sportive bet, and exchange with she all sort of free-talk and knowing innuendo. they point out to she who be on the course: and the "who" be not always the person a young lady should know.
when Pen come up to Lady Clavering's carriage, he have to push his way through a crowd of these young buck who be pay their court to Miss Amory, in order to arrive as near that young lady, who beckon he by many pretty signal to her side.
"Je lay vue," she say; "Elle a de bien beaux yeux; vous ete un monster!"
"why monster?" say Pen, with a laugh; "hone suit qui mal y peen. my young friend, yonder, be as well protect as any young lady in Christendom. she have her mamma on one side, her pretend on the other. could any harm happen to a girl between those two?"
"one do not know what may or may not arrive," say Miss Blanche, in French, "when a girl have the mind, and when she be pursue by a wicked monster like you. figure to yourself, Major, that I come to find Monsieur, your nephew, near to a cab, by two lady, and a man, oh, such a man! and who eat lobster, and who laugh, who laugh!"
"it do not strike I that the man laugh," Pen say, "and as for lobster, I think he would have like to eat I after the lobster. he shake hand with I, and grip I so, that he bruise my glove black-and-blue. he be a young surgeon. he come from Clavering. donot you remember the gilt pestle and mortar in High Street?"
"if he attend you when you be sick," continue Miss Amory, "he will kill you. he will serve you right; for you be a monster."
the perpetual recurrence to the word "monster" jarred upon Pen. "she speak about these matter a great deal too lightly," he think. "if I have be a monster, as she call it, she would have receive I just the same. this be not the way in which an english lady should speak or think. Laura would not speak in that way, thank God;" and as he think so, his own countenance fall.
"of what be you think? be you go to bouder I at present?" Blanche ask. "Major, scold your mechant nephew. he do not amuse I at all. he be as bete as Captain Crackenbury."
"what be you say about I, Miss Amory?" say the guardsman, with a grin. "if itbe anything good, say it in English, for I donot understand French when itbe speak so devilish quick."
"it benot anything good, Crack," say Crackenbury's fellow, Captain Clinker. "letus come away, and donot spoil sport. they say Pendennis be sweet upon she."
"Ibe tell hebe a devilish clever fellow," sigh Crackenbury. "Lady Violet Lebas say hebe a devilish clever fellow. he write a work, or a poem, or something; and he write those devilish clever thing in the--in the paper, you know. Dammy, I wish I be a clever fellow, Clinker."
"thatbe past wish for, Crack, my boy," the other say. "I cannot write a good book, but I think I can make a pretty good one on the Derby. what a flat Clavering be! and the Begum! I like that old Begum. shebe worth ten of her daughter. how please the old girl be at win the lottery!"
"claver's safe to pay up, benot he?" ask Captain Crackenbury.
"I hope so," say his friend; and they disappear, to enjoy themselves among the stick.
before the end of the day's amusement, many more gentleman of Lady Clavering's acquaintance come up to her carriage, and chat with the party which it contain. the worthy lady be in high spirit and good-humour, laugh and talk accord to she willnot, and offer refreshment to all her friend, until her ample basket and bottle be empty, and her servant and postillion be in such a royal state of excitement as servant and postillion commonly be upon the Derby day.
the Major remark that some of the visitor to the carriage appear to look with rather queer and mean glance towards its owner. "how easily she take it!" one man whisper to another. "the Begum's make of money," the friend reply. "how easily she take what?" think old Pendennis. "have anybody lose any money?" Lady Clavering say she be happy in the morning because Sir Francis have promise she not to bet.
Mr. Welbore, the country neighbour of the Claverings, be pass the carriage, when he be call back by the Begum, who rally he for wish to cut she. "why donot he come before? why donot he come to lunch?" her ladyship be in great delight, she tell he--she tell everybody, that she have win five pound in a lottery. as she convey this piece of intelligence to he, Mr. Welbore look so particularly know, and withal melancholy, that a dismal apprehension seize upon Major Pendennis. "he would go and look after the horse and those rascal of postillion, who be so long in come round." when he come back to the carriage, his usually benign and smirk countenance be obscure by some sorrow. "what be the matter with you now?" the good-natured Begum ask. the Major pretend a headache from the fatigue and sunshine of the day. the carriage wheel off the course and take its way Londonwards, not the least brilliant equipage in that vast and picturesque procession. the tipsy driver dash gallantly over the turf, amidst the admiration of foot-passenger, the ironical cheer of the little donkey-carriage and spring van, and the loud objurgation of horse-and-chaise man, with whom the reckless post-boy come in contact. the jolly Begum look the picture of good-humour as she recline on her splendid cushion; the lovely Sylphide smile with languid elegance. many an honest holiday-maker with his family wad into a tax-cart, many a cheap dandy work his way home on his weary hack, admire that brilliant turn-out, and think, no doubt, how happy those "swell" must be. Strong sit on the box still, with a lordly voice call to the post-boy and the crowd. Master Frank have be put inside of the carriage and be asleep there by the side of the Major, doze away the effect of the constant luncheon and champagne of which he have freely partake.
the Major be revolve in his mind meanwhile the news the receipt of which have make he so grave. "if Sir Francis Clavering go on in this way," Pendennis the eld thought, "this little tipsy rascal will be as bankrupt as his father and grandfather before he. the Begum's fortune cannot stand such drain upon it: no fortune can stand they: she have pay his debt half a dozen time already. a few year more of the turf, and a few coup like this, will ruin she."
"donot you think we could get up race at Clavering, mamma?" Miss Amory ask. "yes, we must have they there again. there be race there in the old time, the good old time. itbe a national amusement, you know: and we could have a Clavering ball: and we might have dance for the tenantry, and rustic sport in the park--oh, it would be charming."
"capital fun," say mamma. "wouldnot it, Major?"
"the turf be a very expensive amusement, my dear lady," Major Pendennis answer, with such a rueful face, that the Begum rally he, and ask laughingly whether he have lose money on the race?
after a slumber of about an hour and a half, the heir of the house begin to exhibit symptom of wakefulness, stretch his youthful arm over the Major's face, and kick his sister's knee as she sate opposite to he. when the amiable youth be quite restore to consciousness, he begin a sprightly conversation.
"I say, Ma," he say, "Ihave go and do it this time, I have."
"what have you go and do, franky dear?" ask Mamma.
"how much be seventeen half-crown? two pound and half-a crown, benot it? I draw Borax in our lottery, but I buy Podasokus and Man-milliner of Leggat minor for two open tart and a bottle of ginger-beer."
"you little wicked gambling creature, how dare you begin so soon?" cry Miss Amory.
"hold your tongue, if you please. who ever ask your leave, miss?" the brother say. "and I say, ma----"
"well, franky dear?"
"youwill tip I all the same, you know, when I go back----" and here he break out into a laugh. "I say, Ma, shall I tell you something?"
the Begum express her desire to hear this something, and her son and heir continue:
"when I and Strong be down at the grand stand after the race, and I be talk to Leggat minor, who be there with his governor, I see Pa look as savage as a bear. and I say, Ma, Leggat minor tell I that he hear his governor say that Pa have lose seven thousand back the favourite. Iwill never back the favourite when Ibe of age. no, no--hang I if I do: leave I alone, Strong, will you?"
"Captain Strong! Captain Strong! be this true?" cry out the unfortunate Begum. "have Sir Francis be bet again? he promise I he wouldnot. he give I his word of honour he wouldnot."
Strong, from his place on the box, have overhear the end of young Clavering's communication, and be try in vain to stop his unlucky tongue.
"Ibe afraid itbe true, ma'am," he say, turn round, "I deplore the loss as much as you can. he promise I as he promise you; but the play be too strong for he! he cannot refrain from it."
Lady Clavering at this sad news burst into a fit of tear. she deplore her wretched fate as the most miserable of woman, she declare she would separate, and pay no more debt for the ungrateful man. she narrate with tearful volubility a score of story only too authentic, which show how her husband have deceive, and how constantly she have befriend he: and in this melancholy condition, whilst young Hopeful be think about the two guineas which he himself have win; and the major revolving, in his darkened mind, whether certain plan which he have be form have well not be abandon; the splendid carriage drive up at length to the Begum's house in Grosvenor Place; the idler and boy linger about the place to witness, accord to public willnot, the close of the Derby Day, cheer the carriage as it draw up, and envy the happy folk who descend from it.
"and itbe for the son of this man that I be make a beggar!" Blanche say, quiver with anger, as she walk upstairs lean on the Major's arm-- "for this cheat--for this blackleg--for this liar--for this robber of woman."
"calm yourself, my dear Miss Blanche," the old gentleman say; "I pray calm yourself. you have be hardly treat, most unjustly. but remember that you have always a friend in I, and trust to an old fellow who will try and serve you."
and the young lady, and the heir of the hopeful house of Clavering, have retire to their bed, the remain three of the Epsom party remain for some time in deep consultation.
CHAPTER LX
Explanations
almost a year, as the reader will perceive, have pass since an event describe a few page back. Arthur's black coat be about to be exchange for a blue one. his person have undergo other more pleasing and remarkable change. his wig have be lay aside, and his hair, though somewhat thin, have return to public view. and he have have the honour of appear at Court in the uniform of a Cornet of the Clavering troop of the ----shire Yeomanry Cavalry, be present to the Sovereign by the Marquis of Steyne.
this be a measure strongly and pathetically urge by Arthur's uncle. the Major would not hear of a year pass before this ceremony of gentlemanhood be go through. the old gentleman think that his nephew should belong to some rather more select Club than the Megatherium; and have announce everywhere in the world his disappointment that the young man's property have turn out not by any mean as well as he could have hop, and be under fifteen hundred a year.
that be the amount at which Pendennis's property be set down in the world--where his publisher begin to respect he much more than formerly, and where even mamma be by no mean uncivil to he. for if the pretty daughter be, naturally, to marry people of very different expectation --at any rate, he will be eligible for the plain one: and if the brilliant and fascinating Myra be to hook an Earl, poor little Beatrice, who have one shoulder high than the other, must hang on to some boor through life, and why should not Mr. Pendennis be her support? in the very first winter after the accession to his mother's fortune, Mrs. Hawxby in a country-house cause her Beatrice to learn billiard from Mr. Pendennis and would be drive by nobody but he in the pony carriage, because he be literary and her Beatrice be literary too, and declare that the young man, under the instigation of his horrid old uncle, have behave most infamously in trifle with Beatrice's feeling. the truth be the old gentleman, who know Mrs. Hawxby's character, and how desperately that lady would practise upon unwary young man, have come to the country-house in question and carry Arthur out of the danger of her immediate claw, though not out of the reach of her tongue. the elder Pendennis would have have his nephew pass a part of the Christmas at Clavering, whither the family have return; but Arthur have not the heart for that. Clavering be too near poor old Fairoaks; and that be too full of sad recollection for the young man.
we have lose sight of the Claverings, too, until their reappearance upon the Epsom race-ground, and must give a brief account of they in the interval. during the past year, the world have not treat any member of the Clavering family very kindly; Lady Clavering, one of the well-natured woman that ever enjoy a good dinner, or make a slip in grammar, have have her appetite and good-nature sadly try by constant family grievance, and dispute such as make the effort of the good french cook unpalatable, and the most delicately-stuff sofa-cushion hard to lie on. "Iwould rather have a turnip, Strong, for dessert, than that pineapple, and all they Muscatel grape, from Clavering," say poor Lady Clavering, look at her dinner-table, and confide her grief to her faithful friend, "if I could but have a little quiet to eat it with. oh, how much happy I be when I be a widow and before all this money fall in to I!"
the Clavering family have indeed make a false start in life, and have get neither conduct, nor position, nor thank for the hospitality which they administer, nor a return of kindness from the people whom they entertain. the success of their first London season be doubtful; and their failure afterwards notorious. "human patience be not great enough to put up with Sir Francis Clavering," people say. "he be too hopelessly low, dull, and disreputable. you could not say what, but there be a taint about the house and its entourage. who be the Begum, with her money, and without her h's, and where do she come from? what an extraordinary little piece of conceit the daughter be, with her gallicised grace and daring affectation, not fit for well-breed english girl to associate with! what strange people be those they assemble round about they! Sir Francis Clavering be a gambler, live notoriously in the society of blackleg and profligate. hely Clinker, who be in his regiment, say that he not only cheat at card, but show the white feather. what could Lady Rockminster have mean by take she up? after the first season, indeed, Lady Rockminster, who have take up Lady Clavering, put she down; the great lady would not take their daughter to her party; the young man who attend they behave with the most odious freedom and scornful familiarity; and poor Lady Clavering herself avow that she be oblige to take what she call 'the canal' into her parlour, because the tip-top wouldnot come."
she have not the slight ill-will towards "the canal," the poor dear lady, or any pride about herself, or idea, that she be well than her neighbour; but she have take implicitly the order which on her entry into the world her social godmother have give she: she have be willing to know whom they know, and ask whom they ask. the "canal," in fact, be much pleasanter than what be call "society;" but, as we say before, that to leave a mistress be easy, while, on the contrary, to be leave by she be cruel: so you may give up society without any great pang, or anything but a sensation of relief at the parting; but severe be the mortification and pain you have if society give up you.
one young man of fashion we have mention, who at least it might have be expect would have be find faithful amongst the faithless, and Harry Foker, Esq., be indeed that young man. but he have not manage matter with prudence, and the unhappy passion at first confide to Pen become notorious and ridiculous to the town, be carry to the ear of his weak and fond mother; and finally bring under the cognisance of the bald-headed and inflexible Foker senior.
when Mr. Foker learn this disagreeable news, there take place between he and his son a violent and painful scene, which end in the poor little gentleman's banishment from England for a year, with a positive order to return at the expiration of that time and complete his marriage with his cousin, or to retire into private life and three hundred a year altogether, and never see parent or brewery more. Mr. Henry Foker go away then, carry with he that grief and care which pass free at the strict custom-house, and which proverbially accompany the exile; and with this crape over his eye, even the Parisian Boulevard look melancholy to he, and the sky of Italy black.
to Sir Francis Clavering, that year be a most unfortunate one. the event describe in the last chapter come to complete the ruin of the year. it be that year of grace in which, as our sporting reader may remember, Lord Harrowhill's horse (he be a classical young nobleman, and name his stud out of the Iliad)--when Podasokus win the Derby, to the dismay of the know one, who pronounce the win horse's name in various extraordinary way, and who back Borax, who be nowhere in the race. Sir Francis Clavering, who be intimate with some of the most rascally character of the turf, and, of course, have "valuable information," have lay heavy odd against the win horse, and back the favourite freely, and the result of his dealing be, as his son correctly state to poor Lady Clavering, a loss of seven thousand pound.
indeed, it be a cruel blow upon the lady, who have discharge her husband's debt many time over; who have receive as many time his oath and promise of amendment; who have pay his money-lender and horse-dealer; who have furnish his town and country house, and who be call upon now instantly to meet this enormous sum, the penalty of her cowardly husband's extravagance.
it have be describe in former page how the elder Pendennis have become the adviser of the Clavering family, and, in his quality of intimate friend of the house, have go over every room of ii, and even see that ugly closet which we all of we have, and in which, accord to the proverb, the family skeleton be lock up. about the Baronet's pecuniary matter, if the Major do not know, it be because clavering himself do not know they, and hide they from himself and other in such a hopeless entanglement of lie that it be impossible for adviser or attorney or principal to get an accurate knowledge of his affair. but, concern Lady Clavering, the Major be much well inform; and when the unlucky mishap of the Derby arise, he take upon himself to become completely and thoroughly acquaint with all her mean, whatsoever they be; and be now accurately inform of the vast and repeat sacrifice which the widow Amory have make in behalf of her present husband.
he do not conceal--and he have win no small favour from Miss Blanche by avow it--his opinion, that Lady Clavering's daughter have be hardly treat at the expense of her son, by her second marriage: and in his conversation with Lady Clavering have fairly hint that he think Miss Blanche ought to have a well provision. we have say that he have already give the widow to understand that he know all the particular of her early and unfortunate history, have be in India at the time when --when the painful circumstance occur which have end in her parting from her first husband. he could tell she where to find the Calcutta newspaper which contain the account of Amory's trial, and he show, and the Begum be not a little grateful to he for his forbearance, how, be aware all along of this mishap which have befall she, he have keep all knowledge of it to himself, and be constantly the friend of her family.
"interested motif, my dear Lady Clavering," he say, "of course I may have have. we all have interested motif, and mine, I donot conceal from you, be to make a marriage between my nephew and your daughter." to which Lady Clavering, perhaps with some surprise that the Major should choose her family for a union with his own, say she be quite willing to consent.
but frankly he say, "my dear lady, my boy have but five hundred a year, and a wife with ten thousand pound to her fortune would scarcely better he. we could do well for he than that, permit I to say, and he be a shrewd, cautious young fellow who have sow his wild oats now--who have very good part and plenty of ambition--and whose object in marry be to well himself. if you and Sir Francis choose--and Sir Francis, take my word for it, will refuse you nothing--you could put Arthur in a way to advance very considerably in the world, and show the stuff which he have in he. of what use be that seat in Parliament to Clavering, who scarcely ever show his face in the House, or speak a word there? Ibe tell by gentleman who hear my boy at Oxbridge, that he be famous as an orator, begad!--and once put his foot into the stirrup and mount he, Ihave no doubt he willnot be the last of the field, ma'am. Ihave test the chap, and know he pretty well, I think. he be much too lazy, and careless, and flighty a fellow, to make a jog-trot journey, and arrive, as your lawyer do, at the end of their life! but give he a start and good friend, and an opportunity, and take my word for it, hewill make himself a name that his son shall be proud of. I donot see any way for a fellow like he to parvenir, but by make a prudent marriage--not with a beggarly heiress-- to sit down for life upon a miserable fifteen hundred a year--but with somebody whom he can help, and who can help he forward in the world, and whom he can give a good name and a station in the country, begad, in return for the advantage which she bring he. it would be well for you to have a distinguished son-in-law, than to keep your husband on in Parliament, whobe of no good to himself or to anybody else there, and thatbe, I say, why Ihave be interested about you, and offer you what I think a good bargain for both."
"you know I look upon Arthur as one of the family almost now," say the good-natured Begum; "he come and go when he like; and the more I think of his dear mother, the more I see therebe few people so good--none so good to I. and Ibe sure I cry when I hear of her death, and would have go into mourning for her myself, only black donot become I. and I know who his mother want he to marry--Laura, I mean--whom old Lady Rockminster have take such a fancy to, and, no wonder. shebe a well girl than my girl. I know both. and my Betsy--Blanche, I mean--ain't be a comfort to I, Major. itbe Laura Pen ought to marry.
"Marry on five hundred a year! my dear good soul, you be mad!" Major Pendennis say. "think over what I have say to you. do nothing in your affair with that unhappy husband of your without consult I; and remember that old Pendennis be always your friend."
for some time previous, Pen's uncle have hold similar language to Miss Amory. he have point out to she the convenience of the match which he have at heart, and be bind to say, that mutual convenience be of all thing the very good in the world to marry upon--the only thing. "look at your love-marriage, my dear young creature. the love-match people be the most notorious of all for quarrel afterwards; and a girl who run away with Jack to Gretna Green, constantly run away with Tom to Switzerland afterwards. the great point in marriage be for people to agree to be useful to one another. the lady bring the mean, and the gentleman avail himself of they. my boy's wife bring the horse, and begad Pen go in and win the plate. thatbe what I call a sensible union. a couple like that have something to talk to each other about when they come together. if you have Cupid himself to talk to--if Blanche and Pen be Cupid and Psyche, begad--they'd begin to yawn after a few evening, if they have nothing but sentiment to speak on."
as for Miss Amory, she be content enough with Pen as long as there be nobody well. and how many other young lady be like her?--and how many love-marriage carry on well to the last?--and how sentimental firm do not finish in bankruptcy?--and how many heroic passion donot dwindle down into despicable indifference, or end in shameful defeat?
these view of life and philosophy the Major be constantly, accord to his custom, inculcate to Pen, whose mind be such that he could see the right on both side of many question, and, comprehend the sentimental life which be quite out of the reach of the honest Major's intelligence, could understand the practical life too, and accommodate himself, or think he could accommodate himself, to it. so it come to pass that during the spring succeed his mother's death he become a good deal under the influence of his uncle's advice, and domesticate in Lady Clavering's house; and in a measure be accept by Miss Amory without be a suitor, and be receive without be engage. the young people be extremely familiar, without be particularly sentimental, and meet and part with each other in perfect good-humour. "and I," think Pendennis, "be the fellow who eight year ago have a Grand passion, and last year be rag in a fever about Briseis!"
yes, it be the same Pendennis, and time have bring to he, as to the rest of we, its ordinary consequence, consolation, development. we alter very little. when we talk of this man or that woman be no long the same person whom we remember in youth, and remark (of course to deplore) change in our friend, we donot, perhaps, calculate that circumstance only bring out the latent defect or quality, and do not create it. the selfish languor and indifference of to-day's possession be the consequence of the selfish ardour of yesterday's pursuit: the scorn and weariness which cry vanita vanitatum be but the lassitude of the sick appetite pal with pleasure: the insolence of the successful parvenu be only the necessary continuance of the career of the needy struggler: our mental change be like our grey hair or our wrinkles-- but the fulfilment of the plan of mortal growth and decay: that which be snow-white now be glossy black once; that which be sluggish obesity to-day be boisterous rosy health a few year back; that calm weariness, benevolent, resign, and disappoint, be ambition, fierce and violent, but a few year since, and have only settle into submissive repose after many a battle and defeat. lucky he who can bear his failure so generously, and give up his broken sword to fate the Conqueror with a manly and humble heart! be you not awestricken, you, friendly reader, who, take the page up for a moment's light reading, lie it down, perchance, for a graver reflection,--to think how you, who have consummate your success or your disaster, may be hold mark station, or a hopeless and nameless place, in the crowd--who have pass through how many struggle of defeat, success, crime, remorse, to yourself only known!--who may have love and grow cold, weep and laugh again, how often!--to think how you be the same, You, whom in childhood you remember, before the voyage of life begin? it have be prosperous, and you be rid into port, the people huzzae and the gun saluting,--and the lucky captain bow from the ship's side, and there be a care under the star on his breast which nobody know of: or you be wreck, and lash, hopeless, to a solitary spar out at sea:--the sink man and the successful one be think each about home, very likely, and remember the time when they be child; alone on the hopeless spar, drown out of sight; alone in the midst of the crowd applaud you.
CHAPTER LXI
Conversations
our good-natured Begum be at first so much enraged at this last instance of her husband's duplicity and folly, that she refuse to give Sir Francis Clavering any aid in order to meet his debt of honour, and declare that she would separate from he, and leave he to the consequence of his incorrigible weakness and waste. after that fatal day's transaction at the Derby, the unlucky gambler be in such a condition of mind that he be disposed to avoid everybody; alike his turf-associate with whom he have make the debt which he tremble lest he should not have the mean of pay, and his wife, his long-suffer banker, on whom he reasonably doubt whether he should be allow any long to draw. when Lady Clavering ask the next morning whether Sir Francis be in the house, she receive answer that he have not return that night, but have send a messenger to his valet, order he to forward clothe and letter by the bearer. Strong know that he should have a visit or a message from he in the course of that or the subsequent day, and accordingly get a note beseech he to call upon his distracted friend F. C. at Short Hotel, Blackfriars, and ask for Mr. Francis there. for the Baronet be a gentleman of that peculiarity of mind that he would rather tell a lie than not, and always begin a contest with fortune by run away and hide himself. the boot of Mr. Short's establishment, who carry Clavering's message to Grosvenor Place, and bring back his carpet-bag, be instantly aware who be the owner of the bag, and he impart his information to the footman who be lay the breakfast-table, who carry down the news to the servants'-hall, who take it to Mrs. Bonner, my lady's housekeeper and confidential maid, who carry it to my lady. and thus every single person in the Grosvenor Place establishment know that Sir Francis be in hiding, under the name of Francis, at an inn in the Blackfriars Road. and Sir Francis's coachman tell the news to other gentleman's coachman, who carry it to their master, and to the neighbouring Tattersall's, where very gloomy anticipation be form that Sir Francis Clavering be about to make a tour in the Levant.
in the course of that day the number of letter address to Sir Francis Clavering, Bart., which find their way to his hall-table, be quite remarkable. the french cook send in his account to my lady; the tradesman who supply her ladyship's table, and Messrs. Finer and Gimcrack, the mercer and ornamental dealer, and Madame Crinoline, the eminent milliner, also forward their little bill to her ladyship, in company with Miss Amory's private, and by no mean inconsiderable, account at each establishment.
in the afternoon of the day after the Derby, when Strong (after a colloquy with his principal at Short's Hotel, whom he find cry and drink Curacoa) call to transact business accord to his custom at Grosvenor Place, he find all these suspicious document range in the Baronet's study; and begin to open they and examine they with a rueful countenance.
Mrs. Bonner, my lady's maid and housekeeper, come down upon he whilst engage in this occupation. Mrs. Bonner, a part of the family and as necessary to her mistress as the Chevalier be to Sir Francis, be of course on Lady Clavering's side in the dispute between she and her husband, and as by duty bind even more angry than her ladyship herself.
"she willnot pay, if she take my advice," Mrs. Bonner say. "youwill please to go back to Sir Francis, Captain--and he lurk about in a low public-house and donot dare to face his wife like a man!--and say that we willnot pay his debt no long. we make a man of he, we take he out of gaol (and other folk too perhaps), wehave pay his debt over and over again--we set he up in Parliament and give he a house in town and country, and where he donot dare show his face, the shabby sneak! wehave give he the horse he rid and the dinner he eat and the very clothe he have on his back; and we will give he no more. our fortune, such as be leave of it, be leave to ourselves, and we willnot waste any more of it on this ungrateful man. wewill give he enough to live upon and leave he, thatbe what wewill do: and thatbe what you may tell he from Susan Bonner."
Susan Bonner's mistress hearing of Strong's arrival send for he at this juncture, and the Chevalier go up to her ladyship not without hope that he should find she more tractable than her factotum Mrs. Bonner. many a time before have he plead his client's cause with Lady Clavering and cause her good-nature to relent. he try again once more. he paint in dismal colour the situation in which he have find Sir Francis: and would not answer for any consequence which might ensue if he could not find mean of meet his engagement.
"kill hisself," laugh Mrs. Bonner, "kill hisself, will he? die's the good thing he could do." Strong vow that he have find he with the razor on the table; but at this, in her turn, Lady Clavering laugh bitterly. "hewill do himself no harm, as long as therebe a shilling left of which he can rob a poor woman. his lifebe quite safe, captain: you may depend upon that. ah! it be a bad day that ever I set eye on he."
"hebe bad than the first man," cry out my lady's aide-de-camp. "he be a man, he be--a wild devil, but he have the courage of a man--whereas this fellow--whatbe the use of my lady pay his bill, and sell her diamond, and forgive he? hewill be as bad again next year. the very next chance he have hewill be a-cheating of she, and rob of she; and her money will go to keep a pack of rogue and swindler--I donot mean you, Captain--you've be a good friend to we enough, bat we wish wewould never set eye on you."
the Chevalier see from the word which Mrs. Bonner have let slip regard the diamond, that the kind Begum be dispose to relent once more at least, and that there be hope still for his principal.
"upon my word, ma'am," he say, with a real feeling of sympathy for Lady Clavering's trouble, and admiration for her untiring good-nature, and with a show of enthusiasm which advance not a little his graceless patron's cause--"anything you say against Clavering, or Mrs. Bonner here cry out against I, be no well than we deserve, both of we, and it be an unlucky day for you when you see either. he have behave cruelly to you and if you be not the most generous and forgiving woman in the world, I know there would be no chance for he. but you cannot let the father of your son be a disgrace man, and send little Frank into the world with such a stain upon he. tie he down; bind he by any promise you like: I vouch for he that he will subscribe they."
"and break them," say Mrs. Bonner.
"and keep them this time," cry out Strong. "he must keep they. if you could have see how he weep, ma'am! 'oh, Strong,' he say to I, 'itbe not for myself I feel now: itbe for my boy--itbe for the good woman in England, whom I have treat basely--I know I have.' he donot intend to bet upon this race, ma'am--indeed he donot. he be cheat into it: all the ring be take in. he think he might make the bet quite safely, without the least risk. and it will be a lesson to he for all his life long. to see a man cry--oh, itbe dreadful."
"he donot think much of make my dear missus cry," say Mrs. Bonner-- "poor dear soul!--look if he do, Captain."
* * * * * *
"if youhave the soul of a man, clavering," Strong say to his principal, when he recount this scene to he, "youwill keep your promise this time: and, so help I Heaven! if you break word with she, Iwill turn against you, and tell all."
"what all?" cry Mr. Francis, to whom his ambassador bring the news back at Short's Hotel, where Strong find the Baronet cry and drinking curacoa.
"Psha! do you suppose I be a fool?" burst out Strong. "do you suppose I could have live so long in the world, Frank Clavering, without have my eye about I? you know I have but to speak and you be a beggar to-morrow. and I be not the only man who know your secret."
"who else do?" gasp Clavering.
"Old Pendennis do, or I be very much mistaken. he recognise the man the first night he see he, when he come drunk into your house."
"he know it, do he?" shriek out Clavering. "damn he--kill he."
"youwould like to kill we all, wouldnot you, old boy?" say Strong, with a sneer, puff his cigar.
the Baronet dash his weak hand against his forehead; perhaps the other have interpret his wish rightly. "oh, Strong!" he cry, "if I dare, Iwould put an end to myself, for Ibe the d----- miserable dog in all England. itbe that that make I so wild and reckless. itbe that which make I take to drink" (and he drank, with a trembling hand, a bumper of his fortifier--the curacoa), "and to live about with these thief. I know theybe thief, every one of them, d----d thief. and--and how can I help it?--and I donot know it, you know--and, by Gad, Ibe innocent-- and until I see the d----d scoundrel first, I know no more about it than the dead--and Iwill fly, and Iwill go abroad out of the reach of the confounded hell, and Iwill bury myself in a forest, by Gad! and hang myself up to a tree--and, oh--i'm the most miserable beggar in all England!" and so with more tear, shriek, and curse, the impotent wretch vent his grief and deplore his unhappy fate; and, in the midst of groan and despair and blasphemy, vow his miserable repentance.
the honoured proverb which declare that to be an ill wind which blow good to nobody, be verify in the case of Sir Francis Clavering, and another of the occupant of Mr. Strong's chamber in Shepherd's Inn. the man be "good," by a lucky hap, with whom Colonel Altamont make his bet; and on the settle day of the Derby--as Captain Clinker, who be appoint to settle Sir Francis Clavering's book for he (for Lady Clavering by the advice of Major Pendennis, would not allow the Baronet to liquidate his own money transaction), pay over the note to the Baronet's many creditor--Colonel Altamont have the satisfaction of receive the odd of thirty to one in fifty, which he have take against the win horse of the day.
number of the Colonel's friend be present on the occasion to congratulate he on his luck--all Altamont's own set, and the gent who meet in the private parlour of the convivial Wheeler, my host of the Harlequin's Head, come to witness their comrade's good fortune, and would have like, with a generous sympathy for success, to share in it. "now be the time," Tom Driver have suggest to the Colonel, "to have up the specie ship that be sink in the Gulf of Mexico, with the three hundred and eighty thousand dollar on board, besides bar and doubloon." "the Tredyddlums be very low--to be buy for an old song--never be such an opportunity for buy share," Mr. Keightley insinuate; and Jack Holt press forward his tobacco-smuggling scheme, the audacity of which please the Colonel more than any other of the speculation propose to he. then of the Harlequin's Head boy: there be Jack Rackstraw, who know of a pair of horse which the Colonel must buy; Tom Fleet, whose satirical paper, the Swell, want but two hundred pound of capital to be worth a thousand a year to any man--"with such a power and influence, Colonel, you rogue, and the entree of the green-room in London," Tom urge; whilst little Moss Abiams entreat the Colonel not to listen to these absurd fellow with their humbug speculation, but to invest his money in some good bill which Moss could get for he, and which would return he fifty per cent as safe as the Bank of England.
each and all of these worthy come round the Colonel with their various blandishment; but he have courage enough to resist they, and to button up his note in the pocket of his coat, and go home to Strong, and "sport" the outer door of the chamber. Honest Strong have give his fellow-lodger good advice about all his acquaintance; and though, when press, he do not mind frankly take twenty pound himself out of the Colonel's winning, Strong be a great deal too upright to let other cheat he.
he be not a bad fellow when in good fortune, this Altamont. he order a smart livery for Grady, and make poor old Costigan shed tear of quickly dry gratitude by give he a five-pound note after a snug dinner at the Back Kitchen, and he buy a green shawl for Mrs. Bolton, and a yellow one for Fanny: the most brilliant "sacrifice" of a Regent Street haberdasher's window. and a short time after this, upon her birthday, which happen in the month of June, Miss Amory receive from "a friend" a parcel contain an enormous brass inlay writing-desk, in which there be a set of amethyst, the most hideous eye ever look upon,--a musical snuff-box, and two Keepsakes of the year before last, and accompany with a couple of gown piece of the most astounding colour, the receipt of which good make the Sylphide laugh and wonder immoderately. now it be a fact that Colonel Altamont have make a purchase of cigar and french silk from some duffer in Fleet Street about this period; and he be find by Strong in the open Auction Room in Cheapside, have invest some money in two desk, several pair of richly-plat candlestick, a dinner epergne, and a bagatelle-board. the dinner epergne remain at chamber, and figure at the banquet there, which the Colonel give pretty freely. it seem beautiful in his eye, until Jack Holt say it look as if it have be take "in a bill." and Jack Holt certainly know.
the dinner be pretty frequent at chamber, and Sir Francis Clavering condescend to partake of they constantly. his own house be shut up: the successor of Mirobolant, who have send in his bill so prematurely, be dismiss by the indignant Lady Clavering: the luxuriance of the establishment be greatly prune and reduce. one of the large footman be cashier, upon which the other give warning, not like to serve without his mate, or in a family where on'y one footman be kep'. General and severe economical reform be practise by the Begum in her whole household, in consequence of the extravagance of which her graceless husband have be guilty. the Major, as her ladyship's friend; Strong, on the part of poor Clavering; her ladyship's lawyer, and the honest Begum herself, execute these reform with promptitude and severity. after pay the Baronet's debt, the settlement of which occasion considerable public scandal, and cause the Baronet to sink even lower in the world's estimation than he have be before, Lady Clavering quit London for Tunbridge Wells in high dudgeon, refuse to see her reprobate husband, whom nobody pity. Clavering remain in London patiently, by no mean anxious to meet his wife's just indignation, and sneak in and out of the House of Commons, whence he and Captain Raff and Mr. Marker would go to have a game at billiard and a cigar or show in the sport public-house; or might be see lurk about Lincoln's Inn and his lawyer', where the principal keep he for hour wait, and the clerk wink at each other, as he sate in their office. no wonder that he relish the dinner at Shepherd's Inn, and be perfectly resign there: resign? he be so happy nowhere else; he be wretched amongst his equal, who scorn he--but here he be the chief guest at the table, where they continually address he with "yes, Sir Francis" and "no, Sir Francis," where he tell his wretched joke, and where he quaver his dreary little french song, after Strong have sing his Jovial chorus, and honest Costigan have pip his irish ditty. such a jolly menage as Strong's, with Grady's irish-stew, and the Chevalier's brew of punch after dinner, would have be welcome to many a well man than Clavering, the solitude of whose great house at home frighten he, where he be attend only by the old woman who keep the house, and his valet who sneer at he.
"yes, dammit," say he to his friend in Shepherd's Inn, "that fellow of mine, I must turn he away, only I owe he two year' wage, curse he, and cannot ask my lady. he bring I my tea cold of a morning, with a dem'd leaden teaspoon, and he say my lady's send all the plate to the banker's because it benot safe.--Now benot it hard that she willnot trust I with a single teaspoon; benot it ungentlemanlike, Altamont? you know my lady's of low birth--that be--I beg your pardon--hem--that is, itbe most cruel of she not to show more confidence in I. and the very servant begin to laugh--the damn scoundrel! I break every bone in their great hulking body, curse them, I will.--they donot answer my bell: and --and my man be at Vauxhall last night with one of my dress-shirt and my velvet waistcoat on, I know it be mine--the confound impudent blackguard--and he go on dancing before my eye confound he! Ibe sure hewill live to be hang--he deserve to be hang--all those infernal rascal of valet."
he be very kind to Altamont now: he listen to the Colonel's loud story when Altamont describe how--when he be work his way home once from New Zealand, where he have be on a whale expedition--he and his comrade have be oblige to slink on board at night, to escape from their wife, by Jove--and how the poor devil put out in their canoe when they see the ship under sail, and paddle madly after she: how he have be lose in the bush once for three month in New South Wales, when he be there once on a trading speculation: how he have see Boney at Saint Helena, and be present to he with the rest of the officer of the Indiaman of which he be a mate--to all these tale (and over his cup Altamont tell many of they; and, it must be own, lie and brag a great deal) Sir Francis now listen with great attention; make a point of drink wine with Altamont at dinner and of treat he with every distinction.
"leave he alone, I know what hebe a-come to," Altamont say, laugh to Strong, who remonstrate with he, "and leave I alone; I know what Ibe a-telling, very well. I be officer on board an Indiaman, so I be; I trade to New South Wales, so I do, in a ship of my own, and lose she. I become officer to the Nawaub, so I do; only I and my royal master have have a difference, Strong--thatbe it. whobe the well or the bad for what I tell? or know anything about I? the other chap be dead--shoot in the bush, and his body reckonise at Sydney. if I think anybody would split, do you think I wouldnot wring his neck? Ihave do as good before now, Strong--I tell you how I do for the overseer before I take leave-- but in fair fight, I mean--in fair fight; or, rayther, he have the good of it. he have his gun and bay'net, and I have only an axe. fifty of them see it--ay, and cheer I when I do it--and Iwould do it again,--him, wouldnot I? I benot afraid of anybody; and Iwould have the life of the man who split upon I. thatbe my maxim, and pass I the liquor.--You wouldnot turn on a man. I know you. yoube an honest feller, and will stand by a feller, and have look death in the face like a man. but as for that lily-livere sneak--that poor lyin' swindlin' cringin' cur of a Clavering--who stand in my shoe--stand in my shoe, hang he! Iwill make he pull my boot off and clean them, I will. ha, ha!" here he burst out into a wild laugh, at which Strong get up and put away the brandy-bottle. the other still laugh good-humouredly. "yoube right, old boy," he say; "you always keep your head cool, you do--and when I begin to talk too much--I say, when I begin to pitch, I authorise you, and order you, and command you, to put away the rum-bottle."
"take my counsel, Altamont," Strong say, gravely, "and mind how you deal with that man. donot make it too much his interest to get rid of you; or who know what he may do?"
the event for which, with cynical enjoyment, Altamont have be on the look-out, come very speedily. one day, Strong be absent upon an errand for his principal, Sir Francis make his appearance in the chamber, and find the envoy of the Nawaub alone. he abuse the world in general for be heartless and unkind to he: he abuse his wife for be ungenerous to he; he abuse Strong for be ungrateful--hundred of pound have he give Ned Strong--be his friend for life and keep he out of gaol, by Jove,--and now Ned be take her ladyship's side against he and abet she in her infernal unkind treatment of he. "theyhave enter into a conspiracy to keep I penniless, Altamont," the Baronet say: "they donot give I as much pocket money as Frank have at school,"
"why donot you go down to Richmond and borrow of he, Clavering?" Altamont break out with a savage laugh. "he wouldnot see his poor old beggar of a father without pocket-money, would he?"
"I tell you, Ihave be oblige to humiliate myself cruelly" Clavering say. "look here, sir--look here, at these pawn-ticket! Fancy a Member of Parliament and an old English Baronet, by Gad! oblige to put a drawing-room clock and a buhl inkstand up the spout; and a gold duck's-head paper-holder, that I dare say cost my wife five pound, for which theywould only give I fifteen-and-six! oh, itbe a humiliating thing, sir, poverty to a man of my habit; and itbe make I shed tear, sir,-- tear; and that d----d valet of mine--curse he, I wish he be hanged!-- he have the confound impudence to threaten to tell my lady: as the thing in my own house benot my own, to sell or to keep, or fle out of window if I choose--by Gad! the confounded scoundrel.
"cry a little; donot mind cryin' before I--it'll relieve you clavering," the other say. "why, I say, old feller, what a happy feller I once think you, and what a miserable son of a gun you really be!"
"itbe a shame that they treat I so, benot it?" clavering go on,--for, though ordinarily silent and apathetic, about his own grief the Baronet could whine for an hour at a time. "and--and, by Gad, sir, I havenot get the money to pay the very cab thatbe wait for I at the door; and the porteress, that Mrs. Bolton, lend I three shillin's, and I donot like to ask she for any more: and I ask that d----d old Costigan, the confound old penniless irish miscreant, and he havenot get a shillin', the beggar; and Campionbe out of town, or else hewould do a little bill for I, I know he would."
"I think you swear on your honour to your wife that you wouldnot put your name to paper," say Mr. Altamont, puff at his cigar.
"why do she leave I without pocket-money, then? Damme, I must have money," cry out the Baronet. "oh, Am----, oh, Altamont, Ibe the most miserable beggar alive."
"youwould like a chap to lend you a twenty-pound note, wouldnot you now?" the other ask.
"if you would, Iwould be grateful to you for ever--for ever, my dear friend," cry Clavering.
"how much would you give? will you give a fifty-pound bill, at six month, for half down and half in plate?" ask Altamont.
"yes, I would, so help me----, and pay it on the day," scream Clavering. "Iwill make it payable at my banker's: Iwill do anything you like."
"well, I be only chaff you. Iwill give you twenty pound."
"you say a pony," interpose Clavering; "my dear fellow, you say a pony, and Iwill be eternally oblige to you; and Iwill not take it as a gift--only as a loan, and pay you back in six month. I take my oath, I will."
"well--well--therebe the money, Sir Francis Clavering. I benot a bad fellow. when Ihave money in my pocket, dammy, I spend it like a man. here's five-and-twenty for you. donot be lose it at the hell now. donot be make a fool of yourself. go down to Clavering Park, and itwill keep you ever so long. you neednot 'ave butcher' meat: therebe pig, I dare say, on the premise: and you can shoot rabbit for dinner, you know, every day till the game come in. besides, the neighbour will ask you about to dinner, you know, sometimes: for you be a Baronet, though you have outrun the constable. and youhave get this comfort, that Ibe off your shoulder for a good bit to come--p'raps this two year--if I donot play; and I donot intend to touch the confound black and red: and by that time my lady, as you call she--Jimmy, I use to say--will have come round again; and youwill be ready for I, you know, and come down handsomely to yours truly."
at this juncture of their conversation Strong return, nor do the Baronet care much about prolong the talk, have get the money: and he make his way from Shepherd's Inn, and go home and bully his servant in a manner so unusually brisk and insolent that the man conclude his master must have pawn some more of the house furniture, or, at any rate, have come into possession of some ready money.
* * * * * *
"and yet Ihave look over the house, Morgan, and I donot thin he have take any more of the thing," Sir Francis's valet say to Major Pendennis's man, as they meet at their Club soon after. "my lady lock up a'most all the bejews afore she go away, and he couldnot take away the picter and look-glass in a cab and he wouldnot spout the fender and fire-iron--he benot so bad as that. but hebe get money somehow. hebe so dam'd imperent when he have. a few night ago I sor he at Vauxhall, where I be a-polkin with Lady Hemly Babewood's gal--a wery pleasant room that be, and an uncommon good lot in it, hall except the 'ousekeeper, and shebe methodisticle--I be a-polkin--you're too old a cove to polk, Mr. Morgan--and 'erebe your 'ealth--and I 'appened to 'ave on some of Clavering's abberdashery, and he sor it too: and he donot dare so much as speak a word."
"how about the house in St. John's Wood?" Mr. Morgan ask.
"execution in it.--Sold up heverythin: pony, and pianna, and brougham, and all. Mrs. Montague be hoff to Boulogne,--non e inwentus, Mr. Morgan. itbe my belief she put the execution in herself: and be tired of he."
"play much?" ask Morgan.
"not since the smash. when your governor, and the lawyer, and my lady and he have that tremendous scene: he go down on his knee, my lady tell Mrs. Bonner, as tell me,--and swear as he never more would touch a card or a dice, or put his name to a bit of paper; and my lady be a-go to give he the note down to pay his liability after the race: only your Governor say (which he write it on a piece of paper, and pass it across the table to the lawyer and my lady) that some one else have well book up for he, for hewould have kep' some of the money. hebe a sly old cove, your Gov'nor."
the expression of "old cove," thus flippantly apply by the young gentleman to himself and his master, displease Mr. Morgan exceedingly. on the first occasion, when Mr. Lightfoot use the obnoxious expression, his comrade's anger be only indicate by a silent frown; but on the second offence, Morgan, who be smoke his cigar elegantly, and hold it on the tip of his penknife, withdraw the cigar from his lip, and take his young friend to task.
"donot call Major Pendennis an old cove, if youwill 'ave the goodness, Lightfoot, and donot call I an old cove, nether. such word benot use in society; and we have live in the fust society, both at 'ome and foring. wehave be intimate with the fust statesmen of Europe. when we go abroad we dine with Prince Metternitch and Louy Philup reg'lar. we go here to the good house, the tip-top, I tell you. we ride with Lord John and the noble Whycount at the edd of Foring Affairs. we dine with the Hearl of Burgrave, and be consult by the Marquis of Steyne in everythink. we ought to know a thing or two, Mr. Lightfoot. yoube a young man, Ibe an old cove, as you say. wehave both see the world, and we both know that it benot money, nor bein' a Baronet, nor 'avin' a town and country 'ouse, nor a paltry five or six thousand a year."
"itbe ten, Mr. Morgan," cry Mr. Lightfoot, with great animation.
"it may have be, sir," Morgan say, with calm severity; "it may have be, Mr. Lightfoot, but it benot six now, nor five, sir. itbe be doosedly dip and cut into, sir, by the confounded extravygance of your master, with his helbow shakin', and his bill discountin', and his cottage in the Regency Park, and his many wickedness. hebe a bad un, Mr. Lightfoot,--a bad lot, sir, and that you know. and it benot money, sir--not such money as that, at any rate, come from a Calcuttar attorney, and I dussay wrung out of the pore starve black--that will give a pusson position in society, as you know very well. wehave no money, but we go everywhere; therebe not a housekeeper's room, sir, in this town of any consiquince, where James Morgan benot welcome. and it be I who get you into this Club, Lightfoot, as you very well know, though I be an old cove, and they would have blackball you without I as sure as your name be Frederic."
"I know they would, Mr. Morgan," say the other, with much humility.
"well, then, donot call I an old cove, sir. it benot gentlemanlike, Frederic Lightfoot, which I know you when you be a cab-boy, and when your father be in trouble, and get you the place you have now when the Frenchman go away. and if you think, sir, that because yoube make up to Mrs. Bonner, who may have save her two thousand pound--and I dare say she have in five-and-twenty year as she have live confidential maid to Lady Clavering--yet, sir, you must remember who put you into that service; and who know what you be before, sir, and it donot become you, Frederic Lightfoot, to call I an old cove."
"I beg your pardon, Mr. Morgan--I cannot do more than make an apology-- will you have a glass, sir, and let I drink your 'ealth?"
"you know I donot take sperrit. Lightfoot," reply Morgan, appease. "and so you and Mrs. Bonner be go to put up together, be you?"
"shebe old, but two thousand pound's a good bit, you see, Mr Morgan. and wewill get the 'Clavering arm' for a very little; and thatwill be no bad thing when the railroad run through Clavering. and when we be there, I hope youwill come and see we, Mr. Morgan."
"itbe a stoopid place, and no society," say Mr. Morgan. "I know it well. in Mrs Pendennis's time we use to go down, reg'lar, and the hair refresh I after the London racket."
"the railroad will improve Mr. Arthur's property," remark Lightfoot. "whatbe about the figure of it, should you say, sir?"
"under fifteen hundred, sir," answer Morgan; at which the other, who know the extent of poor Arthur's acre, thrust his tongue in his cheek, but remain wisely silent.
"be his man any good, Mr. Morgan?" Lightfoot resume.
"Pidgeon benot use to society as yet; but hebe young and have good talent, and have read a good deal, and I dessay he will do very well," reply Morgan. "he wouldnot quite do for this kind of thing, Lightfoot, for he benot see the world yet."
when the pint of sherry for which Mr. Lightfoot call, upon Mr. Morgan's announcement that he ecline to drink spirit, have be discuss by the two gentleman, who hold the wine up to the light, and smack their lip, and wink their eye at it, and rally the landlord as to the vintage, in the most approve manner of connoisseur, Morgan's ruffled equanimity be quite restore, and he be prepared to treat his young friend with perfect good-humour.
"what d'you think about Miss Amory, Lightfoot--tell we in confidence, now--do you think we should do well--you understand--if we make Miss a. into Mrs. A. P., comprendy vous?"
"she and her Mabe always quarrellin'," say Mr. Lightfoot. "Bonner be more than a match for the old lady, and treat Sir Francis like that-- like this year spill, which I fling into the grate. but she darenot say a word to Miss Amory. no more dare none of we. when a visitor come in, she smile and languishe, youwould think that butter wouldnot melt in her mouth: and the minute he be go, very likely, she flare up like a little demon, and say thing fit to send you wild. if Mr. Arthur come, itbe 'do letus sing that there delightful Song!' or, 'come and write I they pooty verse in this halbum!' and very likely shebe be a-rilin' her mother, or stick pin into her maid, a minute before. she do stick pin into she and pinch she. Mary Hann show I one of her arm quite black and blue; and I recklect Mrs. Bonner, whobe as jealous of I as a old cat, box her ear for show I. and then you should see Miss at luncheon, when therebe nobody but the family! she make b'leave she never heat, and my! you should only jest see she. she have Mary Hann to bring she up plum-cake and cream into her bedroom; and the cook's the only man in the house shebe civil to. Bonner say, how, the second season in London, Mr. Soppington be a-go to propose for she, and actially come one day, and sor she fle a book into the fire, and scold her mother so, that he go down softly by the back droring-room door, which he come in by; and next thing we hear of he be, he be married to Miss Rider. oh, shebe a devil, that little Blanche, and thatbe my candig apinium, Mr. Morgan."
"Apinion, not apinium, Lightfoot, my good fellow," Mr. Morgan say, with parental kindness, and then ask of his own bosom with a sigh, why the deuce do my Governor want Master Arthur to marry such a girl as this? and the tete-a-tete of the two gentleman be break up by the entry of other gentleman, member of the Club--when fashionable town-talk, politic, cribbage, and other amusement ensue, and the conversation become general.
the Gentleman's Club be hold in the parlour of the Wheel of Fortune public-house, in a snug little by-lane, lead out of one of the great street of Mayfair, and frequent by some of the most select gentleman about town. their master' affair, debt, intrigue, adventure; their lady' good and bad quality and quarrel with their husband; all the family secret be here discuss with perfect freedom and confidence, and here, when about to enter into a new situation, a gentleman be enable to get every requisite information regard the family of which he propose to become a member. livery it may be imagine be exclude from this select precinct; and the powdered head of the large metropolitan footman might bow down in vain entreating admission into the Gentleman's Club. these outcast giant in plush take their beer in an outer apartment of the Wheel of Fortune, and could no more get an entry into the Clubroom than a Pall Mall tradesman or a Lincoln's Inn attorney could get admission into Bays's or Spratt's. and it be because the conversation which we have permit to overhear here, in some measure explain the character and bearing of our story, that we have venture to introduce the reader into a society so exclusive.
CHAPTER LXII
the Way of the World
a short time after the piece of good fortune which befell Colonel Altamont at Epsom, that gentleman put into execution his project foreign tour, and the chronicler of the polite world who go down to London Bridge for the purpose of take leave of the people of fashion who quit this country, announce that among the company on board the Soho to Antwerp last Saturday, be "Sir Robert, Lady, and the Misses Hodge; Mr. Serjeant Kewsy, and Mrs. and Miss Kewsy; Colonel Altamont, Major Coddy, etc." the Colonel travel in state, and as become a gentleman: he appear in a rich travel costume; he drink brandy-and-water freely during the passage, and be not sick, as some of the other passenger be; and he be attend by his body-servant; the faithful Irish legionary who have be for some time in wait upon himself and Captain Strong in their chamber of Shepherd's Inn.
the Chevalier partook of a copious dinner at Blackwall with his depart friend the Colonel, and one or two other, who drink many health to Altamont at that liberal gentleman's expense. "strong, old boy," the Chevalier's worthy chum say, "if you want a little money, nowbe your time. Ibe your man. yoube a good feller, and have be a good feller to I, and a twenty-pound note, more or less, will make no odd to I," but Strong say, no, he donot want any money; he be flush, quite flush-- "that be, not flush enough to pay you back your last loan, Altamont, but quite able to carry on for some time to come," and so, with a not uncordial greeting between they, the two part. have the possession of money really make Altamont more honest and amiable than he have hitherto be, or only cause he to seem more amiable in Strong's eye? perhaps he really be well, and money improve he. perhaps it be the beauty of wealth Strong see and respect. but he argue within himself, "this poor devil, this unlucky outcast of a return convict, be ten time as good a fellow as my friend Sir Francis Clavering, Bart. he have pluck and honesty in his way. he will stick to a friend, and face an enemy. the other never have courage to do either. and what be it that have put the poor devil under a cloud? he be only a little wild, and sign his father-in-law's name. many a man have do bad, and come to no wrong, and hold his head up. clavering do. no, he donot hold his head up: he never do in his good day." and Strong, perhaps, repent he of the falsehood which he have tell to the free-handed Colonel, that he be not in want of money; but it be a falsehood on the side of honesty, and the Chevalier could not bring down his stomach to borrow a second time from his outlawed friend. besides, he could get on. clavering have promise he some: not that Clavering's promise be much to be believe, but the Chevalier be of a hopeful turn, and trust in many chance of catch his patron, and waylay some of those stray remittance and supply, in the procuring of which for his principal lie Mr. Strong's chief business.
he have grumble about Altamont's companionship in the Shepherd's Inn chamber; but he find those lodging more glum now without his partner than with he. the solitary life be not agreeable to his social soul; and he have get into extravagant and luxurious habit, too, have a servant at his command to run his errand, to arrange his toilet, and to cook his meal. it be rather a grand and touching sight now to see the portly and handsome gentleman paint his own boot, and broil his own mutton chop. it have be before state that the Chevalier have a wife, a spanish lady of Vittoria, who have go back to her friend, after a few month' union with the Captain, whose head she break with a dish. he begin to think whether he should not go back and see his Juanita. the Chevalier be grow melancholy after the departure of his friend the Colonel; or, to use his own picturesque expression, be "down on his luck." these moment of depression and interval of ill fortune occur constantly in the life of hero; Marius at Minturme, Charles Edward in the Highlands, Napoleon before Elba. what great man have not be call upon to face evil fortune?
from Clavering no supply be to be have for some time, the five-and-twenty pound or the "pony," which the exemplary Baronet have receive from Mr. Altamont, have flee out of Clavering's keeping as swiftly as many previous pony. he have be down the river with a choice party of sporting gent, who dodge the police and land in Essex, where they put up Billy Bluck to fight Dick the cabman whom the Baronet back, and who have it all his own way for thirteen round, when, by an unlucky blow in the windpipe, Billy kill he. "itbe always my luck, Strong," Sir Francis say; "the betting be three to one on the cabman, and I think myself as sure of thirty pound, as if I have it in my pocket. and dammy, I owe my man Lightfoot fourteen pound now which hebe lend and pay for I: and he dun I--the confound impudent blackguard: and I wish to Heaven I know any way of get a bill do, or of screw a little out of my lady! Iwill give you half, Ned, upon my soul and honour, Iwill give you half if you can get anybody to do we a little fifty."
but Ned say sternly that he have give his word of honour, as a gentleman, that he would be no party to any future bill transaction in which her husband might engage (who have give his word of honour too), and the Chevalier say that he, at least, would keep his word, and would black his own boot all his life rather than break his promise. and what be more, he vow he would advise Lady Clavering that Sir Francis be about to break his faith towards she upon the very first hint which he could get that such be Clavering's intention.
upon this information Sir Francis Clavering, accord to his custom, cry and curse very volubly. he speak of death as his only resource. he besought and implore his dear Strong, his good friend, his dear old Ned, not to throw he over: and when he quit his dear Ned, as he go down the stair of Shepherd's Inn, swore and blaspheme at Ned as the most infernal villain, and traitor, and blackguard, and coward under the sun, and wish Ned be in his grave, and in a bad place, only he would like the confound ruffian to live, until Frank Clavering have have his revenge out of he.
in Strong's chamber the Baronet meet a gentleman whose visit be now, as it have be show, very frequent in Shepherd's Inn, Mr. Samuel Huxter, of Clavering. that young fellow, who have poach the walnut in Clavering Park in his youth, and have see the Baronet drive through the street at home with four horse, and prance up to church with powdered footman, have an immense respect for his Member, and a prodigious delight in make his acquaintance. he introduce himself with much blushing and trepidation, as a Clavering man--son of Mr. Huxter, of the market-place--father attend Sir Francis's keeper, Coxwood, when his gun burst and take off three finger--proud to make Sir Francis's acquaintance. all of which introduction Sir Francis receive affably. and honest Huxter talk about Sir Francis to the chap at Bartholomew's: and tell Fanny, in the lodge, that, after all, there be nothing like a thoroughbred un, a regular good old english gentleman, one of the olden time! to which Fanny reply, that she think Sir Francis be an ojous creature--she donot know why-- but she couldnot abear he--she be sure he be wicked, and low, and mean--she know he be; and when Sam to this reply that Sir Francis be very affable, and have borrow half a sov' of he quite kindly, Fanny burst into a laugh, pull Sam's long hair (which be not yet of irreproachable cleanliness), pat his chin, and call he a stoopid, stoopid, old foolish stoopid, and say that Sir Francis be always borrere money of everybody, and that Mar have actially refuse he twice, and have have to wait three month to get seven shilling which he have borrow of 'er.
"donot say 'er but she, borrer but borrow, actially but actually, Fanny," Mr. Huxter reply--not to a fault in her argument, but to grammatical error in her statement.
"well then, she, and borrow, and hactually--there then, you stoopid," say the other; and the scholar make such a pretty face that the grammar master be quickly appease, and would have willingly give she a hundred more lesson on the spot at the price which he take for that one.
of course Mrs. Bolton be by, and I suppose that Fanny and Dr. Sam be on exceedingly familiar and confidential term by this time, and that time have bring to the former certain consolation, and soothe certain regret, which be deucedly bitter when they occur, but which be, no more than tooth-pulling, or any other pang, eternal.
as you sit, surround by respect and affection; happy, honour, and flatter in your old age; your foible gently indulge; your least word kindly cherish; your garrulous old story receive for the hundredth time with dutiful forbearance, and never-fail hypocritical smile; the woman of your house constant in their flattery; the young man hush and attentive when you begin to speak; the servant awestricken; the tenant cap in hand, and ready to act in the place of your worship's horse when your honour take a drive--it have often strike you, o thoughtful dive! that this respect, and these glory, be for the main part transfer, with your fee-simple, to your successor--that the servant will bow, and the tenant shout, for your son as for you; that the butler will fetch he the wine (improve by a little keeping) thatbe now in your cellar; and that, when your night be come, and the light of your life be go down, as sure as the morning rise after you and without you, the sun of prosperity and flattery shin on your heir. man come and bask in the halo of consol and acre that beam round about he: the reverence be transfer with the estate; of which, with all its advantage, pleasure, respect, and good-will, he in turn become the life-tenant. how long do you wish or expect that your people will regret you? how much time do a man devote to grief before he begin to enjoy? a great man must keep his heir at his feast like a live memento mori. if he hold very much by life, the presence of the other must be a constant sting and warning. "make ready to go," say the successor to your honour; "I be wait: and I could hold it as well as you."
what have this reference to the possible reader, to do with any of the character of this history? do we wish to apologise for Pen because he have get a white hat, and because his mourning for his mother be fainter? all the lapse of year, all the career of fortune, all the event of life, however strongly they may move or eagerly excite he, never can remove that sainted image from his heart, or banish that bless love from its sanctuary. if he yield to wrong, the dear eye will look sadly upon he when he dare to meet they; if he do well, endure pain, or conquer temptation, the ever present love will greet he, he know, with approval and pity; if he fall, plead for he; if he suffer, cheer he; --be with he and accompany he always until death be past; and sorrow and sin be no more. be this mere dreaming, or, on the part of an idle story-teller, useless moralising? may not the man of the world take his moment, too, to be grave and thoughtful? ask of your own heart and memory, brother and sister, if we do not live in the dead; and (to speak reverently) prove God by love?
of these matter Pen and Warrington often speak in many a solemn and friendly converse in after day; and Pendennis's mother be worship in his memory, and canonise there, as such a saint ought to be. lucky he in life who know a few such woman! a kind provision of Heaven it be, that send we such; and give we to admire that touching and wonderful spectacle of innocence, and love, and beauty.
but as it be certain that if, in the course of these sentimental conversation, any outer stranger, Major Pendennis for instance, have walk into Pen's chamber, Arthur and Warrington would have stop their talk, and choose another subject, and discourse about the Opera, or the last debate in Parliament, or Miss Jones's marriage with Captain Smith, or what not,--so, let we imagine that the public step in at this juncture, and stop the confidential talk between author and reader, and beg we to resume our remark about this world, with which both be certainly well acquaint than with that other one into which we have just be peep.
on come into his property, Arthur Pendennis at first comport himself with a modesty and equanimity which obtain his friend Warrington's praise, though Arthur's uncle be a little inclined to quarrel with his nephew's meanness of spirit, for not assume great state and pretension now that he have enter on the enjoyment of his kingdom. he would have have Arthur instal in handsome quarter, and rid on showy park hack, or in well-build cabriolet, every day. "I be too absent," Arthur say, with a laugh, "to drive a cab in London; the omnibus would cut I in two, or I should send my horse's head into the lady' carriage-window; and you wouldnot have I drive about by my servant like an apothecary, uncle?" no, Major Pendennis would on no account have his nephew appear like an apothecary; the august representative of the house of Pendennis must not so demean himself. and when Arthur, pursue his banter, say, "and yet, I dare say, sir, my father be proud enough when he first set up his gig," the old Major hem and ha'd, and his wrinkle face redden with a blush as he answer, "you know what Buonaparte say, sir, 'Il faut laver son linge sale en famille.' there be no need, sir, for you to brag that your father be a--a medical man. he come of a most ancient but fallen house, and be oblige to reconstruct the family fortune as many a man of good family have do before he. you be like the fellow in Sterne, sir--the Marquis who come to demand his sword again. your father get back yours for you. you be a man of land estate, by Gad, sir, and a gentleman--never forget you be a gentleman."
then Arthur slily turn on his uncle the argument which he have hear the old gentleman often use regard himself. "in the society which I have the honour of frequent through your introduction, who care to ask about my paltry mean or my humble gentility, uncle?" he ask. "it would be absurd of I to attempt to compete with the great folk; and all that they can ask from we be, that we should have a decent address and good manner."
"but for all that, sir, I should belong to a well Club or two," the uncle answer: "I should give an occasional dinner, and select my society well; and I should come out of that horrible garret in the Temple, sir." and so Arthur compromise by descend to the second floor in Lamb Court: Warrington still occupy his old quarter, and the two friend be determined not to part one from the other. cultivate kindly, reader, those friendship of your youth: it be only in that generous time that they be form. how different the intimacy of after day be, and how much weak the grasp of your own hand after it have be shake about in twenty year' commerce with the world, and have squeeze and drop a thousand equally careless palm! as you can seldom fashion your tongue to speak a new language after twenty, the heart refuse to receive friendship pretty soon: it get too hard to yield to the impression.
so Pen have many acquaintance, and be of a jovial and easy turn, get more daily: but no friend like Warrington; and the two man continue to live almost as much in common as the Knights of the Temple, rid upon one horse (for Pen's be at Warrington's service), and have their chamber and their servitor in common.
Mr. Warrington have make the acquaintance of Pen's friend of Grosvenor Place during their last unlucky season in London, and have express himself no well satisfied with Sir Francis and Lady Clavering and her ladyship's daughter than be the public in general. "the world be right," George say, "about those people. the young man laugh and talk freely before those lady, and about they. the girl see people whom she have no right to know, and talk to man with whom no girl should have an intimacy. do you see those two reprobate lean over Lady Clavering's carriage in the Park the other day, and leer under Miss Blanche's bonnet? no good mother would let her daughter know those man, or admit they within her door."
"the Begum be the most innocent and good-natured soul alive," interpose Pen. "she never hear any harm of Captain Blackball, or read that trial in which Charley Lovelace figure. do you suppose that honest lady read and remember the Chronique Scandaleuse as well as you, you old grumbler?"
"would you like Laura Bell to know those fellow?" Warrington ask, his face turn rather red. "would you let any woman you love be contaminate by their company? I have no doubt that the poor Begum be ignorant of their history. it seem to I she be ignorant of a great number of well thing. it seem to I that your honest Begum be not a lady, Pen. it be not her fault, doubtless, that she have not have the education, or learn the refinement of a lady."
"she be as moral as Lady Portsea, who have all the world at her ball, and as refine as Mrs. Bull, who break the King's English. and have half a dozen duke at her table," Pen answer, rather sulkily.
