"why should you and I be more squeamish than the rest of the world? why be we to visit the sin of her father on this harmless kind creature? she never do anything but kindness to you or any mortal soul. as far as she know she do she good. she do not set up to be more than she be. she give you the good dinner she can buy, and the good company she can get. she pay the debt of that scamp of a husband of her. she spoil her boy like the most virtuous mother in England. her opinion about literary matter, to be sure, be not much; and I daresay she never read a line of Wordsworth, or hear of Tennyson in her life."
"no more have Mrs. Flanagan the laundress," growl out Pen's mentor; "no more have Betty the housemaid; and I have no word of blame against they. but a high-souled man donot make friend of these. a gentleman donot choose these for his companion, or bitterly rue it afterwards if he do. be you, who be set up to be a man of the world and a philosopher, to tell I that the aim of life be to guttle three course and dine off silver? do you dare to own to yourself that your ambition in life be good claret, and that youwill dine with any, provide you get a stall ox to feed on? you call I a Cynic--why, what a monstrous cynicism it be, which you and the rest of you man of the world admit! Iwould rather live upon raw turnip and sleep in a hollow tree, or turn backwoodsman or savage, than degrade myself to this civilisation, and own that a french cook be the thing in life well worth live for."
"because you like a raw beefsteak and a pipe afterwards," break out Pen, "you give yourself air of superiority over people whose taste be more dainty, and be not ashamed of the world they live in. who go about profess particular admiration, or esteem, or friendship, or gratitude even, for the people one meet every day? if a. ask I to his house, and give I his good, I take his good thing for what they be worth and no more. I do not profess to pay he back in friendship, but in the conventional money of society. when we part, we part without any grief. when we meet, we be tolerably glad to see one another. if I be only to live with my friend, your black muzzle, old George, be the only face I should see."
"you be your uncle's pupil," say Warrington, rather sadly; and you speak like a worldling."
"and why not?" ask Pendennis; "why not acknowledge the world I stand upon, and submit to the condition of the society which we live in and live by? I be old than you, George, in spite of your grizzle whisker, and have see much more of the world than you have in your garret here, shut up with your book and your revery and your idea of one-and-twenty. I say, I take the world as it be, and be of it, will not be ashamed of it. if the time be out of joint, have I any calling or strength to set it right?"
"indeed, I donot think you have much of either," growl Pen's interlocutor.
"if I doubt whether I be well than my neighbour," Arthur continue, "if I concede that I be no better,--i also doubt whether he be well than I. I see man who begin with idea of universal reform, and who, before their beard be grow, propound their loud plan for the regeneration of mankind, give up their scheme after a few year of bootless talking and vainglorious attempt to lead their fellow; and after they have find that man will no long bear they, as indeed they never be in the least worthy to be hear, sink quietly into the rank-and-file,--acknowledging their aim impracticable, or thankful that they be never put into practice. the fierce reformer grow calm, and be faire to put up with thing as they be: the loud radical orator become dumb, quiescent placeman: the most fervent liberal when out of power, become humdrum Conservatives or downright tyrant or despot in office. look at the Thiers, look at Guizot, in opposition and in place! look at the whig appeal to the country, and the whig in power! would you say that the conduct of these man be an act of treason, as the Radicals bawl,--who would give way in their turn, be their turn ever to come? no, only that they submit to circumstance which be strong than they,--march as the world march towards reform, but at the world's pace (and the movement of the vast body of mankind must need be slow), forgo this scheme as impracticable, on account of opposition,--that as immature, because against the sense of the majority,--are force to calculate drawback and difficulty, as well as to think of reform and advances,--and compel finally to submit, and to wait, and to compromise."
"the right Honourable Arthur Pendennis could not speak well, or be more satisfied with himself, if he be First Lord of the Treasury and Chancellor of the Exchequer," Warrington say.
"self-satisfied? why self-satisfied?" continue Pen. "it seem to I that my scepticism be more respectful and more modest than the revolutionary ardour of other folk. many a patriot of eighteen, many a Spouting-Club orator, would turn the bishop out of the House of Lords to-morrow, and throw the Lords out after the bishop, and throw the throne into the Thames after the Peers and the Bench. be that man more modest than I, who take these institution as I find they, and wait for time and truth to develop, or fortify, or (if you like) destroy they? a college tutor, or a nobleman's toady, who appear one fine day as my right reverend lord, in a silk apron and a shovel-hat, and assume benedictory air over I, be still the same man we remember at Oxbridge, when he be truckle to the tuft, and bully the poor undergraduate in the lecture-room. an hereditary legislator, who pass his time with jockey and black-leg and ballet-girl, and who be call to rule over I and his other better because his grandfather make a lucky speculation in the fund, or find a coal or tin mine on his property, or because his stupid ancestor happen to be in command of ten thousand man as brave as himself, who overcome twelve thousand Frenchmen, or fifty thousand Indians--such a man, I say, inspire I with no more respect than the bitter democrat can feel towards he. but, such as he be, he be a part of the old society to which we belong and I submit to his lordship with acquiescence; and he take his place above the good of we at all dinner-party, and there bid his time. I donot want to chop his head off with a guillotine, or to fle mud at he in the street. when they call such a man a disgrace to his order; and such another, who be good and gentle, refined and generous, who employ his great mean in promote every kindness and charity, and art and grace of life, in the kind and most gracious manner, an ornament to his rank--the question as to the use and propriety of the order be not in the least affected one way or other. there it be, extant among we, a part of our habit, the creed of many of we, the growth of century, the symbol of a most complicated tradition--there stand my lord the bishop and my lord the hereditary legislator--what the french call transaction both of them,--representing in their present shape mail-clothe baron and double-sworded chief (from whom their lordship the hereditarie, for the most part, donot descend), and priest, profess to hold an absolute truth and a divinely inherit power, the which truth absolute our ancestor burn at the stake, and deny there; the which divine transmissible power still exist in print--to be believe, or not, pretty much at choice; and of these, I say, I acquiesce that they exist, and no more. if you say that these scheme, devise before printing be know, or steam be bear; when think be an infant, scar and whip; and truth under its guardian be gag, and swathe, and blindfold, and not allow to lift its voice, or to look out or to walk under the sun; before man be permit to meet, or to trade, or to speak with each other--if any one say (as some faithful soul do) that these scheme be for ever, and have be change and modify constantly be to be subject to no further development or decay, I laugh, and let the man speak. but I would have toleration for these, as I would ask it for my own opinion; and if they be to die, I would rather they have a decent and natural than an abrupt and violent death."
"you would have sacrifice to Jove," Warrington say, "have you live in the time of the christian persecution."
"perhaps I would," say Pen, with some sadness. "perhaps I be a coward,-- perhaps my faith be unsteady; but this be my own reserve. what I argue here be that I will not persecute. make a faith or a dogma absolute, and persecution become a logical consequence; and Dominic burn a Jew, or Calvin an Arian, or Nero a Christian, or Elizabeth or Mary a Papist or Protestant; or their father both or either, accord to his humour; and act without any pang of remorse,--but, on the contrary, notion of duty fulfil. make dogma absolute, and to inflict or to suffer death become easy and necessary; and Mahomet's soldier shout, 'Paradise! paradise!' and die on the christian spear, be not more or less praiseworthy than the same man slaughter a townful of Jews, or cut off the head of all prisoner who would not acknowledge that there be but one Prophet of God."
"a little while since, young one," Warrington say, who have be listen to his friend's confession neither without sympathy nor scorn, for his mood lead he to indulge in both, "you ask I why I remain out of the strife of the world, and look on at the great labour of my neighbour without take any part in the struggle? why, what a mere dilettante you own yourself to be, in this confession of general scepticism, and what a listless spectator yourself! you be six-and-twenty year old; and as blase as a rake of sixty. you neither hope much nor care much, nor believe much. you doubt about other man as much as about yourself. be it make of such pococuranti as you, the world would be intolerable; and I have rather live in a wilderness of monkey, and listen to their chatter, than in a company of man who deny everything."
"be the world compose of Saint Bernards or Saint Dominies, it would be equally odious," say Pen, "and at the end of a few score of year would cease to exist altogether. would you have every man with his head shave, and every woman in a cloister,--carrye out to the full the ascetic principle? would you have conventicle hymn twang from every lane in every city in the world? would you have all the bird of the forest sing one note and fly with one feather? you call I a sceptic because I acknowledge what be; and in acknowledge that, be it linnet or lark, or priest or parson, be it, I mean, any single one of the infinite variety of the creature of God (whose very name I would be understand to pronounce with reverence, and never to approach but with distant awe), I say that the study and acknowledgment of that variety amongst man especially increase our respect and wonder for the Creator, Commander, and Ordainer of all these mind, so different and yet so united,--meeting in a common adoration, and offer up, each accord to his degree and mean of approach the Divine centre, his acknowledgment of praise and worship, each singing (to recur to the bird simile) his natural song."
"and so, Arthur, the hymn of a saint, or the ode of a poet, or the chant of a newgate thief, be all pretty much the same in your philosophy," say George.
"even that sneer could be answer be it to the point," Pendennis reply; "but it be not; and it could be reply to you, that even to the wretched outcry of the thief on the tree, the wise and the good of all teacher we know of, the untiring Comforter and Consoler, promise a pitiful hearing and a certain hope. hymn of saint! ode of poet! who be we to measure the chance and opportunity, the mean of do, or even judge, right and wrong, award to man; and to establish the rule for mete out their punishment and reward? we be as insolent and unthinke in judging of man's moral as of their intellect. we admire this man as be a great philosopher, and set down the other as a dullard, not know either, or the amount of truth in either, or be certain of the truth anywhere. we sing Te Deum for this hero who have win a battle, and De Profundis for that other one who have break out of prison, and have be catch afterwards by the policeman. our measure of reward and punishment be most partial and incomplete, absurdly inadequate, utterly worldly, and we wish to continue it into the next world. into that next and awful world we strive to pursue man, and send after they our impotent party verdict of condemnation or acquittal. we set up our paltry little rod to measure Heaven immeasurable, as if, in comparison to that, Newton's mind or Pascal's or Shakspeare's be any lofty than mine; as if the ray which travel from the sun would reach I soon than the man who black my boot. measure by that altitude, the tall and the small among we be so alike diminutive and pitifully base, that I say we should take no count of the calculation, and it be a meanness to reckon the difference."
"your figure fail there, Arthur," say the other, well please; "if even by common arithmetic we can multiply as we can reduce almost infinitely, the Great Reckoner must take count of all; and the small be not small, or the great great, to his infinity."
"I donot call those calculation in question," Arthur say; "I only say that yours be incomplete and premature; false in consequence, and, by every operation, multiply into wide error. I do not condemn the man who murder Socrates and damn Galileo. I say that they damn Galileo and murder Socrates."
"and yet but a moment since you admit the propriety of acquiescence in the present, and, I suppose, all other tyranny?"
"no: but that if an opponent menace I, of whom and without cost of blood and violence I can get rid, I would rather wait he out, and starve he out, than fight he out. Fabius fight Hannibal sceptically. who be his roman coadjutor, whom we read of in Plutarch when we be boy, who scoff at the other's procrastination and doubt his courage, and engage the enemy and be beat for his pain?"
in these speculation and confession of Arthur, the reader may perhaps see allusion to question which, no doubt, have occupy and discompose himself, and which he have answer by very different solution to those come to by our friend. we be not pledge ourselves for the correctness of his opinion, which reader will please to consider be deliver dramatically, the writer be no more answerable for they, than for the sentiment utter by any other character of the story: our endeavour be merely to follow out, in its progress, the development of the mind of a worldly and selfish, but not ungenerous or unkind or truth-avoid man. and it will be see that the lamentable stage to which his logic at present have bring he, be one of general scepticism and sneer acquiescence in the world as it be; or if you like so to call it, a belief qualify with scorn in all thing extant. the taste and habit of such a man prevent he from be a boisterous demagogue, and his love of truth and dislike of cannot keep he from advance crude proposition, such as many loud reformer be constantly ready with; much more of utter downright falsehood in argue question or abuse opponent, which he would die or starve rather than use. it be not in our friend's nature to be able to utter certain lie; nor be he strong enough to protest against other, except with a polite sneer; his maxim be, that he owe obedience to all act of Parliament, as long as they be not repeal.
and to what do this easy and sceptical life lead a man? Friend Arthur be a Sadducee, and the baptist might be in the Wilderness shout to the poor, who be listen with all their might and faith to the preacher's awful accent and denunciation of wrath or woe or salvation; and our friend the Sadducee would turn his sleek mule with a shrug and a smile from the crowd, and go home to the shade of his terrace, and muse over preacher and audience, and turn to his roll of Plato, or his pleasant greek songbook babbling of honey and Hybla, and nymph and fountain and love. to what, we say, do this scepticism lead? it lead a man to a shameful loneliness and selfishness, so to speak--the more shameful, because it be so good-humour and conscienceless and serene. Conscience! what be conscience? why accept remorse? what be public or private faith? mythuse alike envelop in enormous tradition. if see and acknowledge the lie of the world, Arthur, as see they you can with only too fatal a clearness, you submit to they without any protest far than a laugh: if, plunge yourself in easy sensuality, you allow the whole wretched world to pass groan by you unmove: if the fight for the truth be take place, and all man of honour be on the ground arm on the one side or the other, and you alone be to lie on your balcony and smoke your pipe out of the noise and the danger, you have well have die, or never have be at all, than such a sensual coward.
"the truth, friend!" Arthur say, imperturbably; "where be the truth? show it I. that be the question between we. I see it on both side. I see it on the conservative side of the house, and amongst the Radicals, and even on the ministerial bench. I see it in this man, who worship by Act of Parliament, and be reward with a silk apron and five thousand a year; in that man, who, drive fatally by the remorseless logic of his creed, give up everything, friend, fame, dearest tie, close vanity, the respect of an army of churchman, the recognise position of a leader, and pass over, truth-impel, to the enemy, in whose rank he will serve henceforth as a nameless private soldier:--i see the truth in that man, as I do in his brother, whose logic drive he to quite a different conclusion, and who, after have pass a life in vain endeavour to reconcile an irreconcilable book, fling it at last down in despair, and declare, with tearful eye, and hand up to heaven, his revolt and recantation. if the truth be with all these, why should I take side with any one of they? some be call upon to preach: let they preach. of these preacher there be somewhat too many, methink, who fancy they have the gift. but we cannot all be parson in church, that be clear. some must sit silent and listen, or go to sleep mayhap. have we not all our duty? the head charity-boy blow the bellow; the master can the other boy in the organ-loft; the clerk sing out amen from the desk; and the beadle with the staff open the door for his Reverence, who rustle in silk up to the cushion. I willnot cane the boy, nay, or say amen always, or act as the church's champion and warrior, in the shape of the beadle with the staff; but I will take off my hat in the place, and say my prayer there too, and shake hand with the clergyman as he step on the grass outside. donot I know that his be there be a compromise, and that he stand before I an Act of Parliament? that the church he occupy be build for other worship? that the Methodist chapel be next door; and that Bunyan the tinker be bawl out the tiding of damnation on the common hard by? yes, I be a Sadducee; and I take thing as I find they, and the world, and the act of Parliament of the world, as they be; and as I intend to take a wife, if I find one--not to be madly in love and prostrate at her foot like a fool--not to worship she as an angel, or to expect to find she as such--but to be good-natured to she, and courteous, expect good-nature and pleasant society from she in turn. and so, George, if ever you hear of my marrying, depend on it, it willnot be a romantic attachment on my side: and if you hear of any good place under Government, I have no particular scruple that I know of, which would prevent I from accept your offer."
"o Pen, you scoundrel! I know what you mean," here Warrington break out. "this be the meaning of your scepticism, of your quietism, of your atheism, my poor fellow. yoube go to sell yourself, and Heaven help you! you be go to make a bargain which will degrade you and make you miserable for life, and therebe no use talk of it. if you be once bent on it, the devil willnot prevent you."
"on the contrary, hebe on my side, benot he, George?" say Pen with a laugh. "what good cigar these be! come down and have a little dinner at the Club; the chef's in town, and hewill cook a good one for I. no, you willnot? donot be sulky, old boy, Ibe go down to--to the country to-morrow."
CHAPTER LXIII
which account perhaps for chapter LXI
the information regard the affair of the Clavering family, which Major Pendennis have acquire through Strong, and by his own personal interference as the friend of the house, be such as almost make the old gentleman pause in any plan which he might have once entertain for his nephew's benefit. to bestow upon Arthur a wife with two such father-in-law, as the two worthy whom the guileless and unfortunate Lady Clavering have draw in her marriage venture, be to benefit no man. and though the one, in a manner, neutralise the other, and the appearance of Amory or Altamont in public would be the signal for his instantaneous withdrawal and condign punishment,--for the fugitive convict have cut down the officer in charge of him,--and a rope would be inevitably his end; if he come again under british authority; yet, no guardian would like to secure for his ward a wife, whose parent be to be get rid of in such a way; and the old gentleman's notion always have be that Altamont, with the gallow before his eye, would assuredly avoid recognition; while, at the same time, by hold the threat of his discovery over Clavering, the latter, who would lose everything by Amory's appearance, would be a slave in the hand of the person who know so fatal a secret.
but if the Begum pay Clavering's debt many time more, her wealth would be expend altogether upon this irreclaimable reprobate; and her heir, whoever they might be, would succeed but to an emptied treasury; and Miss Amory, instead of bring her husband a good income and a seat in Parliament, would bring to that individual her person only, and her pedigree with that lamentable note of sus. per coll. at the name of the last male of her line.
there be, however, to the old schemer revolve these thing in his mind, another course yet open; the which will appear to the reader who may take the trouble to peruse a conversation, which presently ensue, between Major Pendennis and the honourable Baronet, the Member for Clavering.
when a man, under pecuniary difficulty, disappear from among his usual friend and equals,--dive out of sight, as it be, from the flock of bird in which he be accustomed to sail, it be wonderful at what strange and distant nook he come up again for breath. I have know a Pall Mall lounger and rotten row buck, of no inconsiderable fashion, vanish from amongst his comrade of the Clubs and the Park, and be discover, very happy and affable, at an eighteenpenny ordinary in Billingsgate: another gentleman, of great learning and wit, when outrun the constable (be I to say he be a literary man, some critic would vow that I intend to insult the literary profession), once send I his address at a little public-house call the "Fox under the Hill," down a most darksome and cavernous archway in the Strand. such a man, under such misfortune, may have a house, but he be never in his house; and have an address where letter may be leave; but only simpleton go with the hope of see him.--only a few of the faithful know where he be to be find, and have the clue to his hiding-place. so, after the dispute with his wife, and the misfortune consequent thereon, to find Sir Francis Clavering at home be impossible. "ever since I hast he for my book, which be fourteen pound, he donot come home till three o'clock, and purtend to be asleep when I bring his water of a mornin', and dodge hout when Ibe downstairs," Mr. Lightfoot remark to his friend Morgan; and announce that he should go down to my Lady, and be butler there, and marry his old woman. in like manner, after his altercation with Strong, the Baronet do not come near he, and flee to other haunt, out of the reach of the Chevalier's reproaches;--out of the reach of conscience, if possible, which many of we try to dodge and leave behind we by change of scene and other fugitive stratagem.
so, though the elder Pendennis, have his own ulterior object, be bent upon see Pen's country neighbour and representative in Parliament, it take the Major no inconsiderable trouble and time before he could get he into such a confidential state and conversation, as be necessary for the end which the Major have in view. for since the Major have be call in as family friend, and have cognisance of Clavering's affair, conjugal and pecuniary, the Baronet avoid he: as he always avoid all his lawyer and agent when there be an account to be render, or an affair of business to be discuss between they; and never keep any appointment but when its object be the raising of money. thus, previous to catch this most shy and timorous bird, the Major make more than one futile attempt to hold him;--on one day it be a most innocent-look invitation to dinner at Greenwich, to meet a few friend; the Baronet accept, suspect something, and do not come; leave the Major (who indeed propose to represent in himself the body of friend) to eat his whitebait alone:--on another occasion the Major write and ask for ten minute' talk, and the Baronet instantly acknowledge the note, and make the appointment at four o'clock the next day at Bays's precisely (he carefully underline the "precisely"); but though four o'clock come, as in the course of time and destiny it could not do otherwise, no Clavering make his appearance. indeed, if he have borrow twenty pound of Pendennis, he could not have be more timid, or desirous of avoid the Major; and the latter find that it be one thing to seek a man, and another to find he.
before the close of that day in which Strong's patron have give the Chevalier the benefit of so many blessing before his face and curse behind his back, Sir Francis Clavering, who have pledge his word and his oath to his wife's adviser to draw or accept no more bill of exchange, and to be content with the allowance which his victimised wife still award he, have manage to sign his respectable name to a piece of stamp paper, which the Baronet's friend, Mr. Moss Abrams, have carry off, promise to have the bill "do" by a party with whose intimacy Mr. Abrams be favour. and it chance that Strong hear of this transaction at the place where the writing have be drawn,--in the back-parlour, namely, of Mr. Santiago's cigar-shop, where the Chevalier be constantly in the habit of spend an hour in the evening.
"he be at his old work again," Mr. Santiago tell his customer. "he and Moss Abrams be in my parlour. Moss send out my boy for a stamp. it must have be a bill for fifty pound. I hear the Baronet tell Moss to date it two month back. he will pretend that it be an old bill, and that he forget it when he come to a settlement with his wife the other day. I dare say they will give he some more money now he be clear." a man who have the habit of put his unlucky name to "promise to pay" at six month, have the satisfaction of know, too, that his affair be know and canvass, and his signature hand round among the very bad knave and rogue of London.
Mr. Santiago's shop be close by St. James's Street and Bury Street, where we have have the honour of visit our friend Major Pendennis in his lodging. the Major be walk daintily towards his apartment, as Strong, burn with wrath and redolent of Havanna, stride along the same pavement opposite to he.
"confound these young man: how they poison everything with their smoke," think the Major. "here come a fellow with mustachio and a cigar. every fellow who smoke and wear mustachio be a low fellow. oh! itbe Mr. Strong.--I hope you be well, Mr. Strong?" and the old gentleman, make a dignified bow to the Chevalier, be about to pass into his house; direct towards the lock of the door, with tremble hand, the polished door-key.
we have say that, at the long and weary dispute and conference regard the payment of Sir Francis Clavering's last debt, Strong and Pendennis have both be present as friend and adviser of the Baronet's unlucky family. Strong stop and hold out his hand to his brother negotiator, and old Pendennis put out towards he a couple of ungracious finger.
"what be your good news?" say Major Pendennis, patronise the other still far, and condescend to address to he an observation; for old Pendennis have keep such good company all his life, that he vaguely imagine he honour common man by speak to they. "still in town, Mr. Strong? I hope I see you well."
"my news be bad news, sir," Strong answer; "it concern our friend at Tunbridge Wells, and I should like to talk to you about it. clavering be at his old trick again, Major Pendennis."
"indeed! pray do I the favour to come into my lodging," cry the Major with awakened interest; and the pair enter and take possession of his drawing-room. here seat, Strong unburthene himself of his indignation to the Major, and speak at large of Clavering's recklessness and treachery. "no promise will bind he, sir," he say. "you remember when we meet, sir, with my lady's lawyer, how he wouldnot be satisfied with give his honour, but want to take his oath on his knee to his wife, and ring the bell for a Bible, and swear perdition on his soul if he ever would give another bill. he have be sign one this very day, sir: and will sign as many more as you please for ready money: and will deceive anybody, his wife or his child, or his old friend, who have back he a hundred time. why, therebe a bill of his and mine will be due next week"
"I think we have pay all."
"not that one," Strong say, blush. "he ask I not to mention it, and--and--I have half the money for that, Major; and they will be down on I. but I donot care for it; Ibe use to it. itbe Lady Clavering that rile I. itbe a shame that that good-natured woman, who have pay he out of gaol a score of time, should be ruin by his heartlessness. a parcel of bill-stealer boxer, any rascal, get his money; and he donot scruple to throw an honest fellow over. would you believe it, sir, he take money of Altamont--you know whom I mean."
"indeed? of that singular man, who I think come tipsy once to Sir Francis's house?" Major Pendennis say, with impenetrable countenance. "who be Altamont, Mr. Strong?"
"I be sure I donot know, if you donot know," the Chevalier answer, with a look of surprise and suspicion.
"to tell you frankly," say the Major, "I have my suspicion--I suppose-- mind, I only suppose--that in our friend Clavering's a life--who, between you and I, Captain Strong, we must own about as loose a fish as any in my acquaintance--there be, no doubt, some queer secret and story which he would not like to have know: none of we would. and very likely this fellow, who call himself Altamont, know some story against Clavering, and have some hold on he, and get money out of he on the strength of his information. I know some of the good man of the good family in England who be pay through the nose in that way. but their private affair be no business of mine, Mr. Strong; and it be not to be suppose that because I go and dine with a man, I pry into his secret, or be answerable for all his past life. and so with our friend Clavering, I be most interested for his wife's sake, and her daughter's, who be a most charming creature: and when her ladyship ask I, I look into her affair, and try to set they straight; and shall do so again, you understand, to the good of my humble power and ability, if I can make myself useful. and if I be call upon--you understand, if I be call upon--and--by the way, this Mr. Altamont, Mr. Strong? how be this Mr. Altamont? I believe you be acquaint with he. be he in town?"
"I donot know that I be call upon to know where he be, Major Pendennis," say Strong, rise and take up his hat in dudgeon, for the Major's patronising manner and impertinence of caution offend the honest gentleman not a little.
Pendennis's manner alter at once from a tone of hauteur to one of know good-humour. "ah, Captain Strong, you be cautious too, I see; and quite right, my good sir, quite right. we donot know what ear wall may have, sir, or to whom we may be talk; and as a man of the world, and an old soldier,--an old and distinguished soldier, I have be tell, Captain Strong,--you know very well that there be no use in throw away your fire; you may have your idea, and I may put two and two together and have mine. but there be thing which donot concern he that many a man have well not know, eh, Captain? and which I, for one, willnot know until I have reason for know they: and that I believe be your maxim too. with regard to our friend the Baronet, I think with you, it would be most advisable that he should be check in his imprudent course; and most strongly reprehend any man's departure from his word, or any conduct of his which can give any pain to his family, or cause they annoyance in any way. that be my full and frank opinion, and I be sure it be your."
"certainly," say Mr. Strong, drily.
"I be delighted to hear it; delight that an old brother soldier should agree with I so fully. and I be exceedingly glad of the lucky meeting which have procure I the good fortune of your visit. good evening. thank you. Morgan, show the door to Captain Strong."
and Strong, precede by Morgan, take his leave of Major Pendennis; the Chevalier not a little puzzled at the old fellow's prudence; and the valet, to say the truth, to the full as much perplex at his master's reticence. for Mr. Morgan, in his capacity of accomplished valet, move here and there in a house as silent as a shadow; and, as it so happen, during the latter part of his master's conversation with his visitor, have be stand very close to the door, and have overhear not a little of the talk between the two gentleman, and a great deal more than he could understand.
"who be that Altamont? know anything about he and strong?" Mr. Morgan ask of Mr. Lightfoot, on the next convenient occasion when they meet at the Club.
"Strongbe his man of business, draw the Governor's bill, and indosse them, and do his odd job and that; and I suppose Altamontbe in it too," Mr. Lightfoot reply. "that kite-flying, you know, Mr. M., always take two or three on them to set the paper go. Altamont put the pot on at the Derby, and win a good bit of money. I wish the Governor could get some somewhere, and I could get my book pay up."
"do you think my Lady would pay his debt again?" Morgan ask. "find out that for I, Lightfoot, and Iwill make it worth your while, my boy."
* * * * * *
Major Pendennis have often say with a laugh, that his vale Morgan be a much rich man than himself: and, indeed, by long course of careful speculation, this wary and silent attendant have be amass a considerable sum of money, during the year which he have pass in the Major's service, where he have make the acquaintance of many other valet of distinction, from whom he have learn the affair of their principal. when Mr. Arthur come into his property, but not until then, Morgan have surprise the young gentleman, by say that he have a little sum of money, some fifty or a hundred pound, which he want to lay out to advantage; perhaps the gentleman in the Temple, know about affair and business and that, could help a poor fellow to a good investment? Morgan would be very much oblige to Mr. Arthur, most grateful and oblige indeed, if Arthur could tell he of one. when Arthur laughingly reply, that he know nothing about money matter, and know no earthly way of help Morgan, the latter, with the utmost simplicity, be very grateful, very grateful indeed, to Mr. Arthur, and if Mr. Arthur should want a little money before his rent be pay, perhaps he would kindly remember that his uncle's old and faithful servant have some as he would like to put out: and be most proud if he could be useful anyways to any of the family.
the Prince of Fairoaks, who be tolerably prudent and have no need of ready money, would as soon have think of borrow from his uncle's servant as of steal the valet's pocket-handkerchief, and be on the point of make some haughty reply to Morgan's offer, but be check by the humour of the transaction. Morgan a capitalist! Morgan offer to lend to he--the joke be excellent. on the other hand, the man might be quite innocent, and the proposal of money a simple offer of good-will. so Arthur withhold the sarcasm that be rise to his lip, and content himself by decline Mr. Morgan's kind proposal. he mention the matter to his uncle, however, and congratulate the latter on have such a treasure in his service.
it be then that the Major say that he believe Morgan have be get devilish rich for a devilish long time; in fact, he have buy the house in Bury Street, in which his master be a lodger and have actually make a considerable sum of money, from his acquaintance with the Clavering family and his knowledge obtain through his master that the Begum would pay all her husband's debt, by buy up as many of the Baronet's acceptance as he could raise money to purchase. of these transaction the Major, however, know no more than most gentleman do of their servant, who live with we all our day and be stranger to we, so strong custom be, and so pitiless the distinction between class and class.
"so he offer to lend you money, do he?" the elder Pendennis remark to his nephew. "hebe a dev'lish sly fellow, and a dev'lish rich fellow; and therebe many a nobleman would like to have such a valet in his service, and borrow from he too. and he benot a bit change, Monsieur Morgan. he do his work just as well as ever--hebe always ready to my bell--steal about the room like a cat--hebe so dev'lishly attach to I, Morgan!"
on the day of Strong's visit, the Major bethink he of Pen's story, and that Morgan might help he, and rally the valet regard his wealth with that free and insolent way which so high-place a gentleman might be dispose to adopt towards so unfortunate a creature.
"I hear that you have get some money to invest, Morgan," say the Major.
"itbe Mr. Arthur have be tell, hang he," think the valet.
"Ibe glad my place be such a good one."
"thank you, sir--i've no reason to complain of my place, nor of my master," reply Morgan, demurely.
"yoube a good fellow: and I believe you be attach to I; and Ibe glad you get on well. and I hope youwill be prudent, and not be take a public-house or that kind of thing."
a public-house, think Morgan--I in a public-house!--the old fool!-- Dammy, if I be ten year young Iwould set in Parlyment before I die, that I would.--"No, thank you kindly, sir. I donot think of the public line, sir. and Ihave get my little saving pretty well put out, sir."
"you do a little in the discount way, eh, Morgan?"
"yes, sir, a very little--I--I beg your pardon, sir--might I be so free as to ask a question----"
"speak on, my good fellow," the elder say, graciously.
"about Sir Francis Clavering's paper, sir? do you think hebe any long any good, sir? will my Lady pay on them, any more, sir?"
"what, youhave do something in that business already?"
"yes, sir, a little," reply Morgan, drop down his eye. and I donot mind own, sir, and I hope I may take the liberty of say, sir, that a little more would make I very comfortable if it turn out as well as the last."
"why, how much have you net by he, in Gad's name?" ask the Major.
"Ihave do a good bit, sir, at it: that I own, sir. have some information, and make acquaintance with the fam'ly through your kindness, I put on the pot, sir."
"you do what?"
"I lay my money on, sir--I get all I could, and borrow, and buy Sir Francis's bill; many of them have his name, and the gentleman's as be just go out, Edward Strong, Esquire, sir: and of course I know of the blow-hup and shindy as be take place in Grosvenor Place, sir: and as I may as well make my money as another, Iwould be very much obleege to you if youwould tell I whether my Lady will come down any more."
although major Pendennis be as much surprised at this intelligence regard his servant, as if he have hear that Morgan be a disguised Marquis, about to throw off his mask and assume his seat in the House of Peers; and although he be of course indignant at the audacity of the fellow who have dare to grow rich under his nose, and without his cognisance; yet he have a natural admiration for every man who represent money and success, and find himself respect Morgan, and be rather afraid of that worthy, as the truth begin to dawn upon he.
"well, Morgan," say he, "I mustnot ask how rich you be; and the rich the well for your sake, Ibe sure. and if I could give you any information that could serve you, I would speedily help you. but frankly, if Lady Clavering ask I whether she shall pay any more of Sir Francis's debt, I shall advise and I hope she willnot, though I fear she will--and that be all I know. and so you be aware that Sir Francis be begin again in his--eh--reckless and imprudent course?"
"at his old game, sir--can't prevent that gentleman. he will do it."
"Mr. Strong be say that a Mr. Moss Abrams be the holder of one of Sir Francis Clavering's note. do you know anything of this Mr. Abrams; or the amount of the bill?"
"donot know the bill, know Abrams quite well, sir."
"I wish you would find out about it for I. and I wish you would find out where I can see Sir Francis Clavering, Morgan."
and Morgan say, "thank you, sir, yes, sir, I will, sir;" and retire from the room, as he have enter it, with his usual stealthy respect and quiet humility; leave the Major to muse and wonder over what he have just hear.
the next morning the valet inform Major Pendennis that he have see Mr. Abrams; what be the amount of the bill that gentleman be desirous to negotiate; and that the Baronet would be sure to be in the back-parlour of the Wheel of Fortune Tavern that day at one o'clock.
to this appointment Sir Francis Clavering be punctual, and as at one o'clock he sate in the parlour of the tavern in question, surround by spittoon, Windsor chair, cheerful print of boxer, trot horse, and pedestrian, and the lingering of last night's tobacco fume--as the descendant of an ancient line sate in this delectable place accommodate with an old copy of Bell's life in London, much blot with beer, the polite Major Pendennis walk into the apartment.
"so itbe you, old boy?" ask the Baronet, think that Mr. Moss Abrams have arrive with the money.
"how do you do, Sir Francis Clavering? I want to see you, and follow you here," say the Major, at sight of whom the other's countenance fall.
now that he have his opponent before he, the Major be determined to make a brisk and sudden attack upon he, and go into action at once. "I know," he continue, "who be the exceedingly disreputable person for whom you take I, Clavering; and the errand which bring you here."
"it benot your business, be it?" ask the Baronet, with a sulky and deprecatory look. "why be you follow I about and take the command, and meddle in my affair, Major Pendennis? Ihave never do you any harm, have I? Ihave never have your money. and I donot choose to be dodged about in this way, and domineer over. I donot choose it, and I willnot have it. if Lady Clavering have any proposal to make to I, let it be do in the regular way, and through the lawyer. Iwould rather not have you."
"I be not come from Lady Clavering," the Major say, "but of my own accord, to try and remonstrate with you, Clavering, and see if you can be keep from ruin. it be but a month ago that you swear on your honour, and want to get a Bible to strengthen the oath, that you would accept no more bill, but content yourself with the allowance which Lady Clavering give you. all your debt be pay with that proviso, and you have break it; this Mr. Abrams have a bill of yours for sixty pound."
"itbe an old bill. I take my solemn oath itbe an old bill," shriek out the Baronet.
"you draw it yesterday, and you date it three month back purposely. by Gad, Clavering, you sicken I with lie, I cannot help tell you so. Ihave no patience with you, by Gad. you cheat everybody, yourself include. Ihave see a deal of the world, but I never meet your equal at humbug. itbe my belief you have rather lie than not."
"have you come here, you old--old beast, to tempt I to--to pitch into you, and--and knock your old head off?" say the Baronet, with a poisonous look of hatred at the Major.
"what, sir?" shout out the old Major, rise to his foot and clasp his cane, and look so fiercely, that the Baronet's tone instantly change towards he.
"no, no," say Clavering, piteously, "I beg your pardon. I donot mean to be angry, or say anything unkind, only yoube so damned harsh to I, Major Pendennis. what be it you want of I? why have you be hunt I so? do you want money out of I too? by Jove, you know Ihave not get a shilling,"--and so Clavering, accord to his custom, pass from a curse into a whimper.
Major Pendennis see, from the other's tone, that Clavering know his secret be in the Major's hand.
"Ihave no errand from anybody, or no design upon you," Pendennis say, "but an endeavour, if itbe not too late, to save you and your family from utter ruin, through the infernal recklessness of your course. I know your secret----"
"I donot know it when I marry she; upon my oath I donot know it till the d----d scoundrel come back and tell I himself; and itbe the misery about that which make I so reckless, Pendennis; indeed it be," the Baronet cry, clasp his hand.
"I know your secret from the very first day when I see Amory come drunk into your dining-room in Grosvenor Place. I never forget face. I remember that fellow in Sydney a convict, and he remember I. I know his trial, the date of his marriage, and of his report death in the bush. I could swear to he. and I know that you be no more married to Lady Clavering than I be. Ihave keep your secret well enough, for Ihave not tell a single soul that I know it,--not your wife, not yourself till now."
"Poor Lady C., it would cut she up dreadfully," whimper Sir Francis; "and it benot my fault, Major; you know it benot."
"rather than allow you to go on ruin she as you do; I will tell she, Clavering, and tell all the world too; that be what I swear I will do, unless I can come to some term with you, and put some curb on your infernal folly. by play, debt, and extravagance of all kind, youhave get through half your wife's fortune, and that of her legitimate heir, mind --her legitimate heir. here it must stop. you cannot live together. yoube not fit to live in a great house like Clavering; and before three year' more be over would not leave a shilling to carry on. Ihave settle what must be do. you shall have six hundred a year; you shall go abroad and live on that. you must give up Parliament, and get on as well as you can. if you refuse, I give you my word Iwill make the real state of thing know to-morrow; Iwill swear to Amory, who, when identify, will go back to the country from whence he come, and will rid the widow of you and himself together. and so that boy of yours lose at once all title to old Spell's property, and it go to your wife's daughter. benot I make myself pretty clearly understand?"
"you wouldnot be so cruel to that poor boy, would you, Pendennis?" ask the father, plead piteously; "hang it, think about he. hebe a nice boy: though hebe dev'lish wild, I own hebe dev'lish wild."
"itbe you who be cruel to he," say the old moralist. "why, sir, youwill ruin he yourself inevitably in three year."
"yes, but perhaps I willnot have such dev'lish bad luck, you know;--the luck must turn: and Iwill reform, by Gad, Iwill reform. and if you be to split on I, it would cut up my wife so; you know it would, most infernally."
"to be part from you," say the old Major, with a sneer; "you know she willnot live with you again."
"but why cannot Lady C. live abroad, or at Bath, or at Tunbridge, or at the doose, and I go on here?" Clavering continue. "I like be here well than abroad, and I like be in Parliament. itbe dev'lish convenient being in Parliament. therebe very few seat like mine leave; and if I give it to them, I should not wonder the ministry would give I an island to govern, or some dev'lish good thing; for you know Ibe a gentleman of dev'lish good family, and have a handle to my name, and--and that sort of thing, Major Pendennis. eh, donot you see? donot you think theywould give I something dev'lish good if I be to play my card well? and then, you know, Iwould save money, and be keep out of the way of the confound hell and rouge et noir--and--and so Iwould rather not give up Parliament, please." for at one instant to hate and defy a man, at the next to weep before he, and at the next to be perfectly confidential and friendly with he, be not an unusual process with our versatile-minded Baronet.
"as for your seat in Parliament," the Major say, with something of a blush on his cheek, and a certain tremor, which the other do not see, "you must part with that, Sir Francis Clavering, to--to I."
"what! be you go into the House, Major Pendennis?"
"no--not I; but my nephew, Arthur, be a very clever fellow and would make a figure there: and when Clavering have two member, his father might very likely have be one; and--and should like Arthur to be there," the Major say.
"Dammy, do he know it, too?" cry out Clavering.
"nobody know anything out of this room," Pendennis answer; and if you do this favour for I, I hold my tongue. "if not, Ibe a man of my word, and will do what I have say."
"I say, Major," say Sir Francis, with a peculiarly humble smile "You-- you couldnot get I my first quarter in advance, could you, like the good of fellow? you can do anything with Lady Clavering; and, upon my oath, Iwill take up that bill of Abrams'. the little dam scoundrel, I know hewill do I in the business--he always do; and if you could do this for I, wewould see, Major."
"and I think your good plan would be to go down in September to Clavering to shoot, and take my nephew with you, and introduce he. yes, that will be the good time. and we will try and manage about the advance." (Arthur may lend he that, think old Pendennis. confound he, a seat in Parliament be worth a hundred and fifty pound.) "and, Clavering, you understand, of course, my nephew know nothing about this business. you have a mind to retire: he be a Clavering man and a good representative. for the borough; you introduce he, and your people vote for he--you see."
"when can you get I the hundred and fifty, Major? when shall I come and see you? will you be at home this evening or to-morrow morning? will you have anything here? theyhave get some dev'lish good bitter in the bar. I often have a glass of bitter, it set one up so."
the old Major would take no refreshment; but rise and take his leave of the Baronet, who walk with he to the door of the Wheel of Fortune, and then stroll into the bar, where he take a glass of gin and bitter with the landlady there: and a gentleman connect with the ring (who board at the Wheel of F.) come in, he and Sir Francis Clavering and the landlord talk about the fight and the news of the sporting world in general; and at length Mr. Moss Abrams arrive with the proceed of the Baronet's bill, from which his own handsome commission be deduct, and out of the remainder Sir Francis "stand" a dinner at Greenwich to his distinguished friend, and pass the evening gaily at Vauxhall.
meanwhile Major Pendennis, call a cab in Piccadilly, drive to Lamb Court, Temple, where he speedily be closet with his nephew in deep conversation.
after their talk they part on very good term, and it be in consequence of that unreported conversation, whereof the reader nevertheless can pretty well guess the bearing, that Arthur express himself as we have hear in the colloquy with Warrington, which be report in the last chapter. when a man be tempt to do a tempting thing, he can find a hundred ingenious reason for gratify his liking; and Arthur think very much that he would like to be in Parliament, and that he would like to distinguish himself there, and that he need not care much what side he take, as there be falsehood and truth on every side. and on this and on other matter he think he would compromise with his conscience, and that Sadduceeism be a very convenient and good-humour profession of faith.
CHAPTER LXIV
Phyllis and Corydon
on a picturesque common in the neighbourhood of Tunbridge Wells, Lady Clavering have find a pretty villa, whither she retire after her conjugal dispute at the end of that unlucky London season. Miss Amory, of course, accompany her mother, and Master Clavering come home for the holiday, with whom Blanche's chief occupation be to fight and quarrel. but this be only a home pastime, and the young schoolboy be not fond of home sport. he find cricket, and horse, and plenty of friend at Tunbridge. the good-natured Begum's house be fill with a constant society of young gentleman of thirteen, who eat and drink much too copiously of tart and champagne, who ride race on the lawn, and frighten the fond mother, who smoke and make themselves sick, and the dining-room unbearable to Miss Blanche. she do not like the society of young gentleman of thirteen.
as for that fair young creature, any change as long as it be change be pleasant to she; and for a week or two she would have like poverty and a cottage, and bread-and-cheese; and, for a night, perhaps, a dungeon and bread-and-water, and so the move to Tunbridge be by no mean unwelcome to she. she wander in the wood, and sketch tree and farmhouse; she read french novel habitually; she drive into Tunbridge Wells pretty often, and to any play, or ball, or conjurer, or musician who might happen to appear in the place; she sleep a great deal; she quarrel with Mamma and Frank during the morning; she find the little village school and attend it, and first fondle the girl and thwart the mistress, then scold the girl and laugh at the teacher; she be constant at church, of course. it be a pretty little church, of immense antiquity--a little anglo-Norman bijou, build the day before yesterday, and decorate with all sort of paint window, carve saint' head, gilt scripture text, and open pew. Blanche begin forthwith to work a most correct high-church altar-cover for the church. she pass for a saint with the clergyman for a while, whom she quite take in, and whom she coax, and wheedle, and fondle so artfully, that poor Mrs. Smirke, who at first be charm with she, then bear with she, then would hardly speak to she, be almost mad with jealousy. Mrs. Smirke be the wife of our old friend Smirke, Pen's tutor and poor Helen's suitor. he have console himself for her refusal with a young lady from Clapham whom his mamma provide. when the latter die, our friend's view become every day more and more pronounced. he cut off his coat collar, and let his hair grow over his back. he rigorously give up the curl which he use to sport on his forehead, and the tie of his neckcloth, of which he be rather proud. he go without any tie at all. he go without dinner on Fridays. he read the Roman hour, and intimate that he be ready to receive confession in the vestry. the most harmless creature in the world, he be denounce as a black and most dangerous Jesuit and Papist, by Muffin of the dissenting chapel, and Mr. Simeon Knight at the old church. Mr. Smirke have build his chapel-of-ease with the money leave he by his mother at Clapham. Lord! lord! what would she have say to hear a table call an altar! to see candlestick on it! to get letter sign on the Feast of Saint so-and-so, or the Vigil of Saint what-do-you-call-'em! all these thing do the boy of Clapham practise; his faithful wife follow he. but when Blanche have a conference of near two hour in the vestry with Mr. Smirke, Belinda pace up and down on the grass, where there be only two little grave-stone as yet; she wish that she have a third there: only, only he would offer very likely to that creature, who have infatuate he in a fortnight. no, she would retire; she would go into a convent, and profess and leave he. such bad thought have Smirke's wife and his neighbour regard he; these, think he in direct correspondence with the Bishop of Rome; that, bewail error to she even more odious and fatal; and yet our friend mean no earthly harm. the post-office never bring he any letter from the Pope; he think Blanche, to be sure, at first, the most pious, gifted, right-thinking, fascinating person he have ever meet; and her manner of sing the chant delight he--but after a while he begin to grow rather tired of Miss Amory, her way and grace grow stale somehow; then he be doubtful about Miss Amory; then she make a disturbance in his school, lose her temper, and rap the child's finger. Blanche inspire this admiration and satiety, somehow, in many man. she try to please they, and fling out all her grace at once; come down to they with all her jewel on, all her smile, and cajolery, and coaxing, and ogle. then she grow tired of they and of try to please they, and never have care about they, drop they: and the man grow tired of she, and drop she too. it be a happy night for Belinda when Blanche go away; and her husband, with rather a blush and a sigh, say "he have be deceive in she; he have think she endow with many precious gift, he fear they be mere tinsel; he think she have be a right-think person, he fear she have merely make religion an amusement--she certainly have quite lose her temper to the schoolmistress, and beat Polly Rucker's knuckle cruelly." Belinda fly to his arm, there be no question about the grave or the veil any more. he tenderly embrace she on the forehead. "there be none like thee, my Belinda," he say, throw his fine eye up to the ceiling, "precious among woman!" as for Blanche, from the instant she lose sight of he and Belinda, she never think or care about either any more.
but when Arthur go down to pass a few day at Tunbridge Wells with the Begum, this stage of indifference have not arrive on Miss Blanche's part or on that of the simple clergyman. Smirke believe she to be an angel and wonder of a woman. such a perfection he have never see, and sate listen to her music in the summer evening, open-mouthed, rapt in wonder, tea-less, and bread-and-butter-less. fascinating as he have hear the music of the opera to be--he have never but once attend an exhibition of that nature (which he mention with a blush and a sigh--it be on that day when he have accompany Helen and her son to the play at Chatteris)--he could not conceive anything more delicious, more celestial, he have almost say, than Miss Amory's music. she be a most gifted being: she have a precious soul: she have the most remarkable talent--to all outward seem, the most heavenly disposition, etc. etc. it be in this way that, be then at the height of his own fever and bewitchment for Blanche, Smirke discourse to Arthur about she.
the meeting between the two old acquaintance have be very cordial. Arthur love anybody who love his mother; Smirke could speak on that theme with genuine feeling and emotion. they have a hundred thing to tell each other of what have occur in their life. "Arthur would perceive," Smirke say, "that his--his view on Church matter have develop themselves since their acquaintance." Mrs. Smirke, a most exemplary person, second they with all her endeavour. he have build this little church on his mother's demise, who have leave he provide with a sufficiency of worldly mean. though in the cloister himself, he have hear of Arthur's reputation. he speak in the kind and most sadden tone; he hold his eyelid down, and bow his fair head on one side. Arthur be immensely amuse with he; with his air; with his folly and simplicity; with his blank stock and long hair; with his real goodness, kindness, friendliness of feeling. and his praise of Blanche please and surprise our friend not a little, and make he regard she with eye of particular favour.
the truth be, Blanche be very glad to see Arthur; as one be glad to see an agreeable man in the country, who bring down the last news and story from the great city; who can talk well than most country-folk, at least can talk that darle London jargon, so dear and indispensable to London people, so little understand by person out of the world. the first day Pen come down, he keep Blanche laugh for hour after dinner. she sing her song with redoubled spirit. she do not scold her mother; she fondle and kiss she, to the honest Begum's surprise. when it come to be bedtime, she say, "Deja!" with the pretty air of regret possible; and be really quite sorry to go to bed, and squeeze Arthur's hand quite fondly. he on his side give she pretty palm a very cordial pressure. our young gentleman be of that turn, that eye very moderately bright dazzle he.
"she be very much improve," think Pen, look out into the night, "very much. I suppose the Begum willnot mind my smoking with the window open. shebe a jolly good old woman, and Blanche be immensely improve. I like her manner with her mother tonight. I like her laugh way with that stupid young cub of a boy, whom they oughtnot to allow to get tipsy. she sing those little verse very prettily; they be devilish pretty verse too, though I say it who shouldnot say it." and he hum a tune which Blanche have put to some verse of his own. "ah! what a fine night! how jolly a cigar be at night! how pretty that little Saxon church look in the moonlight! I wonder what old Warrington's doing? yes, shebe a dayvlish nice little thing, as my uncle say."
"oh, heavenly!" here break out a voice from a clematis-cover casement near--a girl's voice: it be the voice of the author of 'Mes Larmes.'
Pen burst into a laugh. "donot tell about my smoking," he say, lean out of his own window.
"oh! go on! I adore it," cry the lady of 'Mes Larmes.' "heavenly night! heavenly, heavenly moon! but I must shut my window, and not talk to you on account of les moeur. how droll they be, les moeur! Adieu." and Pen begin to sing the Goodnight to Don Basilio.
the next day they be walk in the field together, laugh and chattering--the gayest pair of friend. they talk about the day of their youth, and Blanche be prettily sentimental. they talk about Laura, dear Laura--Blanche have love she as a sister: be she happy with that odd Lady Rockminster? wouldnot she come and stay with they at Tunbridge? oh, what walk they would take together! what song they would sing--the old, old song! Laura's voice be splendid. do Arthur--she must call he Arthur--remember the song they sing in the happy old day, now he be grow such a great man, and have such a succe? etc. etc.
and the day after, which be enliven with a happy ramble through the wood to Penshurst, and a sight of that pleasant park and hall, come that conversation with the curate which we have narrate, and which make our young friend think more and more.
"be she all this perfection?" he ask himself. "have she become serious and religious? do she tend school, and visit the poor? be she kind to her mother and brother? yes, I be sure of that, I have see she." and walk with his old tutor over his little parish, and go to visit his school, it be with inexpressible delight that Pen find Blanche seat instruct the child, and fancy to himself how patient she must be, how good-nature, how ingenuous, how really simple in her taste, and unspoile by the world.
"and do you really like the country?" he ask she, as they walk together.
"I should like never to see that odious city again. O Arthur--that be, Mr.--well, Arthur, then--one's good thought grow up in these sweet wood and calm solitude, like those flower which willnot bloom in London, you know. the gardener come and change our balcony once a week. I donot think I shall bear to look London in the face again--its odious, smoky, brazen face! but, heigho!"
"why that sigh, Blanche?"
"never mind why."
"yes, I do mind why. tell I, tell I everything."
"I wish you havenot come down;" and a second edition of 'Mes Soupirs' come out.
"you donot want I, Blanche?"
"I donot want you to go away. I donot think this house will be very happy without you, and thatbe why I wish that you never have come."
'Mes Soupirs' be here lay aside, and 'Mes Larmes' have begin.
ah! what answer be give to those in the eye of a young woman? what be the method employ for dry they? what take place? o ringdove and rose, o dew and wildflower, o wave greenwood and balmy air of summer! here be two batter London rake, take themselves in for a moment, and fancy that they be in love with each other, like Phillis and Corydon!
when one think of country house and country walk, one wonder that any man be leave unmarried.
CHAPTER LXV
Temptation
Easy and frank-speak as Pendennis commonly be with Warrington, how come it that Arthur do not inform the friend and depository of all his secret, of the little circumstance which have take place at the villa near Tunbridge Wells? he talk about the discovery of his old tutor Smirke, freely enough, and of his wife, and of his anglo-Norman church, and of his departure from Clapha to Rome; but, when ask about Blanche, his answer be evasive or general: he say she be a good-natured clever little thing, that rightly guide she make no such bad wife after all, but that he have for the moment no intention of marriage, that his day of romance be over, that he be content with his present lot, and so forth.
in the meantime there come occasionally to Lamb Court, Temple, pretty little satin envelope, superscribe in the neat handwriting, and seal with one of those admirable cipher, which, if Warrington have be curious enough to watch his friend's letter, or indeed if the cipher have be decipherable, would have show George that Mr. Arthur be in correspondence with a young lady whose initial be B. a. to these pretty little composition Mr. Pen reply in his good and gallant manner; with joke, with news of the town, with point of wit, nay, with pretty little verse very likely, in reply to the versicle of the Muse of 'Mes Larmes.' Blanche we know rhyme with "branch," and "stanch," and "launch," and no doubt a gentleman of Pen's ingenuity would not forgo these advantage of position, and would ring the pretty little change upon these pleasing note. indeed we believe that those love-verse of Mr. Pen's, which have such a pleasing success in the 'roseleave,' that charming Annual edit by Lady Violet Lebas, and illustrate by portrait of the female nobility by the famous artist Pinkney, be compose at this period of our hero's life; and be first address to Blanche per post, before they figure in print, cornet as it be to Pinkney's pictorial garland.
"Verses be all very well," the elder Pendennis say, who find Pen scratch down one of these artless effusion at the Club as he be wait for his dinner; "and letter-writing if mamma allow it, and between such old country friend of course there may be a correspondence, and that sort of thing--but mind, Pen, and donot commit yourself, my boy. for who know what the doose may happen? the good way be to make your letter safe. I never write a letter in all my life that would commit I, and demmy, sir, I have have some experience of woman." and the worthy gentleman, grow more garrulous and confidential with his nephew as he grow old, tell many affecting instance of the evil result consequent upon this want of caution to many person in "Society;"--how from use too ardent expression in some poetical note to the widow Naylor, young Spoony have subject himself to a visit of remonstrance from the widow's brother, Colonel Flint; and thus have be force into a marriage with a woman old enough to be his mother: how when Louisa Salter have at length succeed in secure young Sir John Bird, Hopwood, of the Blues, produce some letter which Miss S. have write to he, and cause a withdrawal on Bird's part, who afterwards be unite to Miss Stickney, of Lyme Regis, etc. the Major, if he have not read, have plenty of observation, and could back his wise saw with a multitude of modern instance, which he have acquire in a long and careful perusal of the great book of the world.
Pen laugh at the example, and blush a little at his uncle's remonstrance, say that he would bear they in mind and be cautious. he blush, perhaps, because he have bear they in mind; because he be cautious: because in his letter to Miss Blanche he have from instinct, or honesty perhaps, refrain from any avowal which might compromise he. "donot you remember the lesson I have, sir, in Lady Mirabel's--Miss Fotheringay's affair? I be not to be catch again, uncle," Arthur say with mock frankness and humility. Old Pendennis congratulate himself and his nephew heartily on the latter's prudence and progress, and be pleased at the position which Arthur be take as a man of the world.
no doubt, if Warrington have be consult, his opinion would have be different: and he would have tell Pen that the boy's foolish letter be well than the man's adroit compliment and slippery gallantry; that to win the woman he love, only a knave or a coward advance under cover, with subterfuge, and a retreat secure behind he: but Pen speak not on this matter to Mr. Warrington, know pretty well that he be guilty, and what his friend's verdict would be.
Colonel Altamont have not be for many week absent on his foreign tour, Sir Francis Clavering have retire meanwhile into the country pursuant to his agreement with Major Pendennis, when the ill of fate begin to fall rather suddenly and heavily upon the sole remain partner of the little firm of Shepherd's Inn. when Strong, at part with Altamont, refuse the loan proffer by the latter in the fulness of his purse and the generosity of his heart, he make such a sacrifice to conscience and delicacy as cause he many an after twinge and pang; he and feel--it be not very many hour in his life he have experience the feeling--that in this juncture of his affair he have be too delicate and too scrupulous. why should a fellow in want refuse a kind offer kindly make? why should a thirsty man decline a pitcher of water from a friendly hand, because it be a little soil? Strong's conscience smite he for refuse what the other have fairly come by, and generously proffer: and he think ruefully, now it be too late, that Altamont's cash would have be as well in his pocket as in that of the gambling--house proprietor at Baden or Ems, with whom his Excellency would infallibly leave his Derby winning. it be whisper among the tradesman, bill-discounter, and other who have commercial dealing with Captain Strong, that he and the Baronet have part company, and that the Captain's "paper" be henceforth of no value. the tradesman, who have put a wonderful confidence in he hitherto,--for who could resist Strong's jolly face and frank and honest demeanour?--now begin to pour in their bill with a cowardly mistrust and unanimity. the knock at the Shepherd's Inn chamber door be constant, and tailor, bootmaker, pastrycook who have furnish dinner, in their own person, or by the boy their representative, hold levee on Strong's stair. to these be add one or two person of a less clamorous but far more sly and dangerous sort,--the young clerk of lawyer, namely, who lurk about the Inn, or concert with Mr. Campion's young man in the chamber hard by, have in their dismal pocketbook copy of writ to be serve on Edward Strong, require he to appear on an early day next term before our Sovereign Lady the Queen, and answer to, etc. etc.
from this invasion of creditor, poor Strong, who have not a guinea in his pocket, have, of course, no refuge but that of the Englishman's castle, into which he retire, shut the outer and inner door upon the enemy, and not quit his stronghold until after nightfall. against this outer barrier the foe use to come and knock and curse in vain, whilst the Chevalier peep at they from behind the little curtain which he have put over the orifice of his letter-box; and have the dismal satisfaction of see the face of furious clerk and fiery dun, as they dash up against the door and retreat from it. but as they could not be always at his gate, or sleep on his staircase, the enemy of the Chevalier sometimes leave he free.
Strong, when so pressed by his commercial antagonist, be not quite alone in his defence against they, but have secure for himself an ally or two. his friend be instruct to communicate with he by a system of private signal: and they thus keep the garrison from starve by bring in necessary supply, and keep up Strong's heart and prevent he from surrender by visit he and cheer he in his retreat. two of Ned's most faithful ally be Huxter and Miss Fanny Bolton: when hostile visitor be prowl about the Inn, Fanny's little sister be teach a particular cry or jodel, which they innocently whoop in the court: when Fanny and Huxter come up to visit Strong, they archly sing this same note at his door; when that barrier be straightway open, the honest garrison come out smile, the provision and the pot of porter be bring in, and in the society of his faithful friend the beleaguered one pass a comfortable night. there be some man who could not live under this excitement, but Strong be a brave man, as we have say, who have see service and never lose heart in peril.
but besides ally, our general have secure for himself, under difficulty, that still more necessary aid, a retreat. it have be mention in a former part of this history, how Messrs. Costigan and Bows live in the house next door to Captain Strong, and that the window of one of their room be not very far off the kitchen-window which be situate in the upper story of Strong's chamber. a leaden water-pipe and gutter serve for the two; and Strong, look out from his kitchen one day, see that he could spring with great ease up to the sill of his neighbour's window, and clamber up the pipe which communicate from one to the other. he have laughingly show this refuge to his chum, Altamont; and they have agree that it would be as well not to mention the circumstance to Captain Costigan, whose dun be numerous, and who would be constantly fly down the pipe into their apartment if this way of escape be show to he.
but now that the evil day be come, Strong make use of the passage, and one afternoon burst in upon bow and Costigan with his jolly face, and explain that the enemy be in wait on his staircase, and that he have take this mean of give they the slip. so while Mr. Marks's aide-de-camp be in wait in the passage of no. 3, Strong walk down the step of No. 4, din at the Albion, go to the play, and return home at midnight, to the astonishment of Mrs. Bolton and Fanny, who have not see he quit his chamber and could not conceive how he could have pass the line of sentry.
strong bear this siege for some week with admirable spirit and resolution, and as only such an old and brave soldier would, for the pain and privation which he have to endure be enough to depress any man of ordinary courage; and what vex and rile he (to use his own expression) be the infernal indifference and cowardly ingratitude of Clavering, to whom he write letter after letter, which the Baronet never acknowledge by a single word, or by the small remittance, though a five-pound note, as Strong say, at that time would have be a fortune to he.
but well day be in store for the Chevalier, and in the midst of his despondency and perplexity there come to he a most welcome aid. "yes, if it havenot be for this good fellow here," say strong,--"for a good fellow you be, Altamont, my boy, and hang I if I donot stand by you as long as I live,--i think, Pendennis, it would have be all up with Ned Strong. I be the fifth week of my be keep a prisoner, for I couldnot be always risk my neck across that water-pipe, and take my walk abroad through poor old Cos's window, and my spirit be quite break, sir--dammy, quite beat, and I be think of put an end to myself, and should have do it in another week, when who should drop down from heaven but Altamont!"
"Heaven benot exactly the place, Ned," say Altamont. "I come from Baden-Baden," say he, "and Iwould have a deuced lucky month there, thatbe all."
"well, sir, he take up Marks's bill, and he pay the other fellow that be upon I, like a man, sir, that he do," say Strong, enthusiastically.
"and I shall be very happy to stand a bottle of claret for this company, and as many more as the company choose," say Mr. Altamont, with a blush. "Hallo! waiter, bring we a magnum of the right sort, do you hear? and wewill drink our health all round, sir--and may every good fellow like Strong find another good fellow to stand by he at a pinch. thatbe my sentiment, Mr. Pendennis, though I donot like your name."
"no! and why?" ask Arthur.
Strong press the Colonel's foot under the table here; and Altamont, rather excited, fill up another bumper, nod to Pen, drink off his wine, and say, "he be a gentleman, and that be sufficient, and they be all gentleman."
the meeting between these "all gentleman" take place at Richmond, whither Pendennis have go to dinner, and where he find the Chevalier and his friend at table in the coffee-room. both of the latter be exceedingly hilarious, talkative, and excite by wine; and Strong, who be an admirable story-teller, tell the story of his own siege, and adventure, and escape with great liveliness and humour, and describe the talk of the sheriff's officer at his door, the pretty little signal of Fanny, the grotesque exclamation of Costigan when the Chevalier burst in at his window, and his final rescue by Altamont, in a most graphic manner, and so as greatly to interest his hearer.
"as for I, itbe nothing," Altamont say. "when a ship's pay off, a chap spend his money, you know. and itbe the feller at the black and red at Baden-Baden that do it. I win a good bit of money there, and intend to win a good bit more, donot I, strong? Ibe go to take he with I. Ihave get a system. Iwill make his fortune, I tell you. Iwill make your fortune, if you like--dammy, everybody's fortune. but what Iwill do, and no mistake, boy, I promise you. Iwill put in for that little Fanny. Dammy, sir, what do you think she do? she have two pound, and Ibe blest if she donot go and lend it to Ned Strong! donot she, Ned? letus drink her health."
"with all my heart," say Arthur, and pledge this toast with the great cordiality.
Mr. Altamont then begin, with the great volubility, at great length, to describe his system. he say that it be infallible, if play with coolness; that he have it from a chap at Baden, who have lose by it, it be true, but because he have not enough capital; if he could have stand one more turn of the wheel, he would have have all his money back; that he and several more chap be go to make a bank, and try it; and that he would put every shilling he be worth into it, and have come back to the country for the express purpose of fetch away his money, and Captain Strong; that Strong should play for he; that he could trust Strong and his temper much well than he could his own; and much well than Bloundell-Bloundell or the italian that "stand in." as he empty his bottle, the Colonel describe at full length all his plan and prospect to Pen, who be interested in listen to his story, and the confession of his daring and lawless good-humour.
"I meet that queer fellow Altamont the other day," Pen say to his uncle, a day or two afterwards.
"Altamont? what altamont? therebe Lord Westport's son," say the Major.
"no, no; the fellow who come tipsy into Clavering's dining-room one day when we be there," say the nephew, laugh, "he say he do not like the name of Pendennis, though he do I the honour to think that I be a good fellow."
"I donot know any man of the name of Altamont, I give you my honour," say the impenetrable Major; "and as for your acquaintance, I think the less you have to do with he the well, Arthur."
