Arthur laugh again. "he be go to quit the country, and make his fortune by a gambling system. he and my amiable college acquaintance, Bloundell, be partner, and the Colonel take out Strong with he as aide-de-camp. what be it that bind the Chevalier and Clavering, I wonder?"
"I should think, mind you, Pen, I should think, but of course I have only the idea, that there have be something in Clavering's previous life which give these fellow and some other a certain power over he; and if there should be no such a secret, which affair of our, my boy, dammy, I say, it ought to be a lesson to a man to keep himself straight in life, and not to give any man a chance over he."
"why, I think you have some mean of persuasion over Clavering, uncle, or why should he give I that seat in Parlament?"
"clavering think he benot fit for Parliament," the Major answer. "no more he be. whatbe to prevent he from put you or anybody else into his place if he like? do you think that vernment or the opposition would make any bone about accept the seat if he offer it to they! why should you be more squeamish than the first man, and the most honourable man, and man of the high birth and position in the country, begad?" the Major have an answer of this kind to most of Pen's objection, and Pen accept his uncle's reply, not so much because he believe they, but because he wish to believe they. we do a thing--which of we have not?-- not because "everybody do it," but because we like it; and our acquiescence, alas! prove not that everybody be right, but that we and the rest of the world be poor creature alike.
at his next visit to Tunbridge, Mr. Pen do not forget to amuse Miss Blanche with the history which he have learn at Richmond of the Chevalier's imprisonment, and of Altamont's gallant rescue. and after he have tell his tale in his usual satirical way, he mention with praise and emotion little Fanny's generous behaviour to the Chevalier, and Altamont's enthusiasm in her behalf.
Miss Blanche be somewhat jealous, and a good deal pique and curious about Fanny. among the many confidential little communication which Arthur make to Miss Amory in the course of their delightful rural drive and their sweet evening walk, it may be suppose that our hero would not forget a story so interesting to himself and so likely to be interesting to she, as that of the passion and cure of the poor little Ariadne of Shepherd's Inn. his own part in that drama he describe, to do he justice, with become modesty; the moral which he wish to draw from the tale be one in accordance with his usual satirical mood, viz., that woman get over their first love quite as easily as man do (for the fair Blanche, in their intime conversation, do not cease to twit Mr. Pen about his notorious failure in his own virgin attachment to the Fotheringay), and, number one be withdraw, transfer themselves to number two without much difficulty. and poor little Fanny be offer up in sacrifice as an instance to prove this theory. what grief she have endure and surmount, what bitter pang of hopeless attachment she have go through, what time it have take to heal those wound of the tender little bleed heart, Mr. Pen do not know, or perhaps do not choose to know; for he be at once modest and doubtful about his capability as a conqueror of heart, and averse to believe that he have execute any dangerous ravage on that particular one, though his own instance and argument tell against himself in this case; for if, as he say, Miss Fanny be by this time in love with her surgical adorer, who have neither good look, nor good manner, nor wit, nor anything but ardour and fidelity to recommend he, must she not in her first sickness of the love-complaint have have a serious attack, and suffer keenly for a man who have certainly a number of the showy quality which Mr. Huxter want?
"you wicke odious creature," Miss Blanche say, "I believe that you be enrage with Fanny for be so impudent as to forget you, and that you be actually jealous of Mr. Huxter." perhaps Miss Amory be right, as the blush which come in spite of himself and tingle upon Pendennis's cheek (one of those blow with which a man's vanity be constantly slap his face) prove to Pen that he be angry to think he have be supersede by such a rival. by such a fellow as that! without any conceivable good quality! o Mr. Pendennis! (although this remark do not apply to such a smart fellow as you) if Nature have not make that provision for each sex in the credulity of the other, which see good quality where none exist, good look in donkey' ear, wit in their numskull, and music in their bray, there would not have be near so much marrying and give in marriage as now obtain, and as be necessary for the due propagation and continuance of the noble race to which we belong.
"jealous or not," Pen say, "and, Blanche, I donot say no, I should have like Fanny to have come to a well end than that. I donot like history that end in that cynical way; and when we arrive at the conclusion of the story of a pretty girl's passion, to find such a figure as Huxter's at the last page of the tale. be a life a compromise, my lady fair, and the end of the battle of love an ignoble surrender? be the search for the Cupid which my poor little psyche pursue in the darkness --the god of her soul's longing--the god of the bloom cheek and rainbow pinions,--to result in Huxter smelling of tobacco and gallypot? I wish, though I donot see it in life, that people could be like Jenny and Jessamy, or my Lord and Lady Clementina in the story-book and fashionable novel, and at once under the ceremony, and, as it be, at the parson's benediction, become perfectly handsome and good and happy ever after."
"and donot you intend to be good and happy, pray, Monsieur le Misanthrope--and be you very discontent with your lot--and will your marriage be a compromise"--(asked the author of 'Mes Larmes,' with a charming moue)--"and be your psyche an odious vulgar wretch? you wicke satirical creature, I cannot abide you! you take the heart of young thing, play with they, and fle they away with scorn. you ask for love and trample on it. you--you make I cry, that you do, Arthur, and--and don't--and I willnot be console in that way--and I think Fanny be quite right in leave such a heartless creature."
"again, I donot say no," say Pen, look very gloomily at Blanche, and not offer by any mean to repeat the attempt at consolation, which have elicit that sweet monosyllable "donot" from the young lady. "I donot think I have much of what people call heart; but I donot profess it. I make my venture when I be eighteen, and light my lamp and go in search of Cupid. and what be my discovery of love?--a vulgar dancing-woman! I fail, as everybody do, almost everybody; only it be lucky to fail before marriage than after."
"merci du choix, Monsieur," say the sylphide, make a curtsey.
"look, my little Blanche," say Pen, take her hand, and with his voice of sad good-humour; "at least I stoop to no flattery."
"quite the contrary," say Miss Blanche.
and tell you no foolish lie, as vulgar man do. why should you and I, with our experience, ape romance and dissemble passion? I do not believe Miss Blanche Amory to be peerless among the beautiful, nor the great poetess, nor the most surpass musician, any more than I believe you to be the tall woman in the whole world--like the giantess whose picture we see as we ride through the fair yesterday. but if I donot set you up as a heroine, neither do I offer you your very humble servant as a hero. but I think you be--well, there, I think you be very sufficiently good-look."
"merci," Miss Blanche say, with another curtsey.
"I think you sing charmingly. Ibe sure yoube clever. I hope and believe that you be good-natured, and that you will be companionable."
"and so, provide I bring you a certain sum of money and a seat in Parliament, you condescend to fle to I your royal pocket-handkerchief," say Blanche. "Que d'honneur! we use to call your Highness the Prince of Fairoaks. what an honour to think that I be to be elevate to the throne, and to bring the seat in Parliament as backsheesh to the sultan! I be glad I be clever, and that I can play and sing to your liking; my song will amuse my lord's leisure."
"and if thief be about the house," say Pen, grimly pursue the simile, "forty besetting thief in the shape of lurking care and enemy in ambush and passion in arm, my Morgiana will dance round I with a tambourine, and kill all my rogue and thief with a smile. willnot she?" but Pen look as if he do not believe that she would. "ah, Blanche," he continue after a pause, "donot be angry; donot be hurt at my truth-telling.--Don't you see that I always take you at your word? you say you will be a slave and dance--I say, dance. you say, 'I take you with what you bring:' I say, 'I take you with what you bring.' to the necessary deceit and hypocrisy of our life, why add any that be useless and unnecessary? if I offer myself to you because I think we have a fair chance of be happy together, and because by your help I may get for both of we a good place and a not undistinguished name, why ask I to feign rapture and counterfeit romance, in which neither of we believe? do you want I to come wooing in a Prince Prettyman's dress from the masquerade warehouse, and to pay you compliment like Sir Charles Grandison? do you want I to make you verse as in the day when we be --when we be child? I will if you like, and sell they to Bacon and Bungay afterwards. shall I feed my pretty princess with bonbon?"
"Mais j'adore le bonbon, moi," say the little sylphide, with a queer piteous look.
"I can buy a hatful at Fortnum and Mason's for a guinea. and it shall have its bonbon, its pooty little sugar-plum, that it shall," Pen say with a bitter smile. "Nay, my dear, nay, my dear little Blanche, donot cry. dry the pretty eye, I cannot bear that;" and he proceed to offer that consolation which the circumstance require, and which the tear, the genuine tear of vexation, which now spring from the angry eye of the author of 'Mes Larmes' demand.
the scornful and sarcastic tone of Pendennis quite frightened and overcome the girl. "I--I donot want your consolation. I--I never be--so --spoken to before--by any of my--my--by anybody"--she sob out, with much simplicity.
"anybody!" shout out Pen, with a savage burst of laughter, and Blanche blush one of the most genuine blush which her cheek have ever exhibit, and she cry out, "O Arthur, vous ete un homme terrible!" she feel bewildered, frightened, oppressed, the worldly little flirt who have be play at love for the last dozen year of her life, and yet not displeased at meet a master.
"tell I, Arthur," she say, after a pause in this strange love-making. "why do Sir Francis Clavering give up his seat in Parliament?"
"Au fait, why do he give it to I?" ask Arthur, now blush in his turn.
"you always mock I, sir," she say. "if it be good to be in Parliament, why do Sir Francis go out?"
"my uncle have talk he over. he always say that you be not sufficiently provide for. in the--the family dispute, when your mamma pay his debt so liberally, it be stipulate, I suppose, that you--that be, that I--that is, upon my word, I donot know why he go out of Parliament," Pen say, with rather a forced laugh. "you see, Blanche, that you and I be two good little child, and that this marriage have be arrange for we by our mamma and uncle, and that we must be obedient, like a good little boy and girl."
so, when Pen go to London, he send Blanche a box of bonbon, each sugar-plum of which be wrap up in ready-make french verse, of the most tender kind; and, besides, despatch to she some poem of his own manufacture, quite as artless and authentic; and it be no wonder that he do not tell Warrington what his conversation with Miss Amory have be, of so delicate a sentiment be they, and of a nature so necessarily private.
and if, like many a bad and well man, Arthur Pendennis, the widow's son, be meditate an apostasy, and go to sell himself to--we all know whom,--at least the renegade do not pretend to be a believer in the creed to which he be ready to swear. and if every woman and man in this kingdom, who have sell she or himself for money or position, as Mr. Pendennis be about to do, would but purchase a copy of his memoir, what ton of volume Messrs. Bradbury and Evans would sell!
CHAPTER LXVI
in which Pen begin his Canvass
Melancholy as the great house at Clavering Park have be in the day before his marriage, when its bankrupt proprietor be a refugee in foreign land, it be not much more cheerful now when Sir Francis Clavering come to inhabit it. the great part of the mansion be shut up, and the Baronet only occupy a few of the room on the ground floor, where his housekeeper and her assistant from the lodge-gate wait upon the luckless gentleman in his forced retreat, and cook a part of the game which he spend the dreary morning in shooting. Lightfoot, his man, have pass over to my Lady's service; and, as Pen be inform in a letter from Mr. Smirke, who perform the ceremony, have execute his prudent intention of marry Mrs. Bonner, my Lady's woman, who, in her mature year, be stricken with the charm of the youth, and endow he with her saving and her mature person.
to be landlord and landlady of the Clavering Arms be the ambition of both of they; and it be agree that they be to remain in Lady Clavering's service until quarter-day arrive, when they be to take possession of their hotel. Pen graciously promise that he would give his election dinner there, when the Baronet should vacate his seat in the young man's favour; and, as it have be agree by his uncle, to whom Clavering seem to be able to refuse nothing, Arthur come down in September on a visit to Clavering Park, the owner of which be very glad to have a companion who would relieve his loneliness, and perhaps would lend he a little ready money.
Pen furnish his host with these desirable supply a couple of day after he have make his appearance at Clavering: and no soon be these small fund in Sir Francis's pocket, than the latter find he have business at Chatteris and at the neighbouring watering-place, of which ------shire boast many, and go off to see to his affair, which be transact, as might be suppose, at the county race-ground and billiard-room. Arthur could live alone well enough, have many mental resource and amusement which do not require other person' company: he could walk with the gamekeeper of a morning, and for the evening there be a plenty of book and occupation for a literary genius like Mr. Arthur, who require but a cigar and a sheet of paper or two to make the night pass away pleasantly. in truth, in two or three day he have find the society of Sir Francis Clavering perfectly intolerable; and it be with a mischievous eagerness and satisfaction that he offer Clavering the little pecuniary aid which the latter accord to his custom solicit, and supply he with the mean of take flight from his own house.
besides, our ingenious friend have to ingratiate himself with the townspeople of Clavering, and with the voter of the borough which he hop to represent; and he set himself to this task with only the more eagerness, remember how unpopular he have before be in Clavering, and determine to vanquish the odium which he have inspire amongst the simple people there. his sense of humour make he delight in this task. naturally rather reserved and silent in public, he become on a sudden as frank, easy, and jovial as Captain Strong. he laugh with everybody who would exchange a laugh with he, shake hand right and leave, with what may be certainly call a dexterous cordiality; make his appearance at the market-day and the farmer' ordinary; and, in fine, act like a consummate hypocrite, and as gentleman of the high birth and most spotless integrity act when they wish to make themselves agreeable to their constituent, and have some end to gain of the country-folk. how be it that we allow ourselves not to be deceive, but to be ingratiate so readily by a glib tongue, a ready laugh, and a frank manner? we know, for the most part, that it be false coin, and we take it we know that it be flattery, which it cost nothing to distribute to everybody, and we have rather have it than be without it. Friend Pen go about at Clavering, laboriously simple and adroitly pleased, and quite a different being from the scornful and rather sulky young dandy whom the inhabitant remember ten year ago.
the Rectory be shut up. Doctor Portman be go, with his gout and his family, to Harrogate,--an event which Pen deplore very much in a letter to the Doctor, in which, in a few kind and simple word, he express his regret at not see his old friend, whose advice he want and whose aid he might require some day: but Pen console himself for the Doctor's absence by make acquaintance with Mr. Simcoe, the opposition preacher, and with the two partner of the cloth-factory at Chatteris, and with the Independent preacher there, all of whom he meet at Clavering Athenaeum, which the Liberal party have set up in accordance with the advanced spirit of the age, and perhaps in opposition to the aristocratic old reading-room, into which the Edinburgh Review have once scarcely get an admission, and where no tradesman be allow an entrance. he propitiate the young partner of the cloth-factory, by ask he to dine in a friendly way at the Park; he compliment the Honourable Mrs. Simcoe with hare and partridge from the same quarter, and a request to read her husband's last sermon; and be a little unwell one day, the rascal take advantage of the circumstance to show his tongue to Mr. Huxter, who send he medicine and call the next morning. how delight old Pendennis would have be with his pupil! Pen himself be amuse with the sport in which he be engage, and his success inspire he with a wicked good-humour.
and yet, as he walk out of Clavering of a night, after "preside" at a meeting of the Athenaeum, or work through an evening with Mrs. Simcoe, who, with her husband, be awe by the young Londoner's reputation, and have hear of his social success; as he pass over the old familiar bridge of the rushing Brawl, and hear that well-remember sound of water beneath, and see his own cottage of Fairoaks among the tree, their darkling outline clear against the starlit sky, different thought no doubt come to the young man's mind, and awaken pang of grief and shame there. there still use to be a light in the window of the room which he remember so well, and in which the Saint who love he have pass so many hour of care and yearning and prayer. he turn away his gaze from the faint light which seem to pursue he with its wan reproachful gaze, as though it be his mother's spirit watch and warning. how clear the night be! how keen the star shine! how ceaseless the rush of the flow water! the old home tree whisper, and wave gently their dark head and branch over the cottage roof. Yonder, in the faint starlight glimmer, be the terrace where, as a boy, he walk of summer evening, ardent and trustful, unspotted, untried, ignorant of doubt or passion; shelter as yet from the world's contamination in the pure and anxious bosom of love. the clock of the near town tolling midnight, with a clang, disturb our wanderer's reverie, and send he onwards towards his night's resting-place, through the lodge into Clavering avenue, and under the dark arcade of the rustle lime.
when he see the cottage the next time, it be smile in sunset; those bedroom window be open where the light be burn the night before; and Pen's tenant, Captain Stokes, of the Bombay Artillery (whose mother, old Mrs. Stokes, live in Clavering), receive his landlord's visit with great cordiality: show he over the ground and the new pond he have make in the back-garden from the stable; talk to he confidentially about the roof and chimney, and beg Mr. Pendennis to name a day when he will do himself and Mrs. Stokes the pleasure to, etc. Pen, who have be a fortnight in the country, excuse himself for not have call soon upon the Captain by frankly own that he have not the heart to do it. "I understand you, sir," the Captain say; and Mrs. Stokes, who have slip away at the ring of the bell (how odd it seem to Pen to ring the bell!), come down in her good gown, surround by her child. the young one clamb about Stokes: the boy jump into an arm-chair. it be Pen's father's arm-chair; and Arthur remember the day when he would as soon have think of mount the king's throne as of seat himself in that arm-chair. he ask if Miss Stokes--she be the very image of her mamma--if she can play? he should like to hear a tune on that piano. she play. he hear the note of the old piano once more, enfeeble by age, but he do not listen to the player. he be listen to Laura singing as in the day of their youth, and see his mother bend and beat time over the shoulder of the girl.
the dinner at Fairoaks give in Pen's honour by his tenant, and at which old Mrs. Stokes, Captain Glanders, Squire Hobnel and the clergyman and his lady from Tinckleton, be present, be very stupid and melancholy for Pen, until the waiter from Clavering (who aid the captain's stable-boy and Mrs. Stokes's butler) whom Pen remember as a street boy, and who be now indeed barber in that place, drop a plate over Pen's shoulder, on which Mr. Hobnell (who also employ he) remark, "I suppose, Hodson, your hand be slippery with bear'-grease. hebe always drop the crockery about, that Hodson be--haw, haw!" on which Hodson blush, and look so disconcerted, that Pen burst out laugh; and good-humour and hilarity be the order of the evening. for the second course, there be a hare and partridge top and bottom, and when after the withdrawal of the servant Pen say to the Vicar of Tinckleton, "I think, Mr. Stooks, you should have ask Hodson to cut the hare," the joke be take instantly by the clergyman, who be follow in the course of a few minute by Captains Stokes and Glanders, and by Mr. Hobnell, who arrive rather late, but with an immense guffaw.

while Mr. Pen be engage in the country in the above scheme, it happen that the lady of his choice, if not of his affection, come up to London from the Tunbridge villa bind upon shopping expedition or important business, and in company of old Mrs. Bonner, her mother's maid, who have live and quarrel with Blanche many time since she be an infant, and who now be about to quit Lady Clavering's service for the hymeneal state, be anxious like a good soul to bestow some token of respectful kindness upon her old and young mistress before she quit they altogether, to take her post as the wife of Lightfoot, and landlady of the Clavering Arms.
the honest woman take the benefit of Miss Amory's taste to make the purchase which she intend to offer her ladyship; and, request the fair Blanche to choose something for herself that should be to her liking, and remind she of her old nurse who have attend she through many a wakeful night, and eventful teething, and childish fever, and who love she like a child of her own a'most. these purchase be make, and as the nurse insist on buy an immense Bible for Blanche, the young lady suggest that Bonner should purchase a large Johnson's Dictionary for her mamma. each of the two woman might certainly profit by the present make to she.

it be in the Strand that Mrs. Bonner make her purchase, aid by Miss Blanche, who like the fun very well; and when the old lady have buy everything that she desire, and be leave the shop, Blanche, with a smile face, and a sweet bow to one of the shopman, say, "pray, sir, will you have the kindness to show we the way to Shepherd's Inn?"
Shepherd's Inn be but a few score of yard off, Old Castle Street be close by, the elegant young shopman point out the turning which the young lady be to take, and she and her companion walk off together.
"Shepherd's Inn! what can you want in Shepherd's Inn, Miss Blanche?" Bonner inquire. "Mr. Strong live there. do you want to go and see the Captain?"
"I should like to see the Captain very well. I like the Captain; but it be not he I want. I want to see a dear little good girl, who be very kind to--to Mr. Arthur when he be so ill last year, and save his life almost; and I want to thank she and ask she if she would like anything. I look out several of my dress on purpose this morning, Bonner!" and she look at Bonner as if she have a right to admiration, and have perform an act of remarkable virtue. Blanche, indeed, be very fond of sugar-plum; she would have feed the poor upon they, when she have have enough, and give a country girl a ball-dress, when she have wear it and be tired of it.
"pretty girl--pretty young woman!" mumble Mrs. Bonner. "I know I want no pretty young woman to come about Lightfoot," and in imagination she people the Clavering Arms with a harem of the most hideous chambermaid and barmaid.
Blanche, with pink and blue, and feather, and flower, and trinket (that wondrous invention, a chatelaine, be not extant yet, or she would have have one, we may be sure), and a shot-silk dress, and a wonderful mantle, and a charming parasol, present a vision of elegance and beauty such as bewilder the eye of Mrs. Bolton, who be scrub the lodge-floor of Shepherd's Inn and cause Betsy-Jane and Ameliar-Ann to look with delight.
Blanche look on they with a smile of ineffable sweetness and protection; like Rowena go to see Rebecca; like Marie Antoinette visit the poor in the famine; like the Marchioness of Carabas alight from her carriage-and-four at a pauper-tenant's door, and take from John No II. the packet of Epsom salt for the invalid's benefit, carry it with her own imperial hand into the sick-room-- Blanche feel a queen step down from her throne to visit a subject, and enjoy all the bland consciousness of do a good action.
"my good woman! I want to see Fanny--Fanny Bolton; be she here?"
Mrs. Bolton have a sudden suspicion, from the splendour of Blanche's appearance, that it must be a play-actor, or something bad.
"what do you want with Fanny, pray?" she ask.
"I be Lady Clavering's daughter--you have hear of Sir Francis Clavering? and I wish very much indeed to see Fanny Bolton."
"pray step in, miss.--betsy-Jane, wherebe fanny?"
Betsy-Jane say Fanny have go into no. 3 staircase, on which Mrs. Bolton say she be probably in Strong's room, and bade the child go and see if she be there.
"in Captain Strong's room! oh, let we go to Captain Strong's room," cry out Miss Blanche. "I know he very well. you dearest little girl, show we the way to Captain Strong!" cry out Miss Blanche, for the floor reek with the recent scrubbing, and the goddess do not like the smell of brown-soap.
and as they pass up the stair, a gentleman by the name of Costigan, who happen to be swagger about the court, and give a very knowing look with his "oi" under Blanche's bonnet, remark to himself, "thatbe a devilish foine gyurll, bedad, goan up to Sthrong and Altamont: theybe always have foine gyurll up their stair."
"Hallo--hwhatbe that?" he presently say, look up at the window: from which some pierce shriek issue.
at the sound of the voice of a distressed female the intrepid cos rush up the stair as fast as his old leg would carry he, be nearly overthrow by Strong's servant, who be descend the stair. cos find the outer door of Strong's chamber open, and begin to thunder at the knocker. after many and fierce knock, the inner door be partially unclose, and Strong's head appear.
"itbe oi, I boy. Hwhatbe that noise, Sthrong?" ask Costigan.
"go to the d----!" be the only answer, and the door be shut on Cos's venerable red nose: and he go downstairs mutter threat at the indignity offer to he, and vow that he would have satisfaction. in the meanwhile the reader, more lucky than Captain Costigan, will have the privilege of be make acquainted with the secret which be withhold from that officer.
it have be say of how generous a disposition Mr. Altamont be, and when he be well supply with fund how liberally he spend they. of a hospitable turn, he have no great pleasure than drink in company with other people; so that there be no man more welcome at Greenwich and Richmond than the Emissary of the Nawaub of Lucknow.
now it chance that on the day when Blanche and Mrs. Bonner ascend the staircase to Strong's room in Shepherd's Inn, the Colonel have invite Miss Delaval of the ------ Theatre Royal, and her mother, Mrs. Hodge, to a little party down the river, and it have be agree that they be to meet at Chambers, and thence walk down to a port in the neighbouring Strand to take water. so that when Mrs. Bonner and Mes Larmes come to the door, where Grady, Altamont's servant, be stand, the domestic say, "walk in, lady," with the utmost affability, and lead they into the room, which be arrange as if they have be expect there. indeed, two bouquet of flower, buy at Covent Garden that morning, and instance of the tender gallantry of Altamont, be await his guest upon the table. Grady meanwhile, with a pair of ample varnish boot, have disappear into his master's room. Blanche have hardly the leisure to remark how big the boot be, and how unlike Mr. Strong's.
"the woman's come," say Grady, help his master to the boot.

Grady come out--"he say, will you take anything to drink?" the domestic ask of they; at which Blanche, amuse with the artless question, break out into a pretty little laugh, and ask of Mrs. Bonner, "shall we take anything to drink?"

Grady do not answer: his song be hear from afar off, from the kitchen, his upper room, where Grady be sing at his work.

CHAPTER LXVII
in which Pen begin to doubt about his Election
Whilst Pen, in his own county, be thus carry on his selfish plan and parliamentary scheme, news come to he that Lady Rockminster have arrive at Baymouth, and have bring with she our friend Laura. at the announcement that Laura his sister be near he, Pen feel rather guilty. his wish be to stand high in her esteem, perhaps; than in that of any other person in the world. she be his mother's legacy to he. he be to be her patron and protector in some sort. how would she brave the news which he have to tell she; and how should he explain the plan which he be meditate? he feel as if neither he nor Blanche could bear Laura's dazzle glance of calm scrutiny, and as if he would not dare to disclose his worldly hope and ambition to that spotless judge. at her arrival at Baymouth, he write a letter thither which contain a great number of fine phrase and protest of affection, and a great deal of easy satire and raillery; in the midst of all which Mr. Pen could not help feel that he be in panic, and that he be act like a rogue and hypocrite.
how be it that a simple country girl should be the object of fear and tremble to such an accomplished gentleman as Mr. Pen? his worldly tactic and diplomacy, his satire and knowledge of the world, could not bear the test of her purity, he feel somehow. and he have to own to himself that his affair be in such a position, that he could not tell the truth to that honest soul. as he ride from Clavering to Baymouth he feel as guilty as a schoolboy who donot know his lesson and be about to face the awful master. for be not truth the master always, and do she not have the power and hold the book?
under the charge of her kind, though somewhat wayward and absolute patroness, Lady Rockminster, Laura have see somewhat of the world in the last year, have gather some accomplishment, and profit by the lesson of society. many a girl who have be accustom to that too great tenderness in which Laura's early life have be pass, would have be unfit for the change existence which she now have to lead. Helen worship her two child, and think, as home-breed woman will, that all the world be make for they, or to be consider after they. she tend Laura with a watchfulness of affection which never leave she. if she have a headache, the widow be as alarmed as if there have never be an ache head before in the world. she sleep and wake, read and move under her mother's fond superintendence, which be now withdraw from she, along with the tender creature whose anxious heart would beat no more. and painful moment of grief and depression no doubt Laura have, when she stand in the great careless world alone. nobody heed her grief or her solitude. she be not quite the equal, in social rank, of the lady whose companion she be, or of the friend and relative of the imperious, but kind old dowager. some very likely bear she no goodwill-- some, perhaps, slight she: it might have be that servant be occasionally rude; their mistress certainly be often. Laura not seldom find herself in family meeting, the confidence and familiarity of which she feel be interrupt by her intrusion; and her sensitiveness of course be wound at the idea that she should give or feel this annoyance. how many governess be there in the world, think cheerful Laura,--how many lady, whose necessity make they slave and companion by profession! what bad temper and coarse unkindness have not these to encounter? how infinitely well my lot be with these really kind and affectionate people than that of thousand of unprotected girl! it be with this cordial spirit that our young lady adapt herself to her new position; and go in advance of her fortune with a trustful smile.
do you ever know a person who meet Fortune in that way, whom the goddess do not regard kindly? be not even bad people win by a constant cheerfulness and a pure and affectionate heart? when the babe in the wood, in the ballad, look up fondly and trustfully at those notorious rogue whom their uncle have set to make away with the little folk, we all know how one of the rascal relent, and make away with the other-- not have the heart to be unkind to so much innocence and beauty. oh, happy they who have that virgin love trust and sweet smile confidence in the world, and fear no evil because they think none! Miss Laura Bell be one of these fortunate person; and besides the gentle widow's little cross, which, as we have see, Pen give she, have such a sparkling and brilliant kohinoor in her bosom, as be even more precious than that famous jewel; for it not only fetch a price, and be retain, by its owner in another world where diamond be state to be of no value, but here, too, be of inestimable worth to its possessor; be a talisman against evil, and lighten up the darkness of life, like Cogia Hassan's famous stone.
so that before Miss Bell have be a year in Lady Rockminster's house, there be not a single person in it whose love she have not win by the use of this talisman. from the old lady to the low dependent of her bounty, Laura have secure the goodwill and kindness of everybody. with a mistress of such a temper, my Lady's woman (who have endure her mistress for forty year, and have be claw and scold and jib every day and night in that space of time) could not be expect to have a good temper of her own; and be at first angry against Miss Laura, as she have be against her ladyship's fifteen precede companion. but when Laura be ill at Paris, this old woman nurse she in spite of her mistress, who be afraid of catch the fever, and absolutely fight for her medicine with Martha from Fairoaks, now advanced to be Miss Laura's own maid. as she be recover, Grandjean the chef want to kill she by the number of delicacy which he dress for she, and weep when she eat her first slice of chicken. the swiss major-domo of the house celebrate Miss Bell's praise in almost every european language, which he speak with indifferent incorrectness; the coachman be happy to drive she out; the page cry when he hear she be ill; and Calverley and Coldstream (those two footman, so large, so calm ordinarily, and so difficult to move) break out into extraordinary hilarity at the news of her convalescence, and intoxicate the page at a wine-shop, to fete Laura's recovery. even Lady Diana Pynsent (our former acquaintance Mr. Pynsent have marry by this time), Lady Diana, who have have a considerable dislike to Laura for some time, be so enthusiastic as to say that she think Miss Bell be a very agreeable person, and that grandmamma have find a great trouvaille in she. all this goodwill and kindness Laura have acquire, not by any art, not by any flattery, but by the simple force of good-nature, and by the blessed gift of please and be pleased.
on the one or two occasion when he have see Lady Rockminster, the old lady, who do not admire he, have be very pitiless and abrupt with our young friend, and perhaps Pen expect when he come to Baymouth to find Laura instal in her house in the quality of humble companion, and treat no well than himself. when she hear of his arrival she come run downstairs, and I be not sure that she do not embrace he in the presence of Calverley and Coldstream: not that those gentleman ever tell: if the fractus orbis have come to a smash, if Laura, instead of kiss Pen, have take her scissor and snip off his head--Calverley and Coldstream would have look on impavidly, without allow a grain of powder to be disturb by the calamity.
Laura have so much improve in health and look that Pen could not but admire she. the frank and kind eye which meet his, beam with good-health; the cheek which he kiss blush with beauty. as he look at she, artless and graceful, pure and candid, he think he have never see she so beautiful. why should he remark her beauty now so much, and remark too to himself that he have not remark it soon? he take her fair trustful hand and kiss it fondly: he look in her bright clear eye, and read in they that kindle welcome which he be always sure to find there. he be affect and touch by the tender tone and the pure sparkling glance; their innocence smite he somehow and move he.
"how good you be to I, Laura--sister!" say Pen; "I donot deserve that you should--that you should be so kind to I."
"Mamma leave you to I," she say, stoop down and brush his forehead with her lip hastily. "you know you be to come to I when you be in trouble, or to tell I when you be very happy: that be our compact, Arthur, last year, before we part. be you very happy now, or be you in trouble--which be it?" and she look at he with an arch glance of kindness. "do you like go into Parliament! do you intend to distinguish yourself there? how I shall tremble for your first speech!"
"do you know about the Parliament plan, then?" Pen ask.
"Know?--all the world know! I have hear it talk about many time. Lady Rockminster's doctor talk about it to-day. I daresay it will be in the Chatteris paper to-morrow. it be all over the county that Sir Francis Clavering, of Clavering, be go to retire, in behalf of Mr. Arthur Pendennis, of Fairoaks; and that the young and beautiful Miss Blanche Amory is----"
"what! that too?" ask Pendennis.
"that, too, dear Arthur. Tout se sait, as somebody would say, whom I intend to be very fond of; and who I be sure be very clever and pretty. I have have a letter from Blanche. the kind of letter. she speak so warmly of you, Arthur! I hope--I know she feel what she writes.--When be it to be, Arthur? why do you not tell I? I may come and live with you then, maynot I?"
"my home be your, dear Laura, and everything I have," Pen say. "if I do not tell you, it be because--because--I do not know: nothing be decide as yet. no word have pass between we. but you think Blanche could be happy with I--don't you? not a romantic fondness, you know. I have no heart, I think; Ihave tell she so: only a sober-sided attachment: --and want my wife on one side of the fire and my sister on the other,-- Parliament in the session and Fairoaks in the holiday, and my Laura never to leave I until somebody who have a right come to take she away."
somebody who have a right--somebody with a right! why do Pen, as he look at the girl and slowly utter the word, begin to feel angry and jealous of the invisible somebody with the right to take she away? anxious, but a minute ago, how she would take the news regard his probable arrangement with Blanche, Pen be hurt somehow that she receive the intelligence so easily, and take his happiness for grant.
"until somebody come," Laura say, with a laugh, "I will stay at home and be aunt Laura, and take care of the child when Blanche be in the world. I have arrange it all. I be an excellent housekeeper. do you know I have be to market at Paris with Mrs. Beck, and have take some lesson from M. Grandjean? and I have have some lesson in Paris in sing too, with the money which you send I, you kind boy: and I can sing much well now: and I have learn to dance, though not so well as Blanche; and when you become a minister of state, Blanche shall present I:" and with this, and with a provoking good-humour, she perform for he the last parisian curtsey.
Lady Rockminster come in whilst this curtsey be be perform, and give to Arthur one finger to shake; which he take, and over which he bow as well as he could, which, in truth, be very clumsily.
"so you be go to be marry, sir," say the old lady.
"scold he, Lady Rockminster, for not tell we," Laura say, go away: which, in truth, the old lady begin instantly to do. "so you be go to marry, and to go into Parliament in place of that good-for-nothing Sir Francis Clavering. I want he to give my grandson his seat--why do he not give my grandson his seat? I hope you be to have a great deal of money with Miss Amory. I wouldnot take she without a great deal."
"Sir Francis Clavering be tired of Parliament," Pen say, wince, "and-- and I rather wish to attempt that career. the rest of the story be at least premature."
"I wonder, when you have Laura at home, you could take up with such an affected little creature as that," the old lady continue.
"I be very sorry Miss Amory do not please your ladyship," say Pen, smile.
"you mean--that it be no affair of mine, and that I be not go to marry she. well, Ibe not, and Ibe very glad I be not--a little odious thing-- when I think that a man could prefer she to my Laura, Ihave no patience with he, and so I tell you, Mr. Arthur Pendennis."
"I be very glad you see Laura with such favourable eye," Pen say.
"you be very glad, and you be very sorry. what do it matter, sir, whether you be very glad or very sorry? a young man who prefer Miss Amory to Miss Bell have no business to be sorry or glad. a young man who take up with such a crooked lump of affectation as that little Amory,-- for she be crook, I tell you she is,--after see my Laura, have no right to hold up his head again. where be your friend Bluebeard? the tall young man, I mean,--Warrington, benot his name? why do he not come down, and marry Laura? what do the young man mean by not marry such a girl as that? they all marry for money now. you be all selfish and coward. we run away with each other, and make foolish match in my time. I have no patience with the young man! when I be at Paris in the winter, I ask all the three attache at the Embassy why they do not fall in love with Miss Bell? they laugh--they say they want money. you be all selfish--you be all coward."
"I hope before you offer Miss Bell to the attache," say Pen, with some heat, "you do she the favour to consult she?"
"Miss Bell have only a little money. Miss Bell must marry soon. somebody must make a match for she, sir; and a girl cannot offer herself," say the old dowager, with great state. "Laura, my dear, Ihave be tell your cousin that all the young man be selfish; and that there be not a pennyworth of romance leave among they. he be as bad as the rest."
"have you be ask Arthur why he willnot marry I?" say Laura, with a kindling smile, come back and take her cousin's hand. (she have be away, perhaps, to hide some trace of emotion, which she do not wish other to see.) "he be go to marry somebody else; and I intend to be very fond of she, and to go and live with they, provide he then do not ask every bachelor who come to his house, why he do not marry I?"
the terror of Pen's conscience be thus appease, and his examination before Laura over without any reproach on the part of the latter, Pen begin to find that his duty and inclination lead he constantly to Baymouth, where Lady Rockminster inform he that a place be always reserve for he at her table. "and I recommend you to come often," the old lady say, "for Grandjean be an excellent cook, and to be with Laura and I will do your manner good. it be easy to see that you be always think about yourself. donot blush and stammer--almost all young man be always think about themselves. my son and grandson always be until I cure they. come here, and let we teach you to behave properly; you will not have to carve, that be do at the side-table. Hecker will give you as much wine as be good for you; and on day when you be very good and amusing you shall have some champagne. Hecker, mind what I say. Mr. Pendennis be Miss Laura's brother; and you will make he comfortable, and see that he do not have too much wine, or disturb I whilst I be take my nap after dinner. you be selfish: I intend to cure you of be selfish. you will dine here when you have no other engagement; and if it rain you have well put up at the hotel." as long as the good lady could order everybody round about she, she be not hard to please; and all the slave and subject of her little dowager court tremble before she, but love she.
she do not receive a very numerous or brilliant society. the doctor, of course, be admit as a constant and faithful visitor; the vicar and his curate; and on public day the vicar's wife and daughter, and some of the season visitor at Baymouth, be receive at the old lady's entertainment: but generally the company be a small one, and Mr. Arthur drink his wine by himself, when Lady Rockminster retire to take her doze, and to be play and sing to sleep by Laura after dinner.
"if my music can give she a nap," say the good-natured girl, "ought I not to be very glad that it can do so much good? Lady Rockminster sleep very little of night: and I use to read to she until I fall ill at Paris, since when she will not hear of my sit up."
"why do you not write to I when you be ill?" ask Pen, with a blush.
"what good could you do I? I have Martha to nurse I and the doctor every day. you be too busy to write to woman or to think about they. you have your book and your newspaper, and your politic and your railroad to occupy you. I write when I be well."
and Pen look at she, and blush again, as he remember that, during all the time of her illness, he have never write to she and have scarcely think about she.
in consequence of his relationship, Pen be free to walk and ride with his cousin constantly, and in the course of those walk and ride, could appreciate the sweet frankness of her disposition, and the truth, simplicity, and kindliness of her fair and spotless heart. in their mother's lifetime, she have never speak so openly or so cordially as now. the desire of poor Helen to make an union between her two child, have cause a reserve on Laura's part towards Pen; for which, under the altered circumstance of Arthur's life, there be now no necessity. he be engage to another woman; and Laura become his sister at once,-- hiding, or banish from herself, any doubt which she might have as to his choice; strive to look cheerfully forward, and hope for his prosperity; promise herself to do all that affection might do to make her mother's darling happy.
their talk be often about the depart mother. and it be from a thousand story which Laura tell he that Arthur be make aware how constant and absorb that silent maternal devotion have be; which have accompany he present and absent through life, and have only end with the fond widow's last breath. one day the people in Clavering see a lad in charge of a couple of horse at the churchyard-gate: and it be tell over the place that Pen and Laura have visit Helen's grave together. since Arthur have come down into the country, he have be there once or twice: but the sight of the sacred stone have bring no consolation to he. a guilty man do a guilty deed: a mere speculator, content to lay down his faith and honour for a fortune and a worldly career; and own that his life be but a contemptible surrender--what right have he in the holy place? what boot it to he in the world he live in, that other be no well than himself? Arthur and Laura ride by the gate of Fairoaks; and he shake hand with his tenant's child, play on the lawn and the terrace--Laura look steadily at the cottage wall, at the creeper on the porch and the magnolia grow up to her window. "Mr. Pendennis ride by to-day," one of the boy tell his mother, "with a lady, and he stop and talk to we, and he ask for a bit of honeysuckle off the porch, and give it the lady. I couldnot see if she be pretty; she have her veil down. she be rid one of Cramp's horse, out of Baymouth." as they ride over the down between home and Baymouth, Pen do not speak much, though they ride very close together. he be think what a mockery life be, and how man refuse happiness when they may have it; or, have it, kick it down; or barter it, with their eye open, for a little worthless money or beggarly honour. and then the thought come, what do it matter for the little space? the life of the good and purest of we be consume in a vain desire, and end in a disappointment: as the dear soul's who sleep in her grave yonder. she have her selfish ambition, as much as Caesar have; and die, baulk of her life's longing. the stone cover over our hope and our memory. our place know we not. "other people's child be play on the grass," he break out, in a hard voice, "where you and I use to play, Laura. and you see how the magnolia we plant have grow up since our time. I have be round to one or two of the cottage where my mother use to visit. it be scarcely more than a year that she be go, and the people whom she use to benefit care no more for her death than for Queen Anne's. we be all selfish: the world be selfish: there be but a few exception, like you, my dear, to shine like good deed in a naughty world, and make the blackness more dismal."
"I wish you would not speak in that way, Arthur," say Laura, look down and bend her head to the honeysuckle on her breast. "when you tell the little boy to give I this, you be not selfish."
"a pretty sacrifice I make to get it for you!" say the sneerer.
"but your heart be kind and full of love when you do so. one cannot ask for more than love and kindness; and if you think humbly of yourself Arthur, the love and kindness be--diminish--be they? I often think our dear mother spoil you at home, by worship you; and that if you be--I hate the word--what you say, her too great fondness help to make you so. and as for the world, when man go out into it, I suppose they cannot be otherwise than selfish. you have to fight for yourself, and to get on for yourself, and to make a name for yourself. Mamma and your uncle both encourage you in this ambition. if it be a vain thing, why pursue it? I suppose such a clever man as you intend to do a great deal of good to the country, by go into Parliament, or you would not wish to be there. what be you go to do when you be in the House of Commons?"
"woman donot understand about politic, my dear," Pen say sneer at himself as he speak.
"but why donot you make we understand? I could never tell about Mr. Pynsent why he should like to be there so much. he be not a clever man----"
"he certainly be not a genius, Pynsent," say Pen.
"Lady Diana say that he attend Committees all day; that then again he be at the House all night; that he always vote as he be tell; that he never speak; that he will never get on beyond a subordinate place; and as his grandmother tell he, he be choke with red-tape. be you go to follow the same career; Arthur? what be there in it so brilliant that you should be so eager for it? I would rather that you should stop at home, and write book--good book, kind book, with gentle kind thought, such as you have, dear Arthur, and such as might do people good to read. and if you do not win fame, what then? you own it be vanity, and you can live very happily without it. I must not pretend to advise; but I take you at your own word about the world; and as you own it be wicked, and that it tire you, ask you why you donot leave it?"
"and what would you have I do?" ask Arthur.
"I would have you bring your wife to Fairoaks to live there, and study, and do good round about you. I would like to see your own child play on the lawn, Arthur, and that we might pray in our mother's church again once more, dear brother. if the world be a temptation, be we not tell to pray that we may not be lead into it?"
"do you think Blanche would make a good wife for a petty country gentleman? do you think I should become the character very well, Laura?" Pen ask. "remember temptation walk about the hedgerow as well as the city street: and idleness be the great tempter of all."
"what do--do Mr. Warrington say?" say Laura, as a blush mount up to her cheek, and of which Pen see the fervour, though Laura's veil fall over her face to hide it.
Pen ride on by Laura's side silently for a while. George's name so mention bring back the past to he, and the thought which he have once have regard George and Laura. why should the recurrence of the thought agitate he, now that he know the union be impossible? why should he be curious to know if, during the month of their intimacy, Laura have feel a regard for Warrington? from that day until the present time George have never allude to his story, and Arthur remember now that since then George have scarcely ever mention Laura's name.
at last he cane close to she. "tell I something, Laura," he say.
she put back her veil and look at he. "what be it, Arthur?" she ask --though from the tremor of her voice she guess very well.
"tell I--but for George's misfortune--I never know he speak of it before or since that day--would you--would you have give he--what you refuse I?"
"yes, Pen," she say, burst into tear,
"he deserve you well than I do," poor Arthur groan forth, with an indescribable pang at his heart. "I be but a selfish wretch, and George be well, nobler, truer, than I be. God bless he!"
"yes, Pen," say Laura, reach out her hand to her cousin, and he put his arm round she, and for a moment she sob on his shoulder.
the gentle girl have have her secret, and tell it. in the widow's last journey from Fairoaks, when hasten with her mother to Arthur's sick-bed, Laura have make a different confession; and it be only when Warrington tell his own story, and describe the hopeless condition of his life, that she discover how much her feeling have change, and with what tender sympathy, with what great respect, delight, and admiration she have grow to regard her cousin's friend. until she know that some plan she might have dream of be impossible, and that Warrington, read in her heart, perhaps, have tell his melancholy story to warn she, she have not ask herself whether it be possible that her affection could change; and have be shock and sear by the discovery of the truth. how should she have tell it to Helen, and confess her shame? poor Laura feel guilty before her friend, with the secret which she dare not confide to she; feel as if she have be ungrateful for Helen's love and regard; feel as if she have be wickedly faithless to Pen in withdraw that love from he which he do not even care to accept; humble even and repentant before Warrington, lest she should have encourage he by undue sympathy, or show the preference which she begin to feel.
the catastrophe which break up Laura's home, and the grief and anguish which she feel for her mother's death, give she little leisure for thought more selfish; and by the time she rally from that grief the minor one be also almost cure. it be but for a moment that she have indulge a hope about Warrington. her admiration and respect for he remain as strong as ever. but the tender feeling with which she know she have regard he, be school into such calmness, that it may be say to have be dead and pass away. the pang which it leave behind be one of humility and remorse. "oh, how wicked and proud I be about Arthur," she think, "how self-confident and unforgiving! I never forgave from my heart this poor girl, who be fond of he, or he for encourage her love; and I have be more guilty than she, poor, little, artless creature! I, profess to love one man, could listen to another only too eagerly; and would not pardon the change of feeling in Arthur, whilst I myself be change and unfaithful:" and so humiliating herself, and acknowledge her weakness, the poor girl seek for strength and refuge in the manner in which she have be accustom to look for they.
she have do no wrong: but there be some folk who suffer for a fault ever so trifle as much as other whose stout conscience can walk under crime of almost any weight; and poor Laura choose to fancy that she have act in this delicate juncture of her life as a very great criminal. she determine that she have do Pen a great injury by withdraw that love which, privately in her mother's hearing, she have bestow upon he; that she have be ungrateful to her dead benefactress by ever allow herself to think of another or of violate her promise; and that, consider her own enormous crime, she ought to be very gentle in judge those of other, whose temptation be much great, very likely, and whose motif she could not understand.
a year back Laura would have be indignant at the idea that Arthur should marry Blanche: and her high spirit would have rise, as she think that from worldly motif he should stoop to one so unworthy. now when the news be bring to she of such a chance (the intelligence be give to she by old Lady Rockminster, whose speech be as direct and rapid as a slap on the face), the humble girl wince a little at the blow, but bear it meekly, and with a desperate acquiescence. "he have a right to marry, he know a great deal more of the world than I do," she argue with herself. "Blanche may not be so light-minded as she seem, and who be I to be her judge? I daresay it be very good that Arthur should go into Parliament and distinguish himself, and my duty be to do everything that lie in my power to aid he and Blanche, and to make his home happy. I daresay I shall live with they. if I be godmother to one of their child, I will leave she my three thousand pound!" and forthwith she begin to think what she could give Blanche out of her small treasure, and how good to conciliate her affection. she write she forthwith a kind letter, in which, of course, no mention be make of the plan in contemplation, but in which Laura recall old time, and speak her goodwill, and in reply to this she receive an eager answer from Blanche: in which not a word about marriage be say, to be sure, but Mr. Pendennis be mention two or three time in the letter, and they be to be henceforth, dear Laura, and dear Blanche, and love sister, and so forth.
when Pen and Laura reach home, after Laura's confession (Pen's noble acknowledgment of his own inferiority and generous expression of love for Warrington, cause the girl's heart to throb, and render doubly keen those tear which she sob on his shoulder), a little slim letter be await Miss Bell in the hall, which she tremble rather guiltily as she unseal, and which Pen blush as he recognise: for he see instantly that it be from Blanche.
Laura open it hastily, and cast her eye quickly over it, as Pen keep his fix on she, blushing.
"she date from London," Laura say. "she have be with old Bonner, Lady Clavering's maid. Bonner be go to marry Lightfoot the butler. where do you think Blanche have be?" she cry out eagerly.
"to Paris, to Scotland, to the casino?"
"to Shepherd's Inn, to see Fanny; but Fanny benot there, and Blanche be go to leave a present for she. benot it kind of she and thoughtful?" and she hand the letter to Pen, who read--
"'I see Madame Mere, who be scrub the room, and look at I with very scrubby look; but la belle Fanny be not au logis; and as I hear that she be in Captain Strong's apartment, Bonner and I mount au troisieme to see this famous beauty. another disappointment--only the Chevalier Strong and a friend of his in the room: so we come away after all without see the enchanting Fanny.
"'Je t'envoie mille et mille baiser. when will that horrid canvassing be over? sleeve be worn, etc. etc. etc.'"
after dinner the doctor be read the Times. "a young gentleman I attend when he be here some eight or nine year ago, have come into a fine fortune," the doctor say. "I see here announce the death of John Henry Foker, Esq., of Logwood Hall, at Pau, in the Pyrenees, on the 15th ult."
CHAPTER LXVIII
in which the Major be bid to stand and deliver
any gentleman who have frequent the Wheel of Fortune public-house, where it may be remember that Mr. James Morgan's Club be hold, and where Sir Francis Clavering have an interview with Major Pendennis, be aware that there be three room for guest upon the ground floor, besides the bar where the landlady sit. one be a parlour frequent by the public at large; to another room gentleman in livery resort; and the third apartment, on the door of which "private" be paint, be that hire by the Club of "the Confidentials," of which Messrs Morgan and Lightfoot be member.
the noiseless Morgan have listen to the conversation between Strong and Major Pendennis at the latter's own lodging, and have carry away from it matter for much private speculation; and a desire of knowledge have lead he to follow his master when the Major come to the Wheel of Fortune, and to take his place quietly in the confidential room, whilst Pendennis and Clavering have their discourse in the parlour. there be a particular corner in the confidential room from which you could hear almost all that pass in the next apartment; and as the conversation between the two gentleman there be rather angry, and carry on in a high key, Morgan have the benefit of overhear almost the whole of it and what he hear, strengthen the conclusion which his mind have previously form.
"he know Altamont at once, do he, when he see he in Sydney? clavering benot no more married to my Lady than I be! Altamont's the man: Altamont's a convict; young Harthur come into Parlyment, and the Gov'nor promise not to split. by Jove, what a sly old rogue it be, that old Gov'nor! no wonder hebe anxious to make the match between Blanche and Harthur: why, shewill have a hundred thousand if shebe a penny, and bring her man a seat in Parlyment into the bargain." nobody see, but a physiognomist would have like to behold, the expression of Mr. Morgan's countenance, when this astounding intelligence be make clear to he. "but for my hage, and the confound preudice of society," he say, survey himself in the glass, "dammy, James Morgan, you might marry she yourself." but if he could not marry Miss Blanche and her fortune, Morgan think he could mend his own by the possession of this information, and that it might be productive of benefit to he from very many source. of all the person whom the secret affect, the great number would not like to have it know. for instance, Sir Francis Clavering, whose fortune it involve, would wish to keep it quiet; Colonel Altamont, whose neck it implicate, would naturally be desirous to hush it: and that young hupstart beast, Mr. Harthur, who be for get' into Parlyment on the strenth of it, and be as proud as if he be a duke with half a millium a year (such, we grieve to say, be Morgan's opinion of his employer's nephew), would pay anythink soon than let the world know that he be married to a convick's daughter, and have get his seat in Parlyment by traffic with this secret. as for Lady C., Morgan think, if shebe tired of Clavering, and want to get rid of he, shewill pay: if shebe frightened about her son, and fond of the little beggar, shewill pay all the same: and Miss Blanche will certainly come down handsome to the man who will put she into her right, which she be unjustly defraud of they, and no mistake. "Dammy," conclude the valet, reflect upon this wonderful hand which luck have give he to play, "with such card as these, James Morgan, you be a make man. it may be a reg'lar enewity to I. every one of them must susscribe. and with what Ihave make already, I may cut business, give my old Gov'nor warning, turn gentleman, and have a servant of my own, begad." entertain himself with calculation such as these, that be not a little likely to perturb a man's spirit, Mr. Morgan show a very great degree of self-command by appear and be calm, and by not allow his future prospect in any way to interfere with his present duty.
one of the person whom the story chiefly concern, Colonel Altamont, be absent from London when Morgan be thus make acquainted with his history. the valet know of Sir Francis Clavering's Shepherd's inn haunt, and walk thither an hour or two after the Baronet and Pendennis have have their conversation together. but that bird be fly; Colonel Altamont have receive his Derby winning, and be go to the continent. the fact of his absence be exceedingly vexatious to Mr. Morgan. "hewill drop all that money at the gambling-shop on the rhind," think Morgan, "and I might have have a good bit of it. itbe confound annoying to think hebe go and couldnot have wait a few day long." Hope, triumphant or deferred, ambition or disappointment, victory or patient ambush, Morgan bear all alike, with similar equable countenance. until the proper day come, the Major's boot be varnish and his hair be curl, his early cup of tea be bring to his bedside, his oath, rebuke, and senile satire bear, with silent, obsequious fidelity. who would think, to see he wait upon his master, pack and shoulder his trunk, and occasionally assist at table, at the country-house where he might be stay, that Morgan be rich than his employer, and know his secret and other people's? in the profession Mr. Morgan be greatly respect and admire, and his reputation for wealth and wisdom get he much renown at most supper-table: the young gentleman vote he stoopid, a feller of no idear, and a fogey, in a word: but not one of they would not say amen to the heartfelt prayer which some of the most serious-minded among the gentleman utter, "when I die may I cut up as well as Morgan Pendennis!"
as become a man of fashion, Major Pendennis spend the autumn pass from house to house of such country friend as be at home to receive he; and if the Duke happen to be abroad, the Marquis in Scotland, condescend to sojourn with Sir John or the plain squire. to say the truth, the old gentleman's reputation be somewhat on the wane: many of the man of his time have die out, and the occupant of their hall and the present wearer of their title know not Major Pendennis: and little care for his tradition of "the wild Prince and Poins," and of the hero of fashion pass away. it must have strike the good man with melancholy as he walk by many a London door, to think how seldom it be now open for he, and how often he use to knock at it--to what banquet and welcome he use to pass through it--a score of year back. he begin to own that he be no long of the present age, and dimly to apprehend that the young man laugh at he. such melancholy musing must come across many a Pall Mall philosopher. the man, think he, be not such as they use to be in his time: the old grand manner and courtly grace of life be go: what be Castlewood House and the present Castlewood, compare to the magnificence of the old mansion and owner? the late lord come to London with four postchaise and sixteen horse: all the North Road hurry out to look at his cavalcade: the people in London street even stop as his procession pass they. the present lord travel with five bagman in a railway carriage, and sneak away from the station, smoke a cigar in a brougham. the late lord in autumn fill Castlewood with company, who drink claret till midnight: the present man bury himself in a hut on a Scotch mountain, and pass November in two or three closet in an entresol at Paris, where his amusement be a dinner at a cafe and a box at a little theatre. what a contrast there be between his Lady Lorraine, the Regent's Lady Lorraine, and her little ladyship of the present era! he figure to himself the first, beautiful, gorgeous, magnificent in diamond and velvet, dare in rouge, the wit of the world (the old wit, the old polished gentleman--not the canaille of to-day with their language of the cabstand, and their coat smell of smoke) bow at her foot; and then think of to-day's Lady Lorraine--a little woman in a black silk gown, like a governess, who talk astronomy, and labour class, and emigration, and the deuce know what, and lurk to church at eight o'clock in the morning. Abbots-Lorraine, that use to be the noble house in the county, be turn into a monastery--a regular La Trappe. they donot drink two glass of wine after dinner, and every other man at table be a country curate, with a white neckcloth, whose talk be about Polly Higson's progress at school, or widow Watkins's lumbago. "and the other young man, those lounge guardsman and great lazy dandies-- sprawl over sofas and billiard-table, and steal off to smoke pipe in each other's bedroom, care for nothing, reverence nothing, not even an old gentleman who have know their father and their better, not even a pretty woman--what a difference there be between these man, who poison the very turnip and stubble-field with their tobacco, and the gentleman of our time!" think the Major; "the breed be go--therebe no use for them; theybe replace by a parcel of damned cotton--spinner and utilitarian, and young sprig of parson with their hair comb down their bark. Ibe get old: theybe get past I: they laugh at we old boy," think old Pendennis. and he be not far wrong; the time and manner which he admire be pretty nearly go--the gay young man "larked" he irreverently, whilst the serious youth have a grave pity and wonder at he; which would have be even more painful to bear, have the old gentleman be aware of its extent. but he be rather simple: his examination of moral question have never be very deep; it have never strike he perhaps, until very lately, that he be otherwise than a most respectable and rather fortunate man. be there no old age but his without reverence? do youthful folly never jeer at other bald pate? for the past two or three year, he have begin to perceive that his day be well-nigh over, and that the man of the new time have begin to reign.
after a rather unsuccessful autumn season, then, during which he be faithfully follow by Mr. Morgan, his nephew Arthur be engage, as we have see, at Clavering, it happen that Major Pendennis come back for a while to London, at the dismal end of October, when the fog and the lawyer come to town. who have not look with interest at those load cab, pile box, and crowded child, rattle through the street on the dun October evening; stop at the dark house, where they discharge nurse and infant, girl, matron and father, whose holiday be over? yesterday it be France and sunshine, or Broadstairs and liberty; to-day come work and a yellow fog; and, ye god! what a heap of bill there lie in Master's study! and the clerk have bring the lawyer's paper from Chambers; and in half an hour the literary man know that the printer's boy will be in the passage; and Mr. Smith with that little account (that particular little account) have call presentient of your arrival, and have leave word that he will call to-morrow morning at ten. who amongst we have not say Good-bye to his holiday; return to dun London, and his fate; survey his labour and liability lay out before he, and be aware of that inevitable little account to settle? Smith and his little account in the morning, symbolise duty, difficulty, struggle, which you will meet, let we hope, friend, with a manly and honest heart.--and you think of he, as the child be slumber once more in their own bed, and the watchful housewife tenderly pretend to sleep.
Old Pendennis have no special labour or bill to encounter on the morrow, as he have no affection at home to soothe he. he have always money in his desk sufficient for his want; and be by nature and habit tolerably indifferent to the want of other people, these latter be not likely to disturb he. but a gentleman may be out of temper though he do not owe a shilling and though he may be ever so selfish, he must occasionally feel dispirited and lonely. he have have two or three twinge of gout in the country-house where he have be stay: the bird be wild and shy, and the walking over the plough field have fatigue he deucedly: the young man have laugh at he, and he have be peevish at table once or twice: he have not be able to get his whist of an evening: and, in fine, be glad to come away. in all his dealing with Morgan, his valet, he have be exceedingly sulky and discontent. he have swear at he and abuse he for many day past. he have scald his mouth with bad soup at Swindon. he have leave his umbrella in the railroad carriage: at which piece of forgetfulness, he be in such a rage, that he curse Morgan more freely than ever. both, the chimney smoke furiously in his lodging; and when he cause the window to be fling open, he swear so acrimoniously, that Morgan be inclined to fle he out of window too, through that open casement. the valet swear after his master, as Pendennis go down the street on his way to the Club.
bay's be not at all pleasant. the house have be new paint, and smell of varnish and turpentine, and a large streak of white paint inflict itself on the back of the old boy's fur-collar surtout. the dinner be not good: and the three most odious man in all London--old Hawkshaw, whose cough and accompaniment be fit to make any man uncomfortable; old Colonel Gripley, who seize on all the newspaper; and that irreclaimable old bore Jawkins, who would come and dine at the next table to Pendennis, and describe to he every inn-bill which he have pay in his foreign tour: each and all of these disagreeable personage and incident have contribute to make major Pendennis miserable; and the Club waiter trod on his toe as he bring he his coffee. never alone appear the Immortals. the Furies always hunt in company: they pursue Pendennis from home to the Club, and from the Club home.
whilst the Major be absent from his lodging, Morgan have be seat in the landlady's parlour, drink freely of hot brandy-and-water, and pour out on Mrs. Brixham some of the abuse which he have receive from his master upstairs. Mrs. Brixham be Mr. Morgan's slave. he be his landlady's landlord. he have buy the lease of the house which she rent; he have get her name and her son's to acceptance, and a bill of sale which make he master of the luckless widow's furniture. the young Brixham be a clerk in an insurance office, and Morgan could put he into what he call quod any day. Mrs. Brixham be a clergyman's widow, and Mr. Morgan, after perform his duty on the first floor, have a pleasure in make the old lady fetch he his bootjack and his slipper. she be his slave. the little black profile of her son and daughter; the very picture of Tiddlecot Church, where she be marry, and her poor dear Brixham live and die, be now Morgan's property, as it hang there over the mantelpiece of his back-parlour. Morgan sate in the widow's back-room, in the ex-curate's old horse-hair study-chair, make Mrs. Brixham bring supper for he, and fill his glass again and again.
the liquor be buy with the poor woman's own coin, and hence Morgan indulge in it only the more freely; and he have eat his supper and be drink a third tumbler, when old Pendennis return from the Club, and go upstairs to his room. Mr. Morgan swear very savagely at he and his bell, when he hear the latter, and finish his tumbler of brandy before he go up to answer the summon.
he receive the abuse consequent on this delay in silence, nor do the major condescend to read in the flushed face and glare eye of the man, the anger under which he be labour. the old gentleman's foot-bath be at the fire; his gown and slipper await he there. Morgan kneel down to take his boot off with due subordination: and as the Major abuse he from above, keep up a growl of malediction below at his foot. thus, when Pendennis be cry "confound you, sir, mind that strap--curse you, donot wrench my foot off," Morgan sotto voce below be express a wish to strangle he, drown he, and punch his head off.
the boot remove, it become necessary to divest Mr. Pendennis of his coat: and for this purpose the valet have necessarily to approach very near to his employer; so near that Pendennis could not but perceive what Mr. Morgan's late occupation have be; to which he advert in that simple and forcible phraseology which man be sometimes in the habit of use to their domestic; inform Morgan that he be a drunken beast, and that he smell of brandy.
at this the man break out, lose patience, and fling up all subordination, "Ibe drunk, be I? Ibe a beast, be I? Ibe d----d, be I? you infernal old miscreant. shall I wring your old head off, and drownd yer in that pail of water? do you think Ibe a-go to bear your confound old harrogance, you old Wigsby! chatter your old hivorie at I, do you, you grin old baboon! come on, if you be a man, and can stand to a man. ha! you coward, knife, knife!"
"if you advance a step, Iwill send it into you," say the Major, seize up a knife that be on the table near he. "go downstairs, you drunken brute, and leave the house; send for your book and your wage in the morning, and never let I see your insolent face again. this d----d impertinence of yours have be grow for some month past. you have be grow too rich. you be not fit for service. get out of it, and out of the house."
"and where would you wish I to go, pray, out of the 'ouse?" ask the man, "and willnot it be equal convenient to-morrow mornin'?--tootyfay mame shose, sivvaplay, munseer?"
"Silence, you beast, and go!" cry out the Major.
Morgan begin to laugh, with rather a sinister laugh. "look yere, Pendennis," he say, seat himself; "since Ihave be in this room youhave call I beast, brute, dog: and d----d I, havenot you? how do you suppose one man like that sort of talk from another? how many year have I wait on you, and how many damn and cuss have you give I, along with my wage? do you think a man's a dog, that you can talk to he in this way? if I choose to drink a little, why shouldnot I? Ihave see many a gentleman drunk form'ly, and perap have the abit from they. I benot a-go to leave this house, old feller, and shall I tell you why? the house be my house, every stick of furnitur' in it be mine, excep' your old trap, and your shower-bath, and your wigbox. Ihave buy the place, I tell you, with my own industry and perseverance. I can show a hundred pound, where you can show a fifty, or your damned supersellious nephew either. Ihave serve you honourable, do everythink for you these dozen year, and Ibe a dog, be I? Ibe a beast, be I? thatbe the language for gentleman, not for our rank. but Iwill bear it no more. I throw up your service; Ibe tired on it; Ihave comb your old wig and buckle your old girth and waistband long enough, I tell you. donot look savage at I, Ibe sit in my own chair, in my own room, a-tell the truth to you. Iwill be your beast, and your brute, and your dog, no more, Major Pendennis Alf Pay."
the fury of the old gentleman, meet by the servant's abrupt revolt, have be shock and cool by the concussion, as much as if a sudden shower-bath or a pail of cold water have be fling upon he. that effect produce, and his anger calm, Morgan's speech have interest he, and he rather respect his adversary, and his courage in face he; as of old day, in the fencing-room, he would have admire the opponent who hit he.
