"you be no long my servant," the Major say, "and the house may be yours; but the lodging be mine, and you will have the goodness to leave they. to-morrow morning, when we have settle our account, I shall remove into other quarter. in the meantime, I desire to go to bed, and have not the slight wish for your further company."
"wewill have a settlement, donot you be afraid," Morgan say, get up from his chair. "I benot do with you yet; nor with your family, nor with the Clavering family, Major Pendennis; and that you shall know."
"have the goodness to leave the room, sir--I'm tired," say the Major.
"hah! youwill be more tired of I afore youhave do," answer the man, with a sneer, and walk out of the room; leave the Major to compose himself as good he might, after the agitation of this extraordinary scene.
he sate and muse by his fireside over the past event, and the confound impudence and ingratitude of servant; and think how he should get a new man: how devilish unpleasant it be for a man of his age, and with his habit, to part with a fellow to whom he have be accustom: how Morgan have a receipt for boot-varnish, which be incomparably well and more comfortable to the foot than any he have ever try: how very well he make mutton-broth, and tend he when he be unwell. "Gad, itbe a hard thing to lose a fellow of that sort: but he must go," think the Major. "he have grow rich, and impudent since he have grow rich. he be horribly tipsy and abusive to-night. we must part, and I must go out of the lodging. Dammy, I like the lodging; Ibe use to them. itbe very unpleasant, at my time of life, to change my quarter." and so on, muse the old gentleman. the shower-bath have do he good: the testiness be go: the loss of the umbrella, the smell of paint at the Club, be forget under the superior excitement. "confound the insolent villain!" think the old gentleman. "he understand my want to a nicety: he be the good servant in England." he think about his servant as a man think of a horse that have carry he long and well, and that have come down with he, and be safe no long. how the deuce to replace he? where can he get such another animal?
in these melancholy cogitation the Major, who have don his own dressing-gown and replace his head of hair (a little grey have be introduce into the coiffure of late by Mr. Truefitt, which have give the Major's head the most artless and respectable appearance); in these cogitation, we say, the Major, who have take off his wig and put on his night-handkerchief, sate absorb by the fireside, when a feeble knock come at his door, which be presently open by the landlady of the lodging.
"God bless my soul, Mrs. Brixham!" cry out the Major, startle that a lady should behold he in the simple appareil of his night-toilet. "It-- itbe very late, Mrs. Brixham."
"I wish I might speak to you, sir," say the landlady, very piteously.
"About Morgan, I suppose? he have cool himself at the pump. cannot take he back, Mrs. Brixham. impossible. Iwould determined to part with he before, when I hear of his dealing in the discount business--I suppose youhave hear of they, Mrs. Brixham? my servantbe a capitalist, begad."
"oh, sir," say Mrs. Brixham, "I know it to my cost. I borrow from he a little money five year ago; and though I have pay he many time over, I be entirely in his power. I be ruin by he, sir. everything I have be his. hebe a dreadful man."
"eh, Mrs. Brixham? tout pis--dev'lish sorry for you, and that I must quit your house after lodge here so long: therebe no help for it. I must go."
"he say we must all go, sir," sob out the luckless widow. he come downstairs from you just now--he have be drink, and it always make he very wicked--and he say that you have insult he, sir, and treat he like a dog, and speak to he unkindly; and he swear he would be revenge, and--and I owe he a hundred and twenty pound, sir--and he have a bill of sale of all my furniture--and say he will turn I out of my house, and send my poor George to prison. he have be the ruin of my family, that man."
"Dev'lish sorry, Mrs. Brixham; pray take a chair. what can I do?"
"could you not intercede with he for we? George will give half his allowance; my daughter can send something. if you will but stay on, sir, and pay a quarter's rent in advance----"
"my good madam, I would as soon give you a quarter in advance as not, if I be go to stay in the lodging. but I cannot; and I cannot afford to fling away twenty pound, my good madam. Ibe a poor half-pay officer, and want every shilling I have, begad. as far as a few pound go--say five pound--I donot say--and shall be most happy, and that sort of thing: and Iwill give it you in the morning with pleasure: but--but itbe get late, and I have make a railroad journey."
"God's will be do, sir," say the poor woman, dry her tear. I must bear my fate."
"and a dev'lish hard one it be, and most sincerely I pity you, Mrs. Brixham. I--i'll say ten pound, if you will permit I. good night."
"Mr. Morgan, sir, when he come downstairs, and when--when I besought he to have pity on I, and tell he he have be the ruin of my family, say something which I do not well understand--that he would ruin every family in the house--that he know something would bring you down too--and that you should pay he for your--your insolence to he. I--I must own to you, that I go down on my knee to he, sir; and he say, with a dreadful oath against you, that he would have you on your knee."
"Me?--by Gad, that be too pleasant! where be the confounded fellow?"
"he go away, sir. he say he should see you in the morning. oh, pray try and pacify he, and save I and my poor boy." and the widow go away with this prayer, to pass her night as she might, and look for the dreadful morrow.
the last word about himself excite Major Pendennis so much, that his compassion for Mrs. Brixham's misfortune be quite forget in the consideration of his own case.
"I on my knee?" think he, as he get into bed: "confound his impudence! who ever see I on my knee? what the devil do the fellow know? Gad, Ihave not have an affair these twenty year. I defy he." and the old compaigner turn round and sleep pretty sound, be rather excited and amuse by the event of the day--the last day in Bury Street, he be determined it should be. "for itbe impossible to stay on with a valet over I, and a bankrupt landlady. what good can I do this poor devil of a woman? Iwill give she twenty pound--therebe Warrington's twenty pound, which he have just pay--but whatbe the use? shewill want more, and more, and more, and that cormorant Morgan will swallow all. no, dammy, I cannot afford to know poor people; and to-morrow Iwill say Good-bye--to Mrs. Brixham and Mr. Morgan."
CHAPTER LXIX
in which the Major neither yield his money nor his life
early next morning Pendennis's shutter be open by Morgan, who appear as usual, with a face perfectly grave and respectful, bear with he the old gentleman's clothe, can of water, and elaborate toilet requisite.
"itbe you, be it?" say the old fellow from his bed. "I shallnot take you back again, you understand."
"I ave not the least wish to be take back agin, Major Pendennis," Mr. Morgan say, with grave dignity, "nor to serve you nor hany man. but as I wish you to be comftable as long as you stay in my house, I come up to do whatbe nessary." and once more, and for the last time, Mr. James Morgan lay out the silver dressing-case, and strap the shin razor.
these office conclude, he address himself to the Major with an indescribable solemnity, and say: "thinkin' that you would most likely be in want of a respectable pusson, until you suit yourself, I speak to a young man last night, who be 'ere."
"indeed," say the warrior in the tent-bed.
"he ave live in the fust famlie, and I can wouch for his respectability."
"you be monstrous polite," grin the old Major. and the truth be, that after the occurrence of the previous evening, Morgan have go out to his own Club at the Wheel of Fortune, and there find Frosch, a courier and valet just return from a foreign tour with young Lord Cubley, and for the present disposable, have represent to Mr. Frosch, that he, Morgan, have "a devil of a blow hup with his own gov'nor, and be go to retire from the business haltogether, and that if Frosch want a tempory job, he might probbly have it by apply in Bury Street."
"you be very polite," say the Major, "and your recommendation, I be sure, will have every weight."
Morgan blush; he feel his master be 'a-chaffin' of he.' "the man have await on you before, sir," he say with great dignity. "Lord De la Pole, sir, give he to his nephew young Lord Cubley, and he have be with he on his fore tour, and not wish to go to Fitzurse Castle, which Frosch's chest be delicate, and he cannot bear the cold in Scotland, he be free to serve you or not, as you choose."
"I repeat, sir, that you be exceedingly polite," say the Major. come in, Frosch--you will do very well--Mr. Morgan, will you have the great kindness to----"
"I shall show he what be nessary, sir, and what be customry for you to wish to ave do. will you please to take breakfast 'ere or at the Club, Major Pendennis?"
"with your kind permission, I will breakfast here, and afterwards we will make our little arrangement."
"if you please, sir."
"will you now oblige I by leave the room?"
Morgan withdraw; the excessive politeness of his ex-employer make he almost as angry as the Major's bitter word. and whilst the old gentleman be make his mysterious toilet, we will also modestly retire.
after breakfast, Major Pendennis and his new aide-de-camp occupy themselves in prepare for their departure. the establishment of the old bachelor be not very complicated. he encumber himself with no useless wardrobe. a bible (his mother's), a road book, Pen's novel (calf elegant), and the Duke of Wellington's Despatches, with a few print, map, and portrait of that illustrious general, and of various sovereign and consort of this country, and of the General under whom Major Pendennis have serve in India, form his literary and artistical collection: he be always ready to march at a few hour' notice, and the case in which he have bring his property into his lodging some fifteen year before, be still in the loft amply sufficient to receive all his good. these, the young woman who do the work of the house, and who be know by the name of Betty to her mistress, and of "Slavey" to Mr. Morgan, bring down from their resting-place, and obediently dust and clean under the eye of the terrible Morgan. his demeanour be guard and solemn; he have speak no word as yet to Mrs. Brixham respect his threat of the past night, but he look as if he would execute they, and the poor widow tremblingly await her fate.
Old Pendennis, arm with his cane, superintend the package of his good and chattel, under the hand of Mr. Frosch, and the Slavey burn such of his paper as he do not care to keep; fling open door and closet until they be all empty; and now all box and chest be close, except his desk, which be ready to receive the final account of Mr. Morgan.
that individual now make his appearance, and bring his book. "as I wish to speak to you in privick, perap you will ave the kindness to request Frosch to step downstairs," he say, on enter.
"bring a couple of cab, Frosch, if you please--and wait downstairs until I ring for you," say the Major. Morgan see Frosch downstairs, watch he go along the street upon his errand, and produce his book and account, which be simple and very easily settle.
"and now, sir," say he, have pocket the cheque which his ex-employer give he, and sign his name to his book with a flourish, "and now that account be close between we, sir," he say, "I porpose to speak to you as one man to another"--(Morgan like the sound of his own voice; and, as an individual, indulge in public speaking whenever he could get an opportunity, at the Club, or the housekeeper's room)--"and I must tell you, that Ibe in possession of certe infamation."
"and may I inquire of what nature, pray?" ask the Major.
"itbe valuble information, Major Pendennis, as you know very well. I know of a marriage as be no marriage--of a honourable Baronet as be no more married than I be; and which his wife be married to somebody else, as you know too, sir."
Pendennis at once understand all. "ha! this account for your behaviour. you have be listen at the door, sir, I suppose," say the Major, look very haughty; "I forgot to look at the keyhole when I go to that public-house, or I might have suspect what sort of a person be behind it."
"I may have my scheme as you may have your, I suppose," answer Morgan. "I may get my information, and I may act on that information, and I may find that information valuble as anybody else may. a poor servant may have a bit of luck as well as a gentleman, maynot he? donot you be put on your aughty look, sir, and comin' the aristocrat over I. thatbe all gammon with I. Ibe an Englishman, I be, and as good as you."
"to what the devil do this tend, sir? and how do the secret which you have surprised concern I, I should like to know?" ask Major Pendennis, with great majesty.
"how do it concern I, indeed! how grand we be! how do it concern my nephew, I wonder? how do it concern my nephew's seat in Parlyment: and to subornation of bigamy? how do it concern that? what, be you to be the only man to have a secret, and to trade on it? why shouldnot I go half, Major Pendennis? Ihave find it out too. look here! I benot go to be unreasonable with you. make it worth my while, and Iwill keep the thing close. let Mr. Arthur take his seat, and his rich wife, if you like; I donot want to marry she. but I will have my share, as sure as my name's James Morgan. and if I don't----"
"and if you donot, sir--what?" Pendennis ask.
"if I donot, I split, and tell all. I smash Clavering, and have he and his wife up for bigamy--so help I, I will! I smash young Hopeful's marriage, and I show up you and he as makin' use of this secret, in order to squeeze a seat in Parlyment out of Sir Francis, and a fortune out of his wife."
"Mr. Pendennis know no more of this business than the babe unborn, sir," cry the Major, aghast. "no more than Lady Clavering, than Miss Amory do."
"tell that to the marine, Major," reply the valet; "that cock willnot fight with I."
"do you doubt my word, you villain?"
"no bad language. I donot care one twopence'a'p'ny whether your word's true or not. I tell you, I intend this to be a nice little annuity to I, Major: for I have every one of you; and I benot such a fool as to let you go. I should say that you might make it five hundred a year to I among you, easy. pay I down the first quarter now and Ibe as mum as a mouse. just give a note for one twenty-five. therebe your cheque-book on your desk."
"and therebe this too, you villain," cry the old gentleman. in the desk to which the valet pointed be a little double-barrel pistol, which have belong to Pendennis's old patron; the indian commander-in-chief, and which have accompany he in many a campaign. "one more word, you scoundrel and Iwill shoot you, like a mad dog. stop-- by Jove, Iwill do it now. youwill assault I, will you? youwill strike at an old man, will you, you lie coward? kneel down and say your prayer, sir, for by the Lord you shall die."
the Major's face glare with rage at his adversary, who look terrified before he for a moment, and at the next, with a shriek of "murder!" spring towards the open window, under which a policeman happen to be on his beat. "murder! Police!" bellow Mr. Morgan.
to his surprise, Major Pendennis wheel away the table and walk to the other window, which be also open. he beckon the policeman. "come up. here, policeman," he say, and then go and place himself against the door.
"you miserable sneak," he say to Morgan; "the pistol havenot be load these fifteen year, as you would have know very well, if you have not be such a coward. that policeman be come, and I will have he up, and have your trunk search; I have reason to believe that you be a thief, sir. I know you be. Iwill swear to the thing."
"you give them to I--you give them to I!" cry Morgan.
the Major laugh. "wewill see," he say; and the guilty valet remember some fine lawn-front shirt--a certain gold-head cane--an opera-glass, which he have forget to bring down, and of which he have assume the use along with certain article of his master's clothe, which the old dandy neither wear nor ask for.
Policeman X enter; follow by the sear Mrs. Brixham and her maid-of-all-work, who have be at the door and find some difficulty in close it against the street amateur, who wish to see the row. the Major begin instantly to speak.
"I have have occasion to discharge this drunken scoundrel," he say. "both last night and this morning he insult and assault I. I be an old man and take up a pistol. you see it be not load, and this coward cry out before he be hurt. I be glad you be come. I be charge he with take my property, and desire to examine his trunk and his room."
"the velvet cloak you benot wear these three year, nor the weskit, and I think I might take the shirt, and I--I take my hoath I intend to put back the hopera-glass," roar Morgan, writhe with rage and terror.
"the man acknowledge that he be a thief," the Major say, calmly. "he have be in my service for year, and I have treat he with every kindness and confidence. we will go upstairs and examine his trunk."
in those trunk Mr. Morgan have thing which he would fain keep from public eye. Mr. Morgan, the bill-discounter, give good as well as money to his customer. he provide young spendthrift with snuff box and pin and jewel and picture and cigar, and of a very doubtful quality those cigar and jewel and picture be. their display at a police-office, the discovery of his occult profession, and the exposure of the Major's property, which he have appropriate, indeed, rather than stolen,--would not have add to the reputation of Mr. Morgan. he look a piteous image of terror and discomfiture.
"hewill smash I, will he?" think the Major. "Iwill crush he now, and finish with he."
but he pause. he look at poor Mrs. Brixham's scared face; and he think for a moment to himself that the man bring to bay and in prison might make disclosure which have well be keep secret, and that it be good not to deal too fiercely with a desperate man.
"stop," he say, "policeman. Iwill speak with this man by himself."
"do you give Mr. Morgan in charge?" say the policeman.
"I have bring no charge as yet," the Major say, with a significant look at his man.
"thank you, sir," whisper Morgan, very low.
"go outside the door, and wait there, policeman, if you please.--now, Morgan, you have play one game with I, and you have not have the good of it, my good man. no, begad, youhave not have the good of it, though you have the good hand; and youhave get to pay, too, now, you scoundrel."
"yes, sir," say the man.
"Ihave only find out, within the last week, the game which you have be drive, you villain. Young De Boots, of the Blues, recognise you as the man who come to barrack, and do business one-third in money, one-third in eau-de-Cologne, and one-third in french print, you confound demure old sinner! I donot miss anything, or care a straw what youhave take, you booby; but I take the shot, and it hit--hit the bull's-eye, begad. Dammy, six, Ibe an old campaigner."
"what do you want with I, sir?"
"Iwill tell you. your bill, I suppose, you keep about you in that dem'd great leather pocket-book, donot you? youwill burn Mrs. Brixham's bill?"
"Sir, I benot a-go to part with my property," growl the man.
"you lend she sixty pound five year ago. she and that poor devil of an insurance clerk, her son, have pay you fifty pound a year ever since; and you have get a bill of sale of her furniture, and her note of hand for a hundred and fifty pound. she tell I so last night. by Jove, sir, youhave bleed that poor woman enough."
"I willnot give it up," say Morgan; "if I do i'm----"
"Policeman!" cry the Major.
"you shall have the bill," say Morgan. "yoube not go to take money of I, and you a gentleman?"
"I shall want you directly," say the Major to x, who here enter, and who again withdraw.
"no, my good sir," the old gentleman continue; "I have not any desire to have far pecuniary transaction with you; but we will draw out a little paper, which you will have the kindness to sign. no, stop!--you shall write it: you have improve immensely in writing of late, and have now a very good hand. you shall sit down and write, if you please--there, at that table--so--let I see--we may as well have the date. write 'Bury Street, St. James's, October 21, 18--.'"
and Mr. Morgan write as he be instruct, and as the pitiless old Major continued:--
"'i, James Morgan, have come in extreme poverty into the service of Arthur Pendennis, Esquire, of Bury Street, St. James's, a Major in her Majesty's service, acknowledge that I receive liberal wage and board wage from my employer, during fifteen year.'--you cannot object to that, I be sure," say the Major.
"during fifteen year," write Morgan.
"'in which time, by my own care and prudence,'" the dictator resume, "'I have manage to amass sufficient money to purchase the house in which my master reside, and, besides, to effect other saving. amongst other person from whom I have have money, I may mention my present tenant, Mrs. Brixham, who, in consideration of sixty pound advance by I five year since, have pay back to I the sum of two hundred and fifty pound sterling, besides give I a note of hand for one hundred and twenty pound, which I restore to she at the desire of my late master, Major Arthur Pendennis, and therewith free her furniture, of which I have a bill of sale.'--have you write?"
"I think if this pistol be load, Iwould blow your brain out," say Morgan.
"no, you wouldnot. you have too great a respect for your valuable life, my good man," the Major answer. "let we go on and begin a new sentence.
"'and have, in return for my master's kindness, steal his property from he, which I acknowledge to be now upstairs in my trunk; and have utter falsehood regard his and other honourable family, I do hereby, in consideration of his clemency to I, express my regret for utter these falsehood, and for steal his property; and declare that I be not worthy of belief, and that I hope'--yes, begad--'that I hope to amend for the future. sign, James Morgan.'"
"Ibe d----d if I sign it," say Morgan.
"my good man, it will happen to you, whether you sign or no, begad," say the old fellow, chuckle at his own wit "there, I shall not use this, you understand, unless--unless I be compel to do so. Mrs. Brixham, and our friend the policeman, will witness it, I dare say, without read it: and I will give the old lady back her note of hand, and say, which you will confirm, that she and you be quit. I see there be Frosch come back with the cab for my trunk; I shall go to an hotel.--you may come in now, policeman; Mr. Morgan and I have arrange our little dispute. if Mrs. Brixham will sign this paper, and you, policeman, will do so, I shall be very much oblige to you both. Mrs. Brixham, you and your worthy landlord, Mr. Morgan, be quit. I wish you joy of he. let Frosch come and pack the rest of the thing."
Frosch, aid by the Slavey, under the calm superintendence of Mr. Morgan, carry Major Pendennis's box to the cab in waiting; and Mrs. Brixham, when her persecutor be not by, come and ask a Heaven's blessing upon the Major, her preserver, and the good and quiet and kind of lodger. and have give she a finger to shake, which the humble lady receive with a curtsey, and over which she be ready to make a speech full of tear, the Major cut short that valedictory oration, and walk out of the house to the hotel in Jermyn Street, which be not many step from Morgan's door.
that individual, look forth from the parlour-window, discharge anything but blessing at his parting guest; but the stout old boy could afford not to be frighten at Mr. Morgan, and fling he a look of great contempt and humour as he strut away with his cane.
Major Pendennis have not quit his house of Bury Street many hour, and Mr. Morgan be enjoy his otium in a dignified manner, survey the evening fog, and smoke a cigar, on the door-step, when Arthur Pendennis, Esq., the hero of this history, make his appearance at the well-know door.
"my uncle out, I suppose, Morgan?" he say to the functionary; know full well that to smoke be treason, in the presence of the Major.
"Major Pendennis be hout, sir," say Morgan, with gravity, bow, but not touch the elegant cap which he wear. "Major Pendennis have leave this ouse to-day, sir, and I have no long the honour of be in his service, sir."
"indeed, and where be he?"
"I believe he ave take tempory lodging at Cox's otel, in Jummin Street," say Mr. Morgan; and add, after a pause, "be you in town for some time, pray, sir? be you in Chambers? I should like to have the honour of wait on you there: and would be thankful if you would favour I with a quarter of an hour."
"do you want my uncle to take you back?" ask Arthur, insolent and good-natured.
"I want no such thing; Iwould see him----" the man glare at he for a minute, but he stop. "no, sir, thank you," he say in a soft voice; "itbe only with you that I wish to speak, on some business which concern you; and perhaps you would favour I by walk into my house."
"if it be but for a minute or two, I will listen to you, Morgan," say Arthur; and think to himself, "I suppose the fellow want I to patronise he;" and he enter the house. a card be already in the front window, proclaim that apartment be to be let; and have introduce Mr. Pendennis into the dining-room, and offer he a chair, Mr. Morgan take one himself, and proceed to convey some information to he, of which the reader have already have cognisance.
CHAPTER LXX
in which Pendennis count his Eggs
our friend have arrive in London on that day only, though but for a brief visit; and have leave some fellow-traveller at an hotel to which he have convoy they from the West, he hasten to the Chambers in Lamb Court, which be bask in as much sun as choose to visit that dreary but not altogether comfortless building. freedom stand in lieu of sunshine in chamber; and Templars grumble, but take their ease in their Inn. Pen's domestic announce to he that Warrington be in Chambers too, and, of course, Arthur run up to his friend's room straightway, and find it, as of old, perfume with the pipe, and George once more at work with his newspaper and review. the pair greet each other with the rough cordiality which young englishman use one to another: and which carry a great deal of warmth and kindness under its rude exterior. Warrington smile and take his pipe out of his mouth, and say, "well, young one!" Pen advance and hold out his hand, and say, "how be you, old boy?" and so this greeting pass between two friend who have not see each other for month. Alphonse and Frederic would have rush into each other's arm and shriek Ce bon coeur! ce cher Alphonse! over each other's shoulder. Max and Wilhelm would have bestow half a dozen kiss, scent with Havannah, upon each other's mustachio. "well, young one!" "how be you, old boy?" be what two briton say: after save each other's life, possibly, the day before. to-morrow they will leave off shake hand, and only wag their head at one another as they come to breakfast. each have for the other the very warm confidence and regard: each would share his purse with the other: and hear he attack would break out in the loud and most enthusiastic praise of his friend; but they part with a mere good-bye, they meet with a mere how-d'you-do? and they donot write to each other in the interval. curious, modesty, strange stoical decorum of English friendship! "yes, we be not demonstrative like those confound foreigner," say Hardman: who not only show no friendship, but never feel any all his life long.
"be in Switzerland?" say Pen.
"yes," say Warrington.
"couldnot find a bit of tobacco fit to smoke till we come to Strasburg, where I get some caporal." the man's mind be full, very likely, of the great sight which he have see, of the great emotion with which the vast work of nature have inspire it. but his enthusiasm be too coy to show itself, even to his close friend, and he veil it with a cloud of tobacco. he will speak more fully of confidential evening, however, and write ardently and frankly about that which he be shy of say. the thought and experience of his travel will come forth in his writing; as the learning, which he never display in talk, enrich his style with pregnant allusion and brilliant illustration, colour his generous eloquence, and point his wit.
the elder give a rapid account of the place which he have visit in his tour. he have see Switzerland, North Italy, and the Tyrol--he have come home by Vienna, and Dresden, and the Rhine. he speak about these place in a shy sulky voice, as if he have rather not mention they at all, and as if the sight of they have render he very unhappy. the outline of the eld man's tour thus gloomily sketch out, the young one begin to speak. he have be in the country--very much bored--canvass uncommonly slow--he be here for a day or two, and go on to--to the neighbourhood of Tunbridge Wells, to some friend that will be uncommonly slow, too. how hard it be to make an Englishman acknowledge that he be happy!
"and the seat in Parliament, Pen? have you make it all right?" ask Warrington.
"all right,--as soon as Parliament meet and a new writ can be issue, clavering retire, and I step into his shoe," say Pen.
"and under which king do Bezonian speak or die?" ask Warrington. "do we come out as Liberal Conservative, or as Government man, or on our own hook?"
"Hem! there be no politic now; every man's politic, at least, be pretty much the same. I have not get acre enough to make I a protectionist; nor could I be one, I think, if I have all the land in the county. I shall go pretty much with Government, and in advance of they upon some social question which I have be get up during the vacation;--don't grin, you old cynic, I have be get up the Blue Books, and intend to come out rather strong on the Sanitary and Colonisation question."
"we reserve to ourselves the liberty of vote against Government, though we be generally friendly. we be, however, friend of the people avant tout. we give lecture at the Clavering Institute, and shake band with the intelligent mechanic. we think the franchise ought to be very considerably enlarge; at the same time we be free to accept office some day, when the House have listen to a few crack speech from we, and the Administration perceive our merit."
"I be not Moses," say Pen, with, as usual, somewhat of melancholy in his voice. "I have no law from Heaven to bring down to the people from the mountain. I donot belong to the mountain at all, or set up to be a leader and reformer of mankind. my faith be not strong enough for that; nor my vanity, nor my hypocrisy, great enough. I will tell no lie, George, that I promise you; and do no more than coincide in those which be necessary and pass current, and cannot be get in without recall the whole circulation. give a man at least the advantage of his sceptical turn. if I find a good thing to say in the House, I will say it; a good measure, I will support it; a fair place, I will take it, and be glad of my luck. but I would no more flat a great man than a mob; and now you know as much about my politic as I do. what call have I to be a Whig? Whiggism be not a divine institution. why not vote with the Liberal Conservatives? they have do for the nation what the whig would never have do without they. who convert both?--the Radicals and the country outside. I think the Morning Post be often right, and Punch be often wrong. I donot profess a call, but take advantage of a chance. parlon d'autre choose."
"the next thing at your heart, after ambition be love, I suppose?" Warrington say. "how have our young love prosper? be we go to change our condition, and give up our chamber? be you go to divorce I, Arthur, and take unto yourself a wife?"
"I suppose so. she be very good-natured and lively. she sing, and she donot mind smoking. shewill have a fair fortune--I donot know how much-- but my uncle augur everything from the Begum's generosity, and say that she will come down very handsomely. and I think Blanche be dev'lish fond of I," say Arthur, with a sigh.
"that mean that we accept her caress and her money."
"havenot we say before that life be a transaction?" Pendennis say. "I donot pretend to break my heart about she. I have tell she pretty fairly what my feeling be--and--and have engage myself to she. and since I see she last, and for the last two month especially, whilst I have be in the country, I think she have be grow fonder and fonder of I; and her letter to I, and especially to Laura, seem to show it. mine have be simple enough--no rapture, nor vow, you understand--but look upon the thing as an affaire faite; and not desirous to hasten or defer the completion."
"and Laura? how be she?" Warrington ask frankly.
"Laura, George," say Pen, look his friend hard in the face--"by heaven, Laura be the good, and noble, and dearest girl the sun ever shine upon." his own voice fall as he speak: it seem as if he could hardly utter the word: he stretch out his hand to his comrade, who take it and nod his head.
"have you only find out that now, young un?" Warrington say after a pause.
"who have not learn thing too late, George?" cry Arthur, in his impetuous way, gather word and emotion as he go on. "whose life be not a disappointment? who carry his heart entire to the grave without a mutilation? I never know anybody who be happy quite: or who have not have to ransom himself out of the hand of fate with the payment of some dear treasure or other. lucky if we be leave alone afterwards, when we have pay our fine, and if the tyrant visit we no more. suppose I have find out that I have lose the great prize in the world, now that it cannot be mine--that for year I have an angel under my tent, and let she go?--am I the only one--ah, dear old boy, be I the only one? and do you think my lot be easy to bear because I own that I deserve it? shebe go from we. God's blessing be with she! she might have stay, and I lose she; itbe like Undine: benot it, George?"
"she be in this room once," say George.
he see she there--he hear the sweet low voice--he see the sweet smile and eye shin so kindly--the face remember so fondly--think of in what night-watch--bl and love always--go now! a glass that have hold a nosegay--a bible with Helen's handwriting--be all that be leave he of that brief flower of his life. say it be a dream: say it pass: well the recollection of a dream than an aimless waking from a blank stupor.
the two friend sate in silence a while, each occupy with his own thought and aware of the other's. Pen break it presently, by say that he must go and seek for his uncle, and report progress to the old gentleman. the Major have write in a very bad humour; the Major be get old. "I should like to see you in Parliament, and snugly settle with a comfortable house and an heir to the name before I make my bow. show I these," the Major write, "and then, let old Arthur Pendennis make room for the young fellow; he have walk the Pall Mall pave long enough."
"there be a kindness about the old heathen," say Warrington. "he care for somebody besides himself, at least for some other part of himself besides that which be button into his own coat;--for you and your race. he would like to see the progeny of the Pendennises multiplying and increase, and hop that they may inherit the land. the old patriarch bless you from the Club window of Bays's, and be carry off and bury under the flag of St. James's Church, in sight of Piccadilly, and the cabstand, and the carriage go to the levee. it be an edify ending."
"the new blood I bring into the family," muse Pen, "be rather taint. if I have choose, I think my father-in-law Amory would not have be the progenitor I should have desire for my race; nor my grandfather-in-law Snell; nor our oriental ancestor. by the way, who be amory? Amory be lieutenant of an Indiaman. Blanche write some verse about he, about the storm, the mountain wave, the seaman's grave, the gallant father, and that sort of thing. Amory be drown command a country ship between Calcutta and Sydney; Amory and the Begum benot happy together. she have be unlucky in her selection of husband, the good old lady, for, between ourselves, a more despicable creature than Sir Francis Clavering, of Clavering Park, Baronet, never----" "never legislate for his country," break in Warrington; at which Pen blush rather.
"by the way, at Baden," say Warrington, "I find our friend the Chevalier Strong in great state, and wear his order. he tell I that he have quarrel with Clavering, of whom he seem to have almost as bad an opinion as you have, and in fact, I think, though I will not be certain, confide to I his opinion, that Clavering be an utter scoundrel. that fellow Bloundell, who teach you card-playing at Oxbridge, be with Strong; and time, I think, have bring out his valuable quality, and render he a more accomplished rascal than he be during your undergraduateship. but the king of the place be the famous Colonel Altamont, who be carry all before he, give fly to the whole society, and break the bank, it be say."
"my uncle know something about that fellow--Clavering know something about he. therebe something louche regard he. but come! I must go to Bury Street, like a dutiful nephew." and, take his hat, Pen prepare to go.
"I will walk, too," say Warrington. and they descend the stair, stop, however, at Pen's chamber, which, as the reader have be inform, be now on the low story.
here Pen begin sprinkle himself with eau-de-Cologne, and carefully scent his hair and whisker with that odoriferous water.
"what be the matter? youhave not be smoke. be it my pipe that have poison you?" growl Warrington.
"I be go to call upon some woman," say Pen. "I'm--i'm go to dine with them. they be pass through town, and be at an hotel in Jermyn Street."
Warrington look with good-natured interest at the young fellow dandify himself up to a pitch of completeness; and appear at length in a gorgeous shirt-front and neckcloth, fresh glove, and glisten boot. George have a pair of thick high-low, and his old shirt be tear about the breast, and rag at the collar, where his blue beard have wear it.
"well, young un," say he, simply, "I like you to be a buck; somehow. when I walk about with you, it be as if I have a rose in my button-hole. and you be still affable. I donot think there be any young fellow in the Temple turn out like you; and I donot believe you be ever ashamed of walk with I yet."
"donot laugh at I, George." say Pen.
"I say, Pen," continue the other, sadly, "if you write--if you write to Laura, I wish you would say 'God bless she' from I."
Pen blush; and then look at Warrington; and then--and then burst into an uncontrollable fit of laugh.

George refuse. George have an article to write. George hesitate; and oh, strange to say! at last he agree to go. it be agree that they should go and call upon the lady; and they march away in high spirit to the hotel in Jermyn Street. once more the dear face shine upon he; once more the sweet voice speak to he, and the tender hand press a welcome.
there still want half an hour to dinner. "you will go and see your uncle now, Mr. Pendennis," old Lady Rockminster say. I think we have hear all your story. we have be together for two whole day, and I think we be get tired of each other."
so, obey her ladyship's order, Arthur go downstairs and walk to his uncle's lodging.
CHAPTER LXXI
Fiat Justitia
the dinner be serve when Arthur return, and Lady Rockminster begin to scold he for arrive late. but Laura, look at her cousin, see that his face be so pale and scar, that she interrupt her imperious patroness; and ask, with tender alarm, what have happen? be Arthur ill?
Arthur drink a large bumper of sherry. "I have hear the most extraordinary news; I will tell you afterwards," he say, look at the servant. he be very nervous and agitated during the dinner. "donot tramp and beat so with your foot under the table," Lady Rockminster say. "you have tread on Fido, and upset his saucer. you see Mr. Warrington keep his boot quiet."
at the dessert--it seem as if the unlucky dinner would never be over-- Lady Rockminster say, "this dinner have be exceedingly stupid. I suppose something have happen, and that you want to speak to Laura. I will go and have my nap. I be not sure that I shall have any tea--no. good night, Mr. Warrington. you must come again, and when there be no business to talk about." and the old lady, toss up her head, walk away from the room with great dignity.
George and the other have rise with she, and Warrington be about to go away, and be say "good night" to Laura, who, of course, be look much alarmed about her cousin, when Arthur say, "pray, stay, George. you should hear my news too, and give I your counsel in this case. I hardly know how to act in it."
"itbe something about Blanche, Arthur," say Laura, her heart beat, and her cheek blushing as she think it have never blush in her life.
"yes--and the most extraordinary story," say Pen. "when I leave you to go to my uncle's lodging, I find his servant, Morgan, who have be with he so long, at the door, and he say that he and his master have part that morning; that my uncle have quit the house, and have go to an hotel--this hotel. I ask for he when I come in; but he be go out to dinner. Morgan then say that he have something of a most important nature to communicate to I, and beg I to step into the house; his house it be now. it appear the scoundrel have save a great deal of money whilst in my uncle's service, and be now a capitalist and a millionaire, for what I know. well, I go into the house, and what do you think he tell I? this must be a secret between we all--at least if we can keep it, now that it be in possession of that villain. Blanche's father be not dead. he have come to life again. the marriage between Clavering and the Begum be no marriage."
"and Blanche, I suppose, be her grandfather's heir," say Warrington.
"perhaps: but the child of what a father! Amory be an escaped convict-- Clavering know it; my uncle know it--and it be with this piece of information hold over Clavering in terrorem that the wretched old man get he to give up his borough to I."
"Blanche donot know it," say Laura, "nor poor Lady Clavering?"
"no," say Pen; "Blanche do not even know the history of her father. she know that he and her mother have separate, and have hear as a child, from Bonner, her nurse, that Mr. Amory be drown in New South Wales. he be there as a convict, not as a ship's-captain, as the poor girl think. Lady Clavering have tell I that they be not happy, and that her husband be a bad character. she would tell I all, she say, some day: and I remember she say to I, with tear in her eye, that it be hard for a woman to be force to own that she be glad to hear her husband be dead: and that twice in her life she should have choose so badly. what be to be do now? the man cannot show and claim his wife: death be probably over he if he discover himself: return to transportation certainly. but the rascal have hold the threat of discovery over Clavering for some time past, and have extort money from he time after time."
"it be our friend Colonel Altamont, of course," say Warrington "I see all now."
"if the rascal come back," continue Arthur, "Morgan, who know his secret, will use it over he--and have it in his possession, propose to extort money from we all. the d----d rascal suppose I be cognisant of it," say Pen, white with anger; "ask I if I would give he an annuity to keep it quiet; threaten I, I, as if I be traffic with this wretched old Begum's misfortune, and would extort a seat in Parliament out of that miserable Clavering. good heaven! be my uncle mad, to tamper in such a conspiracy? Fancy our mother's son, Laura, trading on such a treason!"
"I cannot fancy it, dear Arthur," say Laura, seize Arthur's hand, and kiss it.
"no!" break out Warrington's deep voice, with a tremor; he survey the two generous and love young people with a pang of indescribable love and pain. "no. our boy cannot meddle with such a wretched intrigue as that. Arthur Pendennis cannot marry a convict's daughter; and sit in Parliament as member for the hulk. you must wash your hand of the whole affair, Pen. you must break off. you must give no explanation of why and wherefore, but state that family reason render a match impossible. it be well that those poor woman should fancy you false to your word than that they should know the truth. besides, you can get from that dog Clavering--I can fetch that for you easily enough an acknowledgment that the reason which you have give to he as the head of the family be amply sufficient for break off the union. donot you think with I, Laura?" he scarcely dare to look she in the face as he speak. any linger hope that he might have--any feeble hold that he might feel upon the last spar of his wrecked fortune, he know he be cast away; and he let the wave of his calamity close over he. Pen have start up whilst he be speak, look eagerly at he. he turn his head away. he see Laura rise up also and go to Pen, and once more take his hand and kiss it. "she think so too--God bless she!" say George.
"her father's shame be not Blanche's fault, dear Arthur, be it?" Laura say, very pale, and speak very quickly. "suppose you have be marry, would you desert she because she have do no wrong? be you not pledge to she? would you leave she because she be in misfortune? and if she be unhappy, wouldnot you console she? our mother would, have she be here." and, as she speak, the kind girl fold her arm round he, and bury her face upon his heart.
"our mother be an angel with God," Pen sob out. "and you be the dearest and good of woman--the dear, the dear and the good. teach I my duty. pray for I that I may do it--pure heart. God bless you--God bless you, my sister!"
"amen," groan out Warrington, with his head in his hand. "she be right," he murmur to himself. "she cannot do any wrong, I think--that girl." indeed, she look and smile like an angel. many a day after he see that smile--see her radiant face as she look up at Pen--see she put back her curl, blush and smile, and still look fondly towards he.
she lean for a moment her little fair hand on the table, play on it. "and now, and now," she say, look at the two gentlemen--
"and what now?" ask George.
"and now we will have some tea," say Miss Laura, with her smile.
but before this unromantic conclusion to a rather sentimental scene could be suffer to take place, a servant bring word that Major Pendennis have return to the hotel, and be wait to see his nephew. upon this announcement, Laura, not without some alarm, and an appealing look to Pen, which say, "behave yourself well--hold to the right, and do your duty--be gentle, but firm with your uncle"--laura, we say, with these warning write in her face, take leave of the two gentleman, and retreat to her dormitory. Warrington, who be not generally fond of tea, yet grudge that expect cup very much. why could not old Pendennis have come in an hour later? well, an hour soon or later, what matter? the hour strike at last. the inevitable moment come to say Farewell, the hand be shake, the door close, and the friend go; and, the brief joy over, you be alone. "in which of those many window of the hotel do her light beam?" perhaps he ask himself as he pass down the street. he stride away to the smoking-room of a neighbouring Club, and, there apply himself to his usual solace of a cigar. man be brawl and talk loud about politic, opera-girl, horse-racing, the atrocious tyranny of the committee:--beare this sacred secret about he, he enter into this brawl. talk away, each louder than the other. rattle and crack joke. Laugh and tell your wild story. it be strange to take one's place and part in the midst of the smoke and din, and think every man here have his secret ego most likely, which be sit lonely and apart, away in the private chamber, from the loud game in which the rest of we be join!
Arthur, as he traverse the passage of the hotel, feel his anger rouse up within he. he be indignant to think that yonder old gentleman whom he be about to meet, should have make he such a tool and puppet, and so compromise his honour and good name. the old fellow's hand be very cold and shaky when Arthur take it. he be cough; he be grumble over the fire; Frosch could not bring his dressing-gown or arrange his paper as that d----d confound impudent scoundrel of a Morgan. the old gentleman bemoan himself, and curse Morgan's ingratitude with peevish pathos.
"the confound impudent scoundrel! he be drunk last night, and challenge I to fight he, Pen; and, begad, at one time I be so excited that I think I should have drive a knife into he; and the infernal rascal have make ten thousand pound, I believe--and deserve to be hang, and will be; but, curse he, I wish he could have last out my time. he know all my way, and, dammy, when I ring the bell, the confound thief bring the thing I want--not like that stupid german lout. and what sort of time have you have in the country? be a good deal with Lady Rockminster? you cannot do well. she be one of the old school--vieille ecole, bonne ecole, hey? Dammy, they donot make gentleman and lady now; and in fifty year youwill hardly know one man from another. but theywill last my time. I benot long for this business: I be get very old, Pen, my boy; and, gad, I be think to-day, as I be pack up my little library, therebe a bible amongst the book that belong to my poor mother; I would like you to keep that, Pen. I be think, sir, that you would most likely open the box when it be your property, and the old fellow be lay under the sod, sir," and the Major cough and wag his old head over the fire.
his age--his kindness, disarm Pen's anger somewhat, and make Arthur feel no little compunction for the deed which he be about to do. he know that the announcement which he be about to make would destroy the darling hope of the old gentleman's life, and create in his breast a woful anger and commotion.
"hey--hey--I'm off, sir," nod the elder; "but Iwould like to read a speech of yours in the Times before I go--'mr. Pendennis say, unaccustomed as I be to public speaking'--hey, sir? hey, Arthur? Begad, you look dev'lish well and healthy, sir. I always say my brother Jack would bring the family right. you must go down into the west, and buy the old estate, sir. Nec tenui penna, hey? wewill rise again, sir--rise again on the wing--and, begad, I shouldnot be surprised that you will be a Baronet before you die."
his word smote Pen. "and it be I," he think, "that be go to fle down the poor old fellow's air-castle. well, it must be. here goes.--I--I go into your lodging at Bury Street, though I do not find you," Pen slowly began--"and I talk with Morgan, uncle."
"indeed!" the old gentleman's cheek begin to flush involuntarily, and he mutter, "the catbe out of the bag now, begad!"
"he tell I a story, sir, which give I the deep surprise and pain," say Pen.
the Major try to look unconcerned. "what--that story about--about-- what-d'-you-call-'em, hey?"
"about Miss Amory's father--about Lady Clavering's first husband, and who he be, and what."
"Hem--a dev'lish awkward affair!" say the old man, rub his nose. "I --i've be aware of that--eh--confound circumstance for some time."
"I wish I have know it soon, or not at all," say Arthur, gloomily.
"he be all safe," think the senior, greatly relieve. "Gad! I should have like to keep it from you altogether--and from those two poor woman, who be as innocent as unborn babe in the transaction."
"you be right. there be no reason why the two woman should hear it; and I shall never tell they--though that villain, Morgan, perhaps may," Arthur say, gloomily. "he seem disposed to trade upon his secret, and have already propose term of ransom to I. I wish I have know of the matter early, sir. it be not a very pleasant thought to I that I be engaged to a convict's daughter."
"the very reason why I keep it from you--my dear boy. but Miss Amory be not a convict's daughter, donot you see? Miss Amory be the daughter of Lady Clavering, with fifty or sixty thousand pound for a fortune; and her father-in-law, a Baronet and country gentleman, of high reputation, approve of the match, and give up his seat in Parliament to his son-in-law. what can be more simple?"
"be it true, sir?"
"Begad, yes, it be true, of course itbe true. Amory's dead. I tell you he be dead. the first sign of life he show, he be dead. he cannot appear. we have he at a deadlock, like the fellow in the play--the 'Critic,' hey?-- dev'lish amusing play, that 'Critic.' monstrous witty man, Sheridan; and so be his son. by Gad, sir, when I be at the Cape, I remember----"
the old gentleman's garrulity, and wish to conduct Arthur to the cape, perhaps arise from a desire to avoid the subject which be near his nephew's heart; but Arthur break out, interrupt him--"if you have tell I this tale soon, I believe you would have spar I and yourself a great deal of pain and disappointment; and I should not have find myself tie to an engagement from which I cannot, in honour, recede."
"no, begad, wehave fix you--and a man whobe fix to a seat in Parliament, and a pretty girl, with a couple of thousand a year, be fix to no bad thing, let I tell you," say the old man.
"Great Heavens, sir!" say Arthur, "be you blind? cannot you see?"
"see what, young gentleman?" ask the other.
"see, that rather than trade upon this secret of Amory's," Arthur cry out, "I would go and join my father-in-law at the hulk! see, that rather than take a seat in Parliament as a bribe from Clavering for silence, I would take the spoon off the table! see, that you have give I a felon's daughter for a wife; doom I to poverty and shame; curse my career when it might have be--when it might have be so different but for you! donot you see that we have be play a guilty game, and have be overreached;--that in offer to marry this poor girl, for the sake of her money, and the advancement she would bring, I be degrade myself, and prostitute my honour?"
"what in Heaven's name do you mean, sir?" cry the old man.
"I mean to say that there be a measure of baseness which I cannot pass," Arthur say. "I have no other word for it, and be sorry if they hurt you. I have feel, for month past, that my conduct in this affair have be wicked, sordid, and worldly. I be rightly punish by the event, and have sell myself for money and a seat in Parliament, by lose both."
"how do you mean that you lose either?" shriek the old gentleman. "who the devilbe to take your fortune or your seat away from you? by g--, Clavering shall give them to you. you shall have every shilling of eighty thousand pound."
"Iwill keep my promise to Miss Amory, sir," say Arthur.
"and, begad, her parent shall keep theirs to you."
"not so, please God," Arthur answer. "I have sin, but, Heaven help I, I will sin no more. I will let clavering off from that bargain which be make without my knowledge. I will take no money with Blanche but that which be originally settle upon she; and I will try to make she happy. you have do it. you have bring this on I, sir. but you know no well: and I forgive----"
"Arthur--in God's name--in your father's, who, by Heavens, be the proud man alive, and have the honour of the family always at heart--in mine--for the sake of a poor break-down old fellow, who have always be dev'lish fond of you--don't fle this chance away--I pray you, I beg you, I implore you, my dear, dear boy, donot fle this chance away. itbe the making of you. yoube sure to get on. youwill be a Baronet; itbe three thousand a year: dammy, on my knee, there, I beg of you, donot do this."
and the old man actually sink down on his knee, and, seize one of Arthur's hand, look up piteously at he. it be cruel to remark the shake hand, the wrinkle and quivering face, the old eye weep and wink, the broken voice. "ah, sir," say Arthur, with a groan, "you have bring pain enough on I, spare I this. you have wish I to marry Blanche. I marry she. for God's sake, sir, rise! I cannot bear it."
"you--you mean to say that you will take she as a beggar, and be one yourself?" say the old gentleman, rise up and cough violently.
"I look at she as a person to whom a great calamity have befall, and to whom I be promise. she cannot help the misfortune; and as she have my word when she be prosperous, I shall not withdraw it now she be poor. I will not take Clavering's seat, unless afterwards it should be give of his free will. I will not have a shilling more than her original fortune."
"have the kindness to ring the bell," say the old gentleman. "I have do my good, and say my say; and Ibe a dev'lish old fellow. and--and-- it donot matter. and--and Shakspeare be right--and Cardinal Wolsey-- begad--'and have I but serve my God as Ihave serve you'--ye, on my knee, by Jove, to my own nephew--I mightnot have be--good night, sir, you neednot trouble yourself to call again."
Arthur take his hand, which the old man leave to he; it be quite passive and clammy. he look very much oldene; and it seem as if the contest and defeat have quite break he.
on the next day he keep his bed, and refuse to see his nephew.
CHAPTER LXXII
in which the deck begin to clear
when, array in his dressing-gown, Pen walk up, accord to custom, to Warrington's chamber next morning, to inform his friend of the issue of the last night's interview with his uncle, and to ask, as usual, for George's advice and opinion, Mrs. Flanagan, the laundress, be the only person whom Arthur find in the dear old chamber. George have take a carpet-bag, and be go. his address be to his brother's house, in Suffolk. package address to the newspaper and review for which he write lie on the table, await delivery.
"I find he at the table, when I come, the dear gentleman!" Mrs. Flanagan say, "write at his paper, and one of the candle be burn out; and hard as his bed be, he benot in it all night, sir."
indeed, have sit at the Club until the brawl there become intolerable to he, George have walk home, and have pass the night finish some work on which he be employ, and to the completion of which he bend himself with all his might. the labour be do, and the night be wear away somehow, and the tardy November dawn come and look in on the young man as he sate over his desk. in the next day's paper, or quarter's review, many of we very likely admire the work of his genius, the variety of his illustration, the fierce vigour of his satire, the depth of his reason. there be no hint in his writing of the other thought which occupy he, and always accompany he in his work--a tone more melancholy than be customary, a satire more bitter and impatient than that which he afterwards show, may have mark the writing of this period of his life to the very few person who know his style or his name. we have say before, could we know the man's feeling as well as the author's thought--how interesting most book would be!--more interesting than merry. I suppose harlequin's face behind his mask be always grave, if not melancholy--certainly each man who live by the pen, and happen to read this, must remember, if he will, his own experience, and recall many solemn hour of solitude and labour. what a constant care sate at the side of the desk and accompany he! fever or sickness be lie possibly in the next room: a sick child might be there, with a wife watch over it terrify and in prayer: or grief might be bear he down, and the cruel mist before the eye render the paper scarce visible as he write on it, and the inexorable necessity drive on the pen. what man among we have not have night and hour like these? but to the manly heart--severe as these pang be, they be endurable: long as the night seem, the dawn come at last, and the wound heal, and the fever abate, and rest come, and you can afford to look back on the past misery with feeling that be anything but bitter.
two or three book for reference, fragment of tear-up manuscript, drawer open, pen and inkstand, line half visible on the blot-paper, a bit of sealing-wax twist and bite and break into sundry piece--such relic as these be about the table, and Pen fling himself down in George's empty chair--note thing accord to his willnot, or in spite of himself. there be a gap in the bookcase (next to the old College Plato, with the Boniface Arms), where Helen's bible use to be. he have take that with he, think Pen. he know why his friend be go. dear, dear old George!
Pen rub his hand over his eye. oh, how much wise, how much well, how much nobler he be than I! he think. where be such a friend, or such a brave heart? where shall I ever hear such a frank voice, and kind laughter? where shall I ever see such a true gentleman? no wonder she love he. God bless he! what be I compare to he? what could she do else but love he? to the end of our day we will be her brother, as fate will that we can be no more. wewill be her knight, and wait on she: and when webe old, wewill say how we love she. dear, dear old George!
when Pen descend to his own chamber, his eye fall on the letter-box of his outer door, which he have previously overlook, and there be a little note to A. P., Esq., in George's well-know handwriting, George have put into Pen's box probably as he be go away.
"dear Pen,--I shall be half-way home when you breakfast, and intend to stay over Christmas, in Norfolk, or elsewhere.
"I have my own opinion of the issue of matter about which we talk in J----- St. yesterday; and think my presence de trop.
"Vale. G. W."
"give my very good regard and adieux to your cousin."
and so George be go, and Mrs. Flanagan, the laundress, rule over his empty chamber.
Pen of course have to go and see his uncle on the day after their colloquy, and not be admit, he naturally go to Lady Rockminster's apartment, where the old lady instantly ask for Bluebeard, and insist that he should come to dinner.
"Bluebeard be go," Pen say, and he take out poor George's scrap of paper, and hand it to Laura, who look at it--do not look at Pen in return, but pass the paper back to he, and walk away. Pen rush into an eloquent eulogium upon his dear old George to Lady Rockminster, who be astonish at his enthusiasm. she have never hear he so warm in praise of anybody; and tell he with her usual frankness, that she donot think it have be in his nature to care so much about any other person.
as Mr. Pendennis be pass in Waterloo Place, in one of his many walk to the hotel where Laura live, and whither duty to his uncle carry Arthur every day, Arthur see issue from Messrs. Gimcrack's celebrated shop an old friend, who be follow to his brougham by an obsequious shopman bear parcel. the gentleman be in the deep mourning: the brougham, the driver, and the horse be in mourning. grief in easy circumstance and support by the comfortable spring and cushion, be typify in the equipage and the little gentleman, its proprietor.
"what, Foker! Hail, Foker!" cry out Pen--the reader, no doubt, have likewise recognise Arthur's old schoolfellow--and he hold out his hand to the heir of the late lament John Henry Foker, Esq., the master of Logwood and other house, the principal partner in the great brewery of Foker and Co.: the great portion of Foker's Entire.
a little hand, cover with a glove of the deep ebony, and set off by three inch of a snowy wristband, be put forth to meet Arthur's salutation. the other little hand hold a little morocco case, contain, no doubt, something precious, of which Mr. Foker have just become proprietor in Messrs. Gimcrack's shop. Pen's keen eye and satiric turn show he at once upon what errand Mr. Foker have be employ; and he think of the heir in Horace pour forth the gathered wine of his father's vat; and that human nature be pretty much the same in Regent Street as in the Via Sacra.
"Le Roi est mort. Vive le Roi!" say Arthur.
"ah!" say the other. "yes. thank you--very much obliged. how do you do, pen?--very busy--good-bye!" and he jump into the black brougham, and sate like a little black care behind the black coachman. he have blush on see Pen, and show other sign of guilt and perturbation, which Pen attribute to the novelty of his situation; and on which he begin to speculate in his usual sardonic manner.
"yes: so wag the world," think Pen. "the stone close over Harry the Fourth, and Harry the Fifth reign in his stead. the old minister at the brewery come and kneel before he with their book; the drayman, his subject, fle up their red cap, and shout for he. what a grave deference and sympathy the banker and the lawyer show! there be too great a stake at issue between those two that they should ever love each other very cordially. as long as one man keep another out of twenty thousand a year, the young must be always hanker after the crown, and the wish must be the father to the thought of possession. thank Heaven, there be no thought of money between I and our dear mother, Laura."
"there never could have be. you would have spurn it!" cry Laura. "why make yourself more selfish than you be, Pen; and allow your mind to own for an instant that it would have entertain such--such dreadful meanness? you make I blush for you, Arthur: you make me----" her eye finish this sentence, and she pass her handkerchief across they.
"there be some truth which woman will never acknowledge," Pen say, "and from which your modesty always turn away. I do not say that I ever know the feeling, only that I be glad I have not the temptation. be there any harm in that confession of weakness?"
"we be all teach to ask to be deliver from evil, Arthur," say Laura, in a low voice. "I be glad if you be spar from that great crime; and only sorry to think that you could by any possibility have be lead into it. but you never could; and you donot think you could. your act be generous and kind: you disdain mean action. you take Blanche without money, and without a bribe. yes, thank be to Heaven, dear brother. you could not have sell yourself away; I know you could not when it come to the day, and you do not. praise be--be where praise be due. why do this horrid scepticism pursue you, my Arthur? why doubt and sneer at your own heart--at every one's? oh, if you know the pain you give I--how I lie awake and think of those hard sentence, dear brother, and wish they unspoken, unthought!"
"do I cause you many thought and many tear, Laura?" ask Arthur. the fulness of innocent love beam from she in reply. a smile heavenly pure, a glance of unutterable tenderness, sympathy, pity, shine in her face-- all which indication of love and purity Arthur beheld and worship in she, as you would watch they in a child, as one fancy one might regard they in an angel.
"I--I donot know what I have do," he say, simply, "to have merit such regard from two such woman. it be like undeserved praise, Laura--or too much good fortune, which frighten one--or a great post, when a man feel that he be not fit for it. ah, sister, how weak and wicked we be; how spotless, and full of love and truth, Heaven make you! I think for some of you there have be no fall," he say, look at the charming girl with an almost paternal glance of admiration. "you cannot help have sweet thought, and do good action. dear creature! they be the flower which you bear."
"and what else, sir?" ask Laura. "I see a sneer come over your face. what be it? why do it come to drive all the good thought away?"
"a sneer, be there? I be think, my dear, that nature in make you so good and loving do very well: but----"
"but what? what be that wicked but? and why be you always call it up?"
"but will come in spite of we. but be reflection. but be the sceptic's familiar, with whom he have make a compact; and if he forget it, and indulge in happy day-dream, or building of air-castle, or listen to sweet music let we say, or to the bell ring to church, but tap at the door, and say, Master, I be here. you be my master; but I be yours. go where you will you cannot travel without I. I will whisper to you when you be on your knee at church. I will be at your marriage pillow. I will sit down at your table with your child. I will be behind your deathbed curtain. that be what but be," Pen say.
"Pen, you frighten I," cry Laura.
"do you know what but come and say to I just now, when I be look at you? but say, if that girl have reason as well as love, she would love you no more. if she know you as you be--the sully, selfish being which you know--she must part from you, and could give you no love and no sympathy. donot I say," he add fondly, "that some of you seem exempt from the fall? love you know; but the knowledge of evil be keep from you."
"what be this you young folk be talk about?" ask Lady Rockminster, who at this moment make her appearance in the room, have perform, in the mystic retirement of her own apartment, and under the hand of her attendant, those elaborate toilet-rite without which the worthy old lady never present herself to public view. "Mr. Pendennis, you be always come here."
"it be very pleasant to be here," Arthur say; "and we be talk, when you come in, about my friend Foker, whom I meet just now; and who, as your ladyship know, have succeed to his father's kingdom."
"he have a very fine property, he have fifteen thousand a year. he be my cousin. he be a very worthy young man. he must come and see I," say Lady Rockminster, with a look at Laura.
"he have be engage for many year past to his cousin," Lady----"
"Lady Ann be a foolish little chit," Lady Rockminster say, with much dignity; "and I have no patience with she. she have outrage every feeling of society. she have break her father's heart, and throw away fifteen thousand a year."
"throw away? what have happen?" ask Pen.
"it will be the talk of the town in a day or two; and there be no need why I should keep the secret any long," say Lady Rockminster, who have write and receive a dozen letter on the subject. "I have a letter yesterday from my daughter, who be stay at Drummington until all the world be oblige to go away on account of the frightful catastrophe which happen there. when Mr. Foker come home from Nice, and after the funeral, Lady Ann go down on her knee to her father, say that she never could marry her cousin, that she have contract another attachment, and that she must die rather than fulfil her contract. poor Lord Rosherville, who be dreadfully embarrassed, show his daughter what the state of his affair be, and that it be necessary that the arrangement should take place; and in fine, we all suppose that she have listen to reason, and intend to comply with the desire of her family. but what have happened?--last Thursday she go out after breakfast with she maid, and be marry in the very church in Drummington Park to Mr. Hobson, her father's own chaplain and her brother's tutor; a red-haired widower with two child. poor dear Rosherville be in a dreadful way: he wish Henry Foker should marry Alice or Barbara; but Alice be mark with the small-pox, and Barbara be ten year old than he be. and, of course, now the young man be his own master, he will think of choose for himself. the blow on Lady Agnes be very cruel. she be inconsolable. she have the house in Grosvenor Street for her life, and her settlement, which be very handsome. have you not meet she? yes, she din one day at Lady Clavering's--the first day I see you, and a very disagreeable young man I think you be. but I have form you. we have form he, havenot we, Laura? where be Bluebeard? let he come. that horrid Grindley, the dentist, will keep I in town another week."
to the latter part of her ladyship's speech Arthur give no ear. he be think for whom could Foker be purchase those trinket which he be carry away from the jeweller's? why do Harry seem anxious to avoid he? could he be still faithful to the attachment which have agitate he so much, and send he abroad eighteen month back? Psha! the bracelet and present be for some of Harry's old friend of the Opera or the french theatre. rumour from Naples and Paris, rumour such as be bear to Club smoking-room, have announce that the young man have find distraction; or, preclude from his virtuous attachment, the poor fellow have fling himself back upon his old companion and amusement--not the only man or woman whom society force into evil, or debar from good; not the only victim of the world's selfish and wicked law.
as a good thing when it be to be do cannot be do too quickly, Laura be anxious that Pen's marriage intention should be put into execution as speedily as possible, and press on his arrangement with rather a feverish anxiety. why could she not wait? Pen could afford to do so with perfect equanimity, but Laura would hear of no delay. she write to Pen: she implore Pen: she use every mean to urge expedition. it seem as if she could have no rest until Arthur's happiness be complete.
she offer herself to dearest Blanche to come and stay at Tunbridge with she, when Lady Rockminster should go on her intend visit to the reign house of Rockminster; and although the old dowager scold, and order, and command, Laura be deaf and disobedient: she must go to Tunbridge, she would go to Tunbridge: she who ordinarily have no will of her own, and comply smilingly with anybody's whim and caprice, show the most selfish and obstinate determination in this instance. the dowager lady must nurse herself in her rheumatism, she must read herself to sleep, if she would not hear she maid, whose voice croak, and who make sad work of the sentimental passage in the novel--Laura must go,-- and be with her new sister. in another week, she propose, with many love and regard to dear Lady Clavering, to pass some time with dear Blanche.
Dearest Blanche write instantly in reply to dear Laura's no. 1, to say with what extreme delight she should welcome her sister: how charming it would be to practise their old duet together, to wander o'er the grassy sward, and amidst the yellow wood of Penshurst and Southborough! Blanche count the hour till she should embrace her dear friend.
Laura, No. 2, express her delight at dear Blanche's affectionate reply. she hop that their friendship would never diminish; that the confidence between they would grow in after year; that they should have no secret from each other; that the aim of the life of each would be to make one person happy.
Blanche, No. 2, follow in two day. "how provoking! their house be very small, the two spare bedroom be occupy by that horrid Mrs. Planter and her daughter, who have think proper to fall ill (she always fall ill in country-house), and she could not or would not be move for some day."
Laura, No. 3. "it be indeed very provoking.
