Arthur's heart be full of tenderness and affection. she have know Arthur all her life. she would answer"--yes, even in italic she would answer--"for his kindness, his goodness, and his gentleness."
Blanche, No. 3. "what be this most surprising, most extraordinary letter from A. P.? what do dear Laura know about it? what have happen? what, what mystery be envelop under his frightful reserve?"
Blanche, No. 3, require an explanation; and it cannot be well give than in the surprising and mysterious letter of Arthur Pendennis.
CHAPTER LXXIII
Mr. and Mrs. Sam Huxter
"Dear Blanche," Arthur write, "you be always read and dream pretty drama, and exciting romance in real life: be you now prepared to enact a part of one? and not the pleasantest part, dear Blanche, that in which the heroine take possession of her father's palace and wealth, and introduce her husband to the loyal retainer and faithful vassal, greet her happy bridegroom with 'all of this be mine and thine,'--but the other character, that of the luckless lady, who suddenly discover that she be not the Prince's wife, but Claude Melnotte's the beggar's: that of Alnaschar's wife, who come in just as her husband have kick over the tray of porcelain which be to be the making of his fortune--but stay; Alnaschar, who kick down the china, be not a married man; he have cast his eye on the Vizier's daughter, and his hope of she go to the ground with the shattered bowl and tea-cup.
"will you be the Vizier's daughter, and refuse and laugh to scorn Alnaschar, or will you be the Lady of Lyons, and love the penniless Claude Melnotte? I will act that part if you like. I will love you my good in return. I will do my all to make your humble life happy: for humble it will be: at least the odd be against any other conclusion; we shall live and die in a poor prosy humdrum way. there will be no star and epaulette for the hero of our story. I shall write one or two more story, which will presently be forget. I shall be call to the Bar, and try to get on in my profession: perhaps some day, if I be very lucky, and work very hard (which be absurd), I may get a colonial appointment, and you may be an indian Judge's lady. meanwhile. I shall buy back the Pall Mall Gazette; the publisher be tired of it since the death of poor Shandon, and will sell it for a small sum. Warrington will be my right hand, and write it up to a respectable sale. I will introduce you to Mr. Finucane the sub-editor, and I know who in the end will be Mrs. Finucane,--a very nice gentle creature, who have live sweetly through a sad life and we will jog on, I say, and look out for well time, and earn our live decently. you shall have the opera-box, and superintend the fashionable intelligence, and break your little heart in the poet's corner. shall we live over the offices?--there be four very good room, a kitchen, and a garret for Laura, in Catherine Street in the Strand; or would you like a house in the Waterloo Road?--it would be very pleasant, only there be that halfpenny toll at the Bridge. the boy may go to King's College, maynot they? do all this read to you like a joke?
"ah, dear Blanche, it be no joke, and I be sober and tell the truth. our fine day-dream be go. our carriage have whirl out of sight like Cinderella's: our house in Belgravia have be whisk away into the air by a malevolent Genius, and I be no more a member of Parliament than I be a Bishop on his bench in the House of Lords, or a Duke with a garter at his knee. you know pretty well what my property be, and your own little fortune: we may have enough with those two to live in decent comfort; to take a cab sometimes when we go out to see our friend, and not to deny ourselves an omnibus when we be tired. but that be all: be that enough for you, my little dainty lady? I doubt sometimes whether you can bear the life which I offer you--at least, it be fair that you should know what it will be. if you say, 'yes, Arthur, I will follow your fate whatever it may be, and be a loyal and love wife to aid and cheer you' --come to I, dear Blanche, and may God help I so that I may do my duty to you. if not, and you look to a high station, I must not bar Blanche's fortune--I will stand in the crowd, and see your ladyship go to Court when you be present, and you shall give I a smile from your chariot window. I see Lady Mirabel go to the drawing-room last season: the happy husband at her side glitter with star and cordon. all the flower in the garden bloom in the coachman's bosom. will you have these and the chariot, or walk on foot and mend your husband's stocking?
"I cannot tell you now--afterwards I might, should the day come when we may have no secret from one another--what have happen within the last few hour which have change all my prospect in life: but so it be, that I have learn something which force I to give up the plan which I have form, and many vain and ambitious hope in which I have be indulge. I have write and despatch a letter to Sir Francis Clavering, say that I cannot accept his seat in Parliament until after my marriage; in like manner I cannot and will not accept any large fortune with you than that which have always belong to you since your grandfather's death, and the birth of your half-brother. your good mother be not in the least aware--I hope she never may be--of the reason which force I to this very strange decision. they arise from a painful circumstance, which be attributable to none of our fault; but, have once befall, they be as fatal and irreparable as that shock which overset honest Alnaschar's porcelain, and shatter all his hope beyond the power of mending. I write gaily enough, for there be no use in bewail such a hopeless mischance. we have not draw the great prize in the lottery, dear Blanche: but I shall be content enough without it, if you can be so; and I repeat, with all my heart, that I will do my good to make you happy.
"and now, what news shall I give you? my uncle be very unwell, and take my refusal of the seat in Parliament in sad dudgeon: the scheme be his, poor old gentleman, and he naturally bemoan its failure. but Warrington, Laura, and I have a council of war: they know this awful secret, and back I in my decision. you must love George as you love what be generous and upright and noble; and as for Laura--she must be our Sister, Blanche, our Saint, our good Angel. with two such friend at home, what need we care for the world without; or who be member for Clavering, or who be ask or not ask to the great ball of the season?"
to this frank communication come back the letter from Blanche to Laura, and one to Pen himself, which perhaps his own letter justify. "you be spoil by the world," Blanche write; "you do not love your poor Blanche as she would be love, or you would not offer thus lightly to take she or to leave she, no, Arthur, you love I not--a man of the world, you have give I your plight troth, and be ready to redeem it; but that entire affection, that love whole and abide, where--where be that vision of my youth? I be but a pastime of your life, and I would be its all;--but a fleeting thought, and I would be your whole soul. I would have our two heart one; but ah, my Arthur, how lonely yours be! how little you give I of it! you speak of our parting with a smile on your lip; of our meeting, and you care not to hasten it! be life but a disillusion, then, and be the flower of our garden fade away? I have weep--I have pray --I have pass sleepless hour--I have shed bitter, bitter tear over your letter! to you I bring the gushing poesy of my being--the yearning of the soul that long to be love--that pine for love, love, love, beyond all!--that fling itself at your foot, and cry, love I, Arthur! your heart beat no quick at the kneeling appeal of my love!--your proud eye be dim by no tear of sympathy!--you accept my soul's treasure as though 'twere dross! not the pearl from the unfathomable deep of affection! not the diamond from the cavern of the heart. you treat I like a slave, and bid I bow to my master! be this the guerdon of a free maiden--be this the price of a life's passion? ah I! when be it otherwise? when do love meet with aught but disappointment? could I hope (fond fool!) to be the exception to the lot of my race; and lie my fevere brow on a heart that comprehend my own? foolish girl that I be! one by one, all the flower of my young life have fade away; and this, the last, the sweetest, the dear, the fondly, the madly love, the wildly cherished--where be it? but no more of this. Heed not my bleed heart.--bless you, bless you always, Arthur!
"I will write more when I be more collected. my rack brain render think almost impossible. I long to see Laura! she will come to we directly we return from the country, will she not? and you, cold one! B."
the word of this letter be perfectly clear, and write in Blanche's neat hand upon her scented paper; and yet the meaning of the composition not a little puzzled Pen. do Blanche mean to accept or to refuse his polite offer? her phrase either mean that Pen do not love she, and she decline he, or that she take he, and sacrifice herself to he, cold as he be. he laugh sardonically over the letter, and over the transaction which occasion it. he laugh to think how Fortune have jilt he, and how he deserve his slippery fortune. he turn over and over the musky gilt-edge riddle. it amuse his humour: he enjoy it as if it have be a funny story.
he be thus seat, twiddle the queer manuscript in his hand, joke grimly to himself, when his servant come in with a card from a gentleman, who wish to speak to he very particularly. and if Pen have go out into the passage, he would have see, suck his stick, roll his eye, and show great mark of anxiety, his old acquaintance, Mr. Samuel Huxter.
"Mr. Huxter on particular business! pray, beg Mr. Huxter to come in," say Pen, amuse rather; and not the less so when poor Sam appear before he.
"pray take a chair, Mr. Huxter," say Pen, in his most superb manner. "in what way can I be of service to you?"
"I have rather not speak before the flunk--before the man, Mr. Pendennis:" on which Mr. Arthur's attendant quit the room.
"Ibe in a fix," say Mr. Huxter, gloomily.
"indeed."
"she send I to you," continue the young surgeon.
"what, Fanny? be she well? I be come to see she, but I have have a great deal of business since my return to London."
"I hear of you through my governor and Jack Hobnell," break in Huxter. "I wish you joy, Mr. Pendennis, both of the borough and the lady, sir. Fanny wish you joy, too," he add, with something of a blush.
"therebe many a slip between the cup and the lip! who know what may happen, Mr. Huxter, or who will sit in Parliament for Clavering next session?"
"you can do anything with my governor," continue Mr. Huxter. "you get he Clavering Park. the old boy be very much pleased, sir, at your call he in. Hobnell write I so. do you think you could speak to the governor for I, Mr. Pendennis?"
"and tell he what?"
"Ihave go and do it, sir," say Huxter, with a particular look.
"you--you donot mean to say you have--you have do any wrong to that dear little creature, sir?" say Pen, start up in a great fury.
"I hope not," say Huxter, with a hangdog look: "but Ihave marry she. and I know there will be an awful shindy at home. it be agree that I should be take into partnership when I have pass the College, and it be to have be Huxter and Son. but I would have it, confound it. itbe all over now, and the old boy's write I that hebe come up to town for drug: he will be here to-morrow, and then it must all come out."
"and when do this event happen?" ask Pen, not over well pleased, most likely, that a person who have once attract some portion of his royal good grace should have transfer her allegiance, and console herself for his loss.
"last Thursday be five week--it be two day after Miss Amory come to Shepherd's Inn," Huxter answer.
Pen remember that Blanche have write and mention her visit. "I be call in," Huxter say. "I be in the inn look after old cos's leg; and about something else too, very likely: and I meet Strong, who tell I there be a woman take ill in Chambers, and go up to give she my professional service. it be the old lady who attend Miss Amory--her housekeeper, or some such thing. she be take with strong hysteric: I find her kicking and scream like a good one--in Strong's chamber, along with he and Colonel Altamont, and Miss Amory cry and as pale as a sheet; and Altamont fume about--a regular kick-up. they be two hour in the Chambers; and the old woman go whoop off in a cab. she be much bad than the young one. I call in Grosvenor Place next day to see if I could be of any service, but they be go without so much as thank I: and the day after I have business of my own to attend to-- a bad business too," say Mr. Huxter, gloomily. "but itbe do, and cannot be undo; and we must make the good of it"
she have know the story for a month, think Pen, with a sharp pang of grief, and a gloomy sympathy--this account for her letter of to-day. she will not implicate her father, or divulge his secret; she wish to let I off from the marriage--and find a pretext--the generous girl!
"do you know who Altamont be, sir?" ask Huxter, after the pause during which Pen have be think of his own affair. "Fanny and I have talk he over, and we cannot help fancy that itbe Mrs. Lightfoot's first husband come to life again, and she who have just marry a second. perhaps Lightfoot willnot be very sorry for it," sigh Huxter, look savagely at Arthur, for the demon of jealousy be still in possession of his soul; and now, and more than ever since his marriage, the poor fellow fancy that Fanny's heart belong to his rival.
"let we talk about your affair," say Pen. "show I how I can be of any service to you, Huxter. let I congratulate you on your marriage. I be thankful that Fanny, who be so good, so fascinating, so kind a creature, have find an honest man, and a gentleman who will make she happy. show I what I can do to help you."
"she think you can, sir," say Huxter, accept Pen's proffered hand, "and Ibe very much oblige to you, Ibe sure; and that you might talk over my father, and break the business to he, and my mother, who always have she back up about be a clergyman's daughter. Fanny benot of a good family, I know, and not up to we in breeding and that--but shebe a Huxter now."
"the wife take the husband's rank, of course," say Pen.
"and with a little practice in society," continue Huxter, imbibe his stick, "shewill be as good as any girl in Clavering. you should hear her sing and play on the piano. do you ever? Old Bows teach she. and shewill do on the stage, if the governor be to throw I over; but Iwould rather not have she there. she cannot help be a coquette, Mr. Pendennis, she cannot help it. Dammy, sir! Iwill be bind to say, that two or three of the Bartholomew chap, that Ihave bring into my place, be sit with she now: even Jack Linton, that I take down as my good man, be as bad as the rest, and she will go on sing and make eye at he. itbe what bow say, if there be twenty man in a room, and one not take notice of she, she wouldnot be satisfied until the twentieth be at her elbow."
"you should have her mother with she," say Pen, laugh.
"she must keep the lodge. she cannot see so much of her family as she use. I cannot, you know, sir, go on with that lot. consider my rank in life," say Huxter, put a very dirty hand up to his chin.
"Au fait," say Mr. Pen, who be infinitely amuse, and concern whom mutato nomine (and of course concern nobody else in the world) the fable might have be narrate.
as the two gentleman be in the midst of this colloquy, another knock come to Pen's door, and his servant presently announce Mr. Bows. the old man follow slowly, his pale face blushing, and his hand tremble somewhat as he take Pen's. he cough, and wipe his face in his check cotton pocket-handkerchief, and sate down with his hand on his knee, the sunshining on his bald head. Pen look at the homely figure with no small sympathy and kindness. this man, too, have have his grief and his wound, Arthur think. this man, too, have bring his genius and his heart, and lay they at a woman's foot; where she spurn they. the chance of life have go against he, and the prize be with that creature yonder. Fanny's bridegroom, thus mutely apostrophise, have wink meanwhile with one eye at old bow, and be drive hole in the floor with the cane which he love.
"so we have lose, Mr. Bows, and here be the lucky winner," Pen say, look hard at the old man.
"here be the lucky winner, sir, as you say."
"I suppose you have come from my place?" ask Huxter, who, have wink at bow with one eye, now favour Pen with a wink of the other--a wink which seem to say, "infatuated old boy--you understand--over head and ear in love with her poor old fool."
"yes, I have be there ever since you go away. it be Mrs. Sam who send I after you: who say that she think you might be do something stupid--something like yourself, Huxter."
"therebe as big fool as I be," growl the young surgeon.
"a few, p'raps," say the old man; "not many, let we trust. yes, she send I after you for fear you should offend Mr. Pendennis; and I daresay because she think you wouldnot give her message to he, and beg he to go and see she; and she know I would take her errand. do he tell you that, sir?"
Huxter blush scarlet, and cover his confusion with an imprecation. Pen laugh; the scene suit his bitter humour more and more.
"I have no doubt Mr. Huxter be go to tell I," Arthur say, "and very much flattered I be sure I shall be to pay my respect to his wife."
"itbe in Charterhouse Lane, over the baker's, on the right hand side as you go from St. John's Street," continue bow, without any pity. "you know Smithfield, Mr. Pendennis? St. John's Street lead into Smithfield. Doctor Johnson have be down the street many a time with ragged shoe, and a bundle of penny-a-lining for the Gent's Magazine. you literary gent be well off now--eh? you ride in your cab, and wear yellow kid glove now."
"I have know so many brave and good man fail, and so many quack and impostor succeed, that you mistake I if you think I be puff up by my own personal good luck, old friend," Arthur say, sadly. "do you think the prize of life be carry by the most deserving? and set up that mean test of prosperity for merit? you must feel that you be as good as I. I have never question it. it be you that be peevish against the freak of fortune, and grudge the good luck that befall other. itbe not the first time you have unjustly accuse I, Bows."
"perhaps you be not far wrong, sir," say the old fellow, wipe his bald forehead. "I be think about myself and grumble; most man do when they get on that subject. herebe the fellow thatbe get the prize in the lottery; herebe the fortunate youth."
"I donot know what you be drive at," Huxter say, who have be much puzzled as the above remark pass between his two companion.
"perhaps not," say Bows, drily. "Mrs. H. send I here to look after you, and to see that you bring that little message to Mr. Pendennis, which you donot, you see, and so she be right. woman always be; they have always a reason for everything. why, sir," he say, turn round to Pen with a sneer, "she have a reason even for give I that message. I be sit with she after you leave we, very quiet and comfortable; I be talk away, and she be mend your shirt, when your two young friend, Jack Linton and Bob Blades, look in from Bartholomew's; and then it be she find out that she have this message to send. you neednot hurry yourself, she donot want you back again; theywill stay these two hour, I daresay."
Huxter arise with great perturbation at this news, and plunge his stick into the pocket of his paletot, and seize his hat.
"youwill come and see we, sir, willnot you?" he say to Pen. "youwill talk over the governor, willnot you, sir, if I can get out of this place and down to Clavering?"
"you will promise to attend I gratis if ever I fall ill at Fairoaks, will you, Huxter?" Pen say, good-naturedly. "I will do anything I can for you. I will come and see Mrs. Huxter immediately, and we will conspire together about what be to be do."
"I think that would send he out, sir," bow say, drop into his chair again as soon as the young surgeon have quit the room. "and itbe all true, sir--every word of it. she want you back again, and send her husband after you. she cajole everybody, the little devil. she try it on you, on I, on poor Costigan, on the young chap from Bartholomew's. shebe get a little court of them already. and if therebe nobody there, she practise on the old german baker in the shop, or coax the black sweeper at the crossing."
"be she fond of that fellow?" ask Pen.
"there be no accounting for like and dislike," bow answer.
"yes, she be fond of he; and have take the thing into her head, she would not rest until she marry he. they have their bann publish at St. Clement's, and nobody hear it or know any just cause or impediment. and one day she slip out of the porter's lodge and have the business do, and go off to gravesend with Lothario; and leave a note for I to go and explain all thing to her Ma. bless you! the old woman know it as well as I do, though she pretend ignorance. and so she go, and Ibe alone again. I miss she, sir, trip along that court, and come for her singing lesson; and Ihave no heart to look into the porter's lodge now, which look very empty without she, the little flirt thing. and I go and sit and dangle about her lodging, like an old fool. she make them very trim and nice, though; get up all Huxter's shirt and clothe: cook his little dinner, and sing at her business like a little lark. whatbe the use of be angry? I lend them three pound to go on with: for they havenot get a shilling till the reconciliation, and Pa come down."
when bow have take his leave, Pen carry his letter from Blanche, and the news which he have just receive, to his usual adviser, Laura. it be wonderful upon how many point Mr. Arthur, who generally follow his own opinion, now want another person's counsel. he could hardly so much as choose a waistcoat without refer to Miss Bell: if he want to buy a horse he must have Miss Bell's opinion; all which mark of deference tend greatly to the amusement of the shrewd old lady with whom Miss Bell live, and whose plan regard her protegee we have indicate.
Arthur produce Blanche's letter then to Laura, and ask she to interpret it. Laura be very much agitated and puzzle by the content of the note.
"it seem to I," she say, "as if Blanche be act very artfully."
"and wish so to place matter that she may take I or leave I? be it not so?"
"it be, I be afraid, a kind of duplicity which do not augur well for your future happiness; and be a bad reply to your own candour and honesty, Arthur. do you know, I think, I think--I scarcely like to say what I think," say Laura with a deep blush; but of course the blush young lady yield to her cousin's persuasion, and express what her thought be. "it look to I, Arthur, as if there might be--there might be somebody else," say, Laura, with a repetition of the blush.
"and if there be," break in Arthur, "and if I be free once again, will the good and dearest of all women----"
"you be not free, dear brother," Laura say calmly. "you belong to another; of whom I own it grieve I to think ill. but I cannot do otherwise. it be very odd that in this letter she do not urge you to tell she the reason why you have break arrangement which would have be so advantageous to you; and avoid speak on the subject. she somehow seem to write as if she know her father's secret."
Pen say, "yes, she must know it;" and tell the story, which he have just hear from Huxter, of the interview at Shepherd's Inn.
"it be not so that she describe the meeting," say Laura; and, go to her desk, produce from it that letter of Blanche's which mention her visit to Shepherd's Inn. 'another disappointment--only the Chevalier Strong and a friend of his in the room.' this be all that Blanche have say. "but she be bind to keep her father's secret, Pen," Laura add. "and yet, and yet--it be very puzzling."
the puzzle be this, that for three week after this eventful discovery Blanche have be only too eager about her dear Arthur; be urge, as strongly as so much modesty could urge, the completion of the happy arrangement which be to make her Arthur's for ever; and now it seem as if something have interfere to mar these happy arrangement--as if Arthur poor be not quite so agreeable to Blanche as Arthur rich and a member of Parliament--as if there be some mystery. at last she say:
"Tunbridge Wells be not very far off, be it, Arthur? hadnot you well go and see she?"
they have be in town a week, and neither have think of that simple plan before!
CHAPTER LXXIV
show how Arthur have well have take a return-ticket
the train carry Arthur only too quickly to Tunbridge, though he have time to review all the circumstance of his life as he make the brief journey; and to acknowledge to what sad conclusion his selfishness and waywardness have lead he. "here be the end of hope and aspiration," think he, "of romance and ambition! where I yield or where I be obstinate, I be alike unfortunate; my mother implore I, and I refuse an angel! say I have take she; force on I as she be, Laura would never have be an angel to I. I could not have give she my heart at another's instigation; I never could have know she as she be have I be oblige to ask another to interpret her quality and point out her virtue. I yield to my uncle's solicitation, and accept on his guarantee Blanche, and a seat in Parliament, and wealth, and ambition, and a career; and see!--fortune come and leave I the wife without the dowry, which I have take in compensation of a heart. why be I not more honest, or be I not less so? it would have cost my poor old uncle no pang to accept Blanche's fortune whencesoever it come; he cannot even understand, he be bitterly indignant, heart-stricken, almost, at the scruple which actuate I in refuse it. I dissatisfy everybody. a maim, weak, imperfect wretch, it seem as if I be unequal to any fortune. I neither make myself nor any one connect with I happy. what prospect be there for this poor little frivolous girl, who be to take my obscure name and share my fortune? I have not even ambition to excite I, or self-esteem enough to console myself, much more she, for my failure. if I be to write a book that should go through twenty edition, why, I should be the very first to sneer at my reputation. say I could succeed at the Bar, and achieve a fortune by bully witness and twist evidence; be that a fame which would satisfy my longing, or a calling in which my life would be well spend? how I wish I could be that priest opposite, who never have lift his eye from his breviary, except when we be in Reigate tunnel, when he could not see; or that old gentleman next he, who scowl at he with eye of hatred over his newspaper. the priest shut his eye to the world, but have his thought on the book, which be his directory to the world to come. his neighbour hat he as a monster, tyrant, persecutor, and fancy burn martyr, and that pale countenance look on, and light up by the flame. these have no doubt; these march on trustfully, bear their load of logic."
"would you like to look at the paper, sir?" here interpose the stout gentleman (it have a flame article against the order of the black-coat gentleman who be travel with they in the carriage), and Pen thank he and take it, and pursue his reverie, without read two sentence of the journal.
"and yet, would you take either of those man's creed, with its consequence?" he think. "ah I! you must bear your own burthen, fashion your own faith, think your own thought, and pray your own prayer. to what mortal ear could I tell all, if I have a mind? or who could understand all? who can tell another's shortcoming, lose opportunity, weigh the passion which overpower, the defect which incapacitate reason?--what extent of truth and right his neighbour's mind be organise to perceive and to do?--what invisible and forgotten accident, terror of youth, chance or mischance of fortune, may have alter the whole current of life? a grain of sand may alter it, as the flinging of a pebble may end it. who can weigh circumstance, passion, temptation, that go to our good and evil account, save one, before whose awful wisdom we kneel, and at whose mercy we ask absolution? here it end," think Pen; "this day or to-morrow will wind up the account of my youth; a weary retrospect, alas! a sad history, with many a page I would fain not look back on! but who have not be tired or fall, and who have escape without scar from that struggle?" and his head fall on his breast, and the young man's heart prostrate itself humbly and sadly before that throne where sit wisdom, and love, and pity for all, and make its confession. "what matter about fame or poverty!" he think. "if I marry this woman I have choose, may I have strength and will to be true to she, and to make she happy. if I have child, pray God teach I to speak and to do the truth among they, and to leave they an honest name. there be no splendour for my marriage. do my life deserve any? I begin a new phase of it; a well than the last may it be, I pray Heaven!"
the train stop at Tunbridge as Pen be make these reflection; and he hand over the newspaper to his neighbour, of whom he take leave, while the foreign clergyman in the opposite corner still sate with his eye on his book. Pen jump out of the carriage then, his carpet-bag in hand, and briskly determined to face his fortune.
a fly carry he rapidly to Lady Clavering's house from the station; and, as he be transport thither, Arthur compose a little speech, which he intend to address to Blanche, and which be really as virtuous, honest, and well-minded an oration as any man of his turn of mind, and under his circumstance, could have utter. the purport of it was--"Blanche, I cannot understand from your last letter what your meaning be, or whether my fair and frank proposal to you be acceptable or no. I think you know the reason which induce I to forgo the worldly advantage which a union with you offer, and which I could not accept without, as I fancy, be dishonour. if you doubt of my affection, here I be ready to prove it. let Smirke be call in, and let we be marry out of hand; and with all my heart I purpose to keep my vow, and to cherish you through life, and to be a true and a love husband to you."
from the fly Arthur spring out then to the hall-door, where he be meet by a domestic whom he do not know. the man seem to be surprised at the approach of the gentleman with the carpet-bag, which he make no attempt to take from Arthur's hand. "her ladyshipbe not at home, sir," the man remark.
"I be Mr. Pendennis," Arthur say. "where be Lightfoot?"
"Lightfoot be go," answer the man. "my Lady be out, and my order was----"
"I hear Miss Amory's voice in the drawing-room," say Arthur. "take the bag to a dressing-room, if you please;" and, pass by the porter, he walk straight towards that apartment, from which, as the door open, a warble of melodious note issue.
our little Siren be at her piano sing with all her might and fascination. Master Clavering be asleep on the sofa, indifferent to the music; but near Blanche sit a gentleman who be perfectly enrapture with her strain, which be of a passionate and melancholy nature.
as the door open, the gentleman start up with Hullo! the music stop, with a little shriek from the singer; Frank Clavering wake up from the sofa, and Arthur come forward and say, "what, Foker! how do you do, Foker?" he look at the piano, and there, by Miss Amory's side, be just such another purple-leather box as he have see in Harry's hand three day before, when the heir of Logwood be come out of a jeweller's shop in Waterloo Place. it be open, and curl round the white satin cushion within be, oh, such a magnificent serpentine bracelet, with such a blaze ruby head and diamond tail!
"how de-do, Pendennis?" say Foker. Blanche make many motion of the shoulder, and give sign of unrest and agitation. and she put her handkerchief over the bracelet, and then she advance, with a hand which tremble very much, to greet Pen.
"how be dear Laura?" she say. the face of Foker look up from his profound mourning--that face, so piteous and puzzle, be one which the reader's imagination must depict for himself; also that of Master Frank Clavering, who, look at the three interesting individual with an expression of the utmost knowingness, have only time to ejaculate the word, "herebe a jolly go!" and to disappear snigger.
Pen, too, have restrain himself up to that minute; but look still at Foker, whose ear and cheek tingle with blush, Arthur burst out into a fit of laughter, so wild and loud, that it frighten Blanche much more than any the most serious exhibition.
"and this be the secret, be it? donot blush and turn away, Foker, my boy. why, man, you be a pattern of fidelity. could I stand between Blanche and such constancy--could I stand between Miss Amory and fifteen thousand a year?"
"it be not that, Mr. Pendennis," Blanche say, with great dignity. "it be not money, it be not rank, it be not gold that move I; but it be constancy, it be fidelity, it be a whole trustful love heart offer to I, that I treasure--yes, that I treasure!" and she make for her handkerchief, but, reflect what be underneath it, she pause. "I do not disown, I do not disguise--my life be above disguise--to he on whom it be bestow, my heart must be for ever bare--that I once think I love you,--ye, think I be belove by you, I own! how I cling to that faith! how I strove, I pray, I long to believe it! but your conduct always--your own word so cold, so heartless, so unkind, have undeceive I. you trifle with the heart of the poor maiden! you fling I back with scorn the troth which I have plight! I have explain all--all to Mr. Foker."
"that you have," say Foker, with devotion, and conviction in his look.
"what, all?" say Pen, with a meaning look at Blanche. "it be I be in fault, be it? well, well, Blanche, be it so. I willnot appeal against your sentence, and bear it in silence. I come down here look to very different thing, Heaven know, and with a heart most truly and kindly disposed towards you. I hope you may be happy with another, as, on my word, it be my wish to make you so; and I hope my honest old friend here will have a wife worthy of his loyalty, his constancy, and affection. indeed they deserve the regard of any woman--even Miss Blanche Amory. shake hand, Harry; donot look askance at I. have anybody tell you that I be a false and heartless character?"
"I think yoube a----" Foker be begin, in his wrath, when Blanche interpose.
"Henry, not a word!--i pray you let there be forgiveness!"
"yoube an angel, by Jove, yoube an angel!" say Foker, at which Blanche look seraphically up to the chandelier.
"in spite of what have pass, for the sake of what have pass, I must always regard Arthur as a brother," the seraph continue; "we have know each other year, we have tread the same field, and pluck the same flower together. Arthur! Henry! I beseech you to take hand and to be friend! Forgive you!--I forgive you, Arthur, with my heart I do. should I not do so for make I so happy?"
"there be only one person of we three whom I pity, Blanche," Arthur say, gravely, "and I say to you again, that I hope you will make this good fellow, this honest and loyal creature, happy."
"happy! o Heavens!" say Harry. he could not speak. his happiness gush out at his eye. "she donot know--she cannot know how fond I be of she, and--and who be I? a poor little beggar, and she take I up and say shewill try and I--I--love I. I benot worthy of so much happiness. give we your hand, old boy, since she forgive you after your heartless conduct, and say she love you. Iwill make you welcome. I tell you Iwill love everybody who love she. By---, if she tell I to kiss the ground Iwill kiss it. tell I to kiss the ground! I say, tell I. I love you so. you see I love you so."
Blanche look up seraphically again. her gentle bosom heave. she hold out one hand as if to bless Harry, and then royally permit he to kiss it. she take up the pocket-handkerchief and hide her own eye, as the other fair hand be abandon to poor Harry's tearful embrace.
"I swear that be a villain who deceive such a love creature as that," say Pen.
Blanche lay down the handkerchief, and put hand no. 2 softly on Foker's head, which be bent down kiss and weep over hand no. 1. "foolish boy?" she say, "it shall be love as it deserve: who could help love such a silly creature!"
and at this moment Frank Clavering break in upon the sentimental trio.
"I say, Pendennis!" he say.
"well, Frank!"
"the man want to be pay, and go back. hebe have some beer."
"Iwill go back with he," cry Pen. "good-bye, Blanche. God bless you, Foker, old friend. you know, neither of you want I here." he long to be off that instant.
"Stay--I must say one word to you. one word in private, if you please," Blanche say. "you can trust we together, cannot you, Henry?" the tone in which the word Henry be speak, and the appeal, ravish Foker with delight. "trust you!" say he. "oh, who wouldnot trust you! come along, Franky, my boy."
"letus have a cigar," say Frank, as they go into the hall.
"she donot like it," say Foker, gently.
"Law bless you--she donot mind. Pendennis use to smoke regular," say the candid youth.
"it be but a short word I have to say," say Blanche to Pen, with great calm, when they be alone. "you never love I, Mr. Pendennis."
"I tell you how much," say Arthur. "I never deceive you."
"I suppose you will go back and marry Laura," continue Blanche.
"be that what you have to say?" say Pen.
"you be go to she this very night, I be sure of it. there be no deny it. you never care for I."
"et vous?"
"et moi, c'est different. I have be spoil early. I cannot live out of the world, out of excitement. I could have do so, but it be too late. if I cannot have emotion, I must have the world. you would offer I neither one nor the other. you be blase in everything, even in ambition. you have a career before you, and you would not take it. you give it up!-- for what?--for a betise, for an absurd scruple. why would you not have that seat, and be such a puritain? why should you refuse what be mine by right, by right, entendez-vous?"
"you know all, then?" say Pen.
"only within a month. but I have suspect ever since Baymouth--n'importe since when. it be not too late. he be as if he have never be; and there be a position in the world before you yet. why not sit in Parliament, exert your talent, and give a place in the world to yourself, to your wife? I take celui-la. Il est bon. il est riche. Il est--vous le connaissez autant que moi enfin. think you that I would not prefer un homme qui fera parler de moi? if the secret appear I be rich a million. how do it affect I? it be not my fault. it will never appear."
"you will tell Harry everything, willnot you?"
"Je comprend. vous refusez," say Blanche, savagely. "I will tell Harry at my own time, when we be married. you will not betray I, will you? you, have a defenceless girl's secret, will not turn upon she and use it? S'il I plait de le cacher, mon secret; pourquoi le donnerai je? Je l'aime, mon pauvre pere, voyez-vous? I would rather live with that man than with you fade intriguer of the world. I must have emotion--it m'en donne. Il m'ecrit. Il ecrit tres-bien, voyez-vous--comme un pirate-- comme un Bohemien--comme un homme. but for this I would have say to my mother--Ma mere! quitton ce lache mari, cette lache societe--retournon a mon pere."
"the pirate would have weary you like the rest," say Pen.
"eh! il I faut des emotion," say Blanche. Pen have never see she or know so much about she in all the year of their intimacy as he see and know now: though he see more than exist in reality. for this young lady be not able to carry out any emotion to the full; but have a sham enthusiasm, a sham hatred, a sham love, a sham taste, a sham grief, each of which flare and shine very vehemently for an instant, but subside and give place to the next sham emotion.
CHAPTER LXXV
a chapter of match-making
upon the platform at Tunbridge, Pen fume and fret until the arrival of the evening train to London, a full half-hour,--six hour it seem to he; but even this immense interval be pass, the train arrive, the train speed on, the London light come in view--a gentleman who forget his carpet-bag in the train rush at a cab, and say to the man, "drive as hard as you can go to Jermyn Street." the cabman, although a hansom-cabman, say thank you for the gratuity which be put into his hand, and Pen run up the stair of the hotel to Lady Rockminster's apartment. Laura be alone in the drawing-room, reading, with a pale face, by the lamp. the pale face look up when Pen open the door. may we follow he? the great moment of life be but moment like the other. your doom be speak in a word or two. a single look from the eye; a mere pressure of the hand may decide it; or of the lip, though they cannot speak.
when Lady Rockminster, who have have her after-dinner nap, get up and go into her sitting-room, we may enter with her ladyship.
"upon my word, young people!" be the first word she say, and her attendant make wonder eye over her shoulder. and well may she say so; and well may the attendant cast wonder eye; for the young people be in an attitude; and Pen in such a position as every young lady who read this have hear tell of, or have see, or hope, or at any rate deserve to see.
in a word, directly he enter the room, Pen go up to Laura of the pale face, who have not time even to say, what, back so soon? and seize she outstretched and tremble hand just as she be rise from her chair, fall down on his knee before she, and say quickly, "I have see she. she have engage herself to Harry Foker--and--and now, Laura?"
the hand give a pressure--the eye beam a reply--the quivering lip answer, though speechless. Pen's head sink down in the girl's lap, as he sob out, "come and bless we, dear mother," and arm as tender as Helen's once more enfold he.
in this juncture it be that Lady Rockminster come in and say, "upon my word, young people! Beck! leave the room. what do you want poke your nose in here?"

"he act nobly," Laura say.
"I act as she bid I," say Pen. "Hm, hm," reply the old lady to this, look with rather an appeased air at the young people. "it be all very well; but I should have prefer Bluebeard."
and now Pen, to divert the conversation from a theme which be grow painful to some party present, bethink he of his interview with Huxter in the morning, and of Fanny Bolton's affair, which he have forget under the immediate pressure and excitement of his own. and he tell the lady how Huxter have elevate Fanny to the rank of wife, and what terror he be in respect the arrival of his father. he describe the scene with considerable humour, take care to dwell especially upon that part of it which concern Fanny's coquetry and irrepressible desire of captivate mankind; his meaning being, "you see, Laura, I be not so guilty in that little affair; it be the girl who make love to I, and I who resist. as I be no long present, the little siren practise her art and fascination upon other. let that transaction be forget in your mind, if you please; or visit I with a very gentle punishment for my error."
Laura understand his meaning under the eagerness of his explanation. "if you do any wrong, you repent, dear Pen," she say; "and you know," she add, with mean eye and blush, "that I have no right to reproach you."
"Hm!" grumble the old lady; "I should have prefer Bluebeard."
"the past be break away. the morrow be before we. she move with a different step, and bloom with a new beauty. Arthur see the change; and the old Lady Rockminster remark it with her shrewd eye.

Arthur and Laura beg for ten minute more.

with the early morning come Laura and Martha accord to appointment; and the desire sensation be, let we hope, effect in Lamb Court, whence the three proceed to wait upon Mr. and Mrs. Samuel Huxter, at their residence in Charterhouse Lane.

return homeward through Fleet Street, and as Laura be just state to Pen's infinite amusement that Fanny be very well, but that really there be no beauty in her,--there might be, but she could not see it,-- as they be lock near Temple Bar, they see young Huxter return to his bride.
