CHAPTER LXXVI
Exeunt Omnes
our character be all a month old than they be when the last-describe adventure and conversation occur, and a great number of the personage of our story have chance to reassemble at the little country town where we be first introduce to they. Frederic Lightfoot, formerly maitre d'hotel in the service of Sir Francis Clavering, of Clavering Park, Bart., have beg leave to inform the nobility and gentry of ------shire that he have take that well-know and comfortable hotel, the Clavering Arms, in Clavering, where he hop for the continue patronage of the gentleman and family of the county. "this ancient and well-establish house," Mr. Lightfoot's manifesto state, "have be repair and decorate in a style of the great comfort. gentleman hunt with the Dumplingbeare hound will find excellent stabling and loose-box for horse at the Clavering Arms. a commodious billiard-room have be attach to the hotel, and the cellar have be furnish with the choice wine and spirit, select, without regard to expense, by C. L. commercial gentleman will find the Clavering arm a most comfortable place of resort: and the scale of charge have be regulate for all, so as to meet the economical spirit of the present time."
indeed, there be a considerable air of liveliness about the old inn. the clavering arm have be splendidly repaint over the gateway. the coffee-room window be bright and fresh, and decorate with Christmas holly; the magistrate have meet in petty session in the card-room of the old Assembly. the farmer' ordinary be hold as of old, and frequent by increase number, who be pleased with Mrs. Lightfoot's cuisine. her indian curry and Mulligatawny soup be especially popular: Major Stokes, the respected tenant of Fairoaks Cottage, Captain Glanders, H.P., and other resident gentry, have pronounce in their favour, and have partake of they more than once both in private and at the dinner of the Clavering Institute, attendant on the incorporation of the reading-room, and when the chief inhabitant of that flourish little town meet together and do justice to the hostess's excellent cheer. the chair be take by Sir Francis Clavering, Bart., support by the esteemed rector, Dr. Portman; the vice chair be ably fill by Barker, Esq. (support by the Rev. J. Simcoe and the Rev. S. Jowls), the enterprise head of the ribbon factory in Clavering, and chief director of the Clavering and Chatteris Branch of the Great Western Railway, which will be open in another year, and upon the work of which the engineer and workman be now busily engage.
"an interesting event, which be likely to take place in the life of our talented townsman, Arthur Pendennis, Esq., have, we understand, cause he to relinquish the intention which he have of offer himself as a candidate for our borough: and rumour whisper" (say the Chatteris Champion, Clavering Agriculturist, and Baymouth fisherman,--that independent county paper, so distinguished for its unswerve principle and loyalty to the british oak, and so eligible a medium for advertisements)--rumour state, say the C. C. C. A. and B. F., "that should Sir Francis Clavering's fail health oblige he to relinquish his seat in Parliament, he will vacate it in favour of a young gentleman of colossal fortune and relate to the high aristocracy of the empire, who be about to contract a matrimonial alliance with an accomplished and lovely lady, connect by the near tie with the respected family at Clavering Park. Lady Clavering and Miss Amory have arrive at the Park for the Christmas holiday; and we understand that a large number of the aristocracy be expect, and that festivity of a peculiarly interesting nature will take place there at the commencement of the new year."
the ingenious reader will be enable, by the help of the above announcement, to understand what have take place during the little break which have occur in our narrative. although Lady Rockminster grumble a little at Laura's preference for Pendennis over Bluebeard, those who be aware of the latter's secret will understand that the young girl could make no other choice, and the kind old lady who have constitute herself Miss Bell's guardian be not ill pleased that she be to fulfil the great purpose in life of young lady and marry. she inform she maid of the interesting event that very night, and of course Mrs. Beck, who be perfectly aware of every single circumstance, and keep by Martha, of Fairoaks, in the full knowledge of what be pass, be immensely surprised and delighted. "Mr. Pendennis's income be so much; the railroad will give he so much more, he state; Miss Bell have so much, and may probably have a little more one day. for person in their degree, they will be able to manage very well. and I shall speak to my nephew Pynsent, who I suspect be once rather attach to her,--but of course that be out of the question ('oh! of course, my lady; I should think so indeed!')--not that you know anything whatever about it, or have any business to think at all on the subject,--I shall speak to George Pynsent, who be now chief secretary of the Tape and Sealing Wax Office, and have Mr. Pendennis make something. and, Beck, in the morning you will carry down my compliment to major Pendennis, and say that I shall pay he a visit at one o'clock."--"Yes," mutter the old lady, "the Major must be reconcile, and he must leave his fortune to Laura's child."
accordingly, at one o'clock, the Dowager Lady Rockminster appear at Major Pendennis's, who be delighted, as may be imagine, to receive so noble a visitor. the Major have be prepare, if not for the news which her ladyship be about to give he, at least with the intelligence that Pen's marriage with Miss Amory be break off. the young gentleman bethink he of his uncle, for the first time that day it must be own, and meet his new servant in the hall of the hotel, ask after the Major's health from Mr. Frosch; and then go into the coffee-room of the hotel, where he write a half-dozen line to acquaint his guardian with what have occur. "dear uncle," he say, "if there have be any question between we, it be over now. I go to Tunbridge Wells yesterday, and find that somebody else have carry off the prize about which we be hesitate. Miss A., without any compunction for I, have bestow herself upon Harry Foker, with his fifteen thousand a year. I come in suddenly upon their love, and find and leave he in possession.
"and youwill be glad to hear, Tatham write I, that he have sell three of my field at Fairoaks to the Railroad Company, at a great figure. I will tell you this, and more when we meet; and be always your affectionate, --A. P."
"I think I be aware of what you be about to tell I," the Major say, with a most courtly smile and bow to Pen's ambassadress. "it be a very great kindness of your ladyship to think of bring I the news. how well you look! how very good you be! how very kind you have always be to that young man!"
"it be for the sake of his uncle," say Lady Rockminster, most politely.
"he have inform I of the state of affair, and write I a nice note, --ye, a nice note," continue the old gentleman; "and I find he have have an increase to his fortune,--ye; and, all thing consider, I donot much regret that this affair with Miss Amory be manquee, though I wish for it once, in fact, all thing consider, I be very glad of it."
"we must console he, Major Pendennis," continue the lady; "we must get he a wife." the truth then come across the Major's mind, and he see for what purpose Lady Rockminster have choose to assume the office of ambassadress.
it be not necessary to enter into the conversation which ensue, or to tell at any length how her ladyship conclude a negotiation which, in truth, be tolerably easy. there could be no reason why Pen should not marry accord to his own and his mother's wish; and as for Lady Rockminster, she support the marriage by intimation which have very great weight with the Major, but of which we shall say nothing, as her ladyship (now, of course, much advanced in year) be still alive, and the family might be angry; and, in fine, the old gentleman be quite overcome by the determined graciousness of the lady, and her fondness for Laura. nothing, indeed, could be more bland and kind than Lady Rockminster's whole demeanour, except for one moment when the Major talk about his boy throw himself away, at which her ladyship break out into a little speech, in which she make the major understand, what poor Pen and his friend acknowledge very humbly, that Laura be a thousand time too good for he. Laura be fit to be the wife of a king,--Laura be a paragon of virtue and excellence. and it must be say, that when Major Pendennis find that a lady of the rank of the Countess of Rockminster seriously admire Miss Bell, he instantly begin to admire her himself.
so that when Herr Frosch be request to walk upstairs to Lady Rockminster's apartment, and inform Miss Bell and Mr. Arthur Pendennis that the Major would receive they, and Laura appear blush and happy as she hang on Pen's arm, the Major give a shaky hand to one and the other, with unaffected emotion and cordiality, and then go through another salutation to Laura, which cause she to blush still more. happy blush! bright eye beam with the light of love! the story-teller turn from this group to his young audience, and hop that one day their eye may all shine so.
Pen have retreat in the most friendly manner, and the lovely Blanche have bestow her young affection upon a blushing bridegroom with fifteen thousand a year, there be such an outbreak of happiness in Lady Clavering's heart and family as the good Begum have not know for many a year, and she and Blanche be on the most delightful term of cordiality and affection. the ardent Foker press onwards the happy day, and be as anxious as might be expect to abridge the period of mourning which have put he in possession of so many charm and amiable quality, of which he have be only, as it be, the heir-apparent, not the actual owner, until then. the gentle Blanche, everything that her affianced lord could desire, be not averse to gratify the wish of her fond Henry. Lady Clavering come up from Tunbridge. milliner and jeweller be set to work and engage to prepare the delightful paraphernalia of Hymen. Lady Clavering be in such a good humour, that Sir Francis even benefit by it, and such a reconciliation be effect between this pair, that Sir Francis come to London, sate at the head of his own table once more, and appear tolerably flush of money at his billiard-room and gambling-house again. one day, when Major Pendennis and Arthur go to dine in Grosvenor Place, they find an old acquaintance establish in the quality of major-domo, and the gentleman in black, who, with perfect politeness and gravity, offer they their choice of sweet or dry champagne, be no other than Mr. James Morgan. the Chevalier Strong be one of the party; he be in high spirit and condition, and entertain the company with account of his amusement abroad.
"it be my Lady who invite I," say Strong to Arthur, under his voice-- "that fellow Morgan look as black as thunder when I come in. he be about no good here. I will go away first, and wait for you and Major Pendennis at Hyde Park Gate."
Mr. Morgan help Major Pendennis to his great-coat when he be quit the house; and mutter something about have accept a temporary engagement with the Clavering family.
"I have get a paper of your, Mr. Morgan," say the old gentleman.
"which you can show, if you please, to Sir Francis, sir, and perfectly welcome," say Mr. Morgan, with downcast eye. "Ibe very much oblige to you, Major Pendennis, and if I can pay you for all your kindness I will."
Arthur overhear the sentence, and see the look of hatred which accompany it, suddenly cry out that he have forget his handkerchief, and run upstairs to the drawing-room again. Foker be still there; still linger about his siren. Pen give the siren a look full of meaning, and we suppose that the siren understand mean look, for when, after find the veracious handkerchief of which he come in quest, he once more go out, the siren, with a laugh voice, say, "oh, Arthur--Mr. Pendennis--I want you to tell dear Laura something!" and she come out to the door.
"what be it?" she ask, shut the door.
"have you tell Harry? do you know that villain Morgan know all?"
"I know it," she say.
"have you tell Harry?"
"no, no," she say. "you willnot betray I?"
"Morgan will," say Pen.
"no, he willnot," say Blanche. "I have promise he--n'importe. wait until after our marriage--oh, until after our marriage--oh, how wretched I be," say the girl, who have be all smile, and grace, and gaiety during the evening.
Arthur say, "I beg and implore you to tell Harry. tell he now. it be no fault of your. he will pardon you anything. tell he to-night."
"and give she this--Il est la--with my love, please; and I beg your pardon for call you back; and if she will be at Madame Crinoline's at half-past three, and if Lady Rockminster can spare she, I should so like to drive with she in the park;" and she go in, sing and kiss her little hand, as Morgan the velvet-footed come up the carpet stair.
Pen hear Blanche's piano break out into brilliant music as he go down to join his uncle; and they walk away together. Arthur briefly tell he what he have do. "what be to be do?" he ask.
"what be to be do, begad?" say the old gentleman. "what be to be do but to leave it alone? Begad, let we be thankful," say the old fellow, with a shudder, "that we be out of the business, and leave it to those it concern."
"I hope to Heaven shewill tell he," say Pen.
"Begad, shewill take her own course," say the old man. "Miss Amory be a dev'lish wide-awake girl, sir, and must play her own card; and Ibe doosid glad you be out of it--doosid glad, begad. whobe this smoking? oh, itbe Mr. Strong again. he want to put in his oar, I suppose. I tell you, donot meddle in the business, Arthur."
Strong begin once or twice, as if to converse upon the subject, but the Major would not hear a word. he remark on the moonlight on Apsley House, the weather, the cabstand--anything but that subject. he bow stiffly to Strong, and clung to his nephew's arm, as he turn down St. James's Street, and again caution Pen to leave the affair alone. "it have like to have cost you so much, sir, that you may take my advice," he say.
when Arthur come out of the hotel, Strong's cloak and cigar be visible a few door off. the jolly Chevalier laugh as they meet. "Ibe an old soldier, too," he say. "I want to talk to you, Pendennis. I have hear of all that have happen, and all the chop and change that have take place during my absence. I congratulate you on your marriage, and I congratulate you on your escape, too,--you understand I. it be not my business to speak, but I know this, that a certain party be as arrant a little--well--well, never mind what. you act like a man and a trump, and be well out of it."
"I have no reason to complain," say Pen. "I go back to beg and entreat poor Blanche to tell Foker all: I hope, for her sake, she will; but I fear not. there be but one policy, Strong, there be but one."
"and lucky he that can stick to it," say the Chevalier. "that rascal Morgan mean mischief. he have be lurk about our chamber for the last two month: he have find out that poor mad devil Amory's secret. he have be try to discover where he be: he have be pump Mr. Bolton, and make old Costigan drunk several time. he bribe the Inn porter to tell he when we come back: and he have get into Clavering's service on the strength of his information. he will get very good pay for it, mark my word, the villain."
"where be amory?" ask Pen.
"at Boulogne, I believe. I leave he there, and warn he not to come back. I have break with he, after a desperate quarrel, such as one might have expect with such a madman. and Ibe glad to think that he be in my debt now, and that I have be the mean of keep he out of more harm than one."
"he have lose all his winning, I suppose," say Pen.
"no: he be rather well than when he go away, or be a fortnight ago. he have extraordinary luck at Baden: break the bank several night, and be the fable of the place. he lie himself there with a fellow by the name of Bloundell, who gather about he a society of all sort of sharper, male and female, Russians, Germans, French, English. Amory get so insolent, that I be oblige to thrash he one day within an inch of his life. I couldnot help myself; the fellow have plenty of pluck, and I have nothing for it but to hit out."
"and do he call you out?" say Pen.
"you think if I have shoot he I should have do nobody any harm? no, sir; I wait for his challenge, but it never come and the next time I meet he he beg my pardon, and say, 'strong, I beg your pardon; you whop I and you serve I right.' I shake hand: but I couldnot live with he after that. I pay he what I owe he the night before," say Strong with a blush, "I pawn everything to pay he, and then I go with my last ten florin, and have a shy at the roulette. if I have lose, I should have let he shoot I in the morning. I be weary of my life. by Jove, sir, benot it a shame that a man like I, who may have have a few bill out, but who never desert a friend, or do an unfair action, shouldnot be able to turn his hand to anything to get bread? I make a good night, sir, at roulette, and Ihave do with that. Ibe go into the wine business. my wife's relation live at Cadiz. I intend to bring over spanish wine and ham; therebe a fortune to be make by it, sir,--a fortune--herebe my card. if you want any sherry or ham, recollect Ned Strong be your man." and the Chevalier pull out a handsome card, state that Strong and Company, Shepherd's Inn, be sole agent of the celebrate Diamond Manzanilla of the Duke of Garbanzos, Grandee of Spain of the First Class; and of the famous Toboso ham, feed on acorn only in the country of Don Quixote. "come and taste them, sir,--come and try them at my chamber. you see, Ihave an eye to business, and by Jove this time Iwill succeed."
Pen laugh as he take the card. "I donot know whether I shall be allow to go to bachelor' party," he say. "you know Ibe go to----"
"but you must have sherry, sir. you must have sherry."
"I will have it from you, depend on it," say the other. "and I think you be very well out of your other partnership. that worthy Altamont and his daughter correspond, I hear," Pen add after a pause.
"yes; she write he the long rigmarole letter, that I use to read: the sly little devil; and he answer under cover to Mrs. Bonner. he be for carry she off the first day or two, and nothing would content he but have back his child. but she donot want to come, as you may fancy; and he be not very eager about it." here the Chevalier burst out in a laugh. "why, sir, do you know what be the cause of our quarrel and boxing match? there be a certain widow at Baden, a Madame la Baronne de la Cruche-cassee, who be not much well than himself, and whom the scoundrel want to marry; and would, but that I tell she he be marry already. I donot think that she be much well than he be. I see she on the pier at Boulogne the day I come to England."
and now we have bring up our narrative to the point, whither the announcement in the Chatteris Champion have already conduct we.
it want but very, very few day before that blissful one when Foker should call Blanche his own; the Clavering folk have all press to see the most splendid new carriage in the whole world, which be stand in the coach-house at the Clavering Arms; and show, in grateful return for drink, commonly, by Mr. Foker's head-coachman. Madame Fribsby be occupy in make some lovely dress for the tenant' daughter, who be to figure as a sort of bridesmaid' chorus at the breakfast and marriage ceremony. and immense festivity be to take place at the Park upon this delightful occasion.
"yes, Mr. Huxter, yes; a happy tenantry, its country's pride, will assemble in the baronial hall, where the beard will wag all. the ox shall be slay, and the cup theywill drain; and the bell shall peal quite genteel; and my father-in-law, with the tear of sensibility bedew his eye, shall bless we at his baronial porch. that shall be the order of proceeding, I think, Mr. Huxter; and I hope we shall see you and your lovely bride by her husband's side; and what will you please to drink, sir? Mrs. Lightfoot, madam, you will give to my excellent friend and body-surgeon, Mr. Huxter, Mr. Samuel Huxter, M.R.C.S., every refreshment that your hostel afford, and place the festive amount to my account; and Mr. Lightfoot, sir, what will you take? though youhave have enough already, I think; yes, ha."
so speak Harry Foker in the bar of the Clavering Arms. he have apartment at that hotel, and have gather a circle of friend round he there. he treat all to drink who come. he be hail-fellow with every man. he be so happy! he dance round Madame Fribsby, Mrs. Lightfoot's great ally, as she sate pensive in the bar. he console Mrs. Lightfoot, who have already begin to have cause of matrimonial disquiet; for the truth must be tell, that young Lightfoot, have now the full command of the cellar, have none over his own unbridled desire, and be tipple and tipsy from morning till night. and a piteous sight it be for his fond wife to behold the big youth reel about the yard and coffee-room, or drink with the farmer and tradesmen his own neat wine and carefully select stock of spirit.
when he could find time, Mr. Morgan the butler come from the Park, and take a glass at the expense of the landlord of the Clavering Arms. he watch poor Lightfoot's tipsy vagary with savage sneer. Mrs. Lightfoot feel always doubly uncomfortable when her unhappy spouse be under his comrade's eye. but a few month marry, and to think he have get to this! Madame Fribsby could feel for she. Madame Fribsby could tell her story of man every bit as bad. she have have her own woe too, and her sad experience of man. so it be that nobody seem happy altogether; and that there's bitter, as Mr. Foker remark, in the cup of every man's life. and yet there do not seem to be any in his, the honest young fellow! it be brim over with happiness and good-humour.
Mr. Morgan be constant in his attention to Foker. "and yet I donot like he somehow," say the candid young man to Mrs. Lightfoot. "he always seem as if he be measure I for my coffin somehow. pa-in-law's afraid of he; pa-in-law's," ahem! never mind, but ma-in-law's a trump, Mrs. Lightfoot."
"indeed my Lady be," and Mrs. Lightfoot own, with a sigh, that perhaps it have be well for she have she never leave her mistress.
"no, I do not like thee, Dr. Fell; the reason why I cannot tell," continue Mr. Foker; "and he want to be take as my head man. Blanche want I to take he. why do Miss Amory like he so?"
"do Miss Blanche like he so?" the notion seem to disturb Mrs. Lightfoot very much; and there come to this worthy landlady another cause for disturbance. a letter, bear the Boulogne postmark, be bring to her one morning, and she and her husband be quarrel over it as Foker pass down the stair by the bar, on his way to the Park. his custom be to breakfast there, and bask a while in the presence of Armida; then, as the company of Clavering tire he exceedingly, and he do not care for sporting, he would return for an hour or two to billiard and the society of the Clavering Arms; then it would be time to ride with Miss Amory, and, after din with she, he leave she and return modestly to his inn.
Lightfoot and his wife be quarrel over the letter. what be that letter from abroad? why be she always have letter from abroad? who write 'em?--he would know. he donot believe it be her brother. it be no business of his? it be a business of his; and, with a curse, he seize hold of his wife, and dash at her pocket for the letter.
the poor woman give a scream; and say, "well, take it." just as her husband seize on the letter, and Mr. Foker enter at the door, she give another scream at see he, and once more try to seize the paper. Lightfoot open it, shake she away, and an enclosure drop down on the breakfast-table.
"hand off, man alive!" cry little Harry, spring in. "donot lay hand on a woman, sir. the man that lay his hand upon a woman, save in the way of kindness, be a--hallo! itbe a letter for Miss Amory. whatbe this, Mrs. Lightfoot?"
Mrs. Lightfoot begin, in piteous tone of reproach to her husband,--"You unmanly! to treat a woman so who take you off the street. oh, you coward, to lay your hand upon your wife! why do I marry you? why do I leave my Lady for you? why do I spend eight hundred pound in fit up this house that you might drink and guzzle?"
"she get letter, and she willnot tell I who write letter," say Mr. Lightfoot, with a muzzy voice; "itbe a family affair, sir. will you take anything, sir?"
"I will take this letter to Miss Amory, as I be go to the Park," say Foker, turn very pale; and take it up from the table, which be arrange for the poor landlady's breakfast, he go away.
"hebe comin'--dammy, whobe a-comin'? whobe J. A., Mrs. Lightfoot--curse I, whobe J. a.?" cry the husband.
Mrs. Lightfoot cry out, "be quiet, you tipsy brute, do," and run to her bonnet and shawl, throw they on, see Mr. Foker walk down the street, take the by-lane which skirt it, and run as quickly as she could to the lodge-gate, Clavering Park. Foker see a running figure before he, but it be lose when he get to the lodge-gate. he stop and ask, "who be that who have just come in? Mrs. Bonner, be it?" he reel almost in his walk: the tree swam before he. he rest once or twice against the trunk of the naked lime.
Lady Clavering be in the breakfast-room with her son, and her husband yawn over his paper. "good morning, Harry," say the Begum. "here's letter, lot of letter; Lady Rockminster will be here on Tuesday instead of Monday, and Arthur and the Major come to-day; and Laura be to go to Dr. Portman's, and come to church from there: and--whatbe the matter, my dear? what make you so pale, Harry?"
"where be Blanche!" ask Harry, in a sickening voice--"not down yet?"
"blanche be always the last," say the boy, eat muffin; "shebe a regular dawdle, she be. when yoube not here, she lay in bed till lunch-time."
"be quiet, Frank," say the mother.
Blanche come down presently, look pale, and with rather an eager look towards Foker; then she advance and kiss her mother, and have a face beam with her very good smile on when she greet Harry.
"how do you do, sir?" she say, and put out both her hand.
"Ibe ill," answer Harry. "I--I've bring a letter for you, Blanche."
"a letter, and from whom be it, pray? voyon," she say.
"I donot know--I should like to know," say Foker.
"how can I tell until I see it?" ask Blanche.
"have Mrs. Bonner not tell you?" he say, with a shake voice;--"there's some secret. you give she the letter, Lady Clavering."
Lady Clavering, wonder, take the letter from poor Foker's shake hand, and look at the superscription. as she look at it, she too begin to shake in every limb, and with a scared face she drop the letter, and run up to Frank, clutch the boy to she, and burst out with a sob--"take that away--itbe impossible, itbe impossible."
"what be the matter?" cry Blanche, with rather a ghastly smile; "the letter be only from--from a poor pensioner and relative of ours."
"itbe not true, itbe not true," scream Lady Clavering. "no, my frank-- be it, Clavering?"
Blanche have take up the letter, and be move with it towards the fire, but Foker run to she and clutch her arm--"i must see that letter," he say; "give it I. you shallnot burn it."
"you--you shall not treat Miss Amory so in my house," cry the Baronet; "give back the letter, by Jove!"
"read it--and look at she," Blanche cry, point to her mother; "it-- it be for she I keep the secret! read it, cruel man!"
and Foker open and read the letter:--
"I have not write, my darling Betsy, this three week; but this be to give she a father's blessing, and I shall come down pretty soon as quick as my note, and intend to see the ceremony, and my son-in-law. I shall put up at Bonner's. I have have a pleasant autumn, and be stay here at an hotel where there be good company, and which be kep' in good style. I donot know whether I quite approve of your throw over Mr. P. for Mr. F., and donot think Foker's such a pretty name, and from your account of he he seem a muff, and not a beauty. but he have get the rowdy, which be the thing. so no more, my dear little Betsy, till we meet, from your affectionate father, J. Amory Altamont."
"read it, Lady Clavering; it be too late to keep it from you now," say poor Foker; and the distracted woman, have cast her eye over it, again break out into hysterical scream, and convulsively grasp her son.
"they have make an outcast of you, my boy," she say. "theyhave dishonour your old mother; but Ibe innocent, Frank; before God, Ibe innocent. I donot know this, Mr. Foker; indeed, indeed, I donot."
"Ibe sure you donot," say Foker, go up and kiss her hand.
"generous, generous Harry!" cry out Blanche, in an ecstasy. but he withdraw his hand, which be upon her side, and turn from she with a quivering lip. "thatbe different," he say.
"it be for her sake--for her sake, Harry." again Miss Amory be in an attitude.
"there be something to be do for mine," say Foker. "I would have take you, whatever you be. Everything's talk about in London. I know that your father have come to--to grief. you donot think it be--it be for your connexion I marry you? d--- it all! Ihave love you with all my heart and soul for two year, and youhave be play with I, and cheat I," break out the young man, with a cry. "oh, Blanche, Blanche, itbe a hard thing, a hard thing!" and he cover his face with his hand, and sob behind they.
Blanche think, "why donot I tell he that night when Arthur warn I?"
"donot refuse she, Harry," cry out Lady Clavering. "take she, take everything I have. itbe all her, you know, at my death. this boybe disinherit."--(Master Frank, who have be look as scar at the strange scene, here burst into a loud cry.) "take every shilling. give I just enough to live, and to go and hide my head with this child, and to fly from both. oh, theyhave both be bad, bad man. perhaps hebe here now. donot let I see he. clavering, you coward, defend I from he."
Clavering start up at this proposal. "you benot serious, Jemima? you donot mean that?" he say. "you willnot throw I and Frank over? I donot know it, so help I ---. Foker, Ihave no more idea of it than the dead-- until the fellow come and find I out, the d----d escape convict scoundrel."
"the what?" say Foker. Blanche give a scream.
"yes," scream out the Baronet in his turn, "yes, a d----d runaway convict--a fellow that forge his father-in-law's name--a d----d attorney, and kill a fellow in Botany Bay, hang he--and run into the Bush, curse he; I wish hehave die there. and he come to I, a good six year ago, and rob I; and Ihave be ruin myself to keep he, the infernal scoundrel! and Pendennis know it, and Strong know it, and that d----d Morgan know it, and she know it, ever so long; and I never would tell it, never: and I keep it from my wife."
"and you see he, and you donot kill he, Clavering, you coward?" say the wife of Amory. "come away, Frank; your fatherbe a coward. I be dishonour, but Ibe your old mother, and you'll--you'll love I, willnot you?"
Blanche, eploree, go up to her mother; but Lady Clavering shrank from she with a sort of terror. "donot touch I," she say; "youhave no heart; you never have. I see all now. I see why that coward be go to give up his place in Parliament to Arthur; yes, that coward! and why you threaten that you would make I give you half Frank's fortune. and when Arthur offer to marry you without a shilling, because he wouldnot rob my boy, you leave he, and you take poor Harry. have nothing to do with she, Harry. yoube good, you be. donot marry that--that convict's daughter. come away, Frank, my darling; come to your poor old mother. wewill hide ourselves; but webe honest, yes, we be honest."
all this while a strange feeling of exultation have take possession of Blanche's mind. that month with poor Harry have be a weary month to she. all his fortune and splendour scarcely suffice to make the idea of himself supportable. she be weary of his simple way, and sick of coax and cajole he.
"stay, mamma; stay, madam!" she cry out, with a gesture which be always appropriate, though rather theatrical; "I have no heart, have I? I keep the secret of my mother's shame. I give up my right to my half-brother and my bastard brother, yes, my right and my fortune. I donot betray my father, and for this I have no heart. Iwill have my right now, and the law of my country shall give they to I. I appeal to my country's law--yes, my country's law! the persecute one return this day. I desire to go to my father." and the little lady sweep round her hand, and think that she be a heroine.
"you will, will you?" cry out Clavering, with one of his usual oath. "Ibe a magistrate, and dammy, Iwill commit he. herebe a chaise come; perhaps itbe he. let he come."
a chaise be indeed come up the avenue; and the two woman shriek each their loudest, expect at that moment to see Altamont arrive.
the door open, and Mr. Morgan announce Major Pendennis and Mr. Pendennis, who enter, and find all party engage in this fierce quarrel. a large screen fence the breakfast-room from the hall; and it be probable that, accord to his custom, Mr. Morgan have take advantage of the screen to make himself acquaint with all that occur.
it have be arrange on the previous day that the young people should ride; and at the appoint hour in the afternoon, Mr. Foker's horse arrive from the Clavering Arms. but Miss Blanche do not accompany he on this occasion. Pen come out and shake hand with he on the door-step; and Harry Foker ride away, follow by his groom in mourning. the whole transaction which have occupy the most active part of our history be debate by the party concern during those two or three hour. many counsel have be give, story tell, and compromise suggest; and at the end, Harry Foker ride away, with a sad "God bless you!" from Pen. there be a dreary dinner at Clavering Park, at which the lately instal butler do not attend; and the lady be both absent. after dinner, Pen say, "I will walk down to Clavering and see if he be come." and he walk through the dark avenue, across the bridge and road by his own cottage,--the once quiet and familiar field of which be flame with the kiln and forge of the artificer employ on the new railroad work; and so he enter the town, and make for the Clavering Arms.
it be past midnight when he return to Clavering Park. he be exceedingly pale and agitated. "be Lady Clavering up yet?" he ask. yes, she be in her own sitting-room. he go up to she, and there find the poor lady in a piteous state of tear and agitation.
"it be I,--Arthur," he say, look in; and enter, he take her hand very affectionately and kiss it. "you be always the kind of friend to I, dear Lady Clavering," he say. "I love you very much. I have get some news for you."
"donot call I by that name," she say, press his hand. "you be always a good boy, Arthur; and itbe kind of you to come now,--very kind. you sometimes look very like your ma, my dear."
"dear good Lady Clavering," Arthur repeat, with particular emphasis, "something very strange have happen."
"have anything happen to he?" gasp Lady Clavering. "oh, itbe horrid to think I should be glad of it--horrid!"
"he be well. he have be and be go, my dear lady. donot alarm yourself;--he be go, and you be Lady Clavering still."
"be it true? what he sometimes say to I," she scream out,-- "that he----"
"he be marry before he marry you," say Pen. "he have confess it to-night. he will never come back." there come another shriek from Lady Clavering, as she fling her arm round Pen, and kiss he, and burst into tear on his shoulder.
what Pen have to tell, through a multiplicity of sob and interruption, must be compress briefly, for behold our prescribed limit be reach, and our tale be come to its end. with the Branch Coach from the railroad, which have succeed the old Alacrity and Perseverance, Amory arrive, and be set down at the Clavering Arms. he order his dinner at the place under his assumed name of Altamont; and, be of a jovial turn, he welcome the landlord, who be nothing loth, to a share of his wine. have extract from Mr. Lightfoot all the news regard the family at the Park, and find, from examine his host, that Mrs. Lightfoot, as she say, have keep his counsel, he call for more wine of Mr. Lightfoot; and at the end of this symposium, both, be greatly excited, go into Mrs. Lightfoot's bar.
she be there take tea with her friend, Madame Fribsby; and Lightfoot be by this time in such a happy state as not to be surprised at anything which might occur, so that, when Altamont shake hand with Mrs. Lightfoot as an old acquaintance, the recognition do not appear to he to be in the least strange, but only a reasonable cause for further drinking. the gentleman partook then of brandy-and-water, which they offer to the lady, not heed the terrified look of one or the other.
whilst they be so engaged, at about six o'clock in the evening, Mr. Morgan, Sir Francis Clavering's new man, come in, and be request to drink. he select his favourite beverage, and the party engage in general conversation.
after a while Mr. Lightfoot begin to doze. Mr. Morgan have repeatedly give hint to Mrs. Fribsby to quit the premise; but that lady, strangely fascinated, and terrify it would seem, or persuade by Mrs. Lightfoot not to go, keep her place. her persistence occasion much annoyance to Mr. Morgan, who vent his displeasure in such language as give pain to Mrs. Lightfoot, and cause Mr. Altamont to say, that he be a rum customer, and not polite to the sex.
the altercation between the two gentleman become very painful to the woman, especially to Mrs. Lightfoot, who do everything to soothe Mr. Morgan; and, under pretence of give a pipe-light to the stranger, she hand he a paper on which she have privily write the word, "he know you. go." there may have be something suspicious in her manner of handing, or in her guest's of reading, the paper; for when he get up a short time afterwards, and say he would go to bed, Morgan rise too, with a laugh, and say it be too early to go to bed.
the stranger then say he would go to his bedroom. Morgan say he would show he the way.
at this the guest say, "come up. Ihave get a brace of pistol up there to blow out the brain of any traitor or skulking spy," and glare so fiercely upon Morgan, that the latter, seize hold of Lightfoot by the collar, and wake he, say, "John Amory, I arrest you in the Queen's name. stand by I, Lightfoot. this capture be worth a thousand pound."
he put forward his hand as if to seize his prisoner, but the other, double his fist, give Morgan with his left hand so fierce a blow on the chest, that it knock he back behind Mr. Lightfoot. that gentleman, who be athletic and courageous, say he would knock his guest's head off, and prepare to do so, as the stranger, tear off his coat, and curse both of his opponent, roar to they to come on.
but with a pierce scream Mrs. Lightfoot fling herself before her husband, whilst with another and loud shriek Madame Fribsby run to the stranger, and call out "Armstrong, Johnny Armstrong!" seize hold of his naked arm, on which a blue tattooing of a heart and M. F. be visible.
the ejaculation of Madame Fribsby seem to astound and sober the stranger. he look down upon she, and cry out, "itbe polly, by Jove."
Mrs. Fribsby continue to exclaim, "this be not amory. this be Johnny Armstrong, my wicked--wicked husband, married to I in St. Martin's Church, mate on board an Indiaman, and he leave I two month after, the wicked wretch. this be John Armstrong--herebe the mark on his arm which he make for I."
the stranger say, "I be John Armstrong, sure enough, Polly. Ibe John Armstrong, Amory, Altamont--and let them all come on, and try what they can do against a british sailor. Hurray, whobe for it?"
Morgan still call out, "arrest he!" but Mrs. Lightfoot say, "arrest he! arrest you, you mean spy! what! stop the marriage and ruin my lady, and take away the Clavering Arms from we?"
"do he say hewould take away the Clavering Arms from we?" ask Mr. Lightfoot, turn round. "hang he, Iwill throttle he."
"keep he, darling, till the coach pass to the up train. itwill be here now directly."
"d--- he, Iwill choke he if he stir," say Lightfoot. and so they keep Morgan until the coach come, and Mr. Amory or Armstrong go away back to London.
Morgan have follow he: but of this event Arthur Pendennis do not inform Lady Clavering, and leave she invoke blessing upon he at her son's door, go to kiss he as he be asleep. it have be a busy day.
we have to chronicle the event of but one day more, and that be a day when Mr. Arthur, attire in a new hat, a new blue frock-coat and blue handkerchief, in a new fancy waistcoat, new boot, and new shirt-stud (present by the Right Honourable the Countess Dowager of Rockminster), make his appearance at a solitary breakfast-table, in Clavering Park, where he could scarce eat a single morsel of food. two letter be lay by his worship's plate; and he choose to open the first, which be in a round clerk-like hand, in preference to the second more familiar superscription.
note 1 run as follows:--
"Garbanzos Wine Company, Shepherd's Inn.--Monday.
"my dear Pendennis,--In congratulate you heartily upon the event which be to make you happy for life, I send my very kind remembrance to Mrs. Pendennis, whom I hope to know even long than I have already know she. and when I call her attention to the fact, that one of the most necessary article to her husband's comfort be pure sherry, I know I shall have she for a customer for your worship's sake.
"but I have to speak to you of other than my own concern. yesterday afternoon, a certain J. A. arrive at my chamber from Clavering, which he have leave under circumstance of which you be doubtless now aware. in spite of our difference, I could not but give he food and shelter (and he partook freely both of the Garbanzos Amontillado and the Toboso ham), and he tell I what have happen to he, and many other surprising adventure. the rascal marry at sixteen, and have repeatedly since perform that ceremony--in Sydney, in New Zealand, in South America, in Newcastle, he say, first, before he know our poor friend the milliner. he be a perfect Don Juan.
"and it seem as if the commendatore have at last overtaken he, for, as we be at our meal, there come three heavy knock at my outer door, which make our friend start. I have sustain a siege or two here, and go to my usual place to reconnoitre. thank my star I have not a bill out in the world, and besides, those gentry do not come in that way. I find that it be your uncle's late valet, Morgan, and a policeman (I think a sham policeman), and they say they have a warrant to take the person of John Armstrong, alias Amory, alias Altamont, a runaway convict, and threaten to break in the oak.
"now, sir, in my own day of captivity I have discover a little passage along the gutter into Bows and Costigan's window, and I send Jack Alias along this cover way, not without terror of his life, for it have grow very cranky; and then, after a parley, let in mon. Morgan and friend.
"the rascal have be instruct about that cover way, for he make for the room instantly, tell the policeman to go downstairs and keep the gate; and he charge up my little staircase as if he have know the premise. as he be go out of the window we hear a voice that you know, from Bows's garret, say, 'who be ye, and hwhat the divvle be ye at? youwould betther leave the gutther; bedad therebe a man kill himself already.'
"and as Morgan, cross over and look into the darkness, be try to see whether this awful news be true, he take a broomstick, and with a vigorous dash break down the pipe of communication--and tell I this morning, with great glee, that he be remind of that 'aisy sthratagem by remember his dorling Emilie, when she act the pawrt of Cora in the Plee--and by the bridge in Pezawro, bedad.' I wish that scoundrel Morgan have be on the bridge when the General try his 'sthratagem.'
"if I hear more of Jack Alias I will tell you. he have get plenty of money still, and I want he to send some to our poor friend the milliner; but the scoundrel laugh, and say he have no more than he want, but offer to give anybody a lock of his hair. Farewell--be happy! and believe I always truly your, E. Strong."
"and now for the other letter," say Pen. "dear old fellow!" and he kiss the seal before he break it.
"Warrington, Tuesday.
"I must not let the day pass over without say a God bless you, to both of you. may Heaven make you happy, dear Arthur, and dear Laura. I think, Pen, that you have the good wife in the world; and pray that, as such, you will cherish she and tend she. the chamber will be lonely without you, dear Pen; but if I be tired, I shall have a new home to go to in the house of my brother and sister. I be practise in the nursery here, in order to prepare for the part of Uncle George. Farewell! make your wedding tour, and come back to your affectionate G. W."
Pendennis and his wife read this letter together after Doctor Portman's breakfast be over, and the guest be go; and when the carriage be wait amidst the crowd at the Doctor's outer gate. but the wicket lead into the churchyard of St. Mary's, where the bell be peal with all their might, and it be here, over Helen's green grass, that Arthur show his wife George's letter. for which of those two--for grief be it or for happiness, that Laura's tear abundantly fall on the paper? and once more, in the presence of the sacred dust, she kiss and bless her Arthur.
there be only one marriage on that day at Clavering Church; for in spite of Blanche's sacrifice for her dear mother, honest Harry Foker could not pardon the woman who have deceive her husband, and justly argue that she would deceive he again. he go to the Pyramids and Syria, and there leave his malady behind he, and return with a fine beard, and a supply of tarboosh and nargillie, with which he regale all his friend. he live splendidly, and, through Pen's mediation, get his wine from the celebrate vintage of the Duke of Garbanzos.
as for poor cos, his fate have be mention in an early part of this story. no very glorious end could be expect to such a career. Morgan be one of the most respectable man in the parish of St. James's, and in the present political movement have pronounce himself like a man and a Briton. and Bows,--on the demise of Mr. Piper, who play the organ at Clavering, little Mrs. Sam Hunter, who have the entire command of Doctor Portman, bring bow down from London to contest the organ-loft, and her candidate carry the chair. when Sir Francis Clavering quit this worthless life, the same little indefatigable canvasser take the borough by storm, and it be now represent by Arthur Pendennis, Esq. Blanche Amory, it be well know, marry at Paris, and the saloon of Madame la Comtesse de Montmorenci de Valentinois be amongst the most suivis of that capital. the duel between the Count and the young and fiery Representative of the Mountain, Alcide de Mirobo, arise solely from the latter questioning at the Club the title bear by the former nobleman. Madame de Montmorenci de Valentinois travel after the adventure: and Bungay buy her poem, and publish they, with the Countess's coronet emblazon on the Countess's work.
Major Pendennis become very serious in his last day, and be never so happy as when Laura be read to he with her sweet voice, or listen to his story. for this sweet lady be the friend of the young and the old: and her life be always pass in make other life happy.
"and what sort of a husband would this Pendennis be?" many a reader will ask, doubt the happiness of such a marriage and the fortune of Laura. the querist, if they meet she, be refer to that lady herself, who, see his fault and wayward mood--see and own that there be man well than he--love he always with the most constant affection. his child or their mother have never hear a harsh word from he; and when his fit of moodiness and solitude be over, welcome he back with a never-fail regard and confidence. his friend be his friend still,-- entirely heart-whole. that malady be never fatal to a sound organ. and George go through his part of godpapa perfectly, and live alone. if Mr. Pen's work have procure he more reputation than have be acquire by his abler friend, whom no one know, George life content without the fame. if the good man do not draw the great prize in life, we know it have be so settle by the Ordainer of the lottery. we own, and see daily, how the false and worthless live and prosper, while the good be call away, and the dear and young perish untimely,--we perceive in every man's life the maim happiness, the frequent fall, the bootless endeavour, the struggle of Right and Wrong, in which the strong often succumb and the swift fail: we see flower of good blooming in foul place, as, in the most lofty and splendid fortune, flaw of vice and meanness, and stain of evil; and, know how mean the good of we be, let we give a hand of charity to Arthur Pendennis, with all his fault and shortcoming, who do not claim to be a hero, but only a man and a brother.
the END
